places.name: Abano Terme

visits.text: Relax in Abano Terme, a town completely dedicated to spa culture where hundreds of hotels and facilities offer thermal treatments. The Romans were the first to bathe in these volcanic waters, and the tradition has continued unbroken for over two millennia.

Experience the therapeutic mud treatments, thermal pools, and steam grottos that have made this region Europe's largest thermal basin. The mineral-rich waters emerge at temperatures up to 87°C from deep underground sources.

activities.text: Explore the elegant spa town's gardens and promenades, where the wellness tradition blends with refined hospitality. The proximity to the Euganean Hills provides beautiful walking opportunities between treatments.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Duomo di San Lorenzo Abano Terme

visits.text: Basilica of the Holy Sepulchre - Nicknamed the "Jerusalem of Europe," this 9th-century basilica houses in its Romanesque crypt the world's oldest reproduction of Jerusalem's Holy Sepulchre. Legend tells that Matilda of Westphalia, mother of Emperor Otto I, was traveling to Rome with mules laden with gold when the animals refused to advance at Acquapendente. A dream commanded her to build the church here. The 11th-century crypt—24 columns with all-different capitals forming nine naves beneath groin vaults—is a true "stone forest." The current facade is the work of Nicola Salvi, architect of Rome's Trevi Fountain.

Historic center and Torre del Barbarossa - The former double circuit of walls preserves three gates, including the Torre Julia de Jacopo—named after a woman who in 1550 closed the gate under enemy fire to save the town from assault by Orsini troops. On the hill dominating the town, the clock tower is the last vestige of the fortress built by Emperor Henry IV in the 12th century.

Bosco del Sasseto - 12 km from Acquapendente, near the village of Torre Alfina (classified among the Borghi più Belli d'Italia), this monumental forest shelters centuries-old oaks, beeches, and hornbeams whose twisted trunks grow between enormous moss-covered lava boulders. The atmosphere evokes a fairy-tale setting. The neo-Gothic castle of Torre Alfina, restored by Sienese architect Giuseppe Partini, overlooks the wood.

activities.text: Via Francigena stage - Acquapendente is the first Lazio town for pilgrims coming from the north. The Pilgrim House, steps from the cathedral, still welcomes walkers with 25 beds managed by the Confraternity of San Rocco. This is where Saint Roch allegedly performed his first miracle by healing plague victims in the 14th century.

Pugnaloni and traditions - Every third Sunday in May, the Festival of the Madonna del Fiore commemorates the town's liberation from Frederick Barbarossa's yoke in 1166. The "Pugnaloni," giant mosaics composed of flower petals and leaves, are carried in procession through the streets. A tradition unique in all of Italy.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio

places.name: Agnone

visits.text: Pontificia Fonderia Marinelli: founded in 1040, the world's oldest continuously operating bell foundry. The "Pontificia" title was granted by Pius IX in 1924. The lost-wax technique hasn't changed since the Middle Ages: clay mold fired, wax melted away, bronze poured at 1,100°C. The Marinelli have cast bells for the Vatican, French cathedrals and hundreds of European churches.
Museo Internazionale della Campana: adjacent to the foundry, collection of bells from five continents, foundry tools and Marinelli archives spanning ten centuries.

Agnone historic center: Samnite art town with numerous medieval and baroque churches. Goldsmithing and copperworking tradition documented since the Middle Ages. Every December 8th and 24th, the Ndocciata gathers hundreds of bearers carrying giant torches (ndocce) in procession—a fire festival of pre-Roman origin.

activities.text: Explore the artisan workshops downtown: goldsmiths, coppersmiths and potters perpetuating crafts documented since the 11th century.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Marco Evangelista Agnone

places.name: Agrigento

visits.text: Agrigento's Valley of the Temples, founded in 580 BC as Akragas, contains the largest ensemble of Doric temples outside Greece. Pindar described it as "the most beautiful of mortal cities." The UNESCO site sprawls across 1,300 hectares.

The Temple of Concordia, built around 430 BC, ranks among the world's best-preserved Greek temples. Its survival stems from its conversion into a Christian church in the 6th century. All 34 columns, standing 6.70 meters tall, retain their original golden hue.

The Temple of Hera Lacinia, at the ridge's eastern end, dominates the valley from its 25 partially standing columns. The charring marks on its stones date from the fire set by Carthaginians during the city's sack in 406 BC.

The Temple of Olympian Zeus, erected after the victory over Carthage in 480 BC, was the largest Doric temple ever attempted: 113 by 56 meters, never completed. One of its telamones—7.65-meter atlas figures—lies reconstructed on the ground.

Agrigento's Regional Archaeological Museum houses the reconstructed telamon from the Temple of Zeus (7.65 m tall) and an exceptional collection of Greek vases, coins, and sculptures. Its topographical section illuminates the complete urban layout of ancient Akragas.

Agrigento's Hellenistic-Roman quarter, discovered in 1950, reveals a perfectly gridded street plan with perpendicular roads 6 meters wide, underground drainage systems, and the foundations of atrium houses from the 2nd and 1st centuries BC.

activities.text: Lunch on Via Atenea with macco di fave—dried fava bean soup with olive oil and wild fennel, an Agrigento dish of Greek origin.

Excursion to Caltagirone (40 km): its 142 steps of the Santa Maria del Monte Staircase are covered in ceramic tiles illustrating each century of the town's history. Caltagirone ceramics are classified as UNESCO intangible heritage.
Night visit to the Valley of the Temples on Mondays and Fridays from May to October: the illuminated temples create an extraordinary atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily
Complete Tour of Sicily

map_keywords: Scalinata di Santa Maria del Monte Caltagirone

visits.text: Castello Angioino di Agropoli - fortress built by the Byzantines in the 6th century atop a 60-meter promontory commanding the sea, later remodeled by the Angevins in the 14th century and the Aragonese in the 15th. Three square towers and a central keep controlled the entrance to the Gulf of Policastro. The castle serves as the gateway to Cilento, Vallo di Diano and Alburni National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998.

activities.text: Wander through the narrow lanes of the medieval old town perched on its promontory, with sweeping views across the Gulf of Salerno and the Licosa islands, before continuing south toward Palinuro.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to Calabria: The Tyrrhenian Coast

visits.text: Climb to the acropolis of Alatri, built in the 7th century BC by the Italic Hernici people. The outer wall, stretching over 2 km and nearly intact, is formed of polygonal blocks assembled without mortar, some exceeding three tons. It covers approximately 25 hectares and is considered by archaeologists as the largest pre-Roman acropolis in Italy after Athens. Two monumental gateways survive: the Porta Maggiore, whose lintel is a single monolithic block, and a lower pedestrian gate.

The Church of Santa Maria Maggiore was built in the 5th century on the ruins of a temple of Venus, then reconstructed with a 14th-century facade and bell tower dated 1394. Inside: a 13th-century carved wooden group depicting the "Virgin of Constantinople" and a triptych of the Redeemer attributed to Antonio da Alatri (14th-15th century).

Saint Paul's Cathedral, whose origins date to the 10th century, occupies the very esplanade of the acropolis. Its position is exceptional: it stands on the site of the ancient main temple of the Hernician city, within the cyclopean walls.

The Municipal Archaeological Museum, installed in the Palazzo Gottifredo (a Roman-era building), displays finds from excavations conducted on Alatri's territory since the 19th century: Hernician ceramics, coins, funerary stelae, and architectural fragments from the acropolis.

activities.text: Walk the outer perimeter of the cyclopean walls following the trail that circles the hill: the view over the valley of the Ernici Mountains extends to the ridges of southern Lazio, and you can clearly perceive the defensive logic of the Hernician builders who selected their stones based on natural shape, without any cutting.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Visit the Cathedral of San Lorenzo in Piazza Risorgimento, a 15th-century Gothic building that represents the religious heart of Alba. The three-nave interior, in gold and blue colors, elegantly contrasts with the exterior of red brick typical of Piedmontese architecture.

Admire the three medieval towers visible from Piazza Duomo: Torre Bonino, Torre Astesiano, and Torre Sineo, all dating from the 12th century. These stone sentinels bear witness to the turbulent past of a city frequently under attack and therefore heavily defended.

Discover Underground Alba with a fascinating guided tour led by an archaeologist through the city's underground spaces. From the ancient Roman temple to the theater, to the now-vanished medieval towers, an adventure through time to discover the oldest roots of this ancient city.

Explore the Church of the Maddalena on Via Vittorio Emanuele, a jewel of Piedmontese Baroque from the second half of the 18th century by Bernardo Antonio Vittone. The wooden portal on the façade is decorated with three crossed arrows, symbol of Blessed Margaret of Savoy.

activities.text: Immerse yourself in the gastronomic tradition of Alba, a UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy since 2017. Taste the tajarin with butter and truffle, raviolini al plin, Alba-style raw beef, and the famous bonet, accompanied by local wines like Dolcetto d'Alba.

If you visit Alba in autumn, don't miss the International White Truffle Fair, an event that for over 90 years has celebrated the precious tuber of the Langhe with tastings, auctions, and shows in the world capital of truffles.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days

visits.text: Caves of Castellana: discovered on January 23, 1938, by speleologist Franco Anelli who first descended into the chasm called "la Grave," this karst network extends 3,348 meters in length and 122 meters in depth, at 330 meters elevation on the Murge plateau. La Grave itself measures 60 meters deep, 50 wide, and 100 long — a hall so vast Anelli's acetylene lamp couldn't illuminate it entirely. The complete route (about 3 km) ends at the Grotta Bianca, considered among the purest calcite caves in Europe. Constant temperature of 16.5°C inside.

Alberobello — Rione Monti: this neighborhood of more than 1,030 trulli slopes down the southern hillside and has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1996. The trulli, built of dry limestone without mortar from the 16th century onward, answered a fiscal trick: the Counts of Acquaviva d'Aragon allowed peasants to build these dismantleable houses to escape the royal tax on permanent dwellings — until 1797 and the end of feudalism. Look for the Trulli Siamesi, two cones fused at the base but with separate entrances, associated with the legend of a quarrel between two brothers.

Alberobello — Rione Aia Piccola and Trullo Sovrano: the second historic quarter has 590 less-touristy trulli, still inhabited. The Trullo Sovrano is the town's only two-story trullo: its walls reach 2.7 meters thick. Built in the 18th century, it now houses its original furnishings.

Alberobello — Belvedere Santa Lucia and Sant'Antonio church: from this terrace on the hill opposite Rione Monti, you overlook a sea of conical roofs. The Church of Sant'Antonio (1926) is the only church in the world built entirely in trullo style, with a 21-meter interior dome height.

activities.text: Evening stroll through the lanes of Rione Monti: fading light emphasizes the silvery gray of the chianche (limestone slabs) and the symbols painted in white lime on the pinnacles — crosses, stars, primitive figures, characters originally apotropaic to ward off the evil eye.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

visits.text: Alghero's ramparts were built by the Aragonese starting in 1353 when Peter IV of Aragon settled Majorcan and Catalan colonists here, and they've retained over 70% of their original perimeter with seven 16th-century towers still intact. Catalan remains an officially recognized minority language — street signs bear bilingual names, and the Iberian accent lingers in certain families to this day.

The Bastioni Marco Polo run along the sea for several hundred meters above the limestone rocks. At sunset, golden light spills across the waters of the Gulf of Alghero, drawing locals to the evening promenade as it has for centuries.

Capo Caccia is a limestone promontory rising 170 meters straight out of the sea, 24 km from Alghero. At its base opens the Grotta di Nettuno, a karst cave discovered by a local fisherman in the 18th century, accessible by boat or via the 654 steps of the Escala del Cabirol carved into the cliff face in 1959.

Maria Pia Beach stretches for 2 km north of Alghero, backed by a pine forest of umbrella pines planted in the 1930s to stabilize the dunes. Its fine white sand and shallow waters make it the city's most popular family beach.
Le Bombarde Beach, 8 km north of Alghero in Porto Conte Bay, is framed by rocks and Mediterranean scrubland. The rocky seabed makes it an excellent snorkeling spot.

activities.text: Boat crossing from Alghero port to the Grotta di Nettuno: 3-hour round trip with views of the Capo Caccia cliffs and Porto Conte Bay.
Taste aragosta all'algherese in a harborside trattoria — a Catalan lobster recipe with tomatoes, onion, olive oil, and vinegar that's distinct from the rest of Sardinia.
Swimming and snorkeling in the coves of Porto Conte Bay, a protected marine area with some of the clearest waters in northwest Sardinia.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Sardinia Coast: Beaches & Hidden Coves
Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

visits.text: Leave Castelmezzano in the morning heading southeast (1.5 hours' drive). Craco appears in the distance, perched on its spur like a phantom apparition amid the clay hills. This medieval village founded in the 8th century was abandoned in 1963 due to a landslide, then definitively evacuated after the 1980 Irpinia earthquake. The guided tour (mandatory, 60-90 minutes) departs from the Museo Emozionale in the former San Pietro convent. Helmeted, you enter the secured ruins: the Chiesa Madre San Nicola (14th century) with a saint's mummified body, the Norman tower, the Maronna and Grossi palaces. Craco served as a set for Mel Gibson's The Passion of the Christ and appears on the World Monuments Fund Watch List.

Continue to Aliano (30 minutes' drive). The village is perched on a ridge of white clay amid a lunar landscape. This is where Carlo Levi, a Turin physician and painter and anti-fascist, was confined from October 3, 1935 to May 20, 1936. This experience produced Christ Stopped at Eboli. Visit the Carlo Levi Picture Gallery (photos and paintings from exile), his reconstructed house, and the House of Malocchio—a dwelling built in the shape of a human face to ward off evil spirits. At the cemetery, Levi's grave: he wanted to be buried here, in this village he had come to love.

activities.text: In late afternoon, walk to the Calanchi viewpoint. These geological formations—deep gullies in the clay, sculpted by erosion over millions of years—create a landscape of sharp ridges, whitish pinnacles, and desert-like valleys. The silence is total, broken only by the flight of black kites. It's the exact setting described by Levi in his book. Marked trails cross the Regional Natural Park of the Calanchi—bring good shoes and avoid rainy days.

days_plan.itin_title: Basilicata: From Matera to Pollino, Castles, Ghost Towns, and the Lucanian Dolomites

visits.text: Alicudi, the most isolated Aeolian island with 100 permanent residents, has no roads, no cars, no motorcycles — only steep trails and donkeys for transport. Electricity arrived only in 1990, telephones in 1991.

Monte Filo dell'Arpa (675 m), accessible via 2,000 steps carved into the rock from the port, is the only way to reach the agricultural terraces. The climb takes 2 hours and offers views of all the Aeolian Islands.

activities.text: Snorkeling and swimming in the port waters, among the clearest in the archipelago thanks to the absence of pollution and maritime traffic.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Altamura

visits.text: Altamura - Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta: Built by Frederick II between 1232 and 1254 as a palatine chapel — a status that removed it from the Bishop of Bari's authority and placed it directly under imperial authority. In 1248, Pope Innocent IV was forced to accept this fait accompli. The facade features a 14th-century portal carved with 22 Gospel scenes, supported by two stone lions remade in 1533. The upper Gothic section with its two bell towers dates from the 16th century. A 17th-century historian suggested the church occupied the site of a Greek temple dedicated to Castor and Pollux.

Altamura - claustri and historic center: Frederick II founded or refounded Altamura in 1230, bringing in Latin, Greek and Jewish populations. The result was about 80 claustri — small interior courtyards with single entrances, in Greek style (with central well) or Arabic (long and narrow). These micro-community spaces functioned as both social units and defensive structures. In 1993, in the Lamalunga cave on the city's outskirts, the Altamura Man was discovered: a Neanderthal skeleton estimated at 130,000-172,000 years old, one of the oldest and best preserved in Europe.

Gravina in Puglia - canyon and aqueduct bridge: This town is built on the edge of a limestone canyon (the gravina) inhabited since the 8th century BC. The 18th-century aqueduct bridge spanning the ravine is one of the most spectacular structures in inland Puglia — and one of the region's most underrated sites. In 2020, Gravina was chosen as the setting for the James Bond film No Time to Die for a car chase scene on this bridge.

Gravina - San Michele delle Grotte cave church: Carved into the canyon limestone, this church houses human bones stacked on the walls — the presumed victims of a medieval Saracen raid according to local tradition. Partially preserved frescoes testify to a Byzantine monastic presence. The Gravina Sotterranea, a network of caves and tunnels beneath the city, can be explored with a guide.

activities.text: Purchase Altamura DOP bread from an artisanal bakery in the center: This is the only bread in Europe with PDO status. Made from re-milled durum wheat semolina and baked in stone wood-fired ovens, it keeps for an entire week. Horace, traveling on the Via Appia in 37 BC, noted in his Satires that Altamura produced 'the finest bread anywhere' and advised stocking up.

Hiking in the Gravina canyon: A trail descends into the ravine, passes beneath the aqueduct bridge and allows observation of the troglodyte dwellings carved into the canyon walls from below. Allow 1h30 round trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Puglia: Frederick II's Castles, Hidden Hinterland & Forgotten Villages

map_keywords: Chiesa Rupestre San Michele delle Grotte Gravina in Puglia

places.name: Amalfi

visits.text: The Cathedral of Sant'Andrea in Amalfi, built from 987 onward by Duke Mansone I, is the emblem of the ancient Maritime Republic of Amalfi—first of the four Italian Maritime Republics, which in the 10th-11th centuries controlled part of Mediterranean trade and counted approximately 70,000 inhabitants. It's accessed by a 57-step staircase from Piazza Duomo. The Arab-Norman facade blends golden mosaics, horseshoe arches, bicolor stripes, and ceramics. The bronze doors were cast in Constantinople in 1057—the first of their kind in Italy. The Cloister of Paradise (1266-1268), with its interlaced Moorish-influenced arches on twin columns, served as a cemetery for the Amalfitan nobility.

The Arsenal of the Republic (Arsenale della Repubblica), a 12th-century shipyard preserved in its original state, houses the Museum of the Compass and Maritime Duchy. Amalfi's sailors were among the first in the West to use the compass for navigation. Around 1070, Amalfitan merchants established in Jerusalem founded the Hospital of Jerusalem—origin of the Knights Hospitaller of St. John.

Ravello perches at 365 meters above the Tyrrhenian Sea, on a rocky promontory between Amalfi and Minori. Villa Rufolo, built in the 13th century by the Rufolo family—one of the most powerful in medieval Amalfi's Republic—blends Arab, Norman, and Gothic influences in a unique hybrid architecture. Its belvedere overlooking the Gulf of Salerno impressed Richard Wagner during his 1880 visit: he found here the inspiration for Klingsor's magic garden in Parsifal. In his honor, Ravello hosts an international classical music festival each summer in the villa's gardens.

Villa Cimbrone, whose origins date to the 11th century, was profoundly transformed in the early 20th century by British aristocrat Ernest William Beckett, who created gardens, terraces, and a cloister in mixed styles—neo-Gothic, neo-Moorish, Venetian. Its public gardens extend along the cliff edge. The Terrace of Infinity, a balcony lined with marble busts at 365 meters elevation, offers the Amalfi Coast's most famous panorama: the sea and sky merge on the horizon. Virginia Woolf, Greta Garbo, Winston Churchill, and Gore Vidal stayed here.

Atrani, 700 meters from Amalfi on foot along the coast, is Italy's smallest municipality by area. A former stronghold of the Amalfi Republic, it has preserved its labyrinth of lanes, arches, and stairways without the tourist crowds of its neighbor. Its small pebble beach, sheltered in a cliff hollow, is one of the coast's most tranquil.

Minori, 6 km from Amalfi, houses the remains of a 1st-century Roman maritime villa—proof that the Amalfi Coast was already a resort for Roman nobility. The Basilica of Santa Trofimena (11th-12th century) holds the remains of the village's patron saint. Between Minori and Maiori stretches the Sentiero dei Limoni, a marked 2-km path through lemon terraces overlooking the sea.

activities.text: Boat excursion from Amalfi's port to the Grotta dello Smeraldo (Emerald Grotto), a karst cavity discovered in 1932 whose waters take on an intense emerald color thanks to light filtered through an underwater opening at 4 meters depth. Swimming in a cove from the boat.

After Ravello, descend to Maiori for an afternoon of relaxation on the Amalfi Coast's largest beach. Protected from winds in a natural cove, Maiori's sandy beach contrasts with the pebbles of the rest of the coast. Evening stroll along the waterfront promenade.

Beach day at Maiori or Minori—the coast's only two sandy beaches—to conclude the circuit by the sea. Swimming, seafood lunch at a waterfront trattoria, and leisurely return to Amalfi's port in late afternoon.

Hidden Villages of the Amalfi Coast: From Praiano to Salerno

Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

Castello di San Nicola de Thoro Plano Maiori

visits.text: Amantea—a medieval town of 13,000 on a 177-meter promontory above the Tyrrhenian Sea, 30 km from Lamezia Terme airport. The historic center preserves the 15th-century Torre Aragonese and the 13th-century church of San Francesco d'Assisi with a Gothic portal. The town was an important silk production center until the 19th century, exporting to Naples and Genoa.

activities.text: Final swim on Amantea's fine sand beaches before returning to Lamezia Terme airport (30 km).

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

visits.text: Discovery of the hidden beach of Lido Il Faro in Anacapri, surrounded by cliffs and rocky terraces.
Walk to the fort of Punta Carena to admire the Faraglioni and the Tyrrhenian Sea.

activities.text: Swimming and relaxation in a secret cove of Capri.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters

visits.text: Anagni Cathedral was built between 1072 and 1104 by Bishop Pietro da Salerno and consecrated in 1105 by Pope Paschal II. This Romanesque-Gothic marvel stretches 80 meters and features a stunning Cosmatesque floor installed in 1231, along with a ciborium sculpted by Vassalletto in 1267. Anagni was the birthplace of four popes, including Boniface VIII (born c. 1235). Within these walls, Alexander III excommunicated Frederick Barbarossa on March 24, 1160, and Clare of Assisi was canonized in 1255.

Descend into the Crypt of San Magno, built simultaneously with the cathedral (1072-1104) and frescoed between 1237 and 1255 across 540 square meters—often called the Sistine Chapel of the Middle Ages. The transition from Byzantine to Romanesque style unfolds panel by panel. Lighting is switched off every half-hour to protect the pigments, with only twenty minutes of illumination per cycle: time your visit accordingly.

The Palace of Boniface VIII, built in the early 13th century by the Conti family in Gothic style, houses a museum with 13th-century frescoes. This palace was the stage for the infamous 1303 "Slap of Anagni," when Sciarra Colonna, acting on behalf of France's King Philip IV, struck Pope Boniface VIII to humiliate papal authority before royal power.

The Palazzo della Ragione, built around 1160 by Jacopo da Iseo, served as the seat of medieval civic assemblies. Its facade overlooking the main square stands as one of the best-preserved examples of Romanesque civil architecture in the region.

activities.text: Wander through Anagni's historic center from the Porta degli Angeli to Piazza Innocenzo III: cobbled lanes reveal 12th and 13th-century cardinal palaces, medieval fountains, and chiseled limestone doorways that remind you at every turn of the era when this town rivaled Rome itself.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Stroll through Angera's center and along its quays bordering the southern tip of Lake Maggiore on the Lombard side. The small town is dominated from afar by the crenellated silhouette of the Rocca whose swallowtail merlons can be recognized from the lake.

Visit the Rocca Borromea of Angera, a medieval fortress owned by the Borromeo family since 1449. The earliest traces of the building date back to 1066, when it belonged to the archbishops of Milan. The Visconti took control in 1277 after the Battle of Desio—a victory commemorated in the spectacular frescoes of the Hall of Justice, painted shortly after the event by the 'Master of Angera' and constituting one of the major examples of secular painting from the late Lombard Gothic. Vitaliano I Borromeo bought the complex in 1449 for 12,800 imperial pounds. The Rocca is the harmonious result of five building sections constructed between the 11th and 17th centuries.

Spend an hour at the Doll and Toy Museum, the largest in Europe, founded in 1988 by Princess Bona Borromeo Arese. Its 12 rooms distributed across the Visconti and Borromeo wings display over 1,300 pieces—dolls, French and German automatons from the 19th century, toys from around the world—from the 18th century to contemporary creations. A separate room is dedicated to dolls and toys from non-European cultures.

Explore the Rocca's medieval garden, 2,000 m² enclosed between the vineyards and the crenellated walls overlooking the lake. This garden was reconstructed from historical sources—illuminated codices and period documents—and presents the three types of medieval gardens: the Princes' Garden, the Verzière (vegetable garden), and the Herb Garden.

activities.text: Climb to the Rocca's tower viewpoints to admire the panorama over Lake Maggiore and the Alps. The Torre Castellana offers the highest view of both lake shores—the Lombard side with Angera, and the Piedmont side with Arona, where the Rocca d'Arona once stood before Napoleon ordered its demolition in 1800. Together with Angera, it formed the lake's historic lock.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

visits.text: Fortified village of Anghiari: ranked among the most beautiful villages in Italy, Anghiari rises on a hill overlooking the Valtiberina plain, protected by bastions of which a portion survives. The Torre del Campano, dating from the 13th century and rebuilt in the 16th century after its destruction by Vitellozzo Vitelli in 1502, marks the village's silhouette. The Palazzo Pretorio on Piazza del Popolo displays a facade covered in the coats of arms of the Florentine vicars who administered the town after 1440, typical of fortress-towns governed from Florence.

Museum of the Battle of Anghiari — Palazzo del Marzocco: the museum, opened in 2000 and expanded in 2007, traces the history of the battle of June 29, 1440, in which the Florentines led by Micheletto Attendolo defeated the Milanese under Niccolò Piccinino, securing Florence's independence. The battle would have remained a local episode were it not for Leonardo da Vinci, commissioned in 1503 by Gonfalonier Piero Soderini to paint it in the Hall of the Five Hundred of the Palazzo Vecchio. Using experimental encaustic, Leonardo managed only to sketch the "Battle for the Standard" before leaving Florence in 1506; Vasari covered the unfinished work in 1563. The museum holds a 17th-century copy by the Flemish engraver Gérard Edelinck, as well as engravings by Dürer and Chagall inspired by Leonardo's sketches.

Museo Statale di Palazzo Taglieschi: a national museum housed in a Renaissance palace, devoted to the history of Anghiari and the Valtiberina from the Middle Ages to the 18th century. It brings together local sculpture and painting as well as documentation on the tradition of firearm manufacture — Anghiari was in the 18th century a center renowned for the fineness of its arms decorations.

activities.text: Trail of the Battle of Anghiari to the Maestà della Vittoria: a roughly 1-hour hike from the historic center to the exact spot where the Florentines defeated the Milanese in June 1440. The path follows the ancient straight road built in the late 14th century linking Anghiari to Sansepolcro, along which the former Byzantine church of Santo Stefano and traces of Roman centuriation are still visible.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

places.name: Anversa Degli Abruzzi

visits.text: Anversa degli Abruzzi - a village of 400 inhabitants on a rocky spur at 660 meters at the entrance to the Gole del Sagittario. Vestiges of an 11th-century Norman castle dominate the village. The Chiesa di Santa Maria delle Grazie preserves a 12th-century Romanesque portal and 15th-century frescoes.

activities.text: View of the Gole del Sagittario entrance from the village belvedere - the 200-meter-high limestone cliffs framing the torrent are visible from the historic center terraces.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria delle Grazie Anversa

visits.text: Aosta (Augusta Praetoria Salassorum) was founded in 25 BC by the Romans following the final defeat of the Salassi people. The city preserves its Roman grid plan intact: today's streets still follow the ancient cardines and decumani orientation. The Arch of Augustus, erected immediately after the foundation to honor the alliance with the empire, stands 37 feet high with a single 30-foot arch, flanked by ten Corinthian half-columns on a Doric entablature. In the Middle Ages, a crucifix was placed there and the arch took the name Saint-Voût (from Latin votum)—the original 15th-century crucifix is now preserved in the Cathedral Treasury Museum.

The Porta Praetoria, built in 25 BC, was the Roman city's main eastern entrance. It comprises two parallel arches, each with three openings—the central arch for chariots, the two lateral ones for pedestrians. The outer façade partially preserves its original travertine marble facing, revealed during 2012 excavations 10 feet below the current level. The adjacent Roman theater, whose scenic façade reaches 72 feet, the underground cryptoporticus with triple gallery (formerly the Romans' medieval market), and the 2,375 × 1,712-foot wall complete the archaeological circuit.

The Collegiate Church of Sant'Orso, founded on a 4th-5th century paleo-Christian basilica, preserves a Romanesque cloister (1460) with grey marble columns decorated with historiated capitals among northern Italy's most remarkable. Ottonian frescoes from the 11th century adorn the church vaults, depicting Gospel scenes in an expressive style rare at this altitude. The neighboring cathedral (12th century, remodeled 15th-16th centuries) houses in its choir two floor mosaics from the 12th and 14th centuries representing the months of the year, fantastic animals, the Tigris and Euphrates.

The megalithic area of Saint-Martin-de-Corléans, west of Aosta, is one of the Alps' most important prehistoric sites. Used from the fifth millennium BC, it has yielded anthropomorphic stelae remarkably similar to those of Petit Chasseur at Sion (Valais), testifying to trans-Alpine cultural links well before Roman times.

activities.text: Ascend to Pila from Aosta by gondola (17 minutes, 5,905 feet elevation): in summer, 43 miles of marked trails, panoramas across the entire Gran Paradiso and Mont Blanc massifs. Mountain biking domain with possible return on foot to Aosta.

Market at Piazza Chanoux (Aosta's pedestrian heart): local cheeses including Fontina DOP (aged in natural caves), the valley also produces Lard d'Arnad and mocetta (dried chamois meat). Valdostan blood sausage with potatoes and Jambon de Bosses ham (Saint-Rhémy) round out the must-try specialties.

days_plan.itin_title: Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc

visits.text: Aquileia - Roman city once second only to Rome, major archaeological site
National Archaeological Museum - modern museum presenting the treasures of the ancient city
Basilica of Aquileia - patriarchal basilica with extraordinary early Christian mosaics

activities.text: Visit to the Roman Forum and the ancient river port
Ascent to the bell tower for exclusive panoramic view of the site and the lagoon
Discovery of the basilica's mosaics among the most important in the ancient Christian world

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

visits.text: Piazza Grande: Arezzo's central square since the 13th century, tilted with a 10-meter drop from top to bottom. It is bordered by the apse of Santa Maria della Pieve (12th century), the Palazzo della Fraternità dei Laici (Gothic and Renaissance facade, 14th–15th centuries), the Palazzo Pretorio (14th century) and Vasari's Logge, designed in 1573 and completed in 1595. During its construction in 1553, the Medici fortress unearthed the Chimera of Arezzo — a 78.5-cm-tall Etruscan bronze ex-voto, now in the National Archaeological Museum of Florence. Replicas adorn fountains near the station and at Porta San Lorentino.

Basilica of San Francesco and Piero della Francesca's cycle: a Gothic building erected between the late 13th and late 14th centuries, made famous by the Bacci Chapel, whose decoration was entrusted to Piero della Francesca after the death of Bicci di Lorenzo in 1452. Piero executed the 15 scenes of the Legend of the True Cross between 1452 and 1466, covering the three registers of the side walls. The iconographic program, drawn from the Golden Legend of Jacobus de Voragine, follows not chronological order but formal and symbolic correspondences between scenes: the meeting of the Queen of Sheba and King Solomon mirrors the battle of Heraclius, and the two battle scenes echo each other. A ten-year restoration completed in 2000 returned the frescoes to their original colors.

Cathedral of San Donato: one of the most representative examples of Italian Gothic architecture, its construction spanning from the 13th to the 16th century. It houses the stained-glass windows of Guillaume de Marcillat — including the 1524 rose window depicting Honorius III's approval of the Indulgence of Saint Francis — as well as Piero della Francesca's Mary Magdalene fresco, tucked at the back of the left nave, and a painting by Pietro da Cortona depicting the Nativity.

Church of San Domenico and Cimabue's Crucifix: a medieval church founded in the 13th century with austere Gothic architecture. The Cimabue Crucifix — dated by scholars to the decade 1265–1275 — hangs above the high altar. It's one of the Florentine painter's earliest works, already revealing his break from the Byzantine manner: the body of Christ begins to curve naturally, foreshadowing the humanism of Giotto.

Giorgio Vasari House-Museum: the personal residence that the architect, painter and first art critic of the Renaissance — born in Arezzo in 1511 — decorated himself with frescoes of allegories and portraits of great artists. The building, acquired in 1541 and transformed through 1548, reflects Vasari's taste for Mannerist decoration and his relationship to the history of art that he would codify in his Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects (1550).

Santa Maria della Pieve: a 12th-century Romanesque church whose facade of stacked pilasters is adorned with sculptures attributed to Lombard masters. The interior preserves the polyptych by Pietro Lorenzetti (1320–1324), Madonna and Child between Saint John the Baptist, Saint Donatus, Saint John the Evangelist and Saint Matthew, on the high altar. The campanile of the Hundred Holes, pierced by 40 windows across five levels, is one of the most distinctive Romanesque bell towers in Tuscany.

National Archaeological Museum Mecenate: housed in the former convent of San Bernardo, built against the 1st–2nd-century Roman amphitheater. It brings together the Etruscan and Roman collections of the region, including sigillata corallium ceramics — the distinctive production of Arretium whose coral-red color characterized Roman exports — and Etruscan bronzes. The amphitheater exterior, with its surviving arc of seating, can be freely explored.

House of Petrarch and the medieval historic center: Francesco Petrarca, born in Arezzo in 1304, is alongside Dante and Boccaccio one of the three founders of the Italian literary language. His birthplace, at the end of Via dell'Orto, provides access to a biographical museum. The historic center also reveals the medieval Lappoli tower-house on Piazza Grande, and the heritage of a city that was in the Middle Ages a powerful bishopric and a Ghibelline stronghold that resisted the Guelph forces of Florence until the Battle of Campaldino (1289).

activities.text: Giostra del Saracino: a jousting tournament rooted in the Middle Ages, revived in 1931 as a 14th-century reenactment. It takes place twice a year on the Piazza Grande: the penultimate Saturday of June (Joust of San Donato) and the first Sunday of September. The four quarters — Porta Santo Spirito, Porta Sant'Andrea, Porta del Foro and Porta Crucifera — compete in a tournament where mounted knights charge and strike the shield of a pivoting Saracen effigy. The procession mobilizes over 350 costumed participants and 27 horses.

Arezzo Antique Fair: one of the largest antique fairs in Europe, created in 1968 by Ivan Bruschi. On the first Saturday and Sunday of every month, the Piazza Grande and surrounding streets host more than 500 dealers from across Italy, specializing in furniture, paintings, objects and antique jewelry.

Filming locations for Life Is Beautiful: Roberto Benigni's 1997 film, which won three Academy Awards including Best Foreign Language Film, was largely shot in Arezzo. The Piazza Grande serves as the film's central square, and Via Vittorio Veneto was used as the set for the main character's bookshop. Guided film-location tours retrace these sites.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries
Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

visits.text: The acropolis of Civitavecchia, at 627 meters altitude above Arpino, is the original core of the Volscian then Samnite city, dating to the 7th-6th centuries BC. Its mortarless polygonal walls extend approximately 3 km. It contains the only known pre-Roman pointed-arch gate in Europe: an ogival arch cut from cyclopean blocks that existed before this type of arch was theorized by medieval Gothic architects. The 1581 land registry still designated it as Civitas Ciceroniana.

Arpino was the birthplace of three great men of Roman antiquity: Cicero (born January 3, 106 BC), Caius Marius (born 156 BC, seven-time consul), and Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa (born 63 BC, architect of the Pantheon of Agrippa in Rome). Plaques and statues recall these illustrious natives throughout the historic center.

Arpino's historic center features cyclopean walls visible even in the lower town, integrated into the foundations of medieval houses. The lanes of the upper section (Civitavecchia) are partially deserted and preserve an atmosphere of partial abandonment since the 18th century.

activities.text: Each year since 1980, the Certamen Ciceronianum Arpinas brings together high school students from around the world for an international Latin translation competition on texts by Cicero. If you're present in late April, the awards ceremony at the teatro comunale is open to the public.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

places.name: Arqua Petrarca

visits.text: Discover Arquà Petrarca, a beautiful stone village built on a hilltop overlooking the majestic Euganean Hills. This atmospheric settlement is famous as the place where the Herald of the Renaissance, Francesco Petrarca (Petrarch), spent his final years contemplating nature and composing poetry.

Visit Petrarch's House, preserved much as it was when the poet lived here in the 14th century. His tomb in the village square marks the final resting place of one of the founders of humanism and the Italian literary tradition.

activities.text: Wander the narrow stone lanes, enjoying the views that inspired Petrarch's contemplative verse. The village's unspoiled medieval character and poetic associations make it one of the most atmospheric destinations in the Euganean Hills.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Oratorio della Santissima Trinità Arquà Petrarca

places.name: Arsoli

visits.text: Castello Massimo di Arsoli — A medieval fortress with origins tracing back to the 11th century, transformed into a noble residence in the 16th-17th centuries by the Massimo family, one of Rome's most ancient patrician houses, who claim descent from Fabius Maximus. The castle retains terraced gardens with centuries-old trees and a Renaissance loggia overlooking the Aniene valley. Arsoli sits at 473 m altitude on the eastern foothills of the Monti Simbruini.

Centro storico di Arsoli — A medieval village with cobbled lanes, the Chiesa di Santa Croce, and vestiges of fortifications. The village occupies a strategic position on the ancient Via Valeria, the Roman road linking Rome to the Adriatic via the Abruzzo.

activities.text: Hiking in the Monti Simbruini — The Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Simbruini is Lazio's largest natural park, covering 30,000 hectares of forests, high-altitude pastures, and springs. The Aniene River rises in this massif. Marked trails depart from Arsoli toward the wooded plateaus.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

map_keywords: Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Simbruini

visits.text: The Villa Medici 'La Ferdinanda,' built in just four years from 1596 to 1600 by Bernardo Buontalenti for Grand Duke Ferdinand I de' Medici, is the last great Medici villa and the masterwork of Buontalenti's old age—too old to travel, he directed the works from his house on Via Maggio in Florence by correspondence. Its nickname, 'Villa of the Hundred Chimneys,' comes from Ferdinand I's desire to equip each room with its own hearth for winter hunting seasons: the chimneys, all different, bristle from the roof like a crown. The interiors are frescoed by Domenico Cresti (Il Passignano) and Bernardino Poccetti. The villa has been a UNESCO site since 2013.

The Archaeological Museum of Artimino 'Francesco Nicosia,' reinstalled since 2011 in the former grand-ducal stables of the village, displays Etruscan finds from the region: tumuli of Montefortini and Boschetti at Comeana (7th century BC, Orientalizing period), necropolis of Prato Rosello in the Artimino hills, and a 4th-century BC Etruscan red-figure krater with its perfectly preserved bronze symposium set. The Pietramarina archaeological area completes the Carmignano Archaeological Park.

The Pieve di San Leonardo ad Artimino, at the village gates, is one of the most significant examples of Romano-Lombard architecture in Tuscany. Its foundation is attributed to Countess Matilda of Canossa. The sober, compact building contrasts with the Medici Villa facing it.

activities.text: Tasting of Carmignano DOCG in a cellar in the Artimino hills. This red wine produced exclusively in the municipality of Carmignano is one of Italy's oldest appellation wines: Cosimo III de' Medici officially delimited its production zone in 1716. The dominant grape is Sangiovese, often blended with Cabernet Franc or Cabernet Sauvignon introduced to the region as early as the 17th century.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

places.name: Asciano

visits.text: Palazzo Corboli Museum: Housed in a 12th-century palace that belonged to the noble Bandinelli family, this museum unfolds over three floors a collection of sacred art and archaeology. The archaeological section gathers Etruscan and Roman remains from the territory, including Ambrogio Lorenzetti's Badia a Rofeno triptych and the remarkable 14th-century Wheel of Barlaam.

Torre della Mencia and Piazza del Grano: Standing 25 meters high, this medieval tower dominates Asciano's oldest square, once Siena's granary. The Fontana della Mengia at the tower's base, with its stone and bronze Gorgon head, is accompanied by a legend: whoever drinks from this fountain always returns to Asciano.

Abbey of Monte Oliveto Maggiore: Founded in 1313 by Bernardo Tolomei, a Sienese nobleman renouncing his fortune for a hermit's life, this red brick Benedictine abbey emerges from the woods of the Crete Senesi. Its Great Cloister is entirely decorated with frescoes by Luca Signorelli (late 15th century) and Il Sodoma (early 16th century) on the life of Saint Benedict—one of the masterpieces of Italian Renaissance painting. The monks still chant Gregorian vespers.

activities.text: Hiking in the Crete Senesi Park: The Crete Senesi Life Park covers the Asciano territory with marked, not-difficult trails through the clay hills. Trail No. 2, starting from Asciano, offers the widest views of the biancane—these characteristic whitish clay formations—and rejoins the historic center to finish at the Corboli Museum.
Palio dei Ciuchi: Every Sunday in September, Asciano's seven districts compete through the village streets—on donkeyback rather than horseback, a popular wink at Siena's Palio.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Abbazia di Monte Oliveto Maggiore Asciano

places.name: Asiago

visits.text: Sacrario Militare di Asiago - This funerary monument built in 1938 preserves the remains of 54,286 Italian and Austro-Hungarian soldiers who fell on the Asiago Plateau during World War I. The Plateau was one of the war's deadliest theaters: the battles of the Strafexpedition (Austrian offensive of June 1916) and the Italian counter-offensives of 1917-1918 killed tens of thousands for territorial gains of a few hundred meters. The monumental ossuary, with its crypt and galleries, conveys the scale of these battles in what is today a peaceful mountain landscape.

Museo della Grande Guerra di Canove - Inaugurated in 1974, this museum in Canove di Roana presents uniforms, weapons, tactical maps and photographic documents tracing the Plateau's battles from 1915 to 1918. The collections include objects recovered from surrounding battle sites and written testimonies from soldiers on both sides. The museum is one of the most rigorous on the conflict in Veneto.

activities.text: Museo all'Aperto di Monte Zebio - A few kilometers from Asiago, this open-air museum lets you walk through trenches and communication trenches carved into the rock during the Great War. Monte Zebio constituted a strategic Austrian strongpoint between 1916 and 1918. The firing positions, cave shelters and gallery networks are still largely visible. The discovery walk takes about two hours.

Ascent to Forte Verena - This Austro-Hungarian fort, built between 1908 and 1912 at 2,015 meters altitude, was captured by the Italians in 1916 and remained a strategic observation post until the war's end. The climb from Rifugio Verenetta (accessible by car) takes about 1 hour. The summit panorama embraces the entire Plateau, the Venetian plain and, on clear days, the Dolomites. The fort's remains are freely accessible.

Asiago historic center - The town of Asiago was entirely razed during the Great War and rebuilt between 1919 and 1925. Gabriele d'Annunzio called it 'Italy's smallest and brightest city.' Its center, remade in one piece in Liberty and neoclassical style, is exceptional in the mountain landscape. The two main piazzas—Piazza Carli and Piazza Risorgimento—and Corso IV Novembre form a cohesive urban ensemble rare in the alpine zone.

days_plan.itin_title: Vicenza Circuit: Palladian Villas and the Venetian Countryside

map_keywords: Museo Grande Guerra Canove di Roana

visits.text: Experience Asolo, known as the 'City of a Hundred Horizons' and the 'Pearl of the Province of Treviso'. This picture-perfect town in the Dolomite foothills has attracted artists, writers, and royalty for centuries with its beauty and cultural richness.

Explore the historic center with its elegant palazzos, ancient castle, and stunning views across vine-covered hills. The atmospheric streets and squares reveal why this has been a haven for creative spirits throughout history.

activities.text: Soak in the countless horizons visible from various viewpoints around the town - each turn reveals new panoramas of hills, vineyards, and distant mountains that have inspired generations of artists and poets.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

places.name: Asti

visits.text: Admire the Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta in Piazza Risorgimento, an imposing 15th-century Gothic building that represents one of Asti's most important monuments. The three-nave interior, decorated in gold and blue colors, contrasts magnificently with the exterior of characteristic Piedmontese red brick.

Explore the medieval towers that dot the historic center, testimony to Asti's glorious past when it was a powerful city-state. Torre Troyana, Torre Comentina, and Torre Rossa are the most famous and offer evocative glimpses among the historic palaces of the center.

Visit the Palazzo Comunale in Piazza Risorgimento, which houses precious ancient frescoes from the Church of San Domenico, including a moving 14th-century Pietà and a panel depicting the Virgin and Child by Macrino d'Alba from 1501.

activities.text: Participate in a tasting of the wines that have made Asti famous worldwide: Asti Spumante DOCG and Barbera d'Asti. The area's wineries offer food and wine experiences that pair great wines with traditional Piedmontese cuisine.

If you're here in September, don't miss the Palio di Asti, one of Italy's oldest horse races held in central Piazza Alfieri. The event is preceded by a magnificent historical parade with over 1,200 participants in medieval costumes.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days

map_keywords: Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta Asti

visits.text: Atina, holder of the Touring Club's Bandiera Arancione, was founded according to legend by the god Saturn—as were Anagni, Alatri, Arpino, and Ferentino. Cited by Virgil in the Aeneid among the cities allied with Turnus, it was first Volscian then Samnite (Pentri tribe) before being conquered by Rome during the Samnite Wars. Cicero wrote of it: "Atina, mother of many illustrious men, to such an extent that no city of Italy can be said to be richer."

Atina's polygonal walls form a total circuit of over 8,000 meters distributed in two distinct systems: the outer enclosure running along the heights of Monte Morrone, and the inner enclosure defending the hill of Santo Stefano. These works date to the 4th-3rd centuries BC and constitute one of the longest pre-Roman fortification perimeters in southern Lazio.

The Palazzo Cantelmo (ducal palace), built after the 1349 earthquake, features a two-tower facade and Gothic mullioned windows. It illustrates the architectural style of the Angevin lords who governed the region after the destruction caused by the earthquake.

The Giuseppe Visocchi Municipal Archaeological Museum assembles discoveries made on Atina's territory since the 19th century: a Roman funerary lion, elements of a pre-Roman necropolis discovered in 2002, a Roman mosaic uncovered in 1946, and scale models of war machines used by Roman and Samnite armies. Atina has been an episcopal seat since 800, attested by a diploma of Charlemagne.

activities.text: Follow the trail along the best-preserved sections of Atina's polygonal enclosure: blocks of local limestone, some exceeding two cubic meters in volume, interlock without mortar to heights reaching 4 to 5 meters in places. The trail requires no special equipment and offers views over the Comino Valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

places.name: Bagni Di Lucca

visits.text: Historic Thermal Center and Terme Bagni di Lucca: Known since Roman times, Bagni di Lucca's hot springs took off in the 11th century thanks to Countess Matilda of Canossa, before becoming in the 19th century one of Europe's most fashionable spa resorts. Elisa Baciocchi, Napoleon's sister and Princess of Lucca, had the Bernabò establishment built here in 1810, where waters emerge naturally at 39.2°C. The thermal complex has welcomed Byron, Shelley, Montale, Henry James, and Puccini. The English called the place "the Switzerland of Tuscany."

Real Casino of Ponte a Serraglio: Inaugurated in 1839, this casino was one of the very first in Europe—some sources credit Bagni di Lucca with inventing roulette around 1837. The Art Nouveau building, restored and reopened in 2009 after years of closure, overlooks the banks of the Lima and constitutes one of the best-preserved testimonies of 19th-century aristocratic tourism.

Ponte delle Catene (Chain Bridge): This iron suspension bridge designed by Lorenzo Nottolini—the same architect who redesigned Lucca's Piazza dell'Anfiteatro—is one of the oldest metal bridges still standing in Italy. It spans the Lima between the hamlets of Ponte a Serraglio and Villa Fiori.

Italy's first Anglican church and English cemetery: The British community established in Bagni di Lucca from the early 19th century built the country's first Anglican church here. The adjoining cemetery holds the graves of English, American, and other nationalities who chose to end their days in this valley.

activities.text: Hiking in Orrido di Botri: A few kilometers north, this canyon carved by the Pelago torrent through the Apennines offers vertical limestone gorges and waterfalls. The ascent follows the riverbed for several kilometers; a protective helmet is recommended. Access is regulated and best done with a local guide.
Thermal treatments: The Bagni Caldi thermal establishments offer pools, natural steam grottos, and treatments using sulfurous waters whose natural temperature approaches 39°C.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

map_keywords: Real Casino Ponte a Serraglio Bagni di Lucca
Ponte delle Catene Bagni di Lucca
Orrido di Botri Bagni di Lucca

places.name: Baranello

visits.text: Museo Civico Giuseppe Barone: 1,816 artifacts displayed in 28 wooden cases unchanged since the 1897 donation. Architect Barone spent a lifetime collecting Attic vases (6th-5th century BC), medieval bronzes, Pompeii fresco fragments, Capodimonte, Sèvres and Japanese porcelain, plus 43 Neapolitan and Flemish paintings including a Magdalene from Luca Giordano's school. Exceptionally rare in Italy: an intact Wunderkammer presented exactly as its donor conceived it.

Baranello historic center: palazzetti line via Santa Maria, including the former Palazzo Comunale with Florentine façade designed by Barone himself. The village overlooks the Biferno valley, birthplace of the Tintilia grape—nearly extinct after phylloxera, revived by local winemakers since the 2000s.

activities.text: Tintilia wine tasting at local producers: indigenous Molise red grape experiencing a modern renaissance.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria delle Grazie Baranello

visits.text: Discover the Castle of Bardi, the largest and most famous fortress in the Parma province, dramatically positioned on a rocky spur overlooking the confluence of the Ceno and Noveglia rivers. This imposing stronghold, built in the 9th century and expanded over subsequent centuries, dominates the valley with its massive walls and towers, creating one of Italy's most spectacular castle panoramas.

Explore the castle's extensive interiors, which house museums dedicated to the medieval history of the region and the wildlife of the Apennines. The fortress walls stretch for hundreds of meters around the rocky outcrop, while the towers offer breathtaking views over the mountain valleys stretching toward the distant peaks.

activities.text: Wander through the medieval village of Bardi clustered at the foot of the castle, where traditional mountain architecture and ancient churches tell the story of centuries of Apennine community life. The dramatic setting and historical importance make Bardi the essential destination for understanding the feudal history of the Parma mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

visits.text: Duomo di San Cristoforo: Perched at the village's highest point (410 m), this Romanesque cathedral was built in stages from the 11th to 16th centuries in blocks of alberese, a local gray-cream limestone. Its 13th-century marble pulpit, the work of Guido Bigarelli, is supported by four columns, two resting on recumbent lions. Inside: a ciborium of the Holy Oils by Andrea della Robbia, a 14th-century Madonna del Molino, and a medieval polychrome wooden statue of San Cristoforo. From the churchyard, views extend over the entire historic center and, on clear days, to the Apuan Alps.

Palazzo Pretorio and Museo Civico del Territorio: Residence of Florentine commissioners from 1341 to 1859—when Barga chose to submit to Florence rather than Lucca, obtaining exemption from numerous taxes. The palace houses the municipal museum with archaeological collections (prehistory, Ligurians, Romans) and artworks from the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The old medieval prisons are still visitable.

Loggia dei Mercanti and Piazza Salvo Salvi: This loggia with slender columns was built in 1546 on orders from Cosimo I de' Medici to shelter Barga's market, whose main products were silk and salt. The stone lion (Marzocco) on the façade symbolizes Florence's political guardianship. Palazzo Pancrazi, seat of the town hall, displays a diamond-bossed façade characteristic of 16th-century Florentine architecture.

Teatro dei Differenti: Inaugurated in 1689 and rebuilt in 1795, this Italian-style theater is one of the best preserved in Tuscany. It was here that Giovanni Pascoli delivered on November 26, 1911, his famous speech La grande proletaria si è mossa in favor of the Italo-Turkish War in Libya.

Casa Museo Giovanni Pascoli in Castelvecchio (4 km from Barga): Giovanni Pascoli, major poet of Italian Symbolism, purchased this 18th-century country house in 1895 and lived here with his sister Mariù until his death in 1912. He composed here his most celebrated collections, including Myricae and Canti di Castelvecchio, directly inspired by the surrounding landscapes. The museum preserves books, manuscripts, and everyday objects in the state the family left them. Pascoli is buried in the garden chapel, beneath cypresses and wisteria.

Medieval Historic Center of Barga: Classified among "Borghi più belli d'Italia" and labeled Cittaslow (slow cities network for quality of life), Barga has kept its 12th–14th century communal urban fabric almost intact. Enter through Porta Reale—so renamed after Grand Duke Leopold of Lorraine's visit—and climb the cobbled alleys to the Duomo. The presence of a significant Scottish community, heirs of hundreds of Barghigiani who left to settle in Glasgow between the 19th and 20th centuries as ice cream vendors and fish and chips shop managers, has earned Barga the nickname "most Scottish town in Italy."

activities.text: Barga Jazz Festival (August): Since 1986, this internationally renowned jazz festival has animated the squares and alleys of the medieval historic center each summer. Musicians from around the world perform in this medieval setting for a week in August.
Via dei Poeti and Castelvecchio surroundings: Marked trail linking the main sites associated with Pascoli in the countryside around Castelvecchio, with views of the Apuan Alps.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast
Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

visits.text: Bari — Basilica of San Nicola: built from 1087 onward to house the relics of Saint Nicholas of Myra, stolen from Myra (present-day Turkey) by sailors from Bari who beat the Venetians to the same prize. The Romanesque basilica, completed in 1197, is the model that inspired Trani Cathedral and several other Apulian Romanesque buildings. The crypt preserves the saint's relics, venerated by both Catholic and Orthodox Christians. Pope Urban II partially consecrated it in 1089.

Bari Vecchia — old town and Cathedral of San Sabino: the labyrinth of lanes in Bari's old town, built to an irregular plan of Byzantine and medieval origin on a rocky promontory, is one of the densest historic centers in Puglia. The Cathedral of San Sabino (12th century) neighbors the basilica. Orecchiette Street — Via dell'Arco Basso — is known for the women who make pasta by hand in front of their doors.

activities.text: Walk along Lungomare Nazario Sauro before departure: this waterfront runs along Bari's old town for several kilometers. Bari-Karol Wojtyla Airport is 8 km from the center.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

visits.text: Barolo is a village of 700 souls whose name has traveled the world. Castello Falletti, with foundations dating to the tenth century, served as the residence of the powerful Falletti family until the nineteenth century. Juliette Colbert, who married Marquis Carlo Tancredi Falletti in 1807, transformed Barolo from a sweet wine into a dry, age-worthy red, then gifted 325 bottles to King Carlo Alberto of Savoy to introduce it at court - a founding gesture of its worldwide fame. In May 2015, Pope Francis recognized Juliette Colbert as Venerable in her beatification process.

The castle has housed the WiMu (Wine Museum) since September 2010, designed by French museographer François Confino. The route descends from the panoramic third floor to the ancient cellars, encountering multimedia installations that weave wine together with literature, painting, cinema, and gastronomy. The castle's ancestral cellars host the Regional Enoteca of Barolo, representing all 11 production communes of the appellation. For an unusual curiosity, the neighboring Corkscrew Museum displays hundreds of pieces from all eras.

Three kilometers away, climb to the village of La Morra (559m), nicknamed the "balcony of the Langhe." Its panoramic terrace offers one of the widest views over the hills classified as UNESCO World Heritage since 2014. Along the way, don't miss the Cappella Brunate - a small country chapel reinterpreted in contemporary art with vivid colors by two New York artists - and the majestic Cedar of Lebanon planted in 1856 before the chapel. The Big Bench Community project has installed giant benches in the surrounding vineyards, offering viewpoints at the height of the hills.

activities.text: Barolo DOCG tasting in the village's historic cellars or at the castle's Regional Enoteca. Barolo ages a minimum of 38 months in oak (62 months for Riserva) and develops aromas of faded roses, tar, and spices. The Barolo wine festival takes place every second Sunday in September.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days
Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: Su Nuraxi is Sardinia's most complete nuragic complex, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1997. Built from the 17th century BC using basalt blocks, its central tower originally stood 61 feet high. Archaeologist Giovanni Lilliu excavated it between 1950 and 1957 from beneath an earth mound.

The nuragic village surrounding the tower contains over 50 circular huts inhabited from the 13th to 6th centuries BC. The only entrance still accessible is a doorway about 23 feet high, making the fortress virtually impregnable.
Casa Zapata, a 16th-century Aragonese manor built directly atop a second nuraghe, now houses a museum where the ancient remains are visible through glass floors from the very rooms above.

activities.text: Excursion to the Giara di Gesturi, a basalt plateau at 1,640 feet covered with cork oaks and seasonal ponds—one of the last refuges of Sardinia's wild horse, the cavallino della Giara.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

places.name: Bassano Del Grappa

visits.text: Ponte degli Alpini (Ponte Vecchio) - The covered wooden bridge spanning the Brenta is the symbol of Bassano del Grappa. Palladio designed its reconstruction in 1567, on a bridge existing since the Middle Ages. The wooden structure, destroyed several times by floods and wars, has always been rebuilt identically, most recently in 1948 by Alpine troops after its destruction in February 1945. It bears their name for this reason and was declared a national monument in 2019. The bridge connects the two banks where the Brenta narrows, with a direct view of Monte Grappa and the Prealps. The town changed its name from 'Bassano Veneto' to 'Bassano del Grappa' after World War I to honor the soldiers who fell on the mountain.

Museo degli Alpini - Installed in the vaulted stone cellars at the bridge's foot, this small museum preserves uniforms, weapons, documents and photographs tracing the history of the Alpini—the Italian army's mountain troops—since their creation in 1872. The battles of Monte Grappa (1917-1918) occupy a central place: this is where the front line held during the Austrian offensive of the Strafexpedition and the 1918 offensives, just a few kilometers from town.

Castello degli Ezzelini - The ruins of this medieval fortress constitute Bassano's oldest point, dominating the center from the northern hill. The Bolzonella tower, called 'di Ser Ivano', dates from the Middle Ages and served as an independent watchtower. Giuseppe Verdi's first opera, Oberto conte di San Bonifacio, is set within these walls—proof that the castle still fed the imagination in the 19th century. In summer, the open-air theater inside the ramparts hosts the OperaEstate festival.

Palazzo Sturm - This 18th-century Rococo palace, built on the Brenta downstream from the bridge, offers from its courtyard the best viewpoint of the Ponte degli Alpini and the mountains. The interior hosts temporary modern art exhibitions. The outdoor terrace is the best place to photograph the entire bridge with the snowy massif in the background.

activities.text: Visit and tasting at Nardini distillery - Bortolo Nardini founded Italy's first grappa distillery in 1779, at the northern entrance to the Ponte Vecchio, where it still stands today. The guided tour of the historic cellars traces two centuries of distillation: copper stills, maceration jars, production line. The tasting includes the famous Mezzoemezzo, the house aperitif based on rhubarb and citrus, and Tagliatella, a ruby liqueur from a secret late-18th-century recipe.

Poli Museo della Grappa - On the other side of the bridge, the Poli distillery museum traces the history of marc brandy from its medieval origins to modern techniques. The visit ends in the exhibition room with tasting and shop. The Poli distillery, founded in Bassano in 1898, is today among Veneto's most renowned.

Stroll along Viale dei Martiri - This panoramic avenue running along the old town's northern ramparts offers an open view of Monte Grappa massif and the Prealps. Its name comes from a September 1944 episode: in reprisal for a resistance action, the Germans hanged 31 partisans from the avenue's trees. An individualized memorial honors each victim.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Vicenza Circuit: Palladian Villas and the Venetian Countryside

map_keywords: Ponte degli Alpini Bassano del Grappa
Museo degli Alpini Bassano del Grappa
Castello degli Ezzelini Bassano del Grappa
Poli Museo della Grappa Bassano del Grappa

visits.text: Explore the steep lanes and cobbled stairways of Bellagio's old town—'Bellus Loci' (beautiful place) to the Romans, and already a summer residence of Pliny the Younger who loved fishing here. The village occupies the tip of the Lariana promontory, at the exact point where the lake's two southern arms meet in an inverted Y. From Piazza della Chiesa, the 12th-century basilica church of San Giacomo, dedicated to Bellagio's patron saint, displays a characteristic bell tower and triumphal arch. From the Punta Spartivento viewpoint, the panorama embraces both lake branches and the Alps simultaneously.

Visit the gardens of Villa Melzi d'Eril, commissioned in 1808 by Francesco Melzi d'Eril, Duke of Lodi and vice-president of the Italian Republic under Napoleon. The neoclassical villa was completed in 1810 in simple, severe lines; the English-style park surrounding it was designed to harmonize with the lakeshore, and a Japanese garden with pond and maples was added in the northern section. Neoclassical, Renaissance, Egyptian, and Etruscan-Roman sculptures are scattered among Lebanon cedars, camellias, azaleas, and centuries-old trees. The orangery now houses works by Appiani and sculptures by Canova.

Book a guided tour of the gardens of Villa Serbelloni, which extend over 18 hectares atop the promontory with panoramic views of both lake arms. Designed in the 18th century, these Italian-style gardens are accessible only by guided tour organized from the village (Tuesday-Sunday). Napoleon, Austrian Emperor Francis I, and Franz Liszt number among their illustrious 19th-century visitors.

Descend to the hamlet of Pescallo east of Bellagio, a former fishing village accessible from the cemetery via via per Pescallo. Its small beach and sailboats anchored in the bay offer an authentic view of Lake Como's eastern branch.

activities.text: Rent a license-free boat from Bellagio's landing stage to explore small coves inaccessible from shore, cruise along historic villas, and reach isolated beaches on the lake's central branch.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona
Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

visits.text: Area Naturalistica La Bora - a 10-hectare wetland managed by the province of Bologna, featuring breeding ponds for the European pond turtle (Emys orbicularis), a protected species threatened with extinction across northern Italy. The site also hosts a center dedicated to amphibians and aquatic insects.

activities.text: Birdwatching from the purpose-built observation hides: grey herons, kingfishers, and mallards frequent the ponds year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

visits.text: Ride the funicular up to Città Alta, one of Italy's best-preserved medieval villages enclosed within the majestic Venetian Walls, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Wandering through the cobbled streets you'll discover centuries of history and Renaissance art in a timeless atmosphere that has captivated travellers of every era.

Admire Piazza Vecchia, the true civic drawing room overlooked by the Palazzo della Ragione, the Civic Tower (Campanone) and the Angelo Mai Library. Every evening the Campanone bell tolls one hundred times, just as in Venetian times when it signalled the closing of the city gates, evoking an atmosphere steeped in historical charm.

Visit the Basilica of Santa Maria Maggiore with its richly Baroque interiors adorned with tapestries and gilded stuccowork, and the adjacent Cappella Colleoni, a Renaissance masterpiece built as a mausoleum for the condottiere Bartolomeo Colleoni. The chapel's polychrome marble façades rank among the finest in Italy.

Explore the Accademia Carrara, one of Italy's most prestigious art galleries with over 1,800 paintings by artists such as Botticelli, Raphael, Mantegna, Titian and Canaletto. Directly opposite stands the GAMeC, the Gallery of Modern and Contemporary Art, creating a unique dialogue between tradition and modernity.

activities.text: Ride even higher on the San Vigilio Funicular to the Castle, crossing breathtaking landscapes with spectacular views of the city rooftops, medieval towers and the Lombard plain. From the castle ramparts you can enjoy one of the most beautiful sunsets in Lombardy.

Lose yourself in the Lorenzo Rota Botanical Garden, a small urban Eden of 2,400 sq m on the panoramic terraces of Città Alta. From here you can admire a spectacular view of the city and surrounding mountains, with the Venetian Walls seeming to merge with the landscape in perfect harmony.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

visits.text: Mura etrusche - Bettona, which was called Vettona in Umbrian-Etruscan times, preserves sections of 4th-century BC Etruscan walls in local sandstone around its perimeter, covered in the 13th-14th centuries by medieval ramparts that used them as foundations. From Porta Romana, several sections of this irregular polygonal apparatus characteristic of Etruscan construction are still visible. On the outskirts, on the Perugia road at the locality called Colle, an Etruscan tomb entirely built in local sandstone is accessible. In 1352, Perugia almost completely destroyed the town during a siege, leaving only the churches intact.

Belvedere and panorama - From the highest point of the village perched at 353 meters, the view embraces the Umbrian plateau across its full width: Assisi with the Basilica of Saint Francis recognizable in the distance, Foligno, Spello and the foothills of Monte Subasio to the east, the Tiber valley to the northwest and the hills toward Perugia. Bettona was the outpost that controlled, beyond the Tiber, the border between Umbrian territory and Etruscan lands.

Pinacoteca comunale - The 14th-century Palazzo dei Priori houses an art gallery assembling works from the Umbrian and Tuscan schools. Notable pieces include paintings from Giotto's school from the 14th century, a collection of 15th-16th century altarpieces, and detached frescoes from local religious buildings. The Oratory of Saint Andrew preserves a 13th-century carved wooden coffered ceiling with a cycle on the Passion of Christ attributed to Giotto's school (1394).

Centro storico - Via di Mezzo, the former axis of the Roman town, crosses Bettona from Porta Vittorio Emanuele to Porta Romana. The perpendicular alleyways, accessible only on foot, are lined with stone houses and hanging gardens. Piazza Cavour brings together the main civic and religious monuments in a very compact space.

activities.text: Walk along the outer ramparts - The outer rampart path allows you to walk along the medieval fortifications over a large part of the perimeter. In autumn, the vines and olive trees on the slopes color the landscape rust and gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

places.name: Bevagna

visits.text: Piazza Silvestri - This asymmetrical square is one of Umbria's most architecturally coherent. It brings together face to face two 12th-century Romanesque churches built by masters Binello and Rodolfo: San Silvestro (1195, sober façade with elegant portal and three-nave interior with medieval crypt) and San Michele Arcangelo (flat façade with rose window, sculpted portal depicting Saint Michael slaying the dragon, three-nave interior). Between them stands the Palazzo dei Consoli (1270), former Gothic town hall with external staircase and mullioned windows, now home to Teatro Francesco Torti since 1886. A Roman column with Corinthian capital completes the ensemble. Bevagna occupied ancient Mevania, a Roman city on the Via Flaminia, founded at least since the Iron Age.

2nd-century Roman baths - On Via di Porta Guelfa, these public baths rediscovered in 1891 preserve a remarkable black and white mosaic floor depicting fantastic sea creatures: tritons, seahorses, lobsters, squid. They testify to Mevania's prosperity in the 2nd-3rd centuries, when the city drew its wealth from the waterway and commerce.

Medieval craft workshops - The Mercato delle Gaite cultural association has reconstructed medieval craft workshops in the hamlet accessible for visits year-round by reservation: coin-minting workshop, silk-weaving loom (with a wheel of complexity close to an industrial machine), parchment-making, and apothecary's workshop.

Cinta muraria - Bevagna's medieval ramparts, erected on foundations of earlier Roman walls, are almost entirely preserved. Their circumference is punctuated by square, round, and polygonal towers and four Gaite (access gates). It's possible to walk along part of the outer ramparts.

activities.text: Mercato delle Gaite (June) - For ten days in June, Bevagna's four historic quarters recreate medieval life in competition: crafts, cuisine, banners, and games. Residents wear period costumes and workshops operate in public. It's one of Umbria's most authentic medieval recreations.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Bevagna

places.name: Bibbiena
