visits.text: Historic center of Bibbiena: the administrative capital of the Casentino, Bibbiena is documented since 979 and developed as a medieval fortress of the bishops of Arezzo atop a hill overlooking the Archiano river. After the Battle of Campaldino (1289), the Florentine Guelphs sacked it for eight days. Rebuilt, it became one of the strategic castles of the Tarlati da Pietramala in the 14th century before passing to Florence in 1384. Of the original walls, the Tarlati Tower on Piazza Tarlati survives as the sole remnant of the medieval castle. The square offers a view over the entire Casentino valley.

Church of San Lorenzo: erected in the 15th century, it preserves two remarkable glazed terracottas by Andrea della Robbia — a Deposition and an Adoration. These late 15th-century works rank among the finest examples of Della Robbia art in the Casentino.

Church of Saints Ippolito and Donato (Propositura): the main religious building in the historic center, built in the early 12th century on the site of the Tarlati castle chapel, consecrated in 1314. The interior houses a 13th-century wooden sculpture of the Madonna and Child, a triptych by Bicci di Lorenzo and an Annunciation by Balducci.

Casentino Archaeological Museum Piero Albertoni — Palazzo Niccolini: the museum traces the valley's history from prehistory to the Middle Ages through local discoveries. The highlight is the collection of Etruscan bronzes from the Lago degli Idoli (Lake of the Idols), an artificial lake on the flank of Monte Falterona where over 14,000 Etruscan ex-votos were excavated, some of which are now in the British Museum and the Louvre.

activities.text: Sanctuary of Santa Maria del Sasso: a Renaissance complex 1 km from the historic center, built to the design of Giuliano da Maiano in Renaissance style on the site of a Marian apparition in 1347. The sanctuary contains a Madonna and Child by Bicci di Lorenzo, a Nativity of the Virgin by Ligozzi, an Assumption by Fra' Bartolomeo and Fra' Paolino, and a remarkable Renaissance cloister.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

map_keywords: Propositura dei Santi Ippolito e Donato Bibbiena
Santuario di Santa Maria del Sasso Bibbiena

visits.text: The Sanctuary of Oropa, at 1,200 meters altitude in the Biella Prealps, is the largest Marian sanctuary in the Alps and has been part of UNESCO World Heritage since 2003 (Sacri Monti). Its origins reportedly date to the 4th century, when Bishop Eusebius of Vercelli placed a statue of the Virgin there according to tradition. In 1620, during the first coronation of the Black Virgin statue, baroque works accelerated. Filippo Juvarra designed the Porta Regia in the 18th century, Guarino Guarini participated in the overall design. The twelve chapels of the Sacro Monte (built from 1620) recount the life of Mary in polychrome statues and frescoes by Giovanni d'Enrico. The new basilica, consecrated in 1960, rises over 80 meters to its dome.

The medieval quarter of Piazzo, the upper part of Biella accessible by funicular from the lower town, preserves a Piazza Cisterna surrounded by 15th-century arcades, the Romanesque church of San Giacomo, and Palazzo Gromo Ternengo (16th century). The cathedral baptistery, built between the 7th and 11th centuries with a central plan and four apses, is one of the most significant examples of Piedmontese Romanesque art. Above the door, a Roman bas-relief depicting Hercules in reuse testifies to continuity of occupation since antiquity.

Biella is first mentioned in 826 as Bugella, in a donation act by Louis the Pious to a Count Busone. From 1245, city statutes reference weaver and wool worker guilds. In 1695, a silk manufactory was built there; in 1835, the same building was converted to a mechanical wool mill, placing Biella at the forefront of European textile industry. Cittadellarte - Fondazione Pistoletto, installed in a former mill on the Cervo river, is a contemporary art center founded in 1996 by artist Michelangelo Pistoletto.

activities.text: Cable car from Oropa to Rifugio Savoia (1,900 m), then 15-minute hike to Lake Mucrone: panorama over the plain to Turin and Milan on clear days. Return via old shepherd trails.
Forest hike in Burcina-Felice Piacenza Natural Park, south of Biella: reserve known for its spectacular rhododendron collection (blooming May-June). Bronze Age tools have been found in this massif.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

places.name: Bienno

visits.text: Enter Bienno's historic center, built on the geometric grid of a 1st-century BC Roman foundation. The name comes from the Latin 'biennium,' a term designating the canals that channeled waters from the Grigna torrent to the workshops. The defensive Avanzini tower (dating from 1075) and Morandini tower flank the oldest nucleus. Renaissance palace facades preserve exterior frescoes; Palazzo Francesconi Rabajoli, two 16th-century noble houses united, shelters frescoes attributed to Fiamminghino.

Follow Via Artigiani along the Vaso Re, an artificial canal built around the year 1000 to channel water from the Grigna to the forges. In the 17th century, fifteen forges operated in the village. Visit the Fucina Museo, a forge-museum acquired by the municipality in 1985: the enormous hydraulic hammers, driven by the waterwheel according to Archimedes' principle, still function and are demonstrated. This is the heart of the Ethnographic Museum of Iron, Arts, and Popular Traditions of Bienno, founded in 1988.

Visit Bienno's Mill-Museum, the other pillar of the village's industrial heritage, adjacent to the Fucina. Together, these two buildings form the museum's founding nucleus and illustrate the complete chain of metal transformation from ore in the prehistoric Camunian mines to the finished tool.

Climb to the Colle di Cristo Re to see the golden statue of Christ the Redeemer, a 1929 work by Timo Bortolotti. Nearby, the church of the Maddalena is adorned with frescoes. The Gothic church of Santa Maria Annunciata, built in the 15th century with its exposed-stone rose window, preserves frescoes by Romanino and Pietro da Cemmo, two great Lombard painters.

activities.text: Thematic walk along the Vaso Re canal titled 'Discovering Vaso Re, along the story told by water': this marked trail crosses the village's old forges converted into a Forge School and Iron Playroom, allowing you to follow Bienno's industrial history from the Middle Ages to the 1980s, when the last active forges closed.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Annunciata Bienno

visits.text: Duomo di Modena - Romanesque cathedral built from 1099 by architect Lanfranco over the relics of San Geminiano, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1997 along with Piazza Grande and Torre Ghirlandina. The façade and portals are adorned with bas-reliefs by Wiligelmo (circa 1106-1120), considered among the first great works of Romanesque sculpture in Italy.

Torre Ghirlandina - Romanesque-Gothic bell tower rising 89 meters, built between the 12th and 14th centuries, whose name derives from the crown of Gothic balustrades encircling the spire. The tower houses the Secchia Rapita, a wooden bucket stolen from Bologna during the Battle of Zappolino in 1325 - an event that inspired Alessandro Tassoni's mock-heroic poem of the same name in 1622.

Piazza Maggiore di Bologna - central square measuring 115 by 60 meters, surrounded by public buildings constructed between the 13th and 16th centuries: the Palazzo dei Notai (1381), Palazzo d'Accursio (city hall seat since 1336), Palazzo dei Banchi (Vignola façade, 1568), and the Basilica di San Petronio whose unfinished façade still reveals the alternating brick-and-marble pattern.

Due Torri di Bologna - the Torre degli Asinelli (97.2 meters, built between 1109 and 1119) and Torre Garisenda (48 meters, with a 3.2-meter tilt). In the 12th century, Bologna counted over 180 towers built by noble families as symbols of power - only 22 remain today. Dante mentioned Torre Garisenda in the Inferno (Canto XXXI).

Archiginnasio di Bologna - first unified seat of the University of Bologna, built in 1563 by Antonio Morandi. The walls and ceilings are covered with 6,000 stemmi (coats of arms) of students and professors from 1563 to 1737. The Teatro Anatomico (1637), entirely in fir wood, served for public dissections in the presence of the bishop - a cleric blessed each cadaver before each session.

Basilica di San Petronio - Gothic basilica whose construction began in 1390 and was never completed - the lower façade in white and red marble contrasts with the upper section in exposed brick. At 132 meters long and 66 meters wide, it ranks as the world's fifth-largest church. A 66.8-meter astronomical meridian line traced on the floor in 1655 by astronomer Gian Domenico Cassini was used to reform the Gregorian calendar.

Fontana del Nettuno - monumental bronze fountain created by Giambologna (Jean de Boulogne) between 1563 and 1566, commissioned to celebrate papal authority over Bologna. Neptune stands 3.2 meters tall and dominates a 6-meter-high composition of sirens, putti, and dolphins. Authorities of the time required certain anatomical parts to be reduced for reasons of decency.

Santuario della Madonna di San Luca - Baroque sanctuary built between 1723 and 1765 on San Luca hill at 291 meters elevation, connected to Bologna's center by the world's longest portico: 3.8 kilometers and 666 arches constructed between 1674 and 1793. The preserved icon of the Virgin is traditionally attributed to Saint Luke, though historians date it to the 12th century.

Cineteca di Bologna - archive and cinematheque founded in 1963, one of Europe's most important for silent film preservation and digital restoration. The L'Immagine Ritrovata laboratory has restored over 5,000 films since 1992, including Fritz Lang's Metropolis and works by Chaplin.

activities.text: Tasting of aceto balsamico tradizionale di Modena DOP in an acetaia - traditional balsamic vinegar is aged minimum 12 years (Affinato) or 25 years (Extravecchio) in batteries of 5 to 7 barrels of different woods (oak, chestnut, cherry, ash, mulberry).

Walk beneath Bologna's porticoes - 62-kilometer network of covered arcades inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2021, connecting the city's principal monuments. The porticoes were mandated by municipal decree as early as 1288.

Bolognese pasta all'uovo making class - the Bolognese repertoire includes tagliatelle (8mm width according to regulations filed with the Chamber of Commerce in 1972), tortellini, tortelloni, and maltagliati. Several cooking schools in the center offer classes.
Ascent to Santuario di San Luca via the 666-arch portico - 3.8 kilometers from Porta Saragozza with progressive views over Bologna and the Po plain.

days_plan.itin_title: Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine
Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine
10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

visits.text: Before leaving Modena province, devote the morning to the Ferrari Museum in Maranello, located 20 km south of Modena. Opened in 1990, this museum traces the history of the Scuderia and the Prancing Horse through legendary Formula 1 cars, prototypes, and GTs. You can also try a driving simulator. If you're a true enthusiast, combine it with the Enzo Ferrari Museum in central Modena, housed in the founder's birthplace—a combined ticket lets you visit both within 48 hours.

Arriving in Bologna by early afternoon, head to Piazza Maggiore, the city's heart. The Basilica of San Petronio dominates the square with its deliberately unfinished facade—construction began in 1390 and it remains the world's fifth-largest church. Inside, you'll find a meridian line traced on the floor in 1655 by astronomer Cassini, stretching 66 meters long. Entry is free.

The Two Towers are Bologna's symbol. The Torre degli Asinelli, rising 97 meters, is Italy's tallest leaning medieval tower still standing. Climb its 498 steps: the panorama over Bologna's red rooftops, the hills, and on clear days the Alps is exceptional. Beside it, the Torre Garisenda, smaller at 48 meters, leans even more than the Tower of Pisa—it's not accessible for safety reasons. In the 12th century, Bologna had over a hundred such towers; about twenty remain.

Bologna deserves a second day. Start at Palazzo dell'Archiginnasio, the former seat of the University of Bologna—founded in 1088, it's the oldest university in the Western world. Dante, Petrarch, and Copernicus studied here. Inside, don't miss the Teatro Anatomico, a room entirely clad in fir wood adorned with sculptures, where medical professors gave anatomy lectures before tiers of students. The atmosphere is both scholarly and slightly macabre.

Continue to Piazza Santo Stefano, one of Bologna's most beautiful squares. The Basilica of Santo Stefano is actually a complex of several interlocking churches and cloisters, built between the 5th and 13th centuries—sometimes called "the seven churches." The medieval cloister, with its Romanesque columns and central basin, radiates absolute serenity. Take time to sit for a moment.

Pass through Piazza Nettuno to admire the Neptune Fountain, sculpted in bronze by Giambologna between 1563 and 1567. The sea god, trident in hand, dominates a basin adorned with mermaids and dolphins. Just behind stands Palazzo Re Enzo, where Emperor Frederick II's son was imprisoned for 23 years after being captured by the Bolognese in 1249.

Wander through the university quarter around Via Zamboni. With 100,000 students—a quarter of the population—Bologna pulses with distinctive energy. Independent bookshops, lively cafes, walls covered in posters: this is where you'll feel why Bologna also bears the nickname "la Dotta" (the learned one). Continue to Palazzo Poggi, the university's current seat, which houses fascinating scientific collections.

If you're seeking a quiet moment, the Baroque church of San Domenico is worth the detour. It houses the Arca di San Domenico, the monumental tomb of Saint Dominic, worked on by renowned sculptors including the young Michelangelo—three statuettes on the tomb are attributed to him. The wooden choir is also remarkable.

activities.text: Bologna is the only city in the world where you can walk for kilometers without ever being exposed to rain or sun. Its porticoes, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2021, extend over 40 km in the historic center. Let yourself wander beneath these arcades, from Via dell'Indipendenza to Strada Maggiore. For the more athletic, the San Luca portico climbs 3.8 km and 666 arches up to the sanctuary perched on the hill—allow 1h30 round trip.

Bologna earned the nickname "la Grassa" (the fat one) for good reason. Lose yourself in the Quadrilatero, the old medieval guild quarter, now a permanent market where the aromas of cheeses, cured meats, and fresh fish mingle. Taste tortellini in brodo—tiny pasta parcels served in capon broth—and mortadella Bologna IGP, far more refined than the deli meat you might know as "bologna." Pair it all with a glass of Pignoletto, the local sparkling white wine.

Bologna is a city best discovered by bike. The historic center is largely flat and pedestrianized, and operators offer rentals by the hour or day. It's the best way to cover the 40 km of porticoes without exhausting yourself, and to easily reach outlying neighborhoods like the Giardini Margherita, the large public park south of the center.

For complete immersion in Bolognese gastronomy, join a cooking class to learn how to make tagliatelle al ragù—the real ragù Bolognese, a thick and savory sauce served with egg pasta, which bears no resemblance to the "spaghetti Bolognese" found abroad. Operators offer 2- to 3-hour workshops, often in welcoming settings in the historic center.

days_plan.itin_title: Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills
Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills

visits.text: Lago di Bolsena - Europe's largest volcanic lake (114 km², maximum depth 151 m), formed in a caldera of the Volsini mountains approximately 300,000 years ago. Its clear, fish-rich waters contain two islands: Bisentina (former Farnese property, with Renaissance chapels) and Martana (where, according to tradition, Amalasuntha, Queen of the Ostrogoths, was strangled in 535). Swimming is permitted on several black volcanic sand beaches.

Basilica di Santa Cristina in Bolsena - This 11th-century basilica is linked to the Eucharistic Miracle of Bolsena (1263): a Bohemian priest on pilgrimage, doubting transubstantiation, saw the host bleed onto the corporal during Mass. Pope Urban IV, then in Orvieto, instituted the feast of Corpus Christi in response. The Cappella del Miracolo, in the catacombs beneath the church, preserves the altar of the episode. The 4th-century Christian catacombs, carved into tufa, are accessible by guided tour.

Castello Monaldeschi in Bolsena - 13th-century fortress dominating the lake from the medieval town. It houses the Museo Territoriale del Lago di Bolsena, devoted to the geological, archaeological (Etruscan and Roman remains from ancient Volsinii), and natural history of the lake.

Montefiascone - Perched on the crater's southern rim, this town offers circular panoramas over the lake and countryside. The Duomo di Santa Margherita has one of Italy's largest domes after St. Peter's in Rome, visible from afar. It's also the birthplace of Est! Est!! Est!!! di Montefiascone DOC wine, whose name comes from a medieval anecdote: a German bishop en route to Rome sent his cupbearer ahead to mark "Est" (there is some) on taverns with good wine; arriving at Montefiascone, the cupbearer inscribed the word three times, so pleased was he with the wine.

activities.text: Tasting Est! Est!! Est!!! wine - Montefiascone's cellars offer tastings of this DOC dry white, accompanied by local products: acquacotta (Tuscia peasant soup), sbroscia (lake fish soup), and grilled eels.

Boat excursion to Isola Bisentina - Boats depart from the ports of Bolsena and Capodimonte to reach this wooded island housing seven Renaissance chapels, including one attributed to Vignola, and Italian gardens. Booking recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

visits.text: The South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology in Bolzano is housed in a 1912 building (former bank) and has sheltered Ötzi the Iceman since 1998, discovered on September 19, 1991 at 3,210 meters altitude in the Ötztal Alps by German hikers Helmut and Erika Simon. The mummy is preserved in a cold chamber at -6°C and 100% humidity, visible through a small window. Ötzi lived around 3200 BC (Chalcolithic, Copper Age): he was about 45 years old, stood 1.60 m tall, and weighed 50 kg. He was murdered by an arrow with a flint tip piercing his left shoulder blade (discovered by X-ray). His body bore 50 tattoos, probably for medicinal purposes, and he was equipped with a copper ax, bow, arrows, and clothing made of goat, deer, and bear skin. The museum attracts about 260,000 visitors per year.

Piazza Walther is the heart of Bolzano, an elegant 19th-century square where stands the statue of medieval Germanic poet Walther von der Vogelweide (c. 1170-1230), one of the most important authors of minnelieder of the Middle Ages. The Gothic cathedral (Duomo Maria Assunta), begun in the 14th century, is distinguished by its multicolored tile roof and richly decorated tower. The adjacent chapel of San Giovanni preserves a complete cycle of Giotto-school frescoes, one of the oldest in the region.

Via dei Portici (Arcade Street) is Bolzano's main medieval artery, lined with arcades, some dating back to the 13th century. This pedestrian street, characteristic of Tyrolean architecture, houses craft shops, galleries, and cafés. At the northern end, Piazza delle Erbe hosts a morning market where South Tyrolean apples are displayed (the region is Europe's leading producer) along with Speck Alto Adige IGP, the cold-smoked and air-dried ham that defines local gastronomy.
Cable car ascent to Soprabolzano to admire the Dolomites panorama
Discovery of the Renon plateau and its natural earth pyramids
Self-guided visit of Trauttmansdorff Castle and its gardens in Merano
Discovery of Merano's historic center: Duomo San Nicolò, Via dei Portici
Visit of Bolzano's old center: Piazza Walther, Duomo, Piazza delle Erbe
Discovery of Roncolo Castle and visit of a wine cellar with tasting
Self-guided visit of the South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology

activities.text: Scenic route along Lake Garda toward Bolzano
Round-trip excursion from Bolzano to Merano

The Renon cable car, rebuilt in 2009 by the Leitner company with 3S technology, departs steps away from Bolzano's train station. In 12 minutes, its cabins climb from 274 to 1,227 meters altitude over a length of 4,500 meters. It replaced a rack railway inaugurated in 1907, deemed too slow (1h40 journey). At the summit, a small historic narrow-gauge train takes over to cross the plateau and reach the Renon earth pyramids, also called fairy chimneys.

The Renon earth pyramids are clay columns formed from glacial moraines, each topped with a protective rock block that slows erosion. They can reach several tens of meters in height. Three main groups punctuate the plateau, the most impressive being in the Rivellone valley. A trail with wooden boardwalks winds through the forest to offer several viewpoints over these natural formations. From the plateau, the panorama extends over the Dolomites and the Bolzano basin.

days_plan.itin_title: Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice

places.name: Bomarzo

visits.text: Sacro Bosco (Park of the Monsters) of Bomarzo - Created from 1552 onward by Prince Vicino Orsini, this 3-hectare park is the world's oldest modern sculpture garden. After his wife Giulia Farnese's death, Orsini commissioned architect Pirro Ligorio (who completed St. Peter's Basilica after Michelangelo) to create dozens of colossal creatures carved from peperino: an ogre with gaping mouth, an elephant bearing a combat tower, two wrestling giants, a dragon, sphinxes. The leaning house, built on a tilted block, disorients visitors. Abandoned for 400 years, the park was rediscovered by Salvador Dalí in 1938.

Tempietto of the Sacro Bosco - Atop the hill, this small temple attributed to Vignola crowns the route. Its dome echoes in miniature that of Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence. Vicino Orsini dedicated it to his wife's memory.

Etruscan Pyramid of Bomarzo - In the sacred wood, a peperino monolith carved into a stepped pyramid puzzles archaeologists. Likely a sacrificial altar or rock sanctuary, it remains shrouded in mystery. Access via a forest trail.

Vitorchiano, the "suspended village" - 15 minutes from Bomarzo, this grey peperino village seems to merge with the volcanic cliff. Classified among the Borghi più Belli d'Italia and awarded the Touring Club's Bandiera Arancione. The profferli (external staircases), the 13th-century fountain on Piazza Roma, and the Capitoline Wolf engraved on facades recall the unique status of "land faithful to Rome" granted in 1265. Director Mario Monicelli filmed "L'armata Brancaleone" here with Vittorio Gassman in 1966.

Moai of Vitorchiano - In 1990, eleven craftsmen from Easter Island (Rapa Nui) sculpted this 6-meter, 3-ton statue from a local peperino block—the only Moai in the world made outside Easter Island using ancestral techniques. Tuscia's volcanic peperino resembles Rapa Nui stone. The statue stands watch over the belvedere overlooking the Vezza valley.

activities.text: Vitorchiano belvedere - From Largo Padre Ettore Salimbeni (where the Moai stands), the view plunges over the borgo clinging to its cliff, the Vezza valley, and the oak forests of the Cimini Mountains. Tuscia's best photo spot, especially in late afternoon light.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio
Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

map_keywords: Sacro Bosco Parco dei Mostri Bomarzo

places.name: Borgo A Mozzano

visits.text: Ponte della Maddalena (Devil's Bridge): This humpback bridge spans the Serchio with a main arch rising 18.50 meters flanked by four smaller asymmetric arches—a silhouette that has intrigued travelers since the 16th century, notably Michel de Montaigne who described it in his 1581 Travel Journal. The structure probably dates to the 11th century, but its current appearance is due to Castruccio Castracani, lord of Lucca, who had it rebuilt between 1324 and 1327. On the Via Francigena pilgrimage route from Canterbury to Rome, the bridge held during World War II: retreating German troops had mined it but it was never destroyed, which proved crucial for the Allies. Legend tells that the mason, despairing of floods from the Serchio, made a pact with the devil to finish the work in one night—in exchange for the first soul to cross the bridge. The village priest sent a dog across, outwitting the devil. They say this white dog still walks the bridge on October evenings.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

map_keywords: Ponte della Maddalena Borgo a Mozzano
Ponte del Diavolo Borgo a Mozzano

places.name: Borgo San Lorenzo

visits.text: The Pieve di San Lorenzo, founded in 941, is the largest rural Romanesque building in the Florentine countryside. Its hexagonal bell tower with a semi-dodecagonal brick base, placed directly on the apse, is unique in Tuscany. The interior preserves a Madonna attributed to Giotto, a Crucifix from Giotto's school, a Virgin Enthroned attributed to Agnolo Gaddi, and a Virgin with Saints by Matteo Rosselli. The apse is entirely frescoed by Galileo Chini. On the right side facade, a polychrome terracotta tabernacle by Fornaci Chini (1926), created for the centenary of Saint Francis's death, is one of the finest street pieces of the Art Nouveau movement in Tuscany.

The Chini Museum in Villa Pecori Giraldi traces the history of the Fornaci San Lorenzo manufactory, founded in 1906 by Galileo and Chino Chini, two cousins born in Borgo San Lorenzo. Galileo Chini was one of the leading representatives of Italian Art Nouveau: his ceramics—first with floral motifs inspired by Botticelli, then with metallic-luster Deco designs—adorn the Salsomaggiore thermal baths, several Florentine palaces, and Borgo San Lorenzo's town hall. In 1911-1913, the King of Thailand invited him to Bangkok to decorate the Sathon Throne. The villa's rooms are frescoed by the Chini family: a high-quality Saint George cycle, medieval geometric motifs, and coats of arms of Florentine nobility.

activities.text: Art Nouveau Trail of Mugello: a walking or driving route among Chini works scattered throughout the town—Misericordia facade, mayor's office decorated on floors and windows, Santissimo Crocifisso oratory. Borgo San Lorenzo was named 'Città della Ceramica' (City of Ceramics) by the Italian Ministry of Economic Development in 2017.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Pieve di San Lorenzo Borgo San Lorenzo
Villa Pecori Giraldi Borgo San Lorenzo

visits.text: Stroll through Bormio's historic center—the 'Magnifica Terra'—and its Piazza Cavour surrounded by noble palaces with painted facades. The first mentions of Bormio date back to a letter from Cassiodorus; in the Middle Ages, the town was the seat of an autonomous county covering the present territories of Valfurva, Valdidentro, and Livigno. The name Bormio probably derives from the Gaulish 'bormo,' meaning thermal spring. The Bajona Tower and the old Via Roma testify to Lombard medieval architecture. Bormio will be one of the Alpine sites for the Milano-Cortina 2026 Olympics for alpine skiing.

Take time for a soak at the Bagni Vecchi, perched 3 km above the center on the Stelvio road. These thermal baths have been known since pre-Roman antiquity: a bas-relief discovered during the demolition of a local church represents a water god and is dated between the 5th and 1st century BC. Pliny the Elder reportedly mentioned the springs in his writings. Nine natural springs emerge between 1,280 and 1,421 meters altitude at temperatures of 37 to 43°C. The Grotte di San Martino, a natural Turkish bath carved into the rock, is one of the oldest springs; an infinity pool overlooks the entire valley.

activities.text: Ski Bormio's slopes in winter—the domain extends 50 km between 1,225 m and 3,000 m altitude on the flanks of Monte Vallecetta. The Stelvio piste, inaugurated in 1982, hosted the World Alpine Ski Championships in 1985 and 2005; it's part of the World Cup circuit each December. In summer, hike or mountain bike in Stelvio National Park, the largest of Italy's Alpine national parks, created in 1935.

Excursion to Stelvio Pass (Passo dello Stelvio, 2,757 m): the second-highest paved mountain pass in the Alps, it connects Bormio to Bolzano through a succession of 48 hairpin turns on the Lombard side. In summer the pass is open to road traffic and hikers; the summit glacier enables year-round skiing. In cycling, the Stelvio is one of the legendary climbs of the Giro d'Italia.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

visits.text: Castello Malaspina has stood since the late 13th century on Serravalle hill, 260 feet above the Temo River. It contains the chapel of Nostra Signora de Sos Regnos Altos, whose 14th-century frescoes illustrate the rare scene of "The Meeting of the Three Living and the Three Dead," the only known example of this Franciscan theme in Sardinia.

The Sa Costa quarter is Bosa's medieval heart, with ochre, yellow, and rose-colored houses pressed together on the slopes above the Temo. The Ponte Vecchio, built from red trachyte, links this quarter to the quays.

The church of San Pietro extra muros, a 12th-century Romanesque structure, stands apart from the center on the far bank of the Temo, where ancient Roman Bosa once stood. It's one of Sardinia's oldest Christian churches.

activities.text: Boat ride on the Temo, Sardinia's only navigable river, between the city quays and the river mouth at Bosa Marina. Every first Sunday in August, a procession of decorated boats carries the Virgin's statue upriver.

Taste Malvasia di Bosa DOC in one of the wine cellars in the nearby villages of Magomadas or Modolo. This sweet, amber white wine, produced from a grape variety of Greek origin, is one of Italy's rarest wine productions.
Swim at Cala Managu or Bosa Marina beach, about a mile from the center, on a coastline still uncrowded and bordered by basalt cliffs.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

visits.text: Castello Orsini-Odescalchi — A Renaissance fortress begun in 1470 under Napoleone Orsini's commission, likely built by the very masons working simultaneously on the Sistine Chapel. Napoleone's brother, Cardinal Latino Orsini, served as chamberlain to Pope Sixtus IV. Construction was completed in 1495 under his son Gentil Virginio's supervision. The complex incorporates five towers on five corners and three concentric defensive walls. In 1558, Duke Paolo Giordano Orsini married Isabella de' Medici and converted the fortress into a ducal palace, bringing in the Zuccari brothers to fresco several rooms. When the Orsini line died out in 1696, the castle passed to the Odescalchi family, nephews of Pope Innocent XI, who remain the owners today. The museum route covers twenty rooms displaying weapons collections, period furniture, and paintings spanning the 15th to 19th centuries.

Anguillara Sabazia — A lakeside village perched on a promontory of the Sabatini Mountains overlooking Lake Bracciano, with the 16th-century Collegiata dell'Assunzione and medieval lanes clinging to the hillside. From the belvedere in the central piazza, you look straight across the lake to the wooded shores of Parco Regionale di Bracciano-Martignano, designated a protected natural area in 1999.

Trevignano Romano — A small fishing village on the lake's northern shore, with a parish church built on the ruins of a medieval castrum. The main piazza, perched above the water, offers one of the most sweeping panoramas of Lake Bracciano's 56.5 km² expanse. These waters have supplied Rome since the 17th century via the Acquedotto Paolo.

activities.text: Swimming on the shores of Lake Bracciano — A volcanic crater lake 160 meters deep, ranked among the cleanest lakes in central Italy. The best access points are at Trevignano and Anguillara, where you'll find pebble beaches and picnic areas within the natural park.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani
Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

visits.text: Explore the Museo di Santa Giulia, an extraordinary monastic complex and the city's main museum housing treasures of exceptional historical and artistic importance. From the ninth-century Cross of Desiderius to the incredible frescoes of the Nuns' Choir, from the remains of ancient Roman Domus houses to the marvellous interiors of the Basilica of San Salvatore, it is part of the UNESCO site in the heart of Brescia.

Admire the Roman Archaeological Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site comprising Piazza del Foro, the Capitolium with the hall dedicated to the Winged Victory, the Republican Sanctuary, the ancient theatre and the Roman Basilica. This extraordinary complex bears witness to the greatness of Brixia, one of northern Italy's most important cities in the Roman era.

Visit Piazza Loggia, the beating heart of Brescia characterised by a splendid blend of styles with a clear Venetian imprint. The Palazzo della Loggia, built between 1492 and 1570 with contributions from Palladio and Sansovino, dominates the square alongside the Clock Tower with its ancient astronomical clock.

Discover the Pinacoteca Tosio Martinengo, one of northern Italy's most important galleries with works by Raphael, Foppa, Moretto, Romanino, Lotto, Ceruti and Hayez. The route through 21 exhibition halls offers an intense journey from late Gothic to the early nineteenth century through the great Brescian Renaissance painting.

activities.text: Climb to Brescia Castle on the Colle Cidneo, a great fortress built between the fourteenth and sixteenth centuries in a commanding position over the city. A favourite destination for relaxing walks in the greenery, it houses the Luigi Marzoli Arms Museum and offers breathtaking views of the city and the Bergamo valleys.

Stroll through Piazza Paolo VI admiring the Duomo Vecchio, a rare example of Romanesque architecture with a circular plan, and the Duomo Nuovo completed in 1825. Then lose yourself in the ancient Carmine quarter with its evocative lanes, traditional taverns and bohemian atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

visits.text: The Matterhorn (14,692 feet), the Alps' fifth-highest summit, is the world's most recognizable mountain for its nearly perfect pyramidal shape—the result of four intersecting glacial cirques. From Breuil—renamed Cervinia in 1936-37 at the urging of Senator Dino Lora Totino to assert the resort's Italian identity—the Lion Ridge rises toward the summit. On July 17, 1865, Valtournenche guide Jean-Antoine Carrel opened the first Italian route up the Matterhorn via this ridge, three days after Englishman Edward Whymper's triumph from Zermatt. Whymper's descent cost four companions their lives. In 1890, Carrel perished on the mountain he had conquered, caught in a storm while descending after bringing his clients to safety.

Cervinia's Blue Lake, a 45-minute walk from the village, offers the Matterhorn's reflection in turquoise waters. This glacial cirque lake at 7,064 feet is surrounded by a mineral amphitheater whose hues shift from turquoise to cobalt depending on the hour. It's the starting point for trails to the Oriondé (Duca degli Abruzzi, 9,193 feet) and Carrel (12,566 feet) refuges—the two stages of the Matterhorn ascent by the Italian route.

The Matterhorn Alpine Crossing, inaugurated July 1, 2023, links Breuil-Cervinia to Zermatt by cable car for the first time without skis, crossing the Alps' highest border crossing. The Plateau Rosa (11,417 feet) to Klein Matterhorn (12,739 feet) section crosses the Italian-Swiss frontier above the glaciers, with direct views of the Matterhorn from both flanks.

activities.text: Hike to Oriondé Refuge (Duca degli Abruzzi, 9,193 feet) from Breuil: ascend the route of the first alpinists, pass the Carrel cross, view the Matterhorn's south face. Round trip approximately 5-6 hours.

In summer, ride the cable car to Plateau Rosa (11,417 feet): panoramas of Matterhorn and Monte Rosa glaciers, summer ski slope, walking access to the seracs. In winter, international ski domain linked to Zermatt.

days_plan.itin_title: Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc

places.name: Brindisi

visits.text: Coastal drive Monopoli → Brindisi: the route follows the Adriatic coast for 45 km, with possible stops at Spiaggia di Capitolo or Villanova, two uncrowded coves between the two cities.

Manduria — Messapian city and Primitivo wines: Manduria's Messapian ramparts, built between the 5th and 2nd centuries BC, are among the best preserved on the Italian peninsula. They extend for several kilometers around the ancient city and are accompanied by a necropolis of several thousand tombs. Manduria is also the capital of Primitivo di Manduria DOC, a powerful red wine produced from the local Zinfandel grape since the 17th century.

Brindisi — Roman column of the Via Appia: atop the Virgilio stairway overlooking the port, an 18.74-meter marble column traditionally marks the end of the Via Appia, the road that linked Rome to Brindisi from 244 BC onward. Originally two columns stood here. The second collapsed in a 1528 earthquake — its fragments were gifted to Lecce in 1659, where architect Giuseppe Zimbalo incorporated them into the column in Piazza Sant'Oronzo. The original capital of the remaining column is preserved in Palazzo Granafei-Nervegna.

Brindisi — Church of Santa Maria del Casale: 3 km north of the city center, this 13th-century church in Apulian Romanesque-Gothic style is one of the best-preserved medieval monuments in the region. Its interior frescoes from the early 14th century cover the entirety of the walls — notably the Last Judgment in the apse, attributed to Rinaldo da Taranto. The facade in bichrome limestone (light and dark stone alternated) is characteristic of late Romanesque Puglia.

Brindisi — lungomare and historic port: Brindisi's natural harbor, whose shape recalls a deer's antlers (Brunda in Messapian, whence the city's name), was one of the Roman Empire's principal embarkation ports to the East. Virgil died here in 19 BC on his return from Greece — a small house on Piazza Vittorio Emanuele marks the traditional site of his death. The Lungomare Regina Margherita runs along the inner harbor.

activities.text: Primitivo di Manduria DOC tasting: estates around Manduria offer vineyard visits and tastings of this powerful red wine (14 to 17 degrees), produced for centuries on limestone and reddish clay soils. Primitivo is genetically identical to Californian Zinfandel — its presence in Italy is documented from the 17th century onward.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Puglia: Frederick II's Castles, Hidden Hinterland & Forgotten Villages
Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

map_keywords: Chiesa Santa Maria del Casale Brindisi

visits.text: Brisighella greets you with an unforgettable silhouette: three rocky pinnacles of selenite dominate the village, each crowned by a monument—the Rocca Manfrediana fortress, the Clock Tower, and the Sanctuary of Monticino. Ranked among Italy's most beautiful villages, this medieval hamlet in Ravenna province has remained remarkably intact. Start with the Via degli Asini, the village's most famous attraction. This covered, elevated street, built in the 12th century, originally served as a defensive rampart before housing the stables of carters who transported gypsum from nearby quarries with their donkeys. Its arched windows, wooden beams, and irregular paving make it unique in Italy.

Climb up to the Rocca Manfrediana, the 14th-century fortress that dominates the village. From up here, the view over the Lamone valley and hills covered with olive groves and vineyards is superb. The fortress houses the Museum of Man and Gypsum, which traces the thousand-year history of mining this mineral that shaped the village and its landscape. Allow 45 minutes for the climb, visit, and panorama.

Descend and take the path leading to the Torre dell'Orologio, perched on the second rocky pinnacle opposite the fortress. This 13th-century tower has a peculiarity: its clock shows only six hours, a historical detail that intrigues every visitor. The view from its summit complements that from the Rocca.

If you have time, make a detour to the Pieve del Tho (Pieve di San Giovanni in Ottavo), a Romanesque church located a few minutes from the center. Erected between the 8th and 10th centuries, it contains reused Roman fragments and ancient frescoes. It can be visited freely on Sunday afternoons; other days require booking.

activities.text: Brisighella sits within the Regional Park of the Vena del Gesso Romagnola, a striking landscape of white gypsum ridges, caves, and forests. Several hiking trails start directly from the village—the most accessible connects the three hills in a loop of about 1h30. For the more motivated, longer paths wind through the gypsum formations with spectacular views over the Romagna hinterland.

Brisighella is also a village of flavors. Its extra virgin olive oil PDO "Brisighello," obtained from the Nostrana variety cultivated only on these hills, was the first in Italy to obtain PDO certification in 1996. Local producers offer mill visits with tastings. Pair the oil with cured meats from Mora Romagnola, a local pig breed, and a glass of Sangiovese DOC.

days_plan.itin_title: Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills
Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine
10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

places.name: Buggiano Castello

visits.text: The Palazzo Pretorio of Buggiano Castello, a building from the first half of the 14th century, displays on its restored facade 57 coats of arms of the podestàs who governed the commune from the 14th to 17th centuries. It houses the municipal historical archive and 15th-century frescoes in its halls. It's one of the most complete examples of medieval civic architecture in the Valdinievole.

The church of Madonna della Salute e San Nicolao, founded as a Benedictine monastery in the 11th century, features an austere 12th-century Romanesque campanile. The three-nave interior preserves a 13th-century baptismal font, 16th-century paintings by G. Brina, and the remains of an adjoining cloister. The castle is documented from 1038 when the "da Buggiano" family founded the abbey there.

The Rocca and its clock tower, at the village's highest point, are the remnants of the defensive structure erected by the "da Buggiano" family who, in 1167, obtained from the Emperor a diploma confirming their possessions. The clock tower was completed with its mechanism in 1510.

Uzzano Castello, 3km from Buggiano, is a hilltop village perched on the hill dominating the Pescia valley. It's renowned for producing Bianco della Valdinievole, a DOC white wine from Trebbiano Toscano grapes. The medieval castle and its white limestone lanes can be visited in an hour.

activities.text: Walk through the citrus gardens of Buggiano Castello: the exceptionally mild microclimate of the village—sheltered from the north and open to the south—has favored since the Middle Ages the cultivation of orange, lemon and citron trees in private gardens, hence the nickname "Village of Citrus." Every two years, the village cultural association organizes "La campagna dentro le mura" (The countryside within the walls), opening these secret gardens to the public.
Olive oil and wine tasting from the Buggiano hills: the terraced olive groves surrounding the village produce a recognized IGP olive oil. Several estates offer cellar visits and farm tastings.

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

map_keywords: Chiesa della Madonna della Salute Buggiano

places.name: Buonconvento

visits.text: Porta Senese and ramparts: Buonconvento is one of the Italian villages that best preserves its medieval ramparts. Built from the late 13th century by Siena to protect this strategic node of Val d'Arbia on the Via Cassia, the walls still completely encircle the center. The Porta Senese, to the north, preserves its massive original wooden doors—the Porta Romana on the south side was destroyed in 1944 during the German retreat to delay the Allied advance.

Museum of Sacred Art of Val d'Arbia: Housed in the Palazzo Ricci Socini, a 19th-century building restored in Art Nouveau style in 1907 by architect Gino Chierici, this museum is a double curiosity—Sienese paintings from the 13th to 19th centuries displayed in a still-intact Liberty interior. The collection founded in 1926 by Father Don Crescenzio Massari includes the Madonna of Buonconvento by Duccio di Buoninsegna (1290-1295), Pietro Lorenzetti, Luca di Tommé, and canvases from the 17th-century Sienese school.

Church of Saints Peter and Paul and Palazzo Podestarile: The 14th-century church, rebuilt in the 18th century, was according to tradition the death place of Emperor Henry VII of Luxembourg in 1313—an engraved marble stone on its left façade bears the date 1103, possibly that of its foundation. The neighboring Palazzo Podestarile, with its Clock Tower and 25 podestà coats of arms, recalls that Buonconvento was Val d'Arbia's most important administrative center.

activities.text: Nova Eroica: Every spring, Buonconvento hosts this amateur cycling ride on dirt roads (strade bianche) with routes for all levels, including families and children—an accessible version of Gaiole's Eroica.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Museo d'Arte Sacra della Val d'Arbia Buonconvento
Chiesa dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Buonconvento

visits.text: The Castello quarter, founded in 1217 when Pisan nobleman Lamberto Visconti received the hilltop overlooking the city as a gift, forms Cagliari's fortified heart. Its winding lanes embrace seven churches, Aragonese palaces, and the Palazzo Regio, former residence of Spanish viceroys.

Santa Maria Cathedral, built by the Pisans in the 13th century (115 feet long, 111 feet wide, 105 feet high), houses two pulpits carved in the 12th century by Master Guglielmo for Pisa Cathedral, then transferred to Cagliari in 1312. Its crypt preserves the relics of 179 martyrs in individual niches.

Terrazza Umberto I, the former Saint Remy bastion, offers a sweeping 180-degree view from its esplanade across Cagliari's bay, lagoons, and surrounding hills. It's the evening promenade spot for locals.
The Bastione di Saint Remy, built between 1896 and 1902 on the old Spanish fortifications, offers from its terrace one of the widest panoramas over the city, the bay, and the salt lagoons.
The Marina quarter, the former medieval merchants' district between the port and Castello, concentrates the city's best seafood trattorias.

activities.text: Stroll through Parco di Monte Urpinu, a wooded hill in the heart of the city where peacocks and parrots roam semi-wild, with panoramic views over Cagliari's rooftops.
Swim at Poetto Beach, a five-mile ribbon of sand stretching east of the city between the sea and Molentargius Lagoon, frequented by pink flamingos.

Visit the National Archaeological Museum in the Cittadella dei Musei, which holds the world's largest collection of nuragic artifacts, including the famous bronzetti—bronze figurines dating from the 8th century BC.

Visit the San Benedetto covered market, one of Italy's largest indoor food markets (over 86,000 square feet), to pick up Sardinian cheeses, charcuterie, and wines before flying out from Cagliari-Elmas airport, 4 miles from downtown.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland
Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

places.name: Calcata

visits.text: Monterano Antica — A ghost town abandoned after a malaria epidemic in the late 18th century. The ruins include a partially standing baroque church and an ornate fountain featuring a lion, attributed to Bernini's circle. The site sits within the Riserva Naturale Monterano, where collapsed houses disappear beneath advancing vegetation. The gradual abandonment accelerated after 1799 when French troops ravaged the area.

Calcata Vecchia — A medieval village perched on a red tuff spur in the Treja valley, declared uninhabitable by fascist decree in 1935 due to the risk of the cliff collapsing. Its residents were relocated to Calcata Nuova, built 2 km away. In the 1960s-70s, artists, intellectuals, and hippies from across Europe and America recolonized the abandoned houses, transforming the village into a creative community that thrives to this day. The 15th-century Palazzo Baronale degli Anguillara now houses the visitor center for Parco Valle del Treja. The small Chiesa del Santissimo Nome di Gesù, built in the 14th century and restored in 1793, preserves a reliquary in a 15th-century tabernacle. Local legend claims that the Holy Prepuce, stolen during the 1527 Sack of Rome, was kept here until the 1980s.

activities.text: Hiking in the Parco Regionale Valle del Treja — The Treja River, a tributary of the Tiber, has carved gorges through volcanic tuff for several kilometers. Trail 015 leads to the ruins of the ancient Faliscan city of Narce on the rocky spur opposite Calcata, with a necropolis dating to the 8th century BC. The Cascate di Monte Gelato, a series of small waterfalls and pools formed by the river, lie 5 km away.

Visiting artists' workshops in Calcata — The caves and cellars carved into the tuff house workshops for ceramics, sculpture, jewelry-making, and furniture restoration. Several artists welcome visitors into their working spaces, continuing the tradition that repopulated the village since the 1960s.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani
Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

map_keywords: Chiesa del Santissimo Nome di Gesù Calcata

places.name: Calci

visits.text: The Certosa Monumentale di Calci, founded in 1366 by Pisan noble families, stands as one of Italy's best-preserved Carthusian monastic complexes. Transformed into a sumptuous Baroque residence during the 17th century—complete with frescoes, Carrara marble, and elaborate woodwork—it houses 15 monk cells arranged around a grand cloister, a refectory adorned with Bernardino Poccetti's Last Supper (1597), and the sacristy where the Calci Bible is displayed: an illuminated four-volume codex from 1168 that returned here in 2015 after a decade at Pisa's San Matteo Museum. Visits are guided only, by reservation.

The Natural History Museum of the University of Pisa occupies the certosa's former service quarters—granaries, cellars, wine press, stables—converted since 1979. Founded in 1591 by Grand Duke Ferdinand I de' Medici, it preserves four centuries of scientific collections: zoology, paleontology, minerals, and one of Europe's most important cetacean skeleton galleries. The granary, nearly 100 meters long, houses the collection's largest specimens.

The Pieve dei Santi Giovanni ed Ermolao, in the hamlet of La Pieve, is a late 11th-century Romanesque-Pisan building surrounded by terraced olive groves. First documented in 934, the church faces not the canonical west but toward the gate of the old castle—a detail suggesting it predated the fortified village itself.

activities.text: Hike to the Rocca della Verruca, a 13th-century Pisan Republic fortress perched at 537 meters on a rocky spur. Leonardo da Vinci surveyed the site in 1503 for the Florentine army, which made it their final stronghold during Pisa's siege—the fortress capitulated on June 18, 1503, sealing Pisan independence. Two trails connect the site from Calci or Vicopisano; ascent takes about an hour one-way. Views sweep across the Pisan plain, the Arno, and on clear days, the sea.

Stroll through the Valle Graziosa around the charterhouse. Along the stream crossing Calci, more than a hundred mills still operated a century ago. Ancient olive trees on the slopes create one of the most peaceful landscapes in the Pisan Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

map_keywords: Pieve dei Santi Giovanni ed Ermolao Calci

visits.text: Leave Caserta heading east and plunge into the countryside hinterland. After 2 hours driving through the mountains of Irpinia, you reach Calitri—nicknamed the "Positano of Irpinia" by the Italian Touring Club. The village is perched at 530 meters altitude on a tuff ridge, at the border of three regions: Campania, Basilicata, and Puglia. Its triangular silhouette of colorful houses, stacked in parallel tiers, is visible from afar in the landscape.

Climb to Borgo Castello, the upper part of the historic center built around the ruins of the 13th-century medieval castle, erected under Frederick II. The 1980 earthquake devastated this area—then inhabited—and froze it in time. Today, the ruins have been partially restored and the Ceramics Museum is installed there. Calitri is a member of the Italian Ceramic Towns: the pottery tradition dates back to Antiquity, with kilns and workshops documented since the 17th century.

Descend into the labyrinthine centro storico of narrow lanes. You'll encounter portals in carved local stone, noble palaces that survived the earthquakes, and the famous "cupe"—underground passages carved into the rock that once served as cellars. Step through the door of the church of San Canio, the village's patron saint celebrated on May 25, whose Baroque interior contrasts with the exterior sobriety.

Walking the streets, you'll notice caves carved into the tuff beneath the houses. Some still serve to age Caciocavallo di Grotta, a stretched-curd cheese hung in these natural cellars where humidity and constant temperature ensure slow aging. It's a typical product of eastern Irpinia, and local artisans will gladly let you taste it.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a wine tasting from Irpinia at one of the surrounding estates. The region produces three great vintages: Taurasi DOCG (Aglianico, often compared to Barolo), Fiano di Avellino DOCG (mineral and elegant white), and Greco di Tufo DOCG (white with almond notes). Irpinia is one of Italian wine's best-kept secrets—the vineyards grow between 400 and 700 meters altitude, yielding wines of remarkable freshness.

Late in the day, taste local specialties: acqua sala (slices of bread soaked in salted boiling water with hard-boiled eggs and spicy oil), cavatelli with beans, and Calitri's bread, baked in a wood oven according to ancestral tradition, whose aroma fills the lanes on baking days. It's peasant cooking, no frills, that warms the heart.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast

places.name: Camaiore

visits.text: Collegiata Santa Maria Assunta: Founded in the 13th century and profoundly remodeled in the Baroque era, this collegiate church is Camaiore's central monument. Its Romanesque façade contrasts with the decorative richness of the Baroque interior. The adjoining sacred art museum collections preserve furnishings, objects, and paintings from the 14th–16th centuries, including several noteworthy works of local provenance.

San Pietro Abbey: Founded by Benedictine monks between the 8th and 13th centuries, this three-nave Romanesque abbey with bell tower constitutes one of the oldest testimonies of Christianity in Versilia. Accessible on foot from the center via an olive-lined path, it's approached through a 14th-century gate preserving part of the original convent walls. The sober architecture in local limestone reflects Benedictine austerity.

Pieve dei Santi Giovanni e Stefano: Erected in the 12th century at the junction of the Via Francigena (of which Camaiore is the 26th stage), this Romanesque parish church has a bell tower with twin windows on three levels. Inside, the baptismal font was carved from a 3rd-century BC Roman sarcophagus—a characteristic reuse of medieval religious architecture.

Archaeological Museum of Palazzo Tori Massoni: Installed in a Renaissance palace, this museum preserves discoveries covering prehistoric, Ligurian, Etruscan, Roman, and medieval periods. The Camaiore region (from the Roman Campus Major, the great plain) was inhabited since the Neolithic, as attested by finds from the woods of Lucese.

activities.text: Hiking in the hills of Versilia around Camaiore: The hills rising toward the Apuan Alps offer routes through vineyards, olive groves, and perched villages like Monteggiori, Casoli, or Gombitelli, with views of the sea and countryside. Camaiore is an ideal starting point for hikes toward Monte Prana.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

map_keywords: Pieve dei Santi Giovanni e Stefano Camaiore

visits.text: Monastery of Camaldoli: founded in 1046, roughly twenty years after the hermitage, on a former pilgrim hostel called Fontebuona for the quality of its water. The current monastic building was rebuilt from the 16th century and completed in 1611. The church contains seven panels by Giorgio Vasari, including a main altarpiece depicting the Deposition from the Cross. The library, founded in 1622 with a coffered ceiling painted with the Evangelists, held hundreds of codices and incunabula before the Napoleonic suppression of 1810. The monastery's historic pharmacy, heir to a tradition dating back to the Camaldolese origins, produces and sells natural remedies, liqueurs and body-care products according to recipes handed down for centuries.

Eremo di San Romualdo: hermitage founded around 1023 by Saint Romuald of Ravenna at 1,100 meters, 3 km from the communal monastery — the very distance representing the intended separation between eremitic and cenobitic life. Romuald installed the first five hermit cells and the oratory dedicated to the Holy Savior Transfigured, consecrated in 1027 by the Bishop of Arezzo. The current enclosure contains twenty cells. Among the spaces open to visitors: Saint Romuald's former cell, the 16th-century chapter house, and the church whose baroque facade with two towers dates from 1713–1714. The Camaldolese order, a Benedictine branch combining eremitism and cenobitism, is distinguished from other Benedictines by its white habit and full beard.

activities.text: Hike through the Camaldoli forest: the monumental forest surrounding the hermitage is composed mainly of centuries-old white firs planted by the monks for their commercial value — their timber was used notably for the construction of the dome of Florence's Duomo and the fleet of Pisa and Livorno. Marked trails lead to the Laghetto Traversari, an artificial pond dug in the early 15th century by the monks to raise fish, now naturalized and incorporated into the Camaldoli biogenetic reserve.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

visits.text: Head to the Sila plateau, Calabria's "green lung." At 73,695 hectares, this national park extends between 1,000 and 1,928 meters altitude (Monte Botte Donato). Begin with the Giants of Sila Nature Reserve, at Fallistro, between Camigliatello Silano and Silvana Mansio. You'll find 58 Corsican black pines - an endemic variety of the plateau - nearly 400 years old, reaching 45 meters high with trunks 2 meters in diameter. This forest was planted in the 17th century by the Mollo barons. In the 1980s, Baroness Paola Manes-Mollo chained herself to a tree to prevent logging planned by a farmer who wanted to plant potatoes. Allow 45 minutes for the marked trail among these plant cathedrals.

Continue to Lake Cecita, the largest of the Sila's artificial lakes, at 1,130 meters altitude. By the lake, the Cupone Visitor Center offers a botanical garden, a wolf museum, and a wildlife observatory. The Fossiata and Gallopane forests surrounding the lake are among the park's most beautiful. If you're lucky, you'll spot reintroduced deer, roe deer, or black squirrels - an endemic subspecies of southern Italy.

activities.text: Take the road to Lake Arvo (1,278 m altitude), where the village of Lorica offers electric boat rides with spectacular views of the surrounding mountains. The trail around the lake is family-friendly - allow about 2 hours for the complete loop. In summer, operators also offer kayaking and canoeing.

If you pass through San Giovanni in Fiore, step inside the Florense Abbey, founded by Joachim of Fiore around 1189 - this visionary Cistercian monk is one of the most fascinating figures of the Calabrian Middle Ages. The abbey preserves Romanesque and Gothic elements. Also enjoy mountain specialties: mpanata (soup with ricotta and stale bread) and Sila porcini mushrooms.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea

places.name: Camogli

visits.text: Discovery of the picturesque bay of Camogli, its colorful facades and traditional fishing port.
Scuba diving to observe the submerged statue of Christ of the Abyss, emblem of the place.

activities.text: Walk along the maritime promenade and sunset over the Golfo Paradiso.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Maria Assunta Camogli

visits.text: Campli - a medieval town of 7,000 inhabitants at the foot of the Monti Gemelli, between the Siccagno and Fiumicino valleys. The Scala Santa is a replica of Pontius Pilate's staircase in Jerusalem, brought to Rome by Saint Helena in the 4th century. Campli's version, built in 1480 on commission from the Farnese family, has 28 steps that pilgrims climb on their knees.

activities.text: Quick stop at the Palazzo Farnese, a 15th-century Renaissance building testifying to the powerful Roman family's patronage in the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Castello Monforte: quadrangular fortress perched at 794m, built on an earlier Lombard tower. After the 1456 earthquake, Nicola II Monforte reconstructed it with double ramparts and semi-circular towers that also enclosed the main churches—creating a fortified citadel. The Monforte coat of arms (cross and four roses in a four-lobed frame) is visible above the former drawbridge entrance. The great summit tower now houses a weather station at 808m.
Museo Provinciale Sannito: Molise's oldest museum, established during the post-unification cultural movement. Samnite, Roman and medieval collections from regional excavations.
Chiesa San Giorgio: Campobasso's oldest church, documented from the 11th century, Romanesque style. Located directly below the castle.
Chiesa Sant'Antonio Abate (1572): late Renaissance façade and baroque interior with remarkable marble altar. One of many churches marking the climb to the castle.

Medieval historic center: sloping streets around the castle, stone portals adorned with family crests, interior courtyards. Below, the 19th-century Murattian quarter—grid layout with bourgeois villas—contrasts with the upper medieval section.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

visits.text: Fattoria Molisana at Campobasso: dairy specializing in caciocavallo podolico and caciocavallo, stretched-curd cheeses produced from milk of podolica cattle raised free-range in Molise pastures.

activities.text: Guided tasting of caciocavallo and other Fattoria Molisana cheeses, with production workshop visit and explanation of the hand-stretching technique.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

places.name: Campodimele

visits.text: Campodimele, perched at 647 meters between the Ausoni and Aurunci Mountains, holds the European title of "Village of Eternal Youth." In 2000, it counted 14 centenarians among 660 inhabitants. In 1985, the World Health Organization sent researchers on site: they discovered a cholesterol rate half the Italian average and very low blood pressure. A 2012 study by La Sapienza University in Rome identified a mutation of the ANGPTL3 gene that protects against cholesterol and diabetes. The village is accessible only on foot via its cobblestone lanes that wind between stone houses, covered passages, and small gardens.

The Church of San Michele Arcangelo, at the village's highest point, dates from the 11th century and was built on a former pagan worship site. Damaged by an earthquake, it was faithfully rebuilt. Inside, a tabernacle, ancient paintings, and wooden statues merit attention.

The Monastery of Sant'Onofrio, founded in the 11th century, suffered several destructions over the centuries before being restored in the 1980s. Its position below the fortified enclosure offers a viewpoint over the fertile valley below.

activities.text: Walk the medieval ramparts: the fortified enclosure, erected to protect inhabitants from Saracen raids, comprises twelve recently restored cylindrical towers. A path known to locals as the "Lovers' Walk" (Passeggiata dell'Amore) circles around it, offering panoramic views of the green valleys and Aurunci Mountains. On the Municipio square, an elm planted in 1789 allegedly commemorates the French Revolution.

Tasting of local products: the secret of longevity also lies in diet—little red meat, plenty of fruits, vegetables, and legumes from the garden, olive oil from the surrounding terraces. The village is part of "The Most Beautiful Villages in Italy" and has received the orange flag from the Italian Touring Club.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Campodimele

visits.text: Capalbio is a medieval village whose urban layout has remained virtually intact since the 15th century. Its double-walled ramparts with walkway, built between the 11th and 15th centuries, can be walked entirely. The Porta Senese preserves its original massive wooden doors from the 15th century. Inside, the Rocca Aldobrandesca adjoins the Palazzo Collacchioni, a richly frescoed Renaissance noble residence. The village was given in 805 by Charlemagne to the abbey of Saints Anastasius and Vincent alle Tre Fontane in Rome—one of the oldest donation acts in the Tuscan territory.

The church of San Nicola, built in the Middle Ages atop the hill around which the village wraps, is an architectural palimpsest: Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance are layered one upon another. It preserves a fresco cycle from the Umbrian-Sienese school, and the Oratory of Providence outside the walls contains 18th-century frescoes attributed to Pinturicchio's workshop.

Talamone, 25 km north on the coastal road, is a tiny port dominated by the Rocca degli Aldobrandeschi, a medieval fortress in excellent condition perched on a rocky promontory plunging into the sea. In 1860, Garibaldi stopped here with his Thousand to resupply arms before sailing to Sicily—a plaque commemorates the event. The village retains the atmosphere of an authentic fishing port, with a few hundred permanent residents.

activities.text: The Tarot Garden (Giardino dei Tarocchi), 2 km from Capalbio at the foot of the hamlet of Capalbiole, is a sculpture park created by Franco-American artist Niki de Saint Phalle starting in 1979, inspired by Gaudí's Park Güell in Barcelona. The 22 major arcana of the tarot are represented by monumental sculptures covered in ceramic mosaics, mirrors, and colored glass. Saint Phalle lived for several years inside the Judgement sculpture, transformed into an apartment. The garden opened to the public in 1998.

Walk along Capalbio's rampart walkway with panoramas over the Maremman countryside, the Tyrrhenian Sea, and on clear days Giglio Island and Monte Argentario. Local legend reports that Tiburzi, the Maremma's most famous brigand, spent much of his 19th-century flight here.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

visits.text: Villa Baciocchi occupies the site of the former castle of the Della Gherardesca counts, who founded Capannoli in the Middle Ages. The current building, in late Baroque and Neoclassical style, was restructured in the mid-18th century, then acquired in 1833 by Count Felice Baciocchi, husband of Elisa Bonaparte, Napoleon's sister. The villa now houses a Zoological Museum (taxidermied animals and dioramas) and an Archaeological Museum of the Valdera. The botanical park contains 160 plant species and 12 monumental trees.

The hamlet of Santo Pietro Belvedere preserves the urban structure of a medieval castle-borgo mentioned as early as 1175 in a treaty between Pisa and Lucca. The bell tower of San Pietro church, built by Luigi Bellincioni between 1881 and 1883, is founded on the remains of the old castle tower—whose Ghibelline battlements were still visible in 1789. The Oratorio di San Rocco contains a painted panel of the Virgin and Child attributed to the school of Benozzo Gozzoli.

activities.text: The municipality of Capannoli is part of the Pisan Hills Wine Route. Valdera wine estates offer tastings of local wines (Sangiovese, Vermentino, Trebbiano) paired with typical products: olive oil, truffles, pecorino.

Hiking or cycling on marked CAI trails crossing the hills between Capannoli, Peccioli, and Terricciola. The landscape of vineyards, olive groves, and isolated farms corresponds to the classic model of 16th-century Tuscan rural settlement.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

visits.text: Palazzo Farnese - An absolute masterpiece by Jacopo Barozzi da Vignola, this palace built between 1559 and 1575 for Cardinal Alessandro Farnese the Younger rests on the foundations of a pentagonal fortress begun in 1530 by Antonio da Sangallo the Younger. Vignola kept the five-sided shape but hollowed out the center to create an extraordinary circular courtyard with double superimposed porticoes. The Scala Regia, a helical staircase supported by 30 Doric columns, is so wide that - according to legend - the cardinal rode up it on horseback to the piano nobile. The halls are covered with 6,000 m² of Mannerist frescoes by brothers Taddeo and Federico Zuccari. The Sala del Mappamondo presents a map of the known world in 1574 painted by Giovanni Antonio Vanosino, surmounted on the ceiling by a representation of the zodiac. The Sala degli Angeli (or degli Echi) conceals an acoustic effect: two people whispering at opposite corners can hear each other as if standing side by side - a cardinal's stratagem for monitoring conversations.

Giardini Farnesiani - Behind the palace, two secret gardens (one summer, one winter) extend the architecture into the open air, with fountains, boxwood parterres, artificial grottos (Grotto of the Satyrs, Grotto of the Tartars), and statues. Higher up the hillside, the monumental park extends through woods to the Casina del Piacere, an isolated hunting lodge that served as summer residence for President Luigi Einaudi (1948-1955). The gardens are closed on Sundays and holidays.

Via Dritta and the town of Caprarola - Vignola wasn't content with just the palace: he had part of the medieval town razed to trace the Via Dritta, a 500-meter straight artery rising from the lower square to the palace piazzale, creating a theatrical perspective. The tufa and peperino houses of the side alleys preserve the atmosphere of a Tuscia Viterbese village.

activities.text: Tasting of Cimini Mountain hazelnuts - Caprarola lies at the heart of the nocciola dei Cimini production zone, a round, fragrant hazelnut used in creams, cakes, and torroni. The town's bakeries offer them in every form.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

visits.text: Caraglio, at the entrance to Valle Grana, houses the Filatoio Rosso, one of Europe's oldest silk mills, built in 1678. The red brick building now hosts the Piedmont Silk Museum. Valle Grana stretches twenty kilometers between the Cottian and Maritime Alps, climbing to Punta Tempesta (2,679 m).

Climb to the Sanctuary of San Magno at 1,761 meters altitude, at the head of Valle Grana. In 1475, Enrico Allemandi built the first Gothic chapel. Growing devotion required expansion in 1514—the decorations by Giovanni Botoneri, still visible, retain their original colors. The current sanctuary was completed in 1716 with the high altar added in 1775 and side porticos between 1861 and 1868. Excavations in 1894 unearthed a Roman altar dedicated to Mars and coins dating to around 250 AD, proof of occupation since imperial Roman times. Among the ex-votos hung inside, numerous portraits of cattle: the saint is considered protector of livestock.

The alpine pastures surrounding Castelmagno are the birthplace of Castelmagno DOP, a whole cow's milk cheese considered one of Italy's oldest. Produced only in the communes of Castelmagno, Pradleves, and Monterosso Grana according to aging techniques passed down through generations, it obtained protected designation of origin status. The village of San Pietro Monterosso preserves a settlement of dozens of Babaciu, life-size wooden figures inhabiting the alleyways.

activities.text: Ascent to Colle Fauniera (2,481 m) from the Sanctuary of San Magno, a pass famous for Giro d'Italia stages. A monument honors Marco Pantani. 360° views over the plain and border ridges.
Castelmagno DOP tasting with alpine producers in Castelmagno or Pradleves, with visit to an artisanal cheese dairy (aging in natural caves).

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

places.name: Caramanico Terme

visits.text: Caramanico Terme - a thermal village of 1,900 inhabitants on a spur at 650 meters, at the confluence of the Orfento and Orta rivers in the heart of Parco Nazionale della Majella. The sulfurous springs have been exploited since Roman times; modern thermal baths use the waters for respiratory and rheumatological treatments.

Chiesa di Santa Maria Maggiore - a 1476 Gothic building with a façade carved with plant and animal motifs. The interior preserves 15th-century frescoes, a 14th-century wooden crucifix, and a 16th-century Flemish tapestry depicting the Virgin and Child.

activities.text: Hike through the Gola dell'Orfento - a 6-kilometer gorge carved by the Orfento torrent into the Majella limestone, accessible from Caramanico. Habitat of peregrine falcon and otter.
Thermal cure at Caramanico's establishments - sulfurous water treatments recommended for respiratory conditions, open from May to November.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Maggiore Caramanico

places.name: Carmignano

visits.text: Pontormo's Visitation (1528-1530) is preserved in the Church of Saints Michael and Francis in Carmignano—currently undergoing restoration for water infiltration. During the works, the painting is temporarily displayed at the Villa Medici of Poggio a Caiano. The painting (202 × 156 cm, oil on wood panel) depicts the meeting of Mary and Elizabeth in a city street, surrounded by two witnesses with fixed, vacant gazes. Acid colors—reds, greens, and iridescent grays—elongated bodies apparently lifted on tiptoe, monumental drapery: Florentine Mannerism at its most accomplished. American artist Bill Viola drew inspiration from it for his video The Greeting (1995).

The Tumuli of Montefortini and Boschetti at Comeana, in the hills below the village of Artimino, are monumental tombs from the Orientalizing period (7th century BC) among the best preserved in central Tuscany. The Montefortini tumulus, about 60 meters in diameter, houses two burial chambers accessible for visits.

activities.text: Hiking or cycling through the vineyards of the Carmignano DOCG route, between the medieval village of Artimino and the wine estates of the Montalbano hills. In October the Palio dei Ciuchi takes place, a traditional race of the town's four neighborhoods on donkeys in medieval costumes.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Chiesa dei Santi Michele e Francesco Carmignano

places.name: Carpineto Romano

visits.text: Norma and Parco Archeologico di Norba — Norma sits on the 'Rave,' a limestone spur at 433 m plunging over 400 m sheer above the Pontine Plain. One kilometer from the modern village lie the ruins of ancient Norba, founded in the 5th century BC by the Volsci as a fortress controlling the plain. The 2.5 km circuit of opus polygonale walls—massive limestone blocks dry-fitted without mortar—culminates in two acropolises. Norba sided with Marius against Sulla and was destroyed in reprisal around 82 BC; abandoned, it was never rebuilt. From the site, on clear days the panorama stretches to Nettuno, Terracina, and the Pontine Islands.

Segni — A village at 668 m in the Monti Lepini with Cyclopean walls from the Republican Roman era among the best preserved in Lazio. The Porta Saracena, a monumental arch in 3-meter-high polygonal blocks dating to the 4th-3rd century BC, was frequently reproduced by Grand Tour artists. The Cattedrale di San Pietro e Paolo preserves a 12th-century Romanesque apse.

Carpineto Romano — A village at 745 m in the heart of the Monti Lepini, a feudal holding of the Colonna from the 14th to 17th centuries, with a preserved medieval historic center of sloping lanes and several noble palaces. The 16th-century Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta preserves an altarpiece from the Roman school.

activities.text: Hiking the trails of the Monti Lepini around Carpineto — The limestone massif offers routes between 700 and 1,500 m altitude through beech and oak forests. The villages of Segni, Norma, Bassiano, and Sermoneta are all accessible via marked trails of the Comunità Montana dei Monti Lepini.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

map_keywords: Cattedrale di San Pietro e Paolo Segni
Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta Carpineto Romano

visits.text: Before leaving Lunigiana, stop at Bagnone, on the route to Carrara (15 minutes' detour from Pontremoli). This small medieval village clings to a hillside above the torrent of the same name, which rushes down from the Apennines forming natural waterfalls. The castle, dominated by an imposing cylindrical keep, was built in the 14th century by the Malaspina - the powerful feudal family who controlled all of Lunigiana for centuries and sheltered Dante during his exile. The village divides into two parts: the upper section around the castle, and the lower, the old merchant quarter called 'Gutula', with its arcades and artisan workshops. Half an hour suffices to wander the alleys and admire the 14th-century medieval bridge over the torrent.

Arriving in Carrara, head up to the quarries of the Fantiscritti basin.
