Since Roman times (the port of Luna already exported marmor Lunensis under Julius Caesar), the Apuan Alps have been carved by quarries that supplied the raw material for some of the greatest works of Western sculpture. Michelangelo came personally to choose his blocks - it was Carrara marble that gave birth to the David (now in Florence) and the Pietà (St Peter's Basilica, Rome). Donatello, Bernini and Canova used it after him. The open-air museum at Fantiscritti, free of charge, traces the evolution of extraction techniques. The spectacle of white walls cut into giant tiers, like an amphitheatre carved into the mountain, is breathtaking.

Continue to the village of Colonnata, 8 km from Carrara, perched on the mountainside amid the quarries. This hamlet of 300 inhabitants is famous throughout Italy for its lardo - not simple lard, but an exceptional cured meat prepared since around the year 1000. The fat layer from the pig's back is seasoned with aromatic herbs and sea salt, then matured for 6 to 10 months in basins carved from marble, the 'conche'. In the past, this was the quarrymen's meal, which they slipped into their bread loaves with tomatoes to face the steep climbs and exhausting extraction work. Taste it sliced paper-thin on lightly warmed bread.

Descend into Carrara's historic centre to Piazza Alberica, lined with noble palaces with marble facades, then the Duomo (Cathedral of Sant'Andrea), built between the 11th and 14th centuries in local white marble, with its Gothic rose window. The town also preserves the Malaspina-Cybo Castle, transformed into a princely residence during the Renaissance.

activities.text: Tour operators offer guided 4x4 visits into the active quarries, including underground quarries - veritable cathedrals carved into the mountain. The Ravaccione gallery, accessible through the old marble railway tunnels (the 'Marmifera'), is where Michelangelo allegedly spent three years searching for the perfect block for the Pietà. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a complete visit with lardo tasting in Colonnata.

days_plan.itin_title: From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria

places.name: Casale Monferrato

visits.text: Admire the Cathedral of Sant'Evasio, consecrated in 1107, with its grand and rare Narthex that represents a masterpiece of architectural engineering. Inside you can admire a precious 13th-century wooden Crucifix and visit the Cathedral Treasure Museum in the Open Sacristy.

Visit the Jewish Complex, one of the most important in Italy, with the extraordinary Baroque Synagogue built in 1595. The interior decorations of surprising richness testify to the prosperity of the Jewish community of Casale. The complex also includes the Silver Museum and the Museum of Lights.

Explore the Civic Museum and Bistolfi Plaster Cast Gallery, which in addition to the Picture Gallery houses works by Symbolist sculptor Leonardo Bistolfi and an exhibition dedicated to Carlo Vidua, the traveling count who explored the Far East from the 19th century.

Discover the Castle of Monferrato in central Piazza Castello, today home to the Tourist Office, the Regional Enoteca of Monferrato, and cultural events. An unmissable visit to discover the wines and typical products of the territory.

activities.text: Taste the Krumiri Rossi, the symbolic biscuits of Casale Monferrato whose recipe dates back to 1878 by pastry chef Domenico Rossi. This tradition has been carried on since 1953 by the Portinaro Family, guardians of the secret of these unique sweets.

Reach the Sacro Monte di Crea, a UNESCO World Heritage Site in a splendid panoramic position. This devotional complex includes the Sanctuary, 23 chapels, and 5 hermitages built between 1589 and 1612 in a place of ancient Marian worship.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days

map_keywords: Sinagoga di Casale Monferrato Casale Monferrato
Sacro Monte di Crea Serralunga di Crea

visits.text: Take the morning ferry back to Naples, then drive 30 km north to Caserta. The Reggia di Caserta awaits: with its 1,200 rooms spread over 5 floors, 245-meter facade, and more than 2 million cubic meters of volume, it's the world's largest royal residence. Charles of Bourbon had it built from 1752 by architect Luigi Vanvitelli to rival Versailles. The king never slept there—called to the Spanish throne in 1759, he left Naples before construction was completed.

Inside, don't miss the Grand Staircase of Honor, which inspired the one in Saint Petersburg's Alexander Palace. The Palatine Chapel, inspired by Versailles' chapel, features a polychrome marble floor beneath majestic colonnades. The Royal Apartments alternate between 18th-century Baroque decor and Empire style—Joachim Murat, King of Naples from 1808 to 1815, remodeled several rooms. The Royal Theatre, inaugurated in 1769, has a unique feature: its backstage could open onto the park, creating a natural backdrop.

The Royal Park extends 120 hectares behind the palace. A 3-km axis aligns fountains, basins, and monumental cascades in perspective. The Grande Cascade, 78 meters high, is fed by the 38-km-long Carolino Aqueduct. At the foot of the cascade, the Fountain of Diana and Actaeon stages the goddess and her nymphs in a spectacular sculptural group. You can traverse the park on foot, by bicycle, or by carriage. Allow at least 1.5 hours to reach the cascade and return.

Before leaving the park, explore the English Garden, laid out in the late 18th century by botanist John Andrew Graefer on orders from Queen Maria Carolina of Austria. The atmosphere radically differs from the French garden: bodies of water hidden in vegetation, fake ruins, a cryptoporticus imitating a Roman temple, and the Bath of Venus with its statue of the goddess emerging from a basin. More than 200 botanical species coexist here.

activities.text: Late afternoon, drive up to Casertavecchia, the medieval village perched on the hill overlooking the modern town. At 400 meters altitude, this town founded by the Lombards in the 8th century has preserved its medieval charm intact: cobbled lanes, Norman tower, and the splendid 12th-century Romanesque cathedral with its tuff dome and Arab-Norman-inspired interlace. The panorama over the plain of Caserta and distant Vesuvius is worth the climb.

If you still have time, detour to the Belvedere di San Leucio, 3 km north of Caserta. This hamlet was transformed in the late 18th century by Ferdinand IV into a model silk manufactory, with a socially avant-garde set of rules: compulsory education, equal pay for men and women, ban on dowries. The palace and workshops are UNESCO-listed along with the Reggia.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast

places.name: Casole Elsa

visits.text: Medieval walls and circular towers: The ramparts of Casole d'Elsa have a characteristic elliptical shape, about 420 meters long on the main axis and 130 meters wide. Two circular towers built in 1481 on the eastern perimeter are the work of Francesco di Giorgio Martini, the greatest military architect of the Renaissance. Contested between Volterra, Siena, and Florence throughout the 13th century, the town definitively passed under Sienese rule in 1260.

Rocca Senese and Arte Viva Art Gallery: A 14th-century fortress now housing the town hall, the Rocca hosts a collection of drawings and paintings created by children from across the province, gathered by artist Giuseppe Ciani. The façade shows traces of defensive apparatus on corbels shaped like inverted pyramids.

Collegiate Church of Santa Maria Assunta: Consecrated in 1161, the Romanesque-Gothic church houses the cenotaph of Beltramo Aringhieri, a remarkable 14th-century funerary sculpture. In the adjacent presbytery, the Archaeological Museum of the Collegiate displays Etruscan pieces, paintings, and goldwork.

activities.text: Hiking in Selva di Sogno Park: Between Casole and Chiusdino, this forest park features sculptures and murals integrated into an oak wood. Marked trails, free access.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta Casole d'Elsa
Cenotafio di Beltramo Aringhieri Casole d'Elsa
Parco Selva di Sogno Casole d'Elsa

places.name: Cassino

visits.text: Isola del Liri — A unique phenomenon in Italy: an 89-foot waterfall in the town center. The Liri river, at the height of the Castello Boncompagni-Viscogliosi (built around 1000 AD, residence of the Dukes of Sora from 1579), splits into two arms around a travertine outcrop, creating a natural island and two waterfalls: the Cascata Grande (89 feet, vertical drop) and the Cascata del Valcatoio. In the 19th century, hydraulic power attracted French industrialists: Charles Lefebvre of Pontarlier founded a modern paper mill in 1812 whose 'endless' paper was exported to London to print the Times. The town was nicknamed 'Little Paris' or 'Manchester of Italy.' The former Cartiera Lefebvre, swallowed by vegetation, was rediscovered in 1995. The former Cartiera Boimond still preserves its continuous machine. The Liri Blues Festival (since 1988, sister-city partnership with New Orleans) animates the square in July. In May 1799, during the triennio giacobino, over 500 people were massacred in the church.

Abbazia di Montecassino — Italy's oldest monastery, founded in 529 by Saint Benedict of Nursia at 1,692 feet elevation, on the ruins of a Roman temple to Apollo and Jupiter from ancient Casinum. Here Benedict wrote his Rule ('Ora et labora') that structured European monastic life. Benedict and his sister Scholastica are buried beneath the high altar. The monastery was destroyed four times: Lombards (577), Saracens (883), earthquake (1349), Allied bombing on February 15, 1944. That day, 142 heavy and 114 medium bombers dropped 353 tons of bombs believing (wrongly) that Germans occupied the abbey. About 400 civilian refugees perished. The octogenarian abbot Gregorio Diamare survived and refused to sign a Nazi propaganda memorandum. The Battle of Cassino lasted 128 days (Jan-May 1944): over 55,000 Allied dead, 30,000 German. Polish soldiers took the ruins on May 18. Rebuilt identically (plans by Cosimo Fanzago, 17th-18th centuries), consecrated by Paul VI on October 24, 1964. Free entry. The 11th-century bronze door (survivor), the tabernacle by Nicola Salvi, the tomb of Pietro de' Medici, and the Codice Cassinese 512 of the Divine Comedy are among the treasures preserved. The Polish military cemetery (1,072 graves) bears the inscription: 'For our freedom and yours, we Polish soldiers gave our souls to God, our bodies to Italian soil, our hearts to Poland.'

activities.text: Admire the Cascata Grande from Piazza Boncompagni. Cross the Parco Fluviale along the Liri to see the Cascata del Valcatoio. Taste finifini (ultra-thin fettuccine with ragù), a local specialty.

Drive up to Montecassino via the scenic road. Cross successive cloisters: entrance cloister (former Apollo temple), Cistern cloister, Loggia del Paradiso, Benefactors' cloister. Basilica rebuilt with polychrome marbles. Crypt with bronze reliquary urn. Polish military cemetery.

days_plan.itin_title: Ulysses Riviera: Secret Gardens, Ancient Caves & Southern Lazio Islands
Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

map_keywords: Castello Boncompagni Viscogliosi Isola del Liri

places.name: Castagnole Delle Lanze

visits.text: Castagnole delle Lanze takes its name from the Lancia family - from the nickname Lancifero (lance-bearer) awarded to Lord Manfredo at Frederick Barbarossa's court in the twelfth century. Its history then follows that of the commune of Asti from the thirteenth century, then the Visconti in the fourteenth, finally Emanuele Filiberto of Savoy from 1573. The village sits in the UNESCO buffer zone of the Vineyard Landscapes of Langhe-Roero and Monferrato. Its vineyards primarily produce Moscato d'Asti, Barbera d'Asti, and Ruchè - a rare aromatic red wine from a grape variety typical of this zone.

Climb to the historic borgo of San Pietro, perched on the hill at 298 meters. The parish church of San Pietro in Vincoli, built in 1681 in Ligurian-Piedmontese baroque style, houses 230 carved cherubs, gilded stuccoes, and wooden altars inside - unexpectedly rich decor for a village of this size. The nineteenth-century panoramic tower, commissioned by Count Paolo Ballada di Saint Robert for his astronomical observations, dominates the Rimembranza park and offers a 360° view over the UNESCO buffer zone.

Along Via Ener Bettica, the arcades of the historic center are adorned with murals by local artist Vincenzo Piccato - thirteen arches in the colors of the seasons and wine constitute an open-air gallery on the nature of the Langhe and Monferrato. The church of the Confraternity of the Battuti Bianchi (1668), now deconsecrated, hosts exhibitions and concerts; it houses the Museum of Peasant Civilization of the Alto Monferrato.

activities.text: Tasting of Moscato d'Asti DOCG (sweet sparkling wine) and Barbera d'Asti in local cellars. The Adopt a Row of Vines initiative, launched in 2010 by the town hall, allows visitors to adopt ten meters of Barbera and receive ten bottles of wine - unique in Italy.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

map_keywords: Chiesa San Pietro in Vincoli Castagnole delle Lanze
Borgo San Pietro Castagnole delle Lanze
Via Ener Bettica Castagnole delle Lanze
Murales Vincenzo Piccato Castagnole delle Lanze

visits.text: Castel del Monte (AQ) - not to be confused with the fortress in Puglia - a medieval village at 1,345 meters at the foot of Gran Sasso, with 400 permanent inhabitants. The village is historically linked to transhumance: each autumn, shepherds drove their flocks down to Puglia via the Tratturo Magno, a 244-kilometer transhumance route inscribed in national heritage.

activities.text: Tasting of Pecorino Canestrato di Castel del Monte - a PDO sheep's cheese aged in woven baskets (canestri), an emblematic product of local transhumance.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Castel Del Rio

visits.text: Ponte Alidosi - a humpback medieval bridge spanning the Santerno, built in 1499 by Giovanni II Alidosi in a single 41 m arch. Considered one of Emilia-Romagna's best-preserved medieval bridges, it served the Via Emilia montana linking Bologna to Florence for five centuries.

Palazzo Alidosi - a 16th-century Renaissance palace, seat of the Alidosi family who ruled the Santerno valley for two centuries. Houses the Museo della Civiltà del Castagno, dedicated to the history of local chestnut groves, and the Museo della Linea Gotica, documenting the 1944-1945 fighting across the Bolognese Apennines.

Castagneti di Castel del Rio - centuries-old chestnut groves producing the Castagna di Castel del Rio IGP, awarded in 2009. The chestnuts, some over 300 years old, blanket the Apennine slopes between 400 and 900 m altitude. Historically nicknamed 'l'albero del pane' (the bread tree), they sustained mountain communities for centuries.

activities.text: Walk the waymarked trails through the chestnut groves around the village—in autumn (October-November), you can watch the harvest and some producers open their traditional drying sheds to visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Nicolò Castel del Rio

places.name: Castel Di Tora

visits.text: Castel di Tora, ranked among "Italy's Most Beautiful Villages," overlooks the artificial Lake Turano at 1,991 feet. First mentioned in 1035 in the Chronicon Farfense under the name "Castrum Vetus de Ophiano," it belonged to Farfa Abbey. The current name, adopted in 1864, recalls the ancient Sabine city of Tora. The medieval urban fabric remains intact: cobbled alleyways, arches, stairs and stone houses with tile roofs. The polygonal mastio tower dates from the 11th century. On the main square, the Triton fountain dates from 1898.

Cross the narrow land isthmus to reach the ghost village of Antuni, on its promontory in the middle of the lake. The Palazzo del Drago (15th–16th century), which had "365 windows like the days of the year," was mistakenly bombed by the U.S. Air Force in 1944—the pilot was aiming for the bridge—then abandoned by its inhabitants. After 40 years in ruins, it was restored between 2000 and 2002. Below, the Eremo di San Salvatore (14th century) clings to a rock face plunging into the lake.

Colle di Tora, a small village of 350 inhabitants on a tongue of land at the lake's edge, preserves its Romanesque chiesa di San Lorenzo and remarkable views of Castel di Tora across the turquoise waters. The village was nicknamed "Little Switzerland" after the artificial lake was created in the 1930s.

activities.text: Drive or walk around Lago del Turano, an artificial lake created between 1935 and 1938 by the construction of the Posticciola dam. The reservoir holds about 5.8 billion cubic feet of water and is connected to Lago del Salto by a 5.6-mile underground tunnel. The turquoise water, stone villages and mountains of the Riserva Naturale Monte Navegna and Monte Cervia create a spectacular landscape.
Taste lake fish (Turano perch fillet) and the "fagiolo a pisello," a small local bean cultivated only in this valley, star of the September festival.

days_plan.itin_title: Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit

map_keywords: Eremo di San Salvatore Lago Turano

places.name: Castel Gandolfo

visits.text: Ariccia — Piazza di Corte and Palazzo Chigi — A baroque ensemble designed by Gian Lorenzo Bernini for the Chigi family after they acquired the town in 1661, comprising Palazzo Chigi with its two curved wings framing the piazza, and the Chiesa di Santa Maria dell'Assunzione capped by a dome inspired by the Pantheon. Palazzo Chigi preserves a collection of 17th-18th century paintings and period-furnished apartments. To reach Ariccia's center, you cross the Ponte Monumentale, a viaduct 58 m high and 301 m long built in 1847-1854 over the Vallericcia gorge.

Frascati — Ville Tuscolane and Villa Aldobrandini — Frascati developed on the ruins of ancient Tusculum, destroyed by Roman armies in 1191. From the 16th century onward, cardinals and noble families built about a dozen country villas on its hills—the Ville Tuscolane—of which Villa Aldobrandini (1598-1603), designed by Giacomo della Porta and Carlo Maderno, is the most imposing. Its baroque-style terraced gardens with nymphaeum and cascades are open to the public. Cicero already owned a villa at Tusculum where he wrote the Tusculanae Disputationes.

Abbazia Greca di San Nilo at Grottaferrata — A monastery founded in 1004 by Saint Nilus of Rossano, a Basilian monk of Calabrian origin, fifty years before the Great Schism. It remains the last Byzantine-Greek rite monastery in Italy, still in communion with Rome. The abbey church, consecrated in 1024 by Pope John XIX, preserves a 13th-century mosaic on the triumphal arch depicting the Pentecost on a gold ground, attributed to the same workshops as Monreale Cathedral. The crypta ferrata—a Republican-era Roman burial chamber closed by iron grilles—gave Grottaferrata its name.

Palazzo Apostolico di Castel Gandolfo — The papal summer residence since Urban VIII first stayed here in spring 1626, after entrusting the conversion work to Carlo Maderno, architect of St. Peter's facade. The Ville Pontificali complex covers 55 extraterritorial hectares—a status granted by the 1929 Lateran Pacts—including 35 hectares of gardens and 20 hectares of farmland. In 2016, Pope Francis transformed the palace into a museum, ending four centuries of papal summer residence. The gardens include remains of Emperor Domitian's villa (81-96 AD).

activities.text: Walking along Lake Albano's shores from Castel Gandolfo — The largest lake in the Colli Albani at 6 km² surface area and 170 m depth, connected to the sea since antiquity via a 4th-century BC Roman emissary still in operation. The ring road follows the shore for 10 km with views of the tuff cliffs.

Tasting porchetta d'Ariccia IGP in the local fraschette — Ariccia porchetta has held IGP status since 2011. It's prepared with a whole deboned pig, stuffed with rosemary, garlic, and black pepper, rolled and slow-roasted in a wood oven. The fraschette—traditional osterias where you eat elbow-to-elbow—perpetuate a simple dining tradition documented since the 18th century in the Castelli Romani.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria dell'Assunzione Ariccia
Abbazia Greca di San Nilo Grottaferrata

places.name: Castel San Vincenzo

visits.text: Abbey of San Vincenzo al Volturno: founded in the 8th century, one of medieval southern Italy's most powerful Benedictine abbeys. Sacked by Saracens in 881, partially rebuilt. Excavations uncovered a palatial complex and crypts with 9th-century Carolingian frescoes among the most important preserved in Italy.
Lago di Castel San Vincenzo: artificial lake (1950s dam) within Abruzzo, Lazio and Molise National Park. Clear waters framed by Mainarde forests, with views of peaks above 2,000m.
Volturno waterfalls at Cartiera: the Volturno rises in the Mainarde and forms several falls accessible on foot from the lakeshore.

activities.text: Hiking in the National Park: marked forest trails with documented presence of Apennine wolves, Marsican bears and deer.
Water activities on the lake: local operators offer kayaking, sport fishing and rowboat rentals.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Abbazia di San Vincenzo al Volturno Castel San Vincenzo
Lago di Castel San Vincenzo Castel San Vincenzo
Cascate del Volturno Castel San Vincenzo
Parco Nazionale d'Abruzzo Lazio e Molise Castel San Vincenzo

places.name: Castelfranco Di Sotto

visits.text: Castelfranco di Sotto was founded in the first half of the 13th century by four hamlets on the banks of the Arno and Usciana who united to defend themselves from struggles between Guelphs, Ghibellines, Pisa, Lucca, and Florence. Lucca encouraged settlement by exempting from taxes any inhabitant who settled there—hence the name "Castelfranco" (free castle). The original borgo was ringed with walls and 16 towers; the original walls were destroyed by an Arno flood in 1333 and completely rebuilt. The Roman castrum plan—two main axes crossing at the center, four gates oriented to the cardinal points—is perfectly legible today.

The Palazzo Pretorio, on the main square, is the former seat of Florentine dominion. The historic center still preserves three of the four original gates—each corresponding to a contrada—and the medieval underpasses scattered through the alleyways. The Archaeological Museum assembles objects from prehistory to the Middle Ages, including two unique finds: remains of a Roman bridge and a medieval canoe used on the former Lake Bientina.

activities.text: The Riserva Naturale di Montefalcone, the most important of the Cerbaie, is crossed by a section of the medieval Via Francigena. Pine and oak woods, rare fauna, marked trails connecting Castelfranco to Orentano. A one to two-hour walk is enough to reach the first edges from the town center.

Palio dei Barchini con le Ruote: every third Sunday in May, the four contrade (San Michele in Caprugnana, San Martino in Catiana, San Bartolomeo a Paterno, San Pietro a Vigesimo) compete in a race of wheeled little boats through the borgo's streets, accompanied by itinerant dinners. Tradition established since 1987.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

map_keywords: Riserva Naturale di Montefalcone Castelfranco di Sotto

visits.text: Discover Castelfranco Veneto, birthplace of the renowned painter Giorgione and a gorgeous town with an almost intact medieval defensive wall. The substantial brick walls encircle a perfectly preserved historic center filled with architectural treasures.

Visit the Cathedral to see Giorgione's Madonna, the masterpiece Castelfranco Madonna that draws art lovers from around the world. This revolutionary work exemplifies the Venetian High Renaissance style Giorgione pioneered.

activities.text: Walk the walls and explore the charming center, perhaps timing your visit for one of the town's festivals including the popular chocolate fair. The medieval atmosphere provides excellent settings for photography.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

places.name: Castell Arquato

visits.text: Explore the Rocca Viscontea, an imposing medieval fortress built between 1342 and 1349 at the behest of Luchino Visconti of Milan. With its four square corner towers, double defensive walls, and deep moat, it represents one of the best-preserved castles in Italy. Today it houses a fascinating Museum of Medieval Life.

Admire the Palazzo del Podestà in Piazza del Borgo, emblem of civic power built at the end of the 13th century by Alberto Scotti, lord of Piacenza. The elegant exposed brick façade dominates the village's main square, creating one of the most photographed medieval views in Emilia Romagna.

Visit the Collegiate Church of Santa Maria, a place of worship with ancient origins dating back to the 7th century. The sober Romanesque gabled façade decorated with blind arches hides naves adorned with precious 15th-century frescoes of extraordinary beauty.

Discover the Geological Museum housed in the ancient Hospital of Santo Spirito, a 13th-century building that once offered hospitality to pilgrims on the Via Francigena. The paleontological collections bear witness to millions of years of natural history in the Piacenza territory.

activities.text: Stroll through the Garden of Poetry, a large green expanse where you can find a silent refuge among evergreen trees and romantic benches. From here you can admire the entire Val d'Arda and several monuments dedicated to illustrious figures such as the luthier Antonio Stradivari.

Lose yourself in the cobblestone lanes of the village, one of the best-preserved windows onto medieval life in Italy, framed by noble palaces and Romanesque churches expressing the power of the Scotti, Visconti, and Sforza families.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles of the Piacenza Hills: A 4-Day Medieval Road Trip Through Northern Italy
Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta Castell'Arquato

places.name: Castellaro Lagusello

visits.text: Pass through the fortified gate of Castellaro Lagusello, a hamlet of Monzambano in the heart of Mincio Park. The village owes its name to its origin: a 'castelliere' (defensive enclosure empty of dwellings) on a 'lagusello' (small lake). The Scaligeri of Verona built a castle here in the 13th century; the site appears first in a diocesan document from 1145. It then passed to the Visconti, the Gonzaga, and in 1441 to Venice, before being auctioned in 1637 for 545 ducats to Count Arrighi, who transformed the fortress into an inhabited noble court.

From the Clock Tower at the north entrance, walk the cobbled lanes to Villa Arrighi-Toccoli, the hamlet's only patrician residence—still owned by descendants today. The original crenellated walls, four towers, and two medieval rustic houses remain intact. Napoleon I stayed here in 1796 to direct his Po Valley operations; in 1859, Napoleon III and Mac Mahon would establish their headquarters here during the Battle of Solferino.

Visit the San Nicola church, mentioned as early as the 12th century, which preserves a 15th-century gilded wooden sculpture of the Virgin and several 17th-century canvases. From the recently restored bell tower, open April through October on weekends, the view plunges onto the heart-shaped lake ringed with reeds and the morainic hills of the Lake Garda amphitheater.

activities.text: Descend to the heart-shaped lake via the small stairway south of the village: a wooden boardwalk crosses the protected wetland of the Castellaro Lagusello Morainic Complex Nature Reserve (271 hectares). Excavations have uncovered Bronze Age pile-dwelling settlements, now displayed at the Archaeological Museum of Alto Mantovano in Cavriana.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Nicola Castellaro Lagusello

visits.text: Castelli - a village of 1,000 inhabitants at the foot of Monte Camicia (2,564 m), renowned since the 16th century for its majolica ceramics. The Grue, Gentili, and Cappelletti families produced pieces here now preserved in Europe's greatest museums, including London's Victoria & Albert Museum.

Chiesa di San Donato - a small 16th-century church outside the center, whose ceiling is entirely covered with 1,200 majolica tiles painted by the village's master ceramists between 1615 and 1617. Each tile depicts a saint or floral motif, forming Italy's most extraordinary architectural majolica ensemble.

Museo della Ceramica di Castelli - a museum housed in a modern building tracing five centuries of local ceramic production. The collection includes pieces from the 16th-18th centuries, the golden age of Castelli workshops.

activities.text: Visit working artisan workshops in the village: about ten ceramic workshops continue traditional techniques of painting on unfired majolica and wood-fired kiln firing.
Hike toward Monte Camicia from Castelli - starting from the village toward the Gran Sasso alpine pastures, with views over the Vomano valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Castellina In Chianti

visits.text: Via delle Volte: A 200-meter underground gallery carved along the ancient ramparts, designed by Filippo Brunelleschi during the village's 15th-century fortification to withstand gunpowder explosions. Today, its openings onto the Chianti landscape and its traditional taverns make it the village's most striking architectural feature.

Rocca di Castellina: A 15th-century fortress built by Florence to strengthen its southern border against Siena. Its imposing keep dominates the town from the hilltop. Inside, the Archaeological Museum of the Sienese Chianti, opened in 2006, displays discoveries from the Bronze Age through the Middle Ages, including an Etruscan chariot.

Etruscan Tumulus of Montecalvario: A few minutes' walk from the center, this 6th-century BC necropolis contains five tombs with four chambers and one cist, freely accessible. Nearby, the Poggino necropolis at Fonterutoli also holds 6th-century BC tombs.

Church of San Salvatore: The original building dates from the 15th century, but it was almost entirely destroyed during World War II and rebuilt in neo-Romanesque style by Sienese architect Menotti Lucatelli. Inside are preserved a Madonna and Child by Maestro di Signa and a wooden statue of Saint Barnabas.

activities.text: Chianti Classico tasting: Castellina sits at the heart of the Chianti Classico DOCG, whose symbol—the black rooster—takes its name from a medieval legend pitting Siena against Florence. The vineyards surrounding the village offer tastings of Chianti Classico and Chianti Classico PDO olive oil.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Via delle Volte Castellina in Chianti
Rocca di Castellina Castellina in Chianti
Tumulo di Montecalvario Castellina in Chianti
Chiesa di San Salvatore Castellina in Chianti
Piazza del Comune Castellina in Chianti

visits.text: Borgo di Castelluccio - This village of about 30 inhabitants perched at 1,452 meters altitude is Umbria's highest inhabited settlement. It dominates the Pian Grande, a 15 km² karst plateau formed by the drying of a lake about one million years ago. The October 2016 earthquake (magnitude 6.5) destroyed most of the village; its reconstruction spans several years but access to the plateau is restored. 360° panoramic views of the Sibillini Mountains, including the Monte Vettore summit (2,476 m), the watershed between Umbria and Marche.

Piana di Castelluccio - This high karst plateau comprises three plains: Pian Grande, Pian Piccolo, and Pian Perduto. Between late May and mid-July, the simultaneous blooming of lentils (white flowers with bluish reflections), poppies, cornflowers, wild mustard, and Venus's looking-glass covers the 20 km² of cultivated land with a mosaic of colors changing week by week. The phenomenon is due to pesticide-free agriculture practiced since antiquity: farmers have preserved production methods unchanged for 3,000 years (Castelluccio lentil seeds have been found in Neolithic tombs). Each species blooms at a slightly different period, which modifies the dominant colors of the landscape throughout the season.

activities.text: Hiking on the trails of Parco Nazionale dei Monti Sibillini - The national park covers over 70,000 hectares between Umbria and Marche. From Castelluccio, several marked trails reach the ridges overlooking the plateau in 2 to 4 hours. The trail to Rifugio Perugia offers views of Monte Vettore's fossil glacier.

Tasting of lenticchie di Castelluccio IGP - Grown at 1,500 meters altitude on the karst plains, these small round lentils with skin so thin they cook without soaking in 20 minutes obtained IGP label in 1997 (some sources indicate 1999 for the EU version). They are rich in protein, iron, and fiber. Sold directly in the village's few grocery stores or to restaurants around Norcia, which make soups and side dishes with local olive oil and black truffles.

Paragliding and hang gliding above Pian Grande - The plateau's configuration surrounded by reliefs over 2,000 meters generates thermals favorable for free flight. Local operators offer tandem flights allowing you to admire the flowering mosaic from the sky.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

visits.text: Mountain road from Calitri (about 1h40) through the wild landscapes of Basilicata. Arriving in Castelmezzano, the visual impact is immediate: this village of 800 souls is literally embedded in the sandstone spires of the Lucanian Dolomites, as if thrust out of the rock. Its name, from the Latin Castrum Medianum (the castle in the middle), recalls its position between Pietrapertosa and Albano di Lucania. Classified among Italy's "Most Beautiful Villages," it was founded by shepherds fleeing Saracen incursions.

Climb the Gradinata Normanna, a staircase of 54 steps carved directly into the rock, which once led to the watchtower of the Norman-Swabian castle. The ascent is short but impressive—no handrail, vertiginous overhang. From the top, the view plunges over the village, the Basento valley, and the wind-eroded sandstone spires. The visit is only possible with a licensed local guide—book in advance.

Descend to the Chiesa Madre Santa Maria dell'Olmo, built in local stone in the 13th century. Inside, you'll find a polychrome wooden statue of the Virgin and Child dating from the 14th century and a Baroque altar. On the exterior walls, a Templar cross is still visible—the Knights Templar left traces in the village, also visible in the municipal coat of arms which reproduces the Templar seal of two knights on a single horse.

Traverse the village using the lanes that run along the bases of the sandstone monoliths. The juxtaposition of stone houses and massive cliffs is striking—in places, the rock serves as the load-bearing wall of dwellings. Reach the panoramic viewpoint east of the village for an unobstructed view over the entire chain of the Lucanian Dolomites and the village of Pietrapertosa, clinging in an amphitheater on the other side of the valley.

activities.text: If you love adrenaline, the Volo dell'Angelo is the region's star attraction. Hooked to a steel cable stretched between Castelmezzano and Pietrapertosa, you fly over the valley at an altitude of 400 meters, over a distance of 1,452 meters, at speeds reaching 120 km/h. There are two lines: the Peschiere line (Castelmezzano → Pietrapertosa) and the San Martino line (return). The ticket includes both flights and a shuttle between the two villages.

For hikers, the Path of the Seven Stones links Castelmezzano to Pietrapertosa through the valley—allow 1.5 hours one way. Seven stations mark the route, each marked by a sculpture and a poem evoking local legends. The path descends into the wooded valley before climbing back up to the other village. It's a perfect complement to the Volo dell'Angelo—do one in one direction, the other in the other.

days_plan.itin_title: Basilicata: From Matera to Pollino, Castles, Ghost Towns, and the Lucanian Dolomites
From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast

places.name: Castelnuovo Garfagnana

visits.text: Rocca Ariostesca: This trapezoidal fortress has dominated Piazza Umberto I since the 10th century, but its 14th-century reconstruction by Castruccio Castracani gave it its current form. It takes its name from poet Ludovico Ariosto—author of Orlando Furioso—who resided here from 1522 to 1525 as Este governor for Alfonso I d'Este, Duke of Ferrara. Ariosto, who called Garfagnana "land of wolves and brigands," nevertheless wrote several of his Satires here. The central tower, equipped with a civic clock, and the terrace added in 1675 overlooking the square make it one of the most complete examples of communal fortification in the region. Every Thursday, the weekly market is held at the foot of the rocca—a tradition so ancient that a ducal decree once forbade arresting criminals on that day.

Cathedral of Santi Pietro e Paolo: Built in the 15th century in Renaissance style on an 11th-century Romanesque church, then largely remodeled in the Baroque era. The austere gray stone façade contrasts with the richness of the interior: apse redone in the 19th century, Baroque altars, and series of 15th–16th century paintings. The church is a waypoint on the Via Matildica del Volto Santo linking Mantua to Lucca.

Medieval Historic Center and ramparts: The fortified heart of Castelnuovo, at the confluence of the Serchio and Turrite Secca, preserves its medieval alleys, arched gates (Porta dei Borselli), and Clock Tower. The town is the administrative capital of Garfagnana and the meeting point of two historic pilgrimage routes.

Orecchiella Nature Reserve: Created in 1980, this 500-hectare reserve in the Tuscan-Emilian Apennines shelters chamois, deer, foxes, and vegetation of beech forests and alpine meadows. The Corfino visitor center offers exhibitions on local fauna.

Lago di Vagli and the sunken village of Fabbriche di Careggine: The artificial lake created in 1953 for a hydroelectric plant submerged the village of Fabbriche di Careggine. During the decennial draining of the dam, the ruins reappear—a phenomenon that attracts thousands of visitors each time. The site offers panoramas over the Apuan Alps.

activities.text: Sentiero dell'Ariosto to Fortezza di Mont'Alfonso: A 6 km hiking trail dedicated to the poet, starting from Rocca Ariostesca, crossing the bridge over the Turrite Secca and climbing toward the village of Torrite then the bastioned fortress of Mont'Alfonso, built by Alfonso II d'Este between 1579 and 1586, with panoramas over the Apuan Alps and Apennines.
Thursday weekly market: Castelnuovo's market is the largest in Garfagnana, with cheeses, charcuterie (biroldo della Garfagnana, Colonnata lard), and mountain products.

Hiking in the Apuan Alps (Parco Regionale): The regional park protects the white marble peaks dominating Versilia. Trails of varying difficulty lead toward Pania della Croce (1858 m). CAI (Italian Alpine Club) refuges allow outings of 4 to 8 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast
Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

map_keywords: Duomo dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Castelnuovo di Garfagnana
Fortezza di Mont'Alfonso Castelnuovo di Garfagnana
Riserva Naturale dell'Orecchiella Castelnuovo di Garfagnana
Parco Regionale delle Alpi Apuane Castelnuovo di Garfagnana

visits.text: Castelsardo perches on a rocky promontory overlooking the Gulf of Asinara since the 12th century. The Castello dei Doria, built by the powerful Genoese Doria family, now houses the Museum of Mediterranean Basket Weaving, tracing in nine rooms the art of basket making—a craft still alive in the town.

Sant'Antonio Abate Cathedral, perched at the village summit, is recognizable by its blue-green dome visible from the sea. Inside, it preserves the Black Christ of Castelsardo, a 14th-century polychrome wood statue carried in procession on Easter Monday during the Lunissanti.

activities.text: Climb through the labyrinth of cobbled lanes to the promontory's summit, with successive views over the sea, colorful houses, and the Doria castle—one of northern Sardinia's best-preserved villages.
Shop for local crafts in boutiques specializing in traditional basketry made from asphodel, dwarf fan palm, and willow—a technique passed from mother to daughter for centuries.
Swim at Lu Bagnu beach, a few miles from the medieval center, with clear waters facing the Gulf of Asinara.
Dine on seafood in the harbor restaurants, facing the sea that has been the village's main economic activity for generations.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

places.name: Castiglion Fiorentino

visits.text: Torre del Cassero: imposing tower rebuilt around 1350 by the Perugians — the town was then called Castiglion Perugino — on an Etruscan oppidum on the acropolis. Originally 50 meters tall (slightly reduced after a 19th-century lightning strike), it dominates the medieval center ringed by 13th-century ramparts. In 1384, Florence purchased the town from Charles of Durazzo, who needed cash to fund his Hungarian mercenary troops, and renamed it Castiglion Fiorentino.

Vasari Loggia: built in 1513 by the craftsmen Bernardo di Ghieba and Filippo da Bellinzona, it was reworked between 1560 and 1570 by Giorgio Vasari, who had the Medici coat of arms in pietra serena placed on it. Three arches open onto the valley, creating a unique architectural balcony over the Val di Chio, between the Valdichiana and Valtiberina. The interior walls are adorned with polychrome Della Robbia terracotta coats of arms of the Florentine commissioners and podestàs.

Municipal Art Gallery and Museum of Sacred Art of the Pieve: the gallery, housed in the former 12th-century church of Sant'Angelo al Cassero, preserves works by Tuscan masters — Taddeo Gaddi, Bartolomeo della Gatta, Ghirlandaio and Margarito d'Arezzo. The Pieve museum holds the fresco Compianto sul Cristo Morto by Luca Signorelli and a Della Robbia school altarpiece depicting the Baptism of Christ. The church of San Francesco houses a 1548 canvas by Giorgio Vasari — the Virgin with Saint Anne, Saint Anthony and Saint Sylvester — whose preparatory drawing is at the Louvre.

Castello di Montecchio Vesponi: a medieval fortress 3 km south of the town, documented since the 10th century. It was seized and redesigned by Florence in the late 13th century before losing its military function. The enclosure features eight turrets along 263 meters, with a 30-meter keep. Abandoned then repurchased in 1860 by the banker Giacomo Servadio, it's one of the best-preserved medieval fortresses in the province of Arezzo.

activities.text: Palio dei Rioni: an equestrian competition pitting the three historic quarters — Cassero, Porta Fiorentina and Porta Romana — against each other every third Sunday of June on the Piazzale Garibaldi. The tradition dates back to 1864 but the current version was established in 1932.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

map_keywords: Castello di Montecchio Vesponi Castiglion Fiorentino
Chiesa di San Francesco Castiglion Fiorentino

places.name: Castiglione Del Lago

visits.text: Rocca del Leone - This fortress was built in 1247 according to plans by Frate Elia Coppi da Cortona on a limestone promontory that was once Lake Trasimeno's fourth island. Frederick II of Swabia had a first castle erected there—his emblem was the eagle, but the lion symbolized the Empire in Italian tradition, hence the name Castello del Leone. The Rocca presents an irregular pentagonal plan with five towers and a triangular keep about 30 meters high offering a 360° view. A covered passage on murder holes, similar to the one connecting the Vatican to Castel Sant'Angelo, connects it to the neighboring Palazzo della Corgna. Currently used as a natural amphitheater.

Palazzo della Corgna - Built in 1560 by Ascanio della Corgna (nephew of Pope Julius III, Marquis of Castiglione and war captain who died at the Battle of Lepanto in 1571), according to plans attributed to either Vignola or Galeazzo Alessi, this Renaissance palace is one of the best examples of late Mannerism in Umbria and Tuscany. Its eight rooms on the piano nobile are entirely decorated with frescoes by Niccolò Circignani known as Il Pomarancio (1574-1590) and Salvio Savini: mythological scenes, Ascanio's military exploits, Aeneid cycle (allusion to the family's first name—Ascanius is Aeneas's son) and a representation of the Battle of Lake Trasimeno of 217 BC. The Hall of the Acts of Ascanio notably depicts the "duel of the century" during which Ascanio pierces his adversary Giannetto Taddei with a single blow.

Chiesa di Santa Maria Maddalena - Built in 1836 on the ruins of an earlier medieval church, it houses a 1500 altarpiece depicting the Virgin with Child, Saint Anthony Abbot and Saint Mary Magdalene, attributed to Eusebio da San Giorgio, Perugino's student. The work was for some years at the center of an attribution controversy with Raphael.

activities.text: Ferry crossing to Isola Polvese - From Castiglione del Lago's pier, the ferry serves the largest of Lake Trasimeno's three islands (about 70 hectares). The island is a regional nature reserve: a 13th-century medieval castle, the ruins of San Secondo's Olivetan monastery and a centuries-old olive grove coexist with preserved lake flora. Trails allow you to circle it in less than two hours.

Lake cycling tour - A 60-kilometer cycling and pedestrian path allows you to make a complete tour of Lake Trasimeno alternating between beach, fields, and villages. The northern section passes through Tuoro sul Trasimeno, site of the battle of June 24, 217 BC where Hannibal ambushed Consul Caius Flaminius and massacred about 15,000 Roman soldiers in the morning fog—one of the greatest defeats in Roman history.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries
Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

map_keywords: Rocca del Leone Castiglione del Lago
Palazzo della Corgna Castiglione del Lago
Chiesa di Santa Maria Maddalena Castiglione del Lago

places.name: Castro Dei Volsci

visits.text: Castro dei Volsci—whose name derives from the Latin castrum (fortified camp) and the Volscian people who settled here between the 5th and 6th centuries BC—perches at 385 meters on a spur of the Ausoni Mountains. Its medieval historic center, certified as one of I Borghi più Belli d'Italia and awarded the Touring Club's Bandiera Arancione, is organized around the Via Civita: a ring road where the houses facing the valley themselves serve as ramparts. Four gates allow entry to the enclosure; the finest, the Porta della Valle, is a Romanesque pointed arch opening directly onto the Church of Santa Maria.

The Church of San Nicola was founded between 542 and 552 by monks from Montecassino around a simple Benedictine oratory. It preserves 12th-century Romanesque frescoes in distinctly Byzantine style, depicting scenes from the Old and New Testaments. The medieval village gradually developed around this church. Castro subsequently became a fief of the Colonna, Borgia, and then Carafa families.

Scattered across doorways, steps, and walls throughout the borgo, symbols carved in stone testify to the passage of the Knights Templar: the Golgotha (cross on three steps), the Triple Enclosure, and the Flower of Life. Their presence at Castro dei Volsci is explained by the village's strategic position on the route between Rome and the Kingdom of Naples, at the ancient border between the Papal State and the Bourbons.

The Monument to the Mamma Ciociara, inaugurated June 3, 1964 on the village's summit belvedere, is carved in white marble and commemorates the violence suffered by the region's women during World War II—episodes that Alberto Moravia fictionalized in La Ciociara (Two Women) and that Vittorio De Sica brought to the screen. On clear days, the panorama from this viewpoint embraces Montecassino, the Lepini Mountains, and the Ernici.

The Civic Archaeological Museum of Castro dei Volsci assembles finds from the archaeological zone of Santa Maria del Piano: remains of a 1st-century BC Roman villa, an imperial villa, an early Christian basilica, and a necropolis from the 6th-7th centuries. The centerpiece is a 3rd-century marble sarcophagus fragment.

activities.text: Walk to the Pastena Caves, 11 km from Castro dei Volsci in the Regional Natural Park of the Ausoni Mountains: an underground network of chambers and galleries carved by the Fosso Mastro torrent, with stalagmites, crystalline lakes, and the spectacular Blue Lake Hall.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

map_keywords: Porta della Valle Castro dei Volsci
Chiesa San Nicola Castro dei Volsci
Monumento Mamma Ciociara Castro dei Volsci

visits.text: Catania, rebuilt in black Etna lava after the 1693 earthquake, is Sicily's second city. Its Baroque center in white limestone contrasts with black basalt paving—a chromatic pairing unique in Europe. Vaccarini's Piazza del Duomo ranks as Sicily's finest Baroque square.
The Fountain of the Elephant, the city's symbol since 1736, depicts an 8th-century BC basalt elephant bearing a 2nd-century Egyptian obelisk. Legend claims it protects the city from Etna's eruptions.

La Pescheria market, behind the cathedral, is Sicily's most animated fish market. From dawn, whole tuna, swordfish, sea urchins, clams, and octopus sprawl across ice-laden stalls. The vocal and olfactory assault makes it one of the Mediterranean's most intense market experiences.

The 2nd-century AD Roman theatre, partially buried within the Baroque houses of the Cruyllas quarter, presents a unique case of an ancient theatre absorbed by the modern city. Its adjacent odeon served for rehearsals and lectures.

activities.text: Lunch at La Pescheria with spaghetti alle vongole or fried calamari, followed by granita di limone e brioche—a typical Sicilian breakfast also enjoyed as dessert in Catania.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

places.name: Catanzaro Lido

visits.text: Castello Le Castella—a 16th-century Aragonese fortress built on an islet linked to the mainland by a narrow causeway in the Capo Rizzuto marine protected area. The current structure was enlarged by the Aragonese on a 14th-century Angevin tower itself built on Greek foundations. The surrounding sea is part of the Área Marina Protetta Capo Rizzuto, Italy's largest marine protected area at 14,700 hectares.

Capo Colonna—an Ionian promontory bearing the remains of the Temple of Hera Lacinia, a Panhellenic sanctuary founded in the 6th century BCE and frequented by all the Greek colonies of Magna Graecia. A single 8.16-meter Doric column still stands from the original 48—the others were dismantled in the 16th century to build Crotone's cathedral. Hannibal stopped here in 203 BCE during his withdrawal from Italy.

activities.text: Diving or snorkeling in the Área Marina Protetta Capo Rizzuto—the protected zone's seabed preserves Greek and Roman amphorae in situ, with visibility reaching 30 meters in summer.
Swimming on the beaches of Catanzaro Lido at day's end after the visits.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

map_keywords: Castello Le Castella Isola di Capo Rizzuto
Tempio di Hera Lacinia Capo Colonna

places.name: Cella Monte

visits.text: Cella Monte is mentioned as early as 1116 in a diploma of Emperor Henry V. Its name derives either from ancient wine cellars dug into the sandstone (cella in Latin), or from cells of small medieval monasteries that existed on these hills. Cella Monte's geological peculiarity is unique: 17 million years ago, the Monferrato was a sea, and house walls are literally built with marine sandstone containing fossils and shells still visible to the naked eye. This stone, called Pietra da Cantoni, has been the exclusive building material for all local construction since Antiquity.

The infernot are hypogean cellars carved entirely into this Pietra da Cantoni, without light or ventilation, by Monferrato peasants during winters since the late eighteenth century - the era when wine began to be bottled thanks to falling glass costs. These underground storage spaces maintain constant temperature and humidity ideal for conservation. Cella Monte sits in the central zone of the UNESCO Monferrato degli Infernot site, a component of the Piedmont Vineyard Landscapes inscribed in 2014. The Ecomuseum of Pietra da Cantoni, installed in Palazzo Volta (an extraordinary fifteenth-century loggia rediscovered during restoration), opens onto the history of this unique architecture.

The parish church of Santi Quirico e Giulitta, consecrated in 1633, preserves wall frescoes, canvases, and a precious polychrome marble altar. The Casaforte, a fourteenth-century fortified dwelling, and Palazzo Radicati - home of Pietro Secondo Radicati (1671-1729), bishop of Casale Monferrato - testify to the village's seigneurial past. In May, the Le Colline sono in Fiore event transforms facades into a flower competition.

activities.text: Guided visit of an infernot with a local producer, combined with tasting of Barbera d'Asti, Grignolino, and Freisa - the three main Monferrato wines. The first weekend of November, the White Truffle Fair of Valle Ghenza brings together producers and gourmets.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

map_keywords: Chiesa Santi Quirico e Giulitta Cella Monte
Ecomuseo della Pietra da Cantoni Cella Monte

visits.text: Necropoli della Banditaccia - Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2004 (jointly with Tarquinia), this "city of the dead" extends over more than 20 hectares and constitutes the largest organized necropolis of the ancient Mediterranean. Unlike Tarquinia (frescoes), Banditaccia is distinguished by its urbanism: tombs are arranged along streets and squares, forming veritable funerary quarters. The oldest (9th century BC) are simple shafts; the monumental circular tumuli (7th-6th century) reach up to 30 meters in diameter and contain several burial chambers reproducing the interior of Etruscan houses with beds, pillars, doors, and windows carved into tufa. The Tomb of the Reliefs (4th century BC, Matuna family) preserves painted stuccos representing tools, weapons, domestic animals, and kitchen utensils - a 3D inventory of Etruscan daily life.

Museo Nazionale Cerite - Installed in Palazzo Ruspoli in the heart of Cerveteri, this small museum displays finds from excavations at Banditaccia and other necropolises of ancient Caere (one of the twelve members of the Etruscan Dodecapolis): painted ceramics, bucchero nero (typically Etruscan polished black pottery), gold and filigree jewelry, terracotta ex-votos. The most spectacular pieces - the Sarcophagi of the Spouses (6th century BC) - are kept at the Museo di Villa Giulia in Rome and the Louvre in Paris.

Historic center of Cerveteri - The medieval town, built on the ancient acropolis of Caere, dominates the coastal plain. Palazzo Ruspoli (16th century), the Rocca, and the sloping alleys preserve the atmosphere of a rural Lazio village, far from the bustle of Rome located just 45 km away.

activities.text: Walk through the city of the dead - Allow at least 2 hours to walk the necropolis alleys and descend into the main open tombs. The route is outdoors, on grassy terrain and tufa stairs. Some tumuli require ducking to enter. Flashlight useful for the deepest chambers.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

visits.text: Before heading to Cesenatico, make a stop in Cervia, 10 km north along the road. This small seaside town hides an unexpected treasure: its historic salt pans, worked without interruption since Etruscan times. Cervia's salt pans produce a renowned 'sweet' salt—less bitter than ordinary sea salt—thanks to a traditional hand-harvesting method. The visitor centre explains the process and shows the evaporation basins where salt crystallises between June and September. Operators offer boat or bike excursions through the salt pans, which are also a protected wetland—you'll see flamingos, avocets and black-winged stilts.

In Cervia's centre, the Torre San Michele, a 17th-century defensive tower, dominates Piazza Garibaldi. Right next door, the MUSA—Museo del Sale traces Cervia's thousand-year history of salt production, from the Etruscans to current techniques. It's a small but well-done museum that explains why salt was for centuries the white gold of this coast and the object of wars between Comacchio, Cervia and Venice.

Arriving in Cesenatico, head for the canal-port, the city's heart. In 1502, Leonardo da Vinci, then in the service of Cesare Borgia, drew from the top of the fortress a perspective of the city and the construction plan for two jetties to combat silting—a recurring problem for Adriatic ports. Leonardo's drawing, dated 6 September 1502, is preserved in the National Library of Paris. Stroll along the quays: this is Cesenatico's real 'piazza', lined with colourful fishing boats, restaurants and bars.

The Museo della Marineria is Cesenatico's flagship attraction. This maritime museum, unique in Italy, unfolds in two parts: an open-air section on the canal, where about ten traditional Adriatic fishing boats are moored with their coloured sails—spectacular when hoisted—and an indoor section tracing the Romagna coast's maritime history. Allow a good hour for both.

Push on to the Piazza delle Conserve, a little architectural gem: these circular pits dug into the ground once served to preserve fish in ice, before refrigeration was invented. The recently restored square often hosts exhibitions and cultural events. Right next door, the Piazza Pisacane houses the monument to Garibaldi, erected in 1884—on 2 August 1849, Cesenatico supplied the fleeing national hero with provisions and 13 ships to reach Venice by sea.

activities.text: Cesenatico is an active fishing port with 90 vessels. In season, operators offer sea trips of 2 to 5 hours: you board a traditional fishing boat, witness fishing with nets between two boats and taste freshly fried sardines or anchovies on deck, accompanied by local wine. An authentic and convivial experience.

Late in the day, enjoy Cesenatico's beach for a swim in the Adriatic. The Romagna coast is renowned for its fine sandy beaches and shallow waters, ideal for families. In the evening, return to the canal-port quays: as the sun sets, the coloured sails of the maritime museum boats reflect in the water—it's Cesenatico's most photogenic moment.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini

places.name: Cetona

visits.text: Rocca di Cetona: The first mentions of the castle date to the 10th century. In the Middle Ages, Cetona first belonged to the Republic of Orvieto then to that of Siena, which fortified it in the 15th century. The fortress, surrounded by centuries-old holm oaks and now private property, overlooks Piazza Garibaldi—a large oval square laid out in the 16th century, surrounded by the Terrosi, Vitelli palaces and two churches, Santissima Annunziata and San Michele Arcangelo.

Civic Museum of Prehistory of Monte Cetona: Housed in the Palazzo Comunale, this museum documents human occupation of the territory from the Paleolithic to the Bronze Age, from discoveries made in caves and archaeological sites of Monte Cetona during the 20th century. The adjacent natural archaeological park, called Belvédère, includes travertine cavities that served as places of worship and burial.

Collegiate Church of the Santissima Trinità: A 12th-13th century Gothic church that houses a fresco by Pinturicchio, one of the painters of the Sistine Chapel. The San Francesco convent (2 km from the village), founded according to tradition by Saint Francis of Assisi in 1212, marks the border between Tuscany and Umbria.

activities.text: Hike on Monte Cetona (1,148 m): The volcanic mountain dominating the village offers views west toward Val d'Orcia and Monte Amiata, and east toward Valdichiana and Umbria. The first section is drivable, then an hour's walk to the summit.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Museo Civico per la Preistoria del Monte Cetona Cetona

visits.text: Graines Castle at Brusson (4,511 feet) ranks among the Aosta Valley's oldest medieval fortresses, built in the 11th century on a rocky spur at the mouth of Freudière Valley. Classified as a "primitive" castle by Valdostan architectural historians, it consists of a high crenellated tower on a quadrangular plan, a chapel, and walls following the cliff's contours. Its position commanded visual control of the Ayas valley floor and access to the upper Gressoney valley. Around 1200, the Graines family's lands passed to the powerful House of Challant, viscounts of Aosta.

The Ayas valley was colonized by the Walser from the 13th century onward: these mountain people of Germanic origin from the Upper Valais crossed the Alpine passes and settled in the upper valley, leaving their mark in the villages of Mascognaz and Saint-Jacques-des-Allemands (Canton of the Germans). Their rascard—larch granaries on stilts with stone-slab roofs—are still visible at Antagnod and Champoluc. By the 19th century, the Walser language had vanished from this valley, supplanted by Valdostan Franco-Provençal patois.

Verrès Castle (14th century), at the valley entrance, rises from a 260-foot rock dominating the confluence of the Evançon torrent and the Dora Baltea. A rectangular fortress with four corner towers topped by cupolas, built around 1390 by Ibleto de Challant, it now houses a museum devoted to the Aosta Valley's medieval history.

activities.text: Ride the cable car from Champoluc (5,144 feet) to Crest station (6,496 feet), then hike the alpine pastures of the Monterosa domain: panoramas of Monte Rosa glaciers (15,203 feet). In summer, the Tour of Monte Rosa trails depart from this station.
Tour the Chamousira gold mine at Brusson (by reservation): since 1899, over a mile of galleries have been excavated, some accessible with a guide. The mine was still active in the 1970s.

days_plan.itin_title: Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc

visits.text: Ussel Castle, built by Ébal II de Challant in 1343 (dendrochronology-confirmed date) on a serpentinite promontory above Châtillon, marks the Aosta Valley's first monobloc castle—an architectural rupture between castles articulated in multiple volumes (like Fénis) and austere single-mass fortresses (like Verrès). Rectangular in form with a summit band of small blind arches, paired windows with floral decoration, and two cylindrical towers to the south, it passed through Challant hands until François de Challant's death in 1470, then served as prison and barracks before abandonment. In 1983, Baron Marcel Bich—inventor of the Bic pen and Châtillon native—purchased and donated it to the Region, which restored it as an exhibition space. From the terraces: sweeping views across the main valley to the Valtournenche entrance.

Saint-Vincent, just over a mile from Châtillon, has been a renowned spa town since the 18th century. Its waters feed the thermal station, and its casino—Casino de la Vallée—funded the Valdostan regional budget for decades. The village preserves the fresco dedicated to Saint Maurice on the market square, with the motto "in adversity, good heart," added to the coat of arms in 1565 by Duke Emmanuel Philibert.

Fénis Castle, six miles west of Châtillon, is the Aosta Valley's most photographed castle. Built by Aimone de Challant in the 14th century, it features double concentric walls, semi-circular towers, and an inner courtyard decorated with frescoes depicting Saint George slaying the dragon and a court scene with philosophers holding phylacteries—the work of Giacomo Jaquerio, circa 1415-1420.

activities.text: Wine tasting along the Aosta Valley Wine Route: terraced vineyards at Chambave, Nus, and Montjovet produce local DOC wines like Chambave Muscat, Nus Malvoisie, and Torrette made from Petit Rouge. These "heroic" vineyards clinging to Alpine slopes between 1,300 and 2,950 feet have been cultivated by hand for centuries.
Hike the Cammino Balteo trail from Châtillon: discovery route through the Aosta Valley's natural and cultural heritage at mid and low elevations, accessible year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc

visits.text: Chianale - La Cianal in Occitan - clings to the mountainside at 1,800 meters in the upper Varaita valley, a stone's throw from the Colle dell'Agnello pass leading into France. The Varaita torrent splits the village in two halves connected by a stone bridge that forms the village's true heart. The slate houses with lauze stone roofs reflect an unbroken Occitan building tradition dating back to the tenth century, when Saracen raiders ravaged the region. In 1343, Chianale belonged to the five Free Escartons Republics - a Franco-Italian alpine confederation enjoying considerable autonomy granted by the last Dauphin of Viennois, Humbert II. The 1713 Treaty of Utrecht definitively attached the valley to Savoy.

The church of Sant'Antonio, erected in the fourteenth century, features a double-bay bell gable and a triple-crowned Romanesque portal. Inside, an eighteenth-century baroque altar of carved Swiss pine illustrates the richness of local craftsmanship. The Museum of Costume and Textile Crafts, opened in 2009 in the former eighteenth-century Capuchin Mission, exhibits the clothing and traditions of this alpine community.

Two kilometers from the village, the Alevè forest extends across 825 hectares between 1,500 and 2,500 meters elevation. It's the largest Swiss pine forest in the Alps and one of Europe's largest - trees several centuries old that Virgil already mentioned in Antiquity. Start from the hamlets of Castello or Villaretto for the 5-kilometer trail along Castello Lake, created by a dam built in the twentieth century.

activities.text: In summer, hike to Colle dell'Agnello (2,744m) with plunging views over the French Queyras and Monviso (3,841m), nicknamed the "Stone King." In winter, cross-country skiing and ski mountaineering from Chianale (15km of trails). Every January 17th, the blessing of the animals marks Saint Anthony's Day; in February, carnival perpetuates the wolf-return ritual with costumed men and nocturnal village celebrations.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: The church of San Donato has been documented since May 8, 1277. Pope Boniface VIII himself sent it a papal bull in 1301 ordering it to claim the property of Morrona Abbey. The current building dates from complete reconstruction in 1812, which incorporated a castle tower into the apse and moved the early 17th-century baroque high altar, in precious polychrome marble, from the demolished church of San Pietro a Massa. The apse is covered with frescoes by Antonio Gaioni.

The Cappella della Santissima Annunziata is an underground chapel whose construction probably dates to parish expansion work in the early 18th century. It is entirely frescoed by Giovanni Battista Tempesti of Volterra, a cycle covering the large barrel vault and side walls—one of Tempesti's rare works outside major cities.

A few kilometers away, the medieval hamlet of Rivalto (320 m altitude) was built on the ruins of an ancient fortress. This is where Blessed Giordano da Rivalto was born in 1260, a Dominican preacher who, in a sermon delivered in Florence in 1305, mentioned eyeglasses as a recent invention whose inventor he had met—one of the first documented historical mentions of this object. The 18th-century Sanctuary of the Madonna del Carmine, nestled in the woods, offers the wildest viewpoint in the area.

activities.text: Chianni is one of the last municipalities on the Pisan Hills Wine Route. The local extra virgin olive oil, with its intense color and very low acidity, has been produced for centuries; local mills open during the autumn harvest. In November, the Sagra del Cinghiale (wild boar festival) transforms the village into a gastronomic fair, one of Tuscany's oldest.

Hike toward Monte Vaso from Chianni: marked trails through oak and olive woods, with views stretching from Pisa to Volterra. Rivalto's chestnut groves produce superior quality marroni already mentioned in 1428 cadastral records.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

visits.text: Discover Chioggia, like an unspoiled Venice with a slow, gentle pace of life on the southern tip of the Venetian Lagoon. Older than Venice itself, this fishing town is crisscrossed by canals and bridges, retaining the authentic character that the more famous city has lost to tourism.

Visit the Clock Tower Museum housing the oldest working clock in the world, a remarkable medieval timepiece that has measured the hours for over seven centuries. The tower offers views over the town's atmospheric waterways and fishing boats.

Explore Sottomarina, Chioggia's lively beachside suburb featuring a remarkable 10-kilometer-long sandy beach up to 300 meters wide. The sand, rich in minerals including augite and quartz, is renowned for therapeutic sand bathing treatments.

activities.text: Take a boat trip around Chioggia to experience the lagoon landscapes and traditional fishing culture. Watch the daily rhythms of one of Italy's oldest and largest fishing ports, where the wholesale and retail fish markets overflow with the morning's fresh catch.

If visiting in June, experience the Palio della Marciliana, a historic reenactment when hundreds of people in 14th-century costumes transform the town with noble ladies, knights, musicians, games, and parades.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

places.name: Cilento Coast

visits.text: Exploration of the Cilento coastline, UNESCO World Heritage site, between limestone cliffs and isolated beaches.
Observation of picturesque coastal villages and lush Mediterranean vegetation.

activities.text: Swimming and seaside walk in a preserved environment.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters

map_keywords: Baia degli Infreschi Marina di Camerota

visits.text: Balcone delle Marche and Palazzo Castiglioni — Cingoli perches at 631 metres atop Monte Circe, and its nickname of 'Balcony of Le Marche' is immediately justified when you step onto the medieval rampart terrace: on clear days the panorama spans the Adriatic, Monte Conero and the Apennines. A bronze statue of Tito Labieno (by sculptor Nazzareno Rocchetti), Caesar's lieutenant who founded the city at his own expense in the 1st century BC, keeps watch over the view. Nearby, the Palazzo Castiglioni is the birthplace of Francesco Saverio Castiglioni, who became Pope Pius VIII (1761–1830). Built in the late 17th century by merging two noble residences previously separated by the demolished Chiesa di San Giuliano, it retains 19th-century murals and a library that once held 17,500 volumes.

Palazzo Comunale — Sala degli Stemmi and Lorenzo Lotto's Madonna del Rosario — the Palazzo Comunale dates to the 13th century in its oldest section; its façade was redone in 1531 on the orders of governor Egidio Canisio da Viterbo. Inside, the Sala degli Stemmi has a vaulted ceiling decorated with the coats of arms of Cingoli's noble families, with medallions in the corners evoking the city's Roman origins. On display here (moved from the earthquake-closed Chiesa di San Domenico) is Lorenzo Lotto's altarpiece Madonna del Rosario (mid-16th century): a work remarkable for the posture of the Christ Child, the 16 narrative medallions across the top, and the presence of a perfect scale model of the village of Cingoli painted within the composition itself. The Museo Archeologico Statale on the upper floor and in the basement presents prehistoric and Roman finds from the Piano di Fonte Marcosa excavations.

Collegiata di Sant'Esuperanzio — outside the walls, one of the most important religious buildings in Cingoli and a fine example of Marchigian Gothic architecture. Its origins go back to around the year 1000; the current structure is already documented in 1139. The 1295 portal is decorated with bas-reliefs of the saint. Inside, a single nave formed by six pointed arches on rectangular pilasters; in the crypt, the relics of Sant'Esuperanzio, rediscovered during works in 1495. The nearby Chiesa di San Filippo Neri in the village offers a good example of Marchigian baroque: a single nave with lateral absidioles, stucco decoration and vault frescoes by Pier Simone Fanelli and Paolo Marini.

activities.text: Walk the medieval ramparts and watch the sunset over Lago di Castreccioni — the well-preserved walls allow a circular walk around the perimeter with hillside views. Late afternoon, the lake (a few kilometres away, the largest swimmable artificial lake in central Italy) is ideal for an early dinner at a local trattoria, with grilled crescia marchigiana stuffed with local ingredients.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

places.name: Citta Sant Angelo

visits.text: Città Sant'Angelo - a village of 15,000 inhabitants on a ridge at 317 meters, with simultaneous views of the Adriatic Sea and the Gran Sasso peaks. The Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo, rebuilt in the 14th century, features a 40-meter bell tower and a columned portico that forms the main entrance to the fortified town.

activities.text: Stop at Piazza Municipio for views of Gran Sasso and the coast before continuing on.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Città Sant'Angelo

visits.text: Experience Cittadella, home to Europe's best medieval parapet walkway. Here you can circumnavigate the entire town by walking atop its medieval defensive walls - a unique opportunity to understand medieval fortification from the defender's perspective.

Explore the historic center enclosed within the complete circuit of walls, where medieval character has been remarkably preserved. The churches, palazzos, and piazzas within create an authentic atmosphere.

activities.text: In September, don't miss the town-wide medieval fair when historical costumes, crafts, and entertainment transform Cittadella into a living museum of medieval life. In winter, a charming ice rink appears in the central square.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

places.name: Civita Di Bagnoregio

visits.text: Civita di Bagnoregio - Founded by Etruscans over 2,500 years ago, this "Città che Muore" ("dying city," a nickname given by writer Bonaventura Tecchi) counts only about ten permanent residents. The village sits atop a tufa and clay spur that erodes a little more each year. Of the five original medieval gates, only Porta Santa Maria survives. Inside, the Etruscan urban grid (cardo and decumanus) remains visible beneath the medieval overlay.

Civita footbridge - 300 meters long, 70 meters of elevation gain, 42 reinforced concrete pillars: this pedestrian bridge, built in 1965 after Germans destroyed the previous one in 1944, is the only access to the village. The steep climb transforms the approach into a kind of pilgrimage. Approximately 700,000 visitors cross it annually.

Church of San Donato - On the borgo's main square, this church houses a wooden crucifix venerated since the 15th century. According to tradition, it spoke to a pious woman in 1499 to end a plague epidemic. Every Good Friday, the crucifix is carried in procession across the bridge to Bagnoregio.

Cave of San Bonaventura and Bucaione - Beneath Civita's cliff face, an Etruscan chamber tomb bears the name of Saint Bonaventure: tradition holds that here Saint Francis healed the young Giovanni Fidanza, future Doctor of the Church. The "Bucaione," a deep Etruscan tunnel, pierces the rocky base and opens directly onto the Valley of the Calanchi.

activities.text: Sunset over the Valley of the Calanchi - From the Bagnoregio belvedere (before the bridge) or from Civita's Porta Sud, white clay ravines carve a lunar landscape between the Torbido and Chiaro watercourses. Constant erosion transforms this panorama season by season. Plan to be there around 6 PM in summer for raking light.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio
Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

map_keywords: Chiesa San Donato Civita di Bagnoregio

visits.text: Civitella del Tronto - a village of 5,000 inhabitants perched on a travertine spur at 589 meters in the Val Vibrata hills. The medieval center stretches along a narrow ridge with alleyways 1-2 meters wide, lined with medieval houses with white limestone façades.

Fortezza Borbonica - one of Europe's largest military fortresses, 500 meters long and 50 meters wide, covering 25,000 square meters. Built between the 14th and 17th centuries, it resisted all assaults until 1861, capitulating two weeks after the proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy—the last stronghold of the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies to surrender.

activities.text: Excursion to the Gole del Salinello - 5-kilometer limestone gorges carved by the Salinello torrent, accessible from the village of Ripe (6 km away). Marked trail along waterfalls and vertical walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Pozze di Curnila - In the middle Arzino torrent valley, between the villages of San Francesco, Pielungo, and Pert, the water has carved natural pools in the rock in shades of emerald and turquoise. The Financial Times ranked these pozze among Europe's most beautiful wild swimming spots. The multicolored pebbles of the riverbed—pink, green, gray—have served for centuries as raw material for Spilimbergo's mosaicists.

Cascate dell'Arzino - The Arzino torrent rises near Sella Chiampon, fed by two springs: the Roggia del Nanul and the Fontanone. Shortly after its source, a succession of tiered waterfalls tumbles down a gorge (forra) carved into the Dolomia Principale, surrounded by a pre-Alpine beech forest. There's no single great waterfall, but a series of ledges that divide, rejoin, and slide over polished rock.

activities.text: Swimming in the natural pools of the Arzino torrent - The waters are crystal-clear but cold (even in summer). The Curnila pozze are the most accessible and popular. Visit on weekdays to enjoy them in peace.

Pozzis - Cascate dell'Arzino loop hike - From the village of Pozzis (614 m), a trail descends toward the torrent, crosses the stream on large boulders, and follows the gorge through beeches, black hornbeams, and hazels. 7.7 km loop with 155 m elevation gain.

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour
