Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

places.name: Cocconato

visits.text: Cocconato perches at 491 meters above the lower Astigiano, commanding valleys that extend to the Alps on clear days. Its exceptionally mild microclimate, due to protection from surrounding hills, allows palms, olive trees, and mimosas to flourish - hence the nineteenth-century nickname Riviera del Monferrato. The name likely derives from the Latin cum conatu, the effort required to climb to the summit. The counts Radicati family ruled Cocconato from the tenth to fifteenth centuries, granting it de facto quasi-autonomy.

The Torre dei Radicati, last remnant of the medieval castle rebuilt in the nineteenth century, served under Napoleon as an optical telegraph relay, then as a windmill - two successive functions engraved in the crenellated stone that make it the village symbol. The fifteenth-century Gothic Palazzo Comunale, with its elegant terracotta windows and ogival arcades, is one of the rare examples of civil Gothic style in Piedmont. A stone's throw away, the Pieve della Madonna della Neve (twelfth century), surrounded by vineyards, offers a striking panorama over the countryside.

Eight kilometers away, the abbey of Santa Maria di Vezzolano in Albugnano is a Romano-Gothic masterpiece whose legendary foundation is attributed to Charlemagne. The interior presents a carved fourteenth-century rood screen and a fresco cycle of exceptional artistic value - one of medieval Piedmont's most important monuments.

activities.text: Every last Sunday in September, the Palio degli Asini (donkey race) celebrates a tradition dating to the Middle Ages: legend has it that donkeys carried water to extinguish a fire at the castle. In June, the Cocco7 wine and food walk offers 15km through vineyards with seven tastings. Cocconato's Robiola - soft cow's milk cheese - and local prosciutto benefit from the village's particular microclimate.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

map_keywords: Abbazia Santa Maria di Vezzolano Albugnano

visits.text: Gran Paradiso National Park, inaugurated December 3, 1922, is Italy's first national park. Its history is inseparable from the Alpine ibex: in 1856, King Victor Emmanuel II declared these mountains a Royal Hunting Reserve, saving the species from extinction—only a few hundred remained. In 1919, his son Victor Emmanuel III offered the 5,189 acres to the State on condition that a national park be created. In 1945, after World War II reduced the ibex population to 416 individuals, commissioner Renzo Videsott obtained autonomous park management in 1947, enabling gradual recovery. The park today shelters nearly 3,000 ibex—Europe's largest population—along with 8,000 chamois, marmots, lynx, and wolves.

The Cogne valley is the main Valdostan access to the park. At Valnontey, the Paradisia alpine botanical garden (mid-June to mid-September) assembles Alpine flora species, medicinal plants, and lichens. Its name comes from the white lily Paradisea liliastrum, endemic to the park. Cogne's historic center perpetuates the tombolo tradition—bobbin lace—transmitted for centuries.

The Lillaz waterfalls, a 10-minute walk from the eponymous hamlet, drop 492 feet in three successive rocky leaps on the Urtier torrent. Rifugio Vittorio Sella (8,478 feet), originally a royal hunting lodge built in 1861 by order of the king, was converted to a mountain refuge in 1922 at the park's opening.

activities.text: Hike from Valnontey to Rifugio Vittorio Sella (8,478 feet), the park's heart: virtually guaranteed encounters with ibex (males with long curved horns averaging 36 inches) and chamois. Return by the same route.
Short hike to Lillaz waterfalls and Valnontey pastures: wildlife observation in late afternoon, flowering alpine meadows, views of the Grivola (13,022 feet) and its glacier.

days_plan.itin_title: Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc

places.name: Colle Val Elsa

visits.text: Colle Alta and Arnolfo di Cambio's tower-house: The upper town, accessible through the Porta Volterrana (a medieval gate with drawbridge), is one of the best-preserved historic centers in Val d'Elsa. Here stands the 13th-century tower-house where Arnolfo di Cambio, the sculptor and architect behind Florence's Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore and Palazzo Vecchio, was supposedly born in 1245. Inside, frescoes depict medieval battles linked to the town.

Crystal Museum: Housed in the former kilns of the Schmidt glassworks, the first to fire up a furnace in 1820, this museum traces the history of local crystal production up to the introduction of lead crystal manufacturing in 1963. Colle Val d'Elsa today produces nearly all Italian crystal and, according to figures from local players, a significant share of worldwide production—earning it the nickname 'Bohemia of Italy' since the 19th century.

Duomo (Co-Cathedral of Saints Alberto and Marziale) and Palazzo Campana: The neoclassical cathedral in brick and sandstone rises on the remains of an old medieval church. The early 16th-century Palazzo Campana, an example of Tuscan Mannerism, marks the transition between the Borgo di Santa Caterina and the Castello.

Ranuccio Bianchi Bandinelli Archaeological Museum: Housed in the Palazzo Pretorio, this museum displays objects found in the Val d'Elsa territory, from Etruscan bronze mirrors to large Hellenistic vases. The Via delle Volte, in the upper town, is a hundred-meter covered passage with medieval atmospheres, lined with former artisan workshops.

activities.text: Visit to a crystal workshop: The factories in the lower town host groups to observe the different phases of hot work (8:30-11:00 AM) and cold work—engraving, sculpture, grinding. Direct purchases at the workshop avoid intermediaries.

Funicular between Colle Bassa and Colle Alta: Installed in 2006, this free ski lift overcomes a 40-meter elevation gain in a few minutes and operates 24/7. San Gimignano, with its 14 UNESCO-listed medieval towers, is 16 km to the north—a natural excursion from this base.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Colle Alta Colle di Val d'Elsa
Casa-Torre di Arnolfo di Cambio Colle di Val d'Elsa
Museo del Cristallo Colle di Val d'Elsa
Duomo dei Santi Alberto e Marziale Colle di Val d'Elsa
Palazzo Campana Colle di Val d'Elsa
Museo Archeologico Ranuccio Bianchi Bandinelli Colle di Val d'Elsa

visits.text: The Charterhouse of Trisulti, at 825 meters altitude in the forest of the Ernici Mountains, 6 km northeast of Collepardo, was first founded in 996 by Saint Dominic of Sora as a Benedictine abbey. The current construction dates to 1204: Pope Innocent III ordered its reconstruction and entrusted it to the Carthusians. The Church of San Bartolomeo was consecrated in 1211. A national monument since 1873, the monastery passed to the Cistercians of Casamari in 1947, due to insufficient Carthusian vocations.

The monastic pharmacy (spezieria) is the jewel of the site: furnished in the 18th century, it preserves its original cabinetry, labeled jars, alembics, and frescoes painted by Filippo Balbi in the 19th century. The adjoining herb garden supplied the medicinal plants used by the monks. Today it's a living museum that reveals the entire process of monastic remedy-making.

The monastery library preserves several medieval manuscripts and rare books. It's accessible during guided tours and illustrates the central role played by the Carthusians in copying and transmitting texts during the Middle Ages.

The Caves of Collepardo (Grotte dei Bambocci) are a karst network carved into the limestone of the Ernici about 1 km from the village. The main cavity measures 70 meters deep and 100 meters in diameter; stalactite formations have taken on shapes evoking human silhouettes, hence the popular nickname "bambocci" (dolls).

activities.text: Hike through the Ernici state forest around Trisulti: marked trails traverse ancient beech and chestnut groves between 700 and 1000 meters altitude. Flora is particularly dense in spring (anemones, wild orchids), and views over the Ernici ridges open at clearings.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: The Historic Garden of Villa Garzoni is one of Europe's best-preserved Italian baroque gardens, classified as a National Monument in 1909 and 1912, and integrated in 2020 into the European Cultural Route of Historic Gardens. The Garzoni family, originally from Pescia, bought the castle in 1333; major garden works began in 1652 under Romano Garzoni. The steep hillside was transformed into a theatrical green amphitheater: terraces, monumental staircases, artificial grottoes, white mythological statues, water jets, a boxwood theater and labyrinth succeed each other in rigorous baroque staging. Napoleon Bonaparte, King Victor Emmanuel III and the American ambassador number among the villa's illustrious visitors. This is where Angiolina Orzali, Carlo Lorenzini's mother, worked and met her future husband.

The Pinocchio Monumental Park was inaugurated in 1953 for the 70th anniversary of the book's publication, thanks to fundraising among Italian schoolchildren initiated by Pescia mayor Rolando Anzillotti. The bronze sculptures of characters from the tale are signed by major artists including Emilio Greco and Pietro Consagra; architectural installations were entrusted to Marco Zanuso in the 1960s-1970s. The world's tallest wooden Pinocchio, 16 meters high, was inaugurated in 2009 by the Fondazione Collodi.

The Pieve di San Bartolomeo, at the highest point of the medieval village of Collodi (244m altitude), is mentioned from the 12th century. The steeply sloping village—stone houses clustered like a cascade from the pieve down to the Villa Garzoni gardens below—stood on the border between the Republic of Lucca and the Grand Duchy of Tuscany, who disputed it for centuries before Collodi became a Lucchese possession in 1442.

activities.text: Casa delle Farfalle (Butterfly House): a tropical greenhouse installed in the Garzoni garden, where hundreds of butterflies from Asia, Africa and South America fly freely in vegetation recreating their natural habitat. Included in the combined Pinocchio Park ticket.

Wander through Collodi's medieval village: lanes too narrow for cars, limestone staircases and stone arches lead through the traffic-free village from the gardens below to the pieve above. Carlo Lorenzini himself described the village as "a long streak of old houses blackened by time climbing up the back of a steep high hill."

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

visits.text: Visit the Reggia di Colorno, often called the 'Little Versailles of Parma' for its grand French-inspired architecture. This magnificent ducal palace was transformed in the 18th century by the Bourbon rulers into an elegant residence featuring sumptuous state rooms, frescoed ceilings, and richly decorated apartments that rival the great royal palaces of Europe.

Explore the extensive Ducal Gardens surrounding the palace, designed in the formal French style with geometric parterres, fountains, and tree-lined avenues. The gardens offer a peaceful retreat where centuries of horticultural artistry create a harmonious landscape perfect for contemplative walks.

activities.text: Discover the palace's role in regional history as a residence for the Farnese and Bourbon dynasties. The town of Colorno itself, easily accessible by public transport from Parma, offers charming streets and the authentic atmosphere of a historic Emilian settlement.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

visits.text: The Flavian Amphitheatre—called the Colosseum since the Middle Ages, probably in reference to Nero's nearby colossal statue—was built on the site of the Domus Aurea's artificial lake. Vespasian launched construction around 70-72 AD with spoils from the sack of Jerusalem in 70; his son Titus inaugurated the arena in 80 with a hundred days of games during which 9,000 animals perished according to Dio Cassius. The structure measures 189 m by 156 m and 48 m high, with a circumference of 527 m. Its travertine façade is organized across four levels with 80 arches framed by half-columns in ascending orders (Doric, Ionic, Corinthian). Access for spectators was through 76 of the 80 numbered entrances—the other four were reserved for dignitaries. The east gate was the 'gate of life' (gladiators' entrance), the west gate the 'gate of death' (corpse exit). Below the arena lies the hypogeum, a network of corridors and trap-door elevators allowing spectacular appearances of wild beasts and sets. Estimated capacity: 50,000 to 70,000 spectators, divided by social rank.

The Arch of Constantine (315 AD), just outside the Colosseum, is Rome's largest triumphal arch (21 m high, 25.7 m wide). It celebrates Constantine's victory over Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge. Architectural quirk: it's largely built from spolia of earlier monuments—friezes from the emperors Trajan, Hadrian, and Marcus Aurelius were reused, the heads recarved in Constantine's likeness.

activities.text: Tour the hypogeum and upper levels of the Colosseum (special tickets required): the underground corridors reveal the lift mechanisms and animal cages, invisible to the ancient public.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

visits.text: Comacchio will surprise you. Nicknamed 'Little Venice', this fishing town is built on 13 islands linked by canals and bridges, at the heart of the Po Delta. Start with the Ponte Trepponti, the city's emblem: this monumental bridge with five staircases and two towers, built in the 17th century, is Comacchio's historic gateway. From there, let yourself drift along the canals lined with colourful houses—fishermen used to paint their façades with leftover boat paint. An hour is enough to tour the centre, but allow two or three to savour the atmosphere.

Push on to the Manifattura dei Marinati, the old eel-pickling factory turned eco-museum. Eel fishing has regulated life in Comacchio for centuries—a Capitulary from 715 already mentions the city's exclusive right to preserve and trade this fish. You'll discover ancestral fishing and pickling techniques, with the great fires where eels are smoked according to a method dating back to the 17th century. Comacchio's pickled eel is recognised by the Slow Food Presidium.

The Museo Delta Antico, housed in the Palazzo Bellini near the Ponte degli Sbirri, is well worth a visit. It contains the treasures of a Roman ship from the Augustan era, discovered in 1981 while digging a drainage canal, with its entire cargo intact: 102 lead ingots, amphorae, ceramics and everyday objects. It's a fascinating dive into the delta's ancient maritime trade.

Walking back along the canal, don't miss the Porticato dei Cappuccini, a covered promenade of 143 arches built in 1647 as an ex-voto. It leads to the church of Santa Maria in Aula Regia. The cathedral of San Cassiano, whose foundations date back to the 8th century but rebuilt in the 17th, and the Torre dell'Orologio complete the centre's architectural panorama.

activities.text: In the afternoon, set off to explore the Valli di Comacchio by boat. These brackish lagoons, the largest in Italy, stretch over about 13,000 hectares between Ferrara and the Adriatic Sea. Operators offer guided excursions that cross the canals, pass traditional fishermen's huts and lead to the 'lavorieri'—the eel trap systems still used today. You'll encounter grey herons, kingfishers, egrets and, with a bit of luck, the graceful silhouettes of the flamingos that have populated the lagoons since the 1990s.

If you prefer cycling, a signposted trail of about 20 km circles the lagoon. The route is flat and crosses breathtaking landscapes—water mirrors, reeds, fishermen's huts and flocks of birds. Halfway, a small café allows for a break and a taste of grilled eel, Comacchio's unmissable speciality.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini

visits.text: Duomo di Milano - Begun in 1386 under Gian Galeazzo Visconti, this Gothic cathedral required five and a half centuries to complete (finished in 1965). At 157 meters long and 45 meters high under the central nave, it can hold 40,000 people. Over 3,400 statues adorn the interior and exterior, 135 spires pierce the sky, and 96 gargoyles drain rainwater. At the summit, the golden Madonnina (1774) watches over Milan from 108.5 meters. Beneath the cathedral, a 4th-century paleochristian baptistery preserves the font where Saint Ambrose is said to have baptized Saint Augustine in 387. Climb to the rooftop terraces: among the pinnacles of Candoglia marble, on clear days the view reaches all the way to the Alps.

Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II - Inaugurated in 1877, this shopping arcade designed by Giuseppe Mengoni links Piazza del Duomo to Teatro alla Scala beneath a glass and iron vault 47 meters high. The mosaic floor depicts the coats of arms of Milan, Turin, Florence, and Rome. Local tradition: spinning three times on your heel on the Turin bull's attributes supposedly brings good luck.

Quadrilatero della Moda - The four streets forming the heart of Milanese fashion (Via Montenapoleone, Via della Spiga, Via Manzoni, Corso Venezia) occupy the quarter of 18th-century aristocratic palaces. Even without shopping, the neoclassical façades and interior courtyards merit a stroll.

Duomo di Como - The last Gothic cathedral built in Lombardy, begun in 1396. Construction lasted nearly four centuries. The façade blends flamboyant Gothic with Renaissance elements. Inside, 16th-century Flemish tapestries and paintings by Bernardino Luini and Gaudenzio Ferrari.

Broletto and communal tower - Right beside the Duomo, this 13th-century communal palace in polychrome marble bands (white, gray, and pink) served as the seat of city government in the Middle Ages. The adjacent tower punctuates Como's urban landscape.

Tempio Voltiano - This neoclassical temple erected in 1927 on the lake shore celebrates the centenary of Alessandro Volta's death. Born in Como in 1745, the inventor of the electric battery is honored here through period scientific instruments and models of his experiments.

Bellagio, the 'Pearl of the Lario' - Take the ferry from Como (about 40 minutes by hydrofoil). Bellagio occupies the central promontory where Lake Como divides into its two branches. Climb the Salita Serbelloni, a stepped shopping street lined with silk boutiques—Como has historically been Italy's silk capital.

Villa Melzi and its gardens - Built between 1808 and 1810 by architect Giocondo Albertolli for Francesco Melzi d'Eril, vice president of the Italian Republic under Napoleon. The English-style park descends to the lake among Japanese cedars, Chilean palms, giant rhododendrons, and a Japanese pond. Franz Liszt stayed here in 1837 with the Countess d'Agoult; Stendhal found inspiration for his writings. The neoclassical chapel houses the duke's tomb. Don't miss Comolli's 1810 sculpture of Dante and Beatrice, commissioned by Melzi himself.
Basilica San Giacomo - Fine example of Lombard Romanesque style on Bellagio's main square. Its mid-19th-century organ, restored in 2013, resonates during summer concerts.

Passeggiata degli Innamorati in Varenna - This cantilevered walkway, anchored into the rock above the lake, connects the landing pier to the village center over a few hundred meters. Its red railing—the color of love—and metal heart have become Varenna's symbol. Arrive by ferry from Bellagio (15 minutes) for an approach from the water.

Villa Monastero - A former 13th-century Cistercian convent transformed into a noble residence in the 17th century, then into a holiday villa at the turn of the 19th and 20th centuries. Its 2 km lakeside botanical garden shelters tropical and Mediterranean species that thrive in the microclimate. The Casa Museo preserves 14 furnished rooms with period furniture.

Villa Cipressi - Adjacent to Villa Monastero, this villa has become a hotel, but its terraced gardens descending toward the lake remain open to the public. Centuries-old cypresses, sculpted hedges, and fountains cascade down multiple levels with lake views.

Castello di Vezio - Perched on the promontory above Varenna, this medieval castle was reputedly founded by Lombard Queen Theodelinda. The climb takes 30 minutes along a shaded mule track. From the tower, the view embraces all three branches of the lake. The garden harbors strange white plaster sculptures—'ghosts'—remnants of art installations.

Piazza San Giorgio and church - Varenna's main square opens onto the 14th-century church in Lombard Gothic style, adorned with medieval frescoes. The small port of Riva Grande with its colorful boats lies just below.

activities.text: Milan → Como drive - About 80 km, allow 1 hour via the motorway. Arrive in late afternoon for an initial walk along Como's lakefront promenade (Lungolago).

Ferry Como → Bellagio → Como - The boat network (Navigazione Laghi) connects all lake villages. The hydrofoil makes Como-Bellagio in about 40 minutes. The classic ferry takes longer but allows you to admire the villas from the water.
Triangle ferry route Como → Varenna → Bellagio → Como - The ferry service connects the three key lake villages. Combine your Varenna day with a return stop in Bellagio for dinner.

places.name: Compiano

visits.text: Visit the Castle of Compiano, a fortress worthy of note for its significant role in the history of the region. Perched high in the Apennine mountains, this medieval stronghold controlled important routes through the mountains, and its well-preserved structure offers insight into military architecture adapted to the challenging mountain terrain.

Explore the tiny village of Compiano, one of the most remote and atmospheric settlements in the Parma Apennines. The isolation has preserved its medieval character, with stone houses clinging to the mountainside beneath the protective walls of the castle above.

activities.text: Enjoy the panoramic mountain views from the castle's elevated position, where the Apennine peaks and forested valleys create a dramatic natural backdrop. The journey to Compiano itself winds through spectacular mountain scenery, making the drive an integral part of the experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Giovanni Battista Compiano

places.name: Conegliano

visits.text: Begin exploring Prosecco Country at Conegliano, the gateway to 170 kilometers of winding roads through charming villages and vine-draped hillsides. The unique microclimate of these UNESCO-listed slopes produces Italy's most celebrated sparkling wine.
Visit the historic center of Conegliano with its castle, medieval streets, and Renaissance architecture. The old town's atmosphere reflects centuries as a prosperous wine-trading center.

activities.text: Sample prosecco at local wineries and cellars, discovering the differences between DOC and DOCG designations. The rolling hills between here and Valdobbiadene offer some of Italy's most beautiful wine country landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Casa di Cima da Conegliano Conegliano

places.name: Corciano

visits.text: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta - Corciano's parish church houses two major works. The first is the Pala di Corciano, an Assumption altarpiece painted in 1513 by Pietro Vannucci known as Perugino, native of Città della Pieve and Raphael's master: it's one of his last important commissions before his death (1523). The second is the Gonfalone della Madonna della Misericordia painted in 1472 by Benedetto Bonfigli, depicting the Virgin protecting Corciano's inhabitants under her cloak—a processional banner commissioned during an epidemic. These two works make this small church in a hamlet of 20,000 inhabitants a site of artistic pilgrimage.

Torrione and medieval walls - The Torrione (1482), keep of the Santa Maria gate, is Corciano's symbol. Built to reinforce the castle's defenses against recurrent assaults of the late 15th century (in 1416, the condottiere Braccio Fortebraccio da Montone took Corciano after a long siege), it now houses a permanent exhibition center on medieval and Renaissance culture. The complete, concentric walls enclose the hamlet in a triple circle of well-preserved fortifications.

Chiesa-Museo di San Francesco - This 13th-century Gothic church, founded according to tradition after Saint Francis's passage through Corciano in 1223, preserves frescoes datable between the late 13th and early 14th centuries. Its floor is covered with 21 engraved funerary slabs bearing the coats of arms of Corciano's noble families. It now hosts temporary exhibitions and concerts.

Centro storico and Piazza Coragino - The hamlet's heart is organized around Piazza Coragino, dominated by a 15th-century circular well with engraved municipal coats of arms. Facing it are the Palazzo del Comune (16th century, former residence of the Della Corgna dukes) and the Palazzo del Capitano del Popolo (15th century). Corciano is classified among Italy's most beautiful hamlets.

activities.text: Almond tree path and panorama over Lake Trasimeno - From the village, the almond tree path descends toward Monte Malbe through a dense wood. On clear days, the view westward extends to Lake Trasimeno, which Corciano historically regarded as the gateway to the Etruscan lands of Arezzo and Cortona.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Corciano

places.name: Coreglia Antelminelli

visits.text: Museum of the Plaster Figurine and Emigration (Palazzo Vanni): From the 16th century until the 20th, the figurinai of Coreglia Antelminelli crafted plaster statuettes—religious subjects, mythological characters, celebrity busts—and left to sell them in the great cities of Europe and beyond. This migratory phenomenon, unique to this village, led to the founding in 1975 of the museum that today houses 1,300 pieces divided into two sections: one dedicated to productions from the 17th–20th centuries, the other to artisan emigration with period passports, letters, and documents. Among the most unusual pieces: 18th-century cats colored with candle smoke.

San Michele Arcangelo Church and Tower of Castruccio Castracani: Built in the 12th century on the site of an old Antelminelli fortress, San Michele church preserves an Annunciation from the Pisan school (1352), a 14th-century painting of the Archangel Saint Michael, and a pre-Romanesque ambo depicting a cow. The imposing adjoining bell tower—former watchtower of Castruccio Castracani, erected in the 11th century and approximately 25 meters high—dominates the village from the ridge at 595 meters altitude.

Historic Center and panorama over the Serchio valley: The medieval village, classified among "Borghi più belli d'Italia," preserves its vaulted streets, arched gates, and 16th-century palaces (Palazzo Antonimi, Palazzo Vincenti, 1572 town hall). From the square outside the walls, views extend over the entire Serchio valley and the silhouette of the Apuan Alps to the west.

activities.text: Medieval Day on August 15th: Every August 15th, Coreglia Antelminelli organizes a historical reenactment with a procession of halberdiers, artisan market, tastings of local products, and street performances in the medieval village lit by torches.

places.name: Cori

visits.text: The Temple of Hercules, built between 89 and 80 BC during Sulla's dictatorship, commands Cori's acropolis at 398 meters above sea level. Its Doric tetrastyle façade preserves eight intact columns with their entablature; on the door's architrave, the names of the two commissioning magistrates—Marcus Maglius and Lucius Turpilius—remain legible after more than 2,000 years. Originally covered in colored stucco, the structure so impressed Piranesi that he dedicated an entire series of 18th-century engravings to it. Raphael himself is said to have come to measure it when appointed architect of St. Peter's Basilica. The Church of San Pietro, built within the temple's cella, was destroyed by Allied bombing in 1944.

Of the Temple of the Dioscuri, constructed between the 4th and 2nd centuries BC and restored in Corinthian style around 100 BC, two columns of approximately 9 meters tall remain, still bearing a fragment of architrave. An inscription reveals the names of the magistrates who financed the restoration using the temple's treasury. The cella once housed colossal statues of Castor and Pollux in Parian marble, measuring 1.90 meters tall, depicted armed and holding the reins of 1.70-meter horses. The fragments are now displayed in the Museum of the City and Territory.

The Oratory of the Santissima Annunziata, a national monument built around 1400 commissioned by Spanish Cardinal Pedro Fernández de Frías, is nicknamed "the Scrovegni Chapel of southern Lazio." This small rectangle measuring 8 by 4 meters, vaulted with a pointed barrel vault, is entirely covered in late Gothic frescoes depicting scenes from the Old and New Testaments. On the façade, Cori's oldest coat of arms (circa 1440) sits beside the cardinal's inscription requesting a Pater Noster for his soul.

Step inside the Church of Sant'Oliva, built on the podium of a 3rd-1st century BC Roman temple, three columns of which are still visible in the nave. The complex blends Roman temple, 12th-century medieval basilica, 15th-century Augustinian convent, and the Cappella del Crocifisso (1467-1480) with 16th-century frescoes depicting stories from the Old and New Testaments. The Renaissance cloister, the work of Antonio da Como, features 27 different marble capitals. In the Middle Ages, this is where magistrates swore their oath of fidelity to the communal statutes.

The Museum of the City and Territory, inaugurated in 2000 in the former convent of Sant'Oliva, traces Cori's history from the Bronze Age to modern times. It houses a copy of the Capitoline Minerva from the Temple of the Dioscuri—the original is in Rome—gifted by the city of Rome in 1999, as well as a 1:75 scale model of the Temple of the Dioscuri reconstructing its original appearance.

activities.text: Stroll between Cori Monte and Cori Valle, the two distinct quarters separated by an olive grove: the upper borough climbs in a triangle from the Temple of Hercules (397 m), while the lower settlement centers around Santa Maria della Pietà (270-300 m). The medieval lanes, often covered by vaults, preserve 14th and 15th-century houses and a fountain where, according to tradition, Frederick Barbarossa bathed.

Climb to the Sanctuary of Madonna del Soccorso, whose esplanade offers sweeping views of the Lepini Mountains and the Pontine Plain stretching to the sea. The sanctuary commemorates the Virgin's appearance in Cori in 1521, celebrated each year in late June with the Carosello Storico dei Rioni, a historical reenactment in early 16th-century costume.

Pass through Piazza Pozzo Dorico, an ancient underground Roman cistern transformed into a silent, secret little square nestled among the alleyways, then continue along Via delle Colonne where vestiges of the ancient forum emerge between the houses.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast

map_keywords: Museo della Città e del Territorio Cori
Santuario della Madonna del Soccorso Cori

visits.text: Medieval walls and towers — a 912-metre fortified circuit, the best-preserved in Le Marche according to the Touring Club Italiano. Built between the 14th and 15th centuries by Francesco di Giorgio Martini after the village was razed in 1360 by the papal army of Galeotto Malatesta. Three access gates: Porta Santa Maria del Mercato (the best preserved, with traces of a drawbridge), Porta San Giovanni to the north, Porta Nova to the west (late 15th century). Four towers punctuate the circuit: Torre dello Scorticatore (inhabited until World War II by a sheep skinner who lived there in exchange for guarding the gate), Torre dello Sperone (18 m, the highest point of the walls), Torre del Calcinaro and Torre della Ronda. In 1517, the inhabitants held out for twenty days against the siege of Francesco Maria Della Rovere — a feat that earned the village the title of Città, granted by Pope Leo X.

Via La Piaggia — the Cento Scale — a stairway of 109 steps cutting the historic centre in two, from Porta Santa Maria del Mercato down to the heart of the borgo. Halfway, the Pozzo della Polenta: according to legend, a farmer once dropped a sack of flour into the well — it mixed with the water and fed the entire village through a whole winter. At the bottom right, the Casa di Scuretto: only the façade exists, complete with street number. The cobbler Gaetano (nicknamed Scuretto) drank away the money his son had sent from America to build a house. When the son asked for a photograph of the works, Scuretto had a façade erected and was photographed at the window. The house was never built.

Santuario Diocesano di Santa Maria Goretti and Casa Natale — Maria Goretti was born in Corinaldo in 1890, martyred in 1902 at the age of 11, and canonised on 24 June 1950 by Pope Pius XII — one of the rare cases in Church history where a mother attended the canonisation of her own child. The sanctuary holds on its left the remains of her mother Assunta (who died in Corinaldo in 1954), and on its right those of Alessandro Serenelli, the murderer who, released after 27 years in prison, asked for forgiveness. On the central white Carrara marble altar, a silver urn contains the bone of the arm with which the saint tried to defend herself. The casa natale, a few minutes from the centre, displays the kitchen with its fireplace, the birth room with period furniture and the loom used by her mother.

Raccolta d'Arte Civica Claudio Ridolfi — a museum in the former Augustinian convent on the main street. Collection of 16th and 17th-century sacred art, including works by local painter Claudio Ridolfi. One room preserves the elaborate costumes worn each year for the Contesa del Pozzo della Polenta historical re-enactment.

activities.text: Full circuit of the walls along the outer path — walk all 912 metres of the circuit from the outside: a linden-lined avenue runs alongside the walls on the eastern side. From the towers, the view embraces the Marchigian countryside, the rolling green hills and, on clear days, the Apennines with the Monti Azzurri.

Down the Cento Scale and the legends of the borgo — descend the 109 steps of Via La Piaggia from Porta Santa Maria, pause at the Pozzo della Polenta to read the carved legend, then find the façade of the Casa di Scuretto to the right at the bottom of the stairs (50 metres past the well, in the first side lane). The entire borgo can be walked in about an hour and a half.

Contesa del Pozzo della Polenta (third Sunday of July) — a historical re-enactment in period costume commemorating the 1517 victory over Della Rovere's army. Jugglers, ladies, archers, flag-throwers and acrobats fill the village. The four Rioni compete in various trials. Considered the oldest festival in the province of Ancona by local tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

visits.text: Parco Regionale del Corno alle Scale - a 5,000-hectare regional park around Corno alle Scale (1,945 m), the Bolognese Apennines' highest peak. The name comes from the sandstone strata that form natural steps (scale) on the summit's flanks. The park shelters primary beech forests, alpine meadows, and peat bogs, with fauna including red deer, roe deer, and peregrine falcon.

Cascate del Dardagna - a series of seven successive waterfalls along the Dardagna stream in a beech forest at 950 m elevation. The main cascade plunges 35 m over sandstone ledges before forming a natural pool. Reachable on foot from the Pianaccio parking in 30 minutes.

Lago Scaffaiolo - a glacial lake at 1,790 m elevation on the ridge between Emilia-Romagna and Tuscany, one of the Apennines' highest lakes. Known since medieval times for its sudden storms, it was considered an evil place by local populations. The adjacent CAI refuge is open June to September.

activities.text: Hike to the Cascate del Dardagna from Pianaccio—an easy 3 km round-trip trail through beech forest along the stream to the seven cascades.
Climb Corno alle Scale from the Rifugio Cavone—a 5 km hike with 600 m elevation gain, 360° views across the Apennines, the Po plain, and on clear days the distant Alps to the north.
Extend to Lago Scaffaiolo from Corno alle Scale—a 2 km extension from the summit along the ridge to reach the glacial lake at 1,790 m.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

places.name: Cortina Dampezzo

visits.text: Bressanone (Brixen in German) is the oldest town in South Tyrol. Its Gothic cathedral dedicated to Mary and Saint Cassian was rebuilt in Baroque style in the 17th and 18th centuries while preserving its 11th-century Romanesque cloister, considered one of the finest in northern Italy: its double columns frame an interior courtyard adorned with medieval frescoes. The neighboring episcopal palace, residence of prince-bishops since the 10th century, houses the Diocesan Museum with a collection of nativity scenes, some dating back to the 17th century.

Lake Braies (Pragser Wildsee in German) sits at 1,496 meters altitude in the Fanes-Sennes-Braies Nature Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2009. Its intense emerald-green waters owe their color to the fine suspension of limestone particles. The lake measures 1.2 km long by 300 to 400 meters wide, with a maximum depth of 36 meters. It's framed by the vertical wall of Croda del Becco (Seekofel, 2,810 m). It's the starting point for Alta Via n°1 of the Dolomites, the great traverse from Braies to Belluno. A dock and wooden chalet on the north shore have made it the most photographed lake in the Dolomites.

Cortina d'Ampezzo is nicknamed the "Queen of the Dolomites." Perched at 1,210 meters altitude in the heart of a white-rock mountain cirque, it hosted the 1956 Winter Olympics and will co-host them again in 2026 with Milan. Its Corso Italia, lined with luxury boutiques, runs through a center with a Belle Époque resort atmosphere. From town, the vertical faces of the Tofane (3,244 m) dominate to the northwest, the Cristallo (3,221 m) to the northeast.

Lake Misurina (1,754 m altitude) is the first body of water you encounter while climbing toward the Tre Cime. Stretching 900 meters long, framed by the Sesto Dolomites, it reflects the silhouettes of Sorapiss and Cristallo in calm weather. It's from Misurina that the toll road (about €30) leading to Rifugio Auronzo departs—the base for the hike around the Tre Cime.

The Tre Cime di Lavaredo (Drei Zinnen in German) are the symbol of the Dolomites and one of the most photographed rock formations in the world. They consist of three distinct pillars of dolomitic limestone: Cima Grande (2,999 m, the highest, with a nearly vertical 500-meter north face), Cima Ovest (2,973 m), and Cima Piccola (2,857 m). These rocks formed 200 to 220 million years ago by sedimentation in the tropical waters of the Tethys Ocean. Cima Grande was first climbed on August 21, 1869 by Paul Grohmann. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2009. During World War I (1915-1917), the front line ran exactly here: trenches, galleries, and barracks carved into the rock are still visible on the trails.

activities.text: The loop around Lake Braies on foot is 3.9 kilometers and takes 1h15 to 1h30. The west shore, wide and flat, is accessible to all; the east shore is narrower, with steps carved into the rock and ledge passages above the water—more demanding but with the most striking views. In high season (July-September), car access is regulated: shuttles required from valley-bottom parking lots.

The Great Dolomites Road (Strada delle Dolomiti, SS48) links Bolzano to Cortina in 117 kilometers. Construction began in 1901 by decision of the 1897 Austrian provincial law—the primary objective was military, the Austro-Hungarian Empire wanting to control its southern borders. The road was completed in 1909. From Cortina, the loop by car passes through three emblematic passes: Passo Falzarego (2,105 m) with the Lagazuoi cable car (2,835 m, site of World War I battles with tunnels carved into the rock), then Passo Pordoi (2,239 m, highest pass on the route, with a monument to cyclist Fausto Coppi and a cable car to Sass Pordoi at 2,950 m, "terrace of the Dolomites"), and finally Passo Sella (2,244 m) at the foot of the Sella group, with direct views of Sassolungo (3,181 m) and Marmolada (3,343 m, highest peak of the Dolomites).

The classic loop around the Tre Cime starts from Rifugio Auronzo (2,320 m, accessible by car from Misurina via toll road) and covers about 10 kilometers with 500 meters of elevation gain. The trail passes Rifugio Lavaredo, climbs to Forcella Lavaredo (2,454 m) to discover the south face of the peaks, winds around the north to Rifugio Locatelli-Drei Zinnen Hütte (2,405 m) which offers the most famous view: the three towers stand directly ahead, their north walls in full verticality. The descent returns to Rifugio Auronzo via the eastern valley. Allow 3 to 4 hours of walking.

visits.text: MAEC — Museum of the Etruscan Academy and City of Cortona: housed in the 14th-century Palazzo Casali, the museum founded in 1727 was one of the first in Europe to open its collections to the public. The centerpiece is a 4th-century BC Etruscan bronze chandelier with 16 burners adorned with Achelous heads and dolphins, from a sanctuary in the Orvieto area. The Tabula Cortonensis, a bronze plate in eight fragments discovered in 1992, is the third-longest Etruscan text in the world — a legal document recording the transfer of vineyard property. A section is dedicated to Gino Severini, the Futurist painter born in Cortona, who personally bequeathed his works to the city.

Diocesan Museum: housed in the former church of the Gesù, it holds the Annunciation by Fra Angelico, painted around 1430 for the church of San Domenico. A tempera on panel measuring 175 × 180 cm, the work is regarded as the first undisputed masterpiece by the artist and served as a model for a whole series of later variations. It was commissioned by Giovanni di Cola di Cecco, a cloth merchant and member of the San Domenico Confraternity. The landscape in the background depicts Lake Trasimeno as Fra Angelico saw it from the heights of Cortona. The museum also holds nine panels by Luca Signorelli — a Cortona native — including the Compianto (1502) and the Communion of the Apostles (1512).

Etruscan walls and archaeological park: the massive polygonal masonry walls encircling the town date in part from the 4th century BC and constitute one of the best-preserved Etruscan fortified perimeters in Italy. Outside the walls, the funerary tumuli known as Meloni di Sodo contain remarkable burial chambers: Tumulus II of Sodo features a spectacular stairway-terrace decorated with sculptural groups suggesting Eastern influences.

Girifalco Fortress: a Medici fortress perched at the top of the hill, from which the view sweeps across the Valdichiana, Lake Trasimeno and, on a clear day, as far as Monte Amiata. Below, Cortona was in the Middle Ages a rival city to Arezzo and Perugia for control of the lake's eastern shore.

Cortona Cathedral — Cattedrale di San Donato: a Renaissance cathedral with a remarkable interior. It houses a painting by Pietro da Cortona depicting the Nativity, a marble ciborium attributed to the local sculptor Ciuccio di Nuccio, and a mosaic by the Cortona-born Gino Severini depicting the Sacred Heart. The building adjoins the Museo Diocesano, both facing the 2nd-century Roman sarcophagus that, according to Vasari, specifically prompted Donatello and Brunelleschi to travel to Cortona to study it.

Church of San Domenico: a 14th-century Gothic building that originally housed, before their transfer to the Diocesan Museum, Fra Angelico's Annunciation and Triptych. On the exterior portal, a recently restored lunette by Fra Angelico is again visible. Inside, a Luca Signorelli altarpiece and, in the main chapel, a polyptych commissioned by the San Domenico Confraternity remain.

Tanella di Pitagora: an Etruscan tomb in rotunda form from the 2nd century BC, located outside the walls by a stream. Its fanciful name invokes Pythagoras with no historical basis — 15th-century oral tradition held that the Greek philosopher had passed through Cortona. The well-preserved corbelled dome is a rare example of this type of construction in the region.

Farneta Abbey: a Benedictine abbey founded in the 9th century, a few kilometers south of Cortona. It contains a pre-Romanesque crypt among the oldest in Tuscany and a paleontological museum housing fossil remains of Pleistocene fauna discovered nearby, including dwarf elephants and hippopotamuses from the Farneta fauna, which populated the Valdichiana over a million years ago.

activities.text: Cortona On The Move photography festival: every year from mid-July to early October, the festival transforms the squares and lanes of the center into open-air galleries, with exhibitions by international artists. Founded in 2011, it's one of the most important photography events in Italy.

Cortonantiquaria: one of Italy's oldest antique fairs, held annually in August–September at the Palazzo Vagnotti. Since 1964, it has brought together dealers specializing in antiques from the Middle Ages to Art Deco.

Walk along the Via Romea between Castiglion Fiorentino and Cortona: a 24-km stage crossing the Sodo Archaeological Park, allowing you to see the Etruscan tumuli and the remains of Roman centuriation in situ before entering the town through its ancient walls.

Panoramic viewpoints over the Valdichiana: from the upper lanes of the historic center, the gaze sweeps across the fertile Valdichiana plain to the profile of Arezzo to the north and, on a clear day, the Sienese hills to the west. The upper piazza offers one of the widest panoramas in all of eastern Tuscany.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries
Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

visits.text: Cosenza will surprise you. Nicknamed the "Athens of Calabria" during the Middle Ages for its intellectual tradition, the city preserves a labyrinthine centro storico that deserves half a day. Begin with the cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta, on Piazza Duomo. Built in the 11th century, destroyed by the 1184 earthquake, it was reconsecrated in 1222 in the presence of Emperor Frederick II, who gifted for the occasion the Stauroteca - a gold reliquary cross by Palermitan goldsmiths, now displayed in the neighboring Diocesan Museum. The cathedral received the UNESCO title of "Heritage Witness of Culture of Peace" in 2011.

In the cathedral's right nave, pause before the sarcophagus of Meleagro, from the late-antique period, which reportedly contains the remains of Henry VII, son of Frederick II. In the left transept, the tomb of Isabella of Aragon, wife of Philip III, who died in Cosenza in 1271 while returning from the Orient, testifies to French Gothic style. Don't miss the chapel of the Madonna del Pilerio either: this 12th-century Byzantine icon, of the "Galaktotrophousa" type (the nursing Virgin), has been the city's protector since it allegedly ended the plague of 1576.

Walk up Corso Telesio, the historic center's main artery, lined with noble palaces and small shops. At the top, the Castello Svevo dominates the city from its spur. Frederick II made it one of his favorite residences - he compared Cosenza's seven hills to those of Rome. The view from the ramparts encompasses the confluence of the Busento and Crati rivers, the very place where, according to legend reported by historian Jordanes around 550, Alaric I was buried in 410 after the sack of Rome.

Descend to the confluence of the Busento and Crati to see Paolo Grassino's contemporary statue evoking the legend of Alaric. The Visigoth king died of malaria in Cosenza in 410, after sacking Rome. His soldiers allegedly diverted the course of the Busento, dug a royal tomb with his horse and part of the loot - reportedly 25 tons of gold - then restored the river and massacred the slaves to keep the secret. The treasure has never been found, despite Himmler's searches during World War II.

activities.text: Stroll along Corso Mazzini, Cosenza's modern artery, which houses the Bilotti Open-Air Museum (MAB) - contemporary sculptures and installations arranged along the pedestrian street. Then head to the Cosenza National Gallery, in Palazzo Arnone, for its collection of Italian paintings from the Renaissance to the 20th century.

In the evening, settle into a trattoria in the centro storico to taste Cosentine cuisine: cavateddri (homemade pasta), soppressata, and Caciocavallo Silano PDO cheese. Cosenza is also a lively university town - enjoy the atmosphere of the bars and cafés around Piazza dei Bruzi.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea

visits.text: Costa Paradiso is an enclave of stone villas melted into a landscape of intense red-ochre granite and trachyte cliffs, formed by ancient volcanic activity. The coast between Castelsardo and Santa Teresa Gallura along provincial road SP 90 is carved into pinnacles, bays, and sea caves accessible only by trail or by sea.

Li Cossi Beach, the only sandy beach at Costa Paradiso, nestles in a sheltered cove between amphitheater-like pink trachyte cliffs. A panoramic trail of about 800 meters from the parking lot winds through the maquis before revealing the beach from above the rocks. The Li Cossi stream forms a small lagoon behind the beach.

Isola Rossa, 6 km south of Costa Paradiso near Trinità d'Agultu, is a red granite islet that gave its name to the locality. From the harbor jetty, the view of the rock against the surrounding emerald waters is particularly striking.

activities.text: Hike the coastal trail from Li Cossi to the Tinnari natural pool — about 3 km one way from the parking lot, with successive views over the red cliffs and coves.
Snorkeling in the rocky coves around Li Cossi, where the seabed alternates between sand and posidonia-covered rocks with excellent visibility.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Sardinia Coast: Beaches & Hidden Coves

places.name: Courmayeur

visits.text: The Great Saint Bernard Pass (8,114 feet), Switzerland's third-highest road pass, has been traveled since antiquity—the Romans built a road here and constructed a temple dedicated to Jupiter Poeninus whose excavations yielded over 40 bronze ex-votos. The hospice was founded in 1050 by Bernard of Aosta, archdeacon of the cathedral, to welcome and protect travelers crossing the Alps—a mission uninterrupted for nearly a thousand years. In May 1800, Napoleon crossed the pass with 40,000 men, 5,000 horses, and 50 cannons during his second Italian campaign; the episode was immortalized in Jacques-Louis David's painting. The tomb of General Desaix, who died at Marengo weeks later, stands in the hospice stairway.

Saint Bernard dogs were bred at the hospice from the 17th century onward, first as guard dogs, then to accompany the canons on daily patrols searching for travelers in distress in the snow. The breed was selected from dogs probably gifted by Valais families in the 1660s-1670s—first mentioned in 1709. Breeding has been entrusted since 2005 to the Barry Foundation in Martigny. Each summer, a dozen dogs return to the pass where visitors can accompany them on hikes. The hospice museum traces the pass's history from ancient cults to contemporary hospitality.

The Little Saint Bernard Pass (7,178 feet), open in summer, links Courmayeur to Bourg-Saint-Maurice in Savoie. It was one of the main Roman routes to Gaul (Alpis Graia). The hospice of Little Saint Bernard, founded in the 11th century by Bernard of Aosta simultaneously with Great Saint Bernard, houses an alpine botanical garden created by Abbot Chanoux in the late 19th century with over 500 alpine species. A circle of pre-Roman standing stones (Cromlech), analogous to Stonehenge but smaller scale, borders the hospice.

La Thuile, a village at 4,728 feet at the entrance to Val Grisenche, harbored until 1967 a silver-lead mine exploited since the 16th century. Its Franco-Italian ski domain (linked to La Rosière in Savoie) is one of the rare ones crossing an Alpine border. The Château de la Crête, a medieval fortress on the mountainside, guards the Dora de la Thuile gorge.

Morgex and La Salle, in the Valdigne, produce the Blanc de Morgex et de La Salle DOC, Europe's highest vineyard wine (between 2,950 and 4,265 feet elevation). The Prié Blanc grape is cultivated on low pergolas on glacial terraces—one of Europe's rare pre-phylloxera vines, spared by the parasite thanks to altitude. The Cave Mont Blanc de Morgex et de La Salle offers tastings and vineyard tours.

activities.text: Hike around Lake Great Saint Bernard (8,114 feet): 45-minute loop between the lake's two shores, 1931 stone Italian-Swiss border marker, views of the Pennine Alps. In summer, possibility of guided hikes with Saint Bernard dogs.

Visit the hospice treasury: 11th-century objects, travelers' offerings, numismatic and Roman archaeological collections found at the Jupiter Poeninus temple site. The baroque church is Europe's highest and hosts ceremonies for the canons' community.

Pré-Saint-Didier thermal baths, 5.6 miles from Courmayeur: outdoor pools at 99°F with views of Mont Blanc's snow-covered peaks, rock treatments, waterfalls and open-air basins. The sulfurous springs have been exploited since the 16th century.

Departure toward France (Mont Blanc tunnel, 7.2 miles, open 24/7) or Switzerland (Great Saint Bernard pass or tunnel, depending on season). Courmayeur is 2 hours from Turin, 1.5 hours from Geneva, 3 hours from Milan.

days_plan.itin_title: Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc
Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc

map_keywords: Colle del Gran San Bernardo Saint-Rhémy-en-Bosses
Ospizio del Gran San Bernardo Saint-Rhémy-en-Bosses
Lago del Gran San Bernardo Saint-Rhémy-en-Bosses
Colle del Piccolo San Bernardo La Thuile
Ospizio del Piccolo San Bernardo La Thuile

visits.text: Castello Ducale di Crecchio - a 14th-century fortress with four corner towers, dominating the village from a 265-meter hill. The castle houses the Museo dell'Abruzzo Bizantino ed Altomedievale, whose centerpiece is the Reggio treasure, a collection of 7th-century gold jewelry discovered in 1955. The last king of the Two Sicilies, Francesco II, took refuge here in 1861 after the fall of Gaeta.

activities.text: Quick stop in the medieval center's narrow streets before continuing to Penne.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Cattedrale di Cremona and Torrazzo - Romanesque-Gothic cathedral built between 1107 and the 14th century, with the Torrazzo rising 112.7 meters - the world's tallest brick bell tower and Italy's third-tallest campanile. The Torrazzo was constructed between 1250 and 1309 and connected to the cathedral in 1490 by a soaring Gothic tribune. The cathedral façade integrates 12th-century Romanesque elements with 13th-14th century Gothic additions.

Piazza del Comune di Cremona - medieval square surrounded by the Palazzo del Comune (1206), Palazzo dei Giureconsulti (13th century), Romanesque Battistero (1167), and Loggia dei Militi (1292). The ensemble forms one of the best-preserved examples of Lombard medieval civic architecture, with five distinct monuments comprising a single integrated complex.

Museo del Violino - museum housed in the Palazzo dell'Arte dedicated to Cremonese violin-making, whose history traces back to Andrea Amati (circa 1505-1577), the first documented violin maker. Antonio Stradivari (1644-1737) crafted over 1,100 instruments in Cremona, of which approximately 650 are still accounted for worldwide. The museum preserves eleven Stradivari violins, including the Cremonese from 1715, which are regularly played during concerts in the museum's integrated performance hall.

activities.text: Visit to a violin-making workshop in the luthiers' quarter around Piazza del Comune - Cremona still counts about one hundred active luthiers, concentrated in the alleys surrounding the cathedral. Several workshops accept visitors by appointment.

Tasting of Cremona's torrone - nougat made from honey, egg whites, and almonds or hazelnuts, crafted in Cremona since the 15th century according to archival documents dating to 1441, during the wedding banquet of Francesco Sforza and Bianca Maria Visconti.

days_plan.itin_title: Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine

places.name: Crespi D Adda

visits.text: Walk down Crespi d'Adda's main avenue to grasp the master plan of this workers' village founded in 1877 by Cristoforo Benigno Crespi, one of Italy's greatest textile industrialists. His son Silvio, trained in England and Germany, designed its final layout: identical workers' houses arranged in a perpendicular grid, each with a garden and vegetable plot, following a paternalistic model inspired by British company towns.

Step inside the textile factory and its grounds: inaugurated in 1878, it sprawled across nearly two square kilometers and counted 120 mechanical looms, 4,000 workers, and a daily output of 50,000 meters of fabric at its peak. Its monumental entrance features a red wrought-iron gate with floral decorations crafted by Milanese artist Alessandro Mazzucotelli. Power came from a hydroelectric plant Crespi himself built on the Adda River.

Explore the village's communal buildings: the school (where books, meals, and teachers' lodging were covered by the company), the Renaissance-style church faithfully copied from the church in Busto Arsizio, the Crespi family's birthplace, the theater, the food cooperative, the hospital, and the public baths with a covered pool and hot water—all financed entirely by the family. The village was also the first in Italy equipped with Edison-system electric street lighting.

Climb the cypress-lined path to the workers' cemetery, where the community's social hierarchy still reads in stone. The simplest workers' graves stand in rows like a military cemetery, while foremen's and managers' tombs occupy the sides. At the far end, dominating the ensemble, rises the Crespi family's pyramid-shaped mausoleum, encircled by walls that seem to embrace 'their' workers protectively. The entire complex was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1995.

activities.text: Bike ride or walk along the Adda riverbanks around the village, following the industrial canal Crespi dug to power his hydroelectric plant. The landscape blends Lombard meadows, poplars, and industrial ruins—a setting that illuminates why UNESCO called Crespi 'the most complete and best-preserved example of a company town in southern Europe.'

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

map_keywords: Villaggio Crespi d'Adda Capriate San Gervasio
Chiesa di Santa Maria Nascente Crespi d'Adda
Fabbrica Tessile Crespi Capriate San Gervasio

places.name: Cuneo

visits.text: Begin at Piazza Galimberti, whose neoclassical design by Benedetto Brunati was approved in 1832 and implemented over fifty years. The square measures over 23,000 m² and is framed by ten identical arcaded palaces with round-arched vaults—a symmetrical arrangement inherited from the Napoleonic alignment plans of 1801, when the demolition of the ramparts finally permitted the city's southward expansion. Originally named Piazza Nizza, then Piazza Vittorio Emanuele II, it has borne since May 21, 1945 the name of Duccio Galimberti, a Cuneo lawyer and Resistance hero killed in 1944, who lived in Palazzo Osasco facing the square.

Walk the entire length of Via Roma: this medieval artery stretching over 800 meters, now pedestrianized, has always been Cuneo's commercial center. The porticos were gradually added to medieval facades to shelter activities under cover—in some buildings, you can still see the joint between the original masonry and the added arches. The 52-meter-high Civic Tower, built in 1317, and the Cathedral of Santa Maria del Bosco, whose dome and interior frescoes date from the 19th century, punctuate the route. From the tower's summit, accessible by elevator, guards would spot enemies and sound the alarm.

Explore Contrada Mondovì, Cuneo's former Jewish quarter: one of Piedmont's oldest ghettos since the 15th century, it now forms a picturesque pedestrian zone with several historic shops. Continue to the Church of San Francesco, built between the 13th and 15th centuries in Gothic style: its marble portal from 1418, crowned by a rosette pediment, is the city's most important medieval testimony. Its cloister houses the Civic Museum.

Visit the Museo Casa Galimberti, housed in the palace where Duccio Galimberti was born, an anti-fascist resistance fighter shot on December 3, 1944 at age 35. The museum includes a gallery of paintings by Piedmontese artists, the family library, and the hero's mementos. The building stands on Piazza Galimberti, at the very spot where the first call to armed resistance in Italy was pronounced on July 26, 1943.

activities.text: Walk through the Parco Fluviale Gesso e Stura, along the banks of the two torrents that define the plateau on which Cuneo was founded in 1198. Cuneo—whose name comes from the Piedmontese 'cùneo,' meaning wedge or point—occupies precisely the point of this natural triangle formed by the confluence of the Gesso and Stura di Demonte, at 534 meters altitude at the foot of the Maritime Alps.

Aperitivo beneath the porticos of Piazza Galimberti: the arcades allow you to travel from end to end of the square and Via Roma without ever being exposed to the weather—making Cuneo one of the European cities with the longest continuous covered gallery route. Taste the Cuneesi al rhum, small chocolates filled with meringue and rum, a local pastry specialty present in every shop window.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days

map_keywords: Parco Fluviale Gesso e Stura Cuneo

places.name: Cutigliano

visits.text: The Palazzo dei Capitani della Montagna, built in 1377 to house the Florentine governor charged with administering the Pistoiese mountains, displays 95 stone and polychrome terracotta coats of arms on its facade—the heraldic shields of each successive Captain, dated from 1444 to 1742. The first to reside here was Baldo Altoviti, a Florentine nobleman. The Captains' Loggia opposite was where justice was dispensed and notarial acts signed. The Marzocco column, bearing Florence's lion with the Republic's lily, recalls that Cutigliano was an outpost of Florentine power in the mountains.

The Chiesa della Madonna di Piazza (15th century), steps from the Palazzo, preserves a terracotta by Benedetto Buglioni representing the Virgin and Child with Saint Anthony Abbot and Saint Bernardino of Siena. San Bartolomeo church, whose campanile rises from an ancient Lombard watchtower, houses 14th-century works.

activities.text: Stroll through the stone lanes of Cutigliano (678m altitude), certified among Italy's most beautiful villages (Bandiera Arancione). The village preserves its intact medieval layout: green sandstone fountains, vaults, covered passages and hand-cut stone facades.

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

map_keywords: Palazzo dei Capitani della Montagna Cutigliano
Chiesa della Madonna di Piazza Cutigliano

places.name: Delta Del Po

visits.text: Explore the Delta del Po Regional Park, where Italy's longest river meets the Adriatic Sea in a landscape of extraordinary natural beauty. This vast wetland ecosystem is home to over 400 bird species including flocks of wild pink flamingoes that create spectacular displays against the marshy backdrop.

Visit the Giardino Botanico Litoraneo di Porto Caleri, a coastal botanical garden offering walks through lush vegetation at the edge of the sea. The diverse habitats - from dunes to pine forests to marshlands - support unique plant and animal communities.

activities.text: Discover wild beaches, island resorts, and the unique landscape where freshwater and saltwater ecosystems merge. The area offers exceptional opportunities for cycling, birdwatching, and boat excursions through the labyrinthine waterways.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Parco Delta del Po Porto Tolle

places.name: Demonte

visits.text: The Forte Albertino di Vinadio, built between 1834 and 1859 by the Kingdom of Sardinia under King Charles Albert, stands as one of Europe's last great star fortifications. Its pink granite bastions, pierced by 8 km of galleries, could house up to 4,000 soldiers and withstand a six-month siege. The fort was designed to guard the Maddalena Pass (1,996 m) against any French incursion from Dauphiné. It was abandoned at the end of the 19th century without ever facing assault.

The Sanctuary of Sant'Anna di Vinadio (2,035 m altitude) is Europe's highest car-accessible Marian sanctuary. Construction dates to the 15th century at the initiative of the upper valley communes. On the night of July 25-26, the annual Saint Anne gathering draws thousands of pilgrims—a torchlit procession descends toward the plain through darkness. The site sits surrounded by alpine meadows with direct views onto the Franco-Italian border ridges.

The Casa del Contrabbandiere in Argentera is a museum-ecomuseum dedicated to the smugglers of the upper Stura: salt, tobacco, alcohol, and textiles crossed the Maddalena Pass by human or mule back, by night, throughout the 18th and 19th centuries. Exhibits include period equipment, oral testimonies, and maps of clandestine routes. In Aisone, caves carved into molasse have yielded traces of human occupation dating back 9,000 years.

The Terme di Vinadio thermal baths capture sulfurous water at 31°C flowing at 620 liters per minute from a geological fault at 900 meters altitude. The current facility, rebuilt in the 20th century, builds on a spa tradition dating to the 16th century, documented in the House of Savoy archives.

activities.text: Hike to Rifugio Migliorero from Vinadio (Valle Stura): ascent through alpine pastures between 1,400 and 2,100 meters, spotting chamois and marmots. Return via abandoned shepherd huts of the valley.
Session at the Terme di Vinadio after the hike: outdoor and indoor sulfurous water pools, thermal treatments. Reservation recommended in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

map_keywords: Santuario di Sant'Anna di Vinadio Vinadio

places.name: Desenzano Del Garda

visits.text: Explore the Castle of Desenzano del Garda, an imposing fortress erected in the early Middle Ages probably on the remains of an ancient Roman castrum. Of the original construction, portions of walls and truncated corner towers survive; from the panoramic observatory at the top you can enjoy a spectacular view of Lake Garda that amply rewards the climb.

Visit the Cathedral of Santa Maria Maddalena, a splendid example of late Brescian Renaissance built between the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries. Inside it houses important works by Andrea Celesti, and in the Chapel of the Blessed Sacrament you can admire G.B. Tiepolo's celebrated Last Supper from 1738, a masterpiece of eighteenth-century Venetian painting.

Discover the Roman Villa, the most important testimony to the great late-antique villas of northern Italy. Built between the first and fourth centuries AD and accidentally discovered in 1921, it preserves over 240 sq m of extraordinary polychrome mosaics featuring cupids, maenads, satyrs and wild animals, bearing witness to the refinement of Roman life on the lake.

Admire the Giovanni Rambotti Archaeological Museum, rich in prehistoric finds from the Lake Garda area dating mainly from the Bronze Age. Among the treasures it houses is what is considered the oldest wooden plough in the world, a precious testimony to the first farming communities that inhabited these shores.

activities.text: Stroll through the picturesque Porto Vecchio, one of Desenzano's most evocative corners overlooked by the broad arcades of Palazzo Todeschini. The dock took shape in the days of the Venetian Republic; don't miss the curious Stone of the Unemployed from 1555, where town criers and those condemned for bankruptcy would stand.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

map_keywords: Castello di Desenzano Desenzano del Garda
Duomo Santa Maria Maddalena Desenzano del Garda
Museo Archeologico Rambotti Desenzano del Garda

visits.text: Diamante - a coastal town of 5,000 inhabitants whose historic center boasts over 150 wall murals created since 1981 by Italian and international artists as part of the Museo all'Aperto (Open-Air Museum) project. The themes explore local life, mythology, and politics, transforming the narrow lanes into an outdoor art gallery. Diamante is also the world capital of Calabrian peperoncino (chili pepper), celebrated at an annual September festival.

activities.text: Stroll through the historic center's lanes to discover the wall murals - a route map is available from the local tourist office.
Sample Calabrian peperoncino in all its forms: dried, powdered, preserved in oil, and especially as 'nduja, the spicy spreadable sausage typical of the Tyrrhenian coast.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to Calabria: The Tyrrhenian Coast

visits.text: Tre Cime di Lavaredo – the three most famous dolomite towers in the Alps: Cima Grande (2,999m), Cima Ovest (2,973m), and Cima Piccola (2,857m), formed by differential erosion of an Upper Triassic limestone plateau. The north face of Cima Grande (500 meters of vertical wall) is one of the six great north faces of the Alps, first climbed in 1933 by Comici and the Dimai brothers. The toll road from Rifugio Auronzo (2,333m) allows you to reach the trailhead by car.

Lago di Landro (Dürrensee) – 15-hectare glacial lake at 1,408 meters on the road between Dobbiaco and Cortina, with reflections of Tre Cime in calm weather from the north shore. The German name Dürrensee (dry lake) recalls that the lake level drops considerably in summer through evaporation and that the lake was nearly dry before construction of a regulatory dam in the 19th century.

Lago di Braies (Pragser Wildsee) – 32-hectare glacial lake at 1,496 meters in the Foresta di Braies, framed by Sasso del Signore (2,448m) and Swiss stone pine forests. The waters reach emerald green to turquoise depending on light conditions, resulting from dissolved carbonates in glacial meltwater. In May 1945, 139 prisoners of war and important hostages (including members of the Italian government) were liberated here by American troops after being transferred from concentration camps.

activities.text: Hike around Tre Cime from Rifugio Auronzo – 9.5-kilometer loop with 350 meters of elevation gain, passing Rifugio Locatelli (2,405m) with views of the north faces of the three towers.
Walk around Lago di Braies – marked 3.5-kilometer trail around the lake with wooden rowboat rentals from the hotel on the south shore.

days_plan.itin_title: Dolomites Loop: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Pastures & Limestone Peaks

visits.text: Cross the Ponte Vecchio to enter the Terra quarter: this single-arch humpback bridge spanning 33 meters was built in the 15th century atop the ruins of an earlier structure. In 1884, Claude Monet was staying in Bordighera when he painted four canvases of it; in his letters, he described it as 'a jewel of lightness.' From the Ponte Nuovo a few meters downstream, the framing shows the bridge with the castle rising behind—exactly as Monet saw it.

Explore the Terra quarter through the caruggi—covered passages so narrow and dark that some require electric lighting even at midday. The neighborhood wraps in concentric rings around the castle, climbing six stories where space ran short. The Scasasse alley, connecting the bridge to the parish square, was once used by locals to move safely during conflicts. Emerge onto Piazza Mauro to see the Palazzo Doria, the noble residence where Napoleon Bonaparte stayed in 1794, and the small fountain dedicated to Rossese wine, where water flows red.

Climb up to the Castello dei Doria, built from 1151 by the Counts of Ventimiglia, then purchased in 1270 by Oberto Doria—the Genoese captain who had just crushed the Pisan fleet at the Battle of Meloria. The Doria family controlled the village for six centuries, until the 1887 earthquake severely damaged the castle and forced their departure. Inside, three museum rooms trace the history of the castle, the Doria genealogy, and the connection with Monet, including a reproduction of the painting held at the Musée Marmottan in Paris.

Visit the parish church of Sant'Antonio Abate, built in the 15th century by incorporating an angular tower from the old ramparts as the base of the bell tower. The Baroque interior houses a polyptych by Ludovico Brea (1515), leader of the Ligurian-Niçois painting school, depicting Saint Devota—patron saint of Monaco—with eight figures against a gold background. In the adjacent Oratory of San Sebastiano, a wooden statue of Saint Sebastian carved from fig wood by the Genoese Anton Maria Maragliano (1664-1739) is carried in procession every January 20th.

activities.text: Photo walk through the Borgo, the neighborhood on the right bank of the Nervia, more open and luminous than the Terra: from its lanes, the view of the Ponte Vecchio with the castle rising behind is the most photographed scene in the village. On the facade of a Borgo house, look for the fresco by Barbadirame, a student of Picasso, depicting Admiral Andrea Doria—born of Caracosa, a woman from Dolceacqua, who became the greatest sailor in the Mediterranean in the 16th century.

Rossese di Dolceacqua DOC wine tasting: designated in 1972 as the first controlled designation of origin in Liguria, this light, fruity red wine is produced on the hillsides of olive groves and vineyards surrounding the village. Local producers offer tastings in the cellars along Via Roma that connects the square to the castle entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days

visits.text: Dozza is a museum-village in the literal sense. Ranked among Italy's most beautiful villages, this small medieval hamlet perched on a hill between Bologna and Imola has become an open-air art gallery thanks to the Painted Wall Biennial, launched in 1960. Over 200 Italian and international artists have painted house facades, arches, and walls over the decades. The works are everywhere: look up, peer into corners, venture down alleys. Among the best-known are "The Angel of Dozza" by Giuliana Bonazza (1993) and "The Rainbow" by Alfonso Frasnedi (2000). The village consists of two parallel streets, Via XX Settembre and Via De Amicis—you can't get lost.

At the top of the village rises the Rocca Sforzesca, a fortress built in 1245 whose current appearance dates from work by Caterina Sforza starting in 1480. She added the two large round corner towers with machicolations. Inside, you'll discover rooms furnished with furniture from the Campeggi and Malvezzi families who inhabited the fortress until 1960, dungeons and oubliettes, and a picture gallery preserving sketches of the wall frescoes. From the rampart walk, the view over the valley vineyards and hills is remarkable.

In the Rocca's cellars, don't miss the Enoteca Regionale dell'Emilia-Romagna, installed here since 1970. It's the showcase for wines from across the region—over 1,000 labels from 200 producers. You can taste on-site using a self-service system, or simply buy a bottle to take home. The setting of vaulted cellars adds to the pleasure. Try the Sangiovese di Romagna, Albana DOCG (the first Italian white to obtain DOCG status), and Pignoletto.

activities.text: If you visit Dozza in September of an even year, you'll catch the Painted Wall Biennial in full swing: over a weekend, artists paint live on the village facades, before the public's eyes. It's a unique moment when you can watch a work being born and chat with its creator. Even in odd years, the village hosts cultural events and wine tastings.

To close this itinerary in style, treat yourself to lunch at one of the village's restaurants. Romagna cuisine takes center stage: fresh pasta, grilled meats, piadina (Romagna flatbread), and local cheeses. Tables are set on terraces as soon as good weather arrives, with views over the hills. Pair the meal with a glass of regional wine—you're in the heart of the Strada dei Vini e Sapori of the Colli d'Imola.

days_plan.itin_title: Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills
10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

visits.text: The Ponte del Diavolo of Dronero, built in 1428 over the Maira torrent, is the village's centerpiece. Three unequal arches, crenellated in swallowtail style (Ghibelline pattern), rise 18 meters above two irrigation canals. The main arch measures 27 meters in diameter and 6 meters wide. A drawbridge existed on the village side until 1810, its iron hinge discovered beneath the paving. Legend tells that the mayor, desperate as floods swept away every bridge, struck a pact with the devil for the soul of the first being to cross. He threw a bread crust—a stray dog bolted across and fled. The devil, cheated, abandoned the bridge. The original construction receipt is preserved in the municipal archives: 700 florins paid to one magister Antonius.

Espaci Occitan, headquarters of the Piedmont Occitan cultural association, houses the Museo Sòn de Lenga devoted to Occitan history and culture in the region. Dronero appears as Dragonerium from the 12th century, its coat of arms displaying a rampant dragon. The historic center preserves medieval arcades, Gothic capitals, and 15th-century noble palaces.

Climb the Valle Maira toward Chialvetta and Viviere on the old paved mule trail. Along 177 km of marked paths, the Occitan Routes traverse hamlets, votive chapels, and sites linked to the former Marquisate of Saluzzo. The San Salvatore chapel in Macra preserves 14th-15th century frescoes depicting the Dance of Salome and Genesis.

Viewpoint above Acceglio facing the Monviso massif (3,841 m), highest peak of the Cottian Alps. The Maira rises in a glacial basin nearby, its waters flowing between pine forests before carving caves in travertine. The village of Acceglio is one of the valley's most isolated, at 1,220 meters altitude.

activities.text: Hike the Cyclamen Trail starting from Bedale (hamlet of Macra): between July and September, meadows of orchids and cyclamens beneath ash and wild cherry trees. Duration about 3h, moderate elevation gain.
Introduction to rock climbing on the valley's equipped cliffs (beginner to intermediate routes, top-ropes installed). Local providers offer half-day guided sessions.
