In June, don't miss the Fiera degli Acciugai (Anchovy Fair) in Dronero, recalling the valley inhabitants' old trade: in winter, they descended to Liguria to buy salt-preserved anchovies, which they resold throughout the region. It's believed the anchovies originally served to conceal smuggled salt.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

places.name: Erto E Casso

visits.text: Diga del Vajont - dam witness to the tragedy of October 9, 1963 which claimed 1917 victims
Erto e Casso - evocative villages above the dam, witnesses to the catastrophe
Landslide site - slope of Monte Toc from which the giant landslide broke away

activities.text: Guided tour recommended to understand the tragedy and its context
Discovery of the history of Longarone, town razed by the wave at the bottom of the valley
Contemplation and reflection on this major industrial catastrophe of the 20th century

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

map_keywords: Diga del Vajont Erto e Casso

visits.text: Pieve di Santa Anatolia — the oldest building in the village, Romanesque in origin and documented as early as 1180. Its 13th-century stone portal is one of the most complete Romanesque examples in the province of Macerata. Embedded in the base of the bell tower is a 1st-century Roman inscription, proof of Augustan-era occupation. The interior, entirely rebuilt in the 18th century, retains the baptismal font and the altar of the patron saint.

Palazzo Varano (Town Hall) — a 14th-century fortified residence of the Da Varano lords of Camerino. During restoration after the 1997 earthquake, medieval frescoes were uncovered depicting a cavalry parade with Giulio Cesare da Varano in old age and his son Giovanni Maria — the only chivalric fresco cycle of its kind in Le Marche. The entire municipal council chamber is decorated with this 15th-century sequence.

Casa delle Tre Porte and the Fonte del Borgo — a medieval house still inhabited today on Corso Vittorio Emanuele II, with three arches serving three distinct purposes: the everyday door, the bridal door, and the 'porta del morto' (walled up and reopened only for the passage of a coffin). The 13th-century village fountain in river stone has two separate basins — one for people, one for animals.

Il Roccone and Eremo di San Cataldo — the Roccone is a 13th-century polygonal watchtower with angular spurs on the slopes of Monte Corsegno, reachable on foot from the centre. The Eremo di San Cataldo, at 766 metres, built over a medieval defensive tower, is dedicated to the Irish saint the community proclaimed as patron in the 17th century. Legend holds he stopped a boulder threatening the village — a large rock bearing the 'imprints of the saint' is visible along the path.

activities.text: Stroll through the 'filetta' — Esanatoglia's nickname for its spindle-shaped elongated form. Corso Vittorio Emanuele II runs from Porta Sant'Andrea at the top to Porta Panicale at the bottom, with side streets branching off perpendicularly toward the seven bell towers that punctuate the village skyline.

Try the Frostingo — an ancient sweet made with nuts, dried fruits and spices — along with local specialities: cotiche with beans, tagliatelline al tartufo nero and artisan honey (Esanatoglia is known as the 'City of Honey').

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

visits.text: Favignana, the main Aegadian island, witnessed the Battle of the Aegates in 241 BC — antiquity's first decisive naval battle, where Rome defeated Carthage at sea for the first time. Since 2005, 200 bronze rams from sunken warships have been recovered from the seabed.

The yellow tuff quarries, excavated since the 16th century, have created a unique landscape of canyons and vertical terraces. Some have been transformed into hanging gardens; others, now flooded, form natural swimming pools.
Cala Rossa, a cove with red-orange waters caused by iron oxides in the submerged tuff rocks, is Favignana's most spectacular swimming spot. Accessible by bike from the port (4 km).

The Ex-Stabilimento Florio, a 19th-century industrial tuna processing plant converted into a museum, documents the mattanza — the ritual bluefin tuna fishing practiced since Arab times. The labyrinth of nets, the rais (fishing chief) ritual, and period tools are preserved through archival audiovisual materials.

activities.text: Bike rental for the island circuit (27 km): flat, well-marked route connecting Cala Azzurra, Cala Rossa, and Cala Rotonda. Favignana is flat and bans motorcycles in high season.

Ferry excursion to Levanzo (20 min, Liberty Lines) for the morning: visit the Grotta del Genovese with its 10,000 BC prehistoric frescoes — the Mediterranean's most important cave paintings. Afternoon return.
Snorkeling at Cala Rotonda, east of Favignana, with seagrass meadows and visibility up to 25 meters.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria
Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Feltre

visits.text: Discover Feltre, a charming town huddled on a small hill and picturesquely surrounded by the powerful peaks of the Dolomites. Tall stone houses with frescoed façades and whimsical chimneys capture the imagination, while the stunning main square - Piazza Maggiore - anchors the historic center.
Explore the museums, churches, lovely theatre, and medieval streets that give Feltre its distinctive character. The blend of Venetian elegance and Alpine setting creates a unique atmosphere.

activities.text: If you have a car, venture to the nearby 11th-century Sanctuary of St. St. Vittore and Corona, a remarkable Romanesque church in a peaceful rural setting with important early frescoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Santuario dei Santi Vittore e Corona Feltre

visits.text: Ferrara is one of the great cities of the Italian Renaissance, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1995. Start with the Castello Estense, the imposing red-brick castle that dominates the city centre. Built in 1385 by the Marquis Niccolò II d'Este following a violent popular uprising, it's the only castle in Italy still surrounded by water-filled moats. Inside, you'll discover the sumptuous ducal apartments with painted ceilings, the kitchens and—in a darker vein—the underground dungeons where the Este family imprisoned their enemies. Climb to the top of the Torre dei Leoni for exceptional panoramic views across the entire city.

A stone's throw from the castle, the cathedral of San Giorgio blends Romanesque and Gothic styles. Its marble façade, begun in the 12th century, is adorned with elaborate sculptures depicting biblical scenes, including a striking Last Judgement. Notice that the lower section is Romanesque, with rounded arches, while the upper levels are Gothic, with pointed arches. The stone lions guarding the main entrance are copies—the originals are preserved inside.

Don't miss the Palazzo Schifanoia, whose name literally means 'escape boredom'—this was the residence where the Este came to be entertained. Built in 1385 by Alberto V d'Este and then enlarged by Borso d'Este in the 1470s, it houses the Hall of the Months, a fresco cycle painted by the finest artists of the Ferrara school, including Francesco del Cossa. Each panel represents a month of the year with its zodiac sign, fieldwork and court entertainments. It's one of the most beautiful ensembles of secular painting from the Italian Renaissance.

Lose yourself in the Via delle Volte, one of the most atmospheric medieval streets in Italy. About 2 km long, it's punctuated by brick arches that connect buildings above your head—these passages once linked merchants' warehouses to their homes on the other side of the street. The atmosphere is unique, especially late in the day. If you have time, push on to the Palazzo dei Diamanti (8,500 stone points on the façade, designed by Biagio Rossetti in 1493)—it mainly hosts temporary exhibitions, so check the programme before going.

activities.text: Ferrara is the cycling city par excellence—they say every resident owns three bikes. Rent a bicycle to ride the 9 km of Renaissance ramparts that encircle the old town. This green circuit, almost entirely intact, offers changing views of interior gardens, tiled roofs and the surrounding countryside. It's the best way to understand why UNESCO described Ferrara as 'an outstanding example of a planned Renaissance city'.

Ferrara's cuisine has its own specialities, distinct from the rest of Emilia-Romagna. Try cappellacci di zucca—ravioli stuffed with pumpkin, a local classic—and salama da sugo, a long-simmered sausage served with mashed potato. Wash it down with a glass of Fortana, the local red wine from the delta. The trattorias in the medieval centre, around Via delle Volte, are your best bet.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini

places.name: Ferrazzano

visits.text: Castello Carafa: rebuilt between late 15th and early 16th centuries by the Carafa family on an earlier Samnite fortification. Two asymmetrical circular towers, stone bridge replacing the former drawbridge. The castle remains inhabited. Listed as nationally significant by the Ministry of Cultural Heritage.

Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta (founded 1005): Romanesque portal, sculpted capitals and medieval marble pulpit with four columns. Ferrazzano is nicknamed "lu Spione"—the sentinel—for its commanding position at 872m, visible across the entire plain. Robert De Niro's great-great-grandfather hailed from here.

Piazza Spensieri and Teatro del Loto: the panoramic terrace overlooks Campobasso, the Matese massif and the Maiella. Teatro del Loto—Libero Opificio Teatrale Occidentale—born from transforming a 1950s parish hall, is often cited as Italy's most beautiful small theater.

activities.text: Walk down from Ferrazzano to Campobasso via the Viale delle Rimembranze: roughly 6 km through pine forest, a local classic for enjoying the panorama on descent.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Ferrazzano

visits.text: The archaeological area of Fiesole—Roman theater, thermal baths, Etruscan-Roman temple, and museum—spreads across three hectares in the heart of the hillside. Fiesole (ancient Faesulae) was founded by the Etruscans between the 9th and 8th centuries BC and only became a Roman colony in the 1st century BC, after the Romans had already settled in the valley below to establish Florence. The theater, carved into the hillside in the 1st century BC, still hosts performances each summer during the Estate Fiesolana festival. The layered Etruscan-Roman temple—first dedicated to Minerva (a bronze owl attests to this), then converted to a Roman temple in the 1st century BC—was destroyed by fire following the Roman conquest. Cyclopean Etruscan walls remain visible on the site's northern edge. The museum, built in 1914 as an Ionic temple, preserves a fragment of a bronze Capitoline wolf and the Costantini collection of Greek and Etruscan ceramics.

The Villa Medici in Fiesole, built between 1451 and 1457 by Michelozzo for Giovanni di Cosimo de' Medici—son of Cosimo the Elder—is the first pleasure villa of the Renaissance: it broke definitively with the medieval fortress model by making the loggia and panorama the pivots of its architecture. Lorenzo the Magnificent inherited it in 1469 and turned it into a meeting place for the Platonic Academy: Marsilio Ficino, Pico della Mirandola, and Poliziano philosophized here. The villa has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2013. A singular detail: this is where the Medici first introduced citrus cultivation to Tuscany, imported from southern Italy. An assassination attempt on Lorenzo the Magnificent by the Pazzi family took place during a gathering within these walls.

Villa Peyron al Bosco di Fontelucente, 2 km from central Fiesole, is a 20th-century Italian garden designed between 1934 and 1960 by Paolo Peyron—without an architect, following only his own instinct—across 40 hectares of wooded estate. Thirty terraced levels descend toward Florence, linked by double-hedged boxwood parterres, 29 fountains fed by a 16th-century spring, statues in Vicenza stone (replacing originals destroyed during the war), and an artificial lake. Bequeathed to the Bardini-Peyron Foundation in 1998, the complex is classified as an exemplary 20th-century garden by Florence's Monuments Authority.

activities.text: Panoramic views of Florence from Piazza Mino da Fiesole or from the Villa Medici gardens, with vistas over Brunelleschi's dome, the bell towers of Santa Croce, and the Arno Valley extending to the Apennines. Evening light is particularly striking.
Hike the Sentiero dei Borghi trail between Fiesole and Settignano, through olive groves and historic villa gardens. The path follows the Etruscan walls for part of the route.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

places.name: Figline Valdarno

visits.text: Figline Valdarno is the birthplace of Marsilio Ficino (1433-1499), the Neoplatonist philosopher who, under the patronage of Cosimo de' Medici then Lorenzo the Magnificent, translated into Latin the entire works of Plato (1482 edition), Plotinus's Enneads, and the Corpus Hermeticum—thus restoring to the West texts lost since Antiquity. He directed the Platonic Academy of Florence, founded by Cosimo de' Medici in 1459. The main square bears his name. The Collegiate Church of Santa Maria (founded 1257) houses the Madonna Enthroned by the Master of Figline (1317) and a terracotta Saint Joseph attributed to Andrea della Robbia.

The Castello di Sammezzano, 8 km north in Leccio (municipality of Reggello), is a Moorish castle transformed between 1853 and 1889 by Marquis Ferdinando Panciatichi Ximenes d'Aragona—a Florentine noble of Spanish origin. Inspired by the Alhambra in Granada, he had 365 rooms built decorated with arabesque ceilings, stalactite vaults, polychrome mosaics, and horseshoe arches, employing only local artisans. In 1878, King Umberto I was received here. The castle is currently closed to the public for restoration work (new ownership since April 2025, reopening envisioned at the earliest in 2028). The 65-hectare park—one of Tuscany's largest, with Italy's largest group of giant sequoias—remains accessible.

activities.text: Hiking in Castello di Sammezzano park among giant sequoias, romantic alleys, and Moorish architectural elements of the garden (bridge, artificial grotto with statue of Venus, pools). Ideal in spring and autumn.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Figline Valdarno

visits.text: Filicudi, a 9.5 km² island with 200 permanent residents, remains one of the least touristy Aeolian islands. Its agricultural terraces, abandoned in the 1950s, now bloom with wild capers and fig trees. A single road connects the port to Valdichiesa at 390 meters elevation.

The Grotta del Bue Marino (Sea Ox Cave, named for the now-extinct Mediterranean monk seal), accessible only by sea, is a 35-meter-deep marine cavern whose walls reflect turquoise water in shimmering patterns.

activities.text: Hike to Monte Fossa Felci di Filicudi (773 m) from the port: 2h30 round-trip on a trail through terraced vineyards and caper plants, with views of Alicudi and Salina.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Fiorenzuola Di Focara

visits.text: Village gateway and the Dante plaque — the only true promontory on the northern Adriatic between Trieste and Ancona's Monte Conero, 177 metres above the cliff. The village appeared between the 10th and 13th centuries as part of a defensive system alongside Casteldimezzo, Gradara and Granarola, at the crossroads between the jurisdictions of Ravenna and Pesaro, later between the Malatesta of Rimini and those of Pesaro. At the village entrance, a stone plaque engraves the lines from Canto XXVIII of Dante's Inferno (vv. 88–89): Guido da Montefeltro foretells that the winds of Focara would be so deadly that no prayer or vow would suffice. 'Focara' derives either from the fuochi — fires lit at the summit to guide sailors by night — or from the fornacelle where bricks were fired (from the Romagnol fuchèr).

Pentagonal wall circuit and Sant'Andrea campanile — of the original five bastions, three survive. The walls along the cliff edge are being gradually eroded by the sea below. 17th-century doorways with inscriptions decorate the lanes. The church of Sant'Andrea dates to the 12th century — demolished by the 1916 earthquake, only its bell tower remains standing, its clock still working, striking the hour in this near-silent village.

Belvedere on the cliff — Sentiero dell'Amore — The village lanes describe concentric semicircles converging toward the cliff's edge, then rising back toward the old bell tower. From the belvedere at the wall's edge, the drop to the sea is sheer and vertiginous. The Sentiero dell'Amore is a path laid along the old rampart walkway, running along the cliff face.

activities.text: Descent to the beach at Fiorenzuola — the wild beach of Fiorenzuola di Focara is accessible on foot from the village via the Strada della Marina. A must on any day spent at Focara. Turquoise water, shallow seabed, within the Parco Naturale del Monte San Bartolo: ranked among the 40 most beautiful beaches in Europe by The Guardian in 2019. In summer, a shuttle connects the village to the beach.

Hiking in the Parco del Monte San Bartolo — from Fiorenzuola, several trails in the Parco Naturale Regionale del Monte San Bartolo head north toward Casteldimezzo or south toward Pesaro. Trail 151 runs along the coast, alternating between Mediterranean scrubland and open views over the Adriatic.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Campanile di Sant'Andrea Fiorenzuola di Focara

visits.text: Lucca - morning stop en route to Florence (Lucca sits on the A11 highway, halfway between Pisa and Florence). City Walls - Passeggiata sulle Mura: Lucca's ramparts form a 4.2-kilometer Renaissance enclosure built between 1504 and 1645. Unlike most Italian fortifications, they were never breached and remain virtually intact. Their flat crown, 30 meters wide and shaded by centuries-old plane trees, was converted into a public promenade in the 19th century. Eleven star-shaped bastions punctuate the circuit, designed to withstand artillery using the military engineering techniques of the era.

Torre Guinigi - Built in the second half of the 14th century by Paolo Guinigi, Lord of Lucca from 1400 to 1430, the Guinigi Tower rises 45 meters in red Romano-Gothic brick. Its absolute singularity is the hanging garden at its summit: seven holm oaks (Quercus ilex), symbols of rebirth in the Guinigi family tradition, grow in a soil-filled chamber 45 meters above ground. In the early 15th century, Lucca counted nearly 250 towers; the Guinigi is one of the few still standing. Local legend claims that the tallest tree was planted by Paolo Guinigi himself, and that it lost all its leaves when the lord was imprisoned by Francesco Sforza. The 230 steps lead to a panorama over the entire medieval center.

Torre delle Ore - Lucca's medieval public clock tower dates from the 14th century and was one of Tuscany's first mechanical public clocks. The climb is optional but offers a different view over the rooftops and the nearby Torre Guinigi.

San Michele in Foro - Built on the site of the Roman forum, the church of San Michele in Foro is one of the finest achievements of Pisan Romanesque architecture in Lucca. Its four-tiered facade, adorned with twisted columns, marble lozenges, and polychrome inlays, was erected between the 11th and 14th centuries. The facade is deliberately higher than the nave to impress merchants in the square.

Piazza dell'Anfiteatro - This elliptical square, unique in the world, is built on the foundations of a 1st-century AD Roman amphitheater that could hold up to 10,000 spectators. The tiers were gradually integrated into medieval houses, whose facades still follow the exact curve of the ancient arena. Four vaulted passages, heirs to the Roman entrances, are the only access points to the enclosed square.

Cathedral of San Martino - The Cathedral of Lucca preserves one of the most venerated relics of the Middle Ages: the Volto Santo, a cedar wood crucifix representing the face of Christ, which tradition attributes to Nicodemus, a disciple of Jesus. The tomb of Ilaria del Carretto (1406), a masterpiece by Jacopo della Quercia, is one of the most moving examples of Italian Gothic sculpture.

Ponte Vecchio and Lungarno - The Ponte Vecchio owes its existence to an earlier Roman bridge built around 120 AD on the Via Cassia. Destroyed by a flood in 1333, it was rebuilt in stone in 1345 by Taddeo Gaddi and Neri di Fioravante with three arches, the largest measuring 30 meters. In 1593, Ferdinand I de' Medici, bothered by the smells of butchers and fishmongers when crossing the Vasari Corridor above, ordered that only jewelers and goldsmiths could keep shops there—a regulation still in effect today. The Vasari Corridor, built in 1565, runs above the bridge for nearly a kilometer.

Piazza del Duomo - Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore, Brunelleschi's Dome, Giotto's Campanile - The Cathedral of Florence, whose construction began in 1296 under Arnolfo di Cambio, is crowned by Brunelleschi's dome completed in 1436: with its 45-meter diameter and 91-meter height, it remains the world's largest brick dome. Brunelleschi had to invent new construction techniques because no contemporary knew how to close such a cupola. Giotto's Campanile, begun in 1334, reaches 84 meters and offers from its 414 steps a direct view of the dome. The Baptistery of San Giovanni, whose 'Gates of Paradise' were sculpted by Ghiberti between 1425 and 1452, is one of the city's oldest buildings.

Mercato Centrale (San Lorenzo) - The halls of the Mercato Centrale, built in iron and glass between 1870 and 1874 by architect Giuseppe Mengoni (also architect of Milan's Galleria Vittorio Emanuele), constitute one of Tuscany's liveliest covered food markets.

Piazza della Signoria - Loggia dei Lanzi - The Palazzo Vecchio, whose construction began in 1299 under Arnolfo di Cambio, was the seat of the Florentine Republic and later of the Grand Duchy of Tuscany. Its 94-meter tower is offset from the facade because it incorporates a pre-existing medieval tower. In the square, a circular plaque marks the spot where Savonarola was hanged and burned in 1498. The Loggia dei Lanzi houses Benvenuto Cellini's Perseus (1554) and Giambologna's Rape of the Sabine Women (1583).

Palazzo Pitti and Boboli Gardens - The Palazzo Pitti, whose construction began in 1458 for banker Luca Pitti on a design attributed to Brunelleschi, was acquired by the Medici in 1560. It's the largest palace in Florence with a 205-meter facade. The Boboli Gardens, laid out from 1549, constitute one of the first examples of Italian-style gardens with their geometric paths, fountains, and statues.

Oltrarno Quarter - The neighborhood situated beyond the Arno retains a more authentic atmosphere. The Basilica of Santo Spirito, designed by Brunelleschi in 1436, and the alleys around Piazza Santo Spirito concentrate artisan workshops, antique dealers, and trattorias frequented by Florentines.

activities.text: Bike ride on Lucca's ramparts - The complete circuit of the ramparts is 4.2 km and takes 30 to 45 minutes by bike. Rental shops are located at the main city gates.

Walking loop through Florence's historic center (Duomo → Signoria → Ponte Vecchio → Oltrarno) - The entire historic center of Florence has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1982. The loop covers approximately 3 km.

days_plan.itin_title: Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome
Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome
Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

visits.text: Fiuggi, renamed by royal decree in 1911 (previously called Anticoli di Campagna), is famous for two thermal springs: the Boniface VIII Spring and the Anticolana Spring. Pontifical archives preserve over 187 payment orders signed by Boniface VIII between 1299 and 1302 to have Fiuggi water transported to Rome—he suffered from kidney stones. Michelangelo, in a 1549 letter, wrote of this water: it dissolves stone. The current thermal zone covers 94,000 square meters, and the first facility opened in 1911.

The historic center of Fiuggi Città, perched at 747 meters elevation, preserves its medieval fabric with sloping lanes, stone arches, and Piazza Spaccasassi—whose name, meaning "stone-breaker," is a direct reference to the diuretic properties of the local water.

activities.text: Stroll through the thermal park of the Boniface VIII Spring to drink the water directly from the source: very lightly mineralized (dry residue of 123 mg/l), it has been renowned since the Middle Ages for facilitating the elimination of urinary stones. The shaded Belle Époque walkways wind past marble fountains and early 20th-century spa pavilions.

Lake Canterno, about 6 km from Fiuggi, is the only natural lake in southern Lazio not fed by a river but by underground springs. Its wooded shores are traced by an easy 7-km trail through a protected wetland populated by grey herons and mallards.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Visit Follina, one of Italy's most beautiful villages, centered around a magnificent medieval abbey. The Cistercian Abbey of Santa Maria dates to the 12th century, its Romanesque-Gothic cloister remaining remarkably preserved as a place of profound spiritual atmosphere.

Explore nearby Cison di Valmarino, another officially designated beautiful village dominated by Castel Brando - one of Italy's largest castles, now functioning as a luxury hotel. The village streets retain their medieval character.

Discover the village of Mel if time permits, completing a trio of the most beautiful villages in the Veneto's northern hills. The surrounding vineyards and hills create a peaceful countryside setting.

activities.text: If visiting during the Artigianato Vivo festival (held twice yearly in Cison di Valmarino), experience hundreds of artisans and craftsmen taking over the village streets with their traditional skills and handmade wares.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Castello Baronale Caetani, a rare example of a fortress built on flat ground, rises on Fondi's perimeter walls. Its base is a parallelepiped mastio in opus quadratum dating from the late 12th century, completed in the 15th century by the Caetani with an imposing cylindrical tower and machicolated crenellations. The grand rocca has three round bastions. A passetto—a covered walkway reconstructed in wood and steel on the original model—connects the castle to the neighboring Palazzo Caetani, offering a bird's-eye view of the town and countryside. The castle houses the Civic Museum with artifacts from protohistoric to medieval times.

The Cathedral of San Pietro, the historic center's cathedral, was erected on the site of an ancient temple dedicated to Jupiter. Fondi is linked to the Western Schism: in the 14th century, a group of dissident cardinals elected the antipope Clement VII here, dividing Christendom for decades.

The Museum of Medieval Judaism, housed in the former synagogue called "Casa degli Spiriti" in the La Giudea quarter, traces the Jewish presence in Fondi over nearly a thousand years, from the 5th to the 16th century. The community included dyers, fabric makers, merchants, and moneylenders active in Fondi's economy. After the expulsion decrees of 1541 under Charles V and the malaria epidemic of 1633, the inhabitants dispersed. The museum occupies five rooms on two levels in the restored premises of the former synagogue.

activities.text: Walk through the La Giudea quarter, the former Jewish ghetto located in the northeast corner of the historic center. Its uniqueness lies in being integrated into the urban fabric rather than isolated like in most European cities. The low houses, on two floors, connect to one another: the ground floor served as shop or workshop, the upper floor as dwelling. The exterior staircases of the houses converge toward the square of the former synagogue.

Stroll around Lago di Fondi, the largest of the three lakes surrounding the town (along with Lago Lungo and Lago di San Puoto). The plain of Fondi, enclosed in a semicircle by the Ausoni Mountains, is one of the main market gardening centers of southern Lazio.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast

places.name: Fontana Di Trevi

visits.text: The Trevi Fountain (26 m high, 49 m wide) is Rome's largest Baroque fountain and the terminal point of the Acqua Vergine aqueduct, built in 19 BC by Agrippa. Nicola Salvi won the competition organized by Clement XII in 1730—he beat Alessandro Galilei, initially favored but rejected by the public because he was Florentine, not Roman. Salvi died in 1751 without seeing his work completed; Giuseppe Pannini finished it in 1762. The central figure is not Neptune but Oceanus, god of the primordial seas. The two hippocampi symbolize the sea's changing moods—one agitated, the other calm. Salvi placed a large travertine vase shaped like an ace of cups in front of a barber's shop whose owner harassed him with criticism, permanently blocking his view of the construction site. The fountain collects about 1.5 million euros in coins per year, donated to Caritas.

The Vicus Caprarius—City of Water—is an underground archaeological site discovered during construction work in 1999, right next to the Trevi Fountain. You can see remnants of a 2nd-century Roman insula and the conduits of the Acqua Vergine that still feed the fountain today. A direct window into Rome's underground, visited by few tourists.

activities.text: Toss a coin over your left shoulder with your back to the fountain: the tradition, popularized by the film La Dolce Vita (1960), ensures a return to Rome. Come at dawn to avoid crowds—the fountain is accessible outside peak hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Chiesa dei Santi Vincenzo e Anastasio Roma

visits.text: Explore the Castle of Fontanellato, a perfectly preserved medieval fortress surrounded by a wide moat filled with water. This 14th-century stronghold houses an exquisite chamber decorated with frescoes by Parmigianino depicting the myth of Diana and Actaeon, considered one of the finest examples of Mannerist painting in Italy.

Visit the Labirinto della Masone, the world's largest green labyrinth covering over eight hectares with bamboo plants reaching heights of fifteen meters. Created by art collector Franco Maria Ricci, this extraordinary maze also houses a museum of art, a publishing archive, and offers panoramic views from the rooftop terrace over the intricate pathways below.

activities.text: Wander through the charming historic center of Fontanellato, where medieval porticoes line the main square surrounding the castle. The town has maintained its authentic character, with traditional shops and the peaceful atmosphere of a fortified village unchanged for centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

visits.text: The Santuario di Fonte Colombo, clinging to the slopes of Monte Rainiero at 1,801 feet, is known as the "Franciscan Sinai." In September 1223, Saint Francis of Assisi withdrew here with Brother Leo into a natural crevice in the rock—the Sacro Speco—to draft the definitive Rule of the Order of Friars Minor after 40 days of fasting and prayer. Pope Honorius III approved this Regola Bollata that same year. The complex includes the church consecrated on July 19, 1450 by Cardinal Nicholas of Cusa, featuring a 15th-century lunette depicting the Virgin between Saint Francis and Saint Louis of Toulouse, plus stained glass windows designed by Duilio Cambellotti in 1926.

Descend the steep stairway to the Sacro Speco, the chapel of San Michele that envelops the grotto where, according to tradition, Christ appeared to Francis to confirm the Rule. Further down, Brother Leo's cave preserves in the rock the imprint the monk supposedly left when he raised his head during the vision. Climbing back up, you'll pass the holm oak where Christ is said to have appeared, then the chapel of the Maddalena, an 11th-century Gothic structure with primitive frescoes and a Tau traced on the window—the sign Francis used as his personal seal.

The Santuario di Greccio, perched like an eagle's nest at 2,182 feet on a cliff forested with holm oaks, is the "Franciscan Bethlehem." On Christmas night 1223, Saint Francis created history's very first living nativity scene here, with the help of local lord Giovanni Velita: an ox, a donkey, a manger and hay in a mountain grotto. According to Thomas of Celano's account, the simulacrum of the Christ Child came to life in Francis's arms. The Cappella del Presepio, built in 1228 (the year of his canonization), preserves a remarkable fresco from Giotto's school attributed to the Master of Narni (1409), showing in two scenes the Nativity of Bethlehem and the Nativity of Greccio. Pope Francis signed his 2019 apostolic letter Admirabile signum on the meaning of the nativity scene here.

activities.text: Walk through the forest of ancient holm oaks linking Fonte Colombo sanctuary to the Fonte delle Colombe, the crystal-clear spring that gave the place its name. When Francis first climbed here in 1217, he saw white doves drinking from it. The path is punctuated by three 17th–18th century chapels adorned with terracotta reliefs depicting the saint's life in the Rieti area.

days_plan.itin_title: Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit

places.name: Forgaria Nel Friuli

visits.text: Lago di Cornino - This tiny lake born about 10,000 years ago during glacier retreat glows intense turquoise year-round, fed by underground aquifers that maintain the water between 8 and 9°C permanently. Water laden with calcium sulfate from gypsum in the Tagliamento basin gives it crystalline clarity. The lake is oligotrophic (nutrient-poor), but still shelters the white-clawed crayfish. At Christmas, divers place an underwater nativity scene on the lake bottom.

Riserva Naturale Regionale del Lago di Cornino - This 487-hectare reserve sits at the extreme southeastern margin of the Carnic Pre-Alps. The free visitor center houses aviaries for recovering raptors, a museum dedicated to the Progetto Grifone (Griffon Vulture Project), a feeding station for vultures, and a capanno (blind) for bird observation.

activities.text: Trail around Lake Cornino - An easy 2.5 km circuit (about 50 minutes) loops the lake, passes a panoramic platform, follows a stretch of the Tagliamento, and crosses a grove with picnic tables. Swimming prohibited.

Griffon vulture observation (grifoni) - The Progetto Grifone, launched in the late 1980s, is Italy's first reintroduction program for these birds. Since then, 75 vultures have been released, and the colony now numbers about 150 individuals in winter and over 200 in spring-summer, with around thirty nesting pairs in the Tagliamento valley. With nearly 3-meter wingspans and weighing 7 to 12 kg, grifoni exploit thermal currents to cover hundreds of kilometers daily. Observation is recommended in the morning, during feeding time.

days_plan.itin_title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

map_keywords: Lago di Cornino Forgaria nel Friuli

visits.text: The Roman Forum is the nerve center of ancient Rome, formerly a marshy zone drained by the Cloaca Maxima of the Tarquins in the 6th century BC. For nearly a thousand years, it was the site of all political, judicial, and religious decisions of the Empire. The Via Sacra (600 m) crosses it from the Colosseum to the Capitol: this is where triumphant generals paraded laden with spoils. Among the still-visible monuments: the Temple of Saturn (5th century BC, one of Rome's oldest), the Curia of Julius Caesar (rebuilt 29 BC, Senate seat), the Arch of Septimius Severus (203 AD), the Temple of Vesta where the Vestals tended the sacred fire, and the Basilica of Maxentius whose three coffered vaults reached 35 m high.

The Palatine Hill—included in the same ticket as the Forum—is the hill where, according to tradition, Romulus founded Rome in 753 BC. Augustus was born here and built his apartments, then his successors settled here with increasingly sumptuous palaces. From the summit, the view embraces the Forum below, the Circus Maximus on the other side, and on clear days, the hills of the Roman countryside.

activities.text: Look on the floor of the Basilica Julia (south side of the Forum) for game boards carved by Roman soldiers who passed the time between judicial hearings—some are still perfectly legible.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

places.name: Forte Dei Marmi

visits.text: Fortino (Fort Lorenz): At the center of Piazza Garibaldi, this quadrangular fort was built in the late 16th century on orders of Grand Duke Pietro Leopoldo of Tuscany, initially to protect the coastline and store marble from the Apuan Alps before loading it onto ships. The town takes its name from this fort—Forte dei Marmi literally means "fort of the marbles." Until April 26, 1914, Forte dei Marmi was attached to Pietrasanta; it became an independent municipality on that date. The fort today houses the Museum of Satire and Caricature, founded on the collections of the Political Satire Prize created in 1973.

Pontile (historic pier): Built between 1867 and 1877 to load marble from the Apuan Alps directly onto ships, this wharf equipped with rails and a steam crane was destroyed during World War II and rebuilt in 1955. The walk on the jetty, with the Apuan Alps behind and the Tyrrhenian Sea ahead, is the emblematic panorama of Forte dei Marmi.

Parco della Versiliana: On the border between Forte dei Marmi and Marina di Pietrasanta, this park of umbrella pines spanning several dozen hectares was frequented by literary and artistic figures of the 19th and 20th centuries. It now hosts a renowned summer cultural festival (theater, concerts, exhibitions) throughout Italy.

activities.text: Beach and swimming at Versilia: The beach establishments of Forte dei Marmi, with their white-striped tents and wooden cabanas, are set on a fine sand beach of about 5 km. The sandy seabed slopes gently into the Tyrrhenian Sea.

Excursion to the marble quarries of Carrara: About 20 km north, the quarries of the Apuan Alps have been exploited since Roman times. Michelangelo came to personally select his blocks. Guided tours by all-terrain vehicle allow approach to active quarry faces.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

map_keywords: Parco della Versiliana Marina di Pietrasanta

places.name: Frontino

visits.text: Torre Civica covered in Virginia creeper — the symbol of Frontino: a 14th-century watchtower and campanile entirely covered in American Virginia creeper, turning from deep green in summer to blazing red in autumn. The smallest village in the province of Pesaro-Urbino (300 inhabitants), perched at 519 metres on a rocky spur above the Mutino torrent. The streets are paved with river stones from the Mutino — the same stones that built the houses. A Montefeltro border castle, always loyal against the Malatesta. In 1451, Malatesta soldiers scaled the walls at night during the winter truce. A woman leaving at dawn raised the alarm — the villagers, trapped inside, climbed onto the rooftops and hurled roof tiles and stones. Six soldiers were captured, five executed. Federico da Montefeltro exempted the village from taxes for ten years.

Chiesa Parrocchiale dei Santi Pietro e Paolo — tucked under a small portico along the main vicolo. It holds a 1610 canvas by Antonio Cimatori, known as il Visaccio, a pupil of Federico Barocci — the most important painter of Urbino after Raphael — depicting the Madonna col Bambino e i Santi Ubaldo e Francesco.

Fontana by Franco Assetto and Museum — Franco Assetto (1911–1993), a Turinese artist who lived in the United States, donated a large part of his work to the municipality. The monumental fountain of 1971 in the small western piazzetta is a 'scultura d'acqua' — a term Assetto himself coined. The museum displays works spanning Surrealism, ethnic art, matter-based Informel and Pop Art: mahogany and plastic wall sculptures, paper cut-out drawings, ceramics, jewellery.

Convento di Montefiorentino — founded by Saint Francis in 1213, one of the largest convents in Le Marche, set among woodlands a few kilometres from the village. The cappella dei conti Oliva and the altarpiece by Giovanni Santi (Raphael's father) are centrepieces of Montefeltro Renaissance art. The great polyptych by Alvise Vivarini that once belonged to this church is now held at the Galleria Nazionale d'Arte di Urbino. Since 1981, the Premio Nazionale di Cultura Frontino-Montefeltro, founded by literary critic Carlo Bo, is held here each year (third Sunday of September).

activities.text: Walk through the village and discover the spaventapasseri — Frontino is known for its scarecrows (spaventapasseri) placed throughout every alley, each representing local characters and traditions. In July–August, the Festival Internazionale degli Spaventapasseri. The village takes about 30 minutes to explore — slowly, for the mountain air and views toward Monte Carpegna.

Hiking in the Parco del Sasso Simone e Simoncello — Frontino sits at the heart of this nature park. Several marked trails lead to the two Sassi — massive rock formations rising dramatically from the hill landscape. Routes for all levels, with panoramic views across the Montefeltro.

Sagra del Tartufo Nero (first weekend of August) — the summer black truffle of the Montefeltro is the area's signature product. The first August weekend festival is the village's main annual celebration, with tastings and local producer stands.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Chiesa dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Frontino

visits.text: The Castle of Fumone—officially Castello Longhi-De Paolis—was built in the 9th-10th centuries and served as a papal fortress for over five hundred years. Pope Celestine V, who abdicated on December 13, 1294 after just five months in office (the episode for which Dante cites him in his Inferno as "he who made the great refusal"), was imprisoned here by his successor Boniface VIII. He died on May 19, 1296 after ten months of captivity. Benedict XVI laid his pallium on Celestine's tomb in 2009 during a pilgrimage. In 1584, Sixtus V entrusted the castle to the Longhi family to preserve it as a memorial site.

The hanging garden, created by the Longhi family in the 17th century by uniting the walkways, moat, and four interior spaces of the towers, is presented as one of the highest private gardens in Europe. From its terraces at 800 meters altitude, the view sweeps across all of Ciociaria, from the Sacco Valley to the Lepini Mountains.

activities.text: Explore Fumone's medieval borgo: the village houses have gradually merged with the castle walls, making it difficult to distinguish between town and fortress. Stepped lanes lead to viewpoints over the surrounding valleys and pass 17th-century votive niches embedded in the rock.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Medieval Gaeta — The 'Pearl of the Tyrrhenian,' cited in Virgil's Aeneid, which derives its name from Caieta, Aeneas's nurse. Autonomous duchy in the Middle Ages (9th-12th centuries) with its own currency (the Follaro), the city traded throughout the Mediterranean. The Castello Angioino-Aragonese dominates the town: 13th-century core (Charles of Anjou, 1279) expanded by Alfonso of Aragon (1440), fortified by Charles V (150,000 sq ft). Gaeta was 'the key to the Kingdom of Naples.' Pope Pius IX took refuge here from 1848 to 1850; Francis II of Bourbon capitulated here on February 13, 1861—one of the final acts of Italian unification. The Cattedrale (SS. Erasmo e Marciano) has an Arab-Norman campanile and a baroque crypt from 1620 with frescoes by Giacinto Brandi. The church of the Annunziata (1321, Gothic façade) houses the Cappelletta d'Oro, where Pius IX and John Paul II both prayed. The church of San Giovanni a Mare summarizes the architectural influences of the 'city of a hundred churches' with its Arab-inspired dome.

Montagna Spaccata and Grotta del Turco — On Monte Orlando (Riviera di Ulisse regional park). The Santuario della SS. Trinità (11th century, founded with a Benedictine convent) is built at the edge of three deep fissures in the limestone cliff: legend says they opened at Christ's death. Descending the main fissure, you see the 'Mano del Turco'—a handprint in the rock, linked to a legend of a skeptical sailor's conversion whose hand sank into the stone. Saint Philip Neri is said to have lived here; his stone 'bed' is visible. To the left of the church, a 300-step stairway descends to the Grotta del Turco, a sea cave with emerald waters (access sometimes closed for safety). At the summit of Monte Orlando: the Mausoleo di Lucio Munazio Planco, circular tomb of a general of Julius Caesar who died in Gaeta in 22 BC.

activities.text: Get lost in the medieval quarter of Sant'Erasmo: alleys, towers, stone staircases, 12th-13th century constructions. Lunch of tiella (octopus and escarole pie, eaten with hands) and Gaeta olives. Stroll along the lungomare Giovanni Caboto.

Descend into the Montagna Spaccata fissure, search for the Mano del Turco. If open, descend the 300 steps to the Grotta del Turco and its turquoise reflections. Climb to Plancus's Mausoleum for the panorama over the gulf.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast
Ulysses Riviera: Secret Gardens, Ancient Caves & Southern Lazio Islands

places.name: Gaiole In Chianti

visits.text: Greve in Chianti - Piazza Matteotti - Considered the gateway to Chianti from Florence, Greve has been inhabited since Etruscan times. Its triangular square, bordered by asymmetrical arcades that testify to different owners over the centuries, served as the central market for the entire region since the Middle Ages. The Castello di Verrazzano, visible in the surroundings, is linked to the memory of Giovanni da Verrazzano, navigator born here around 1485 who explored the east coast of North America in 1524 and discovered the bay now occupied by New York.

Castellina in Chianti - Via delle Volte - Perched on its hill at the historic border between Florence and Siena, Castellina possesses a 15th-century medieval fortress. Its Via delle Volte, a vaulted medieval corridor carved into the ramparts for about 150 meters, served as a defensive passage and interior route. The archaeological museum of Chianti preserves Etruscan objects from the 7th century BC found in the tumulus of Montecalvario.

Radda in Chianti - Former capital of the Chianti League - Radda in Chianti was the capital of the Chianti League, a military organization founded in the 12th century to defend the region against incursions from Siena. The League's emblem, the Gallo Nero (black rooster), is today the official label of Chianti Classico DOCG. The Palazzo del Podestà, whose facade is covered with coats of arms of successive governors, dominates the main square.

Gaiole in Chianti - Birthplace of Chianti Classico - For centuries, Gaiole was the supply market for the surrounding castles. The Castello di Brolio, property of the Ricasoli family since the 12th century, is Italy's oldest winery still in operation. It was Bettino Ricasoli, future Prime Minister of Italy, who codified the recipe for Chianti Classico in 1872.

activities.text: Panoramic drive on SR222 Chiantigiana - The Strada Chiantigiana (SR222) connects Florence to Siena over 69 kilometers through the Chianti hills. Winding between Sangiovese vineyards, cypress trees, and sleeping castles, it traverses a territory shaped by the centuries-old rivalry between Florence and Siena.

Chianti Classico DOCG tasting at a winery - The cellars around Gaiole (Castello di Brolio, Castello di Ama, Badia a Coltibuono) offer tours and tastings. Chianti Classico is a DOCG requiring a minimum of 80% Sangiovese.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages

map_keywords: Via delle Volte Castellina in Chianti
Palazzo del Podestà Radda in Chianti
Castello di Brolio Gaiole in Chianti

places.name: Gallipoli

visits.text: Santa Maria di Leuca — cape viewpoint and basilica: the southernmost point of Puglia, where the Adriatic meets the Ionian Sea, is crowned by the Basilica of Santa Maria de Finibus Terrae. The Apulian Aqueduct, 524 kilometers long, ends here with an artificial cascade pouring into the sea from the basilica's terrace. Many pilgrims arrive on foot from cities as distant as Lecce or Taranto.

Gallipoli — old town on limestone island: the city's name derives from Greek Kallè Polis ("beautiful city"). The historic center occupies a limestone islet connected to the mainland by a 17th-century bridge. Thanks to its port, Gallipoli became from the late 16th century onward the leading olive oil export center in the entire Mediterranean, the oil serving to fuel lamps throughout Europe. The baroque palaces of Via Antonietta De Pace testify to this wealth.

Gallipoli — Aragonese castle and Greek fountain: at the bridge entrance, the square-plan castle with its four corner towers was remodeled many times from its Byzantine construction onward, through Aragonese modifications of the 15th century and the addition of a fifth circular tower on the sea side in the 16th century. The Fontana Greca, on Piazza Alda Moro between the island and the Borgo, is considered the oldest monumental fountain in Italy still preserved in place, dating to the Greco-Roman period and remodeled in 1560.

Gallipoli — Cathedral of Sant'Agata: built in the 17th century of Lecce stone (local carparo), the building is the most representative example of Salento baroque in Gallipoli. The interior holds twelve baroque altars, a coffered ceiling in gilded wood, and canvases by Giovanni Andrea Coppola, a local painter trained in Naples, as well as works by Neapolitans Nicola and Giovanni Malinconico. The polychrome marble altar of the presbytery is the work of Cosimo Fanzago of Bergamo.

Porto Cesareo — marine reserve and torre: this small fishing village is surrounded by a marine reserve since 1997. The 16th-century Aragonese tower, built on an islet facing the harbor, is accessible by boat. The shallow waters of the bay are among the clearest on the Ionian coast.

activities.text: Sunset from Spiaggia della Purità: this town beach runs directly along the old town ramparts on the Ionian side. It's the hour when fishermen ready their blue boats in the old port for the next morning's outing.

Beach day — Punta Prosciutto and Pescoluse: Punta Prosciutto, 15 km south of Gallipoli, is a long white sand beach bordered by pine groves, with shallow waters particularly suited to swimming. Pescoluse (Marina di Morciano), nicknamed the "Maldives of Salento" by Italian guides for its sandbars emerging in turquoise waters, lies 35 km south.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley
Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

map_keywords: Santuario Santa Maria de Finibus Terrae Castrignano del Capo
Riserva Marina Porto Cesareo Porto Cesareo

visits.text: Garessio spills across four distinct quarters - Borgo Maggiore, Ponte, Poggiolo, and Valsorda - divided by the rushing Tanaro River. Enter the medieval center through Porta Rose, the ancient main gateway where legend tells of a lady who fired a fatal arrow from the battlements at an enemy knight during the sixteenth-century Spanish Wars. The partially medieval tower crowns Piazza San Giovanni, where the Confraternity church displays a monumental painting gifted by designer Giorgetto Giugiaro - a native son - depicting the Deposition of Christ. This ritual, the Mortorio, written in 1433 and performed every four years, ranks among Piedmont's oldest sacred reenactments.

In Borgo Ponte, the parish church of Santa Caterina, built between 1723 and 1740 by architect Francesco Gallo, presents a terracotta facade animated by four empty niches - a remarkable example of late Piedmontese baroque. Beyond the stone bridge spanning Rio Mauro, the church of Santa Maria Extra Moenia has stood since the eleventh century; it's one of the region's oldest buildings. The town hall houses a pinacotheca and the Libro della Catena, a tenth-century legal text bound by a chain to the judge's seat and consulted during trials - an exceptionally well-preserved document.

Five kilometers south in the Casotto valley, the Charterhouse of Santa Maria del Casotto was founded in 1183 through donations from local lords. Pillaged under Napoleon, it was purchased by the House of Savoy in 1847 and transformed into a hunting lodge for Victor Emmanuel II. Now owned by the Piedmont Region and part of the UNESCO Royal Residences circuit since 1997, it can be visited with its original furnishings intact.

activities.text: Hike to the Gray caves and visit Garessio's geo-speleological museum, which displays fossils and prehistoric tools attesting to human occupation in the region for millennia, including Ursus spelaeus bones recovered from local caves.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: Genoa's historic center covers 113 hectares and forms one of Europe's most extensive medieval ensembles. Its caruggi—narrow, often shadowy lanes that cut through the urban fabric like fissures—open onto unexpected squares and ornate façades. The entire area was designated a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2006. The cathedral of San Lorenzo, built between the 12th and 16th centuries, displays a façade with alternating bands of black-and-white marble characteristic of Ligurian Romanesque-Gothic style.

Via Garibaldi—formerly Strada Nuova, laid out in the 16th century—gathers the Palazzi dei Rolli, 42 Renaissance and Baroque palaces inscribed on the UNESCO list. In 1576, the Senate of the Republic of Genoa decreed a roster (the 'Rollo') designating aristocratic families obliged to host visiting dignitaries in their homes—a system unique in Europe, a kind of royal palace dispersed across private residences. Peter Paul Rubens, who stayed in Genoa between 1604 and 1606, was so impressed he published a compendium of plans and elevations of 31 of these palaces to spread the model in Flanders.

The Porto Antico, redesigned by architect Renzo Piano for the 1992 International Exposition, transformed old port warehouses into a cultural and leisure waterfront. The Bigo—a spidery metal structure with a panoramic cabin 40 meters high—evokes the historic port's cranes. The Genoa Aquarium, one of Europe's largest with over 12,000 marine animals, occupies a building designed by Renzo Piano.

activities.text: Savor focaccia alla genovese and pesto alla genovese in a caruggi trattoria. Genoese pesto—Ligurian DOP basil, garlic, pine nuts, Parmesan, pecorino, and olive oil—has been protected by European geographical indication since 2023. Genoese focaccia, thin and drizzled with olive oil and salt, differs markedly from its regional variants.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome

visits.text: Gerace—a Byzantine village perched at 480 meters on a rocky spur inaccessible on three sides, founded in the 9th century by inhabitants of Locri fleeing Saracen raids. The nearly intact medieval core preserves 23 documented churches, noble palaces, and stone lanes for a current population of 2,700.

Cattedrale di Gerace—the largest Norman cathedral in Calabria, built by Roger I between 1045 and 1060 on the foundations of an earlier Greek temple, then enlarged in the 13th century. At 63 meters long, it rests on 26 ancient columns of granite and cipolin marble from Locri Epizefiri. The 11th-century three-nave crypt is one of the best preserved in southern Italy.

Locri Epizefiri—ruins of the Greek colony founded around 680 BCE on the Ionian coast, the first Greek city to adopt a written code of laws with the Laws of Zaleucus (around 650 BCE). The 230-hectare archaeological site includes temples, a 4th-century theater, a necropolis, and a museum displaying ceramics, coins, and votive offerings.

activities.text: Wandering through Gerace's medieval lanes between half-restored noble palaces and gardens suspended on the rocky spur.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

places.name: Giglio Castello

visits.text: Giglio Castello, a fortified medieval village at 400 meters elevation in the island's center, is surrounded by 13th-century walls whose certain sections exceed 10 meters in height. Giglio Island, whose name derives not from the "lily" (giglio) but from the Greek aigulion ("goat"), was given by Charlemagne to a Roman abbey in 805 before passing to the Aldobrandeschi, the Pisans, the Orsini, the Piccolomini, then to Eleanor of Toledo, wife of Cosimo I, in 1558.

The Rocca Pisana (also called Rocca Aldobrandesca), a fortress built by the Pisans in the 12th century at the village's highest point, was designed to withstand long sieges: inside were a deep cistern, a vegetable garden, food stores, a poultry yard, and a dovecote, all ensuring self-sufficiency in case of attack. It communicated with the coast by fire and smoke signals.

The church of San Pietro Apostolo (15th century), in the alleys of Giglio Castello, houses the relics of San Mamiliano, patron saint of the island whose cult is one of the oldest and most vibrant in the Tuscan archipelago, as well as a gold chalice offered by Pope Innocent XIII. A Corinthian capital from the Roman villa of Giglio Porto is embedded in the walls.

Giglio Porto, the landing point for ferries from Porto Santo Stefano (crossing about 1 hour), retains the atmosphere of an authentic fishing port with its colorful houses and the medieval Torre del Saraceno. In the Cala del Saraceno behind the Medici tower, the Cetariae—fish-farming basins carved into rock by the Romans in the 1st century AD for the Domizi Enobarbi family—are visible in the port's clear waters.

activities.text: Ferry crossing from Porto Santo Stefano to Isola del Giglio. The crossing takes about an hour and offers views of Monte Argentario, the Tuscan archipelago, and on clear days, Corsica and Montecristo Island.

Hiking trail Giglio Porto - Giglio Castello: a 45-minute climb on a stone-paved path traversing Mediterranean scrub (garrigue, mastic, strawberry trees). The view over the coast and archipelago widens as you ascend. The same trail continues to Giglio Campese (west coast) with its large sandy beach and the 18th-century Torre di Campese—last remnant of a pirate attack in 1799.

Campese beach, the largest beach in the Tuscan archipelago, in an open bay on the island's west side. Fine sand, clear waters, sunset facing the sea. In late afternoon, the same ferries return to Porto Santo Stefano.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Pietro Apostolo Giglio Castello
Spiaggia del Campese Isola del Giglio

visits.text: Piazza della Transalpina - symbolic square where the last wall between East and West Europe fell
Former wall route - line of bricks on the ground marking the old border, like in Berlin
Stazione Transalpina - railway station on Slovenian territory, symbol of the city's division after 1947

activities.text: Discovery of the history of Gorizia, a city cut in two by the Treaty of Paris after World War II
Visit to the preserved fragment of the wall that separated the city until Slovenia's entry into the EU
Reflection on the symbol of a united Europe and the end of the Iron Curtain

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

visits.text: Rocca Malatestiana — State museum — A 12th-century medieval fortress with a square plan and a powerful polygonal torrione to the northeast, reached by drawbridge. The castle passed from the Malatesta to the Sforza and then the Della Rovere families, and is now a State museum with 14 rooms furnished with antiques and works of art. Key highlights: the Camera di Francesca — with the lectern that held the 'galeotto' book (the love story of Lancelot and Guinevere that the two lovers were reading when discovered), and the trapdoor through which Paolo tried in vain to flee (his cloak caught on a nail); the Camerino di Lucrezia Borgia, covered in frescoes attributed to Amico Aspertini depicting Lucrezia as the goddess Fortuna; the Sala della Giustizia with the altarpiece by Giovanni Santi (Raphael's father), Madonna in Trono con Bambino e Santi; and the chapel with an Andrea della Robbia glazed terracotta from the 1480s. The rampart walkways are open to visitors and offer views over the hills and Adriatic coast.

The legend of Paolo and Francesca — Francesca da Polenta, daughter of the lord of Ravenna, was promised to Gianciotto Malatesta (known as 'lo zoppo' — the lame). To avoid a possible refusal, the Malatesta sent his brother Paolo (known as 'il Bello') as proxy suitor. Love blossomed between Francesca and Paolo. Gianciotto surprised and killed them both — probably in September 1289, though some scholars date the events between 1283 and 1285. Dante immortalises them in Canto V of the Inferno among the lustful, elevating their story to a universal symbol of pure love. Boccaccio, Petrarch, Silvio Pellico and D'Annunzio later made their own works from this tragedy.

Medieval village and Torre dell'Orologio — the borgo spreads between two rings of walls over roughly 800 metres of perimeter. Corso Umberto I runs through the village from the Torre dell'Orologio to the rocca, lined with craft shops, restaurants and inns. No cars pass inside the walls. The village was voted 'Borgo dei Borghi' in 2018.

Museo Storico and Grotte — beneath the village, a network of 16 caves carved into the rock for more than 1,500 years — probably used for secret gatherings and as Byzantine places of worship under the influence of nearby Ravenna. Ten are accessible, one is included in the museum visit. Exhibition of torture instruments, medieval weapons and objects of daily life.

activities.text: Guided tour of the Rocca Malatestiana — a theatrical journey through 14 furnished rooms following the story of Paolo and Francesca. The guided visit lasts approximately 1h30 and includes the rampart walkways. Entry: €11 (mastio and walkway access). Advance booking recommended in high season.

Passeggiata degli Innamorati — circular path — a 3-kilometre walk that circles the entire village outside the walls, following the old rampart paths. Named after Paolo and Francesca. The loop starts and ends at the Torre dell'Orologio, with continuous views over the Marchigian hills.

Assedio al Castello (summer — August) — re-enactment of the 43-day siege of 1446, during which Francesco Sforza, allied with Federico da Montefeltro, deployed cannons, bombards and schioppi around the rocca before being forced to withdraw. Period costumes, historical procession, drums, jugglers. The village transforms into a medieval encampment.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

visits.text: Grado - island connected to the mainland by a thin artificial strip over the lagoon, former port of Aquileia
Historic center of Grado - maze of Venetian calli bearing witness to ties with the Serenissima
Basilica of Sant'Eufemia - Romanesque basilica at the heart of the historic center

activities.text: Tasting of boreto - traditional fish soup from Grado in downtown restaurants
Boat excursion to the Sanctuary of Barbana - Marian sanctuary on a lagoon island 25 minutes away
Walk along the lungomare and fine sand beaches, ideal for families
Discovery of the Grado thermal baths, excellence of the local tourist offering

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

places.name: Grazie Mantoue

visits.text: Enter the basilica of the Sanctuary of the Beata Vergine delle Grazie, built between 1399 and 1406 by Francesco I Gonzaga, Captain of Mantua, as an ex-voto for the end of a plague epidemic. The architecture is austere Lombard Gothic with a single nave. Inside, however, the density is staggering: a wooden gallery carved in 1517 by Brother Francesco da Acquanegra runs along all the walls, its niches holding some sixty statues in wax, papier-mâché, and wood—illustrious figures who obtained graces, miraculous pilgrims, and ex-votos of every sort. From the ceiling, suspended among Murano glass chandeliers, hangs a stuffed crocodile: several legends circulate about its origin, including one about a reptile pulled from the Mincio by fishermen.

Seek out the tomb of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478-1529), author of The Courtier, in the first chapel on the right—designed by Giulio Romano. Also observe, on the outer portico facade, commemorative plaques from the Battle of Curtatone on May 29, 1848, where a handful of Tuscan volunteers resisted Marshal Radetzky's army for seven hours before surrendering when ammunition ran out. Pope Pius II and Emperor Charles V numbered among the illustrious visitors whose statues adorn the interior.

Stroll through the hamlet of Grazie di Curtatone, part of the Curtatone municipality 9 km from Mantua. Its lanes open onto a vast esplanade facing the basilica, which comes alive each August 15th for the Fiera delle Grazie—a fair instituted in 1425 by Marquis Gian Francesco Gonzaga—and for the Raduno Nazionale dei Madonnari, a gathering of street artists who cover the asphalt with large sacred reproductions in chalk. Pope John Paul II visited the sanctuary on June 22, 1991.

activities.text: Explore the marshes and reedbeds of the Mincio around the sanctuary: a secret passage to the right of the portico provides access to the natural panorama where the Mincio begins widening to form the three lakes surrounding Mantua. In July and August, the water surface becomes covered with lotus flowers. The area forms part of the Mincio Park.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

map_keywords: Santuario della Beata Vergine delle Grazie Curtatone
Riserva Naturale Valli del Mincio Mantova

places.name: Grazzano Visconti

visits.text: Explore the Castle and Historic Park of Grazzano Visconti, the most precious part of this unique village. The 14th-century castle, completely restored by Giuseppe Visconti di Modrone in eclectic style, offers guided tours through the courtyard, the richly furnished reception rooms, and the bedrooms on the first floor.

Wander through the Castle Park, 15 hectares of English and Italian gardens designed by Giuseppe Visconti. Statues, vases, columns, fountains, and an extraordinary variety of trees and shrubs create a fairytale atmosphere, completed by the delightful little girls' cottage that seems straight out of a storybook.

Visit the Wax Museum with statues of figures connected to Piacenza's history, and the Torture Museum in the Palazzetto dell'Istituzione, small but complete, with torture instruments accompanied by explanatory panels. Adjacent is an Escape Room themed around the Inquisition.

Admire the Parish Church of Saints Cosmas and Damian, the oldest building in the village with 17th-century renovations. Inside, the Chapel of Our Lady of Lourdes houses numerous ex-votos, testimony to popular devotion through the centuries.

activities.text: Lose yourself in the white lanes of the village, admiring the cottages with frescoed façades, the arcades, and the statues. In the Cortevecchia you'll find the statue of Aloisa, the famous ghost of Grazzano Visconti, and a small museum of agricultural tools under the long portico.

Explore the numerous craft shops and souvenir stores, open in every season on weekends. This rare example of revivalist architecture, recognized as a City of Art by the Emilia Romagna Region in 1986, will make you feel catapulted back in time 700 years.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles of the Piacenza Hills: A 4-Day Medieval Road Trip Through Northern Italy

map_keywords: Castello di Grazzano Visconti Grazzano Visconti
Chiesa dei Santi Cosma e Damiano Grazzano Visconti

visits.text: Valle del Lys (Gressoney Valley) was colonized in the 13th century by the Walser, a Germanic population originating from the Valais canton in Switzerland. Departing from Zermatt via the Theodul Pass (3,317 m) to the west and Monte Moro (2,984 m) to the east, they settled in a then-inhospitable region in exchange for rights and freedoms granted by local feudal lords. They still speak titsch (in Gressoney) and töitschu (in Issime) today, Alemannic dialects close to German—in Issime, titsch is still taught in primary school.

Castel Savoia in Gressoney-Saint-Jean was built between 1899 and 1904 by Queen Margherita of Savoy, wife of King Umberto I, who particularly appreciated this valley for her summer stays. Its neo-Gothic towers and English gardens contrast with the alpine landscape. The queen received botanists and alpinists here. Period furniture is largely preserved.

The hamlet of Alpenzu (1,776 m), above Gressoney-La-Trinité, is one of the best-preserved Walser villages in the Alps. Its stadel—larch granaries on stilts—date from the 16th-17th centuries. The Walzer Weg (Walser Path) links Gressoney-Saint-Jean to Alpenzu crossing alpine pastures and larch forests. The Walser Kulturzentrum in Gressoney-Saint-Jean documents the architecture, traditional costume, and language of this community.

activities.text: Hike the Walzer Weg from Gressoney-Saint-Jean to Alpenzu: ascent through alpine pastures, view of the Monte Rosa glacier. Duration about 3h round trip.
In summer, the cable car from Gressoney-La-Trinité climbs to 3,200 meters on the Monte Rosa massif, offering direct access to glaciers and high-mountain routes.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

places.name: Greve In Chianti

visits.text: Piazza Matteotti in Greve in Chianti has been the commercial center of the vineyard for five centuries. Its atypical triangular shape—surrounded by arcades under which merchants displayed wines and oils—is crossed by the Via Chiantigiana, the only road between Florence and Siena until the 18th century. At the center stands a statue of Giovanni da Verrazzano, born in 1485 at the nearby Castello di Verrazzano, who discovered New York Bay in 1524—his name is preserved in the Verrazano-Narrows Bridge between Brooklyn and Staten Island. In 1716, Cosimo III de' Medici officially delimited the Chianti production zone here, in the first European legal act delimiting a wine appellation.

The medieval village of Montefioralle, 2 km from Greve, is classified among Italy's Most Beautiful Villages. Still enclosed by its medieval walls, it preserves a compact plan of stone houses and streets virtually unchanged since the 13th century. The family of Amerigo Vespucci, the Florentine navigator who gave his name to America, originated from this village.

activities.text: Tasting of Chianti Classico DOCG in cellars of hillside estates. In 1906, major landowners founded the Chianti Wine Union here, precursor to today's Consorzio. The Gallo Nero (Black Rooster) label, emblem of the town, certifies the strictly local origin of the wines. The Chianti Classico Expo, every second weekend of September, brings together about sixty producers in the main square.

The Abbey of Badia a Passignano, 8 km northwest of Greve, is a Vallombrosian abbey founded in the 11th century. The Antinori group vinifies Chianti Classico Riserva Badia a Passignano in its medieval cellars, and produces its Tignanello nearby—one of the first Super Tuscans. Estate visits and tastings can be reserved.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Borgo di Montefioralle Greve in Chianti
Museo del Vino Greve in Chianti

visits.text: Rocchetta Mattei - an eclectic castle built from 1850 onward by Count Cesare Mattei (1809-1896) atop the ruins of a medieval fortress at 370 m elevation. Mattei, inventor of electrohomeopathy (an alternative medicine he marketed worldwide), financed the construction of this residence-laboratory for 46 years. The architecture blends medieval Gothic, Moorish style (domes, horseshoe arches, Hispano-Moorish ceramics), and Renaissance elements in a labyrinth of 60 rooms.

Museo Giorgio Morandi a Grizzana - the Bolognese painter Giorgio Morandi (1890-1964), famous for his still lifes of bottles and carafes, spent every summer in Grizzana from 1913 to 1964. The municipal museum presents reproductions of his works and documents his relationship with the Bolognese Apennine landscape that influenced his earthy color palette.

activities.text: Take a guided tour of Rocchetta Mattei—the route winds through decorated halls, the Moorish room with Spanish ceramics, the neo-Gothic chapel, and terraces with views over the Reno valley.
Hike the ridge trails around Grizzana with views across the Reno and Setta valleys—the wooded hillscape that inspired Morandi for half a century.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

places.name: Grottammare

visits.text: Piazza Peretti — the beating heart of the borgo antico, framed by the town hall, the Chiesa di San Giovanni Battista and the Teatro dell'Arancio. From the theatre's loggia, the view sweeps all the way down to the Riviera delle Palme.

Teatro dell'Arancio — built in the final decade of the 18th century by Ticinese architect Pietro Maggi, this intimate theatre takes its name from an orange tree that once stood in the square, whose care was entrusted each year to a local family in exchange for a tax exemption. During the Spanish flu epidemic (1918–1920), with no wood available for coffins, the interior woodwork was stripped out entirely. Restored in 2003, it now houses the Gianni Ottaviani museum space.

Torrione della Battaglia — a 16th-century fortification tower commanding sweeping views of the coastline. Its rooms hold 250 works by local sculptor Pericle Fazzini (1913–1987), the man behind the monumental silver Resurrection in the Vatican's Nervi Hall.

Museo Sistino — housed inside the Chiesa di San Giovanni Battista on Piazza Peretti, it brings together sacred objects from the village's churches, including a chalice donated by Pope Sixtus V, who was born in Grottammare in 1521.

Resti del Castello — at the hilltop, the ruins of a fortress built between the 8th and 9th centuries by the monks of Farfa as a defence against Saracen raids. The surrounding park offers the highest viewpoint over the sea.

activities.text: Wander the alleyways of the borgo antico between brick houses and orange trees, following two marked trails: one tracing the footsteps of Pope Sixtus V, the other leading to Fazzini's sculptures scattered throughout the village.
Taste the brodetto alla sambenedettese — the iconic fish soup of the Picene coast — and the frustingo, a Christmas cake made with dried figs, walnuts, raisins and cooked wine.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Giovanni Battista Grottammare

visits.text: Discover the Grotte di Caglieron, where wooden walkways lead through a series of caves with countless waterfalls streaming down rocky gorge sides. This area of extraordinary natural beauty combines geological wonders with human history stretching back centuries.

Explore the unique cheese caves where a local dairy company matures cheese in the natural cool humidity. You can purchase slabs of this cave-aged cheese, adding a delicious souvenir to your nature adventure.

activities.text: Hike the trails through this remarkable gorge where water, rock, and time have created a magical landscape. The combination of natural caves and quarried grottos offers insights into both geological and human history.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

places.name: Guardiagrele

visits.text: Guardiagrele - a town of 9,000 inhabitants at 576 meters, at the eastern foot of Majella, renowned since the Middle Ages for its goldsmiths and ironworkers. Nicola da Guardiagrele (c. 1385-1462), a goldsmith considered one of the greatest 15th-century Italian artists, was born here. His repoussé silver works are preserved in museums in Chieti, L'Aquila, and the Vatican.

Collegiata di Santa Maria Maggiore - a 12th-century Romanesque cathedral with a 14th-century Gothic portal attributed to the school of Nicola da Guardiagrele. The 42-meter octagonal bell tower dominates the historic center. Preserves a silver polyptych by Nicola da Guardiagrele (1434).

activities.text: Discover the Presentosa in goldsmith workshops - this traditional gold or silver filigree jewel, circular and adorned with geometric motifs, is Abruzzo's emblematic jewelry piece, offered to women to ward off the evil eye.
Tasting of Sise delle Monache - a three-humped pastry filled with ricotta cream and chocolate, a specialty invented by Clarissan nuns from the local convent in the 18th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Maggiore Guardiagrele

visits.text: Guarene dominates the right bank of the Tanaro from a strategic position that allowed the Radicati to establish a fortress here in the Middle Ages. In the eighteenth century, Count Carlo Giacinto Roero di Guarene - friend and student of court architect Filippo Juvarra - decided to transform the fortress into a prestige residence. Work began on September 13, 1726, the date and hour recorded in the archives. The building, 25 meters high over three levels and surrounded by Italian gardens begun in 1740, is a rare example of Piedmontese baroque commissioned by an aristocrat who was himself an amateur architect.

In 1773, King Victor Amadeus III of Sardinia and the Queen visited the castle, leaving traces in the furnishings of several rooms. From the terraces, the view embraces 60 kilometers of UNESCO-classified territory - from the Langhe of Barolo to Barbaresco, the Monferrato, and the Alpine chain from the plain of Alba and beyond. Today converted into a luxury hotel and listed as a historic monument since 2011, the castle preserves all its period furniture, tapestries, and family portraits of the Roero.

On the hill of San Licerio, the Parco d'Arte presents works by Italian and international artists installed among Nebbiolo vines and woods. Born in 1975 from a local painting competition, the collection has grown to constitute a unique free open-air museum in the Roero.
