activities.text: Tasting of Roero Arneis - Piedmont's great dry white - and Roero Rosso made from Nebbiolo in local cellars. Walk through the Roero vineyards along the "Rocche," spectacular sandy cliffs carved by erosion that characterize this north side of the Tanaro.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

places.name: Imperia

visits.text: Climb to the Parasio, the medieval promontory of Porto Maurizio, via the spiral road or the elevator from sea level. This quarter constitutes the original nucleus of Imperia: for centuries Porto Maurizio was an autonomous port and the Mediterranean's leading center for olive oil production and trade, under the protection of the Republic of Genoa. Only in 1923 did a government decree merge it with Oneglia—its Piedmontese rival separated by 3 kilometers—to form the municipality of Imperia. Inhabitants of the two towns still call each other 'ciantafourche' and 'cacelotti,' nicknames inherited from centuries of rivalry.

Enter the Basilica of San Maurizio, the largest church in Liguria, built between 1781 and 1838 on the site of an earlier chapel. Its neoclassical facade dominates the square at the Parasio's exit. Inside: frescoes, paintings, and 19th-century sculptures. On the adjacent Via Santa Caterina, visit the birthplace of San Leonardo da Porto Maurizio, born in 1676 in the Parasio and canonized in the 19th century: the house converted into a museum preserves his habit, his hair shirt, and the funeral cast of his face.

From the Logge di Santa Chiara, the panoramic loggias of the Convent of the Sisters of Santa Chiara atop the Parasio, the view embraces the Ligurian coast on both sides. Nearby, the Tower of Prarola rises directly from the sea west of the historic center: this cylindrical watchtower was built to monitor Saracen pirate raids, a constant threat on this coast until the 17th century.

Visit the Museo dell'Olivo Carlo Carli in Oneglia, housed in a Liberty-style palazzo and spread across eighteen rooms. The Carli brothers, olive oil industrialists, assembled a collection tracing olive cultivation techniques from Ancient Greece to the 20th century: Roman amphorae, parts of animal-drawn or hydraulic mills, documents on cosmetic and medicinal uses of oil. A reconstruction of a Roman ship's hold and a 17th-century press stand in the courtyard. The Taggiasca olive, cultivated in these hills since its Benedictine introduction in the 7th century, was the source of the entire region's historic wealth.

Visit the Basilica of San Giovanni Battista in Oneglia, consecrated in 1762 by architect Gaetano Amoretti and elevated to basilica status in 2017. This late Genoese Baroque building houses polychrome marbles, frescoes, and an 1874 three-keyboard organ. For centuries Oneglia was the main port of Piedmont under the Savoys, rival to Porto Maurizio which answered to Genoa—a rivalry so intense that in 1792, a French fleet bombarded Oneglia without touching neutral Porto Maurizio.

Stroll through Villa Grock, commissioned in the 1920s by Adrien Wettach—known as Grock—one of Europe's most famous clowns, born in Switzerland in 1880. The villa mixes Art Nouveau, references to the circus world, and elements recalling Dalí, Picasso, and Gaudí. It now serves as a museum of clowning and an architectural monument.

activities.text: Walk the Passeggiata degli Innamorati, the path that follows the cliffs at the base of the Parasio and connects Porto Maurizio to Borgo Marina. This promenade suspended between the medieval quarter and the sea offers the best views of the coast, particularly at sunset.

Sketching or photography session by Porto Maurizio's harbor, equipped with a long protective pier whose lighthouse provides the best viewpoint over the entire coast. Since 1986, Imperia has periodically hosted the Vele d'Epoca, a gathering of historic sailboats over 20 meters arriving from around the world.

Wander beneath the arcades of Piazza Dante in Oneglia: this lively square is Oneglia's commercial and social heart, surrounded by cafes and boutiques. Wednesday mornings host the local producers' market—Taggiasca olives, olive oil, vegetables, and cured meats from the hinterland.

Bike or walk a section of the Riviera dei Fiori cycle path between Imperia and San Lorenzo al Mare: the Imperia section, inaugurated in 2025, connects the two parts of the city and links to the existing route toward Sanremo. This stretch passes converted former stations and follows the sea for nearly the entire route.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days
Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days

map_keywords: Basilica di San Giovanni Battista Oneglia

visits.text: The Basilica-Sanctuary of Santa Maria all'Impruneta was consecrated on January 3, 1060, on the site of an Etruscan place of worship. Its foundation rests on the legend of the discovery of an icon of the Virgin during construction: the oxen carrying the materials knelt at a precise point, indicating the site of the sanctuary. This Madonna—a 12th-century painted panel—has since been the principal Marian devotion of Tuscany. In 1711, she was proclaimed co-patron of Florence. The left chapel preserves a Madonna and Child by Giovanni Pisano (1317); the Treasury Museum houses a cross attributed to Ghiberti and illuminated choir books from the 14th-16th centuries.

Impruneta has been the world's principal center of terracotta production since at least 1098 (first documented mention). The local clay, refractory and extremely durable, was used by Brunelleschi for the tiles of the dome of Santa Maria del Fiore, and by the Della Robbia workshops for their glazed ceramics. The oil jars and garden vases of Impruneta adorn the gardens of Tuscany's greatest villas.

activities.text: Visit an active terracotta workshop in Impruneta: throwing, drying, firing, and glazing according to traditional techniques. Production covers jars, tiles, sculptures, and garden pots—the same ones that adorned the Uffizi and Medici palaces.

The Fair of Saint Luke, October 18 in Impruneta, is one of Tuscany's oldest fairs, attested since the Middle Ages: originally a cattle fair linked to the Madonna procession, it now brings together markets, banquets, and horse races on Piazza Buondelmonti.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

visits.text: Ingria, at 827 meters elevation in Val Soana, counts 46 permanent residents - but groups together some thirty hamlets scattered across the slopes, linked by ancient trails. This commune in Gran Paradiso National Park is one of Canavese's most isolated and best-preserved villages. The uncertain etymology suggests either the Lombard Ingrich, or the Latin ingredire, linked to the Roman bridge spanning the Soana torrent - true gateway to the valley. The local language, Valsoanin, is a variant of Arpitan (Franco-Provençal), still spoken in the hamlets.

The 1719 bell tower, standing opposite the parish church of San Giacomo, is the village symbol. The permanent outdoor exhibition Le case raccontano presents panels with old black-and-white photographs captioned in Arpitan and Italian, along the alleys. The most typical hamlets like Camprovardo (three- to four-story houses connected by exterior wooden staircases) and Pasturera (sixteenth-seventeenth century round-arch stone arcades) are discovered on foot.

activities.text: Hike to Punta Arbella or Monte Betassa from Ingria, in Gran Paradiso National Park - one of Italy's oldest parks (1922), created to protect the ibex after Carlo Felice's 1821 hunting ban. The Soana torrent is suitable for canoeing depending on water levels. In August, Cose d'altri tempi (Tchouse d'auti ten) is a Franco-Provençal festival with music, dance, and ancient crafts.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

places.name: Iseo

visits.text: Monte Isola is the largest inhabited island in European lakes: 5 km², 600 meters at its highest point, about 1,800 inhabitants spread across 11 hamlets. No cars circulate here. The first document mentioning the island dates from 905, when it was property of the monastery of San Salvatore in Brescia. The tradition of fishing net manufacturing has been documented here for over 600 years; the local company La Rete, which revived the activity in the 1970s, has become Europe's leading manufacturer of sports nets. In 2016, artists Christo and Jeanne-Claude installed "The Floating Piers" here, yellow floating walkways connecting the island to the mainland.

The Sanctuary of Madonna della Ceriola stands at the island's highest point, at 600 meters altitude. It's Monte Isola's oldest church, founded in the 13th century. It preserves a 12th-century wood sculpture. The ascent from Peschiera Maraglio by trail requires about 400 meters of elevation gain and offers views over both shores of Lake Iseo.

In Pisogne, at the northern end of Lake Iseo, the church of Santa Maria della Neve houses a fresco cycle painted by Girolamo Romanino between 1532 and 1534: the entirety of the walls and vaults is covered with scenes of Christ's Passion, with sibyls and prophets in imposing poses on the Gothic vaults. Critic Giovanni Testori nicknamed the ensemble the "Sistine Chapel of the poor." The church, built in the late 15th century at the request of Pisogne's inhabitants, is free to enter.

activities.text: The ferry to Monte Isola departs from Sulzano, a few kilometers from Iseo. The crossing takes less than 10 minutes. On the island, the coastal perimeter loop on foot or by bike is about 9 kilometers and takes 2h30 to 3h: you pass through the fishing villages of Sensole, Carzano (where shipyards still build mahogany wooden boats according to a 350-year tradition) and Siviano.

days_plan.itin_title: Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice

map_keywords: Santuario Madonna della Ceriola Monte Isola
Chiesa Santa Maria della Neve Pisogne

places.name: Isernia

visits.text: Museo Nazionale del Paleolitico and Isernia La Pineta site: discovered in 1978 during highway construction. The 300 m² paleosuolo (occupation layer) yielded thousands of bones from ancient elephants, rhinoceros, hippopotamus, bison—hunting meal remnants of Homo heidelbergensis. A child's milk tooth dated to 586,000 years ago is Italy's oldest human remain. The museum (1987, Palladio Architecture Prize) integrates a direct view of ongoing excavations, still active.
Fontana Fraterna: 14th-century medieval fountain at the historic center's heart, partly composed of Roman and early Christian reused materials. Symbol of the city.
Isernia historic center: Piazza Celestino V, San Pietro Apostolo cathedral and medieval quarters. Isernia was an important Samnite capital before becoming a Roman colony in 263 BC.

activities.text: Guided tour of the Isernia La Pineta excavation site under its protective pavilion: archaeologists from the University of Ferrara work there each season. Possible to observe stratigraphic sections and bones in situ.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Cattedrale di San Pietro Apostolo Isernia

places.name: Isola Del Liri

visits.text: The Cascata Grande of Isola del Liri plunges 27 meters right in the town's historic center—a phenomenon unique in Italy. The waterfall is produced by the bifurcation of the Liri River, which splits into two arms around a natural island: the left arm forms the Cascata Grande, while the right feeds the Cascata del Valcatoio (or Gualcatojo), converted in the 19th century into hydroelectric power. A painting of the waterfall by Isabelle Bidault is now preserved in the Louvre; Philipp Hackert also depicted it.

The Boncompagni-Viscogliosi Castle is documented from the 12th century and has served as a noble residence since the year 1000. It sits precisely at the bifurcation of the Liri, atop both waterfalls. Its medieval tower and Renaissance halls are visible from the riverbanks.

The Museum of Paper and Telecommunications Civilization, housed in the former Béranger-Coste paper mill, traces the industrial history of Isola del Liri: in the 19th century, the town was one of Europe's largest paper manufacturing centers. In the early 20th century, it became the first city in the world to produce and distribute hydroelectric power for both public and private use.

activities.text: Walk down the banks of the Liri on both sides of the island to see both waterfalls from water level: the Cascata Grande from the bridge on Via Argine, and the Cascata del Valcatoio from the trail on the right bank. Morning light on the main falls is particularly photogenic between 9 and 11 a.m.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

map_keywords: Cascata del Valcatoio Isola del Liri
Museo della Civiltà della Carta Isola del Liri

visits.text: Riserva Naturale Foce dell'Isonzo - one of Europe's most important wetlands
Isola della Cona - reserve favored by ornithologists with free-roaming white horses from Camargue

activities.text: Observation of migratory and resident birds in the nature reserve
Bike ride along Valle Cavanata on the cycling path
Observation of white horses living freely on the island
Discovery of the varied fauna, both migratory and resident, in this wetland

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

places.name: Itri

visits.text: Monte San Biagio, a small village with labyrinthine alleyways climbing a hill between the Ausoni Mountains. The Church of San Giovanni Battista dominates the village. From Viale Littoria below, the panorama embraces the plain of Fondi, the three lakes, and the coast stretching to the Pontine Islands on clear days.

Castello di Itri, perched on Colle Sant'Angelo, has controlled the Via Appia since the 9th century. The first nucleus—a pentagonal tower—was erected in 882 by Duke Docibilis I of Gaeta. His grandson Marino I added around 950 a second tower, taller and wider. In the 13th century, the Caetani built the residential section and the cylindrical tower called the "Tower of the Crocodile": according to legend, prisoners condemned to death were thrown in as prey to a reptile kept in its waters. The walkway connects the towers and offers views to the coast.

The Church of San Michele Arcangelo, in Itri's upper section, is the village's oldest sacred building, dating back to the 11th century. In Arab-Norman style with three naves, it preserves 14th-century frescoes attributed to the Neapolitan Roberto Oderisio. Its square bell tower, decorated with colored majolica plates, is positioned centrally against the façade.

The Museum of Brigandage recounts the life of Michele Pezza, known as Fra' Diavolo, born in Itri on April 7, 1771. Initially wanted for murder, he became a colonel in the Neapolitan army against Napoleon's troops, using the castle's underground passages to ambush enemies on the Via Appia. Captured in 1806, he was hanged in Naples' Piazza del Mercato at age 35. Auber's opera "Fra Diavolo" (1830) and the Laurel and Hardy film (1933) made his name famous worldwide.

activities.text: Climb to the Sanctuary of Madonna della Civita, at 673 meters altitude on Monte Fusco, 13 km from the village in the Aurunci Mountains Park. According to tradition, two Basilian monks fleeing iconoclasm hid an icon of the Virgin and Child from Constantinople; a deaf-mute shepherd found it on the mountain and recovered his hearing and speech. The first historical document of the sanctuary dates from 1174. The icon, called Madonna Nera, was crowned in 1777 and 1877. From the forecourt, spectacular views of the mountains and the Gulf of Gaeta.

Hike along the Via Appia Antica: in the Gola di Sant'Andrea, between Itri and Fondi, a 2-km section of the ancient route has been excavated and enhanced. The Roman basalt paving winds between opus quadratum constructions and polygonal walls. On the ruins of a 1st-century BC Roman villa stands a fortino used by Fra' Diavolo as a rear base against the French in 1798.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Itri

places.name: La Maddalena

visits.text: The town of La Maddalena, the archipelago's only settlement, preserves in the Church of Santa Maria Maddalena a crucifix and two candlesticks offered by Admiral Nelson in 1804, who called this harbor "the most beautiful in the world" and used it as a fleet base while pursuing the French.

On Caprera Island, the Compendio Garibaldino is the house where Giuseppe Garibaldi spent the last 26 years of his life, from 1856 until his death on June 2, 1882 at 6:21 PM. The bedroom clock remains stopped at that hour. His red shirt, bed, wheelchair, and the rough granite tomb where he's buried are on display.

La Maddalena Archipelago National Park, created in 1994, groups 62 islands and islets along 112 miles of granite coastline. Spiaggia Rosa on Budelli Island, famous for its pink sand made from coral and shell fragments, is now protected and accessible only by sea.

activities.text: Ferry crossing from Palau (15 minutes) to the main island of La Maddalena.

Boat excursion around Spargi, Budelli, and Santa Maria islands, accessible only by sea. Spargi Island preserves the wreck of a 2nd-century BC Roman ship whose cargo of amphorae has been partially recovered.
Swim at Cala Coticcio on Caprera Island, nicknamed "Sardinian Tahiti" for its transparent waters in shades of turquoise and deep green. Access is on foot via a trail or by sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Sardinia Coast: Beaches & Hidden Coves
Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

map_keywords: Chiesa Santa Maria Maddalena La Maddalena

places.name: La Morra

visits.text: Admire the viewpoint at Piazza Castello, the most famous panoramic terrace in the Langhe offering a 360-degree view over vineyards dotted with villages and castles. On clear days, the gaze extends to the Alps, offering one of Piedmont's most breathtaking natural spectacles.

Climb the 31-meter-high Bell Tower, built in 1710, to enjoy an even wider view of the surrounding hills. Near the viewpoint stands the monument to the winegrower, a bronze statue paying tribute to those who work the vines and produce Barolo.

Visit the Church of San Martino, an elegant Baroque building with a brick façade and interiors decorated with precious frescoes. It's one of the most representative churches in the area and houses artworks of great value.

activities.text: Seek out the giant benches of the Big Bench Community Project scattered around La Morra. These colorful installations, created to enhance the landscape of the Langhe, allow you to enjoy spectacular views of the vineyard-covered hills from an unusual and fun perspective.

Join a tasting at the historic cellars of La Morra, one of the most important areas for Barolo DOCG production. Cantina Mascarello, Cantina Marcarini, and Cantina Oddero offer guided tours with tastings and vineyard walks.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Martino La Morra

visits.text: Technical Naval Museum of the Italian Navy - Considered one of the world's oldest naval museums, the Museo Tecnico Navale is housed in the military naval arsenal built in 1869. Across more than 3,000 square meters, it preserves ship models, navigation instruments, 28 figureheads (the oldest dating to 1738), and a section dedicated to Guglielmo Marconi, who conducted his first long-distance wireless communication experiments in the Gulf of La Spezia in 1897.

Castello San Giorgio and Archaeological Museum - Documented from the second half of the 14th century, the Castello San Giorgio houses the Civic Archaeological Museum with collections from Lunigiana from Prehistory to the Middle Ages, notably the famous anthropomorphic statue-menhirs from the Copper and Bronze Ages. The ramparts offer a panorama over the Gulf of La Spezia.

Via del Prione and historic center - The main pedestrian artery of La Spezia's historic center is lined with arcades and 19th-century palaces. The Museo Civico Amedeo Lia, installed in a 17th-century Franciscan convent, preserves a collection spanning the 13th to 18th centuries including works by Tintoretto and Titian.

Harbor promenade and Passeggiata Morin - From the Molo Italia, a panorama extends over the Cinque Terre. The Gulf of La Spezia, nicknamed the 'Gulf of Poets' since Dante, Shelley, and Byron stayed here, is one of the largest protected natural gulfs on the Ligurian coast.

Riomaggiore - Marina and stairway alleys - The southernmost village of the Cinque Terre was reportedly founded in the 8th century by Greek refugees. Its five- to six-story houses, painted in ochre, yellow, and terracotta, are built directly on rock—an ancient technique that allowed sailors to recognize their home from the sea. The Via dell'Amore, a ledge carved into the cliff during the construction of railway tunnels in the 1920s, is partially reopened.

Manarola - Belvedere and postcard view - Manarola is renowned for its production of Sciacchetrà, a sweet, amber-colored white wine obtained by drying the grapes. Its belvedere on the rocky promontory is the most famous photographic viewpoint of the Cinque Terre. The church of San Lorenzo, built in 1338, preserves a medieval rose window and bell. The giant Christmas nativity scene installed on the cliffs every year since 1961 is visible from several kilometers at sea.

Corniglia - Village perched 100 meters above the sea - Corniglia is the only one of the five villages without direct access to the sea. Perched at 100 meters altitude, it is preceded by the Lardarina, a staircase of 382 steps from the station. The 14th-century church of San Pietro is adorned with a rose window in white Carrara marble. Less crowded than its neighbors, Corniglia offers the calmest atmosphere of the Cinque Terre.

Vernazza - Port, Piazza Marconi, and Castello Doria - Vernazza is regularly cited among Italy's most beautiful villages. Its natural port, protected by a medieval breakwater, is the only true mooring harbor of the Cinque Terre. The Castello Doria, built in the 11th century on the rocky ledge, is one of the oldest fortifications of the Cinque Terre and offers the most iconic view of the village and harbor.

Monterosso al Mare - Medieval borgo and Fegina beach - Monterosso is the largest of the five villages, divided into two parts: the medieval borgo (Colle di San Cristoforo) and the beach resort of Fegina, which has the only true sandy beach of the Cinque Terre. The statue called Il Gigante, a 14-meter reinforced concrete sculpture representing Neptune, was erected in 1910. The 14th-century church of San Giovanni Battista in Ligurian Gothic style striped with white and green marble is the largest in the Cinque Terre. The hinterland of Monterosso produces the Monterosso lemon, a local variety used for limoncino.

activities.text: Logistics reconnaissance for the Cinque Terre - La Spezia Centrale station is the main departure point for the Cinque Terre. The regional train serves all five villages in a maximum of 20 minutes. The Cinque Terre Card (train + trail access) is available at the station.

Cinque Terre Card train between villages (south) - The regional train connects La Spezia to Riomaggiore, Manarola, and Corniglia in less than 15 minutes. The Cinque Terre Card allows unlimited boarding.

Hike Manarola → Corniglia (trail 586/506) - The route takes the heights from Manarola, crossing terraced vineyards, holm oak forest, and a pass at 360 meters before descending to Corniglia. Allow 2h30-3h.

Hike Sentiero Azzurro Monterosso → Vernazza - This 3.6 km section is one of the most spectacular and demanding of the Sentiero Azzurro. The trail climbs through terraces of lemon and olive trees, skirts cliffs above the drop, crosses the hamlet of Prevo, then descends toward Vernazza by a steep staircase. Total elevation gain of about 400 meters. Allow 1h30-2h.
Cinque Terre Card train between villages (north) - The train connects Vernazza and Monterosso to La Spezia in 20 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages

Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome
Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome

places.name: La Verna

visits.text: Sanctuary of La Verna — Basilica and Chapel of the Stigmata: Mount Alverne was given to Saint Francis in 1213 by Count Orlando Cattani di Chiusi, who described it as "a solitary and wild place suited to those who wish to do penance." It was here, on September 17, 1224 — two years before his death — that Francis received the stigmata of the Passion, becoming the first stigmatist officially recognized by the Catholic Church. The Chapel of the Stigmata marks the exact spot of the miracle, indicated by a tablet in the floor. The great Basilica dedicated to Santa Maria Assunta was built from 1348 and completed in 1509 thanks to donations from the Senate of Florence. It was raised to the rank of minor basilica by Pope Benedict XV in 1921. Inside, 15th-century glazed terracottas by Andrea della Robbia decorate the chapel and the basilica — including the large Crucifixion altarpiece, one of the master's last works.

Small church of Santa Maria degli Angeli: the original nucleus of the complex, commissioned by Francis himself in 1216. This is the chapel where the eremitic life on the mount began. It is the starting point of the daily 3 PM procession to the Chapel of the Stigmata.

activities.text: Monumental forest of La Verna: protected by the Franciscan brothers for centuries, this forest of ancient beeches and firs includes trees reaching 50 meters in height. Marked trails cross the forest and lead to the summit of Monte Penna (1,283 m), from which the view extends over the entire Casentino and Valtiberina. Deer, wild boar, wolves and numerous bird species inhabit these woods.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria degli Angeli La Verna

places.name: Labro

visits.text: The village of Labro, a "stone borgo," rises at 2,060 feet between Monte Terminillo and Lago di Piediluco. First mentioned in 956 when Emperor Otto I enfeoffed the site to Aldobrandino de' Nobili, it preserves its intact medieval terraced plan: castle and tower at the top, noble palaces in the middle, common houses below. Nearly abandoned in the 1960s, it was the first village in Lazio to be entirely restored, thanks to the initiative of a Belgian architect who used materials and forms faithful to the original. A Bandiera Arancione from the Touring Club Italiano, Labro is also certified among Italy's "most beautiful skies" for stargazing.

Push open the door of Palazzo Nobili Vitelleschi, a manor house founded between the 10th and 11th centuries, still inhabited by descendants of the family. The rooms preserve the complete family archive and refined furnishings. The Chiesa di Santa Maria Maggiore (1508), whose portal was the old castle entrance, crowns the village's highest point with a panorama from Terminillo to Lake Piediluco.

Lago di Piediluco, at 1,230 feet elevation, is considered the most beautiful natural lake in central Italy. Its calm waters, nestled among wooded hills, serve as a basin for national rowing competitions. The small village of Piediluco, with its colorful houses at the water's edge and the ruined medieval Rocca at the summit, is accessible in 5 miles from Labro.

The Cascata delle Marmore, 4 miles from Terni and about 30 minutes from Labro, is Europe's highest man-made waterfall at 541 feet, divided into three cascades. It was created in 271 BC by Roman consul Manius Curius Dentatus, who dug a canal to drain the stagnant waters of Lacus Velinus—the ancient marshy lake of the Rieti plain—into the Nera river below. The water is now regulated by a hydroelectric plant and the falls operate on a precise schedule. Two viewpoints allow observation: the Belvedere Superiore (at the top, level with the falls) and the Belvedere Inferiore (at the bottom, facing the wall of water). A network of trails connects the two viewpoints.

activities.text: Walk along Labro's parapet path, locally called the "Passeggiata dell'Amore" (Lovers' Walk), which follows the medieval walls with plunging views over the Fuscello valley and Lake Piediluco. The Torrione watchtower, at the highest point, offers a 360° panorama.

Hike between the two viewpoints of the Cascata delle Marmore on the park trails. The main route (Sentiero 1) climbs in switchbacks through the humid vegetation to the Balcone degli Innamorati (Lovers' Balcony), a rocky terrace behind the curtain of water.
Boat or kayak tour on Lago di Piediluco, a natural lake fed by the same Velino waters. The wooded shores and the medieval Rocca overlooking the village offer a peaceful setting, far from crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit
Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Maggiore Labro

visits.text: Explore the Park of the Hundred Lakes (Parco dei Cento Laghi), a stunning natural area in the Parma Apennines featuring dozens of glacial lakes scattered across the mountain landscape. Lago Santo Parmense, the largest and most accessible, sits at over 1,500 meters altitude surrounded by forests and peaks, offering a refreshing mountain escape especially welcome on hot summer days.

Hike through the alpine-like scenery where crystal-clear lakes mirror the surrounding peaks and forests. The park encompasses some of the highest terrain in the Parma province, with trails leading to panoramic viewpoints on Mount Aquilotto and other summits offering sweeping vistas over the lake-dotted landscape.

activities.text: Discover the diverse mountain flora and fauna protected within the park boundaries. The area offers exceptional hiking opportunities ranging from gentle lakeside walks to challenging peak ascents, all set within landscapes that feel far removed from the plains of Emilia-Romagna below.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

visits.text: Lago di Suviana and Lago del Brasimone - two artificial lakes created in the 1920s-1930s for hydroelectric power in the Bolognese Apennine valleys. Lake Suviana (capacity 32 million m³) is ringed by beech and conifer forests between 500 and 900 m altitude. Lake Brasimone, smaller, is managed as a nature reserve with restricted access to protect aquatic wildlife.

activities.text: Swimming and boating on Lake Suviana from the Bargi beaches—canoes and pedalos in summer, forest trail hiking in any season.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

places.name: Lago Di Cornino

visits.text: Lago di Cornino - small lake of glacial origin with crystal-clear waters of incredible turquoise
Riserva Naturale del Lago di Cornino - reserve housing a griffon vulture reintroduction project

activities.text: Lake tour on an easy trail suitable for all levels
Observation of grifoni (griffon vultures) in the large aviaries of the visitor center
Discovery of the vulture reintroduction program in natural habitat

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

map_keywords: Lago di Cornino Forgaria nel Friuli

visits.text: Conclude your grand tour at Lake Fimon, the oldest lake in Northern Italy at 35,000 years of age - predating even Lake Garda. This tranquil natural setting offers a peaceful escape for walking, picnicking, or simply contemplating the landscape.

Explore the shores and surrounding Berici Hills, where nature and agriculture create a pastoral landscape within easy reach of Vicenza. The lake's ancient origins add geological interest to its natural beauty.

activities.text: Enjoy a leisurely walk around the lake, perhaps with a picnic. The peaceful atmosphere provides a perfect finale to your exploration of the Veneto's extraordinary diversity of cultural and natural treasures.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: The Teatro del Silenzio is a natural amphitheater created in 2006 at the initiative of Alberto Bartalini and tenor Andrea Bocelli, born in Lajatico on September 22, 1958. The concept: a theater that remains silent 364 days a year and comes alive once each summer for a single concert. Tuscan travertine block terraces surround an artificial basin at whose center a different contemporary artist's sculpture is installed each year. The first edition, July 27, 2006, was dedicated to Bocelli's father. Since then, Placido Domingo, José Carreras, Dua Lipa, and Zubin Mehta have performed here.

The borgo of Lajatico, perched on a spur overlooking the Valdera, was first a castle of the Bishop of Volterra before being conquered by Pisa then Florence. The civic tower, Palazzo Pretorio, and cobbled alleyways form the heart of a village of about 1,000 inhabitants that claims, statistics in hand, to be Italy's municipality with the highest average per-capita income.

activities.text: Hike to the Rocca di Pietracassia, a ruined medieval fortress at 550 meters altitude in the Valdera woods, about 40 minutes' walk from Lajatico. Built in the Lombard era to control the route to Montecatini's copper mines, it was disputed and sold several times before passing definitively to the Pisans in 1434. Its name comes from a fissure (cassia) in a limestone spur.

The mofeta di Borboi, between Lajatico and Orciatico: a geothermal carbon dioxide emission typical of ancient volcanic zones. The stream water appears to boil from gas rising from underground. Best seen between October and May, when the watercourse is fuller.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

visits.text: Nicastro—the historic heart of Lamezia Terme and former capital of the Norman County of Calabria in the 11th century. The Castello Normanno-Svevo, built by Roger I around 1060 on a 320-meter spur, served as Frederick II of Hohenstaufen's residence during his Calabrian visits in the 13th century. The devastating 1638 earthquake—one of Italy's deadliest with 10,000 deaths in the Savuto Valley alone—reduced the castle to ruins that still brood over the valley today.

activities.text: Swimming at Gizzeria Lido beach, 10 km from the airport—a sweep of fine sand along the Tyrrhenian coast, perfect for unwinding after arrival before heading south to Pizzo the next day.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

places.name: Lanciano

visits.text: Lanciano - a town of 35,000 inhabitants whose medieval center is divided into four historic quarters: Civitanova, Lancianovecchia, Sacca, and Borgo. In the Middle Ages, the town was an important commercial crossroads hosting international fairs that attracted Venetian and Oriental merchants.

Santuario del Miracolo Eucaristico - an 8th-century building housing what is considered the first documented Eucharistic miracle in Catholic history, dated 750 AD. A Basilian monk reportedly witnessed the host transform into flesh and wine into blood. The relics are preserved in gilded urns, scientifically examined in 1971: analyses identified human cardiac tissue of blood type AB.
Torri Montanare - two 14th-century medieval towers connected by an arch, remnants of the town's ancient Angevin fortifications. They constitute Lanciano's visual emblem.

Cattedrale della Madonna del Ponte - a baroque cathedral built between 1687 and 1791 on a 1st-century AD Roman bridge with five arches, still visible below. This juxtaposition of two eras is unique in the region.

activities.text: Walk through the Lancianovecchia quarter, the oldest district, with its medieval alleyways, carved portals, and 15th-century palaces.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Cattedrale della Madonna del Ponte Lanciano

places.name: Lanzo Torinese

visits.text: The Ponte del Diavolo of Lanzo Torinese, built from 1378 to 1380 over the Stura di Lanzo, rises 16 meters above the river. Its construction cost 1,400 florins, financed by a wine tax for ten years. It allowed travelers to avoid the hostile territories of the princes of Acaja and Montferrat when reaching Turin. In 1564, a gate was installed on the bridge to block travelers during plague epidemics. Its name comes from a natural cavity 7 meters high and 6 meters at the base carved by waters in the left wall—the Giant's Cauldron—which locals attributed to the devil.

The Civic Tower of Aymon de Challant (1329-1357), 20.50 meters tall, was the gateway to the medieval borough. It bears the name of its builder, castellan of Lanzo from 1332 to 1357 in service to Margaret of Savoy. The church of Loreto was built in 1618 in just three months by the inhabitants, modeled on the Santa Casa of Loreto, at the initiative of Margaret of Savoy. Via San Giovanni Bosco is the old heart of town with its terracotta porticos, ogival windows, and medieval decorations.

The Ecomuseum of the History of Alpinism of the Valli di Lanzo, installed at the headquarters of the Italian Alpine Club of Lanzo, traces the history of these valleys considered the cradle of Piedmontese alpinism. Over 360 marked trails covering 1,000 km constitute the hiking network. The ethnographic museum of textile art is housed in the 17th-century Palazzo degli Estensi.

The Casa del Grissino in Lanzo recalls the invention of the Turin breadstick: physician Pecchio reportedly developed this thin, crispy bread to treat young Vittorio Amedeo II, unable to digest ordinary bread. The Lanzo grissino is distinct from the rubatà grissino of Chieri.

activities.text: Hike to Lago Monastero from Ala di Stura (Val Grande di Lanzo): alpine lake surrounded by rhododendrons, panorama over high-altitude pastures. Ala di Stura preserves medieval buildings and was connected to Turin by the Turin-Ceres railway in 1916.
Via ferrata or mountain safety workshop in the Valli di Lanzo (several equipped sectors available depending on season, inquire with local providers).

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

map_keywords: Torre di Aymone di Challant Lanzo Torinese
Ala di Stura Ala di Stura

places.name: Lari

visits.text: The Castello dei Vicari has dominated Lari since the year 1000—the first documented mention dates to 1043, when land was sold for a sword. After Florence took Lari in 1406, it became the seat of a Florentine vicariate administering almost the entire Tuscan coast from Livorno to Pontedera. The inner courtyard bears 92 ceramic coats of arms of the vicars who governed here for over four centuries. The cistern built in 1448, topped with the Pitti and Scali family arms, sits halfway up the 95 access steps. The complex served as a prison until World War II, damaged by bombing. Open to visitors since 1991.

Grand Duke Leopold II, during his visit, wrote: "I arrived at Lari and from its fortress was shown a vast country, the most beautiful part of Tuscany." The terrace indeed offers a 360° panorama: from the Pisan Mountains to the Balze of Volterra, from the Livorno coast to Peccioli.

The Porta Pisana, also called Maremmana, is the only one of the three original gates still equipped with a counter-gate. The tight space between the two openings created a trap for attackers who had breached the first defense. The Porta Volterrana, demolished in 1798, still had a drawbridge at that date.

The 15th-century parish church of San Leonardo and Santa Maria Assunta preserves paintings by Melani and two marble statues attributed to Andrea Pisano. It was severely damaged by the 19th-century earthquake that also remodeled the neighboring market loggia.

activities.text: Tasting of Lari cherries: 19 local varieties, some found only in this area of the Pisan hills. Local producers sell baskets, jams, and liqueurs in the village square. The cherry festival is one of Tuscany's oldest.
Stroll through the borgo's medieval alleyways, whose original stone structure and Renaissance-Gothic dwellings have remained intact—the world wars never touched the village.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Lari

places.name: Laveno Mombello

visits.text: Visit the prepositural Church of Saints Philip and James, known as the old church, a religious building dating from 1315 that is the historic spiritual heart of Laveno. Beside it stands the Church of Sant'Ambrogio, built in 1940 by architect Paolo Mezzanotte in a modern style, together forming the sacred heritage of this charming lakeside town.

Discover Villa De Angeli Frua, an elegant eighteenth-century residence donated to the municipality and now home to the public library. The magnificent Parco delle Terrazze surrounding it offers breathtaking views of Lake Maggiore, with an evocative entrance tunnel that in spring is draped in blooming wisteria, creating a fairy-tale setting.

Ride the cable car up to Poggio Sant'Elsa for a privileged perspective of Lake Maggiore. This panoramic excursion offers spectacular views of the blue lake waters, the Borromean Islands in the distance and the Alpine skyline, delivering an unforgettable experience for nature and photography lovers.

activities.text: Stroll along the Laveno lakefront, one of Lake Maggiore's most evocative promenades offering views of the town, Stresa on the opposite shore and the famous Borromean Islands. From here ferries depart for Verbania, giving you the chance to explore both sides of the lake.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

map_keywords: Chiesa Santi Filippo e Giacomo Laveno-Mombello

visits.text: Stroll through Lazise, whose Scaliger Castle (not open for visits) and defensive walls create a picturesque medieval ensemble on Lake Garda's shores. The atmospheric lakefront promenade offers views across the water to the distant mountains, perfect for a relaxing evening passeggiata.

Visit the Church of San Nicolò, an ancient Romanesque church within the castle walls featuring medieval frescoes. The intimate scale and historic atmosphere capture the authentic character of a traditional lakeside community.

activities.text: Enjoy the beautiful lakeside setting, perhaps with a gelato from one of the local shops. The colorful boats bobbing in the harbor and the castle silhouette create postcard-perfect views at every turn.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

places.name: Le Castella

visits.text: First head to Capo Colonna, 10 km south of Crotone, on the promontory that once closed the Gulf of Taranto. Here stood one of the most important sanctuaries of Magna Graecia: the temple of Hera Lacinia, built around 470-460 BC. Of this Doric temple that counted 6 columns by 14, only a single 8-meter-high column remains today, standing before the Ionian Sea for 25 centuries. It was from here that Hannibal embarked for Carthage after his Italian campaign, engraving his exploits on a bronze column. The site's Archaeological Museum displays finds from the excavations, including a gold diadem and bronze votive objects.

In town, the National Archaeological Museum of Crotone traces the history of Kroton, a city founded around 710 BC by Achaean colonists from the Peloponnese. The city was famous in antiquity for its athletes - Milo of Croton won the Olympic wrestling games six times - and for Pythagoras, who founded his school there. On the second floor, the rooms dedicated to the sanctuary of Hera Lacinia present the gold diadem and a mysterious Nuragic bronze ship, testimonies of Mediterranean exchanges.

End the day at Le Castella, 35 km south of Crotone. The Castello Aragonese stands on an islet connected to land by a thin sandy isthmus - the image is spectacular. The foundations date back to the Magna Graecia era (5th century BC), and Hannibal himself allegedly took refuge here. The current core dates from the Angevin period (13th century), reinforced by the Aragonese in the 15th century with spurred bastions. Climb to the top of the tower for a circular view over the Capo Rizzuto marine protected area - Italy's largest - and its turquoise waters.

activities.text: Enjoy the Le Castella beach for a swim in the crystal-clear waters of the marine reserve. Operators offer boat trips around the castle and protected seabeds. If you're a diver, the Capo Rizzuto marine area offers snorkeling spots accessible directly from the beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea

map_keywords: Capo Colonna tempio Hera Lacinia Crotone

places.name: Lecce

visits.text: Lecce — Basilica of Santa Croce: begun in 1549 and completed in 1695, this facade is the defining monument of barocco leccese — a style born from the marriage between the malleability of Lecce stone (pietra leccese), a soft yellowish limestone easy to carve, and competition between religious orders. The facade, work of several successive architects including Gabriele Riccardi (lower part) and Cesare Penna (upper part), arrays ranks of grotesque figures, telamons, fantastic animals, and floral motifs across two levels.

Lecce — Piazza del Duomo: this enclosed square constitutes one of Italy's most coherent baroque ensembles. The Cathedral of Maria Santissima Assunta, rebuilt by Giuseppe Zimbalo between 1659 and 1670, has two facades — one sober on the north side, the other richly decorated on the west. The 68-meter bell tower (1661-1682), also by Zimbalo, dominates the ensemble. The Palazzo del Seminario, built between 1694 and 1709 by Giuseppe Cino, closes the square on the right.

Lecce — Piazza Sant'Oronzo and Roman amphitheater: Lecce's central square layers testimonies from five eras. The Roman amphitheater, discovered by chance in 1929 during construction of the Bank of Italy, measured 102 by 83 meters and could seat about 25,000 spectators. It dates to the 2nd century AD. The column of Sant'Oronzo, of Roman origin, was brought from Brindisi in the 17th century — it's one of the two columns that marked the end of the Via Appia at Brindisi. The Sedile, former Venetian town hall (1588-1592), now houses the tourist office.

Lecce — Church of San Matteo and baroque quarter: built from 1667 to the plans of Giovann'Andrea Larducci, San Matteo is the only church in Lecce to adopt a convex facade in the lower part and concave in the upper part, in the style of Borromini. Porta Napoli, a baroque triumphal arch erected in 1548 at the northern entrance to the city in honor of Charles V, marks the beginning of the baroque route from the north.

Galatina — Basilica of Santa Caterina d'Alessandria: built from 1384 by Raimondello Orsini del Balzo upon his return from the Holy Land, this basilica is one of the best-preserved Gothic-Byzantine fresco ensembles in the Mezzogiorno. Frescoes cover the entire interior walls in multiple registers: scenes from the life of Saint Catherine, the Apocalypse, the life of Christ. The Orsini family, who controlled Salento in the 15th century, made the church their dynastic pantheon.

Galatina — historic center and Piazza Orsini: the town retains a medieval center with Piazza Orsini as the central space. Galatina is also known as the cradle of Salento tarantella (la pizzica), a ritual dance traditionally practiced during the Feast of Saint Paul in late June. According to tradition, people bitten by a tarantula (or "tarantate") healed through dance.

Nardò — Piazza Salandra and baroque cathedral: Piazza Salandra, with its Colonna dell'Immacolata (1769) and baroque palaces, is considered one of the most beautiful squares in Salento. The cathedral, founded in 1080 by the Normans and remodeled in the 17th-18th centuries, retains a Byzantine crypt with rupestrian frescoes. Nardò is also the starting point for the beaches of Porto Selvaggio and Santa Maria al Bagno on the Ionian coast.

activities.text: Baroque walking loop: from Piazza del Duomo to Santa Croce, passing through Castello Carlo V, Porta Napoli, and Corso Vittorio Emanuele. The golden evening light on pietra leccese is the best hour to photograph the facades.

Olive oil and Salento wine tasting at a masseria: the territory around Lecce produces Primitivo and Negroamaro. Estates offer tours of centuries-old olive trees and tastings of Terra d'Otranto DOP olive oil.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley
Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Caterina d'Alessandria Galatina

visits.text: Levanto is the Ligurian coast's well-kept secret - the northern gateway to Cinque Terre, but with a far more relaxed atmosphere. The town has a beautiful sandy beach (rare in the region), a medieval historic centre with frescoed noble houses, and the church of Sant'Andrea (13th century), built in white marble and green serpentine in the Ligurian Gothic style, with a carved rose window and portal. Stroll along Via Garibaldi and its arcades, then climb to the medieval castle for views over the bay.

From Levanto, the path to Monterosso via Punta Mesco is one of the most beautiful hikes on the Ligurian coast. The promontory of Punta Mesco, with the ruins of the hermitage of Sant'Antonio, offers panoramic views over the entire Cinque Terre coast on one side and the Gulf of Levanto on the other. The path (about 3 hours return) crosses Mediterranean maquis, with maritime pines and constant panoramas over the turquoise sea below.

activities.text: Spend the morning on Levanto beach - a beautiful sandy stretch with free-access sections and equipped zones. The waves are regular, making it a popular surf spot. The water is clear and the view of hills covered with terraced vineyards completes the picture.

In the afternoon, grab a bike for the Levanto-Bonassola-Framura cycle path - a spectacular 5.5 km seaside route, through old railway tunnels transformed into a trail, alternating lit galleries and open views onto coves. Bike hire available in Levanto. The path is flat and accessible to all - ideal in late afternoon for the golden light.

days_plan.itin_title: From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria

visits.text: Pontile di Lido di Camaiore: The wooden pier of Lido di Camaiore, symbol of the seaside resort, extends into the Tyrrhenian Sea facing the Apuan Alps. Less frequented than those of Forte dei Marmi or Viareggio, this promenade on stilts offers a peaceful panorama over the Versilia coastline.

activities.text: Beach and waterfront: Lido di Camaiore stretches for several kilometers of fine sand between the Tyrrhenian Sea and the pine forest. Less fashionable than Forte dei Marmi, the resort offers family beach establishments with all the infrastructure of traditional Italian beach culture. The coastal bike path links Lido di Camaiore to Forte dei Marmi in the north and Viareggio in the south.

Versilia bike path: The path runs along the waterfront for over 20 km, from Viareggio to Marina di Pietrasanta, allowing you to connect the main seaside resorts of Versilia by bike while riding directly along the sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

visits.text: Stroll through the historic center of Limone Piemonte, a village in the Vermenagna Valley at 1,000 meters altitude on one of the historic routes linking France, Piedmont, and the Ligurian coast—once traveled by convoys transporting salt from the Mediterranean inland. Inhabited since Roman times, Limone was one of the first villages in the Southern Alps to develop mountain tourism. Restaurants, shops, and refuges form a compact village center reachable from the train station within minutes on foot.

Visit the Museo dello Sci Agostino Bottero, opened in 2012, which traces the history of skiing in the Riserva Bianca from the first Alpine troops' training sessions in 1907 on the slopes of Monte Vecchio, to the 2006 Winter Olympics that modernized the resort. In 1937, 'lo slittone'—a large motorized sled—was the first mechanical lift installed in southern Piedmont, allowing riders to climb 700 meters of elevation.

activities.text: Skiing or snowboarding session on the Riserva Bianca slopes (season December to early April): the ski area extends from 1,050 to 2,085 meters altitude over 80 kilometers of slopes divided into 41 runs served by 16 lifts. The difficulty distribution is 25% beginner, 55% intermediate, and 20% red and black runs. From the summits—notably Mont Alpetta at 2,070 meters—on clear days, the Mediterranean and Corsica are visible to the south, the Matterhorn and Monte Rosa to the north. Italian and French national teams have conducted training camps here.

Outside ski season, snowshoe or hiking trek on one of the 12 marked trails around Limone and Limonetto, some leading to 19th-century military forts built around the Colle di Tenda—Fort Alto, Fort Pernante, Fort Giaura, and Fort Margherita to the west, Fort Taburda and Fort Pepino to the east. These defensive works of the Kingdom of Sardinia then the Kingdom of Italy controlled the historic pass between Italy and France.
Excursion to the Cascate del Saut, waterfalls located in the valley near Limone Piemonte, particularly impressive in winter under snow and in early spring during the melt.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days

places.name: Limone Sul Garda

visits.text: Explore the historic center of Limone sul Garda, nestled between limestone cliffs and the lake. The village name doesn't derive from lemons but probably from the Latin 'limes' (border) or the Celtic 'lemos' (elm). Until 1932, the village was road-inaccessible and could only be reached by boat or mountain paths—a fact that contributed to an exceptional genetic peculiarity: in 1974, researchers from the University of Milan discovered ApoA-1 Milano in local residents, a mutation of the apolipoprotein A-I protein inherited from a common ancestor born around 1780. This protein eliminates arterial fats at high speed, conferring on its carriers natural resistance to cardiovascular disease despite very low HDL cholesterol. About 60 out of 1,100 residents still carry it today.

Climb to the Limonaia del Castèl, an 18th-century lemon greenhouse bought and restored by the municipality in 1995. Limonaie are open-air structures facing due south, with thick walls, tall white stone columns, and wooden planks placed in winter to protect the trees from cold. Franciscan monks introduced lemon cultivation on these shores as early as the 13th century, making Limone one of the northernmost points in the world for commercial citrus growing. Goethe described these terraced gardens in September 1786 during his Italian Journey. The limonaia now houses an educational pathway and more than a hundred producing lemon trees.

Descend to Limone's small historic harbor, the village's original heart. The San Rocco church, on its terrace by the water, offers a vantage point on Monte Baldo across the lake. At the northern edge of the village, a museum recounts the history of fishing and PDO olive oil production, another local specialty.

activities.text: Walk the Busatte-Tempesta panoramic walkway, inaugurated July 14, 2018, after two years of construction costing 7 million euros. Suspended from the cliff face about 50 meters above the lake on a 2-kilometer route, it follows the municipality to the Trentino border. The round-trip from the village center is entirely on foot.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

map_keywords: Limonaia del Castel Limone sul Garda
Chiesa di San Rocco Limone sul Garda
Pista Ciclabile Busatte Tempesta Riva del Garda

visits.text: Lipari, the largest Aeolian island with 10,000 residents, serves as the transportation hub for the entire archipelago. Its citadel crowns a 40-meter obsidian rock that has witnessed Greek settlement (580 BC), Roman fortification, Arab conquest, Norman rule, and Spanish occupation. The current ramparts date from the 16th century, erected under Charles V.

The Aeolian Archaeological Museum, housed within the citadel walls, holds the Mediterranean's most comprehensive collection of prehistoric and ancient island artifacts. Its obsidian trade section reveals Neolithic-era commerce routes stretching from Lipari's volcanic glass quarries to Provence and Spain — evidence of the first maritime trade networks in the Inner Sea.

The Quattrocchi belvedere, 3 km from the port, frames the Aeolians' most photographed vista: twin rock arches rising from the sea with Vulcano's smoking cone on the horizon. The name references a local tradition where couples come to see with "four eyes rather than two."

Lipari's pumice quarries on the island's northern flank supplied volcanic stone throughout the Mediterranean for millennia. White pumice cliffs plunge directly into the sea — since the 2000s, extraction has been regulated to preserve the dramatic landscape.

Spiaggia Bianca, a beach of white pumice sand and pebbles at the quarry base, lies a 30-minute walk from Canneto village. Its shallow, crystal-clear waters are perfect for snorkeling among obsidian rock formations.

activities.text: Tasting of Malvasia delle Lipari DOC at island cellars — a naturally sweet wine made from grapes dried on straw mats, cited in Homer's Odyssey and among the Mediterranean's most ancient wines still produced today.
Hike to Spiaggia Valle Muria, a secluded beach of black lava sand and white pumice accessible only on foot (1h30 one-way) or by boat. One of the archipelago's most pristine snorkeling spots.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria
Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Lizzano In Belvedere

visits.text: Santuario della Madonna del Faggio - a hermitage built in the 17th century in the Bolognese Apennine beech forests at 1,150 m elevation, according to legend at the spot where woodcutters discovered a terracotta image of the Virgin beneath a great beech tree. The 4 km access path from Lizzano passes through dense beech forest.

Castello Manservisi - a 19th-century neo-Gothic castle overlooking the village of Lizzano, restored and open for visits. It preserves works by the local painter Giacomo Lolli (1859-1940), including landscapes of the Bolognese Apennines.

activities.text: Hike to the Santuario della Madonna del Faggio from Lizzano—4 km through beech forest with 300 m elevation gain.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

map_keywords: Santuario della Madonna del Faggio Lizzano

places.name: Lucca

visits.text: Piazza dell'Anfiteatro: Lucca's oval plaza is no reconstructed set piece—it traces the exact perimeter of a 2nd-century AD Roman amphitheatre that once held 10,000 spectators. Medieval residents built their houses directly against the ancient stone tiers, erasing the arena but preserving its elliptical form. The 19th-century restoration by architect Lorenzo Nottolini cleared the square and opened the four arched passages that still produce the element of surprise described by Grand Tour travelers. Enter through one of the vaulted passages to understand how an entire city grew from the bones of a Roman monument.

San Martino Cathedral: Founded in the 6th century by Bishop Fridian, rebuilt from the 11th century onward, and completed in the 16th–17th centuries, the cathedral displays a distinctive asymmetrical façade—the architect had to narrow the final arcade to fit the pre-existing bell tower, creating an elegant dissonance. Inside, the main treasure is the Volto Santo, a 13th-century wooden crucifix traditionally attributed to Nicodemus and carried in a candlelit procession every September 13th since the Middle Ages. In the sacristy, the tomb of Ilaria del Carretto (1406) sculpted by Jacopo della Quercia shows the young wife of Paolo Guinigi lying with a dog at her feet—a symbol of marital fidelity—in a style that would influence Donatello.

Torre Guinigi: The Guinigi family's red brick tower, erected in the late 14th century, reaches 44.25 meters. Its unique feature is a garden of holm oaks planted at the summit—a symbol of rebirth chosen by the Guinigi, then lords of Lucca. Paolo Guinigi, who ruled the city from 1400 to 1430, made this tower the emblem of his mercantile and banking power. Climb the 230 steps for sweeping views of tiled rooftops, ramparts, and, on clear days, the Apuan Alps.

San Michele in Foro Church: Built on the site of the ancient Roman forum, San Michele displays a 12th-century Pisan-Lucchese façade with four tiers of sculpted columns, each different from the next. Throughout the Middle Ages, the building also served as the courthouse for the Republic of Lucca. Inside: a 14th-century frescoed Virgin and Child and a glazed ceramic Madonna by Luca della Robbia.

Puccini's Birthplace (Museo Puccini): Giacomo Puccini was born on December 22, 1858, in this apartment on Corte San Lorenzo, where the Puccini family had served for five generations as organists and chapel masters at the cathedral. The museum preserves the Steinway & Sons piano purchased in 1901 on which he composed Turandot, opera manuscripts, costumes, and stage photographs. Puccini bought back the family apartment after his sister sold it—proof of the composer's attachment to his native city.

Palazzo Mansi - National Museum: Built in the 17th century for a wealthy family of Lucchese merchants, this palace preserves its state apartments almost intact—music room with allegorical frescoes, hall of mirrors, painting collections from the Medici and Grand Duchy holdings. Lucca built its fortune between the 13th and 15th centuries on silk trade, exporting fabrics as far as England and Flanders. This palace is the direct architectural expression of that wealth.

Palazzo Pfanner and Gardens: Built in 1660 for the Moricani family, then purchased in the mid-19th century by the Austrian-origin Pfanner family—who still own it. It opens onto one of the rare Italian-style gardens still preserved within the walls, with a central fountain surrounded by statues of deities and seasons. Jane Campion's film Portrait of a Lady was partly shot here.

Via Fillungo and Historic Center: The main artery of the pedestrian center concentrates historic cafés, artisan shops, and fragments of medieval architecture. The Torre delle Ore, whose first clock dates to 1390, marks its northern end. Puccini frequented Caffè Di Simo, an establishment opened in 1875 whose Art Nouveau storefront is still visible.

Villa Guinigi - National Museum: Former country residence of Paolo Guinigi, lord of Lucca from 1400 to 1430, built outside the medieval walls in the style of Venetian Palladian villas with an arcaded loggia. The museum houses collections ranging from 8th-century BC Etruscan discoveries to medieval sculptures from churches suppressed during the Napoleonic era.

activities.text: Bike ride on the ramparts: Built between 1513 and 1650 along a 4.2 km circuit, Lucca's Renaissance walls constitute one of the best-preserved examples of modern fortifications in Europe. Thirty meters wide at the base and 12 meters high, they never saw combat—Lucca maintained its independence through diplomacy until 1799. Today they're transformed into a tree-lined promenade bordered by plane trees and oaks, accessible on foot or by bike. Rentals are available at the main gates.

Colline Lucchesi DOC wine tasting: The Colline Lucchesi appellation covers the hills immediately north and east of Lucca. It produces Trebbiano-based whites and Sangiovese-based reds cultivated on these slopes since Roman times. Several estates in the province offer tastings.

Antiques market: Every third Sunday of the month, the antiques market spreads across the main squares of the historic center—Piazza San Martino, Piazza San Giovanni, Piazza Bernardini—with several hundred vendors. It's one of the most frequented antiques markets in Tuscany.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast
Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast
Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele in Foro Lucca

visits.text: Trani - Frederick II's Swabian castle: Built in 1233 on the seafront, this square-plan castle with corner towers faces the cathedral from the same esplanade. The two buildings position spiritual and temporal power face to face. The castle now hosts temporary exhibitions and provides access to the ramparts with views over the harbor.

Trani - old port and Jewish quarter: Trani was one of the most active commercial ports on the Adriatic in the Middle Ages. Its Jewish quarter, one of the best preserved in southern Italy, houses the 12th-century Scolanova Synagogue. Converted into a church for centuries, it was returned to the Jewish community in 1993. A second medieval Jewish building, the Sant'Anna Synagogue, stands in the same quarter.

Castel del Monte - Frederick II's octagonal castle: Built around 1240 on the Andria hill at 540 meters altitude, this castle is an architectural enigma, UNESCO-listed since 1996. Its form — a perfect octagon flanked by eight 24-meter octagonal towers — has no equivalent in medieval military architecture. Frederick II ordered its construction by letter dated January 29, 1240 from Gubbio. It lacks a moat, drawbridge and defensive positions for archers: this is no ordinary fortress. The most probable hypothesis is that it represents a crown in stone — the imperial crown of the Holy Roman Empire is itself octagonal. The castle was used as a state prison under Charles I of Anjou, who imprisoned Manfred of Sicily's sons there. Umberto Eco drew inspiration from it for the library in his novel The Name of the Rose (1980).

Troia - Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta: Built from 1093 on a Byzantine fortress, this cathedral is considered a masterpiece of Apulian Romanesque. Its eleven-spoke rose window — unique in Puglia — comprises eleven columns radiating at 32.72° angles each, connected by pierced stone diaphragms, each different from the others: twenty-two distinct decorative patterns achieved purely through carving. The rose window appeared on old Italian 5,000-lire banknotes. The bronze doors of the main portal (1119) are the work of Oderisio of Benevento and feature 28 panels adorned with religious scenes and winged dragons with serpent tails.

activities.text: Walk along the ramparts of Lucera fortress at sunset: From the Angevin walls, the view embraces the Tavoliere delle Puglie, the great agricultural plain of northern Puglia, as far as the Apennines. Frederick II died in December 1250 at Castel Fiorentino, just 7 miles from these walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Puglia: Frederick II's Castles, Hidden Hinterland & Forgotten Villages

places.name: Lucignano

visits.text: Elliptical layout of Lucignano: the village, Etruscan in origin and developed between the 13th and 16th centuries, is one of the best-preserved examples of medieval town planning in Tuscany. Its streets coil in four concentric elliptical rings around the hilltop, like a snail shell. This singular layout earned it the Bandiera Arancione label from the Italian Touring Club. Its position on the boundary of the former territories of Florence, Arezzo, Siena and Perugia gave it a strategic importance still visible in the thickness of its ramparts.

Municipal Museum — the Albero d'Oro: on the ground floor of the former Palazzo Pretorio, this 2.70-meter gilded copper reliquary is one of the masterworks of Italian goldsmithing. Created between 1350 and 1471 — a 120-year project — thanks to a bequest from one Madonna Giacoma, it was intended for the church of San Francesco and held Franciscan relics and splinters of the Cross of Christ. Its tree form draws on the Lignum Vitae (1259–1260) by Saint Bonaventure. The tree is displayed in the Sala delle Udienze, the former tribunal hall decorated with a fresco cycle (1410–1479) depicting illustrious figures from Antiquity, from Solomon to Aristotle. The museum, founded in 1924, also holds two panels by Luca Signorelli and works from the 14th-century Sienese school.

Church of San Francesco: built in the 12th century, recognizable by its travertine and local sandstone facade. Inside, a polyptych attributed to Luca di Tommè depicting a Madonna holding the castle of Lucignano recalls the town's role as a marquisate contested between Siena and Florence until the Battle of Scannagallo in the 16th century.

activities.text: Walk through the concentric rings: following the four elliptical streets that encircle the village from the outside is the only way to perceive the exceptional urban layout. The Medici Fortress, built in the 16th century on orders from Cosimo I de' Medici, offers a panorama over the Valdichiana and Lake Trasimeno.

Palio delle Contrade of Lucignano: a medieval historical reenactment in costume held every year in May. In September, the Festa degli Sposi commemorates the symbolic link between the Albero d'Oro and love, celebrated since the Japanese designer Yumi Katsura chose Lucignano for a bridal gown presentation.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

map_keywords: Collegiata di San Michele Arcangelo Lucignano

places.name: Lugo Di Vicenza

visits.text: Villa Godi Malinverni - This villa built between 1537 and 1542 in Lonedo, a hamlet of Lugo di Vicenza, is the first villa designed by Andrea Palladio—he wasn't yet thirty. The commission came from brothers Girolamo, Pietro and Marcantello Godi, from a powerful noble family specializing in silk trade across Europe. The facade is sober, almost austere, with two massive projecting blocks framing a central loggia with three arches, without the classical ornaments that would characterize his mature works. Palladio himself notes in the Four Books of Architecture that it 'stands on a hill with an admirable view, beside a river that serves for fishing.' The interior is decorated with frescoes by Gualtiero Padovano, Giovanni Battista Zelotti and Battista del Moro. A park pavilion houses the Pierluigi Malinverni paleontological museum, which preserves notably the world's longest fossil palm, and a second museum is dedicated to the British presence in this territory during the Great War, with documents on the Prince of Wales (future Edward VIII) and pacifist writer Vera Brittain.

Buso della Rana - In Monte di Malo, about twenty kilometers from Lugo, opens the entrance to Veneto's longest cave. The speleological network extends over more than 10 kilometers of galleries. The portion accessible to the public, about 500 meters of equipped galleries, crosses rooms of limestone concretions—stalactites, stalagmites, columns—formed over millions of years. The constant temperature of 9°C makes it an appreciated excursion in summer. The guided visit lasts about an hour.

activities.text: Visit to Villa Godi's historic gardens - The villa's centuries-old park, recently restored thanks to PNRR funds as part of the 'Gardens of Palladio' project, alternates Italian gardens with regular embroidery, alleys of century-old boxwood and an English wooded park with trees planted in the 19th century. A fountain, a network of paths and a view over the Astico valley and Vicentine Prealps complete the walk.

Exploring the Buso della Rana galleries - Guided cave tours depart every hour from the prepared entrance. The route crosses several remarkable rooms including the 'Sala del Bivio' and 'Sala delle Cascate,' with explanations of the karst geology of the Asiago Plateau, of which the Buso is one of the natural drains.

days_plan.itin_title: Vicenza Circuit: Palladian Villas and the Venetian Countryside

map_keywords: Villa Godi Malinverni Lonedo di Lugo Vicentino
Buso della Rana Monte di Malo
Giardini Villa Godi Lonedo di Lugo Vicentino
Chiesa di San Michele Lugo di Vicenza

visits.text: Admire the Palazzo Verbania, an Art Nouveau architectural jewel built in the nineteenth century that regularly hosts art exhibitions and cultural events. This elegant historic building is the cultural heart of the town, bearing witness to the period of great prosperity Luino enjoyed as a major trading centre on Lake Maggiore.

Visit the Church of San Pietro, one of the most significant religious buildings in the old town preserving precious artistic testimonies of local devotion. Together with the Church of San Giuseppe, it forms the sacred heritage of this fascinating lakeside town.

Don't miss the famous Luino Market, one of the largest open-air markets in the area and among the oldest in Europe. Every Wednesday the town centre streets come alive with stalls offering local produce, handicrafts and gastronomic specialities, attracting visitors from across the region and nearby Switzerland.

activities.text: Stroll along the magnificent Luino lakefront, one of Lake Maggiore's most beautiful promenades winding between elegant historic buildings and lush green spaces. The view over the crystal-clear waters and surrounding mountains invites moments of pure relaxation and contemplation.

Venture onto the panoramic trails around Luino, such as the walk to Colmegna along the lake or the hike to Monte Bedea and the charming Laghetto di Moncucco, immersed in Alpine scenery of extraordinary beauty.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

places.name: Macerata Feltria

visits.text: The Castello — early medieval nucleus — Macerata Feltria is built on the ruins of Pitinum Pisaurense, a Roman municipium founded in the 3rd century BC. 'Pitinum' from the Greek for 'pine' (the surrounding forests), 'Pisaurus' the old name for the Foglia river. Pliny listed it among the cities of the sixth Augustan region. Destroyed by the Goths of Vitige in 552 and rebuilt by the Lombards on the adjacent hill — the present Castello. The settlement is divided in two: the medieval Castello above (11th century) and the Borgo or Mercatale below, extended in the 16th century around the old market. The 14th-century Ponte Lucchini connects the two. Enter the Castello by this bridge, then the chiesa e convento di San Francesco (mid-14th century), then the lanes toward the Torre Civica.

Torre Civica and Palazzo del Podestà — Museo Civico — the Torre Civica (11th century, bell chamber added in the 18th century) and the Palazzo del Podestà (13th–14th century) dominate the Piazza Abstemio at the heart of the Castello. The civic museum occupies both buildings: eight rooms tracing the history of Pitinum from prehistory through the Roman period, with inscriptions, coins, bronze statuettes and everyday objects from the 1990–1997 excavations. The palaeontological section in the tower holds fossils collected by Arnaldo and Gino Rinaldi in the Montefeltro valleys. Six medieval tombs (12th–13th century) faithfully reconstructed in the museum rooms.

Pieve di San Cassiano in Pitino — excavations of Pitinum — an 11th-century Romanesque three-nave pieve built directly over the ruins of the Roman municipium. Several fragments of early medieval carved relief with braids and scrollwork are visible on the façade. Inside, a 15th-century marble statue of San Sebastiano and early medieval architectural fragments. Beneath the pieve, archaeologists from the University of Urbino uncovered a paved Roman road in an east–west orientation (possibly the decumanus maximus) and a medieval necropolis — sarcophagi visible in a consolidated cross-section. The most significant areas are protected under glass and lit. Open access, 2 km along the SP6 toward Carpegna.

Arco dei Pelasgi — a stone arch at the Castello entrance, commemorating the Pelasgian people who, according to local tradition, founded ancient Pitinum before the Romans. A symbolic marker of a historical depth stretching back, by legend, to a pre-Roman civilisation.

activities.text: Visit the Museo Civico then the Pitinum excavations — start with the eight rooms in the Palazzo del Podestà, then climb the Torre Civica for the palaeontological section. Drive the 2 km along the SP6 to the Pieve di San Cassiano to see the excavations and the Roman road in situ. Allow half a day for the full experience.

Pitinum Thermae — a thermal spa established in 1993, drawing on the natural sulphurous springs of Certalto and Apsa. Sulphurous-bicarbonate calcium-magnesium waters with anti-catarrhal and antioxidant properties. Inhalation treatments, mud baths, hydrotherapy pools. An ideal way to end the day after exploring the village.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Pieve di San Cassiano in Pitino Macerata Feltria

places.name: Magliano In Toscana

visits.text: Magliano in Toscana is surrounded by one of Tuscany's best-preserved medieval walls, built in the 9th-10th centuries by the Aldobrandeschi then considerably reinforced by the Republic of Siena in the 15th century. The walkway runs the entire perimeter. Two monumental gates frame Corso Garibaldi: the Porta di San Martino to the north offers a remarkable view over the Maremman countryside; the Porta San Giovanni to the south guards access to the vineyards. The village occupies the site of ancient Roman Heba, itself heir to an Etruscan settlement whose several necropolises have been unearthed in the surroundings.

The church of San Giovanni Battista, on Piazza della Repubblica, has been documented since the 11th century. Its facade was restored in the Sienese style in 1471, and its interior preserves stucco statues and a Renaissance baptismal font from 1493. The church of Pieve di San Martino, older, presents characteristic Romanesque austerity. A few minutes' walk away, the small Civic Archaeological Museum houses the famous Magliano Disc—an Etruscan lead artifact bearing a long circular inscription in both directions, of still-debated interpretation—whose original is preserved at the Archaeological Museum in Florence.

activities.text: The Olivo della Strega (Witch's Olive Tree), just outside Magliano's walls in an olive grove behind the Oratory of Santissima Annunziata, is a monumental tree whose circumference exceeds 8 meters. Medieval tradition held it to be the nocturnal meeting place of women accused of witchcraft. It's one of the oldest and most massive trees in Tuscany.

The ruins of San Bruzio Benedictine abbey, 5 km from Magliano in an olive grove, are accessible on foot from the village. Founded around the year 1000, this abbey retains an intact sacred aura despite its ruined state: its gray travertine nave, its arcades open to the sky, and its centuries-worn capitals make it one of the most melancholic and beautiful places in the Maremma.

Tasting of Morellino di Scansano DOCG at wineries in the Colli di Maremma zone: this Sangiovese-dominated red, produced on the hills around Magliano and Scansano, has enjoyed DOCG status since 2006. The Vinellando, a wine festival held every year in late August through Magliano's streets, offers the opportunity to discover all the producers in one evening.
