places.name: Maiori

visits.text: Maiori - a town of 5,500 with the longest beach on the Amalfi Coast - 2,750 feet of fine sand, unlike the pebble beaches of neighboring villages. The Torre Normanna, an 11th-century Norman tower built to watch for Saracen raids, rises 130 feet above the seafront. The town was almost entirely destroyed by a catastrophic flood in October 1954 that killed 318 people in the Reghinna valley.

Collegiata di Santa Maria a Mare - a church founded in the 9th century on a rocky outcrop overlooking Maiori's harbor, rebuilt in the 16th-17th centuries. It preserves a 9th-century Byzantine icon of the Virgin, the Madonna dell'Assunta, revered as the protector of the coast's sailors. The 12th-century bell tower, the only surviving medieval element, is covered in green and yellow majolica tiles characteristic of the Amalfi Coast.

Cetara - a fishing village of 2,200 specializing in tuna and anchovy fishing since the Middle Ages, its name likely derived from the Latin cetaria (tuna fishery). The colatura di alici di Cetara, a fish sauce produced by fermenting salted anchovies for at least 12 months in chestnut barrels, received IGP status in 2020. The technique has been documented in the archives of the Benedictine monastery of Santissima Trinità di Cava since the 11th century.

activities.text: Swimming at Maiori beach - the Amalfi Coast's longest sandy beach, with shallow water extending 160 feet from shore.
Tasting colatura di alici in Cetara's specialty shops - amber fish sauce sold in small bottles, drizzled over pasta as a salt substitute.

Hiking from Maiori to the Benedictine abbey of Santa Maria de Olearia (10th century) - a rock-cut monastic complex carved into the cliff 1.2 miles from town, with three levels of chapels adorned with medieval frescoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages of the Amalfi Coast: From Praiano to Salerno

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria a Mare Maiori
Abbazia Santa Maria de Olearia Maiori

places.name: Manfredonia

visits.text: Lucera - Roman amphitheater and historic center: Lucera was a prosperous Roman colony (Luceria) whose Augustan-era amphitheater is among the best preserved in southern Italy. The Gothic Cathedral of San Francesco d'Assisi, built by Charles II of Anjou in 1302 on the ruins of the great mosque of the Saracen colony, is one of the rare testimonies of Angevin architecture in Puglia.

Manfredonia - Swabian-Angevin castle and Daunian stelae museum: Founded by Manfred of Sicily (natural son of Frederick II) around 1256 after the destruction of ancient Siponto by an earthquake. The castle houses the Daunian stelae — carved funerary slabs from the 8th-6th centuries BC depicting figures, animals and ritual scenes. These are among the oldest figurative testimonies in Puglia, from a people about whom almost nothing is yet known.

Siponto - Basilica of Santa Maria di Siponto: Located 3 km south of Manfredonia, this 11th-century Romanesque basilica is one of the rare examples of religious architecture built shortly after the year 1000 in Puglia. It was abandoned when Manfredonia was founded and has remained virtually intact. Its square plan over a crypt, with Byzantine and Oriental influences, makes it an object of architectural study.

activities.text: Panoramic descent from Monte Sant'Angelo to the coast: The road from the sanctuary to Manfredonia offers successive views over the Gulf of Manfredonia, the Tavoliere plain and, on clear days, as far as the Basilicata Apennines.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Puglia: Frederick II's Castles, Hidden Hinterland & Forgotten Villages

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Maria Maggiore di Siponto

visits.text: Transition day: the morning is devoted to the drive from Matera to the Tyrrhenian coast (about 2.5 hours via the Basentana and A2). You pass from the mineral world of the Sassi to the blue of the Mediterranean. Arrive in Maratea around noon, lunch on fresh fish at the port, and begin visits in early afternoon. Maratea is nicknamed the "pearl of the Tyrrhenian"—it's Basilicata's only access to the western sea, on 32 km of wild coastline.

Climb to the summit of Monte San Biagio (623 m) to see the Cristo Redentore. Standing 21.20 meters tall with an arm span of 19 meters, this statue in Carrara marble and cement was created by Florentine sculptor Bruno Innocenti between 1963 and 1965. It's the third-tallest statue of Jesus in Europe. Distinctive feature: unlike Rio's Christ from which it draws inspiration, its arms are slightly folded backward, giving it an almost floating appearance. The view from the base embraces the entire coast.

Below the statue, the Basilica of San Biagio has preserved the relics of Maratea's patron saint since 732 AD. Descending, you'll pass through the ruins of Maratea castle, destroyed by the French in 1806. The path offers spectacular viewpoints at every turn.

Descend to the historic center (Il Borgo), a labyrinth of medieval alleyways. Maratea is called the "city of 44 churches"—an impressive number for 5,000 inhabitants. Wander between Piazza Vitolo and Piazza Buragli, enter the baroque chapels you encounter.

activities.text: Finish on one of Maratea's beaches. The Spiaggia Nera (Black Beach), with dark volcanic sand, offers a striking contrast with the turquoise water. For more tranquility, head to the cove of Cala Jannita or Acquafredda beach. Operators offer boat trips to explore sea caves inaccessible by land.

days_plan.itin_title: Basilicata: From Matera to Pollino, Castles, Ghost Towns, and the Lucanian Dolomites
From Naples to Calabria: The Tyrrhenian Coast

visits.text: Marettimo, the westernmost Aegadian island, is the wildest. Its 800 residents live by fishing and low-key tourism. No roads — only marked trails — the island is explored on foot. The water clarity reaches 35-40 meters visibility.

The Castello di Marettimo, a 9th-century Arab fortress expanded by the Normans in the 11th century, houses a small 11th-century Norman church in original condition — one of Sicily's oldest and least-known Christian edifices.

activities.text: Boat tour of the sea caves — Grotta del Cammello, Grotta della Bombarda, Grotta Perciata — whose limestone ceilings are pierced with light shafts illuminating emerald waters.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Marina Di Pisa

visits.text: Bocca d'Arno - Where the Arno River meets the Tyrrhenian Sea, this engineered river mouth owes its existence to Ferdinand I de' Medici, who ordered it relocated roughly 1,500 meters north in 1606 to protect Pisa from flooding. Along the riverbank, traditional fishing nets called 'retoni' hang like portals above the water—relics of an activity that has shaped local life for centuries. On clear days, Corsica, Elba, and the island of Gorgona materialize on the horizon.

Piazzale delle Baleari and the Art Nouveau Waterfront - Marina di Pisa emerged as a seaside resort in the late 19th century when Gaetano Ceccherini commissioned the first wave of villas here after 1869. The seafront is lined with some of the finest Liberty-style buildings on the Tuscan coast, including Villa Bondi, built in 1909 on Piazza Baleari for Angelo Bondi, a Florentine terracotta industrialist. Poet Gabriele D'Annunzio stayed here and drew inspiration from the nearby pine forest to compose 'La pioggia nel pineto' (Rain in the Pine Grove), one of the most celebrated Italian poems of the 20th century.

Piazza dei Miracoli - The Leaning Tower of Pisa, begun on August 9, 1173 and completed in 1372 after two centuries of construction, stands 56 meters tall on its lowest side and weighs approximately 14,500 tons. It began tilting during construction of the third floor around 1178 because the soft alluvial soil couldn't support its weight. Stabilization work between 1990 and 2001 reduced the tilt by 44 centimeters, bringing it to roughly 4 degrees. The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta rises to 77 meters and towers over the campanile. The entire Piazza dei Miracoli has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987.

Baptistery of San Giovanni in Pisa - Started in 1152 by architect Diotisalvi, who also designed the tower, the Baptistery of Pisa is Italy's largest at 54 meters high and 107 meters in circumference. Its construction spanned two centuries, which explains the coexistence of Romanesque and Gothic styles in its galleries. Its acoustic properties are remarkable: the double-shell dome creates a reverberation lasting several seconds, allowing a single voice to form a complete chord.

Lungarno of Pisa - The ochre and terracotta facades lining the Arno in Pisa form one of the most photographed urban panoramas in Tuscany. Legend has it that Galileo, born in Pisa in 1564, observed the oscillations of a chandelier in the nearby cathedral—an experience traditionally presented as the starting point of his pendulum research.

Orto Botanico di Pisa - Founded in 1544 by Cosimo I de' Medici on the proposal of botanist Luca Ghini, the Botanical Garden of Pisa is one of the world's oldest scientific gardens still in operation. It covers about 3 hectares in the historic center and houses a collection of over 3,000 plant species, including a magnolia planted in the 18th century that stands as one of Europe's oldest specimens.

activities.text: Bike ride Marina di Pisa → Pisa via the Ciclopista del Trammino - This 13 km bike path follows the Arno along the former route of the steam tram inaugurated in 1892, which connected Pisa to the sea and ceased operation in 1932. The route passes through the pine forest of San Rossore Regional Park before reaching downtown Pisa.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages

map_keywords: Piazzale delle Baleari Marina di Pisa

visits.text: Piazza degli Scacchi and Castello Inferiore - The main square of Marostica is paved with a giant chessboard measuring 16 meters on each side, in pink Conco marble and white Asiago marble, with 2-meter squares. This is where the Partita a Scacchi takes place every even year on the second weekend of September: a chess game played with 600 costumed actors in 15th-century dress, live horses and archers in the towers. The legend dates to 1454: two warriors, Rinaldo d'Angarano and Vieri da Vallonara, both in love with Lionora, daughter of castellan Taddeo Parisio, were forbidden to duel and instead played out their fate on a chessboard before the townspeople. The modern tradition dates back to 1923. The Castello Inferiore (14th century) dominates the square and houses a museum of the event's costumes.

Castello Superiore and medieval walls - A climb of about 20 minutes on foot along the rampart path connects the lower castle to the Castello Superiore, perched at 350 meters altitude on the hill behind the town. The upper castle dates from the 14th century, when Marostica was under the rule of Verona's Scaligeri before passing to Milan's Visconti and then to the Republic of Venice in 1404. A statue of St. Mark's lion on the square recalls four centuries of Venetian rule. The ramparts connecting the two castles are almost entirely preserved and offer panoramic views over the Brenta plain and the foothills of the Asiago Plateau.

activities.text: Tasting Marostica IGP cherries - The cherries of Marostica, grown on the sunny hills north of town, were the first in Italy to obtain IGP (Protected Geographical Indication) status for this fruit. The cherry festival is held in May; the rest of the year, local producers sell jams, liqueurs and dried cherries in the center's grocery shops. The main variety, Durone Nero di Vignola, is harvested in June.

Walk along the ramparts - The rampart path connecting the two castles along the hillside can be walked as a loop from Piazza degli Scacchi. Views over the red-tiled roofs, the hillside vineyards and the Brenta plain. The climb is steep in places but accessible to all.

days_plan.itin_title: Vicenza Circuit: Palladian Villas and the Venetian Countryside

places.name: Martina Franca

visits.text: Gioia del Colle - Norman-Swabian castle and archaeological museum: Rebuilt by Frederick II upon his return from the sixth crusade in 1230, this quadrangular-plan castle is one of the best preserved from the imperial fortification program. According to tradition, Bianca Lancia, the emperor's mistress and mother of Manfredi of Sicily, resided here and gave birth in the tower called the Empress Tower. In the basement, a cell displays two stone reliefs that, according to legend, reproduce Bianca Lancia's breasts. The ground floor rooms house the archaeological museum with Peucetian finds from the Monte Sannace site (7th-3rd centuries BC). Pier Paolo Pasolini filmed certain scenes from The Gospel According to St. Matthew (1964) here.

Martina Franca - Palazzo Ducale and Baroque center: Founded in 1300 by Philip I of Anjou, the town became a duchy of the Caracciolo family in 1507. The ducal palace, begun in 1668 by Duke Petracone V Caracciolo on the foundations of the 1338 Orsini castle, was designed by architect Giovanni Andrea Carducci, who reportedly benefited from advice from his friend Gianlorenzo Bernini. The original project envisioned 365 rooms — one for each day of the year. Three rooms are decorated with frescoes by Domenico Carella depicting mythological, biblical and Arcadian scenes. The inner courtyard has hosted the Festival della Valle d'Itria every summer since 1976, dedicated to bel canto opera.

Martina Franca - San Martino Basilica and Baroque alleyways: The 18th-century Baroque basilica dominates Piazza Plebiscito. The Porta di Santo Stefano, the main Baroque gate to the center, commemorates according to legend Saint Martin's intervention in helping the town repel French mercenaries in 1529. Martina Franca's historic center offers a Baroque distinct from Lecce — more restrained, with white facades and wrought-iron balconies — and virtually no tourists.

activities.text: Tasting Martina Franca capocollo and Itria Valley wines: Martina Franca IGP capocollo is aged in natural galleries with myrtle leaves. The vineyards here produce Locorotondo DOC (white) and Primitivo. Several valley farms offer tastings and tours.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Puglia: Frederick II's Castles, Hidden Hinterland & Forgotten Villages

map_keywords: Basilica di San Martino Martina Franca
Porta di Santo Stefano Martina Franca

places.name: Massa Marittima

visits.text: San Cerbone Cathedral sits diagonally across Piazza Garibaldi—a deliberate perspective trick: the architect spaced the facade pilasters unevenly so it appears perfectly symmetrical when viewed from the square's center. Built from the 11th century in Pisan Romanesque style, Giovanni Pisano redesigned the third register of the facade in 1287 and extended the apse in the Gothic forms we see today. It measures 58.72 meters long by 18 meters wide. Inside, the Ark of San Cerbone (1324) by Goro di Gregorio stands as one of the pinnacles of Italian Gothic sculpture. The Maestà attributed to Duccio di Buoninsegna (1316) hangs in the left chapel, while the travertine baptismal font by Giroldo da Arogno dates from 1267.

Piazza Garibaldi, formerly Piazza del Duomo, is the Maremma's most cohesive medieval square. It brings together the Palazzo del Podestà (early 13th century, its facade covered with coats of arms of successive podestas), the Palazzo del Comune, and the Loggia del Mercato. Massa Marittima was a free city in the 13th century before being conquered by Siena in 1335—precisely when the Sienese demolished the upper third of the Torre del Candeliere to punish the rebellious inhabitants.

The Palazzo del Podestà houses Massa Marittima's Archaeological Museum, with a prehistoric section featuring a reconstructed cave and an Etruscan section displaying artifacts from local sites. The town owes its medieval wealth to its argentiferous lead mines in the Metalliferous Hills—the Latin name Massa Metallorum bears witness.

The Museum of Sacred Art in San Pietro all'Orto, adjacent to the Palazzo del Podestà, has housed since 2005 the Maestà by Ambrogio Lorenzetti (c. 1335), a masterpiece of Sienese painting, along with the original Giovanni Pisano sculptures that once adorned the cathedral facade before their removal. The neighboring Augustinian church of Sant'Agostino (late 13th century) features a remarkable pentagonal apse and preserves relics of Saint Bernardino of Siena.

activities.text: The Fonti dell'Abbondanza, a public fountain built in 1265 under Pisan podestà Ildebrandino, served as the walled city's only water source. During restoration in 1999, workers discovered beneath several layers of plaster a 5 x 6 meter fresco dating between 1265 and 1335: the Albero della Fecondità (Tree of Fertility), depicting a tree whose fruits are twenty-five phalluses that women are trying to pick. Interpreted as an apotropaic allegory of abundance, it's one of the rare examples of 13th-century secular painting to survive.

The Torre del Candeliere (Candlestick Tower), erected in the 13th century as a symbol of communal authority, takes its name from the custom of maintaining a permanent fire that made it resemble a candle. After the Sienese conquest of 1335, its top was demolished—today it stands a third shorter than originally. The arch connecting it to the Cassero Senese (Sienese fortress) was built by Siena to watch over the inhabitants. From the tower, on clear days, the view reaches as far as Elba Island.

The Museo della Miniera, installed in a reconstructed underground gallery beneath the town, traces the millennial history of ore extraction in the Metalliferous Hills. The guided tour descends into the galleries and presents the tools and techniques for extracting argentiferous lead, copper, and pyrite that made Massa's fortune from the 9th to 20th centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

map_keywords: Cattedrale di San Cerbone Massa Marittima
Museo di Arte Sacra Massa Marittima

visits.text: Piazza Enrico Mattei — the heart of Matelica. Four palaces of entirely different styles face each other here, making it visually unique. On the left, the Palazzo Ottoni (1472), built by architects Costantino and Giovan Battista da Lugano for the Ottoni family — with its loggia and rear courtyard linked by an aerial walkway to another family property. Opposite, the Palazzo del Governo (1271), extended by the Loggia d'Ascanio Ottoni, so shallow it serves no practical purpose — pure urban ornament. To the right, the Palazzo Comunale, acquired from the Scotti di Narni family by the municipality in 1606. At the centre, the Fontana Ottagonale (1587), in white stone, by Lattanzio Ventura — the same architect who worked on the Santa Casa at Loreto.

Museo Piersanti — one of the finest museums in Le Marche. Monsignor Venanzio Filippo Piersanti, born in Matelica in 1688, began assembling a collection of art and historical objects in his palazzo in the 17th century. The museum spans three ground-floor rooms and sixteen on the upper floor. The palace has kept its domestic spaces intact: kitchen, cellar with winemaking equipment, bread tools, curing implements. An 1763 inventory documents everything that existed then, livestock included. The museum currently displays the Globo di Matelica — on loan since the closure of Palazzo Finaguerra after the 2016 earthquake: a 29-cm sphere of Greek marble found in 1985 during consolidation works. Dated to the 1st–2nd century AD, it is engraved with lines calculating hours of daylight, solstices, equinoxes and the passage of the sun through the zodiac constellations. One of the rarest objects in the world — a spherical sundial that is simultaneously astronomical and astrological, and whose commissioner and purpose remain unknown.

Teatro Piermarini — inaugurated in 1812, designed by Giuseppe Piermarini, the architect of Milan's La Scala. Excavations beneath the stage have uncovered Iron Age dwelling remains and the ruins of a 1st–2nd century Roman bath complex.

activities.text: Verdicchio di Matelica tasting — Matelica produces one of Le Marche's two Verdicchio DOC/DOCG wines. Different from the Jesi version, it is more structured and mineral, from an inland valley between the Sibillini and Monte San Vicino. The largest producer is the Cantina Belisario cooperative. Pair it with local honey: Matelica is nicknamed the 'City of Honey' for its chestnut, acacia and especially oak honeydew honeys — bitter, rare and prized by connoisseurs.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

places.name: Matera

visits.text: Morning drive from Aliano to Matera (1 hour 15 minutes). The arrival is spectacular: the Sassi—troglodyte districts carved into the limestone of a canyon—appear suddenly. Drop your bags and devote the afternoon to a first exploration of Sasso Barisano, the larger and more restored of the two troglodyte quarters. Its dwellings, carved into calcarenite (soft limestone), have been occupied since the Paleolithic and inhabited continuously until 1952, when the government evacuated 15,000 people living in deplorable conditions. Since the 1990s, the district has been rehabilitated—the caves have become hotels, restaurants, and workshops.

Climb to the Cattedrale di Matera (13th century), in Apulian Romanesque style, which dominates the Sassi from the summit of Civita, the rocky promontory between the two quarters. Its facade features a beautiful 16-ray rose window. From the parvis, the view plunges over Sasso Barisano below—it's one of the city's most iconic images.

Second day in Matera, devoted to Sasso Caveoso (south side), wilder and less touristy. Here you'll find the most impressive rock churches. The Chiesa di Santa Maria de Idris, carved into a rocky outcrop in the 13th century, preserves Byzantine frescoes from the 12th to 17th centuries. Below, the crypt of San Giovanni in Monterrone is even older. Allow a good hour to explore this maze of caves, staircases, and passages carved into stone.

Visit a Casa Grotta (reconstructed cave house) to understand how Sassi inhabitants lived until the 1950s: a single room for the entire family, livestock housed in the alcove at the back, an ingenious rainwater collection system via channels carved in the rock. The constant humidity and absence of natural light had created a catastrophic sanitary situation.

For contemporary art lovers, MUSMA (Museum of Contemporary Sculpture) is installed in the caves of Palazzo Pomarici, in Sasso Caveoso. Contemporary sculptures exhibited in cavities carved into rock millennia ago. The contrast between the prehistoric setting and modern works is striking.

activities.text: Enjoy sunset over the Sassi. The best spot is the Belvedere Murgia Timone, on the other side of the Gravina (the canyon), accessible by car from the SP3 road toward Laterza (10 minutes). From this natural viewpoint, the frontal view of the entire city illuminated by the low light is one of the most photographed panoramas in southern Italy. In summer, stay until the Sassi light up at dusk—the spectacle is magical.

Keep the rest of the day free to wander the Sassi at your own pace. Matera is a city that rewards exploration: the streets are sloped, the staircases countless, and beauty reveals itself in details—a half-open cave door, a carved limestone balcony, an unexpected view of the canyon. Get deliberately lost in Sasso Caveoso, push open the doors of artisan workshops, settle on a terrace in a cave-bar for coffee or aperitivo facing the tuff rooftops.

If you have the energy, cross the Gravina on foot to reach the Parco della Murgia Materana. The trail leads to rock churches lost in the scrubland, including the Chiesa della Madonna delle Tre Porte with its 12th-century frescoes. Allow 2 hours round trip. This isn't essential if you enjoyed the viewpoint the previous day, but the reverse perspective—seeing Matera from afar—is unique.

places.name: Medicina

visits.text: Oasi del Quadrone - a 270-hectare nature reserve established in 1985 by Italy's bird protection league (LIPU), one of Emilia-Romagna's first protected wildlife oases. The artificial wetland, surrounded by cereal fields and sunflowers, has recorded over 180 bird species, including little egrets and black-crowned night herons.

activities.text: Follow the 3 km waymarked trail from the visitor center through diverse habitats: reed beds, wet meadows, and willow copses. Interpretive panels explain the wildlife and ecosystems.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Medicina

visits.text: Arrive in Melfi, gateway to northern Basilicata, nestled at the foot of Monte Vulture, an extinct volcano. The Norman-Swabian castle dominates the town from atop its volcanic hill. With its 10 towers—7 rectangular and 3 pentagonal—and a moat once crossed by a drawbridge, it's one of the largest castles in southern Italy. Built by the Normans in the 11th century, it was expanded by Frederick II between 1221 and 1225, who constructed the Tower of the Seven Winds and made it his summer residence. Here, the emperor promulgated the Constitutions of Melfi (Liber Augustalis) in 1231, the first organized legal code of the Middle Ages, drafted with the help of Pier delle Vigne—whom Dante would later place in Hell.

Inside the castle, the National Archaeological Museum of Melfese displays finds from the Vulture region. Don't miss the Sarcophagus of Rapolla (2nd century AD), a Roman marble sarcophagus from Asia Minor discovered in 1856—one of the most important in southern Italy. The rooms also showcase Daunian pottery, bronze armor, and gold and amber jewelry dating from the 8th to 4th centuries BC.

Descend into Melfi's medieval borgo, still encircled by its ramparts. Pass through the Porta Venosina, one of the best-preserved medieval gates, and climb the narrow streets lined with noble palaces featuring sculpted stone portals. The cathedral, founded by Robert Guiscard in the 11th century, hosted no fewer than five papal councils between 1059 and 1137—including the 1089 council where Pope Urban II preached the First Crusade.

activities.text: In late afternoon, drive 20 minutes south to reach the Laghi di Monticchio, two volcanic lakes nestled in the ancient craters of Monte Vulture. The water is a deep green, surrounded by dense forests of oaks and beeches. On the shores of Lago Piccolo, the Abbey of San Michele, founded by Benedictine monks in the 8th century, clings to the cliff above the water. Allow an hour for a stroll around the lakes.

days_plan.itin_title: Basilicata: From Matera to Pollino, Castles, Ghost Towns, and the Lucanian Dolomites

visits.text: Stroll along Menaggio's lakefront, a promenade bordered by flowerbeds and painted houses extending from Piazza Garibaldi. Menaggio forms the third point of the 'golden triangle' with Bellagio and Varenna; its ferry port connects these three villages in 15 to 30 minutes. The upper part of the town, medieval in aspect with its alleys and stone buildings, preserves the ruins of a 12th-century castle. The local baroque church, painted pink, is adorned with paintings on columns and walls up to the dome above the altar.

Climb to Loveno, a hamlet of Menaggio, to visit the gardens of Villa Mylius Vigoni. The villa was purchased in 1829 by Enrico Mylius, fascinated by its position. Designed between 1855 and 1865 by architect Giuseppe Balzaretti over 8 hectares in an amphitheater, the romantic park is one of the most intact examples of this type in Lombardy: centuries-old trees, fountains, marble statues, sculptural groups, and small commemorative temples. The villa was bequeathed in 1983 to the Federal Republic of Germany and now houses the Villa Vigoni Italian-German Center for European Excellence.

activities.text: Hike in Val Sanagra, a natural wildlife reserve accessible from Menaggio, following the Sanagra torrent to the Tobi waterfalls through forests and ancient rural villages. The trail is family-friendly for the first few kilometers, then becomes more technical at the height of Sass Corbee. A shorter variant from Croce leads to the Chiesetta degli Alpini and Monte Crocetta (views of Lake Como from the WWI Cadorna Line defense positions).

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona
Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

visits.text: Tappeinerweg – a 4-kilometer panoramic promenade built in 1893 at the initiative of Dr. Franz Tappeiner, a Viennese physician and botanist, along the heights of the Passer River's left bank, 100-300 meters above the city. The subtropical vegetation—palms, cacti, agaves, lemon trees—thrives thanks to Merano's exceptional microclimate (270 sunny days per year, 300mm rainfall), created by a mountain corridor sheltered from northern winds by the Ötztal Alps.

Passeggiata Lungo Passirio and historic center – the banks of the Passer River were transformed in the late 19th century into therapeutic promenades for spa visitors, lined with chestnut and willow trees. Merano became the most fashionable spa resort in the Austro-Hungarian Empire after Empress Elisabeth of Austria (Sissi) visited in 1870 and 1871, popularizing the grape cure still practiced every autumn. The historic center preserves the Gothic arcades of Via dei Portici (14th-15th centuries) and the 14th-century Duomo San Nicola.

Giardini di Castel Trauttmansdorff – 12-hectare terraced botanical gardens on the Maia Alta hillside, created in 1906 around the castle where Empress Elisabeth stayed in 1870. The castle was purchased by the Province of Bolzano in 1980 and the gardens opened to the public in 2001. The 80 themed zones showcase 1,000 plant species from five continents, including 20-meter bamboos, giant sequoias, and collections of heritage roses.

Castello Principesco (Landesfürstliche Burg) – residential castle built in 1470 by Sigismund, Archduke of Tyrol, on the foundations of a 13th-century fortified house. One of the rare preserved examples of Tyrolean Gothic residential architecture, featuring carved wooden interior halls, 15th-century ceramic stove tiles, and original furnishings. Property of the Province of Bolzano since 1948.

activities.text: Hike the Tappeinerweg from Merano's center to Brunnenburg Castle – a 6-kilometer loop with 200 meters of elevation gain, offering views over the Passer Valley and terraced vineyards.

Wine tasting in the Burggrafenamt – the wine region around Merano has produced Meraner Wein DOC since 1971, featuring rare varietals like Vernatsch (Schiava) cultivated in low Tyrolean pergola style since the Middle Ages.

days_plan.itin_title: Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Dolomites Loop: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Pastures & Limestone Peaks

places.name: Mercatello Sul Metauro

visits.text: Piazza Garibaldi and the medieval heart — Mercatello sul Metauro was born in 1235 around the ancient pieve di San Pietro d'Ico — later the Collegiata dei Santi Pietro e Paolo — as a market village in the Metauro valley. 'Little market' is the literal meaning of its name. In the 13th century it became the common home of seven surrounding castles, transformed into a fortified commune. The generously proportioned Piazza Garibaldi, large for a village of this size, testifies to that past importance. It is lined by the Palazzo Gasparini (17th century, Tuscan style), the Palazzo Comunale — which preserves the Pergamena Stefani recording the founding on 21 March 1235 — and the Pieve Collegiata. The Monte di Pietà of 1516 retains its original period interior fittings.

Pieve Collegiata dei Santi Pietro e Paolo — a Romanesque structure with origins in the 10th century, rebuilt in Gothic style in 1363 — both phases are legible on the outer walls. Façade from 1927. In the large nave consecrated in 1730, the Maria Immacolata painted in 1556 by Raffaellino del Colle, one of Raphael's last pupils. The centrepiece: the Madonna delle Grazie, a Byzantine icon from the mid-13th century (141 × 50 cm), preserved in a 17th-century carved and gilded wooden reliquary.

Chiesa di San Francesco and Museo d'Arte Sacra — a gem of 13th–14th-century primitive Romanesque-Gothic architecture. In the choir hangs the Crocifisso of 1309 by Giovanni da Rimini, a pupil of Giotto — one of the ten most important Gothic crucifixes in Italy, where Giotto's lesson in human truth meets the decorative tradition of Ravenna. The museum also holds a Polyptych from the Rimini school, frescoes by Umbrian-Marchigian artists, panels by Pandolfi and Guerrieri, and a 13th-century Madonna col Bambino by Bonaventura di Michele.

Via del Metauro, Porta Metauro and the 'porte del morto' — Via del Metauro is the village's medieval street, reached through Porta Metauro — the only one of the original four gates still standing. All along this 13th–14th-century street, look for the porte del morto: doorways walled up beside the main entrance, reopened only long enough to carry out a corpse, then bricked up again. A central Italian medieval tradition intended to prevent the soul of the dead from returning through the habitual door.

Three-arch Roman bridge over the Metauro — from Porta Metauro, the Roman bridge appears immediately: three massive arches spanning the Metauro, the longest river in Le Marche. Nearby, the convent of the Clarissan Capuchins stands on the birthplace of Santa Veronica Giuliani (1660–1727), a mystic canonised in 1839.

activities.text: Walk down to the Metauro and the Cascata del Sasso — cross Porta Metauro, walk over the Roman bridge and follow the Metauro riverbank downstream. A few kilometres toward Sant'Angelo in Vado, the Cascata del Sasso (also known as Balza del Metauro) is signposted from the main road: 12 metres high, 60 metres wide, considered one of the most beautiful waterfalls in Italy. Two viewpoints accessible on foot — one elevated for a bird's-eye view, one at the base for a head-on perspective.

Visit the Museo d'Arte Sacra di San Francesco — an hour inside the church-museum to see Giovanni da Rimini's Crocifisso in its original architectural setting, then explore the full sacred art collection spanning the 13th to 17th centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Collegiata dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Mercatello sul Metauro
Chiesa di San Francesco Mercatello sul Metauro

visits.text: Messina Cathedral, rebuilt after the 1908 earthquake and 1943 bombings, preserves its bell tower housing the world's largest mechanical astronomical clock, built by Strasbourg's Ungerer in 1933. Each day at noon, its automatons—the lion of Messina, the rooster, death, the Three Kings—perform for 15 minutes.

The Strait of Messina, 3 km wide at its narrowest point between Sicily and Calabria, ranks among the world's busiest maritime passages with 200,000 vessels annually. Its opposing currents create the ancient Charybdis—a whirlpool identified by Homer in the Odyssey. The Calabrian coast is visible to the naked eye from the quays.

The Regional Museum of Messina preserves two Caravaggios painted during the artist's 1609 Sicilian sojourn—The Adoration of the Shepherds and The Raising of Lazarus—commissioned by local confraternities. These works rank among the master's final paintings, created one year before his death.

activities.text: Seafood lunch featuring fish caught in the strait: pesce spada (swordfish) alla messinese—with capers, olives, tomatoes, and pine nuts—a specialty exclusive to the strait where swordfish migrate from May to August.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

visits.text: The Duomo di Milano, begun in 1386 under Duke Gian Galeazzo Visconti's orders, mobilized hundreds of artisans from across Europe over five centuries—from Burgundy, the Rhineland, Bohemia—to erect one of the world's largest Gothic cathedrals: 158 meters long, 108.5 meters to the tip of the Madonnina, 3,400 statues distributed across 135 spires and pinnacles, entirely clad in pink Candoglia marble quarried from the shores of Lake Maggiore. Napoleon Bonaparte financed the completion of the façade in 1805 to be crowned King of Italy here. Climbing to the rooftop terraces brings you face-to-face with these sculptures and offers sweeping views across Milan's urban fabric.

The Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II, inaugurated on September 15, 1867 by King Victor Emmanuel II, connects Piazza del Duomo to Piazza della Scala across 196 meters. Its architect Giuseppe Mengoni died tragically in a fall from scaffolding the day before the triumphal arch was completed in December 1877. The central glass-and-iron dome soars to 47 meters—one of the first of its kind in Europe. On the floor, a mosaic depicts the bull from Turin's coat of arms: tradition holds that spinning your heel on the animal's genitals brings good luck, which explains why the motif requires regular restoration.

The Brera district grew up around its Academy of Fine Arts, founded in 1776, which drew painters and sculptors until this quarter became Milan's bohemian heart. Its cobbled lanes still shelter numerous art galleries, antique dealers, and artisan workshops. The Pinacoteca di Brera, housed in the baroque palazzo of the same name, holds one of Italy's most important collections of Italian painting.

The Castello Sforzesco originated from a fortress built in 1358 by Galeazzo II Visconti. After the fall of the Visconti in 1447 and a brief republican period that saw the castle demolished, Francesco Sforza had it entirely rebuilt from 1450, entrusting Florentine architect Filarete in 1452 with the construction of the 70-meter central tower. His successor Ludovico il Moro commissioned Leonardo da Vinci to decorate several halls, including the Sala delle Asse (1496–1497) with frescoes depicting intertwined tree branches. The castle now houses seven municipal museums, including one devoted to Michelangelo's final unfinished work, the Pietà Rondanini.

The church of Santa Maria delle Grazie was built from 1463 by architect Guiniforte Solari, then profoundly remodeled in the late 15th century by Bramante, who added a large apse with hemispherical dome. Ludovico il Moro had made it the Sforza family mausoleum after his wife Beatrice d'Este died in 1497. In the adjoining refectory, Leonardo da Vinci painted The Last Supper between 1495 and 1498 on commission from Ludovico: a tempera-on-plaster mural measuring 460 × 880 cm. He deliberately abandoned the traditional fresco technique in order to refine and rework his painting, which fragility set in by the 16th century. King Louis XII of France wanted to detach it from the wall to carry it off—an operation deemed impossible. Visits are limited to groups of 25 people maximum, advance booking mandatory.

activities.text: Stroll along the Navigli at sunset—the old canal network dug from the 12th century onward to transport Candoglia marble to the Duomo construction site. At its peak the network stretched over 150 kilometers of navigable waterways. The remodeled embankments now host bars and restaurants that come alive during aperitivo hour.

Explore Milan's main neighborhoods on foot or by historic tram (Line 1) to grasp the contrasts between the neoclassical architecture downtown, the early-20th-century Art Nouveau buildings, and the contemporary towers of the Porta Nuova district.

days_plan.itin_title: Italian Lakes Road Trip: Como, Maggiore, Orta and Garda
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona
Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine
Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome
Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome

visits.text: Villa Romana di Minori - a 1st-century AD Roman seaside villa, the only archaeological site on the Amalfi Coast open to the public. This 21,500-square-foot complex includes baths with hypocaust underfloor heating, reception halls decorated with frescoes and opus sectile floors, and a terraced garden overlooking the sea. Unearthed in 1932 during construction work, the villa likely belonged to a wealthy Roman merchant engaged in maritime trade across the Tyrrhenian Sea.

Basilica di Santa Trofimena - a basilica built in the 9th century to house the relics of Saint Trophimena, a 3rd-century Sicilian martyr whose body, according to legend, drifted to Minori in an unmanned boat. The 9th-century crypt preserves the original white marble sarcophagus. The current building, reconstructed after the 1954 earthquake, faithfully reproduces the proportions of the early Christian basilica.

activities.text: Tasting ndunderi di Minori - ricotta-filled pasta documented since the Middle Ages, considered one of Italy's oldest stuffed pasta shapes, served with tomato-basil sauce or ragù. The recipe is registered in Italy's traditional agri-food products (PAT) registry.
Strolling Minori's lungomare among fishing boats and potted lemon trees in front of the houses - this 1,300-foot waterfront is one of the few on the coast accessible on foot without stairs.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages of the Amalfi Coast: From Praiano to Salerno

places.name: Minturno

visits.text: The Archaeological Complex of Minturnae, on the Via Appia at km 156, preserves the vestiges of the Roman port-city founded in 295 BC on the ruins of an Auruncian town. The 1st-century AD Roman theater, built in opus reticulatum, accommodated more than 4,000 spectators; its restored cavea still serves as a setting for summer performances since 1960. Beneath the tiers, the Antiquarium displays statues, ex-votos, epigraphs, and coins recovered from the Garigliano River. An original section of the Via Appia, paved with basalt blocks, crosses the site. It was at Minturnae that General Gaius Marius, fleeing Sulla's assassins, hid in the marshes before being discovered—an episode recounted by Plutarch.

The Church of San Pietro Apostolo, the main church of Minturno's hilltop village, stands at the heart of the medieval historic center. Some of its marbles come directly from the ancient site of Minturnae, reused over the centuries in local constructions.

The Castello Baronale dominates the historic center from an elevated position. The village of Minturno was called Traetto until 1879, a name inherited from the Middle Ages when inhabitants of the ancient city, driven to the heights by the Lombards around 580 and by the swamping of the plain, rebuilt their village on the hill.

activities.text: Walk through Minturno's historic center (formerly Traetto), a hilltop village with picturesque alleyways and views over the Garigliano valley. The Memory Trail passes commemorative plaques recalling World War II events; near the archaeological site stands the British Commonwealth War Cemetery, where 2,049 soldiers who fell in 1943-1944 rest.

Pass by the Ponte Real Ferdinando over the Garigliano, Italy's first iron suspension bridge, designed by engineer Luigi Giura and inaugurated in 1832 by King Ferdinand II of the Two Sicilies. The bridge connected the Papal States to the Kingdom of Naples. Destroyed during World War II, it was restored in 1998 and can be visited.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Pietro Apostolo Minturno

visits.text: Discover Villa Foscari La Malcontenta, another stunning Palladian masterpiece rising elegantly from the banks of the Brenta Canal. This 16th-century villa showcases Andrea Palladio's genius for proportion and harmony, with breathtaking interior frescoes that rank among the finest examples of Venetian villa decoration.

Explore the Brenta Riviera, the historic waterway connecting Padua to Venice that was once lined with over 70 noble villas. The canal served as the summer escape route for Venetian aristocracy, who would travel by burchiello (traditional boat) to their countryside retreats.

activities.text: Consider a scenic boat cruise along the Brenta Canal, following the historic route that Venetian nobles traveled for centuries. The journey passes multiple villas and offers a unique perspective on this remarkable concentration of architectural heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Leaving Parma, stop in Reggio Emilia, the city that gave birth to the Italian flag. Head straight to Piazza Prampolini, the city's heart, where the cathedral and town hall stand. Inside this communal palace is the Sala del Tricolore, where on January 7, 1797, representatives from Reggio, Modena, Bologna, and Ferrara adopted the green-white-red flag as the emblem of the Cispadane Republic—forerunner of unified Italy. The room, designed by architect Lodovico Bolognini in 1774, is free to visit. The small adjacent Tricolore Museum traces the flag's history with period documents.

A few steps from Piazza Prampolini, step into the Basilica della Ghiara, one of the region's finest Baroque buildings. Built between 1597 and 1619, it preserves remarkable frescoes and a miraculous image of the Virgin. The contrast with the Romanesque austerity of the neighboring cathedral is striking. Allow 20 minutes.

Arriving in Modena by midday, make your way to Piazza Grande. Modena's cathedral, begun in 1099, is a masterpiece of Lombard Romanesque art, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List since 1997 along with its bell tower, the Ghirlandina, and the square itself. Inside, don't miss the richly sculpted 13th-century rood screen that separates the nave from the elevated choir above the crypt—quite rare in Italian churches. Cathedral entry is free.

The Torre Ghirlandina, the cathedral's bell tower, rises 86 meters and dominates all of Modena. If you have the energy to climb, the view over the city's terracotta rooftops and surrounding hills is worth the effort. Allow 30 minutes for the ascent and descent.

The Palazzo Ducale, imposing residence of the Este dukes who ruled Modena from 1598 to 1859, sits just a few minutes' walk away. The palace now houses the Military Academy—you can't visit inside, but the facade and courtyard merit a detour. The Este family had made Modena a refined little capital after being forced to leave Ferrara.

activities.text: Modena is the capital of traditional balsamic vinegar PDO, a product that bears no resemblance to the industrial balsamic vinegar sold in supermarkets. Here, grape must (from Trebbiano and Lambrusco) is slowly cooked then aged for 12 to 25 years in batteries of small barrels made from oak, cherry, juniper, and mulberry, stored in house attics. Local producers open their doors to reveal this fascinating process. Book ahead—it's an experience you won't forget.

For lunch or late afternoon, visit the Mercato Albinelli, Modena's historic covered market open since 1931. Under its beautiful halls, you'll find stalls selling cured meats, cheeses, fresh pasta, and of course balsamic vinegar. You can buy on the spot and sit down to eat—a very local way to discover Modenese gastronomy. Try the tortellini, the region's specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills

visits.text: Mombaldone is the only village in the Langa Astigiana still encircled by its intact original walls, with its urban plan unchanged since foundation. The first written mention dates to 991, in the foundation act of the abbey of San Quintino in Spigno Monferrato. In 1208, the fief passed to the Del Carretto family, Marquises of Finale Ligure. In the seventeenth century, the village alone resisted Napoleonic troops - armor and muskets from French soldiers are still found in the Bormida plain - while the rest of Piedmont opened its doors to the victorious army. A once-imposing square tower, now partially ruined, still dominates the village summit.

Walk through the single main street from which branch alleys and vaulted passages, interior courtyards and portals carved from centuries-old wood. Notice the engraved lintels and stone arches testifying to unchanged medieval craftsmanship. The Palazzo Fortezza, partially inhabited by descendants of the Del Carretto family - whose name spans more than eight centuries of continuous presence - faces the main street.

Leaving the village, climb a few hundred meters toward the surrounding countryside. The calanchi are deep ravines carved by erosion in the gray sandstone of the Langa Astigiana hills - a lunar and overwhelming landscape, unique in this wine region. Herds of goats whose milk produces Robiola di Roccaverano DOP graze these slopes.

activities.text: Hiking or cycling in the Bormida di Spigno valley, on trails crossing fields and vineyards. Canoeing on the Bormida for the more athletic, possible depending on water levels. In August, the Historia Montis Baudonis historical reenactment and classical music festival animate the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: Monacilioni: medieval village of fewer than 300 inhabitants, stone houses clinging to the hill above the Biferno plain. Chapel of Santa Reparata, which houses the relics of Santa Benedetta.

activities.text: Wander the lanes and viewpoints of the village.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

visits.text: Rocca Roveresca — masterpiece of Renaissance military architecture — Built between 1482 and 1492 on the orders of Giovanni Della Rovere — nephew of Pope Sixtus IV and son-in-law of Federico da Montefeltro, Duke of Urbino — by Sienese architect Francesco di Giorgio Martini. The mastio is octagonal with a broad base, its ten irregular trapezoidal faces tapering upward in a helical spiral: a geometry designed to deflect cannonballs by presenting oblique angles rather than flat walls. Martini himself wrote in his treatise of 'geometrie che disegno mostrar non può' — forms that drawing alone cannot convey. A torricino di rinfianco protects the walkway leading to a semi-circular torrione, connected by an entrance ravelin. Seen from above, the ensemble traces a crossbow — Martini's habitual signature. The rocca was never struck by a single cannonball. Converted into a prison from 1631 until the 19th century after the Della Rovere line died out. Left unfinished following the simultaneous deaths of both Martini and Giovanni Della Rovere in 1501. In the moat, since 2000, reconstructed war machines built after Martini's original drawings.

Museum of historical re-enactment — 5 levels — From the basement to the top floor of the mastio: an armoury in the ravelin (a mascolo — the terminal section of a bombard weighing more than six quintals — along with moschettoni, falconetti, spingarde, halberds and ornately decorated bladed weapons), then four floors of reconstructed Renaissance scenes: a wedding banquet, a prison and torture chamber, an oven, a munitions store. Period-costumed mannequins inhabit every room. Single ticket including the Museo Civico and the village theatre.

Piazza Della Rovere and Porta Cavour — the main square was created in the 17th century by vaulting over the rocca's northern moat. It still bears the name of the commissioning family. Porta Cavour, the village gateway, is a well-preserved circular cassero.

activities.text: Full visit of the Rocca Roveresca — walk all five levels of the mastio, through the narrow passages and spiral staircases that give a concrete sense of life in a 15th-century military castle. End on the camminamenti di ronda to observe from outside the mastio's ten irregular faces and the war machines in the moat.

Caccia al Cinghiale — historical re-enactment (13–15 August) — Three days of Renaissance living history: Mondavio's historical archers in period costume, tournaments, crossbow competitions, banquets with local products in a village transformed into a 15th-century settlement.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

places.name: Mondolfo

visits.text: Double ring of walls — Mondolfo is one of the few villages in Le Marche with two concentric circuits of walls. The inner ring dates in part to the 6th century (partially visible from the Byzantine period), the outer to the 15th-century medieval construction. The 19th-century Porta Fanestre and Porta Santa Maria give access to the outer enclosure. The walkways along the walls lead to a belvedere from which, on a clear day, the Croatian coast is visible.

Palazzo Comunale and Torre Civica — a neo-Gothic building from 1931, rebuilt with bricks salvaged from the original structure destroyed by an earthquake the same year. Three symbols decorate the façade: the municipal coat of arms in gold and azure (the colours of fields and sea), the campanone San Gervaso, and a stone escutcheon held in a lion's mouth — the city's heraldic device. The central square bears the name of Piazza Mario del Monaco.

Bastione di Sant'Anna and Belvedere — a former military bastion converted into an Italian-style garden with a limonaia (orangery). The belvedere at the top offers a panorama over the valley, the coast at Marotta and Monte Conero to the south. On clear days the Croatian coastline is visible on the horizon.

Galleria senza soffitto — an open-air museum spread throughout the village. Murals, black-and-white photographs by Mario Giacomelli — a photographer from nearby Senigallia, one of the major figures in 20th-century Italian photography, whose series Io non ho mani che mi accarezzano il volto was inspired by a poem by David Maria Turoldo. The Scalinata del Pianoforte is one of the village's most photographed landmarks.

Chiesa di Sant'Agostino — a large single-nave church with barrel vault and twelve gilded carved wooden altars. Two 17th-century paintings stand out: Madonna del Rosario and Madonna col Bambino. Above the entrance, a 1776 organ by Gaetano Callido, one of the greatest Venetian organ builders of the 18th century. The campanone known as 'Cristo Re' is the one that rings on Sundays in a concert of bells.

Abbazia di San Gervasio di Bulgaria — a 5th–6th-century abbey a few hundred metres from the centre, founded on the Via Flaminia. Its basilica plan is directly inspired by the great basilicas of Ravenna. In the crypt, the largest Ravenna-style sarcophagus in Le Marche, dating to the 6th century, decorated with Christian symbols, crosses, laurel wreaths and two peacocks symbolising immortality. One of the oldest archaeological sites in the province.

activities.text: Walk the camminamenti di ronda to the belvedere — follow the walls from inside the village along the old walkways to the highest panoramic point. Along the way, an old cannon recalls the bastion's defensive past.

Museo Civico — the 1858 clock mechanism — installed in the cloister adjoining the Chiesa di Sant'Agostino. The centrepiece: an 1858 clock movement, still working, wound by hand, chiming every 15 minutes. Exhibition on accordion makers — a craft tradition of the region.

La Cacciata — historical re-enactment (summer) — a Renaissance living history event featuring the pallone col bracciale (a ball game played with a leather brace, once as popular in 16th-century Italy as football is today), sword tournaments, archery and flag-throwing. Banquets with local products.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Abbazia di San Gervasio di Bulgaria Mondolfo

visits.text: Monforte d'Alba - the Mons Fortis - takes its name from the ramparts that once crowned the hill at 480 meters. In 1028, Milan's Archbishop Aribert d'Intimiano besieged the village to eradicate a Cathar community. The deported population, condemned to the stake in Milan after refusing to recant, suffered an episode that preceded the Béziers massacres by two centuries. The medieval center has preserved its fan-shaped plan following the contour lines, with tortuous alleys bordered by carefully restored stone buildings.

The Auditorium Horszowski is an open-air venue carved into the hill's natural morphology - a 800-seat cavea whose exceptional acoustics are produced by the terrain configuration between the Romanesque bell tower (sole remnant of the old church demolished in the early twentieth century), the baroque oratory of Santa Elisabetta, and the walls of Palazzo Scarampi. Pianist Mieczysław Horszowski, then 91 years old, inaugurated it in 1986. Since then, the Monfortinjazz festival has welcomed internationally renowned artists every summer, from Paolo Conte to Caetano Veloso.

Palazzo Scarampi, built in the eighteenth century on the ruins of the medieval castle (itself rebuilt after 1706 by the Del Carretto family then restored in 1833), now houses the town hall. Its facade opens directly onto the auditorium and serves as a backdrop for concerts. Right beside it, the oratory of Saint Augustine and Saint Boniface presents a brick facade and a bronze portal with medieval symbols.

activities.text: Wine walk through the UNESCO-classified Langhe vineyards (2014) - Monforte is one of the 11 Barolo DOCG production communes. Local estates offer cellar visits with tastings of Barolo, Nebbiolo, and Dolcetto d'Alba.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

places.name: Monopoli

visits.text: Polignano a Mare — clifftop old town: founded by Greeks as Neapolis apula, the town perches on 20-to-25-meter tufa cliffs. Romans ran the Via Traiana through here — the stone bridge spanning Lama Monachile beach is a direct remnant. Venetians resided here from 1506 onward — the Palazzo del Doge on the main square preserves the memory. The old town is a maze of lanes where poetic inscriptions are painted on the walls.

Polignano a Mare — Lama Monachile and viewpoints: this white-pebble cove framed by limestone cliffs takes its name from the monk seal (monachile) that populated the coast until the Middle Ages. The Bourbon bridge spanning it dates to the 19th century. Terrazza Santo Stefano and the Modugno belvedere offer the best elevated views. Polignano's coast holds 80 natural caves carved into the limestone, including Grotta Palazzese — converted into a ballroom in 1730 by the Duke Leto of Naples.

Monopoli — Charles V Castle and old port: built between 1552 and 1567 on Charles V's orders to defend the Adriatic coast against Ottoman incursions, the Castello di Carlo V is integrated into the urban fabric of the waterfront. The old port, ringed by medieval ramparts, maintains an animation of fishing and pleasure boats. Cala Porta Vecchia beach opens directly beneath the old ramparts.

Monopoli — historic center and Cathedral of Maria Santissima della Madia: in the 16th century, according to local legend, a raft bearing a Byzantine icon of the Virgin washed ashore at Monopoli. The baroque cathedral (17th-18th century) was built in her honor. The icon, named Madonna della Madia, is still preserved inside.

activities.text: Boat excursion from Polignano a Mare: coastal outings let you see the municipality's 80 caves from the water, including Grotta Azzurra Monachile and Grotta Palazzese viewed from the sea. Kayak or paddleboard options available from Cala Paura beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

map_keywords: Centro Storico Polignano a Mare Polignano a Mare

visits.text: Monreale Cathedral, built by William II between 1172 and 1189, shelters 6,340 square meters of Byzantine mosaics—the largest mosaic surface in the world after Istanbul's Hagia Sophia. The 130 scenes from the Old and New Testaments were created by Constantinople craftsmen under the direction of Benedictine monks.

Monreale's cloister, formed by 228 twin columns—no two identical—blends mosaics, Arab interlacing, lion heads, and Apocalypse scenes. The southwest corner houses an Arab fountain with a twisted column, the sole remnant of the original Norman royal palace's Islamic architecture.

activities.text: Lunch at the trattorias surrounding the cathedral with views over the Conca d'Oro—the golden basin of orange and lemon groves encircling Palermo—before driving to Trapani (100 km, ~1h40).

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

visits.text: Visit Monselice Castle, housing one of the finest collections of medieval arms and armor in the Veneto, second only to the Doge's Palace in Venice. The castle's remarkable fireplaces are themselves works of art, showcasing the refined craftsmanship of medieval artisans.

Climb the Seven Churches Sanctuary (Santuario delle Sette Chiese), a pilgrimage route of seven chapels ascending the hillside that once granted the same indulgences as visiting Rome's seven pilgrimage churches. The Via Sacra offers beautiful views of the surrounding hills.
Explore the Old Cathedral and the elegant Villa Duodo, designed by Vincenzo Scamozzi, surrounded by gardens and offering panoramic views from its hillside position.

activities.text: Walk the fortified historic center, where medieval walls, towers, and gates create an authentic atmosphere. The town's strategic hilltop position made it an important defensive stronghold for centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

places.name: Montagnana

visits.text: Discover Montagnana, renowned for producing some of Italy's finest prosciutto and encircled by one of Europe's best-preserved medieval defensive walls. The nearly complete circuit of walls, punctuated by 24 towers, creates a dramatic silhouette visible for miles across the Veneto plain.

Explore the castle, the elegant main square, and the stunning Cathedral with its notable works of art. The town center retains its medieval character within the protective embrace of the mighty fortifications.

activities.text: Experience the annual Prosciutto Festival celebrating the local cured ham tradition. The town's Prosciutto Veneto Berico-Euganeo DOP is produced following centuries-old methods in the unique microclimate of the Euganean Hills.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Duomo di Santa Maria Assunta Montagnana

places.name: Montalcino

visits.text: Fortress of Montalcino: Built in 1361 by reusing earlier 13th-century structures, this pentagonal fortress was the last bastion to resist during the fall of the Republic of Siena to the Medici in 1555—Sienese refugees held out here until 1559. Its interior enoteca allows you to taste Brunello in the very symbol of republican resistance. From the ramparts, the view stretches across Val d'Orcia, Monte Amiata, and the Maremma.

Piazza del Popolo and Palazzo dei Priori: The 13th-century communal palace, adorned with heraldic coats of arms from Montalcino's history, has a slender bell tower whose carillons still punctuate the life of the town. It's the central square of a network of narrow, steep lanes typical of Sienese medieval urbanism.

Civic and Diocesan Museum of Sacred Art: Housed in the former 14th-century convent of Sant'Agostino (with its two 16th-century cloisters), this museum preserves over 200 works on three floors, including the 12th-century Crucifix of Sant'Antimo, a Madonna by Simone Martini, and the Coronation of the Virgin by Bartolo di Fredi.

Abbey of Sant'Antimo: Ten kilometers south via the SP55, in a valley hollow planted with cypresses and olive trees, this Benedictine Romanesque abbey is mentioned as early as 813 in an act of Louis the Pious. The main church, built in the 12th century in the Cistercian Burgundian style, is one of the finest examples of French Romanesque architecture in Italy. Since 1992, a community of Premonstratensian canons has sung the Gregorian offices—vespers at 6:30 PM can be heard from afar.

activities.text: Brunello di Montalcino tasting: Brunello is produced exclusively from Sangiovese Grosso on the commune's territory. Clemente Santi was the first to get it recognized in 1869. The fortress cellars or surrounding estates (Poggio di Sotto, Ciacci Piccolomini) offer tastings of the current vintage and riserva.
Sagra del Tordo: Every last Sunday in October, Montalcino's four districts compete in archery in this medieval festival commemorating the region's hunting traditions.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Museo Civico e Diocesano d'Arte Sacra Montalcino

places.name: Monte Sole

visits.text: Parco Storico di Monte Sole - a 6,300-hectare park between the Reno and Setta valleys, established in 1989 as a memorial to the Marzabotto massacre. Between September 29 and October 5, 1944, SS troops from the 16th Panzergrenadier Division murdered 770 civilians (women, children, elderly) in the Monte Sole villages—the largest massacre of civilians in Western Europe during World War II. The devastated villages of Marzabotto, Caprara, and Casaglia are preserved as ruins.

activities.text: Walk the 8 km waymarked memorial trail linking the park's ruined villages, with interpretive panels at massacre sites. The silence of the oak and chestnut forest that has reclaimed the ruins makes the visit particularly haunting.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Martino Monte Sole

places.name: Montecarlo Toscana

visits.text: Rocca di Cerruglio: The village of Montecarlo owes its name to Charles IV of Luxembourg—Mons Charoli in Latin—who contributed in 1333 to liberating Lucca from Pisan occupation. The Republic of Lucca then had the destroyed village rebuilt on Cerruglio hill and erected this three-tower fortress: the semi-circular keep, the Tower of the Apparition, and the Tower of Saint Barbara, the latter named for a legend that the Virgin appeared to defend the village during a 15th-century Pisan siege. The fortress is open to visitors with views over the plain of Lucca and the Valdinievole.

Collegiata di Sant'Andrea: In the heart of the village, this collegiate church founded in the 14th century preserves its original Gothic façade and portal, remodeled in Baroque style in the 18th century. Inside, a fresco of the 15th-century Florentine school depicts the Madonna del Soccorso snatching a child from the devil's claws. Two plaques commemorate visits by poet Giuseppe Ungaretti in the 1960s and composer Giacomo Puccini.

Teatro dei Rassicurati: Built in the early 18th century, this tiny Italian-style theater ranks among the five smallest in Italy. It takes its name from the Academy of the Rassicurati, a local learned society founded in 1690. Puccini visited several times. The auditorium, with its lacquered wooden boxes and ceiling frescoes, has remained almost unchanged since construction.

Pieve di San Piero in Campo: At the foot of the hill, this 12th-century Romanesque parish church with three naves is one of the oldest Christian buildings in Lucca province. Its sober limestone façade and austere interior contrast with the Baroque exuberance of the collegiate church perched atop the village.

activities.text: Montecarlo DOC tasting at family estates: Montecarlo's winemaking tradition dates to Roman times, but it was in 1870 that a local landowner imported French grape varieties from Bordeaux (Sauvignon, Sémillon, Cabernet, Merlot) and Burgundy (Pinot Blanc, Pinot Gris) to blend with Tuscan Trebbiano. Montecarlo Bianco DOC—recognized in 1969—has been celebrated since the 15th century: Pope Gregory XII praised it in the early 1400s. Village estates welcome visitors year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

map_keywords: Pieve di San Piero in Campo Montecarlo

places.name: Montecassiano

visits.text: Palazzo dei Priori — a Gothic 14th-century building dominating the Piazza Unità d'Italia, with crenellated crown, a banditore balcony and a grand connecting arch to the Palazzo Compagnucci. A Roman funerary stele of Cassia Orestina, preserved inside the palazzo, testifies to the village's post-Roman origins — traditionally said to have been founded by nobles fleeing the destruction of Helvia Ricina in 408. The Pinacoteca Civica Girolamo Buratto, in the Pompeian-decorated rooms of Palazzo Compagnucci, holds a rare work by Ioannes Hispanus (a 16th-century Iberian painter, Madonna in Trono con Santi) and paintings by Girolamo Buratto, a pupil of the Pomarancio.

Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta — founded by the monks of Fiastra Abbey. It holds the village's masterpiece: an enamelled and painted terracotta altarpiece by Fra' Mattia della Robbia (1527–1532), the only authenticated work by one of the last members of Florence's famous della Robbia dynasty.

Museo di Arte Sacra 'Giovanni da San Guglielmo' — installed in the 16th-century Chiesa di San Giovanni Battista. It holds the oldest bell in Le Marche, cast in 1358, along with reliquaries, chalices and liturgical goldsmithery by master silversmith Domenico Piani.

Mura e Parco del Cerreto — 15th-century walls with three crenellated gate towers (Porta San Giovanni, Porta Diaz, Porta Battisti). From the terrace of the Parco del Cerreto (1.2 hectares), panoramic views west to the Sibillini and east to the Adriatic.

activities.text: Walk the concentric lanes of the village — Montecassiano is shaped like an onion, with alleyways spiralling toward the central piazza. Via Catena, Vicolo Santa Chiara, Vicolo San Francesco: look for the inner courtyards visible through half-open doorways.

Try the sughitti — sweet polenta made with grape must, cornmeal and walnuts, prepared after the grape harvest since at least the 15th century. Once a peasant dish offered to local dignitaries as a mark of esteem.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta Montecassiano

visits.text: The Terme Tettuccio constitutes the most representative building of European Belle Époque thermalism: rebuilt in the 1920s by architect Ugo Giovannozzi, it combines a circular colonnade, an English-style garden with fountains, and four drinking galleries where the waters of the Tettuccio, Rinfresco, Regina and Leopoldina springs flow. The interior frescoes and mosaics are the work of Galileo Chini, Italy's leading Art Nouveau artist. Montecatini has been inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list since 2021 in the "Great Spas of Europe" category.

Montecatini Alto, accessible via the funicular inaugurated in 1898—the oldest still in service according to Wikipedia—is the ancient medieval village perched at 300 meters altitude above the spa town. The town walls were razed in 1554 by Cosimo I's Florentines during their conquest of the territory. The village preserves some cobbled lanes, the Rocca, and panoramic views across the entire Valdinievole. The 8-minute journey aboard the historic red carriages "Gigio" and "Gigia" is an experience in itself.

The Viale Verdi and its "Passi di Gloria": since 2011, over 200 bronze plaques embedded in the sidewalks between Piazza del Popolo and the Terme Tettuccio trace the visits of the most notable personalities—from Giuseppe Verdi to Puccini, from Ruggero Leoncavallo to Marie Curie, from Gary Cooper to Audrey Hepburn, all who came to take the waters. The early 20th-century Palazzo Municipale, designed by Raffaello Brizzi, borders this promenade.

activities.text: Thermal cure or wellness session at Terme Tettuccio or Terme Excelsior: Montecatini's four springs, which rise from depths of over 60 meters, produce sulfate-saline bicarbonate-sodium waters, traditionally used for digestive, hepatic and metabolic conditions. The thermal season runs from May to October.
The Teatro Verdi, built in 1950 in the thermal park, hosts the summer music season. Puccini, who stayed regularly in Montecatini, reportedly said it was one of his favorite places to compose.

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

places.name: Montecosaro

visits.text: Chiesa di San Rocco — built in 1447 on an octagonal plan, on the path leading up to the historic centre. It holds magnificent frescoes attributed to Simone de Magistris (1576), a Marchigian painter trained under Lorenzo Lotto. In the apse, the village of Montecosaro itself appears as a backdrop in a religious scene — a rare 16th-century visual document of how the village once looked.

Complesso Agostiniano (Palazzo Comunale) — a medieval complex comprising the Chiesa di Sant'Agostino and a former convent now serving as the town hall. The church, though restructured in 1773, retains a gilded Byzantine-style reliquary and a Gaetano Callido organ (1792). Inside the palazzo, a 2nd-century white marble Roman sarcophagus was recovered from the Chienti valley.

Collegiata di San Lorenzo and Teatro delle Logge — the collegiata was built in 1723 over the ruins of the old pieve, of which the 10th-century Romanesque campanile survives. It holds a pre-Giotto crucifix (12th–13th century) with Byzantine influences. The adjoining Teatro delle Logge, inaugurated in 1809 (stalls and three horseshoe tiers of iron boxes), was the site in 1568 of the execution of Enea Galizia and twelve conspirators, hanged from the railings after their failed uprising against the Cesarini family.

Abbazia di Santa Maria a Piè di Chienti (Montecosaro Scalo) — 4 km below the village, one of the finest Cluniac architectural achievements in Le Marche, consecrated in 1125. First documented in 936. Its two-storey interior (women's galleries, timber roofs, alabaster windows) blends Lombard and Burgundian influences. The bell tower's campana carries the date 1425 and the oldest known municipal coat of arms.

activities.text: Stroll through the lanes between the 13th-century Porta San Lorenzo (the last surviving gate, now embedded in the Marinozzi palazzo) and the Parco del Cassero at the highest point, with panoramic views over the Sibillini and the Adriatic.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Abbazia di Santa Maria a Piè di Chienti Montecosaro Scalo

visits.text: The gateway — the only entrance to the castello — Montefabbri first appears in documents in 1216, having developed from the 9th–10th century around the pieve of San Gaudenzio. The name comes from Monte Fabrorum — the mountain of the Fabbri family, the earliest feudal lords. In 1578, the Duke of Urbino granted the village as a fief to Francesco Paciotti, a civil and military architect who brought a long period of prosperity and ceramic craftsmanship. The gateway is the only opening in the enclosure wall. The medieval arch once supported a drawbridge — the niches where the bridge arms rested are still visible in the vestibule. On the valley-facing façade above the arch, a small 15th-century sandstone carving depicts a Madonna lattante. On the inner façade, the white marble arms of Count Francesco Paciotti preside over the entrance. In 1799, insurgents from the surrounding countryside captured Napoleonic soldiers here who had sought refuge on the walls after the sack of Urbino.

Pieve di San Gaudenzio — the original nucleus of the village — one of the four oldest pievi in the Archdiocese of Urbino, founded between the 7th and 8th centuries and dedicated to the first bishop of Rimini, martyred in 360. Rebuilt and extended in 1570, as recorded by a tablet at the back of the church. The interior preserves 16th–17th-century works and a collection of scagliole — stucco decorations considered the oldest in Le Marche. The Gothic chapel of Santa Marcellina (12th century) has held the saint's remains since 2 November 1666. The baptismal font is carved from an ancient Roman marble cippus. The 15th-century bell tower (25 m) dominates the village.

Belvedere over the Foglia and Apsa valleys — at 319 metres on the ridge separating the two valleys, Montefabbri has remained virtually unchanged since the 15th century — 70 inhabitants, no new construction for decades, only careful restorations. The lanes radiate from the pieve like spokes, with tightly packed fired-brick houses pressing against the walls. From the village's high points, the view spans one side across the Foglia valley toward Pesaro, and the other across the Apsa valley toward Urbino.

activities.text: Full walk through the village — Montefabbri can be explored in 30 to 45 minutes. Enter through the single gateway, walk the inner walls, visit the pieve and its scagliole, then step outside along the outer enclosure to see the Madonna lattante. The village, with its 70 residents, flowering balconies and deep quiet, is one of the most intimate stops along the entire itinerary.

A cena in famiglia — storytelling dinner (seasonal event) — the association 'Un paese cento storie' opens the homes of Montefabbri residents to strangers for a shared dinner: the grandparents tell the history of the village over a meal. Seasonal initiative — contact the Comune di Vallefoglia for details.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

places.name: Montefalco

visits.text: Complesso Museale di San Francesco - This Gothic church built between 1335 and 1338 by the Friars Minor was converted into a municipal museum in 1990. Its apse preserves Benozzo Gozzoli's most important fresco cycle in Umbria: in 1450-1452, this Florentine painter (student of Fra Angelico in Florence) covered the five sections of the apse with twenty episodes from the Life of Saint Francis in three ascending registers, symbolically rising from Birth to celestial glory. In the scene of the meal at the knight Celano's (episode 12), a carafe of red wine is visible on the table—likely an allusion to local Sagrantino. On the counter-façade, a 1503 Nativity by Perugino (Pietro Vannucci) completes the pictorial ensemble. The Franciscan cellars discovered during 2006 restoration yielded a pressing vat and period tools.

Piazza del Comune - This semicircular square, also called Piazza del Certame, is the hamlet's heart. It's surrounded by the Palazzo del Comune with its bell tower, the Municipal Theater, and Palazzo Bernardino de Cuppis. All of Montefalco's streets converge here from the ramparts. A covered passage connects the palace to the vineyard sector below.

Santuario di Santa Chiara della Croce - This sanctuary houses the chapel decorated with 14th-century frescoes of the Franciscan order, attributed to a student of Giotto. Adjacent, a cloistered Poor Clares monastery preserves a vegetable garden and a stone cloister. It's one of the most silent spiritual places in central Umbria.

Ramparts and panorama over the "Ringhiera dell'Umbria" - Montefalco's nickname comes from the expression Ringhiera dell'Umbria (Umbria's balcony): from its medieval ramparts at 473 meters altitude, the 360° view covers Assisi, Spoleto, Foligno, the Sibillini Mountains and, on clear days, Perugia. The town itself owes its name to Frederick II of Swabia who, visiting in the 13th century, noted the abundance of falcons in the region and baptized the hamlet Montefalco in their honor.

activities.text: Tasting of Sagrantino di Montefalco DOCG - Sagrantino is a black-berry grape variety native to this single micro-region of Umbria, one of the most tannic of all Italian grapes. Its red DOCG wine (Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita) comes from long vinification and barrel aging. Wine estates surrounding the hamlet offer guided tastings with vineyard and cellar visits.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

map_keywords: Santuario di Santa Chiara della Croce Montefalco

places.name: Montefalcone Nel Sannio

visits.text: Village of Montefalcone nel Sannio: shepherds' village perched on a ridge above 800m, on the Molise-Abruzzo border. 360° panorama over the Trigno and Sangro valleys.
Monte La Rocchetta: launch site for tandem paragliding flights above the Molise hills. The reliability of thermals makes it a favorite among regional clubs.
