activities.text: Tandem paragliding flight from Monte La Rocchetta: 20 to 40 minutes above the ridges, supervised by local instructors.
Walk the panoramic trails around the village.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Monte La Rocchetta Montefalcone nel Sannio
Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Montefalcone nel Sannio

visits.text: Rocca dei Papi - Fortress built in the 13th century by Pope Innocent III at Montefiascone's highest point. A favored summer residence for several popes thanks to fresh breezes and an impregnable position. Archaeological excavations are uncovering vestiges of Etruscan occupation. Climb to the tower's summit: the panorama embraces all of Lake Bolsena, the volcanic domes, and Orvieto in the distance.

Cathedral of Santa Margherita - Its 17th-century dome, visible throughout the region, dominates Montefiascone's profile. Architect Michele Sanmicheli designed the general structure in the early 16th century. A fire ravaged it in 1670; it was restored and reopened in its current form in 1893. The neoclassical portal dates from the mid-19th century.

Church of San Flaviano - An original construction on two superimposed levels: Romanesque-Gothic arcades below, 16th-century loggia above—and the two churches face opposite directions. The walls preserve medieval frescoes. Near the entrance, look for the tombstone of German bishop Johannes Defuk (died 1113), origin of the Est! Est!! Est!!! wine legend.

activities.text: Panorama over Lake Bolsena - Europe's largest volcanic lake (114 km², 151 meters deep) spreads at Montefiascone's feet. Two small islands, Martana and Bisentina, emerge from the waters. From the Rocca dei Papi terrace, you can take in the entire caldera.

Est! Est!! Est!!! wine tasting - The DOC appellation (since 1966) takes its name from a 12th-century legend: Bishop Johannes Defuk sent a scout ahead to mark good wine inns with "Est!" (there is!) on the door. At Montefiascone, the scout wrote "Est!" three times, so pleased was he with the wine. The bishop settled there... and never left. The white wine, based on Trebbiano and Malvasia, is produced on volcanic soils around the lake.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio

places.name: Montefiore Dellaso

visits.text: Polo Museale di San Francesco — housed in a 14th–16th-century former convent, this museum holds Carlo Crivelli's triptych, painted around 1471–1475 for the Chiesa di San Francesco. Originally a five-panel polyptych, it was sold off piece by piece in the mid-19th century — the Franciscan fathers accepted over a thousand scudi, ostensibly to fund convent repairs. The central Pietà now hangs in the National Gallery in London. The six remaining panels include a figure of the Maddalena widely regarded as one of the most beautiful Crivelli ever painted.

Chiesa di San Francesco — built between 1247 and 1303 through alms collected by the friars. It was for this church's altar that Crivelli created his polyptych. Adjacent to the museum, the church retains several 16th-century chapel works.

Collegiata di Santa Lucia — the main church of the village, dominating the historic centre. Its interior decoration was enriched during the 1900 Holy Year by artists of the Fermana school: a coffered stucco ceiling, frescoes by Luigi Fontana depicting the life of Saint Lucy, and a 17th-century walnut choir.

Belvedere De Carolis — a panoramic viewpoint over the Valdaso valley, named in honour of Adolfo De Carolis (1874–1928), the Symbolist painter born in Montefiore and D'Annunzio's celebrated illustrator. The Valdaso still holds the intact hillside and farmhouse landscapes that Renaissance painters once used as backdrops in their works.

activities.text: Wine tasting from the Valdaso: Falerio DOC and Rosso Piceno, in a territory where vineyards descend in terraces to the valley floor.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Polo Museale di San Francesco Montefiore dell'Aso
Chiesa di San Francesco Montefiore dell'Aso
Collegiata di Santa Lucia Montefiore dell'Aso

visits.text: Continue your wellness journey in Montegrotto Terme, another thermal town offering modern spa facilities rooted in ancient curative traditions. The therapeutic properties of the local thermal springs have attracted visitors seeking health and relaxation for millennia.

Visit the Butterfly House and Fairy Forest, a delightful attraction combining a large butterfly conservatory with a whimsical forest inhabited by fantastical creatures. Hundreds of colorful tropical butterflies flutter freely around visitors in the warm, humid environment.

activities.text: Explore the archaeological remains of Roman thermal baths discovered in the area, testament to the ancient appreciation of these healing waters. The surrounding Euganean Hills provide a scenic backdrop for spa relaxation.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Piazza del Comune — Palazzetto del Podestà and Torre Civica — the 14th-century Palazzetto with its five-arch loggia and pointed Gothic windows houses the Pinacoteca Civica 'Corrado Pellini' on the upper floor. Its centrepiece is the Madonna Immacolata by Flemish painter Ernest Van Schaych (1631). The Torre Civica bears the oldest coat of arms in the village and a clock, crowned with swallowtail Ghibelline battlements.

Teatro Storico Nicola Degli Angeli — inside the Palazzo Comunale, rebuilt during the Napoleonic period (1807–1814) by architect Ireneo Aleandri. Two tiers of boxes, ceiling frescoes by Domenico Ferri (1887). Aleandri also designed the theatre in Senigallia and the Fontana del Pasquino in Rome.

Chiesa di San Francesco (13th century) — founded in 1251 and later remodelled in the baroque style. The 18th-century wooden choir, four statues of the Theological Virtues (1752) and the 1753 organ form a harmonious ensemble. Above the high altar, a Madonna del Latte whose attribution remains debated among local scholars.

Abbazia di San Firmano — 3 km away, founded around 980 by the Grimaldi family and rebuilt in the 13th century in Romanesque style. The crypt holds the relics of San Firmano, the village's patron saint. On the apse vault, a fresco by Giacomo di Nicola da Recanati (1456) depicts the Madonna in Trono tra San Firmano e San Sebastiano. The abbey overlooks an agricultural landscape that has remained almost entirely unchanged.

activities.text: Urban trekking along the 15th-century town walls (roughly 1 km), with towers of rectangular and pentagonal base — the pentagonal ones marking the period of Malatesta rule. Near Porta del Trebbio, the visible join between two sections of wall built at different periods.
Taste the carciofo di Montelupone — the local artichoke celebrated each spring — and the vincisgrassi, layered pasta with mixed meat ragù and offal, the defining dish of the Macerata tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

places.name: Monteprandone

visits.text: Piazza dell'Aquila — the village's main square, dominated by the Palazzo Comunale and the 16th-century Palazzo Campanelli, recognisable by the sundial painted on its façade.

Santuario di San Giacomo della Marca — a Franciscan convent founded in 1449 by San Giacomo himself, following special authorisation from Pope Nicholas V. Below the chapel altar rests the saint's body, repatriated from Naples in 2001. The 19th-century cloister is decorated with a tempera fresco cycle by Emidio Tegli (1848) depicting key moments of his life in vivid popular scenes.

Museo Civico dei Codici di San Giacomo — preserves 61 manuscripts from the saint's personal library, a collection that once held over 180 codices in this small convent, including Latin and Greek classics and an excerpt from the Qur'an. A remarkable library for a 15th-century Franciscan friar.

Collegiata di San Niccolò — the village's main church, rebuilt in the 19th century on a foundation dating to the year 1000. The neoclassical interior is the work of Pietro Maggi — the same architect behind Grottammare's Teatro dell'Arancio.

activities.text: Walk along the 15th-century town walls with a triple panorama stretching across the Adriatic, the Sibillini Mountains and the Gran Sasso.
Sample the olives all'ascolana — the Piceno region's signature dish: large olives stuffed with meat, breaded and fried. Monteprandone is one of their historic heartlands.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Santuario di San Giacomo della Marca Monteprandone

visits.text: Piazza Grande: At the hilltop (605 m), this Renaissance square is surrounded by the 15th-century Palazzo Comunale (a miniature Sienese version of Florence's Palazzo Vecchio, with panoramic tower), the Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta begun in 1592 by Ippolito Scalza, Palazzo Contucci by Antonio da Sangallo, and Palazzo Tarugi. At the center, the Pozzo dei Grifi e dei Leoni, a Renaissance well designed by Sangallo.

Historic cellars of Vino Nobile: Beneath the Renaissance palaces of the center, a network of tunnels carved into tuff and brick vaults houses the oak barrels in which Vino Nobile di Montepulciano DOCG rests. The Contucci family has produced wine here since the 11th century; the Cantina De' Ricci extends over several medieval levels. Vino Nobile, based on Prugnolo (local Sangiovese), was already being exported to the Medici court in the 15th century.

Tempio di San Biagio: Five hundred meters below the ramparts, this Renaissance church begun in 1518 by Antonio da Sangallo the Elder is one of the finest examples of central-plan Renaissance architecture in Italy—inspired by Bramante's Tempietto. The travertine façade dominates the Valdichiana vineyards.

Strade bianche and Val d'Orcia: Montepulciano is the natural starting point for exploring the white roads from Val di Chiana to Val d'Orcia. The road toward Monticchiello (4 km) runs along vineyards and cypresses with views of classic Tuscan panoramas.

activities.text: Vino Nobile DOCG tasting: The cellars in the center offer guided tours of the tunnels carved into tuff with comparative tastings of Rosso di Montepulciano DOC, Vino Nobile DOCG, and Vino Nobile Riserva.

Bravio delle Botti: Every last Sunday in August, Montepulciano's eight districts compete by pushing 80 kg barrels along Via Gracciano nel Corso to Piazza Grande—a tradition documented since the 14th century.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

places.name: Monterchi

visits.text: Museum of the Madonna del Parto: a 260 × 203 cm fresco painted around 1460 by Piero della Francesca for the church of Santa Maria di Momentana, located away from the village — a site linked to traditions of Marian devotion and fertility rites. It was painted for the hometown of the painter's mother, according to Vasari during a return to Sansepolcro upon her death. Analysis of the fresco suggests it was executed in roughly seven days. It depicts the pregnant Virgin standing, opening her mantle with one hand, flanked by two symmetrical angels produced from the same cartoon reversed. The church was demolished in 1785 to make way for a cemetery; the fresco, forgotten in the masonry of a funerary chapel, was only rediscovered in 1889. After restoration, it was transferred in 1993 to the former village primary school, now converted into a museum. A legal dispute between the municipality of Monterchi, the Catholic Church and the Ministry of Culture over its ownership remains unresolved.

Historic center of Monterchi: perched on an isolated hill on the border with Umbria, the village retains a typically medieval character despite several earthquakes in the 19th and 20th centuries. Its name derives from Mons Herculis — Hill of Hercules — recalling presumed ancient cults on this promontory. The Museum of the Balance, housed in the center, holds one of Europe's most important collections of antique scales and weights, with an interactive educational trail.

activities.text: Exploring the Valtiberina countryside: the surroundings of Monterchi are dotted with Romanesque parish churches, castles and rural hamlets typical of the upper Tiber valley landscape. The road between Arezzo and Monterchi passes through several well-preserved farming villages, with panoramas over the wooded hills that herald the Tuscan Valtiberina.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

map_keywords: Museo della Madonna del Parto Monterchi

places.name: Monteriggioni

visits.text: Medieval walls of Monteriggioni: Built between 1214 and 1219 by the Republic of Siena to defend against Florence, the circular wall measures 570 meters in perimeter and rises 2 meters thick. Its 14 square towers, regularly spaced, have remained almost intact since the 13th century. Dante Alighieri immortalized them in the Inferno of the Divine Comedy, comparing these giant towers to the guardians of the abyss—'Monteriggioni di torri si corona.' From the ramparts open to the public, the view sweeps across Chianti and Val d'Elsa.

Monteriggioni in Arme Museum: Housed in the village's former buildings, this museum presents life-size reproductions of medieval and Renaissance armor and weaponry, siege machines, and knight's equipment. According to local legend, a Ubisoft developer stayed here and drew inspiration from the place to make it Ezio Auditore's birthplace in Assassin's Creed II—a story not officially confirmed by Ubisoft, but one that has introduced Monteriggioni to new generations.

Church of Santa Maria Assunta and Piazza Roma: At the heart of the walls, the 13th-century church blends Romanesque and Gothic on a simple stone façade. Piazza Roma, with its central well and a handful of palaces, sums up the scale of the village—three parallel streets connect the two gates, Porta Franca on the Siena side and Porta di Ponente on the Florence side.

activities.text: Walk on the ramparts: The accessible section runs along the north, east, and west faces for a partial loop of about 500 meters with views of Chianti Colli Senesi vineyards. In July, the medieval festival Monteriggioni di Torri si Corona transforms the village into a life-size recreation of 13th-century crafts.

Local products tasting: The village produces Chianti Colli Senesi Monteriggioni DOC and extra virgin olive oil from the olive trees surrounding the ramparts—shops on Via Matteotti offer these specialties.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province
Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Monteriggioni

places.name: Montopoli In Val Darno

visits.text: Montopoli was nicknamed the "castello insigne" by Boccaccio, impressed by its powerful fortifications. Disputed between Pisa, Florence, and Lucca since the 11th century—the Arno once flowed beneath its eastern walls—the town resisted numerous sieges. The Torre di San Matteo (15th century), built by the Florentine Republic, is today the best viewpoint over the Pisan Valdarno, reaching the Apuan Alps on clear days. The Arco di Castruccio Castracani marks the spot where the famous condottiere was wounded during the 1328 siege.

The Battle of San Romano (1432), fought near Montopoli between Florence and the Milanese troops of Niccolò Piccinino, was immortalized by Paolo Uccello in three paintings now preserved at the Uffizi in Florence, the Louvre, and London's National Gallery. The town voluntarily submitted to Florence in 1349, after the decisive battle. In 1412, podestà Jacopo degli Albizzi divided Montopoli into two peoples—Santo Stefano and San Giovanni—whose rivalry is reenacted every September during the Disfida con l'Arco.

The Museo Civico in Palazzo Guicciardini displays archaeological finds dating back to the 3rd century BC (Etruscan period, Volterran style), medieval collections, and a section devoted to local ceramics—Montopoli had a tradition of kilns and potters from the 16th century, perpetuated in the 19th century by Dante Milani.

activities.text: Disfida con l'Arco: every second Sunday in September, Montopoli's two historic quarters (Santo Stefano and San Giovanni) compete in an archery tournament in medieval costume, preceded by a parade. The event commemorates the 1412 decision that divided the town.

Stroll through the centro storico: 17th-century noble palaces, Villa Capponi Piaggio (16th century, acquired in 1953 by Enrico Piaggio), and the Tenuta di Verramista with its oaks, chestnuts, and white firs exceedingly rare in the Pisan plain.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

map_keywords: Palazzo Guicciardini Montopoli in Val d'Arno
Villa Capponi Montopoli in Val d'Arno
San Romano Montopoli in Val d'Arno

places.name: Morano Calabro

visits.text: Begin your Calabrian journey at Morano Calabro, perched at 694 meters altitude in the heart of Pollino National Park. Nicknamed the "Presepe del Pollino" (the Nativity scene of Pollino), the village forms a maze of red-roofed houses clinging to a conical hill. Climb up to the ruins of the Norman-Swabian castle (12th-13th century), expanded in the 16th century by the Sanseverino princes: the view from the summit encompasses the entire Coscile valley and the peaks of Pollino. Then descend toward the church of San Bernardino da Siena, founded in 1452, whose golden stone ogival portal and ship's hull ceiling - unique in Calabria - are worth the visit alone. Don't miss the Polyptych by Bartolomeo Vivarini (1477), painted in Murano, which gleams with gold on the altar. Allow about 2 hours to explore the historic center at a leisurely pace.

Continue to the collegiate church of Santa Maria Maddalena, recognizable from afar by its dome covered with green and ochre majolica tiles. Inside, you'll find a Madonna degli Angeli by Antonello Gagini dated 1505 and, in the San Silvestro chapel, the Sanseverino Polyptych. On your way down, pass by the church of Saints Peter and Paul, the village's oldest, founded around the year 1000 - it preserves four marble statues by Pietro Bernini, father of the famous Gian Lorenzo.

A 20-minute drive away, the village of Civita transports you to another world. Founded in the late 15th century by Albanian refugees fleeing the Ottoman occupation, it's one of the oldest Arbëreshë communities in Italy. Wander through the lanes organized in gjitonia - these semicircular neighborhoods around a mother house and a small square. Look closely at the façades: the "Case Kodra" (named after artist Ibrahim Kodra) have human faces formed by windows and chimneys. These sculpted comignoli are genuine works of art, with each family decorating them according to their social status.

From Civita's center, descend to the Ponte del Diavolo spanning the Raganello gorges. According to legend, the devil built this bridge in one night in exchange for the soul of the first person to cross - but the clever owner sent a sheep across instead, and the furious devil tried to destroy his own work without succeeding. Beyond the legend, the panorama from the bridge over the vertiginous canyon walls is breathtaking. Arrive early morning or late afternoon to avoid summer crowds.

activities.text: If you enjoy thrills, operators offer canyoning excursions in the Raganello gorges - expect 3 to 4 hours of walking in water between the canyon walls. Check weather conditions before committing: after heavy rains, the gorges may be closed. Alternatively, a hiking trail accessible from Civita follows the Raganello ridge with plunging views over the canyon.

End the day in Civita by visiting the Arbëreshë Ethnic Museum, which displays traditional embroidered costumes, gold jewelry of oriental craftsmanship, and everyday objects. Don't leave the village without tasting local specialties: handmade pasta and soppressata accompanied by Pollino rosé wine.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea

map_keywords: Collegiata Santa Maria Maddalena Morano Calabro

visits.text: Torre Eptagonale — a 25-metre 12th-century tower with seven sides, unique in Europe. Today it houses the TOMAV (Torre Moresco Arti Visive), a contemporary art centre. From the top, on a clear day, the eye travels from Monte Conero to the Gran Sasso — and all the way to the Croatian coast. The Ghibelline battlements visible today were rebuilt in the early 20th century after the original arabesque spire collapsed.

Piazza Castello — the village's only square, triangular in shape, with a 16th-century portico that is all that remains of the left nave of the demolished Chiesa di Santa Maria in Castro. Under the groin vaults, a fresco by Vincenzo Pagani depicts the Madonna con Bambino.

Torre dell'Orologio and ex-Chiesa di Santa Sofia — a 14th-century tower marking the original village gateway, flanked by a 16th-century portico. Passing under the tower leads to the former Chiesa Santa Sofia, now a performance space nicknamed 'lu teatrì'. The church retains a fresco from the school of Carlo Crivelli (15th century).

Sala Consiliare of the Palazzo Comunale — preserves an altarpiece by Vincenzo Pagani (1540) depicting the Madonna in Gloria con i Santi Lucia, Lorenzo, Rocco e Nicolò. Pagani, born in Monterubbiano around 1490, is the author of both frescoes visible in the village.

activities.text: Taste the ciauscolo — a soft pork salami you spread like pâté, seasoned with pepper and garlic — a signature product of the Valdaso, along with the organic peaches from the Val d'Aso cultivated at the foot of the village.
Walk the rampart path around the village (elliptical in shape, roughly 600 inhabitants) with views down over the Valdaso, its orchards and olive groves.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

places.name: Morro Dalba

visits.text: Camminamento di Ronda 'La Scarpa' — Morro d'Alba claims a record unique in Italy: the only fortified village whose entire inner walkway is covered and lined with arcades along its full length. The irregular pentagonal wall circuit with six bastions dates to the 13th–15th centuries. The covered inner walkway, known as 'La Scarpa' (from the scarped inclination of the walls), runs 300 metres, punctuated by six watchtowers and a long row of windows opening onto the countryside — the Sibillini on one side, the Conero and the sea on the other. Two panoramic towers bear the names Torre Teodorico (east, hillside views, now a cultural venue) and Torre Federico I (west, sunset views).

Sotterranei di Morro d'Alba — beneath the village houses, inhabitants carved out from the 14th to 15th centuries a labyrinth of caves and galleries on multiple levels — an entire underground city. These spaces served to preserve food, shelter livestock in winter and take refuge during attacks. In the galleries, the oldest known bombarda (primitive firearm) in Italy was discovered — the original is now at the Museo Storico Nazionale di Artiglieria in Turin. Part of the galleries houses the Museo Utensilia, which documents the life of Marchigian sharecroppers (mezzadri) through seven reconstructed environments: a press, weaving workshop, cellar, barn and farmyard, with scythes, baskets, barrels, ploughs and agricultural tools.

Piazza Tarsetti and Torre Civica — the village's main square. The fountain bears a graffito by Maestro Enzo Cucchi, born in Morro d'Alba in 1949, one of the leading figures of Italian Transavanguardia. The clock tower preserves a 1504 bas-relief of a rampant lion — the symbol of Jesi, to which Morro d'Alba still belonged at that time. Opposite, the Chiesa di San Gaudenzio (1763), recently restored, holds several paintings including Claudio Ridolfi's altarpiece (1630) Incoronazione della Vergine e Santi, and a 17th-century canvas depicting San Michele Arcangelo, the village's patron saint.

activities.text: Lacrima di Morro d'Alba DOC tasting — Lacrima is a red wine with violet hues, fruity and lightly tannic, from an ancient grape variety whose skin is so fragile it tears at full ripeness, allowing the juice to seep out — hence the name. More than ten wineries offer visits and tastings: the Antica Cantina Sant'Amico (founded 1850, one of the oldest in Le Marche) is an ideal starting point. Morro d'Alba also falls within the production zone of Verdicchio dei Castelli di Jesi DOC.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Camminamento di Ronda La Scarpa Morro d'Alba

visits.text: Begin your Neapolitan stay at the Cappella Sansevero, a small Baroque jewel tucked away in an alley in the historic center. The centerpiece is Giuseppe Sanmartino's Veiled Christ (1753), a strikingly realistic marble sculpture—the veil seems genuinely draped over the body. Prince Raimondo di Sangro, who commissioned the work, was also an alchemist: downstairs, you'll see two anatomical machines with eerily preserved circulatory systems. Book your tickets online: the chapel is tiny and queues can be long. Arrive right at opening time at 9 a.m.

Next, dive into Spaccanapoli, the arrow-straight street that literally splits Naples in two, dating back to Greco-Roman times. You're crossing the heart of the UNESCO-listed historic center, amid Baroque palaces, churches, and laundry hanging from windows. Detour down Via San Gregorio Armeno, famous for its nativity scene workshops—a tradition stretching back to the 18th century. Every shop overflows with terracotta figurines, including contemporary personalities reimagined as nativity characters.

Step inside the Basilica of Santa Chiara and its Clarisses' Cloister, a haven of peace amid Naples' tumult. The cloister, remodeled in the 17th century, is lined with octagonal columns covered in hand-painted majolica tiles depicting pastoral and mythological scenes. Orange trees offer welcome shade. Just across the street, the Chiesa del Gesù Nuovo surprises with its diamond-point facade—this is the former Sanseverino palace, converted into a church by the Jesuits in 1584.

Walk up Via Toledo, Naples' main shopping artery. Over a kilometer long, it connects Piazza del Plebiscito to the archaeological museum, cutting through the city from south to north. Halfway up, veer into the Quartieri Spagnoli: this grid of narrow alleys laid out in the 16th century to house Spanish garrisons is Naples at its most authentic. Laundry strung between buildings, street shrines to Maradona, street art, and the scent of ragù wafting from kitchens.

This morning, head to the Museo Archeologico Nazionale, one of Europe's greatest archaeological museums. You'll find the Farnese Collection with its colossal sculptures—the Farnese Hercules stands 3.17 meters tall—and above all the mosaics and frescoes recovered from Pompeii and Herculaneum. Don't miss the Secret Cabinet, long closed to the public, which displays Roman erotic art. Allow at least 2 hours, and arrive at opening to avoid the crowds.

After lunch, take the central funicular from Via Toledo up to Vomero. In a few minutes, you shift from street chaos to hilltop calm. The Certosa di San Martino, a Carthusian monastery founded in 1325 and remodeled in Baroque style in the 17th century, offers the finest view in Naples: the gulf, Vesuvius, Capri, the historic center at your feet. The museum inside displays a collection of 18th-century Neapolitan nativity scenes of stunning refinement.

Right next door, Castel Sant'Elmo, a star-shaped fortress built by the Angevins in the 14th century then restructured by the Spanish in 1537, dominates the entire city from 250 meters above sea level. Climb onto the ramparts for a 360° panorama—this is the most comprehensive viewpoint over Naples and its bay. On clear days, you can make out the islands of Procida and Ischia.

Descend to Piazza del Plebiscito, Naples' largest square at 25,000 m². On one side, the Palazzo Reale, residence of the Spanish viceroys then the Bourbons; on the other, the colonnade of the Basilica of San Francesco di Paola, directly inspired by Rome's Pantheon. Just beside it, the Teatro San Carlo, inaugurated in 1737—41 years before Milan's La Scala—is the world's oldest opera house still in operation.

End the day with a stroll along the Lungomare to Castel dell'Ovo, Naples' oldest fortress, perched on the islet of Megaride. Legend has it that Virgil hid a magic egg in the foundations—as long as the egg holds, Naples holds. Climb onto the ramparts for a panoramic view over the gulf, Vesuvius, and Capri. Entry is free and it's one of the best spots for sunset.

Leave Positano early morning and drive toward Pompeii (about 1h15). The archaeological site extends over 66 hectares, of which 45 have been excavated—it's the only ancient city in the world offering such a complete vision of a Roman town. The eruption of Vesuvius in AD 79 buried Pompeii under 6 meters of ash and pumice. About 20,000 inhabitants lived there; more than 1,100 bodies have been found since excavations began in 1748. Arrive right at opening at 9 a.m. and allow at minimum 3 to 4 hours.

Start with the Forum, the heart of public life: the basilica (courthouse), the temples of Jupiter and Apollo, the covered market (macellum). You're walking on the same basalt paving stones as the Romans, with chariot wheel ruts still visible. The raised stones in the streets served as pedestrian crossings—Pompeii had a wastewater drainage system running through the streets.

Don't miss the House of the Vettii, recently restored, one of Pompeii's most richly decorated domus. The Vettii brothers, former slaves who became wealthy merchants, had their walls painted with frescoes in the Pompeian "fourth style"—mythology, genre scenes, still lifes. The interior garden with its water jets and columns has been reconstructed based on archaeological traces.

Head to the Amphitheatre, the oldest existing stone Roman amphitheater in the world, built in 80 BC—nearly 150 years before the Colosseum. It could accommodate up to 20,000 spectators. Right beside it, the Grande Palestre (gymnasium) with its plane trees planted by the Romans, some of whose fossilized trunks are still visible.

Pass through the Garden of the Fugitives (Orto dei Fuggiaschi), where 13 plaster casts reproduce the bodies of victims caught by the pyroclastic flow as they attempted to flee toward the Nocera gate. These casts, obtained by pouring plaster into cavities left by decomposed bodies—a technique invented by Giuseppe Fiorelli in 1863—are profoundly moving.

Finish with the Villa of the Mysteries, on the northwest periphery of the site. This large suburban villa houses Pompeii's most famous fresco: a cycle of 29 life-size figures painted on a Pompeian red background, likely representing a Dionysian initiation rite. The quality of preservation and intensity of colors, after nearly 2,000 years, are astonishing.

activities.text: You can't leave Naples without tasting true Neapolitan pizza. Via dei Tribunali is packed with historic pizzerias. The dough is soft, the edges puffed (the cornicione), wood-fired baking lasts 60 to 90 seconds at 485°C. Order a Margherita—San Marzano tomato, buffalo mozzarella, basil—and you'll understand why Neapolitan pizza has been UNESCO Intangible Heritage since 2017. Expect queues at the most famous spots.

Late in the day, embark on a walking street food tour of Naples. Sample sfogliatella riccia (crispy layered pastry filled with ricotta and semolina, scented with orange blossom), cuoppo fritto (cone of fried seafood), and pizza a portafoglio (pizza folded in four, eaten standing up). Via Toledo and its surroundings have the best stalls.

Descend beneath the city with a visit to underground Naples (Napoli Sotterranea). Under Piazza San Gaetano, 40 meters deep, you'll explore a network of galleries carved into tuff in Greek times (4th century BC), later used as a Roman cistern, then as an air-raid shelter during World War II. Some passages are very narrow—wear comfortable clothing.

If your flight or train isn't until the next day, you can climb Vesuvius late afternoon. From the national park entrance, a 1-km trail (about 30 minutes' walk) leads to the crater rim, at 1,281 meters altitude. The crater measures 700 meters in diameter and 200 meters deep. In clear weather, the view embraces the entire Gulf of Naples, Capri, the Phlegraean Fields, and the mountains of Basilicata. Vesuvius is continental Europe's only active volcano—its last eruption was in 1944.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast
From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast
From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

visits.text: Rocca Albornoz - This imposing fortress was built in the 14th century by Cardinal-Legate Egidio Albornoz to establish papal dominance over central Italy. Perched at the edge of the rocky promontory dominating the town and the Nera river, it presents a quadrilateral plan flanked by four corner towers. Its thick walls and ramparts offer panoramic views of the Nera valley and the Narni hills. It served for a long time as a prison; in the 16th century, condemned prisoners whose names remain carved into the walls were detained there.

Centro storico di Narni - The medieval town, founded on the site of ancient Roman Narnia (from which C.S. Lewis took the name Narnia for his novel), is organized around Piazza dei Priori with its 13th-century Palazzo del Podestà and its loggia. Stone alleyways span vaulted passages, fountains, and small silent squares. Narni once controlled the Via Flaminia, the strategic road axis linking Rome to the Adriatic: whoever held Narni held the road to Rome.

Narni Sotterranea - In May 1979, six young speleologists from Narni climbed a wall on the edge of San Bernardo park and discovered, behind a walled-up door indicated by an old gardener, a 12th-13th century church entirely covered with frescoes, forgotten beneath the San Domenico convent. Thirty years of research in the Vatican and Trinity College Dublin archives identified, in an adjacent cell, graffiti in Masonic-alchemical symbols left by an Inquisition prisoner named Giuseppe Lombardini—persecuted by the Dominican order that precisely managed this convent. The guided tour (1h30) also traverses a Roman cistern from the Republican era and the Hall of Torments, where the Holy Office Tribunal interrogated its suspects between the 16th and 19th centuries. Only one visit is possible at a time; reservation required.

Ponte d'Augusto - At the entrance to the lower town, a single arch 30 meters in span, remnant of a 1st-century AD Roman bridge that once crossed the Nera in five arches, still stands in the vegetation. It's one of the best-preserved testimonies of Roman road engineering on the Via Flaminia.

activities.text: Swimming at the Mole di Narni (Stifone) - 5 km from Narni, the Nera river forms remarkably transparent turquoise pools among white limestone rocks. This natural site, once a Roman shipyard area according to ancient sources, is accessible by a path descending from the village of Stifone. The water, fed by karst springs, remains cool even in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

visits.text: Navelli - a medieval village of 400 inhabitants at 970 meters, on a spur between Gran Sasso and Valle dell'Aterno. The Piana di Navelli produces Zafferano dell'Aquila PDO, introduced to the region in the 14th century by a Dominican monk returning from Spain. This saffron, hand-harvested in October, is considered among the world's finest for its safranal content.

activities.text: Purchase Navelli PDO saffron from local cooperatives - several producers sell directly from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Neive - the pais di sgnuret (village of little lords) - perches in the heart of the western Langhe, divided between an old town on the hilltop and Borgonuovo below. The upper old town was the chosen residence of nobility, who left a series of aristocratic palaces and mansions in terracotta along the steep alleys. The original castle was destroyed in 1276 during a war between the communes of Asti and Alba, but remnants of the medieval ricetto partially survive.

Piazza Italia, a true eighteenth-century drawing room bordered by elegant palaces, is the village heart. The thirteenth-century Clock Tower rises here - a Roman funerary plaque embedded in its wall testifies to the continuity of occupation since Antiquity. The fortified house of the Conti Cotti di Ceres, built in the thirteenth century near this tower by a banking family, houses the text by Francesco Cotti (late seventeenth century) that constitutes one of the oldest Piedmontese writings on vine cultivation. The church of the Archconfraternity of San Michele, built in the second half of the eighteenth century by architect Giovanni Antonio Borgese, is worth visiting for its interior stuccoes.

The Casa della Donna Selvatica museum, with free entry, traces local history and presents the production of Donna Selvatica grappa, the typical brandy of the Langhe. The hills around Neive produce the sector's four great DOCG wines: Barbaresco, Barbera, Moscato d'Asti, and Dolcetto d'Alba.

activities.text: Visit the cellars of Barbaresco (the "neighboring village" 5km away) where the Regional Enoteca of Barbaresco occupies the nave of the former church of San Donato. Barbaresco's medieval tower, 36 meters high and the tallest in Piedmont, offers in season a 360° panorama over the Alpine arc and the vines below.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days
Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

places.name: Nemi

visits.text: Lanuvio — A hilltop village at 324 m in the southern Colli Albani, with a 17th-century Collegiata dell'Assunzione and the vestiges of an ancient sanctuary dedicated to Juno Sospita. This cult was so important that newly elected Roman consuls traveled to Lanuvio to perform a sacrifice before taking office. 20th-century excavations unearthed ex-votos and coins spanning several centuries of devotion.

Nemi — A picturesque village at 521 m overlooking the crater lake from a rocky ridge, famous since antiquity for the sanctuary of Diana Nemorensis, whose priests obtained their position by killing their predecessor in single combat—a ritual documented by Strabo and Suetonius that inspired James Frazer's comparative anthropology of religions. The historic center preserves medieval lanes and the 15th-century Chiesa di Santa Maria del Popolo.

Museo delle Navi Romane di Nemi — A museum built between 1933 and 1939 specifically to house two ceremonial galleys commissioned by Caligula (37-41 AD) for Lake Nemi, measuring respectively 71.30 × 20 m and 73 × 24 m—extraordinary dimensions for lake vessels. Extracted from the lakebed in 1929-1931 after partial drainage of the lake, both original ships were entirely destroyed by fire on the night of May 31-June 1, 1944, under circumstances still debated (retreating German troops or Allied shells). The museum now displays 1:5 scale models, recovered fragments, anchors, lead pipes bearing Caligula's initials, and a partial reconstruction of the stern.

activities.text: Walking around Lake Nemi — A small volcanic lake of 1.67 km² and 33 m depth, surrounded by oak and chestnut forests. The ring road passes the old wild strawberry farms (fragoline di Nemi), cultivated since the 15th century and celebrated annually at the Sagra delle Fragole in early June.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria del Popolo Nemi

places.name: Nerola

visits.text: Castello Orsini di Nerola — A medieval fortress overlooking the village from a hill in the Monti Lucretili, held by the powerful Orsini family from the 13th century. The castle witnessed the 1483 birth of Felice della Rovere, illegitimate daughter of Cardinal Giuliano della Rovere, the future Pope Julius II. Felice became a remarkable historical figure in her own right, serving as governor of the State of Bracciano and emerging as one of the most influential women in early 16th-century Rome.

Centro storico di Nerola — A compact village with medieval lanes, the Chiesa di San Nicola di Bari featuring a 13th-century Romanesque portal and bell tower, and several 16th-17th century noble palaces. The village preserves its medieval radial plan, with houses clustered tightly around the castle.

activities.text: Views over the Monti Lucretili from Nerola's heights — The vista stretches across the wooded ridges of Sabina, the Aniene valley, and on clear days, all the way to Rome. The Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Lucretili extends immediately to the north, its slopes cloaked in oak and hornbeam forests.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Nicola di Bari Nerola
Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Lucretili

visits.text: Discover Noale, featuring a fortress, a moat, colorful frescoed houses, and a nearby nature reserve with ponds and hundreds of bird species. This peaceful town offers an authentic Veneto experience away from tourist crowds.

Explore the medieval fortifications and charming center, where life moves at a gentle pace and traditional Italian culture remains vibrant. The nature reserve Oasi di Noale provides excellent birdwatching opportunities.

activities.text: Spend a quiet day in the bosom of the Veneto countryside, combining cultural sights with natural beauty. The town makes an easy combination with nearby Castelfranco Veneto.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Noto, rebuilt from scratch after the 1693 earthquake, represents Sicilian Baroque's crowning achievement. Its Corso Vittorio Emanuele, a 700-meter artery oriented east-west to capture evening light, is lined with palaces and churches whose local limestone façades turn honey-gold at sunset.
Palazzo Nicolaci di Villadorata, built in 1737, boasts Sicily's most ornate façade: its six balconies rest on consoles sculpted as horses, lions, mermaids, and chimeras.

activities.text: Visit to the Vendicari Nature Reserve, 12 km away, with its salt lagoons, ruins of a 9th-century Arab tonnara (tuna fishery), and fine sandy beaches accessible on foot.
Lunch in Marzamemi, a tuna-fishing village founded by Arabs in the 10th century, with its low lava-stone houses surrounding the piazza.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

visits.text: Museo Civico della Ceramica di Nove - Nove owes its existence to the Brenta. The river's alluvial deposits provided the clay, quartz and calcium carbonate for ceramic pastes; hydraulic power drove the preparation mills; boats transported wood for the kilns and finished products to Venice and Europe. The Antonibon manufactory, founded in the 1720s, was the first to produce Dutch-style majolica in response to the fashion for Chinese porcelain. It delivered services to the Viennese court and European embassies. The museum presents a collection of Nove ceramics from the 18th century to today, showing the evolution of styles from Baroque faience to Art Deco forms.

Villa Angarano - This patrician villa on the Brenta's left bank, two kilometers from Nove, was begun by Palladio for the Angarano family around 1548 but remained unfinished. The lateral barchesse (agricultural outbuildings) that survive are attributed to Palladio; the central body was taken up and completed by Baldassarre Longhena, architect of the Salute in Venice, in the 17th century. The villa belongs to the UNESCO perimeter of Veneto's Palladian villas. It still produces wine today and offers tastings.

activities.text: Ceramics workshop in Nove - Artisanal ceramic production continues in Nove in about ten workshops open to visitors. Several organize throwing, glazing and firing demonstrations for groups, and offer practical workshops of one or two hours. The tradition of the cuco, the small fischietto (whistle) in terracotta shaped like a rooster or Napoleonic figure, remains a local specialty.

Sentiero del Tabacco along the Brenta - From Bassano's Ponte Vecchio, this path follows the Brenta's right bank for 35 km to the village of Costa di Valstagna. It owes its name to the tobacco cultivation that once occupied the dry-stone terraces (masiere) carved into the slopes. These terraces, now partly wooded, remain visible throughout the route. A one-hour round-trip section from Nove suffices to understand the landscape shaped by centuries of mountain agriculture.

days_plan.itin_title: Vicenza Circuit: Palladian Villas and the Venetian Countryside

places.name: Nuoro

visits.text: The Museum of Sardinian Life and Folk Traditions, built in the 1950s on Sant'Onofrio hill, is the island's most complete ethnographic collection. Its six spaces display embroidered costumes, coral and filigree jewelry, carnival masks, ceremonial breads, and musical instruments including the launeddas, a triple-reed flute with a single reed.

The birthplace of Grazia Deledda, in the historic Seuna quarter, became a museum in 1983. This writer born in Nuoro in 1871 received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1926—the first Italian woman to do so. Her diploma, Nobel medal, and the kitchen described in her autobiographical novel Cosima are on display.

Monte Ortobene rises 3,133 feet above the city. A bronze statue of Christ the Redeemer has stood there since 1901. Every last Sunday in August, the Feast of the Redeemer gathers thousands in traditional costumes from all over Sardinia.

activities.text: Visit MAN, the Art Museum of the Province of Nuoro, preserving over 600 works by Sardinian artists from the 19th century to today, including sculptures by Francesco Ciusa, awarded at the 1907 Venice Biennale.
Taste Cannonau di Sardegna DOC, a red wine with notes of dark fruits and spices produced in the Barbagia region from one of the island's most ancient grape varieties.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

map_keywords: Museo della Vita e delle Tradizioni Popolari Sarde Nuoro

places.name: Offagna

visits.text: Rocca medievale di Offagna — in 1454, Ancona obtained from Pope Nicholas V the small village of Offagna, positioned on an arenite spur 300 metres above sea level. In just two years, the maritime republic built a quadrangular fortress with a massive central mastio rising more than 30 metres over five floors, built entirely in brick. The entrance is at the fourth level — making the stronghold virtually impregnable. Declared a National Monument in 1902. Today the Rocca houses the Museo delle Armi Antiche (a collection spanning prehistory to the modern era, born from a collaboration between the Accademia di Oplologia e Militaria di Ancona and the State Museums of San Marino) and allows visitors to climb to the walkway and mastio terrace for a 360° view over the countryside, the Sibillini and the Conero.

Piazza del Maniero and underground gallery — the village's main square, dominated by the Rocca. On its flank stands the Chiesa San Tommaso Apostolo (17th century, fired brick), with an altarpiece depicting San Bernardino da Siena offering the village to the Madonna. Beneath the Torre dell'Orologio, a dark gallery cuts through the arenite rock: a tunnel carved in the 17th century, attributed by local legend to the secretive Giacomini sect, said to be haunted by a ghost guarding a hidden treasure. To the east of the square, via del Monastero leads to the former convent of Santa Zita and the Chiesa del SS. Sacramento (late 18th century), both built by architect Andrea Vici.

Museo di Scienze Naturali Luigi Paolucci — located on via del Monastero, this museum preserves part of the collection of Ancona naturalist Luigi Paolucci (1849–1935), who claimed to have catalogued every species of fauna and flora in Le Marche. The rooms display now-extinct species, 19th-century scientific instruments and an exhibition documenting the Liberation of Ancona in 1944, with photographs and weaponry from the Italian Campaign.

activities.text: Feste Medievali d'Offagna (last week of July) — one of the most celebrated medieval re-enactments in Le Marche. For several days the village is transformed: horsemen and ladies in the streets, costumed merchants and craftsmen, falconry, jugglers, medieval taverns with period banquets. The Contesa tra i Rioni pits the neighbourhoods against each other in various contests. The 'dolce della Contesa' with Moretum (blackberry and honey wine) is the festival's signature treat. Year-round, the crescia — a grilled flatbread filled with local cured meats — can be sampled throughout the village. Offagna even created an Accademia della Crescia to protect the traditional recipe.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Museo di Scienze Naturali Luigi Paolucci Offagna

places.name: Offida

visits.text: Chiesa di Santa Maria della Rocca — built in 1330 on the ruins of a Lombard fortress, perched on a spur with sheer cliff faces on all sides. This brick Romanesque-Gothic church conceals a low-vaulted crypt below, its chapels covered in 14th-century frescoes by the Maestro di Offida. Enter from the rear: the main portal leads directly down into the crypt.

Piazza del Popolo — the central square, anchored by the 11th–12th-century Palazzo Comunale with its arcaded loggia. Inside is the Teatro Serpente Aureo, a small 19th-century baroque theatre whose name evokes a local legend about a golden serpent said to haunt the surrounding hills.

Museo del Merletto a Tombolo — set inside the Palazzo De Castellotti-Pagnanelli. Bobbin lace has been made in Offida since the 15th century; the oldest known examples are the camail collars of San Giacomo della Marca, now held at Monteprandone. A gown entirely crafted by Offida lacemakers was worn by Naomi Campbell at a London fashion show.

Mura Castellane — the 12th-century walls encircling the historic centre. At their base stands the Monumento alle Merlettaie (1983) by sculptor Aldo Sergiacomi, depicting three women from three generations at work — a tribute to the transmission of a living craft.

activities.text: Wine tasting at the Enoteca Regionale delle Marche, installed in the cellars of a 14th-century former Franciscan convent. Offida's territory is the most vine-planted in the entire province: Offida Pecorino DOCG and Rosso Piceno Superiore DOC find their finest expression here.

Watch the lacemakers at work in the village: stroll along Corso Serpente Aureo and Via del Merletto, where women still sit in summer on their doorsteps, the tombolo cushion on their knees, the wooden bobbins clicking in an unbroken rhythm.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria della Rocca Offida
Museo del Merletto a Tombolo Offida

visits.text: The Basilica of San Simplicio, built from gray granite between the late 11th and early 12th centuries on a hill that served as a necropolis since Carthaginian times, is Gallura's most important Romanesque monument. It's dedicated to Simplicio, Olbia's first bishop, martyred under Emperor Diocletian. His relics were discovered in the crypt in 1614.
The San Simplicio necropolis, visible beneath glass panels in front of the basilica, has yielded some 450 tombs spanning 2,000 years of Olbia's history, from the Phoenicians through the Middle Ages.
The Church of San Paolo Apostolo, in the historic center, is recognizable by its 1939 dome entirely clad in multicolored majolica tiles. It was built over the remains of a Roman temple.

activities.text: Visit the Olbia Archaeological Museum, tracing the port city's history from nuragic times through Rome, with a section dedicated to Roman shipwrecks discovered in the harbor.
Swim at Porto Istana or Pittulongu beaches, located a few miles north of Olbia, with shallow waters and fine sand—the gateway to the Costa Smeralda.
Stroll along Corso Umberto I, the main artery of the historic center lined with palaces and café terraces, to Piazza Matteotti.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

visits.text: Descend the paved road of the Olgiasca peninsula to the Cistercian Abbey of Piona, nestled at the foot of Monte Legnone on the edge of a sheltered Lake Como cove. A monastic community is attested at this site as early as the 7th century, founded by Bishop Agrippino of Como. The current Romanesque church, a single nave 27 meters long, was consecrated in 1138 after reconstruction by Cluniac monks from San Pietro in Vallate. Its cloister, built between 1252 and 1257 by Prior Bonaccorso de Canova, features columns with varied capitals mixing Romanesque, nascent Gothic, and transalpine decorative influences—one of the best-preserved examples of Lombard Romanesque art in a lakeside monastic setting. Byzantine frescoes from the 12th-13th centuries survive in the apse.

Visit the shop of the Cistercian monks of Piona—the same ones who still inhabit the abbey—where liqueurs distilled according to traditional monastic recipes, medicinal herbs, honeys, and cosmetics produced within the priory are offered. The chapter house, meeting place and site of the superior's election, retains its wooden benches from the sacristy of San Zeno in Verona.

activities.text: Easy loop hike on the Olgiasca peninsula from the village, following trail #7 through woods and clearings to the abbey in about 45 minutes. The return route takes the paved road, with plunging views of the Laghetto di Piona—a nearly enclosed cove of Lake Como—and the snow-capped Orobie Alps. Total circuit about 1h30, without particular difficulty.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

places.name: Opi

visits.text: Opi - a village of 400 inhabitants at 1,250 meters, perched on a promontory-shaped rocky spur in the heart of Parco Nazionale d'Abruzzo, Lazio e Molise, founded in 1923. The medieval town with narrow alleyways offers direct panoramic views of the Valle del Sangro and the park's beech forests.

activities.text: Starting point for wildlife observation in the national park: Marsican bear (about 50 individuals in the park), Apennine wolf, and red deer are regularly spotted in nearby alpine pastures.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Parco Nazionale d'Abruzzo Lazio e Molise

places.name: Orgosolo

visits.text: Orgosolo, a village of 5,000 inhabitants perched at 2,000 feet in Barbagia, has been covered since 1969 with 250 murals painted on building facades. The movement began with an art teacher, Francesco Del Casino, who had posters commemorating the anti-fascist Resistance pasted up—then painted them directly on the walls. Each mural tells a local or global episode: the repression of Sardinian shepherds, May 1968, Pinochet's Chile.
The Sa Dommo e sos Corraine House-Museum preserves ten period rooms intact—kitchen, master bedroom, granary, weaving workshop—frozen in the peasant and pastoral life of the early 20th century.

activities.text: Hike on the Montes plateau, a vast forest of holm oaks and pines above Orgosolo, with views over the Supramonte massif and Monte Corrasi (4,800 feet).
Lunch at a Barbagia farm with plateau specialties: wood-roasted porceddu, aged sheep's cheese, pane carasau, and red Cannonau wine.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

map_keywords: Museo Sa Dommo e sos Corraine Orgosolo

visits.text: The Tower of Mariano II, standing 62 feet high, is one of two surviving remnants of the city's medieval fortifications. Built in the 13th century under King Mariano II of Arborea, it guarded the Porta Manna, the city's main gate. The bell at its summit dates from 1430.

The Antiquarium Arborense preserves archaeological treasures looted from the Tharros site in the 19th century by private collectors, including Lord Vernon in 1851. Part of the haul ended up in the British Museum and the Louvre; what remained in Sardinia is displayed here.

The Tharros site, on the Sinis Peninsula, layers traces of a nuragic village, an 8th-century BC Phoenician town, a Carthaginian fortress, and a Roman city with its basalt-paved streets, thermal baths, and castellum aquae. Abandoned around the year 1000 in the face of Saracen raids, its stones were used to build Oristano.

activities.text: Stroll along Corso Umberto I and Piazza Eleonora d'Arborea, where a statue honors Eleanor, the 14th-century judge who promulgated the Carta de Logu, one of Europe's earliest legal codes.
Swim at Is Arutas, a beach on the Sinis Peninsula whose sand is formed from grains of white and pink quartz, the result of erosion from the surrounding granite cliffs.
Taste Vernaccia di Oristano, a DOC white wine produced from ancient grape varieties—one of Sardinia's oldest wines, with an oxidative character similar to Sherry.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

visits.text: The historic center of Orosei, perched on a hill 1.2 miles from the coast, is a well-preserved ensemble of stone houses, churches, and interior courtyards. The Church of San Giacomo Maggiore, whose bell tower dominates the town, dates from the 16th century.

The Gulf of Orosei stretches for 25 miles of limestone cliffs plunging into the sea, punctuated by coves accessible only by boat. Cala Gonone, a seaside resort at the foot of Supramonte belonging to the municipality of Dorgali, is the departure port for all excursions.

activities.text: Boat excursion from Cala Gonone to the gulf's coves: Cala Luna (blonde sand and caves in the cliff), Cala Sisine, Cala Mariolu with white and pink pebbles, and Cala Goloritzé whose 469-foot limestone pinnacle is classified as natural heritage.

Hike through the Su Gorropu gorge, a canyon with limestone walls reaching 1,640 feet high—one of Europe's deepest. The trail starts from the Ghenna Silana pass (3,280 feet elevation) and descends 2.5 miles to the gorge entrance.

Visit the Grotta del Bue Marino, a sea cave accessible by boat from Cala Gonone. Its walls preserve Neolithic engravings depicting a "Dance of the Sun," dated to 1800 BC. The name refers to the monk seal, which lived here until the 1970s.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

places.name: Orta San Giulio

visits.text: Orta San Giulio spreads across a wooded peninsula on the eastern shore of Lake Orta - the smallest of northern Italy's great lakes. Friedrich Nietzsche stayed here in 1882 and walked the hills arm-in-arm with Lou Andreas-Salomé; here he began conceiving Thus Spoke Zarathustra. Piazza Motta, closed to traffic and open to the lake, is bordered by the Palazzo della Comunità della Riviera (1582) whose first floor houses a baroque fresco representing the Virgin with Saints Francis and Giulio. The alleys that suddenly open onto the lake or interior courtyards form a preserved medieval labyrinth. The village ranks among the Borghi più belli d'Italia.

The island of San Giulio, 400 meters offshore (275m long, 140m wide), is reached by boat from Piazza Motta. Legend has it that the Greek missionary Giulio, wanting to build his hundredth church here in 390 AD, crossed the lake on his cloak, drove out the serpents and dragons infesting the island, and founded the edifice where he was buried. The Romanesque basilica of San Giulio, with foundations dating to the fourth century, houses salvaged ancient columns and medieval frescoes. The Benedictine abbey Mater Ecclesiae - a cloistered community - occupies most of the island. The perimeter path is marked by the Via del Silenzio and the Via della Meditazione.

The Sacro Monte of Orta, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2003, rises on the wooded hill dominating the village at 400 meters elevation. Commissioned by Capuchin friar architect Padre Cleto, the sanctuary was built between 1590 and 1760: it comprises 20 chapels decorated with frescoes and adorned with more than 900 life-size painted terracotta statues illustrating the life of Saint Francis of Assisi - from birth to canonization. It's the only alpine Sacro Monte entirely dedicated to a single saint. The church of San Nicolao at the summit, built in 1602-1607 modeled on the lower basilica of Assisi, houses a fourteenth-century wooden Pietà at the origin of local worship.

Back in the village, Via Bossi (n°11), the seventeenth-century Villa of the same name hides a coffered ceiling adorned with frescoes and a lakeside garden. The parish church of Santa Maria Assunta (fifteenth century, facade remodeled in 1940) presents a three-nave interior covered with seventeenth-eighteenth century frescoes. The Torre di Buccione, an isolated medieval tower in the woods on the eastern shore, is accessible on foot from the panoramic road. To the north, the Sanctuary of Madonna del Sasso (eighteenth century) dominates the wild western shore and offers a panorama over the entire body of water.

activities.text: Navigate Lake Orta to reach San Giulio island (paid crossing, a few minutes). The regular navigation service (Navigazione Lago d'Orta) also connects Pella, Lagna, and San Filiberto on the opposite shore. Lake circuit by bike on the panoramic road. The sanctuary of Madonna del Sasso, perched west of the lake, offers a panorama over the entire body of water.

Complete circuit of Lake Orta by car or bike (40km panoramic road). Swimming in the lake waters from Gozzano beach to the south. Garden enthusiasts can visit the villas and botanical gardens dotting the shores.

days_plan.itin_title: Italian Lakes Road Trip: Como, Maggiore, Orta and Garda
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona
Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy
Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

map_keywords: Isola San Giulio Orta San Giulio
Basilica San Giulio Isola San Giulio
Via del Silenzio Isola San Giulio
Sacro Monte di Orta Orta San Giulio
Chiesa Santa Maria Assunta Orta San Giulio
Palazzo della Comunità Orta San Giulio
Giardini Villa Motta Orta San Giulio

visits.text: Lago di Carezza (Karersee) – a 3-hectare glacial lake at 1,519 meters altitude in the Rosengarten Dolomites, whose waters shift from emerald green to deep blue depending on light and season. Local legend tells of a sorcerer who tried to seduce a water nymph by disguising himself as a rainbow—the nymph fled and the furious sorcerer shattered his rainbow into pieces in the lake, explaining the colorful reflections. Protected as a natural monument since 1980, the lake is fed by underground springs with no visible tributaries.

Santa Maddalena in Funes and Chiesa San Giovanni in Ranui – a hamlet of 500 inhabitants at 1,300 meters in Val di Funes, with the San Giovanni in Ranui church (1744) built on a rocky spur before the north face of the Geisler/Odle peaks (3,025m). The baroque church with its onion-domed bell tower is the subject of the world's most reproduced photograph of the Dolomites, with the Odle group as backdrop. The Val di Funes road, built only in 1928, connected hamlets previously accessible only on foot or horseback.

Alpe di Siusi (Seiser Alm) from Compatsch – Europe's largest alpine plateau with 56 square kilometers at 1,680-2,350 meters altitude, closed to motor traffic in season (accessible by shuttle or cable car from Ortisei and Castelrotto). The plateau has been used for grazing since the Neolithic—remains of Bronze Age pastoral huts have been uncovered during excavations. In summer, 1,400 cows and 700 horses graze here between June and September.

activities.text: Walk around Lago di Carezza – marked 1.2-kilometer trail around the lake with a viewpoint on the north shore offering the classic view of the Latemar reflection.
Easy hike on the Alpe di Siusi from Compatsch – 6-kilometer loop across the plateau with views of Sassolungo (3,181m) and Sciliar (2,563m), with minimal elevation change.

days_plan.itin_title: Dolomites Loop: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Pastures & Limestone Peaks

places.name: Orvieto

visits.text: Duomo di Orvieto - Pope Nicholas IV laid the first stone of this Gothic cathedral on November 13, 1290, to house the corporal stained with blood from the miracle of Bolsena (1263). Its construction spanned three centuries, involving Fra Bevignate, Lorenzo Maitani (who arrived in 1308), Andrea Pisano, and Andrea Orcagna. The triptych façade—one of Italy's most spectacular—is divided into three superimposed registers of bas-reliefs, golden mosaics, and four pilasters carved by Maitani between 1325 and 1330. The central rose window, Orcagna's work, is framed by 52 sculpted heads symbolizing the weeks of the year. The flanks alternate white marble and black basalt, a legacy of the Sienese tradition. Inside, the Cappella San Brizio preserves Luca Signorelli's fresco cycle of the Last Judgment (1499-1502), considered one of the Italian Renaissance's major works. Sigmund Freud mentions this chapel in The Psychopathology of Everyday Life, recounting how he forgot Signorelli's name.

Pozzo di San Patrizio - In 1527, after the Sack of Rome by Charles V's troops, Pope Clement VII took refuge in Orvieto and ordered this well's construction to guarantee water during a siege. Florentine architect Antonio da Sangallo the Younger dug between 1527 and 1537 a cylinder 13 meters in diameter and 54 meters deep into the volcanic tuff of the plateau. His technical solution is remarkable: two independent helical ramps of 248 steps each wind around the central well, illuminated by 72 large windows, allowing mules to descend and ascend with water jars without ever crossing paths. The well was never actually used for military purposes: Clement VII reconciled with Charles V before its completion. Its current name, given in the 19th century by monks from the neighboring Servite convent, was inspired by the legend of St. Patrick's Purgatory in Ireland.

Cappella Nova (San Brizio) - Accessible from the cathedral, this chapel in the right transept (built from 1408) is the jewel of the visit. Fra Angelico began its vault in 1447 before being recalled to Rome by the pope. In 1499, Luca Signorelli took over and spent two years covering the walls with scenes from the Apocalypse and the Last Judgment of unprecedented violence and anatomical precision. Michelangelo drew direct inspiration from it for the Sistine Chapel.

Orvieto Sotterranea - Beneath the upper town's streets, more than 1,200 artificial cavities carved into the tuff constitute a network whose origins go back 2,500 years. Local speleologists began exploring and documenting it in the late 1970s; about 440 cavities have since been mapped and studied. Guided tours traverse Etruscan cisterns from the 5th century BC, water collection tunnels, medieval olive presses, and waste pits that have yielded exceptional stratigraphy of daily life over two millennia. The temperature remains constant throughout the year.

Necropolis of the Crucifix of Tuff - At the foot of the cliffs supporting the city, this Etruscan necropolis (6th-3rd century BC) aligns over 200 tuff tombs arranged in an orthogonal plan, like a miniature city of the dead. Each tomb bears the name of the deceased and their clan carved into the lintel. The Etruscan city that overlooked these tombs was called Velzna; the Romans destroyed it in 264 BC and deported its inhabitants to the site of present-day Bolsena.

activities.text: Orvieto Classico DOC tasting in a tuff-carved cellar - Orvieto's vineyards have been producing wine since antiquity (one still has an Etruscan cellar). Wine cellars in the historic center offer tastings of the dry white Orvieto Classico DOC, a blend of Grechetto and Trebbiano, which Pope Paul III Farnese particularly appreciated.

Panoramic views from the ramparts of Rocca Albornoz - The medieval fortress built by papal legate Albornoz in the 14th century offers circular views of the tuff plateau, the Paglia valley, and the Tuscan hills. The circuit from Porta Rocca to the northern ramparts takes about 40 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

map_keywords: Necropoli del Crocifisso del Tufo Orvieto

visits.text: Ostana counts barely fifty permanent residents today - down from 1,200 in 1921 - yet this Occitan village perched at 1,250 meters beneath Monviso has become an international model of alpine renaissance. In 1985, mayor Giacomo Lombardo and architect Renato Maurino launched a restoration project following strict rules respecting original materials and volumes. This continuous effort transformed Ostana into an "open-air alpine architecture laboratory" in Legambiente's words, which awarded it the Bandiera Verde in 2005. In 2008, Ostana joined the Borghi più belli d'Italia network.

Wander through the hamlets scattered across the slope: La Villo (the administrative center with the town hall flying the Occitan flag), Champanho surrounded by maples and ash trees, Miribrart completely restored with European funds and home to a film school founded in 2012. In the small chapel of San Bernardo, a recently uncovered medieval fresco represents a rare testament to high-mountain sacred art. The ethnographic museum I Rënèis, housed in the town hall, presents traditional ways of life in the Po valley.

activities.text: Hike the Monviso Horizon route, a 50-kilometer loop connecting all the upper Po valley villages, also suitable for mountain biking. In winter, ski touring and snowshoeing at the foot of Monviso. Ostana is also a recognized climbing destination with summer routes and winter ice cascades.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: Locorotondo — circular historic center: the town's name contracts "Luogo rotondo" (round place), referring to the circular plan of the medieval borgo perched on its hill. Characteristic houses bear the name cummerse: pitched roofs covered with flat stone tiles, an architecture unique to Locorotondo that distinguishes it from its flat-roofed neighbors. The mother church of San Giorgio (17th-19th century) occupies the highest point of the promontory. Ranked among Italy's most beautiful villages, the town commands the entire Itria Valley.

Cisternino — old town: another village ranked among Italy's most beautiful, Cisternino presents a medieval center with whitewashed lanes of almost Oriental appearance. Its Romanesque campanile from the 12th century, remnant of the church of Santa Maria, towers over Piazza Garibaldi. The village is also known for its macellerie con fornello — butcher shops with built-in grills where you choose your meat and have it cooked on the spot, a culinary tradition unique to Puglia.

Ostuni — Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta and ramparts: perched at 218 meters on its three hills, Ostuni owes its white color to a medieval health decision — facades were covered with lime during the 15th-17th century plague epidemics, lime having disinfectant properties. The late Gothic cathedral, built in 1400, stands out for its facade with three portals and its rose window in limestone lacework. Founded by the Messapians around 600 BC, the city was destroyed by Hannibal during the Punic Wars then rebuilt by the Greeks.

Ostuni — Museum of Civilizations and burial of Delia: in the former church of San Vito Martyr is preserved the burial of Delia, a 25,000-year-old pregnant woman found in the cave of Santa Maria di Agnano, a few kilometers from the city. The nearly complete skeleton, with the fetus visible, is accompanied by a headdress adorned with hundreds of small shells and flint tools. One of the best-preserved Paleolithic testimonies in Italy.

Ostuni — sea and olive grove viewpoints: from the Aragonese ramparts built in 1539 to resist Turkish incursions, the view extends across millennial olive groves and, in clear weather, to the Adriatic Sea 8 kilometers away. Piazza della Libertà marks the entrance to the historic center and serves as the town's heart.

Torre Guaceto Nature Reserve: about ten kilometers from Ostuni, this marine and terrestrial reserve protects a coastal wetland, a 16th-century Aragonese watchtower, and a low coast alternating rocky coves and sandy stretches. Access to the main beach is on foot or by bike from the parking area.

activities.text: Driving loop through the Itria Valley between Locorotondo and Ostuni: secondary roads cross agricultural properties dotted with isolated trulli, dry stone walls, and centuries-old olive trees with twisted trunks. Some of these olives are more than 1,000 years old according to botanical assessments.

Beach day — Torre Canne and Ostuni Mare: 7 km from Ostuni, the northern Ionian coast offers fine sandy beaches between pine forests and dunes. Torre Canne takes its name from an old coastal watchtower. It's the most accessible beach from Ostuni, with wilder coves reachable on foot toward Torre Pozzella.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley
Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

visits.text: Otranto — Cathedral of Santa Maria Annunziata: built by Normans in 1068 on an early Christian site, the cathedral is listed on UNESCO's tentative World Heritage list for its floor mosaic. Commissioned in 1163 by the archbishop and executed between 1163 and 1165 by the monk Pantaleone using 600,000 limestone tesserae, this 1,000-square-meter mosaic represents the tree of life of Gilgamesh supported by two Indian elephants. Around the tree mingle biblical, mythological, and historical figures: Adam and Eve, King Arthur, Cain and Abel, Alexander the Great, the signs of the Zodiac. In the Chapel of Martyrs are preserved the bones of 800 citizens beheaded by the Turks in 1480 for refusing to convert to Islam — canonized by Pope Francis in 2013.

Otranto — Aragonese castle and ramparts: built between 1485 and 1498 on Ferdinand I of Aragon's orders to prevent another Ottoman attack after the 1480 massacre, the castle is the work of architects Ciro Ciri and Francesco di Giorgio Martini. Its bastioned ramparts constitute an early example of military architecture transitioning between medieval and artillery-era design. The walk along the ramparts overlooks the old town and harbor.

Cava di Bauxite di Otranto: 2.6 km south of the center, this former bauxite quarry operated from 1940 to 1976 has left an emerald lake at the bottom of an excavation with rust-red walls. The chromatic contrast between oxidized rock and green water results from the water table and runoff. View from the elevated belvedere.

Torre Sant'Andrea — faraglioni and Otranto's north coast: 14 km north of Otranto, this coastal site is known for its natural arches and limestone faraglioni rising straight from turquoise water. The coastal path between Torre Sant'Andrea and the site of Roca Vecchia (Messapian archaeological site with the Grotta della Poesia, a cave with rupestrian inscriptions from the 2nd millennium BC) runs on foot along the cliffs.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling around the faraglioni of Torre Sant'Andrea: the natural arches create shallow zones with crystalline water, accessible by swimming from the rocky shore. Water shoes recommended.

Swimming on the Adriatic coast of Salento north of Otranto: between Torre Sant'Andrea and Santa Cesarea Terme, the coast alternates rocky coves and small beaches accessible on foot. Santa Cesarea Terme, 20 km south, is a former spa town with Moorish-style villas from the early 20th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

visits.text: Pacentro - a village of 1,200 inhabitants built entirely from white Majella stone at 720 meters altitude. The Castello Caldoresco, with its three 14th-century cylindrical towers, dominates the village. Madonna Ciccone, whose ancestors are from Pacentro, visited the village in 1995 to trace her roots.

activities.text: Climb to the castle towers for panoramic views of Valle Peligna and the Majella foothills.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Discover Padova, one of Italy's oldest cities with legendary origins traced to the Trojan warrior Antenor. The UNESCO-listed Scrovegni Chapel houses Giotto's revolutionary 14th-century frescoes that changed Western art forever with their emotional realism and innovative use of color. Book your timed entry in advance for this artistic treasure.

Explore the Palazzo della Ragione, a vast medieval law court featuring one of the largest unsupported roofed halls in Europe, its walls covered with allegorical frescoes and celestial symbols. Below the arcades, discover Sotto il Salone - said to be Europe's oldest indoor food market where artisan shops have operated for generations.

Visit the Basilica of Saint Anthony (Il Santo), a major pilgrimage destination housing the saint's marble tomb and chapels frescoed by Giotto and other masters. The basilica combines Romanesque, Gothic, and Byzantine architectural elements into a unique synthesis of styles.

Stroll through Prato della Valle, one of Europe's largest squares, featuring the central Isola Memmia ringed by a canal and 78 statues of Padova's most illustrious figures. This grand public space transforms from market venue to evening promenade as locals gather.

activities.text: Experience the Padova Urbs Picta UNESCO trail connecting eight sites of 14th-century frescoes, including the glowing dome of paradise by Giusto de' Menabuoi in the Baptistery. The 48 or 72-hour card provides access to all sites.

Visit Palazzo del Bo, seat of the University of Padua founded in 1222, to see the Teatro Anatomico - the world's first permanent anatomy theatre - and Galileo's actual lecturing podium in guided tours of this historic academic institution.

Explore the Orto Botanico, the world's oldest university botanical garden still in its original location, a UNESCO World Heritage Site founded in 1545 with over 6,000 species including a palm tree planted in 1585 that inspired Goethe.

Stop at Caffè Pedrocchi, the legendary café open continuously from 1831 to 1916, where poets, professors, and revolutionaries gathered. The Sala Verde famously welcomed even those who couldn't afford coffee - giving rise to the Italian expression 'essere al verde' (to be broke).

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Templi di Paestum - a 120-hectare Greco-Roman archaeological site designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1998, preserving three of the finest Greek temples anywhere in the world. The Greek colony of Poseidonia was founded around 600 BCE by settlers from Sybaris. The Temple of Neptune (Hera II), built circa 450 BCE in the Doric style, stands as one of the most complete Greek temples in existence, with all 36 of its honey-colored travertine columns still standing tall against the southern Italian sky.

Tempio di Era (Basilica) - the oldest of Paestum's three temples, dating to 550 BCE, featuring 50 sturdy columns characteristic of archaic Doric architecture. For centuries it was mistakenly called the 'Basilica' by 18th-century archaeologists who failed to recognize the earliest forms of Greek temple design in its unusual proportions.
