Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Paestum - museum founded in 1952 that houses the Tomba del Tuffatore (Tomb of the Diver, 480 BCE), the only known example of Greek figurative painting on stone slabs from Magna Graecia. The five painted slabs depict a symposium scene and a lone diver launching himself toward the sea - interpreted as an allegory of the soul's passage from life to death.

activities.text: Walk along Paestum's ancient Greek defensive walls - 4.75 km of travertine ramparts dating to the 4th century BCE, with four monumental gates partially preserved, accessible on foot from the archaeological site.

Sample mozzarella di bufala Campana DOP at farms around Paestum - the Sele plain surrounding Paestum is one of the historic production zones for buffalo mozzarella, whose milk contains 8% fat compared to just 3.5% in cow's milk.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to Calabria: The Tyrrhenian Coast

visits.text: Toiano is a ghost village perched among the Pisan hills and tuff calanchi reminiscent of Volterra's Balze. Tuff spurs up to 40-50 meters high line the access track, 3 meters wide. The village, inhabited until the 1960s (521 souls in the 19th century), was gradually abandoned when Valdera factories—including Piaggio—drew labor to the plain. Its history was marked by the unsolved 1947 murder of "beautiful Elvira" (Elvira Orlandini, 22), a case that mobilized all of postwar Italy and remains a cold case. The borgo is listed by FAI (Fondo per l'Ambiente Italiano) among the "places of the heart" to be preserved.

The Pieve di San Martino of Palaia, outside the walls, was built between 1270 and 1300 to Andrea Pisano's designs—it is listed in the catalog of Italian national monuments. A Romanesque building with three naves of five bays, ending in a raised presbyterium with three chapels. On the floor, funerary slabs of the village's illustrious figures; on the walls, those of Palaia's parish priests.

Villa Saletta, another ghost village near Palaia, is an example of a completely self-sufficient 16th-17th century villa-fattoria belonging to the Florentine Ricciardi family. The main square, manor villa, and clock tower have remained virtually intact. Films have been shot here, including the Taviani brothers' La Notte di San Lorenzo and Virzì's Me and Napoleon. In summer, a popular theater festival brings it to life.

activities.text: Visit the Museum of Rural Civilization in Montefoscoli, installed in a house dating from the year 1000: cellars, oil mill, agricultural tools, reconstruction of the Valdera peasant world. Next door, the "Casa Museo Vaccà Berlinghieri" celebrates Andrea Vaccà, famous surgeon at the University of Pisa and personal physician to Paolina Bonaparte. Best visited early morning before heading to Toiano and Villa Saletta.

Hike on wooded trails between Palaia and its hamlets—Alica, San Gervasio, Colleoli. The Tuscan expression "Peggio Palaia" (worse than Palaia) dates, according to tradition, from the 1435 siege by Niccolò Piccinino, who took the castle by starvation after failing to force it.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

visits.text: Palena - a village of 1,500 inhabitants at 875 meters in the heart of Parco Nazionale della Majella, entirely rebuilt after its near-total destruction by German troops in 1943. The Castello Ducale, the only surviving medieval building, houses the Museo Geopaleontologico with Majella fossils.

Area Faunistica dell'Orso Marsicano - a natural enclosure of several hectares where Marsican bears unfit for wild life are housed, visible from a marked discovery trail. The Marsican bear (Ursus arctos marsicanus) is an endemic subspecies of Abruzzo, with a total population estimated at 50-60 individuals.

activities.text: Hike to the Eremo della Madonna dell'Altare - a 16th-century hermitage perched on a rocky spur at 1,100 meters, accessible by a marked 2-kilometer trail from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Palermo Cathedral layers five architectural styles across seven centuries: Norman, Arab, Catalan Gothic, Baroque, and Neoclassical. Its southwest tower bears a Koranic inscription carved into marble—a remnant from the Arab period (827-1072). The imperial tombs house the remains of Frederick II of Hohenstaufen and Roger II of Sicily.

The Quattro Canti, an octagonal crossroads built between 1608 and 1620 by the Spanish viceroy, divides Palermo into four mandamenti (districts). Each façade features a fountain, one of the four seasons, a Spanish king, and a neighborhood patron saint.

The Pretoria Fountain, commissioned in 1554 for a Florentine villa then reassembled in Palermo in 1573, contains 644 sculpted elements depicting river deities and sea monsters. Local clergy nicknamed it the "Fountain of Shame" due to its nude figures.

La Martorana, founded in 1143 by Admiral George of Antioch, preserves Sicily's oldest Norman mosaics, including one depicting Roger II crowned by Christ—a unique image of a Norman king in Byzantine imperial dress.

Ballarò Market, Palermo's largest street market, sprawls through several alleys of the Albergheria quarter and dates back to Arab times. Vendors shout their wares in Sicilian-Arabic dialect while stalls overflow with local produce and North African spices.

The Norman Palace, seat of the Sicilian Parliament since 1947, was erected by Arab emirs in the 9th century then expanded by Roger II in 1130. Its Palatine Chapel gleams with 6,340 square meters of Byzantine mosaics on gold ground—the most accomplished synthesis of Arab-Norman-Byzantine art in Europe.

The Capuchin Catacombs, excavated beneath the convent in 1599, display 8,000 mummies dressed in period clothing along underground corridors. Little Rosalia Lombardo, who died in 1920, remains so perfectly preserved that biochemists studied her until 2009 to unlock the embalming formula.

The Teatro Massimo, inaugurated in 1897, is Italy's largest opera house and Europe's third largest. Its neoclassical Doric façade serves as the final scene in Coppola's Godfather III. Its acoustics, among the world's finest, result from the elliptical shape of the hall.

activities.text: Palermo street food tasting at Ballarò Market: panelle (chickpea fritters), crocchè (potato croquettes), sfincione (thick anchovy pizza), and arancini.
Evening stroll through La Vucciria, Palermo's oldest market (14th century), which transforms at night into a buzzing scene of street food vendors and wine bars.

Visit Palazzo Abatellis, a 15th-century Catalan-Gothic palace converted into Sicily's regional art museum. It houses the Triumph of Death, an anonymous 15th-century fresco depicting a skeletal horseman mowing down nobility—one of medieval Italy's most powerful works.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily
Complete Tour of Sicily

visits.text: Guadagnolo — Lazio's highest village at 1,218 m altitude in the Monti Prenestini, reached via a winding road from Capranica Prenestina. On clear days, the panoramic belvedere sweeps across a 180° arc covering Rome, the Tyrrhenian Sea, and the Apennines. The village's permanent population barely exceeds twenty residents.

Castel San Pietro Romano — A tiny village at 763 m overlooking Palestrina from the summit of Monte Ginestro, listed among the Borghi più Belli d'Italia. Medieval houses step along the rocky ridge, incorporating vestiges of ancient Praeneste's acropolis, with polygonal walls in opus cyclopeum identical to those of the sanctuary below.

Santuario della Fortuna Primigenia — The largest religious complex in Republican Roman Italy, built in the late 2nd century BC across seven cascading terraces on Monte Ginestro's flank. The sanctuary dedicated to Fortuna Primigenia—'Jupiter's firstborn' and oracular goddess—was consulted throughout the Empire. The oracle functioned through the drawing of wooden lots (sortes), and Cicero reported that Romans considered no site more suited to divination. The structure employed Roman concrete made with pozzolana on a grand scale for the first time, its durability proven by its preservation two millennia later. In 1944, Mussolini demolished several hundred medieval houses obstructing the complex, revealing the terraces in their current state.

Museo Archeologico Nazionale Prenestino and the Nile Mosaic — The museum, installed in Palazzo Barberini built directly atop the ancient structures, preserves on its third floor the Nile Mosaic, a Hellenistic masterpiece measuring 5.85 m × 4.31 m. Created in the 1st century BC by Alexandrian craftsmen, the mosaic depicts the Nile's course from the Ethiopian sources to the delta, populated with hippos, crocodiles, pygmy hunters, and a walled city flanked by four colossal statues. Discovered in 1507 still in place within the sanctuary, it was cut into 27 pieces in 1625 by Palestrina's bishop, transported to Rome, then returned to Palestrina by Cardinal Francesco Barberini in 1640 after several restorations. It has been displayed here since 1956.

activities.text: Walking through Palestrina's historic center — The medieval town developed directly atop the sanctuary's ramps and terraces, such that some cellars and basements of houses still reveal Roman masonry in opus incertum. The Cattedrale di Sant'Agapito, dedicated to the 2nd-century local martyr, preserves a paleo-Christian crypt.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

visits.text: Capo Palinuro - a 230-meter limestone promontory at the southern tip of Cilento, named according to Virgil's Aeneid (Book V) for Palinurus, Aeneas's helmsman who drowned here during the voyage to Italy. The cape shelters a system of 25 sea caves carved into the cliffs, including the Grotta Azzurra with its phosphorescent electric-blue waters, the Grotta del Sangue with red iron-oxide concretions, and the Grotta delle Ossa containing prehistoric human remains.

Arco Naturale di Palinuro - a natural limestone arch 12 meters high rising from the sea on the western side of the cape, sculpted by marine erosion over millennia. Accessible only by boat, it stands as one of the most photographed landmarks on the Cilento coast.

activities.text: Boat excursion around Capo Palinuro to explore the sea caves, pass beneath the natural arch, and swim in coves unreachable by land.
Hike the Capo Palinuro trail from the village - 4 km to the lighthouse (built 1867) with views across the Gulf of Policastro and, on clear days, to the Aeolian Islands.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to Calabria: The Tyrrhenian Coast

visits.text: Piazza Grande di Palmanova - At the precise geometric center of this star-shaped fortress city founded on October 7, 1593 by the Venetian Republic, the hexagonal plaza forms the heart of an architectural masterpiece. The foundation date commemorates the 1571 Battle of Lepanto victory over the Ottomans. The original name "Palma" symbolized that triumph; Napoleon later added "nova" two centuries on. Stand on the central platform to grasp the perfect symmetry of six radiating streets leading toward nine defensive bastions.

Duomo del Santissimo Redentore - Dominating Piazza Grande, this church traditionally attributed to Vincenzo Scamozzi, Palladio's pupil, features a deliberately squat bell tower: it couldn't be visible from outside the ramparts, denying potential attackers a targeting reference point. This single detail encapsulates Palmanova's dual nature as both Renaissance utopian city and military machine.

Fortezza di Palmanova - Three concentric rings form three nested nine-pointed stars. The first two were built by Venice between 1593 and 1690, the third added by Napoleon between 1806 and 1813 under General Chasseloup's direction. Each curtain wall measured roughly 400 meters—exactly matching the cannon range of the era, so a single bastion could defend the entire adjacent front. UNESCO-listed since 2017, Palmanova remains the only perfectly preserved star-shaped Renaissance fortress city in existence.

Museo della Grande Guerra e della Fortezza - Housed in the dungeons of Porta Cividale, this museum chronicles the fortress's military history from its 1593 founding through the 20th century. Weapons, uniforms, and documents illustrate the Venetian, Napoleonic, and Austro-Hungarian periods.

Bastioni e Gallerie di Contromina - Descend into the underground galleries of Baluardo Donato: these secret tunnels connected the bastions to the 4-kilometer outer moat, allowing soldiers to move under cover. The pointed bastion design deflected cannonballs, reducing impact force. Above, the rampart walkway offers a 4.3-kilometer circuit on foot or by bike around the entire fortress.

Porta Aquileia, Porta Udine, and Porta Cividale - The three monumental gates facing Aquileia, Udine, and Cividale were the fortress's only entry points. Each was protected by counter-gates and drawbridges. Push through to appreciate the wall thickness and the perspectives opening toward Piazza Grande.

activities.text: Complete rampart circuit on foot or by bike - The Anello delle Fortificazioni (4.3 km) traces all three Venetian and Napoleonic defensive rings, past bastions, ravelins, and lunettes. Interpretive panels line the route. Each September, the "Palma alle Armi" historical reenactment draws over a thousand costumed participants in 16th-century dress.

days_plan.itin_title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

places.name: Palmi

visits.text: Palmi—a town of 19,000 perched on a 240-meter promontory above the Tyrrhenian Sea, with sweeping views of the Aeolian Islands and the Strait of Messina. The Museo Calabrese di Etnografia e Folklore, founded in 1965 in the Casa della Cultura Leonida Repaci, houses one of Calabria's most important ethnographic collections: traditional costumes, musical instruments, rural life objects, and a section dedicated to the Varia di Palmi—an 18-meter processional machine inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Heritage list in 2013.

activities.text: Stop at Palmi's belvedere for the view across the Strait of Messina to Mount Etna before descending toward Scilla.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

map_keywords: Casa della Cultura Leonida Repaci Palmi

visits.text: The prehistoric village at Capo Milazzese, perched on an eastern promontory, dates to the Bronze Age (15th-13th century BC). Its 23 elliptical dry-stone huts rank among the Mediterranean's best-preserved prehistoric settlements.

Cala Junco, a cove accessible on foot (20 minutes from the port), forms a natural pool enclosed by granite and obsidian cliffs. Its intense blue-green water makes it Panarea's most photographed beach.

activities.text: Boat excursion around the satellite islets — Basiluzzo, Lisca Bianca, Dattilo, and Bottaro. The islets harbor underwater thermal springs where the sea bubbles between the rocks.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

visits.text: Pantelleria, a volcanic island 70 km from Tunisia, is nicknamed "the Black Pearl of the Mediterranean" for its basalt rock. Its Dammusi — domed stone dwellings inherited from 9th-century Arab architecture — collect rainwater and maintain coolness through 90 cm-thick walls.

Specchio di Venere (Venus's Mirror), a crater lake at 836 meters elevation, reaches 40°C in geothermal zones. Its gray-green silt, used as a body mask since antiquity, is harvested directly by bathers.

The Mursia prehistoric site includes a Bronze Age village (2000-1000 BC) and a sesi — a funerary monument unique to Pantelleria, built of dry-stacked volcanic stone, whose function remains debated by archaeologists.
Cala Gadir on the northeast coast is a small black rock cove where underwater geothermal springs heat the sea to 30-35°C. Accessible on foot from Gadir village in 10 minutes.

Montagna Grande, the island's highest point at 836 meters, offers views of the Tunisian coast on clear days from its hiking trail. The Mediterranean forest of kermes oak and mastic that covers it is among the most intact in the central Mediterranean.

activities.text: Bathing at the Thermae San Calogero, natural 60°C geothermal springs in a black stone cave on the north coast, used since the Bronze Age.
Tasting Passito di Pantelleria DOC, a sweet white wine produced from Zibibbo grapes sun-dried in July — the only Sicilian wine awarded the grand prize by the International Union of Oenologists.
Island circuit by scooter (50 km, circular coastal road) to reach Cala dei Cinque Denti — a cove with five finger-like lava rocks emerging from the sea, accessible on foot via a 20-minute trail.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria
Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Pantheon

visits.text: The Pantheon is the best-preserved building from ancient Rome. The first temple was erected by Agrippa in 27 BC and burned in 80 AD. The current structure is the one Hadrian rebuilt between 118 and 125 AD—yet he kept Agrippa's inscription on the pediment, erasing his own name. Its hemispherical unreinforced concrete dome (43.30 m in diameter) remains the world's largest in this category. The interior height exactly equals the diameter, inscribing a perfect sphere within the rotunda. The central oculus (8.92 m diameter) is the only source of light: rain enters directly and drains through 22 nearly invisible outlets in the original marble floor. Each year at Pentecost, a shower of rose petals descends through the oculus. Raphael is buried here, along with Italian kings Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I. Michelangelo called this building an 'angelic and not human design.'

Santa Maria sopra Minerva, steps from the Pantheon, is Rome's only Gothic church. Built in the 13th century atop the ruins of a temple to Minerva, it contains Michelangelo's Christ Bearing the Cross (1521), the Caraffa Chapel decorated by Filippino Lippi, and Fra Angelico's tomb. In front of the façade, Bernini's marble elephant bearing the obelisk (1667) is one of Rome's most whimsical monuments.

activities.text: Watch the Pantheon's oculus in the rain: water falls directly onto the ancient marble floor and vanishes into Roman drains that still function perfectly. The effect is mesmerizing.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Maria sopra Minerva Roma
Chiesa di Sant'Ignazio di Loyola Roma

places.name: Parco Maremma

visits.text: Exploration of Parco Naturale della Maremma and hike to the wild beach of Marina di Alberese.
Observation of local wildlife and landscapes between sea and forest.

activities.text: Moment of relaxation on the beach bordered by driftwood and natural dunes.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters

map_keywords: Spiaggia di Marina di Alberese Alberese

visits.text: Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta - Romanesque cathedral built between 1059 and 1106, with a 13th-century Gothic bell tower. The central dome was decorated between 1526 and 1530 by Correggio, who depicted the Assumption of the Virgin with 150 figures in trompe-l'oeil creating the illusion of an opening to heaven. Titian, upon seeing this fresco, reportedly declared it worth its weight in gold - at the time, roughly 1,000 ducats.

Battistero di Parma - octagonal baptistery in pink Verona marble built between 1196 and 1307 by Benedetto Antelami, with five sculpted portals and 16 façade loggias representing the months, seasons, and zodiac signs. The 14-meter-diameter interior dome is covered with 13th-century Byzantine-style frescoes on a gold background. It stands as one of the rare medieval Italian baptisteries preserved with its complete iconographic program.

Camera di San Paolo - room in the San Paolo convent decorated in 1519 by Correggio for Abbess Giovanna da Piacenza. The frescoes depict putti in garlands of vine leaves and mythological figures, in a humanist program unusual for a monastic space. The room remained closed to the public for two centuries after the convent's dissolution.

Palazzo della Pilotta and Teatro Farnese - unfinished Farnese palace built between 1583 and 1622, whose interior Teatro Farnese (1618) ranks among Europe's first permanent Baroque theaters with a 40-meter-wide stage and wooden cavea seating 4,500 spectators. Entirely destroyed by 1944 bombing, the theater was reconstructed identically between 1956 and 1962 using original fragments.

activities.text: Tasting of Prosciutto di Parma DOP and Parmigiano Reggiano DOP in historic center delicatessens - prosciutto aged minimum 12 months in the hills south of Parma, Parmigiano produced exclusively in the provinces of Parma, Reggio Emilia, Modena, Bologna, and Mantua.

Visit to a Parmigiano Reggiano cheese dairy in the countryside around Parma - local producers offer production and aging facility tours, with tastings of cheeses at different stages (12, 24, and 36 months).

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth
Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine

places.name: Parme

visits.text: Begin your discovery of Parma at Piazza del Duomo, one of Italy's most beautiful squares. The cathedral, built between 1060 and 1073 in pure Romanesque style, welcomes you with its simple facade in pale stone. Look up inside to discover the dome painted by Correggio in the early 16th century—an Assumption of the Virgin so breathtaking it seems to open onto the heavens themselves. Entry is free, so take your time soaking it all in.

Right beside the cathedral stands the octagonal baptistery in pink Verona marble, the work of Benedetto Antelami, begun in 1196. This monument bridges Romanesque and Gothic styles and ranks among Italy's finest baptisteries. Step inside: the frescoes covering the walls and dome are remarkable, and the light filtering through the windows creates an extraordinary atmosphere. Allow about 30 minutes; a combined ticket includes the adjacent diocesan museum.

Head next to Palazzo della Pilotta, just a hundred meters from the main square. This vast 16th-century complex, built by the Farnese family, houses the National Gallery, the archaeological museum, and above all the Teatro Farnese. Built in 1618 by Giovan Battista Aleotti on orders from Duke Ranuccio I, this entirely wooden theater could seat 4,000 spectators—in a city that had only 20,000 residents. It was used just nine times over a century. Almost completely destroyed by bombing on May 13, 1944, it was reconstructed identically in the 1950s. It's the world's first theater with a permanent proscenium arch. They even staged mock naval battles here thanks to a system that could flood the pit.

Don't miss the Basilica of Santa Maria della Steccata, erected in 1521. This Renaissance church shelters an image of the Virgin venerated for centuries in Parma. You'll also see frescoes by Parmigianino in the apse. Entry is free and the visit is quick—allow 20 minutes.

Finish with a stroll through Piazza Garibaldi, the lively heart of the historic center. Entirely paved, it's bordered by the Palazzo del Comune and the Governor's Palace with its clock tower. This is where locals gather for aperitivo. From here, Via Farini—a pedestrian shopping street—leads you to the Teatro Regio, built in 1829 on the initiative of Marie Louise of Austria, Napoleon's wife and Duchess of Parma.

activities.text: Parma is the capital of Italy's Food Valley, and you can't leave town without tasting its two treasures: Parmigiano Reggiano and Prosciutto di Parma. Tour operators offer combined visits to the city outskirts to discover a cheese dairy and a ham curing facility—allow half a day. Alternatively, head to the covered market or gourmet food shops in the center for a more flexible tasting. Pair everything with a glass of local Lambrusco.

Treat yourself to a walk in the Ducal Park, Parma's grand Italian-style garden located just across the Torrente Parma. Its tree-lined paths, fountains, and small lake make it the perfect spot to digest after all those tastings. The Ducal Palace, once Marie Louise's residence, can be viewed from outside—it now houses the Carabinieri headquarters.

days_plan.itin_title: Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills

map_keywords: Basilica Santa Maria della Steccata Parma

visits.text: Villa Manin - This monumental complex of 8,500 square meters and 18 hectares was built in the 17th century by Friulian nobleman Antonio Manin. The forecourt, inspired by St. Peter's Square in Rome, opens onto a semicircular colonnade. The central building was redesigned in the 18th century by Giorgio Massari, one of Venice's great Settecento architects. Napoleon Bonaparte lodged here for two months in 1797 with Joséphine de Beauharnais, conducting negotiations that led to the Treaty of Campoformio signed October 17, 1797—ending a thousand years of Venetian Republic. Napoleon's bedroom and period bed remain on display.

Cappella Sant'Andrea - Built in 1708 by Domenico Rossi outside the square courtyard, this octagonal chapel adorned with marble statues by sculptor Pietro Baratta was connected by corridors to the villa's upper floors, allowing the Manin family to attend services without mingling with Passariano villagers.

Permanent collections - The former stables house a collection of antique carriages and a 350-piece armory from Udine's Casa della Contadinanza. Since 2004, the villa has also served as a Contemporary Art Center with rotating exhibitions featuring international artists and park installations.

activities.text: Stroll through the French-style gardens - Designed in 1714 by a French architect modeled on Versailles, the park blends centuries-old trees, small lakes, hillocks crowned with mythological statuary, and vast lawns. In spring, the central meadow carpets itself with yellow daffodils. Even Carlo Goldoni was dazzled by the site's magnificence during a visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

visits.text: The MACCA (Museo d'Arte Contemporanea a Cielo Aperto) of Peccioli brings together over 70 permanent installations scattered throughout the municipal territory. The Anfiteatro Fonte Mazzola, built in 2007 in tuff slabs without concrete using bio-construction techniques, can accommodate 2,000 spectators with the hills as backdrop. In July it hosts the 11 Lune festival. In 2024, Peccioli won RAI's national "Il Borgo dei Borghi" competition.

The medieval borgo of Peccioli has been inhabited since the Neolithic, with attested Etruscan and Roman occupations. Its Piazza del Popolo brings together the Palazzo Pretorio, the Pieve di San Verano, and the Archaeological Museum—excavations have yielded Greek and Etruscan ceramics. Walking Patrick Tuttofuoco's Endless Sunset footbridge, which connects the centro storico to the modern part in a ribbon of colors, is one of the most surprising strolls in the Pisan hills.

The hamlet of Ghizzano houses permanent works by Alicja Kwade and Patrick Tuttofuoco, and house façades bear the signature of British artist David Tremlett. The entire territory of Peccioli functions as a museum without walls—the "Sistema Peccioli": revenues from the waste treatment and recovery plant (biogas, recycling) have financed culture, art, and public facilities since the 1990s, a model cited as a reference at the European level.

activities.text: 11 Lune Festival at the Anfiteatro Fonte Mazzola, every July: theater, music, and dance by national and international artists, with the Valdera hills as natural backdrop. The festival attracts about 15,000 spectators per edition.
Wine and local product tasting in the cellars of the Pisan Hills Wine Route. In October, the Sagra del Tartufo brings Peccioli alive with markets, tastings, and performances.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

visits.text: Penne - the city of red brick, built across four hills (Sacro, Romano, Castello, and Cappuccio) at 438 meters altitude. The entire historic center, ranked among Italy's most beautiful, is constructed from locally fired bricks that give the streets a characteristic orange hue, unique in Abruzzo.

Cattedrale di San Massimo - a Romanesque cathedral rebuilt in the 12th-13th centuries on the foundations of a Roman temple. The red brick façade and 14th-century octagonal bell tower make it the most representative building of Penne's architecture.

Chiesa dell'Annunziata - a 15th-century church with a late Gothic carved portal, central to Holy Week ceremonies with the Good Friday procession that has united the entire town since the 16th century.

activities.text: Visit the Riserva Naturale Lago di Penne - a 146-hectare WWF reserve around an artificial lake created in 1959. Home to grey herons, redshanks, and otters. Visitor center with exhibitions on local fauna.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Pergola

visits.text: Museo dei Bronzi Dorati della Città di Pergola — the major archaeological treasure of Le Marche. In 1946, farmers near Cartoceto di Pergola unearthed while ploughing four gilded bronze statues from the 1st century BC: an equestrian warrior, a noblewoman and two divine or priestly figures, in exceptional condition. Their origin is still debated: some historians identify the equestrian group as depicting Publius Quinctilius Varus, governor of Syria. They are the only gilded equestrian bronzes from Roman antiquity preserved anywhere in the world. Displayed since 1999 in a museum purpose-built for them.

Duomo di Santa Maria Assunta and historic centre — the cathedral was rebuilt in the 19th century on a medieval foundation, with a freestanding 12th-century campanile. The interior holds a Crucifixion attributed to Raffaellino del Colle, a pupil of Raphael. The Piazza Municipale is lined with Renaissance palaces, including the Palazzo dei Priori with its loggia.

Convento di San Francesco — founded in the 13th century, the church preserves 15th-century frescoes and works recovered from rural churches across the Pergola area. The cloister is one of the few intact Franciscan ensembles surviving in the province of Pesaro-Urbino.

activities.text: Walk through the historic centre to admire the arcaded houses typical of the Pergola area, and look for Roman milestones repurposed as door lintels in several façades — traces of the Via Flaminia that once ran nearby.

Taste the formaggio di fossa from Pergola — cheese aged in tufa pits — and the crescia al formaggio, a wood-fired leavened bread with pecorino and eggs, an Easter speciality throughout the Pesaro area.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Duomo di Santa Maria Assunta Pergola

visits.text: Pesariis - mountain village in Carnia, birthplace of the world-renowned Solari clock factory
Museo dell'Orologeria - museum dedicated to clockmaking in the land of clocks
Tour of the 15 monumental clocks - circuit through the village discovering the giant clocks

activities.text: Visit to the clockmaking museum tracing the history of this local industry
Walk in Val Pesarina in search of monumental clocks
Discovery of Solari clocks present in train stations and airports worldwide

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

visits.text: Pescasseroli - the unofficial capital of Parco Nazionale d'Abruzzo, Lazio e Molise, with 2,200 inhabitants at 1,167 meters. Birthplace of Benedetto Croce (1866-1952), a major philosopher and historian of liberal Italy. Its historic center with 18th-century houses is the liveliest and best-preserved of the park's villages.

Museo Naturalistico del Parco - the national park's museum presenting local fauna and flora, with taxidermied specimens of Marsican bear, wolf, and lynx. Ideal for understanding conservation issues before exploring the territory.
Area Faunistica del Lupo - a 2-hectare enclosure on the village outskirts where injured or non-releasable Apennine wolves live in a semi-natural habitat. Open year-round.

activities.text: Hike on the B1 trail toward Valle di Rose - 3-hour round trip through beech forests with high probability of observing deer and roe deer. Marsican bears frequently visit this area in spring.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Peschiera Del Garda

visits.text: Explore the Fortress of Peschiera del Garda, an extraordinary example of military architecture enclosing the entire old town. Its walls, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2017, bear witness to centuries of history: from Roman fort to Scaliger stronghold, from sixteenth-century Venetian fortress to Habsburg citadel. Enter through the monumental Porta Verona or Porta Brescia to discover this defensive gem.

Visit the Sanctuary of the Madonna del Frassino, a splendid monumental church nestled in nature at the foot of the Lake Garda hills. Built between 1511 and 1514 on the spot where the Madonna appeared to the farmer Bartolomeo Broglia, the sanctuary kept by the Franciscan Friars Minor features a single nave and two marvellous frescoed chapels.

activities.text: Stroll along the Lungolago Mazzini, a wonderful lakeside promenade stretching about 700 metres from the old town to the Lido dei Cappuccini. This panoramic route, perfect at sunset, is lined with elegant establishments and offers spectacular views of the crystal-clear waters of Lake Garda.

Relax at the Lido Cappuccini, one of the lake's most popular bathing establishments where you can hire sun loungers and pedalos, swim in the clear waters and enjoy a refreshing break on the pebble beach, with lifeguard service and a restaurant right on the waterfront.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

map_keywords: Fortezza di Peschiera Peschiera del Garda
Santuario Madonna del Frassino Peschiera del Garda

visits.text: Pescia Cathedral is mentioned as early as 951 in the form "Sita loco Piscia." Elevated to episcopal status by Pope Benedict XIII in 1726, it preserves its sober baroque architecture. Pescia was declared a noble city by Cosimo III de' Medici in 1699 and received on that occasion the Porta Fiorentina, a commemorative triumphal arch with the peculiarity of not being connected to any walls—built solely to celebrate the elevation in status.

The Palazzo del Vicario and Piazza Grande form the heart of Pescia's civic center, structured in the 13th century in "fifths"—four quarters on the right bank of the Pescia river and one on the left bank—with a fortified bridge ensuring the connection. This double town center (religious cathedral pole / civic palazzo pole) is an urban organization preserved intact since the Middle Ages.

San Francesco church preserves a collection of 14th-15th century paintings including the Triptych by Bonaventura Berlinghieri (1235), one of the earliest representations of Saint Francis of Assisi painted during his lifetime or shortly after his death, commissioned by the Franciscans of Pescia.

activities.text: Pescia's flower market: the city is Italy's capital of cut flowers, primarily carnations. The tradition dates back to the 1920s-1930s when, after the decline of silk manufactures and paper mills, farmers converted their land to horticulture. The first covered reinforced concrete market, built in 1950-1951 on plans that won awards in Brazil in 1953, is now reopened as a multipurpose cultural space. The region exports millions of flowers and indoor plants throughout Europe.

Stroll along the lungofiume of the Pescia: the river flows through the city between the two medieval poles, beneath ancient bridges. The landscaped banks offer a pleasant walk between gardens and palace facades.

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

places.name: Pescocostanzo

visits.text: Pescocostanzo - a village of 1,000 inhabitants at 1,400 meters in the Altipiani Maggiori d'Abruzzo, considered one of Abruzzo's best-preserved towns. The historic center dates mainly from the 17th century, rebuilt after the 1706 earthquake in a sober baroque style with local stone houses and wrought-iron balconies.

Collegiata di Santa Maria del Colle (La Collegiata) - an 11th-century basilica rebuilt between the 14th and 17th centuries. The interior features a 17th-century gilded coffered ceiling, carved wooden choir stalls, and wrought-iron grilles from local workshops considered masterpieces of Abruzzese ironwork.

Museo del Tombolo - the bobbin lace museum, an art practiced in Pescocostanzo since the 17th century. The village's lacemakers produced commissions for the royal courts of Naples and Madrid. A few artisans still perpetuate the tradition.

activities.text: Walk through the Bosco di Sant'Antonio - a monumental 1,200-hectare forest of black pines, beeches, and maples 6 km from the village, one of Italy's oldest managed forests, with some trees exceeding 500 years.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria del Colle Pescocostanzo

places.name: Petritoli

visits.text: Tre Archi — the village gateway: three neo-Gothic arches built in 1874, flanked by two 15th-century cylindrical towers. Local tradition holds that the name Petritoli comes from the merging of three ancient castles — Petrosa, Petrania and Petrollavia — represented by the three blue lozenges on its coat of arms.

Torre Civica and Piazza della Rocca — a tower with a square base, hexagonal body and cylindrical top, at the village's highest point. From here the view stretches from the Sibillini to the Adriatic. At the tower's foot, look for the porta del morto: a small opening in the wall reserved exclusively for the passage of the deceased, so their spirit would not linger in the house.
Convento delle Clarisse — built between the 16th and 17th centuries, now the town hall. The building retains several rooms with artworks and a council chamber of elegant, restrained beauty.

Chiesa di Santa Maria dei Martiri — built at the end of the 16th century with an adjoining Franciscan convent. Inside: a 17th-century wooden octagonal coffered ceiling painted in tempera on Franciscan themes, and a Callido organ from the 18th century — one of the finest instruments in the province.

Teatro dell'Iride — a small 19th-century Italian-style theatre with gilded wooden boxes and balconies, still in use today. Across the street, notice the Palazzo Vitali Rosati with its Venetian-inspired façade decoration, built in the late 19th century by the Vitali family.

activities.text: Excursion to the Santuario della Madonna della Liberata, a few kilometres from the village on a wooded hillside. Inside, a votive painting from 1529 by Giovanni Battista Morale gives thanks to the Virgin for protecting the inhabitants from French troops and plague. The holy water stoup is carved from a Roman funerary cippus.
Taste the olive oil of the Valdaso: Petritoli is surrounded by olive groves and mulberry trees, at the heart of a little-industrialised farming landscape.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria dei Martiri Petritoli

places.name: Pettorano Sul Gizio

visits.text: Pettorano sul Gizio - a medieval village of 900 inhabitants at 720 meters overlooking the confluence of the Gizio and Sagittario rivers. The 13th-century castle, successively owned by the Cantelmo and Caracciolo families, preserves its square keep and curtain walls. At its foot, the Riserva Naturale Monte Genzana Alto Gizio protects ancient beech forests.

activities.text: Stop at Piazza del Municipio to view the castle-village ensemble from the central fountain before continuing on.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Riserva Naturale Monte Genzana Alto Gizio

places.name: Piacenza

visits.text: Admire Piazza Cavalli, the beating heart of Piacenza dominated by two majestic Baroque equestrian statues of Alessandro and Ranuccio Farnese. Commissioned in 1600 and crafted by Mochi da Montevarchi over 13 years of work, they represent the third and fourth Duke of Parma and Piacenza in a breathtaking masterpiece of bronze casting.

Visit the Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta and Santa Giustina, one of the best-preserved examples of Romanesque architecture in Italy. Built between the 11th and 12th centuries, it conceals behind its sober façade a richly frescoed interior, crowned by the extraordinary dome painted by Guercino in the 17th century. Climb the 136 steps to reach the 27-meter-high dome.

Explore the Museums of Palazzo Farnese, a sumptuous period building housing seven different exhibition areas: the Picture Gallery, the Carriage Museum, the Civic Museum, the Rizzi Vaccari Collection, the Ceramics Museum, the Risorgimento Museum, and the Archaeological Museum. A journey through centuries of Piacenza's history and art.

Discover the Ricci Oddi Modern Art Gallery, a museum established in 1931 that houses the famous Portrait of a Lady by Gustav Klimt. The painting, stolen in 1997 and found over twenty years later in a hidden trapdoor within the museum itself, has been visible again since 2020 and is one of the most mysterious masterpieces in art history.

activities.text: Stroll beneath the arcades of the historic center to the Basilica of Santa Maria di Campagna, a jewel of Renaissance art with its frescoed chapel blending religious and pagan figures. Since 2024, visitors can climb to the dome to admire the details of the masterpieces from a unique perspective.

Visit the Municipal Theater, inaugurated in the early 19th century and considered among the most beautiful in Italy, as Stendhal himself proclaimed. The three-quarter ellipse auditorium, revolutionary for its time, offers extraordinary acoustics and interior decorations of meticulous elegance.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles of the Piacenza Hills: A 4-Day Medieval Road Trip Through Northern Italy
Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Maria di Campagna Piacenza

visits.text: Rocca Aldobrandesca: A quadrilateral fortress in lava stone first mentioned in 1194 in a diploma of Emperor Henry VI, it successively belonged to the Abbey of San Salvatore, the Aldobrandeschi family, the Orsini of Pitigliano, then the Republic of Siena which seized it between 1415 and 1430. Its two towers—the large one serving as keep, and the small one defending the gate—are characteristic of Aldobrandeschi fortresses on Amiata. Now a museum, it offers panoramic views of the Paglia valley.

Church of San Francesco and Palazzo Bourbon del Monte: The church, from a 15th-century convent, has a façade preceded by a simple loggia and preserves fragments of frescoes from the 14th-century Sienese school. The Palazzo Bourbon del Monte, at the village's edge, was the residence of feudal lords from 1601 to the late 18th century, when Grand Duke Leopold I made Piancastagnaio the capital of an autonomous community.

activities.text: Hiking in the chestnut woods of Monte Amiata: The forests surrounding Piancastagnaio are dominated by the Amiata chestnut, a protected species. Trails depart from the village for loops of 5 to 10 km up to the first slopes of the extinct volcano (1,738 m).

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

places.name: Piazza Armerina

visits.text: The Villa Romana del Casale, 5 km south of Piazza Armerina, shelters 3,500 square meters of in-situ 4th-century Roman mosaics—the world's largest surface. Its hunting scenes, athletes, and the famous "bikini girls"—ten women exercising—represent a unique depiction in ancient art. UNESCO-listed since 1997.

Piazza Armerina, founded by Normans in the 11th century, preserves a Baroque center with its blue-domed cathedral dominating the hill. The town counted 40 noble families in the 17th century for just 5,000 inhabitants—hence its nickname "city of one hundred thousand."

activities.text: Lunch in the upper town with pasta al ragù di maiale nero dei Nebrodi—black pig from the Nebrodi mountains, an Iberian breed raised semi-wild in Sicily's interior forests.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

map_keywords: Villa Romana del Casale Piazza Armerina
Mosaico delle Ragazze in Bikini Piazza Armerina
Duomo di Piazza Armerina Piazza Armerina

places.name: Piazza Di Spagna

visits.text: The Scalinata di Trinità dei Monti (1723-1726) ranks among Europe's grandest monumental staircases: 135 travertine steps divided into 11 ramps that serpentine and converge, designed by Francesco De Sanctis with funds bequeathed by French diplomat Étienne Gueffier. The original plan called for a statue of Louis XIV at the summit; Pope Innocent XIII refused, and a garden was planted instead. At the base, the Barcaccia fountain (1629) by Pietro Bernini—father of the more famous Gian Lorenzo—depicts a half-submerged boat commemorating the 1598 Tiber flood that allegedly beached a vessel at this very spot. Each April, the entire staircase vanishes beneath azaleas during the annual exhibition celebrating Rome's birthday.

The church of Trinità dei Monti, built between 1502 and 1585 at the request of Charles VIII of France, stands as one of the rare examples of late French Gothic style visible in Rome. Its Renaissance façade with twin bell towers—the work of Giacomo della Porta and Carlo Maderno—remains under French state care to this day. Inside, six chapels house remarkable artworks. In front of the entrance, the Sallustiano obelisk (13.91 m) is a Roman creation imitating Egyptian hieroglyphs, gifted to Pope Clement XII by the Ludovisi family in 1783.

activities.text: Climb the scalinata early in the morning, before the tourist rush, to watch Rome stretch out beneath Via dei Condotti: the street's vanishing perspective at dawn is one of the city's most photographed vistas.

Coffee at Caffè Greco (Via dei Condotti 86), open since 1760—Rome's oldest café and Italy's second oldest. Stendhal, Goethe, Byron, Liszt, and Keats all took their coffee here. The walls are covered with portraits and dedications from artists who frequented the place.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Scalinata di Trinità dei Monti Roma
Chiesa della Trinità dei Monti Roma

places.name: Piazza Navona

visits.text: Piazza Navona occupies the exact footprint of the Stadium of Domitian, built in 86 AD for Greek-style games that could accommodate 30,000 spectators. Its elongated shape (roughly 275 m by 55 m) is the direct imprint of that structure. At the center, the Fountain of the Four Rivers (1648-1651) is Gian Lorenzo Bernini's masterwork, commissioned by Pope Innocent X Pamphilj: four white marble giants represent the Nile (Africa, veiled because its source was unknown), the Ganges (Asia, holding an oar), the Danube (Europe, touching the pope's coat of arms), and the Rio de la Plata (Americas, holding a bag of gold). A 16-meter obelisk from the Circus of Maxentius crowns them. The legend of the Bernini-Borromini rivalry is apocryphal: the fountain was completed before Borromini began the façade of neighboring Sant'Agnese. The heavy statues rest on a hollow travertine base—visually impossible, a deliberate demonstration of Bernini's genius.

The church of Sant'Agnese in Agone (begun 1652, Borromini's façade) marks the spot where, according to tradition, the young Agnes was exposed naked before her martyrdom under Diocletian, miraculously protected by her hair which grew to cover her.

activities.text: Eat or drink in the alleys behind the square (Via del Governo Vecchio) rather than on the piazza's terraces: authentic Roman atmosphere, prices cut in half.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Chiesa di Sant'Agnese in Agone Roma

visits.text: The Vittoriano—or Altare della Patria—is the neoclassical monument erected between 1885 and 1935 in homage to Victor Emmanuel II, first king of unified Italy. Designed by Giuseppe Sacconi after his victory in the 1882 competition, it's built from white Botticino marble (Brescia)—a choice that outraged Romans accustomed to local travertine. The structure measures 135 m wide and 70 m high. Romans nicknamed it the 'typewriter' or 'wedding cake.' Its construction required demolishing a medieval quarter and revealed, during excavations, a section of the Servian Wall (6th century BC)—both walls are still visible in the foundations. The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, installed in 1921, is permanently guarded. At the inauguration, a banquet was held inside the bronze equestrian statue of Victor Emmanuel—guests entered through the horse's mouth.

activities.text: Ascend to the panoramic terrace (paid elevator access or free stairs to the intermediate level): stunning views of the Imperial Forums, the Colosseum, and Rome's hills.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

places.name: Piazzola Sul Brenta

visits.text: Marvel at Villa Contarini, a palace so vast it resembles a royal residence of a small country. This monumental Baroque villa features stunning architecture, extensive grounds, and regularly hosts one of Italy's largest antique and vintage markets.
Explore the villa's grand facades, ornate interiors, and the surrounding park. The scale and ambition of this aristocratic estate rivals any palace in Europe.

activities.text: Time your visit for one of the monthly antique markets held in front of the villa, combining architectural appreciation with treasure hunting among vintage and antique wares.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Parco Villa Contarini Piazzola sul Brenta

visits.text: Picinisco, certified as one of I Borghi più Belli d'Italia, occupies a ridge at 850 meters altitude in the Comino Valley, at the heart of the Abruzzo, Lazio and Molise National Park. Its name first appears in 1017 in a donation to the Abbot of Montecassino. The castle has four towers, the oldest—la Torretta—dating to the 11th century; a larger tower was built in the 15th century, incorporating it. The village is crossed by five historic gates: Porta Saracina, Porta Codarda, Porta Rione, Porta della Prece, and Porta della Piazza.

The Church of San Lorenzo was built in 1305 and expanded several times. Its bell tower, dated to the 11th century, originally served as a military watchtower before being integrated into the religious building.

The writer D.H. Lawrence stayed in Picinisco in 1919 and completed his novel The Lost Girl there. The house where he lodged is now an agriturismo. Additionally, some 60% of Italians in Scotland originate from Picinisco: a massive exodus at the end of the 19th century and after World War II populated Scottish cities with families from this single village.

activities.text: Hike in the Abruzzo, Lazio and Molise National Park starting from Picinisco: marked trails climb toward the high-altitude pastures of Monte Meta (2241 m), where wolves and Apennine chamois are regularly observed. The park is one of Italy's oldest (founded in 1923) and one of the last refuges of the Apennine brown bear.

Taste Pecorino di Picinisco, a sheep's milk cheese recognized by local producers as a high-altitude specialty: the sheep graze on aromatic Apennine herbs between May and October, giving the cheese a slightly herbaceous flavor and a natural ocher-colored rind.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Piazza Pio II and Renaissance ensemble: In 1459, Pope Pius II—born Enea Silvio Piccolomini in the village of Corsignano—commissioned Bernardo Rossellino, a student of Leon Battista Alberti, to transform his birthplace into a model city. In just three years (1459-1462), Rossellino erected the cathedral, Palazzo Piccolomini, Palazzo Borgia, and the trapezoidal Piazza—the first concrete realization of Renaissance humanist urban ideals. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1996.

Cathedral of the Assumption: Built between 1459 and 1462 by Rossellino, its exterior façade is purely Renaissance in travertine, while the three-nave interior draws on the late Gothic of South German churches—a compromise wanted by Pius II, influenced by German cardinal-philosopher Nicholas of Cusa. It houses the Assumption by Il Vecchietta, the Madonna in Majesty by Matteo di Giovanni, and Flemish tapestries in the basement.

Palazzo Piccolomini: Pope Pius II's summer residence built in 1459 by Rossellino on the model of Florence's Palazzo Rucellai. The triple-level loggia on the south side opens onto a hanging garden with panoramic views of Val d'Orcia, Monte Amiata, and Enea Silvio's birthplace, Corsignano. The interior preserves period furniture, a library, and a weapons collection.

activities.text: Fiera del Cacio: On the first Sunday in September, Pienza's six districts compete in the game of cacio al fuso—rolling a wheel of Pecorino di Pienza as far as possible—along with tastings and markets.
Pecorino di Pienza tasting: Pienza's sheep cheese comes in fresh, semi-stagionato, and stagionato varieties. The shops on Corso Rossellino offer tastings with Val d'Orcia DOC wines.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

places.name: Pietra Di Bismantova

visits.text: Experience the Pietra di Bismantova, a dramatic isolated rock formation rising from the Apennine foothills that inspired Dante's description of Purgatory in the Divine Comedy. This massive sandstone plateau, reaching 1,047 meters in altitude with sheer cliffs on all sides, creates one of the most distinctive natural landmarks in the entire region.

Explore the trails leading around and atop this remarkable geological formation, where centuries of erosion have sculpted the distinctive flat-topped profile. The hermitage of Santa Maria carved into the rock face adds spiritual significance to this already awe-inspiring natural monument.

activities.text: Hike to the summit plateau for panoramic views extending across the Apennines and toward the distant Po Valley. The Pietra di Bismantova offers climbing routes on its impressive cliff faces and easier walking paths that circle the base, making it accessible to visitors of all abilities.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

map_keywords: Pietra di Bismantova Castelnovo ne' Monti
Eremo di Bismantova Castelnovo ne' Monti
Santuario della Beata Vergine Castelnovo ne' Monti
Sentiero della Pietra Castelnovo ne' Monti

visits.text: Pietrabbondante archaeological area: federal sanctuary of the Pentri Samnites, 2nd-1st century BC. Two superimposed terraced temples and an Italic theater overlooking the Trigno valley. The tiers incorporate honorary seats with armrests sculpted as griffins and lion heads—among the finest examples of Samnite sculpture.

Italic theater: one of the best-preserved from pre-Roman Italy. Stage, vomitories and tiers in local stone still in place. The frontal view of the Apennines from the seats creates an exceptional panorama.

activities.text: Wander freely across the site's terraces among grasses and oaks. Lightly visited, allowing unhurried exploration.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

visits.text: Pietracamela - a village of 300 inhabitants at 1,005 meters altitude, perched on a rocky spur at the foot of Corno Piccolo (2,655 m) in the Gran Sasso. The medieval center preserves characteristic dry-stone houses built side by side and vaulted alleyways. Starting point for climbers ascending Corno Grande (2,912 m), the roof of the Apennines.

activities.text: Stop at Prati di Tivo (1,450 m), a ski station and alpine meadow 4 km from the village, with sweeping views of the Gran Sasso walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Piazza del Duomo and Duomo San Martino: Pietrasanta's rectangular square is the artistic heart of the town. San Martino Cathedral, whose construction dates to the 14th century, presents a façade clad in local marble and an interior enriched with 19th-century frescoes and artworks from preceding centuries. The brick bell tower, commissioned around 1520 from Donato Benti—a collaborator of Michelangelo—conceals an extraordinary self-supporting helical staircase in brick, long overlooked. It was during the period when Michelangelo stayed in Pietrasanta to extract marble destined for the façade of San Lorenzo in Florence that this staircase was designed, according to most historians.

Museo dei Bozzetti (Museum of Sketches) - Chiostro di Sant'Agostino: Created in 1984 in the former convent of Sant'Agostino (14th century), this museum unique in the world documents the creative process of more than 350 Italian and international sculptors who have worked in Pietrasanta. It preserves over 700 maquettes and plaster models that preceded marble or bronze works now dispersed in museums and public spaces worldwide. Botero, Mitoraj, Folon, Cascella, Pomodoro, Miró, and Henry Moore are represented.

Marble and bronze workshops - Parco Internazionale della Scultura Contemporanea: Pietrasanta houses some fifty marble sculpture workshops and artistic bronze foundries. The town, nicknamed "Little Athens," has welcomed since the 15th century—when Michelangelo stayed here to work marble from nearby quarries—sculptors from around the world. Henry Moore was among the first modern foreigners to discover Pietrasanta in the 1950s; he was followed by Noguchi, Miró, Mitoraj, and Botero. The International Park of Contemporary Sculpture presents over 70 monumental works donated to the town, visible in the streets and squares.

Museo Archeologico Bruno Antonucci (Palazzo Moroni, 16th century): Located on Piazza del Duomo, this museum presents collections ranging from prehistory to the medieval era, with Etruscan and Roman pieces discovered in the Versilia territory.

activities.text: Stroll among art galleries and sculptors' workshops: Pietrasanta counts more than one gallery per 1,200 inhabitants—a record density even in Tuscany. Several workshops open their doors to the public, allowing observation of artisans working marble or bronze.

Thursday weekly market and antiques market (1st Sunday of month): The Thursday market covers Piazza del Duomo and adjacent streets with food, crafts, and local products. The first Sunday of the month, antique dealers set up in the same squares.

days_plan.itin_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast

visits.text: Piglio is the heartland of Cesanese del Piglio, Lazio's first and only red wine DOCG (awarded in 2008, after DOC status dating to 1973). The indigenous Cesanese d'Affile grape has been cultivated on the slopes of the Ernici Mountains since antiquity: Emperor Nerva built a palace at Piglio to secure his wine supply, and Popes Innocent III and Boniface VIII were documented enthusiasts. The vineyards cover approximately 15,300 hectares across Piglio, Serrone, Acuto, Anagni, and Paliano.

Serrone, perched on the slopes of Monte Scalambra just a few kilometers from Piglio, offers a sweeping panorama from its belvedere over the Sacco Valley and the ridges of the Ernici. The village preserves its tight medieval layout with lanes of local limestone and several 17th-century palaces.

activities.text: Visit a DOCG-zone winery to discover the three expressions of Cesanese del Piglio: the base version (minimum 12°, red and black fruit aromas, silky tannins), the Superiore (minimum 13°), and the Riserva (minimum 14°, aged at least 20 months). This ruby-hued grape with violet reflections carries a slightly bitter finish—a distinctive character that local winemakers proudly claim as their signature.

Hike through the vineyards of the Ernici Mountains on marked trails connecting Piglio to Serrone via terraced vineyards: in autumn, harvest activity is still visible in some parcels, and cellars welcome drop-in visitors without appointments.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Piazza del Duomo—nicknamed Piazza dei Miracoli, a name invented by writer Gabriele D'Annunzio in his 1910 novel—gathers four Romanesque buildings constructed between the 11th and 14th centuries on a single lawn carpet, inscribed together on the UNESCO World Heritage list since 1987. The cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta, begun in 1064 by architect Buscheto after the Pisan fleet's victory over the Saracens in Sicily, is the founding monument of the Pisan Romanesque style characterized by blind arcades and pale marble bands.

The Baptistery, begun in 1153 by architect Diotisalvi, is Italy's largest: 54.86 meters in diameter and 55 meters high. Its exceptional acoustics—a guide can produce a three-note chord simultaneously by singing in echo—has attracted visitors since the Middle Ages. Inside, the pulpit sculpted by Nicola Pisano in 1260 marks a decisive break with Byzantine art by returning to direct study of ancient Roman sarcophagi, opening the path to Renaissance sculpture.

The Leaning Tower, begun in 1173 and completed in 1372 after two long interruptions due to unstable alluvial terrain, measures 55.86 meters on the south side and 58.36 meters on the north—the visible asymmetry reflects successive corrections attempted during construction to compensate for subsidence. It weighs approximately 14,500 tons and had 294 steps until its closure in 1990. Consolidation work conducted through 2001, consisting of removing earth from beneath the higher side, reduced the tilt from 5.5° to 3.97°, stabilizing it for at least two hundred years. Galileo supposedly conducted his falling-bodies experiments from the summit, according to unverified tradition.

The Camposanto—literally 'holy cemetery'—is a monumental rectangular cloister begun in 1278. According to legend, its soil was brought back from the Holy Land by Archbishop Ubaldo Lanfranchi at the end of the Crusades so the dead could rest in sacred earth. Its galleries sheltered 14th-century frescoes including the famous Triumph of Death attributed to Buonamico Buffalmacco, severely damaged by fire during a 1944 bombing—the explosion melted the roof lead onto the paintings—then partially restored.

activities.text: Pisa retains a little-visited historic center beyond the Piazza dei Miracoli: the Arno embankments, the lanes of the Borgo Stretto quarter, and the markets around Piazza delle Vettovaglie offer a glimpse of daily life in a Tuscan university town.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome

places.name: Pistoia

visits.text: Piazza del Duomo brings together the four pillars of medieval power in a single theatrical space: San Zeno Cathedral, the Baptistery, Palazzo del Podestà, and Palazzo dei Vescovi. The campanile tells the city's history in stone—originally a Lombard defensive tower, then heightened in the 12th and 13th centuries in Pisan Romanesque style. Each floor marks a different era, from the austere lower masonry to the elegant Pisan arcading at the summit.

San Zeno Cathedral shelters one of medieval Europe's most extraordinary goldsmithing treasures: the Silver Altar of Saint James, begun in 1287 and enriched over two centuries by Tuscany's finest goldsmiths, including a young Filippo Brunelleschi who worked on it around 1399-1400. The crypt holds the relics of Saint Zeno, while Coppo di Marcovaldo's monumental Crucifix from the 1270s dominates the central nave with its hieratic figures against a shimmering gold background.

The Ospedale del Ceppo, founded according to tradition in 1277—its name comes from a dry tree stump that miraculously flowered to mark the site—operated as a hospital for over seven centuries until 2013. Its early 16th-century loggia bears one of the Renaissance's finest glazed terracotta friezes: commissioned from 1511 by the monk Leonardo Buonafede, it united the rival workshops of the Della Robbia and Buglioni families. Giovanni della Robbia created the 1525 roundels depicting scenes from Mary's life; the narrative frieze illustrates the Seven Works of Mercy in 16th-century settings and costumes, a vivid documentary of daily life in Renaissance Pistoia.

The Hospital Museum del Ceppo, installed in the former wards since 2017, displays several hundred surgical instruments from the 17th to early 20th centuries, exhibited in bed-shaped vitrines along the old men's ward. The visit culminates in the neoclassical oval Anatomical Theater, built 1785-1787, where medical students learned anatomy. A wooden and leather birthing machine, loaned to London's Science Museum in 1913 and returned to Pistoia a century later, is now on display.

San Giovanni Fuorcivitas—"outside the walls," as it was originally built beyond the first Lombard ramparts—presents one of the most spectacular side facades in Pisan Romanesque architecture: a densely packed alternation of white marble and green Prato serpentine bands, constructed from the 12th to 14th centuries. The north portal's architrave, carved in 1166 by Gruamonte with the Last Supper, is one of the earliest examples of Pistoiese Romanesque sculpture. Inside, three masterpieces await: Fra Guglielmo da Pisa's pulpit (1270), disciple of Nicola Pisano; the central holy water font attributed to Nicola Pisano and his workshop; and Luca della Robbia's Visitation in glazed terracotta (1445), the first surviving freestanding sculpture created by the Della Robbia workshop using the invetriatura technique.

The Basilica della Madonna dell'Umiltà traces its origin to a miraculous image of the Virgin dated 1490. The foundation stone was laid in 1495 following plans by Giuliano da Sangallo. Cosimo I later commissioned Giorgio Vasari to roof the building: the dome he constructed from 1561, visible across the entire city, deliberately references Brunelleschi's in Florence and ranks among Tuscany's largest domes.

The Antico Palazzo dei Vescovi, facing the cathedral across Piazza del Duomo, houses the Cathedral Museum with its collections of sacred goldwork and medieval art. Excavations in the basements have uncovered superimposed Roman and Lombard remains, visible in the underground archaeological route.

San Bartolomeo in Pantano—"in the swamp," as it was built on marshy ground outside the first ramparts in the 8th century—preserves the third great Pistoiese pulpit: created in 1250 by Guido da Como, it's the oldest of the three major medieval pulpits surviving in the city, with reliefs of the Annunciation, Nativity and Adoration of the Magi set against polychrome marble panels.

Marliana, a farming village at 500 meters altitude on the north slope of Montalbano, is surrounded by olive groves and vineyards producing the oils and wines of the Pistoiese hills. Its medieval Rocca commands views across the Pistoia plain to the Apennines. The village long served as one of the wine-producing outposts supplying Pistoia's merchants.

activities.text: Pistoia Sotterranea: beneath the Ospedale del Ceppo's forecourt runs a network of brick-vaulted tunnels following the ancient course of the Brana torrent, channeled and covered as the city expanded. The public route covers about 650 meters of the total 1,200-meter network, with panels showing the successive phases of urban expansion and the remnants of walls, towers and bridges integrated into the underground.

The Pieve di Sant'Andrea houses the absolute masterpiece of Pistoiese pulpits: Giovanni Pisano's pulpit, completed in 1301. Its reliefs of shattering expressiveness—the Massacre of the Innocents, the Nativity, the Crucifixion—achieve a sculptural depth and dramatic tension unmatched in early 14th-century Tuscany. Pistoia is the only city in the world to possess so many major medieval pulpits within such a compact area.

The Civic Museum of Ancient Art (Palazzo del Podestà) preserves works from the 13th to 16th centuries, including the famous altarpiece by Giuliano da Rimini and panels by Pistoiese masters. The 13th-century palace facade, with its rounded arches and massive tower, stands as one of Tuscany's best-preserved examples of communal palace architecture.

Wander through the daily market in Piazza della Sala, where stalls cluster around a medieval well surrounded by arcades. Here Pistoia shows its living face, amid displays of citrus fruits, cheeses and cured meats from the Pistoiese Apennines.

Pistoia Zoo, founded in 1970, occupies 7 hectares in the northern part of the city and is home to over 500 animals of 100 species. It's known for its endangered species conservation programs and naturalistic habitats, ranking among central Italy's most active zoological institutions.

Walk through the nursery district around Pistoia: the plain between Pistoia and Pescia forms Italy's main ornamental plant nursery zone, exporting millions of plants annually throughout Europe. Marked paths wind through greenhouses and ornamental plant fields from October to April.

The Embroidery Museum (Palazzo Rospigliosi) traces the history of Pistoiese embroidery, a craft of excellence that dressed European courts from the 17th century. The Rospigliosi family, one of whose members became Pope Clement IX in 1667, were among the great patrons of this art.

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas
Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Giovanni Fuorcivitas Pistoia
Chiesa di San Bartolomeo in Pantano Pistoia

places.name: Pitigliano

visits.text: Pitigliano bursts into view around the road's bend like a vision: houses grafted onto the edge of a volcanic tufa spur, the whole carried by the Medici aqueduct that springs toward the town. This aqueduct, commissioned by the Orsini and completed by the Medici between 1636 and 1639, forms 15 arches—two monumental and thirteen smaller—and constitutes the site's postcard image. It terminates at Piazza della Repubblica with the Fontana delle Sette Cannelle (16th century), whose seven spouts shaped as animal heads represented the seven historic quarters.

The Palazzo Orsini, a former Aldobrandeschi fortress transformed into a noble residence by the Orsini family between the 14th and 16th centuries, houses two museums: the Civic Archaeological Museum (with finds from the Poggio Buco necropolis) and the Palazzo Orsini Museum (21 rooms of frescoes, 15th-century paintings, goldsmith work, antique furniture, textiles donated by local parishes). The Orsini coat of arms above the portal depicts a bear—but the Maremman sculptor who executed it had never seen one; the animal rather resembles a dog.

The Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul, whose origins trace back to the 13th century, presents an 18th-century Baroque facade and a 16th-century Renaissance portal. The interior blends several styles accumulated through restorations. The church of San Rocco, the town's oldest construction, and the sanctuary of Madonna delle Grazie outside the walls complete the village's religious circuit.

The Jewish ghetto and Little Jerusalem: first protected by the Orsini who refused to close its gates despite papal directives, Pitigliano's Jewish community settled in the 15th century, fleeing persecution from the Papal States. It prospered until Italian unification. The synagogue, built in 1598, was restored in 1995 and now serves as a museum of local Jewish culture. Underground, you can visit the unleavened bread oven (forno delle azzime), the kosher wine cellar, the butcher shop, and the women's ritual bath (mikvé), all carved into the tufa. The sfratto—a dry honey-and-walnut biscuit—is the local specialty of Jewish origin: in the 17th century, Medici soldiers would knock (sfrattare = to evict) on ghetto doors at sunset to force Jews to return home. The pastry takes the shape of a soldier's staff.

activities.text: Walk through the Vie Cave around Pitigliano: a network of paths carved by hand into tufa rock by the Etruscans, with walls sometimes reaching 20 meters high over several kilometers. The Via Cava di San Giuseppe and Via Cava di Fratenuti, west of the village on either side of the river, are the most accessible. These passages served as communication routes between settlements and necropolises, probably also as a defensive system against cavalry attacks.

Tasting of Bianco di Pitigliano DOC, a Trebbiano Toscano-based white wine produced in natural caves carved into the tufa beneath the village. The underground cellars ensure stable temperature and humidity—ideal conditions for vinification.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

map_keywords: Cattedrale dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Pitigliano

visits.text: Castello Aragonese di Pizzo—an Aragonese fortress built in 1486 by Ferdinand I on a rocky promontory commanding the Gulf of Sant'Eufemia. In this castle's courtyard, Joachim Murat, King of Naples and Napoleon's brother-in-law, was arrested on October 8, 1815, after attempting to reclaim his throne with 250 men landed from Corsica. Five days later, on October 13, he faced a firing squad at age 48. His detention cell remains preserved with personal artifacts.

Chiesa di Piedigrotta—a cave church carved into the volcanic tuff cliffs at sea level, founded in the 17th century according to legend by Neapolitan sailors shipwrecked here, who sculpted a Virgin into the rock in gratitude. Inside, dozens of tuff sculptures depict religious scenes and contemporary figures, including a 17th-century Pietà and 20th-century additions that blur sacred and secular.

Centro storico di Pizzo—a medieval town perched on 35-meter cliffs, with Piazza della Repubblica surrounded by 18th-century palazzi. The town's fame rests on centuries of bluefin tuna fishing in the Gulf of Sant'Eufemia, with traditional mattanze (collective tuna hunts) documented since the 16th century.

activities.text: Tasting the tartufo di Pizzo—an ice cream creation born in Pizzo in the 1950s: a chocolate-hazelnut gelato sphere with a molten chocolate core, dusted in bitter cocoa. The gelaterias around Piazza della Repubblica still guard the original recipe.
Sunset walk along Pizzo's lungomare with views across the Gulf of Sant'Eufemia to the Aeolian Islands hovering on the horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

visits.text: Pizzo Calabro is perched on a promontory above the Gulf of Sant'Eufemia, on the Tyrrhenian Sea. Begin with the Castello Murat, an Aragonese fortress built in 1492 by Ferdinand I of Aragon. It was here that Joachim Murat - Napoleon's brother-in-law and King of Naples - met a dramatic end. Landing at Pizzo on October 8, 1815 with 250 men to attempt to reconquer his throne, he was arrested, tried by a military commission, and shot on October 13 in the castle courtyard, just five days after his arrival. The interior museum reconstructs his final moments - he asked to write a last letter to his wife in Trieste, with locks of hair in the envelope.

One kilometer from the center, descend to the Chiesa di Piedigrotta, carved into the tufa just meters from the sea. According to tradition, 17th-century shipwrecked sailors sculpted this chapel to thank the Virgin for their rescue. The interior is populated with statues and bas-reliefs carved directly into the rock - a place between folk art, faith, and geology that resembles nothing else in Italy.
Climb back to the Duomo di San Giorgio Martire, which houses Joachim Murat's tomb, then stroll through the colorful lanes of the historic center, among arches, flowered balconies, and sea views.

activities.text: Settle on Piazza della Repubblica, Pizzo's beating heart, to taste the famous tartufo di Pizzo. This frozen dessert, born in the 1950s from an improvisation by pastry chef Giuseppe De Maria called "Don Pippo" (he had run out of molds and shaped the ice cream by hand), became the first ice cream in Europe to obtain IGP certification. It's a hazelnut and chocolate ice cream hemisphere with a molten chocolate heart, rolled in bitter cocoa. Each artisanal gelateria has its recipe - locals don't agree on the best, but the debate is part of the charm.

End the day at Pizzo beach or the Passerella, a pier offering superb views of the cliffs and castle at sunset. Pizzo is also a tuna fishing port - in the evening, trattorias serve pesce spada (swordfish) and alici (anchovies) freshly caught.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea

places.name: Poggio A Caiano

visits.text: The Villa Medici at Poggio a Caiano, designed by Giuliano da Sangallo for Lorenzo the Magnificent beginning in 1485 and completed under Pope Leo X in 1519, is the prototype of the Renaissance villa: raised on an arcaded podium with an Ionic loggia on the facade, it established the relationship between building and landscape that would influence all European villas for two centuries, including Palladio's. A UNESCO site since 2013, it was the scene of three historic Medici marriages: Alessandro de' Medici and Margaret of Austria (1536), Cosimo I and Eleanor of Toledo (1539), Francesco I and Bianca Cappello (1579).

The Salone di Leone X, on the first floor, houses one of the most important fresco cycles of Florentine Mannerism. The painters were summoned under Leo X (son of Lorenzo the Magnificent) around 1520 according to a program by Paolo Giovio: Pontormo painted Vertumnus and Pomona (an allegory of the seasons and agriculture), Franciabigio the Triumph of Cicero, Andrea del Sarto the Tribute to Caesar (alluding to gifts received by Lorenzo from the Sultan of Egypt in 1487). Leo X died in 1521, the works stopped. They were only completed in 1578-1582 by Alessandro Allori under Francesco I. Since 2007, the second floor houses the Museum of Still Lifes: 200 paintings from the late 16th to mid-18th century from the Medici collections.

activities.text: Walk in the villa park and visit the former stables renovated in 2000. The garden preserves rare plant species and terracotta statues. The complex includes the Cascine di Tavola—Medici agricultural estate—which defined the model of the 'villa-azienda' combining prestigious residence with productive rural exploitation.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Salone di Leone X Poggio a Caiano
Giardino della Villa Medicea Poggio a Caiano
Museo della Natura Morta Poggio a Caiano

visits.text: The Istituto Lama Tzong Khapa, founded in 1977 by Lamas Thubten Yeshe and Thubten Zopa Rinpoche, is Europe's largest center for Tibetan Buddhist studies. It bears the name of Tsongkhapa, founder of the Gelugpa monastic order. The Dalai Lama has taught here on several occasions. The library, meditation halls, gompa (prayer room), and retreat cottages spread across the Pisan hills 40 km south of Pisa. Courses in Buddhist philosophy, psychology, and meditation are offered year-round.

The statue of Cenresig (Avalokiteshvara, the Buddha of Compassion), inaugurated by the Dalai Lama in June 2014, is the tallest in Europe. Its story is singular: created by film set designer Dante Ferretti for Martin Scorsese's Kundun (1997), it risked destruction after filming in Morocco. Saved and transported to Italy, it was restored by sculptor Alessio Pazzini according to strict Buddhist iconographic canons.
