activities.text: Participate in a guided meditation session in the institute's gompa, open to visitors on certain days according to the Tibetan calendar. Prayer flags, white stupas, and prayer wheels surrounding the main building create an unexpected atmosphere in the heart of Tuscany.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

visits.text: The Roman bridge at Pont-Saint-Martin, built around the 1st century BC under Augustus to span the Lys torrent, stands as one of Italy's best-preserved Roman arches. Anchored directly into bedrock on both banks, it soars 82 feet above the water with a single span stretching 115 feet. At its base, you can still see slots carved into the rock where wooden support beams were fitted during construction. The bridge formed part of the Route des Gaules linking Eporedia (Ivrea) to Augusta Praetoria (Aosta), then onward to the Great and Little Saint Bernard passes. Though replaced for traffic in 1836, it survived even the Allied bombing of May 25, 1944, which devastated much of the village. Legend, celebrated annually during the historic carnival, tells how Saint Martin negotiated the bridge's construction with the devil: he threw bread across the gorge, and a starving dog raced over first, claiming the promised soul. Outwitted, the devil vanished into the Lys waters.

Fort Bard rises from a rock outcrop commanding the valley's narrowest point. The first defensive works date to the 10th century (first documented in 1034, belonging to Viscount Boso of Aosta). In May 1800, the fort—defended by just 400 soldiers under Captain Stockard von Bernkopf—held up Napoleon's reserve army for two weeks, forcing 40,000 men and 50 cannons to bypass the fortress under cover of night, their cannon wheels wrapped in hay to muffle the sound. Napoleon ordered the fort razed after his victory. King Charles Albert of Sardinia had it rebuilt between 1830 and 1838, designed by engineer Francesco Antonio Olivero. Decommissioned in 1975, purchased by the Region in 1990, and renovated over 10 years at a cost of 45 million euros, it reopened in 2006 as a cultural hub. The Museum of the Alps (in the Carlo Alberto building) offers an interactive journey through Alpine geography, cultures, and glaciers from Slovenia to France. The medieval village of Bard at its foot is accessible by panoramic elevator.

Two miles from the fort at Donnas, the Route des Gaules is carved directly through living rock for 725 feet, topped by an arched passage 13 feet thick, 13 feet high, and nearly 10 feet wide. It's one of the most spectacular testimonies to Roman road engineering in the Alps, still visible in its original state.

activities.text: Hike in Mont Avic Natural Park, established in 1989 and accessible from Pont-Saint-Martin: over 30 lakes and peat bogs, vast Swiss pine forests, wild landscapes untouched by tourist infrastructure. One of the Aosta Valley's most pristine natural spaces.

Historic Carnival of Pont-Saint-Martin (February/March): over 1,000 costumed performers recreate the town's Roman history. On Shrove Tuesday night, an effigy of the devil is burned beneath the Roman bridge arch in a spectacular pyrotechnic display.

days_plan.itin_title: Aosta Valley Circuit: From Roman Gateway to Mont Blanc

visits.text: Pontremoli is Tuscany's northernmost town, nestled at the foot of the Apennines on the border with Liguria and Emilia-Romagna. This ancient stop on the Via Francigena - the medieval pilgrimage route between Canterbury and Rome - retains its historic centre crossed by the Magra river and its tributaries, spanned by medieval stone bridges. Start by climbing to the Castello del Piagnaro, whose name comes from the 'piagne', sandstone slabs used for roofing in Lunigiana. Built around the year 1000 to control the mountain passes, this castle dominates the village and houses the Museum of Lunigiana Statue-Menhirs, founded in 1975 by Professor Augusto Cesare Ambrosi.

The museum displays over 80 statue-menhirs, human figures carved from sandstone between the 4th and 1st millennium BC - one of Europe's most important collections of megalithic art. These abstract silhouettes, with their 'carabiniere hat' shaped heads and U-shaped faces, represent men armed with daggers and women adorned with jewellery. Their exact meaning remains partly enigmatic: protective deities? Ancestors? Territorial markers? The museum staging, renovated in 2015, plunges the statues into semi-darkness with lighting that reveals every detail - allow a good hour to explore thoroughly.

Descend into Pontremoli's centre to visit the Duomo, designed in the 17th century by Alessandro Capra from Cremona, with its broad dome and Latin cross plan. On the edge of town, stop at the church of the Santissima Annunziata, which preserves a small polychrome marble temple attributed to Jacopo Sansovino. Then wander through the medieval alleys, beneath the arcades, among the stone bridges and towers - Pontremoli has retained its authentic atmosphere as a pilgrims' staging post on the road to Rome.

activities.text: Spend your evening in Pontremoli savouring testaroli, Lunigiana's absolute speciality. These are thick pancakes made from flour and water, cooked between two scorching cast-iron plates (the 'testi'), then cut and reheated in boiling water like pasta, served with pesto, olive oil or pecorino cheese. It's one of Italy's most ancient dishes - some consider it the ancestor of all pasta.

If you arrive early in the day and weather permits, push on to the Giaredo gorges just outside Pontremoli - a succession of canyons where the Gordana torrent has carved the rock for dozens of metres. The path follows the cliff walls and offers total escapism. Allow 1.5 hours return for the section accessible without equipment.

days_plan.itin_title: From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria

visits.text: Crossing and Porto di Ponza — Ferry from Formia (2h30 on traghetto, 1h15 on aliscafo) to the largest of the Pontine Islands (2.9 sq mi, 928 feet at Monte La Guardia). The porto borbonico (1772-1793), built on a Roman port by Ferdinand IV's engineers (Antonio Winspeare and Francesco Carpi), is an amphitheater of colorful houses—one of the Mediterranean's most beautiful boat arrivals. The island, inhabited since the Neolithic, was colonized by the Phoenicians, then the Greeks (8th century BC), then the Romans (312 BC) who made it a resort and exile destination. Benedictine monks founded the abbey of Santa Maria, destroyed by Saracens. After centuries of pirate raids (the Turks blew up the port tower in 1655), the Bourbons repopulated the island in 1771 with settlers from Ischia (20 boats) and Torre del Greco (50 boats for coral fishing). Ischian surnames (Scotto, Migliaccio, Mazzella) and the Neapolitan dialect survive.

Grotte di Pilato and island tour — Hypogean and underwater archaeological complex from the 1st century AD: 5 caves carved into the tuff, connected by underwater tunnels and an external basin. Called 'Antico Murenaio Romano': the Romans bred moray eels here, a sacred fish. The main basin had 5 openings closed by sluice gates (saracinesche) to control water and transfer fish. In 1997, at the bottom of the main cave, a white marble statue (Apollo or Dionysus), a tuff altar and oil lamps were discovered—suggesting cultic use. The caves were connected by a stairway to Emperor Augustus's villa on the Collina della Madonna. The name 'Pilato' comes from a legend: young Pontius Pilate, a Roman noble, was sent to Ponza to quell a revolt and gained his surname before being sent to Palestine. The Cisterna della Dragonara (town center) is one of three large Roman cisterns. Continuing the tour by gozzo: Chiaia di Luna (white tuff cliff in a half-moon, 330 feet high), Piscine Naturali (volcanic rock pools), Cala Feola (only sandy beach), Faraglioni della Madonna.

activities.text: Climb the colorful lanes of the centro: church of SS. Silverio e Domitilla, Giardino Botanico. Rent a gozzo (~€80-150/day) or take an organized excursion for the island tour.

Gozzo tour with swimming stop at Grotte di Pilato (mask and snorkel to explore underwater tunnels). Chiaia di Luna for the monumental cliff. Swimming at Piscine Naturali. Fish dinner at sunset on the port.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters
Ulysses Riviera: Secret Gardens, Ancient Caves & Southern Lazio Islands

visits.text: Castle of the Conti Guidi of Poppi: first mentioned in writing in 1169, when it belonged to the abbey of San Fidele di Strumi. By the late 12th century, it had passed to the Conti Guidi, a feudal family whose territories stretched from the banks of the Po to the Apennines. The tower and keep were erected in the second half of the 13th century — the architect Arnolfo di Cambio is said to have used it as a prototype for the Palazzo Vecchio in Florence, which he designed shortly after. The Guidi Chapel is covered in frescoes by Taddeo Gaddi, a pupil of Giotto. Dante took refuge in the castle of Poppi during his exile, in 1307 and again in 1311; tradition holds that he composed Canto XXXIII of the Inferno here, dedicated to Count Ugolino della Gherardesca. A bust of the poet on the square before the castle recalls this connection. In 1440, the Guidi sided with the Milanese at Anghiari and were exiled, ending 350 years of dominion over the Casentino.

Pieve di San Pietro a Romena: one of the finest Romanesque parish churches in the Casentino, 5 km from Poppi in the commune of Pratovecchio. Tradition holds it was built at the request of Countess Matilda of Canossa. Dante mentions it in the Divine Comedy (Canto XXX of the Inferno), in connection with the counterfeiting of gold florins carried out at the nearby castle of Romena by Master Adam of Brescia on behalf of the Guidi di Romena — around 1280 at least. The sandstone building, with its three apses and plain facade, is a representative example of Tuscan Romanesque architecture.

Castello di Romena: a Conti Guidi di Romena fortress founded by the Marquises of Spoleto probably in the 11th century, atop a 626-meter hill. The castle reached its peak in the 13th century, in Dante's time. It retains three large fortified towers and the remains of three concentric walls. In 1902, the poet Gabriele d'Annunzio stayed there as a guest of the Goretti counts and wrote part of Alcyone.

Pratovecchio: birthplace of Paolo di Dono, known as Paolo Uccello (c. 1397), master of Renaissance perspective. The medieval village houses two convents of Camaldolese and Dominican nuns, and several remarkable artworks in its churches. The castle of Porciano, a 13th-century Guidi fortress, commands the valley from a neighboring hill.

activities.text: Hike in the Casentino Forests National Park from Poppi: the park, covering the forested massifs between Tuscany and Romagna, is one of the best-preserved forests in central Italy. Deer, wild boar, wolves and golden eagles inhabit these beech and fir woods. The Sasso Fratino Integral Reserve, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is off-limits to the public, but peripheral trails cross ancient-growth forests.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

places.name: Porlezza

visits.text: Explore the Grotte di Rescia, a fascinating system of natural cavities rich in stalactites and stalagmites bearing witness to millennia of geological activity. This underground spectacle offers a unique experience among limestone formations of bizarre and evocative shapes, wrapped in an atmosphere of mystery and natural wonder.

Visit the Church of San Vittore il Moro, the religious heart of the Porlezza community built in the seventeenth century over a pre-existing fifth-century structure. Inside you can admire the frescoes by Giulio Quaglio di Laino depicting episodes from the saint's life, a precious Baroque altar, eighteenth-century stuccowork and fine wooden sculptures.

Discover the Oratory of San Maurizio Martire, a small church set just outside the centre that still preserves its original layout. This gem of religious architecture is a precious testimony to local devotion through the centuries.

activities.text: Enjoy the Lombard shore of Lake Lugano, a body of water shared between Italy and Switzerland offering views of rare beauty. Porlezza is a renowned holiday resort prized for its crystal-clear waters, equipped beaches and relaxed atmosphere that draws visitors from all over Europe.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

map_keywords: Chiesa San Vittore il Moro Porlezza

visits.text: Terme di Porretta - a spa town in the upper Reno valley, whose sulfurous waters have been known since antiquity and exploited in organized fashion since the 14th century. The current spa buildings, built in late-19th and early-20th-century liberty style, offer treatments for respiratory and digestive ailments. The town developed as a resort for the Bolognese bourgeoisie from 1864 onward with the opening of the Porrettana railway line.

activities.text: Take the waters at the Porretta spa—treatments using sulfurous-saline-bromine-iodine waters recommended for respiratory conditions, open March to November.

Discover the Porretta Soul Festival—since 1959, every July Porretta Terme has hosted a soul, rhythm and blues, and gospel festival that drew artists including James Brown, Otis Redding, and Wilson Pickett. The festival, born from a local spa doctor's passion for African-American music, is now one of Europe's oldest soul festivals.
Hike the waymarked trails through the Reno valley from Porretta—chestnut and oak forests, perched villages, and views to the Apennine ridge.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

places.name: Porto Cervo

visits.text: Porto Cervo was designed by architect Luigi Vietti and inaugurated in 1964, two years after the creation of the Consorzio Costa Smeralda on March 14, 1962, by Prince Karim Aga Khan IV. The Aga Khan had discovered these deserted shores in the late 1950s aboard a 12-meter sailboat and decided to create an anti-Côte d'Azur. The Consorzio still manages 3,114 hectares today, of which 96% remain undeveloped.

The Stella Maris church, designed by architect Michele Busiri Vici and inaugurated in 1968, overlooks Porto Cervo's old harbor from its hilltop. Its clean white facade and square bell tower exemplify the pan-Mediterranean style the Aga Khan wanted so that each building would integrate with the granite landscape.

Spiaggia del Principe (Prince's Beach) is said to have been the Aga Khan's favorite beach, where he regularly went with his architects during the site's early explorations. Accessible via a 10-minute walk from the road, its intense blue-green waters are framed by pink granite rocks.
Capriccioli Beach, at the back of a sheltered bay, is a succession of small coves separated by rocks you walk along. Its shallow, clear waters make it an excellent snorkeling spot.
Liscia Ruja is the longest beach on the Costa Smeralda at about 800 meters of golden sand. Bordered by juniper and mastic trees, it faces the Mortorio and Soffi islets in the open sea.

activities.text: Stroll through Porto Cervo's piazzetta, the heart of the village designed by Vietti in 1964, whose arcades and white-plastered facades reproduce the vernacular architecture of Gallura.
Swimming and snorkeling in the Costa Smeralda's coves — Capriccioli and Spiaggia del Principe count among the clearest seabeds in the Mediterranean.
Visit the village of San Pantaleo, 10 km inland, a pink granite village with narrow alleys known for its craft market and local artists' galleries.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Sardinia Coast: Beaches & Hidden Coves

map_keywords: Piazzetta di Porto Cervo Porto Cervo

visits.text: Porto Ercole is guarded by three Spanish fortresses built in the 16th century during the Hispanic domination of the State of Presidi. Forte Stella—whose six-pointed star shape allowed enemy targeting from each point—was begun in 1548 by Giovan Battista Belluzzi and completed by Giovanni Camerini under Cosimo I de' Medici; it's the most accessible and most photographed. The Rocca Spagnola, an irregular four-bastion star form, dominates the borgo antico. Forte Filippo, on the northern promontory, was built for Philip II of Spain according to a design by Giovanni Camerini.

The borgo antico of Porto Ercole, accessible only on foot from the Porta Pisana topped by a clock tower, is a labyrinth of alleys and staircases clinging to the hillside. Piazza Santa Barbara, above the bastion of the same name, offers the village's most iconic panorama over the bay and fortresses. The Palazzo dei Governatori, built in the early 16th century under Siena, served as residence of the Spanish commander and then the summer governor of the State of Presidi. The church of Sant'Erasmo (17th century), at the village's highest point, is the tallest building.

Caravaggio died in Porto Ercole on July 18, 1610, at age 39, in circumstances still debated. Fleeing a death sentence for murder, he had left Naples with three paintings intended for Cardinal Scipione Borghese, the price of an expected papal pardon. His death certificate, found in 2001 in the Sant'Erasmo parish register, indicates he died at the Hospital of Santa Maria Ausiliatrice, probably from malaria. A monument honors him in the historic center.

activities.text: The Duna Feniglia Nature Reserve is a wooded dune 6 km long separating the open sea from the Orbetello lagoon. A dirt path traverses it under pines, inhabited by semi-wild fallow deer. According to local tradition, it was on this beach that Caravaggio was found dying in 1610—though his death certificate found in 2001 indicates he died in hospital, the exact circumstances remaining debated. Car access is prohibited: the dune is explored on foot or by bicycle.

Orbetello, a town perched on the narrow sand strip between the two tomboli of Argentario, is a discovery in its own right: 16th-century Spanish walls, compact old town, lagoon classified as a nature reserve (flamingos, herons, egrets). From the lagoon's edge, the view of Monte Argentario's green promontory rising from the sea is one of the most beautiful on the Tuscan coast.

Spiaggia Lunga beach, accessible from Porto Ercole by a steep path descending behind Forte Stella. Sand and pebble beach below the fortress, surrounded by Mediterranean scrub. Further along the same trail, the Acqua Dolce beach, accessible behind the Hotel Pellicano, is one of Argentario's most preserved coves.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

visits.text: Exploration of the Porto Selvaggio nature reserve and its rocky coves.
Walk through Mediterranean scrubland and pine forests.

activities.text: Discovery of sea caves and swimming in the clear waters of Salento.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters

visits.text: Today, dedicate the entire day to Portovenere and Palmaria island - take your time. Portovenere is UNESCO-listed alongside Cinque Terre. A practical tip: the access road is a dead-end often jammed in summer. Arrive early morning, or better still, take the boat from La Spezia (regular shuttles, 30 minutes). The village stretches along a row of medieval tower-houses with colourful facades, squeezed together facing the harbour. This 'palazzata' - continuous facade of fortified houses - served as a defensive wall on the sea side.

Climb to the church of San Pietro, perched on a rocky spur at the tip of the promontory - built in the 13th century in black and white marble in the Ligurian Gothic style, it overlooks the sea with Palmaria island as backdrop. The spot is extraordinary at sunset, but even in daylight the light on the rocks and sea is striking. Below, the Grotta Arpaia (known as 'Byron's Cave') is a natural cavity where the English poet Lord Byron came to meditate and swim - he swam across the gulf to Lerici, according to legend.

Climb up to the Castello Doria, a 12th-century Genoese fortress dominating the village from the hilltop. The view from the ramparts embraces the entire Gulf of Poets, Palmaria island and, on clear days, the Cinque Terre coast to the north. Descending, stroll along Via Capellini, the village's main street, lined with shops and trattorias.

activities.text: In the afternoon, take the boat from Portovenere harbour to Palmaria island (5-minute crossing). It's the largest of the Ligurian islands, UNESCO-listed. The island offers hiking trails through Mediterranean maquis, swimming coves accessible on foot, and the Grotta Azzurra (Blue Grotto), visitable by boat. The path circling the island takes about 2 hours - otherwise, just enjoy the cove on the Portovenere side for a swim. Last boat back around 6pm in summer.

Dine in Portovenere as evening falls, facing the harbour. Ligurian specialities are essential: focaccia, trofie with pesto, fritto misto of gulf fish, Monterosso anchovies. The waterfront terraces are magical when the lights from the tower-houses reflect in the harbour.

days_plan.itin_title: From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria

places.name: Positano

visits.text: Drive up to Ravello first thing in the morning, perched 350 meters above the sea, 7 km from Amalfi. Villa Rufolo, built in the 13th century, inspired Richard Wagner for Klingsor's garden in Parsifal—he stayed there in 1880. Every summer, a classical music festival is held there on a terrace suspended above the sea. The gardens, with their medieval towers and palm trees, offer a vertiginous panorama over the Gulf of Salerno.

A few minutes' walk away, Villa Cimbrone boasts the famous Terrazza dell'Infinito—the Terrace of Infinity. Bordered by 18th-century marble busts, it overlooks the sea from 350 meters high. Gore Vidal, who stayed there regularly, described it as the most beautiful view in the world. The gardens mix statues, neoclassical temples, and grottoes in a romantic setting. Arrive early—the terrace fills up quickly.

Head back down toward the coast and stop at the Grotta dello Smeraldo (Emerald Grotto) at Conca dei Marini, 4 km from Amalfi. This sea cave, discovered in 1932, owes its name to the emerald color the water takes under the effect of light filtering through an underwater opening. You reach it by elevator carved into the cliff or by boat from the sea. Inside, stalactites and stalagmites frame a turquoise pool.

Continue west on the SS163. Stop at Praiano, between Amalfi and Positano. This village clinging to the mountain is far less touristy than its neighbors. The church of San Gennaro, with its colorful majolica dome, dominates a small square from which the view over the coast is superb. Below, the cove of Marina di Praia—a tiny beach squeezed between two cliffs—is one of the coast's most photogenic spots.

Devote the morning to Positano itself. The former fishing village became a chic resort in the 1950s when writer John Steinbeck introduced it to the world in an article for Harper's Bazaar. Stroll the stepped lanes among artisan shops, art galleries, and bougainvillea. For more tranquility, follow the cliff west to Fornillo beach, smaller and less crowded than Spiaggia Grande.

activities.text: Arrive in Positano late afternoon. Descend the stairs to the Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta, recognizable by its yellow, green, and blue majolica dome gleaming in the sun. Inside, a 13th-century Byzantine icon of the Black Madonna. Continue to Spiaggia Grande to enjoy the golden evening light on the village—this is when the colors are most beautiful and the crowds begin to disperse.

Set out on the Sentiero degli Dei (Path of the Gods), one of Italy's most beautiful hikes. The 7-km trail links Bomerano (hamlet of Agerola) to Nocelle (above Positano), at 630 meters altitude. Allow 2 to 3 hours walking on a moderate-difficulty path, among rosemary bushes, olive trees, and vertiginous coastal panoramas. Legend has it that this path was traced by gods descending from heaven to save Ulysses from the sirens' song. From Nocelle, 1,700 steps descend to Positano.

Late in the day, treat yourself to a tasting of artisanal limoncello. The Amalfi Coast produces sfusato amalfitano lemons, larger and more fragrant than ordinary lemons. Limoncello is prepared by macerating the zest in pure alcohol for at least a month. Several local producers offer tastings accompanied by lemon cakes—a gentle end to the day after the morning's exertion.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast
From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast
Hidden Villages of the Amalfi Coast: From Praiano to Salerno
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

map_keywords: Grotta dello Smeraldo Conca dei Marini

places.name: Praia A Mare

visits.text: Isola di Dino - a 50-hectare limestone island 400 meters offshore from Praia a Mare, accessible only by boat. The system of sea caves perforating the island includes the Grotta Azzurra, whose waters glow electric blue through light refraction on the white seabed - a phenomenon comparable to Capri's famous Blue Grotto.

activities.text: Boat excursion around Isola di Dino with swimming stops in hidden coves and a visit to the Grotta Azzurra - departures from Praia a Mare beach.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to Calabria: The Tyrrhenian Coast

map_keywords: Isola di Dino Praia a Mare

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santo Stefano in Prato is the only Italian cathedral with an outdoor pulpit—on the right corner of the facade, under a bronze canopy by Michelozzo, the 'Pergamo del Santo Cingolo'—from which the Holy Belt of the Virgin is solemnly displayed five times a year to the people of Prato. The relic, brought from Jerusalem in 1141 by merchant Michele Dagomari, triggered construction of the Chapel of the Belt (14th century), frescoed by Agnolo Gaddi. The main chapel houses Filippo Lippi's fresco cycle (1452-1466): Stories of Saint Stephen and Saint John the Baptist, which Vasari judged 'the most excellent of all his things.' During these years, Lippi painted Lucrezia Buti, a nun from the convent of Santa Margherita, as his model for the Virgin—and abducted her on the very day of the Belt procession in 1456. Cosimo de' Medici intervened with Pope Pius II to obtain their pardon.

The Imperial Castle of Frederick II is the only medieval building of this type preserved in Tuscany and the sole example of a Swabian fortress north of the Alps. Built between 1237 and 1248 for Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II of Hohenstaufen, it served to collect taxes and duties on the territory and to symbolize imperial power against papal authority. Square plan, four corner towers, no ornamentation: an architecture of power.

The Textile Museum traces 700 years of Prato's cloth-making history, which made Prato the European capital of textiles in the Middle Ages. The city owes its medieval wealth to merchant Francesco Datini (1335-1410)—whose house-museum is also open to the public—who built one of Europe's first multinational enterprises with branches in Barcelona, Valencia, Genoa, Paris, and London. His 150,000 business letters preserved in the Prato State Archives form the world's most important medieval commercial archive.

activities.text: Luigi Pecci Center for Contemporary Art, on Prato's outskirts: one of the few Italian museums entirely dedicated to contemporary art since 1988. The ring-shaped 2016 extension by Dutch architect Maurice Nio doubles the exhibition space to 3,000 m².

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

visits.text: The Parco Mediceo di Pratolino (Villa Demidoff), a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2013, is the only major Medici park whose original villa was demolished—in 1821 by the Lorraine dynasty, who found the upkeep too expensive. The 30-hectare estate was created by Francesco I de' Medici beginning in 1568: he commissioned Bernardo Buontalenti to design a 'garden of marvels' with hydraulic surprise fountains, artificial grottoes, and water features that would spread the fashion for scherzi d'acqua (water tricks) throughout Europe. Montaigne visited Pratolino in 1580-1581 and left an enthusiastic description in his Travel Journal.

The Colossus of the Apennines, carved from rock between 1579 and 1583 by Jean de Boulogne (Giambologna), stands 14 meters tall. The statue embodies the Apennine mountain chain: its skin covered in simulated stalactites and algae shows it emerging from the mountain itself. The interior contains three levels of secret rooms: a lower grotto once adorned with frescoes and mother-of-pearl, two mid-level salons where musicians performed during Medici festivities, and a fireplace in the skull—when lit, smoke would pour from the giant's nostrils. Construction reportedly cost double that of the Uffizi. An old Italian rhyme laments that such a masterpiece is hidden in the woods rather than displayed in the city.

The Buontalenti Chapel, with its hexagonal plan surrounded by a loggia, is one of the few original buildings from the Medici estate still standing. The Cupid Grotto, also by Buontalenti (1577), once contained a pivoting bronze statue that would spray visitors who stepped on a hidden trigger plate. The Statue of the Mugnone, personifying the local river by Giambologna, was reconstructed around 1830.

activities.text: Free walking in the park among centuries-old trees—oaks, cedars, horse chestnuts. The park is open free of charge on weekends and certain weekdays; deer, foxes, and hares roam semi-wild within its grounds.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

visits.text: Pretoro - a village of 900 inhabitants at 590 meters, clinging to the eastern slopes of Majella. Nicknamed 'il paese presepe' ('the nativity village') for its stone houses stacked on the hillside that form, from the valley below, a crèche-like silhouette. Home to the Museo del Lupo, one of Italy's rare museums entirely dedicated to the Apennine wolf.

activities.text: Stop at the belvedere overlooking the Aventino valley with views of the Majella's limestone walls before descending toward Guardiagrele.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Priverno

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santa Maria Annunziata, reached by a broad staircase dominating the main square, is Priverno's mother church. It preserves elements of medieval architecture remodeled over the centuries. Several artists from Priverno worked here, including the painter Pietro Coleberti, active in the 15th century and author of frescoes throughout the region.

The Church of San Giovanni Evangelista preserves frescoes dating from the 13th to 15th centuries. The church testifies to Priverno's artistic activity during the Middle Ages, when the town supplied master builders to the region's major Cistercian construction projects.

The Archaeological Museum of Priverno, housed in the municipal palace, traces the territory's history from the first Volscian settlements to the Roman era through a collection of ceramics, inscriptions, and everyday objects from the excavations of Privernum.

The Archaeological Park of Privernum, a few kilometers from the village, lets you walk among the vestiges of the Roman city founded after the conquest of the Volscians. You can make out the mosaics of patrician domus, the bases of a theater, and the remains of a thermal complex, evidence of a prosperous city along the Via Appia.

activities.text: Visit Fossanova Abbey, 5.5 km south of Priverno. Founded in 1135 when Pope Innocent II ceded a Benedictine monastery to the Cistercians of Saint Bernard of Clairvaux, the new church was consecrated in 1208 by Innocent III. Built entirely of travertine, it represents the earliest example of Cistercian Gothic art in Italy. The central nave, with seven bays covered by ribbed vaults, is stripped of all decoration according to the austere Cistercian rule. The cloister has three Romanesque sides and a fourth Gothic side added in the 13th century by Roman marmorari. It was in the abbot's house that Saint Thomas Aquinas died on March 7, 1274, struck down by fever en route to the Council of Lyon; his cell is now a visitable chapel.

Stroll through Priverno's alleyways, a village perched on a slope of the Lepini Mountains. The center preserves its medieval layout with narrow lanes, vaulted passages, and limestone palaces. The Svicolando festival brings the streets to life each summer with performances and art installations.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast

map_keywords: Cattedrale di Santa Maria Annunziata Priverno
Chiesa di San Giovanni Evangelista Priverno

visits.text: Dedicate the morning to the Museo e Real Bosco di Capodimonte, perched on the hill of the same name. The palace, built in 1738 for Charles of Bourbon, houses the Farnese Collection of paintings: works by Caravaggio, Titian, Raphael, and Botticelli. The surrounding 134-hectare park is Naples' green lung—perfect for catching your breath between galleries. Allow 2 to 3 hours for the museum alone. Entry is free the first Sunday of each month.

Descend toward the Sanità district to visit the Catacombe di San Gennaro, southern Italy's largest catacombs. Carved into tuff from the 2nd century onward, they extend over two levels and preserve remarkable early Christian frescoes. This is where San Gennaro, Naples' patron saint, was buried in the 5th century. The guided tours are run by a local cooperative that has revitalized the entire neighborhood.

In the same neighborhood, stop outside the Palazzo dello Spagnolo, a masterpiece by architect Ferdinando Sanfelice (1738). Its monumental "falcon's wing" (ali di falco) staircase is one of Naples' most photographed. Fifty meters further, the Palazzo Sanfelice offers a similar staircase. These two palaces illustrate Neapolitan Baroque at its most theatrical.

Head to Marina Corricella, the island's most photogenic fishing village. No cars circulate here—you descend via staircases lined with arches and loggias. This is where Il Postino (The Postman, 1994) starring Philippe Noiret was filmed. Settle on a terrace facing the colorful boats for a coffee. The best viewpoint is from the terrace near the church of Santa Maria delle Grazie Incoronata, with its majolica dome.

Climb up to Terra Murata, the island's highest point at 90 meters. This fortified village built in the 15th century protected inhabitants from pirates. You'll find the Abbazia di San Michele Arcangelo, dedicated to the island's patron saint, with its cloister and religious artworks. From the ramparts, the view embraces the entire Gulf of Naples, Capri, Ischia, and Vesuvius.

Right next to the abbey, the Palazzo d'Avalos, a 16th-century palace built by the noble family of the same name, served as a prison from 1830 to 1988. Inmates there learned linen working, a craft tradition of Procida. Guided tours including the cells are organized, along with contemporary art exhibitions. From the palace, descend toward Casale Vascello, the island's oldest quarter, with its pastel-toned houses featuring external "giraffe neck" (a collo di giraffa) staircases.

activities.text: Mid-afternoon, head to Molo Beverello (Naples port) and board the ferry to Procida—about 40 minutes' crossing. The island measures barely 4 km² and has 10,000 inhabitants. Arriving at Marina Grande, the spectacle begins immediately: pastel facades—yellow, pink, blue—stacked one atop another. Drop your bags at your accommodation and use the evening light for a first stroll around the harbor.

In the afternoon, take bus L1 or L2 from Marina Grande to reach Spiaggia di Chiaiolella, in the south of the island. This is Procida's largest sandy beach, bordered by a tranquil marina. The water is calm and shallow—perfect for swimming. From there, you can see the islet of Vivara, a nature reserve shaped like a crescent, remnant of an ancient volcanic crater.

Taste the lingua di bue (ox tongue), Procida's signature pastry: crispy puff pastry filled with lemon custard made from the island's lemons. Procida's limone pane is unique—its thick, fragrant peel also flavors granitas, ice creams, and liqueurs. Settle by the harbor to enjoy your treat facing the sea.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast
From Naples to the Lucanian Dolomites: Secret Campania and the Amalfi Coast

visits.text: Cappelle Medicee - Medici funeral chapels attached to the Basilica di San Lorenzo, with the Sagrestia Nuova designed by Michelangelo between 1520 and 1534 to house the tombs of Lorenzo II and Giuliano II de' Medici. The allegorical figures of Day, Night, Dawn, and Dusk (1524-1534), sculpted in white Carrara marble, were left unfinished at Michelangelo's flight from Florence in 1534.

Greve in Chianti - borgo of 14,000 inhabitants on the SR222 Chiantigiana, whose triangular arcaded square from the 19th century is surrounded by wine and olive oil producers. The Chianti Classico DOCG zone was delimited by grand ducal decree as early as 1716 by Cosimo III de' Medici - one of the first controlled wine appellations in history.

Panzano in Chianti - hilltop borgo perched at 478 meters elevation between Greve and Castellina, with 360-degree views over the Chianti hills from the central piazza and medieval castle ruins. The village overlooks one of the most renowned Chianti Classico production zones.

Radda in Chianti - medieval borgo of 1,600 inhabitants at 530 meters elevation, former capital of the Lega del Chianti since 1384. The 15th-century Palazzo del Podestà, whose façade is covered with 51 stemmi in pietra serena of successive podestas, dominates the central piazza.

activities.text: Scenic Chianti drive on SR222 Chiantigiana between Florence and Radda - 60 kilometers through vineyards, olive groves, and cypress alleys between Greve, Panzano, Castellina, and Radda.

Chianti Classico DOCG tasting at a wine estate around Radda - the Gallo Nero (black rooster), symbol of the appellation since the 13th century, appears on the label of all producers belonging to the consortium founded in 1924.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province
Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine

visits.text: Fortress of Radicofani: Perched at 896 meters on a basalt cliff, this is one of the most spectacular positions in all of Tuscany. First mentioned in 973, transferred to the Carolingians in the 9th century, it passed to the Papal State in 1153 then to Siena in the late 15th century. In 1297, Messer Ghino di Tacco—a gentleman bandit from Torrita di Siena, mentioned by both Dante in the Divine Comedy and Boccaccio in the Decameron—seized it and made it his base for years of benevolent brigandage. The Cassero Museum inside traces the site's history from the Bronze Age to the 16th century.

Medieval village and Medici Post: The town at the Rocca's foot preserves stone lanes and palaces including the Palazzo Pretorio and the 16th-century Villa Medici. The Casale della Posta (Osteria Grossa), built by the Medici in the 1500s and framed by a double row of cypresses, served as a postal relay for Via Francigena travelers until the 19th century—Casanova, Stendhal, Dickens, and Goethe stayed here.

activities.text: View from the Rocca tower: The view from the summit extends to Monte Cetona, Val d'Orcia, Monte Amiata, and on clear days, Lakes Trasimeno and Bolsena. Radicofani is one of the five Val d'Orcia communes listed as UNESCO World Heritage Sites since 2004.

Bagni San Filippo thermal baths: Twenty kilometers northwest of Radicofani, these natural pools of 48°C sulfurous water are freely accessible in a forest setting. The white travertine waterfalls have shaped natural basins known since antiquity.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

visits.text: Ragusa Ibla, rebuilt after the 1693 earthquake, is one of eight Val di Noto towns inscribed on the UNESCO list in 2002. Its golden limestone Baroque palaces and sloping alleys form one of Italy's most cohesive urban ensembles.

Ibla's San Giorgio Cathedral, Rosario Gagliardi's masterpiece completed in 1775, soars in three superimposed orders across 43 meters above a monumental staircase. Gagliardi, self-taught, knew Roman Baroque only through engravings.
The Ponte dei Cappuccini offers plunging views over Ibla's brown tile roofs and wooded ravines. This bridge provides the vantage point for the town's iconic photographs.

activities.text: Evening stroll to the Giardini Iblei, a public garden suspended at the ravine's edge with views of the town's three Baroque bell towers.
Tasting of Modica chocolate—prepared cold without butter or milk according to the Aztec recipe transmitted by the Spanish in the 16th century—and Ragusano DOP cheese.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

visits.text: Borgo dei Ruscelli - The first mentions of Rasiglia date back to the early 13th century under the name Curtis de Rasilia, in the archives of Sassovivo Abbey. The hamlet, a frazione of Foligno at 600 meters altitude, owes its identity to the Capovena spring, which gushes in the upper part of the village and divides into a series of channels, small waterfalls, and wooden bridges crossing the stone alleyways. These waters fed a flourishing textile industry from the Middle Ages: fulling mills, spinning mills, dyeworks, and wool factories operated until the mid-20th century. At its peak, the village had three wool factories with dyeworks, three grain mills, a post office, and a bank branch. The 1997 earthquake nearly finished it off; its gradual renaissance through cultural tourism is described locally as the "miracolo Rasiglia."

Peschiera and ruins of the Trinci castle - After crossing the entire village, the various streams converge in a large artificial basin, the Peschiera, before joining the Menotre river. On the hill, the remains of the castle built by the Trinci lords (masters of Foligno from 1305 to 1439) today consist of a section of walls and two small defensive towers, partially restored in the 1990s. The castle controlled the Via della Spina, a commercial route linking the Adriatic to Rome. The entire center is pedestrian.

Traditional weaving workshop - The village preserves and showcases the instruments of the medieval textile industry: carding machines, reels, wooden looms, and a mechanical Jacquard loom from the early 20th century. A small hydroelectric plant at the foot of the spring, built in the early 20th century to power the wool factories, is also visible.

activities.text: Stroll through the alleyways along the channels - The pedestrian circuit (about 1 km) winds from the Capovena spring to the Peschiera along the two old 15th-century mills, the public washhouse, and stone houses with moss-covered walls. In winter, the hamlet hosts a living nativity scene in the alleyways, recreating traditional crafts in period costumes.

Hike to the Menotre waterfalls (Altolina) - A few kilometers downstream from Rasiglia, the Menotre river forms a series of waterfalls in a wooded canyon accessible from the Oasi dell'Altolina in Belfiore di Foligno. Two entrances allow you to explore the canyon from upstream or downstream.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

visits.text: Rassa sits at the confluence of the Sorba and Gronda torrents in Valsesia, at 917 meters elevation. Since November 2024, it has joined the Borghi più belli d'Italia circuit - the first and only village in Vercelli province to receive this distinction. The village divides into five cantons each bearing its own name. Its territory forms a special nature protection zone. The region proudly carries the nickname La valle dei tremendi (the valley of the terrible ones) - a reputation built notably by the inhabitants' resistance to the troops of Fra Dolcino and his Dulcinian disciples, medieval heretics who took refuge in these valleys around 1304 and held out against the crusade of the bishop of Vercelli for two winters.

The parish church of Santa Croce, existing since 1596 and partially rebuilt in the nineteenth century after a fire, preserves eighteenth-century frescoes by Antonio Orgiazzi - an artist who also worked at the Sacro Monte of Varallo. The single-arch stone bridge over the Sorba is described by specialists as a jewel of alpine civil engineering. The Ecomuseum of Wood in Rassa is installed in the Brasei hydraulic sawmill, built in the seventeenth century and operating until the 1980s - last survivor of a series of sawmills that once paced the village economy. The Bottega del Patel, a small carpentry workshop with its period tools, completes the visit.

activities.text: Hike in Val Sorba and Val Gronda, both classified as special protection zones. The bike path linking Alpe di Mera to Sorbella and Rassa allows a cycling outing in an unaltered mountain landscape. Outside the village, an old lime kiln and a carbonera (wood carbonization oven) can be observed on the trail to rifugio Heidi Rassa.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

places.name: Ravenne

visits.text: Ravenna is the world capital of mosaics, and that's no exaggeration. Eight of its monuments are UNESCO World Heritage Sites—all built between the 5th and 6th centuries, when the city was successively capital of the Western Roman Empire, the Ostrogothic Kingdom and the Byzantine Exarchate. Begin with the Basilica of San Vitale, consecrated in 548 under Archbishop Maximian. Its octagonal architecture blends Roman and Byzantine traditions. The mosaics in the presbytery will leave you speechless: Emperor Justinian and Empress Theodora, haloed in gold, have been gazing at you for 1,500 years. Take time to sit and observe the details—the embroidery on the garments, the expressions on the faces, the changing reflections in the light.

Just behind San Vitale, the Mausoleum of Galla Placidia is one of Ravenna's most striking experiences. From the outside, this small cruciform brick building is nothing special—12 metres by 10. But push open the door and you enter a starry sky: the dome, covered in mosaics on a midnight-blue background with golden stars and a Latin cross, dates from the 5th century. These are Ravenna's oldest mosaics, stunningly well preserved. Galla Placidia, daughter of Emperor Theodosius I, had this mausoleum built, but her remains probably never rested here. Arrive early or late in the day—the space is small and queues can be long.

Continue to the Neonian Baptistery (or Baptistery of the Orthodox), the oldest monument still standing in Ravenna. Built in the 5th century, it preserves beneath its dome a mosaic depicting the baptism of Christ surrounded by the twelve apostles, on a golden background. The contrast between the austere brick exterior and the rich interior is characteristic of Ravenna.

The Basilica of Sant'Apollinare Nuovo, built by the Ostrogothic king Theodoric in the 6th century as a palatine chapel, features two long mosaic processions running along the nave: 26 martyrs on one side, 22 virgins on the other, advancing towards Christ and the Virgin. Byzantine influence mingles here with the Roman tradition in fascinating ways.

Make a detour to Dante's Tomb, a small neoclassical marble temple built in 1780 on Via Dante Alighieri. The father of the Italian language, exiled from Florence in 1302, spent his final years in Ravenna under the protection of the da Polenta family. It was here that he completed the Divine Comedy, and here that he died in 1321. Every 14 September, the anniversary of his death, the city of Florence offers the olive oil that fuels the tomb's votive lamp—a gesture of reparation for the exile they inflicted on him. Entry is free.

Late in the day, take the road (or bike—5 km on a pleasant cycle path) to the Basilica of Sant'Apollinare in Classe, Ravenna's eighth UNESCO monument. This imposing 6th-century edifice stands alone in the middle of the plain, with its characteristic cylindrical bell tower. The apse mosaic is of rare poetry: the Transfiguration of Christ is represented in a flowery meadow setting with sheep symbolising the apostles, on a golden background. Don't miss it—it's one of Ravenna's jewels, often overlooked because it's off-centre.

activities.text: Between visits, treat yourself to a break on Piazza del Popolo, Ravenna's living room. Lined with elegant buildings and its Venetian columns, it's the perfect spot for a coffee or aperitivo. The pedestrian streets around the square house interesting shops—notably contemporary mosaic workshops where craftspeople perpetuate the city's thousand-year tradition. Some offer a few hours' introduction to create your own mosaic.

Romagna cuisine in Ravenna deserves dedicated time. Try cappelletti in brodo (small stuffed pasta in broth), piadina romagnola (filled wheat flatbread), passatelli (breadcrumb and parmesan pasta in broth) and grilled fish from the Adriatic. The best tables are around Piazza del Popolo and in the adjacent pedestrian alleys. A glass of Sangiovese or Albana DOCG pairs perfectly.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini

map_keywords: Basilica di Sant'Apollinare in Classe Ravenna

visits.text: Reggio Calabria is the grand finale of your road trip. Head immediately to the National Archaeological Museum of Magna Graecia (MArRC), one of Italy's most important archaeological museums. You came for the Riace Bronzes, and they don't disappoint. Discovered on August 16, 1972 by an amateur diver at 8 meters depth off Riace Marina, these two 5th-century BC Greek bronze warriors measure nearly 2 meters tall. Their eyes in calcite and glass paste, their copper lips, warrior A's silver teeth - every anatomical detail is obsessively precise. Access to the room is regulated: no more than 20 visitors at a time, 20 minutes maximum. Arrive at opening to avoid the wait.

The rest of the museum is well worth the visit: four floors cover Calabria's history from the Neolithic to Greek colonization. Don't miss the rooms dedicated to the sanctuaries of Croton and Locri, the bronze heads from Porticello (5th century BC), and the bronze tablets from Locri, which document a Zeus temple's finances.

Leave the museum and walk along the Lungomare Falcomatà, which Gabriele D'Annunzio called "the most beautiful kilometer in Italy." This promenade lined with palm trees and century-old ficus runs along the Strait of Messina - on clear days, you see Sicily so close it seems within reach, with Etna as backdrop. Look for the Fata Morgana phenomenon, a mirage that makes the Sicilian coast appear raised and distorted above the sea.

Walk up Corso Garibaldi, an elegant pedestrian artery lined with shops and cafés, to the Castello Aragonese. Then visit Reggio's cathedral, one of Calabria's largest, rebuilt after the devastating 1908 earthquake that razed the city by 90%.

activities.text: End your Calabrian journey with an aperitivo on the Lungomare at sunset, facing the Strait of Messina. The traces of Roman baths and Greek walls visible along the promenade recall that Reggio (Rhegion) was founded in the 8th century BC - it's one of Italy's oldest cities.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea
Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

places.name: Rieti

visits.text: The Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta, consecrated in 1152, blends Romanesque and Baroque styles. Bernini contributed to the interior decoration. Beneath the nave, the crypt (or lower basilica) reveals the primitive foundations. The massive yet slender Romanesque bell tower dominates the historic center.

Rieti Sotterranea, accessible from Piazza Cavour, unveils the remains of the Roman viaduct built to prevent the Via Salaria from bogging down at the city entrance. This powerful engineering work allowed the consular salt road to penetrate Reate and continue toward Rome. The guided tour passes through underground chambers where archaeological layers are stacked across several centuries.

The Teatro Flavio Vespasiano, inaugurated on September 20, 1893 with Mascagni's Cavalleria Rusticana, was designed by Milanese architect Achille Sfondrini, who had already created Rome's Teatro dell'Opera. The horseshoe-shaped hall seats about 230 on the main floor, with three tiers of 22 boxes each and a gallery. The great dome is adorned with a tempera by Giulio Rolland (1901) celebrating the triumph of Titus and Vespasian after the capture of Jerusalem. The historic painted curtain by Antonino Calcagnadoro (1910), measuring 44 × 25 feet, was restored in 2019. The theater is renowned for its acoustics: in 2002, violinist Uto Ughi awarded it Italy's first national Acoustics Prize.

The Palazzo Vescovile, built in 1283 near the cathedral, is fronted by a majestic arcaded portico. It houses the Museo Diocesano and the Pinacoteca with Renaissance works. Vignola's Loggia offers a beautiful viewpoint over the old town.
The Chiesa di San Francesco, erected in 1245, is the first mendicant order convent in Rieti. It preserves remarkable frescoes from Giotto's school, recently restored.

The Santuario della Foresta, north of Rieti, is the third of the four Franciscan sanctuaries of the Valle Santa. Here Saint Francis, nearly blind from trachoma contracted in the East, composed the Canticle of the Creatures in 1225, one year before his death. According to tradition, he also performed the miracle of the grapes: the vintner offered his grapes to the friars who pressed them and obtained miraculous wine in quantities far exceeding the usual harvest.

The Santuario di Poggio Bustone (convento di San Giacomo), the fourth sanctuary of the Valle Santa, is perched at 2,690 feet above the village of the same name. Francis first arrived in the Rieti valley here in 1209, greeting the inhabitants with the famous words "Buongiorno, buona gente!" (Good morning, good people!). He received the certainty of forgiveness for his sins here. The Sacro Speco, a meditation cave accessible by a steep forest trail, offers a panorama over the entire Rieti plain.

The Riserva Naturale dei Laghi Lungo e Ripasottile, in the heart of the Rieti basin, protects over 7,400 acres of wetlands. The two small lakes, remnants of the ancient Lacus Velinus drained by the Romans in the 3rd century BC by digging the canal to the Cascata delle Marmore, are surrounded by reed beds and water-loving woodlands sheltering rich birdlife, especially in winter.

activities.text: Stroll through Rieti's medieval historic center, the Umbilicus Italiae—the navel of Italy according to Varro. Via Roma follows the ancient Via Salaria; admire the Palazzo Vecchiarelli designed by architect Carlo Maderno. Piazza San Rufo is considered the exact geographic center of the Italian peninsula. The 13th-century walls still encircle the ancient core.
Taste Rieti specialties: fregnacce alla sabinese (crepes stuffed with ragù), strozzapreti with olive and mushroom ragù, and Terzetti Reatini biscuits, a local specialty flavored with spices.
Bird watching in the Lungo and Ripasottile lakes reserve. In winter, grey herons, bitterns, coots and wigeons frequent the reed beds. Wooden observation points line the trails.

days_plan.itin_title: Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit
Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit
Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

map_keywords: Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta Rieti

visits.text: Devote the morning to the Republic of San Marino, the world's oldest sovereign state and the oldest still-functioning republic. According to tradition, a Dalmatian stonemason named Marinus founded a Christian community atop Mount Titano in 301 AD to escape the persecutions of Emperor Diocletian. Since then, this micro-republic of 33,000 inhabitants has never lost its independence. The historic centre and Mount Titano (750 m) have been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2008. Park in Borgo Maggiore and take the cable car—the 2-minute ascent offers spectacular panoramas and saves you the traffic jams at the summit.

San Marino's Three Towers are the republic's symbol and stand on the three peaks of Mount Titano. La Guaita (first tower), the oldest, dates from the 11th century and served as a prison until 1970. Climb it for a panorama embracing all of Romagna to the Adriatic Sea—on clear days, you can glimpse the Croatian coast. La Cesta (second tower), perched on Titano's highest point, houses a small museum of antique weapons. The path linking the two towers follows the ridge and offers vertiginous views.

Head down to Piazza della Libertà, San Marino's heart, dominated by the Palazzo Pubblico—seat of government since 1894. The changing of the guard takes place every half-hour in summer. The Basilica of San Marino (19th century, neoclassical) preserves the relics of the founding saint. Wander through the cobbled alleys that climb and descend between the crenellated ramparts before heading down towards Rimini.

Arriving in Rimini in early afternoon, start with the Arch of Augustus, erected in 27 BC—it's the oldest triumphal arch still standing in Rome. Its Corinthian half-columns and rounded arch mark the southern entrance to the ancient city. From there, walk up Corso d'Augusto, the main street that follows the route of the ancient Via Flaminia, towards the historic centre.

At the centre's other end, the Bridge of Tiberius spans the Marecchia river with its five massive Istrian stone arches. Begun under Augustus and completed under Tiberius in 21 AD, it has been in continuous service for 2,000 years—cars still cross it daily. It's one of the best-preserved Roman bridges in the world. Come in the evening when it's illuminated and reflected in the water.

Just beyond the Bridge of Tiberius, the Borgo San Giuliano is Rimini's old fishermen's quarter, transformed into one of the city's most charming corners. Its brightly coloured low houses, cobbled alleys and murals pay homage to Fellini and his films—you'll recognise scenes from Amarcord and La Dolce Vita on the walls. The atmosphere is intimate, working-class and perfect for a terrace dinner.

Devote this morning to Rimini's Renaissance and cinematic heritage. The Tempio Malatestiano is far more than a simple cathedral. Sigismondo Malatesta, the powerful lord of Rimini, had Leon Battista Alberti transform a Gothic church into a sumptuous Renaissance temple from 1450. The white marble exterior is inspired by Roman triumphal arches. Inside, you'll find a crucifix painted by Giotto and the Malatesta family's funeral chapels. Sigismondo wanted to make it the Renaissance's most beautiful tomb—Pope Pius II excommunicated him for his pride.

The Castel Sismondo, the imposing fortress built in 1437 by Sigismondo Malatesta himself (he personally supervised the works), dominates the city centre. Since 2021, it has housed the Fellini Museum, an immersive and multimedia journey dedicated to the director born in Rimini in 1920. Fellini's visionary universe unfolds between the castle, Piazza Malatesta and the Fulgor cinema—the theatre where young Federico discovered cinema. Even if you're not a Fellini devotee, the experience is worthwhile.

Piazza Cavour is Rimini's medieval heart, an elegant urban salon bordered by the Palazzo dell'Arengo (13th century), the Palazzo del Podestà and the Teatro Galli, faithfully reconstructed after war damage and reopened in 2018. At the centre stands the Fontana della Pigna, a 14th-century white marble fountain that was long the city's only source of drinking water.

If you have a little time, detour to the remains of the Roman amphitheatre, located between the historic centre and the sea. Built in the 2nd century AD, it could accommodate about 12,000 spectators. Only the foundations and a few arches remain, but it's a powerful reminder of Rimini's importance in imperial Rome—the city stood at the crossroads of the Via Emilia and Via Flaminia, two of the Empire's most strategic roads.

activities.text: On the road between San Marino and Rimini, stop at one of the Romagna hills' vineyards. The region produces Sangiovese di Romagna, a full-bodied red, and Albana DOCG, the first Italian white to obtain this designation. Operators offer tastings with views over the hills—a beautiful transition between mountain and sea.

Enjoy your first evening in Rimini in the Borgo San Giuliano. The terrace tables in the cobbled alleys, overlooking the illuminated Bridge of Tiberius, are the best place to taste Romagna cuisine: piadina, passatelli in brodo, grilled Adriatic fish. The neighbourhood's atmosphere is warm and working-class, far from the seaside bustle of the waterfront.

In the afternoon, if you fancy some relaxation before departure, Rimini has 15 km of fine sandy beaches along the Adriatic. The coast is organised into numbered 'bagni' with deckchairs, umbrellas and sometimes beach volleyball courts or beach bars. Even out of season, a stroll along the seafront with views of the Grand Hotel—immortalised by Fellini in Amarcord—remains a pleasant moment.

To close your itinerary, do your last shopping at Rimini's covered market, near Piazza Cavour, or treat yourself to a final espresso in one of the square's cafés. If you're leaving late in the day, Rimini station offers direct connections to Bologna (1h15), Ravenna (1h) and beyond. Federico Fellini Airport is a few minutes from the centre.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini
From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini
Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Before reaching Cinque Terre, stop at Lerici in the morning - it's on the route, on the other side of the Gulf of Poets. This fishing village is dominated by an imposing 13th-century castle, built by the Pisans and enlarged by the Genoese, which today houses a palaeontology museum. This is where Percy Bysshe Shelley spent his final months before drowning in the gulf in 1822, aged 29. The small bay of San Terenzo, nearby, preserves Villa Magni where the poet resided. Stroll along the seafront and at the castle's foot - allow 45 minutes.

Park your car in La Spezia or Riomaggiore (car parks uphill, traffic is banned from the villages) and take the train. Riomaggiore is the first Cinque Terre village coming from the south: its pastel-coloured tower-houses tumble down a narrow gorge to a tiny harbour wedged between rocks. Climb to the upper part of the village for the church of San Giovanni Battista (1340) and the castle ruins, where the coastal view is spectacular. In the evening, descend to the harbour to see the fishing boats hauled onto the concrete ramp - the atmosphere is particularly beautiful in the golden light.

Take the train (5 minutes) to Manarola, perhaps the most photographed village in Cinque Terre. Its pink, yellow and orange houses stack up on a rocky spur above a natural harbour. The most famous viewpoint is reached by following the path towards Corniglia: from there, you embrace the entire village with the sea as backdrop. Manarola is also the heart of Sciacchetrà production, a rare sweet wine from grapes dried on the terraces - if you find some, taste it, production is tiny.

Start the day with Corniglia, the only Cinque Terre village not at sea level - it's perched at 100 metres altitude, accessible via the Lardarina, a staircase of 382 steps from the station (or by shuttle bus, check timetables). Best to go in the morning, fresh and rested. Quieter than its neighbours, Corniglia is surrounded by vineyards and orchards. Via Fieschi, its main street, crosses the village end to end to a panoramic terrace overlooking the sea. It's the most 'authentic' of the five villages, with fewer tourists and more locals.

Train to Vernazza (7 minutes), often considered the jewel of Cinque Terre. It's the only village with a true natural harbour, bordered by a tiny sandy beach squeezed between houses. The medieval towers rising above the rooftops recall that Vernazza was a Genoese naval base in the 11th century. Climb to the Castello Doria (11th century), perched on the promontory - the circular view of the harbour, pastel houses and rocky coast is the iconic image of Cinque Terre. Descending, pass by the church of Santa Margherita d'Antiochia (1318), built on a rock above the sea.

Final village: Monterosso al Mare, the largest and most 'beach resort' of the five. It's divided into two parts: the old village (narrow streets, medieval Aurora tower, Capuchin convent housing a painting attributed to Van Dyck) and Fegina, the modern part, which has the only real sandy beach in Cinque Terre. On the promontory separating the two, look for the giant statue of Neptune (Il Gigante), 14 metres high, sculpted in reinforced concrete in 1910.

activities.text: Swim from the rocks at Riomaggiore or Manarola - there are no sandy beaches, but the water is crystal-clear and swimming spots are easily accessible. The best areas are the harbour ramps in both villages. Bring water shoes.

If the path is open, the Riomaggiore → Manarola section (the famous Via dell'Amore, often closed for works) is short and easy. Otherwise, take the train without regret - 5 minutes and you're there. In the evening, dine in Riomaggiore at one of the trattorias in the upper village, with views over the rooftops and sea.

The Vernazza-Monterosso path is the most spectacular section of the Sentiero Azzurro - about 2 hours' walking with climbs and descents through maquis, vertiginous panoramas over the sea and terraces. It's the most difficult section but also the most beautiful. Leave from Vernazza after your visit, you'll arrive at Monterosso in time for the beach.

Enjoy Fegina beach in Monterosso - it's the only place in Cinque Terre where you can lay your towel on sand. Parts of the beach are free access (at the eastern end, near Neptune), others equipped with deckchairs. Late afternoon, take the train back to Riomaggiore - the journey takes just 15 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria
From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria

places.name: Riva Del Garda

visits.text: Riva del Garda occupies the northern tip of Lake Garda, sheltered by cliffs on three sides. The Torre Apponale on Piazza III Novembre dates from 1220: standing 34 meters tall, it's topped with a copper angel blowing a trumpet, the town's emblem. Its 165 steps provide access to a plunging view over the medieval harbor and surrounding mountains. The square is surrounded by 14th-century arcades and framed by Palazzo Pretorio (1375, built by the Scaligeri) and Palazzo del Comune rebuilt by the Venetians in the 15th century.

La Rocca, a 12th-century fortress surrounded by a moat with a drawbridge, is Riva's oldest monument. Its position at the lake's edge, with its feet almost in the water, is unique in the region. Today converted into a museum (MAG Museo Alto Garda), it houses archaeological collections covering Prehistory and Antiquity, a picture gallery, and sections devoted to the region's history up to World War II. Works by Hayez and Vincenzo Vela are on display.

The Venetian Bastione, accessible on foot or by a panoramic elevator carved into the rock, is a fortress perched 160 meters up on the slopes of Monte Rocchetta. Built by the Venetians in the early 16th century to control the lake's northern access, it's now abandoned but its terrace offers a vertiginous view over Riva and the lake. Goethe, Nietzsche, Thomas Mann, and Kafka all stayed in Riva del Garda, drawn by the mild climate sheltered by the Alps.

The Castello Scaligero of Malcesine stands on a rocky spur rising 30 meters above the lake, on the Venetian shore. The main tower, pentagonal in shape, measures 31 meters at the base and rises 70 meters above lake level. The fortress changed hands among Lombards, Franks, Della Scala, Venetians, Visconti, French, and Austrians. Classified as a national monument since 1902, it now houses a natural history museum and a Goethe Room: in September 1786, the German poet stopped in Malcesine to sketch the castle, was taken for an Austrian spy by suspicious locals, and had to explain himself to local authorities—he recorded the episode in detail in his "Italian Journey."

The Palazzo dei Capitani, built by the Scaligeri in the 13th century and beautifully restored by the Venetians in the 15th century, was the residence of the lake captain charged with controlling trade in goods. Its classic Venetian architecture façade opens directly onto Malcesine harbor, one of the most picturesque on Lake Garda.

activities.text: The Strada del Ponale is a former service road carved into the rock in 1851 to link Riva to the Ledro valley. Closed to traffic in the 1980s after construction of a road tunnel, it's now one of the most spectacular hikes in the region: the track follows a cliff face plunging into the lake for 3 kilometers, with tunnels carved through the rock and 180° views over Lake Garda and the Trentino mountains. The starting point is at the western exit of Riva.

The Malcesine-Monte Baldo cable car, inaugurated in 1962, is the only one in the world whose cabin on the second section performs a complete 360° rotation during ascent—a world first put into service in 2002. In 15 minutes, it climbs to 1,760 meters altitude on Monte Baldo (2,218 m), nicknamed the "Garden of Europe" due to its some 3,600 plant species, about twenty of which are endemic. The panorama from the summit embraces the entirety of Lake Garda, the Brenta Dolomites to the north, and—on clear days—the Apennines to the south.

days_plan.itin_title: Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice

map_keywords: Rocca di Riva Riva del Garda
Piazza III Novembre Riva del Garda

places.name: Rocca Di Papa

visits.text: Monte Porzio Catone — A Castelli Romani village at 451 m with an astronomical observatory inaugurated in 1938, operated by INAF (National Institute for Astrophysics). The Cantina Sociale, a wine cooperative founded in 1934, is one of the most important producers of Frascati DOC, an appellation created in 1966.

Monte Compatri — A village at 576 m among the highest in the Colli Albani, with the 17th-century Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta and a 360° belvedere covering Rome, Lake Albano, and the Apennines. The historic center, sheltered from winds by a natural spur, preserves quiet medieval streets.

Rocca di Papa — A village at the foot of Monte Cavo, the 949 m summit of the Colli Albani and the ancient mons Albanus—sacred site of the Latins where the Feriae Latinae were held, annual festivals of the Latin federation presided over by Rome since the 6th century BC. A Roman-paved via sacra still climbs Monte Cavo's flank from Rocca di Papa. The historic center rises in an amphitheater on the slope with 17th-18th century tuff houses.

activities.text: Hiking up Monte Cavo on foot from Rocca di Papa — A 5 km round-trip trail on the Roman paved via sacra, passing through the Parco dei Castelli Romani's oak forest to the 949 m summit where a radio antenna occupies the site of the ancient Temple of Jupiter. Panoramic views over Rome, both volcanic lakes, and the sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Monte Porzio Catone
Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Monte Compatri

places.name: Rocca San Giovanni

visits.text: Rocca San Giovanni - a tiny hilltop village of 2,300 inhabitants perched at 155 meters, whose fortified center once protected the Benedictine abbey of San Giovanni in Venere, founded in the 7th century. The Piazza degli Eroi brings together the Palazzo Municipale and the medieval Torre Civica in a compact ensemble.

activities.text: Panoramic stop on the ramparts with views over the Adriatic Sea and the trabocchi of the Costa dei Trabocchi.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Piazza degli Eroi Rocca San Giovanni

places.name: Roma

visits.text: The Colosseum—Flavian Amphitheatre by its original name—is the largest amphitheater ever built in antiquity. Its construction began around 70–72 AD under Emperor Vespasian, on the site of Nero's Domus Aurea artificial lake, and was completed in 80 under his son Titus. Its dimensions: 189 meters long, 156 meters wide, 50 meters high, with an estimated capacity of 50,000 to 80,000 spectators. The outer walls combine three superimposed architectural orders: Doric, Ionic, Corinthian. Inside, a network of underground tunnels (the hypogeum) allowed gladiators and animals to rise directly into the arena through trapdoors. Used for gladiatorial combat, animal hunts, and executions until the 6th century, it subsequently served as a stone quarry—some of the stripped travertine blocks went into building St. Peter's Basilica.

The Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, included on the same ticket as the Colosseum, form the archaeological heart of ancient Rome. The Forum was the civic, religious, and commercial center of the Roman Republic and Empire for more than ten centuries, from the 7th century BC to the 7th century AD. The Palatine is the hill on which, according to tradition, Romulus founded Rome on April 21, 753 BC; it later became the residence of emperors, whose ruined palaces still occupy the entire hill. The Arch of Constantine, adjacent to the Colosseum, was erected in 315 to celebrate the emperor's victory over Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge in 312.

The Pantheon is the best-preserved ancient building in Rome. A first temple was built on this site in 27 BC by Marcus Agrippa, general and son-in-law of Augustus—his name still appears on the entrance frieze (M.AGRIPPA.L.F.COS.TERTIVM.FECIT). Destroyed by fire, it was entirely rebuilt by Emperor Hadrian between 118 and 125 AD. Its unreinforced Roman concrete dome, 43.3 meters in diameter, remains the world's largest in that material. Its interior height equals its diameter. The central oculus, 8.92 meters in diameter, is the only light source; the floor, slightly domed at the center, directs rainwater toward a peripheral drainage system. In 609, Pope Boniface IV transformed the temple into a church, which ensured its preservation. It houses the tombs of Raphael (died 1520) and the first two kings of unified Italy.

Piazza Navona is built on the site of Domitian's stadium (1st century AD), whose elongated form is still perfectly legible in the square's plan—276 meters long, 54 meters wide. Its three Baroque fountains were commissioned under Pope Innocent X: the Fountain of the Four Rivers (1651), Bernini's masterpiece whose four allegorical figures represent the Nile, Ganges, Danube, and Rio de la Plata, occupies the square's center.

The Trevi Fountain marks the terminus of the Aqua Virgo aqueduct, built in 19 BC by Marcus Agrippa to supply his baths—this 22-kilometer aqueduct still functions today. The monumental fountain was commissioned in 1730 by Pope Clement XII from architect Nicola Salvi, financed through a Vatican lottery draw, and officially inaugurated in 1762 by Clement XIII—Salvi had died in 1751 without seeing it completed. It measures 20 meters wide and 26 meters high. The coin-toss tradition, popularized by William Wyler's film Roman Holiday (1953) and Federico Fellini's La Dolce Vita (1960), generates about one million euros annually, donated to Caritas.

St. Peter's Basilica and Vatican Square constitute Catholic Christianity's largest architectural ensemble. The current basilica, built between 1506 and 1626 on the foundations of a 4th-century paleochristian basilica, mobilized the greatest Renaissance and Baroque architects: Bramante, Raphael, Sangallo, Michelangelo (who designed the dome, completed in 1590 by Giacomo della Porta after his death), Maderno, and Bernini, who also conceived the elliptical colonnade of the square (1656–1667). Michelangelo's dome, 42 meters in interior diameter, draws direct inspiration from Brunelleschi's in Florence. The Pietà, sculpted by Michelangelo between 1498 and 1499 when he was 23 years old, stands in the first chapel on the right.

The Vatican Museums gather one of the world's most important collections of art and antiquities, assembled over several centuries by popes. The Sistine Chapel, intended for papal ceremonies, owes its renown to the ceiling frescoes painted by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1512 at Pope Julius II's request—a four-year project during which the artist worked lying on scaffolding. The nine central panels depict Genesis, from back to entrance. The Last Judgment, painted on the back wall between 1536 and 1541 under Paul III, was created 24 years after the ceiling by a 61-year-old Michelangelo.

Villa Borghese, an 80-hectare park landscaped from 1606 for Cardinal Scipione Borghese, houses the Galleria Borghese, one of Rome's most concentrated museums. The cardinal had assembled his collection partly through legal seizures or pressure on artists—he forced Raphael to surrender the Deposition he'd just delivered to the original patron. The six ground-floor rooms gather Bernini sculptures including Apollo and Daphne (1622–1625) and Pluto and Proserpina (1621–1622), admirable for their technical virtuosity.

Piazza di Spagna owes its name to the Spanish Embassy to the Holy See that stood there since the 17th century. The monumental staircase linking the square to the Trinità dei Monti church—135 steps built between 1723 and 1726 thanks to a bequest from French diplomat Étienne Gueffier—is Rome's largest Baroque staircase. At the foot of the steps, the Barcaccia fountain (1627), attributed to Pietro Bernini, father of the famous Gian Lorenzo, represents a partially submerged boat referencing a Tiber flood.

activities.text: The Trastevere district, on the Tiber's right bank, is one of the rare sectors of Rome whose urban fabric has retained a medieval scale. Its cobbled lanes of sanpietrini (basalt paving stones) and lively evening squares make it a starting point for exploring popular Rome, far from the major archaeological sites. The basilica of Santa Maria in Trastevere, founded in the 4th century, is one of Rome's oldest churches and preserves 12th-century Byzantine mosaics.

Baroque Rome can be discovered on foot along an hour-long route linking the Pantheon to Piazza Navona then to Piazza Farnese and Campo de' Fiori—a lively morning market square with at its center the statue of philosopher Giordano Bruno, burned alive here in 1600 by the Inquisition. This part of Rome retains a density of Baroque palaces, fountains, and churches unmatched in Europe.

Castel Sant'Angelo, the former mausoleum of Emperor Hadrian built around 135 AD and transformed into a papal fortress in the Middle Ages, is connected to the Vatican by the Borgo's secret passage (the 'Passetto') that popes used in times of danger. From its summit it offers a 360-degree view of Rome and the Tiber. The Sant'Angelo Bridge leading to it, adorned with ten angels bearing the instruments of the Passion sculpted from Bernini's models around 1669, is one of Rome's most successful Baroque axes.

The Via Appia Antica, the first major Roman road built in 312 BC to link Rome to Brindisi, is lined at its exit from Rome with ancient tombs, Christian catacombs, and pine groves.
