Renting a bike at Porta San Sebastiano and riding the first few kilometers on the original basalt paving stones—some dating from the Republican era—gives a sense of what these roads meant for the Roman Empire's cohesion.

places.name: Ronciglione

visits.text: Historic center of Ronciglione - Named "Borgo dei Borghi" by RAI television, this town of 8,500 inhabitants blends medieval and Renaissance architecture on a volcanic spur at 441 meters elevation. Climb Corso Umberto I (nicknamed "Montecalvo")—the steep alleys reveal palaces of the Farnese family, who transformed Ronciglione into a prosperous industrial hub with ironworks, paper mills, and armories along the Rio Vicano.
Fontana Grande - Attributed to architect Antonio Gentili da Faenza (school of Vignola), this monumental fountain marks the passage between the lower Renaissance town and the upper medieval quarter.

Museo delle Ferriere (Ironworks Museum) - Housed in the town's oldest forge, this museum traces Ronciglione's industrial epic. From the 15th century onward, 15 forges operated along the Rio Vicano. The last one closed only in 1992. The building preserves the hydraulic mechanisms that transformed water power into energy.

Cathedral of San Pietro e Santa Caterina - The cathedral dominates the main square. Its baroque interior houses 17th-century paintings. In 1728, Pope Benedict XIII elevated Ronciglione to the rank of "Città" (city).

activities.text: Lake Vico circuit - This volcanic crater lake, formed approximately 100,000 years ago, is Italy's highest and one of its best-preserved. Protected as a nature reserve since 1982, it shelters herons, falcons, and tortoises. According to legend, Hercules planted his club in the ground; when he pulled it out, water gushed forth and created the lake. Marked trails, equipped beaches, and picnic areas line the shores.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio

map_keywords: Duomo San Pietro e Santa Caterina Ronciglione

visits.text: Rosazza is a village of about a hundred inhabitants in the upper Cervo valley (province of Biella), carved from local syenite. It owes its fame to Federico Rosazza Pistolet (1813-1899), Kingdom senator, Mazzinian philanthropist, and Grand Master of Biellese freemasonry. After the deaths of his wife and daughter, he devoted the last thirty years of his life to architecturally refounding his native village, in collaboration with painter-architect Giuseppe Maffei, according to an aesthetic blending Masonic symbolism and romantic neo-Gothic style. Today Rosazza is twinned with Rennes-le-Château in France.

Decipher the symbols carved into every building: compasses and squares, five-petaled roses (lodge emblem), hourglasses, five-pointed stars, and a swastika - a Neolithic cosmic symbol used by freemasons - adorn the facade of the parish church of Saints Peter and George, built between 1876 and 1880 on a former cemetery. Its interior ceiling is painted midnight blue and scattered with thousands of stars, like an invitation to spiritual elevation. The church connects to the village by a bridge decorated with crosses and five-pointed stars.

Federico Rosazza's neo-Gothic castle, completed in 1899 the year of his death, is a labyrinth of esoteric references with its false romantic ruins, high tower, and moss-covered walls - a composition seemingly extracted from a fantasized medieval tale. The monumental cemetery, accessible via a three-arch stone bridge adorned with hourglasses, houses the Rosazza family tombs in a setting of crypts and Masonic funerary symbolism. The Palazzo Comunale, where Rosazza held his lodge meetings, still houses period decor.

activities.text: From Rosazza, panoramic road to the Sanctuary of Oropa (one of Italy's most important Marian sanctuaries, at 891 meters) via a road built between 1889 and 1898 by Federico Rosazza himself, passing through a 367-meter gallery dug by hand. Hike to the alpine pastures and Alpe Desate.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: From Maratea, reach the western slope of Pollino National Park (about 1 hour 15 minutes of mountain driving). With its 192,000 hectares spread between Basilicata and Calabria, it's Italy's largest national park. The massif culminates at Serra Dolcedorme at 2,267 meters. Base yourself in Rotonda, a small gateway town to the park—it has nothing touristy in itself, but it's the best base camp for exploring Pollino, with accommodations, restaurants, and the visitor center.

Drive to San Costantino Albanese (30 minutes from Rotonda), one of the last Arbëreshë (Albanian) villages in Basilicata. Founded in the 15th century by Albanian refugees fleeing the Ottoman invasion, it still preserves the language, traditional costumes, and Byzantine rite. The church of the Madonna della Stella celebrates its services in Old Albanian. During certain festivals, inhabitants wear gold-embroidered costumes centuries old.

Explore Pollino's eastern slope. Drive to Terranova di Pollino (30 minutes from Rotonda), a lost village at 926 meters altitude, departure point for trails to the summits. The medieval village, clinging to the rock, has preserved its stone alleyways and mountain water fountains. It's one of Basilicata's least populated municipalities—you'll encounter practically no one.

Descend to Viggianello, one of Pollino's "Most Beautiful Villages of Italy." The ancient Castrum Byanelli, mentioned in 1079, preserves a Sanseverino castle transformed into a noble residence in the 16th century. The centro storico's alleyways, lined with Byzantine churches (including San Sebastiano, remodeled in the 15th century) and noble palaces, offer a concentrate of Lucanian medieval architecture. The surrounding beech and fir forests are among the densest in southern Italy.

activities.text: In the afternoon, hike toward the loricato pines (Pinus heldreichii), Europe's oldest trees. Some Pollino specimens are over 900 years old—twisted trunks, bleached by sun and wind, growing on ridges between 1,800 and 2,200 meters altitude. The trail from Piano Ruggio (1,550 m) is the most accessible: allow 2 to 3 hours round trip to reach Serra di Crispo, nicknamed the "Garden of the Gods." From the summit, the view simultaneously embraces the Tyrrhenian coast to the west and the Ionian coast to the east.

If you're athletic, descend the Raganello Gorges, on Pollino's Calabrian side (1 hour drive from Rotonda, via Civita). This 17 km canyon, up to 700 meters deep in places, is traversed by canyoning with a licensed guide. The lower route (3 hours, accessible to beginners) crosses passages of crystal-clear water between vertiginous limestone walls. Civita, the Albanian village above the gorges, is worth a stop for its architecture and panoramic bridge suspended over the void.

For a more contemplative end to your trip, opt for a walk in the beech forests of Pollinello or a horseback ride in the high-altitude meadows. Several agriturismi around Rotonda and Viggianello offer equestrian outings. In the evening, taste the peperone di Senise IGP (dried pepper, base of Lucanian cuisine) and Pane di Matera IGP.

days_plan.itin_title: Basilicata: From Matera to Pollino, Castles, Ghost Towns, and the Lucanian Dolomites
Basilicata: From Matera to Pollino, Castles, Ghost Towns, and the Lucanian Dolomites

visits.text: Discover Rovigo, a provincial capital often overlooked in favor of Venice and Verona but rich with unexpected treasures. Torre Donà, at 66 meters, remains one of Italy's tallest medieval brick towers - a remnant of the city's 10th-century castle that once dominated the landscape.

Visit La Rotonda (Church of Madonna del Soccorso), designed by a pupil of Palladio to house a miraculous image of the Madonna. The church's entire interior is covered with remarkable 17th-century frescoes creating an immersive artistic experience.

Explore the Pinacoteca dei Concordi in the Renaissance Palazzo Roverella, housing originals by Tiepolo, Giovanni Bellini, and Palma the Elder. The archaeological collection and temporary exhibitions add depth to this cultural visit.

activities.text: Wander the historic center admiring remnants of medieval fortifications including Porta San Bortolo, Torre Grimani, and Torre Pighin. The main Piazza Vittorio Emanuele II offers beautiful buildings for photography, especially during the peaceful afternoon riposo hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Sabbioneta - Renaissance ideal city built from nothing between 1554 and 1591 by Vespasiano Gonzaga (1531-1591), inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2008 alongside Mantua. Vespasiano, a prince and condottiere in the service of Philip II of Spain, conceived his city according to the humanist theories of Alberti and Vitruvius - orthogonal plan, hexagonal fortifications spanning 2.5 kilometers, monumental public buildings designed for a city of 10,000 inhabitants that today numbers only 4,000.

Palazzo Ducale - palace of Vespasiano Gonzaga built between 1568 and 1578, featuring the Hall of Rivers decorated with allegorical frescoes and a cycle of full-length portraits of the Gonzaga sovereigns. The gilded hall, with its golden coffered ceiling, served as the throne room.

Teatro Olimpico di Sabbioneta - one of Europe's first permanent covered theaters, built by Vincenzo Scamozzi in 1590 on commission from Vespasiano, with a capacity of 250 spectators. The antique-style interior decoration - colonnaded loggias, painted friezes - ranks among the best-preserved from the late Renaissance. Not to be confused with Palladio's Teatro Olimpico in Vicenza (1580).

Galleria degli Antichi - 97-meter-long gallery built in 1584 by Vespasiano to house his collection of antiquities and sculptures, one of the first museum galleries in European history. The 97-meter length was calculated to allow cavalry exercises indoors during inclement weather.

Chiesa dell'Incoronata - octagonal church built between 1586 and 1588 to house the mausoleum of Vespasiano Gonzaga, with a bronze equestrian statue of the founder (1588) and frescoes by Bernardino Campi. The octagonal form, symbolizing resurrection, draws direct inspiration from Florence's baptistery.

activities.text: Walk along Sabbioneta's ramparts - the 2.5-kilometer hexagonal fortifications with five bastions and two gates (Porta Vittoria and Porta Imperiale) feature an accessible walkway offering views across the Po plain.

places.name: Salerno

visits.text: Vietri sul Mare - a town of 8,000 at the eastern gateway to the Amalfi Coast, the historic center of majolica ceramic production since the 16th century. Potter workshops produce tiles, tableware, and decorative objects in glazed terracotta featuring fish, lemons, and maritime scenes. The Museo della Ceramica, housed in Villa Guariglia in Raito, displays pieces from the 16th to 20th centuries including a collection of ceramics made by German artists who took refuge in Vietri in the 1930s.

Cattedrale di San Matteo in Salerno - a cathedral built between 1076 and 1085 by Robert Guiscard, the Norman warlord who made Salerno his capital. The crypt houses the relics of Saint Matthew the Evangelist, brought from the East in 954. An atrium lined with 28 ancient columns salvaged from Paestum precedes the facade. The cathedral witnessed the coronation of numerous Norman princes and sheltered Pope Gregory VII in exile in 1084.

Scuola Medica Salernitana - Western Europe's first secular medical school, active from the 9th to 13th centuries. Legend attributes its founding to four masters from different traditions: Greek, Latin, Arab, and Hebrew. The Regimen Sanitatis Salernitanum, a preventive medicine treatise written between the 10th and 12th centuries, was translated into 16 languages and remained Europe's standard medical manual until the 1600s. The Museo Virtuale della Scuola Medica Salernitana traces the institution's history.

Castello di Arechi - a 6th-century Byzantine fortress perched at 860 feet overlooking the Gulf of Salerno, expanded by the Lombard prince Arechi II in the 8th century who made it the residence of the Principality of Benevento. The square towers and ramparts, preserved along 2,600 feet, offer 180-degree views over the Gulf of Salerno and the Picentini Mountains.

activities.text: Strolling Salerno's Lungomare Trieste - a palm-lined 2-mile waterfront promenade, redesigned in 2008 and regularly cited among southern Italy's most beautiful.
Visiting Salerno's covered market in the centro storico - a 19th-century food hall with local producers selling mozzarella, Cetara anchovies, citron, and limoncello from the Amalfi Coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages of the Amalfi Coast: From Praiano to Salerno

map_keywords: Museo della Ceramica Vietri sul Mare
Museo Virtuale della Scuola Medica Salernitana

places.name: Salina

visits.text: Salina, the greenest Aeolian island with its twin extinct volcanic cones — Monte Fossa delle Felci (962 m) and Monte dei Porri (860 m) — takes its name from ancient salt pans. Its dense vegetation of capers, vines, and fig trees contrasts sharply with neighboring islands' arid landscapes.

The Pollara cove on the northwest coast served as the natural set for Michael Radford's film Il Postino (The Postman, 1994). Its volcanic tuff cliffs plunge into emerald waters, creating one of the Mediterranean's most stunning natural amphitheaters.
Lingua village in the south is the production center for Salina IGP capers — considered Italy's finest by top chefs — cultivated on volcanic terraces since antiquity.

Monte Fossa delle Felci (962 m) is the Aeolian Islands' highest peak. The ascent from Santa Marina through vineyards and giant ferns (felci) takes 2 hours to reach a 360° panorama of all seven islands. On clear days, the Calabrian coast appears on the horizon.

activities.text: Tasting granita alle more di gelso (mulberry sorbet) and Malvasia delle Lipari DOC at Santa Marina's cafés — specialties exclusive to Salina.
Swimming at Rinella, the black sand beach in the village of the same name on Salina's south coast, accessible by local bus from Santa Marina.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria
Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

map_keywords: Santuario della Madonna del Terzito Salina

visits.text: Mondaino - This fortified village conceals a rare architectural curiosity: a semicircular arcaded square (Piazza Maggiore), shaped like a half-moon, designed in the 18th century. Surrounded by brick porticoes, it served as a covered market and gathering place. The Rocca Malatestiana, at the summit, offers a viewpoint over the wooded hills of the Valconca. The small Paleontological Museum displays marine fossils from the Miocene and Pliocene found in the surrounding hills.

Montegridolfo - This tiny fortified village, enclosed by almost intact walls, can be visited in a few minutes but concentrates a dense charm: flowered lanes, clock tower, ochre stone walls, and open views over the hills. The Museum of the Gothic Line, installed in a former church, traces the passage of the German defensive line Gothic Line in 1944 through objects, photographs, and World War II testimonies.

Saludecio - Perched at 343 meters altitude, this walled medieval village has become an open-air museum: colorful murals painted on façades represent the great inventions of the 19th century (the telephone, the vaccine, cinema, the automobile). The Chiesa di San Biagio, the parish church, preserves sacred art works. The Torre dell'Orologio marks the entrance to the historic center.

activities.text: Circuit among the three villages - The rolling road linking Mondaino, Montegridolfo, and Saludecio crosses landscapes of hills cultivated with olive groves and vineyards, with panoramas over the Romagna coast on one side and the ridges of the Montefeltro on the other. Total distance of about 25 km.

Tasting olive oil and Sangiovese - The hills between Saludecio and Mondaino produce a full-bodied, fruity Sangiovese di Romagna DOC. Farms offer tastings combining local extra virgin olive oil, formaggio di fossa (cheese aged in pits since the Middle Ages), and red wine.

days_plan.itin_title: Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Saluzzo served as capital of an independent marquisate for over three centuries (founded by Manfred I around 1125, annexed to France in 1548, then to Savoy in 1601 by the Treaty of Lyon). Its medieval historic center climbs cobbled lanes toward the Castiglia, the marquises' fortress built in 1270 and radically remodeled in 1492 by Ludovico II for the arrival of his bride Marguerite de Foix. The 55-meter circular tower dominates the entire plain to Monviso. The castle houses two museums unique in Italy: the Museum of Chivalric Civilization and the Prison Memory Museum. The civic tower of 1462 and its 14th-century ramparts are remarkably preserved.

Casa Cavassa is a 15th-century Renaissance palace, residence of the Cavassa family in service to the marquises. Its interior courtyard features wall frescoes, wooden balconies running the full width of facades, and coffered ceilings. It houses the Civic Museum with the Labors of Hercules in grisaille by Flemish painter Hans Clemer and the Madonna della Misericordia. The pediment bears the French motto 'droit quoi qu'il soit,' trace of the period of French rule.

The Abbey of Staffarda, 9 km from Saluzzo in the commune of Revello, is one of Italy's most complete medieval monastic complexes. Founded in 1135 by Manfred I on marshland given to the Cistercians, it was for three centuries a major agricultural and commercial center—the monks drained 1,300 hectares and created a covered market whose Gothic arcades survive. The Romano-Gothic church with three naves alternates gray stone and red brick; each pillar differs from the others. The high altar preserves the Polyptych of Pascale Oddone (1531), eight ovals of gilded and polychrome carved wooden statues. In 1690, French General Catinat took the abbey during the Battle of Staffarda, burning the archives and refectory.

Castello della Manta, 5 km south of Saluzzo, is a 13th-century fortified house transformed into a lordly residence around 1420 by Valerano di Saluzzo, son of Marquis Thomas III. Its baronial hall is covered with a late Gothic fresco cycle depicting the nine Heroes and nine Heroines in near life-size, with the Fountain of Youth—a rare example of early 15th-century secular painting. Belongs to FAI (Italian Environment Fund).

activities.text: Stroll through Saluzzo's medieval center: from Piazza Castello, climb to the castello via cobbled lanes with views of Monviso. Pass through Piazzetta dei Mondagli and the birthplace of Silvio Pellico, poet and patriot whose My Prisons inspired Risorgimento revolutionaries.

Wine tasting from the Marquisate: Pelaverga, Quagliano, and Chatus, native grape varieties from the Saluzzo hills in DOC Colline Saluzzesi. Accompany with tomini (small valley cheeses) and local hazelnut gianduja.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days
Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

places.name: San Casciano Bagni

visits.text: Etruscan-Roman Sanctuary of Bagno Grande: Excavations conducted from 2019 uncovered in November 2022 more than twenty-four bronze statues dated from 200 BC to 100 AD, perfectly preserved in the mud of thermal springs—including five nearly a meter tall representing Apollo, Hygieia, and matrons. With over 5,000 pieces in gold, silver, and bronze, Jacopo Tabolli's team (University for Foreigners of Siena) called this discovery 'the most important since the Riace bronzes in 1972.' The sanctuary, founded in the 3rd century BC by the Etruscans, was developed by the Romans under the name Balnea Clusinae. The thermal water (42°C) preserved the Etruscan and Latin inscriptions of donor families.

Archaeological site of Bagno Grande: The bronzes from the 2022-2024 excavations are circulating in a traveling exhibition (Quirinale, Naples, Reggio Calabria, Berlin in 2025) while the permanent museum—planned in a 16th-century palace in the historic center—is under construction, with a first inauguration expected in late 2026. Meanwhile, the site itself is visitable via guided tours organized every Saturday in summer by the municipal archaeological group, departing from the Stanze Cassianensi at the Palazzo Comunale.

activities.text: Thermal baths: The springs of San Casciano dei Bagni have been known since the 9th century BC and are still used today for their therapeutic properties. Modern thermal establishments welcome visitors year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

places.name: San Daniele Del Friuli

visits.text: Centro storico di San Daniele del Friuli - Perched on a hill between the Carnic mountains to the north and the Adriatic to the south, this borough owes its fame to prosciutto DOP but conceals a far richer heritage. The position creates a unique microclimate: warm sea breezes meet cold Alpine currents, generating the gentle ventilation that cures the ham during its 13-month aging.

Biblioteca Guarneriana - Founded October 7, 1466 when humanist Guarnerio d'Artegna bequeathed his 173 manuscript codices to his community, this is Friuli's oldest public library and one of Italy's first. The idea was revolutionary: at that time, libraries were private collections. It now holds about 12,000 antique books, including a 12th-century Byzantine Bible, one of the oldest Florentine copies of the Divine Comedy (late 14th century), illuminated Books of Hours on calfskin vellum, and a wooden room with secret doors sheltering once-forbidden texts.

Chiesa di Sant'Antonio Abate - Nicknamed the "Sistine Chapel of Friuli," this single-nave church was consecrated in 1308 as the hospital chapel, damaged in 1348, then reconsecrated in 1470. Its walls are covered with three fresco cycles: those by Vitale da Bologna (late 14th century) and especially those by Martino da Udine, known as "Pellegrino da San Daniele," painted between 1497 and 1522. The choir vault presents Prophets and Evangelists, and the rear wall a large Crucifixion.

Portonat - Near Piazza Dante, this massive square-based tower (torrione) was erected around 1580, facing toward Gemona. It formed part of the borough's medieval defensive system and today offers an architectural landmark in the network of ancient lanes.

Ponte di Pinzano al Tagliamento - About ten kilometers south, this bridge spans the Tagliamento and offers one of the finest panoramas of the river. Flowing 170 km, the Tagliamento is considered Europe's last major natural braided river: its multiple channels trace gravel meanders bordered by riparian vegetation.

Castello di Villalta - On the heights between Fagagna and San Daniele, this medieval castle dominates the Friulian plain. Its towers and battlements emerge from the vegetation, offering a viewpoint over the surrounding moraine hills.

activities.text: Prosciutto di San Daniele DOP tasting - The borough's salumifici open their doors for tastings. The prosciutto is produced solely with Italian pork leg and sea salt, without preservatives, following centuries-old know-how. In 1563, during the Council of Trent, the Patriarch of Aquileia sent 12 San Daniele prosciutti to the prelates by mule. In 1797, Napoleon's officers plundered a large quantity along with the Guarneriana's treasures.

days_plan.itin_title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

places.name: San Felice Circeo

visits.text: Antica Norba — Archaeological site on a plateau in the Lepini Mountains, towering 1,640 feet above the Pontine plain with sheer cliffs. Roman colony founded in the 5th century BC, nicknamed 'the Republican Pompeii of Lazio.' Its cyclopean walls in opus polygonal (massive limestone blocks fitted without mortar) extend 1.5 miles and enclose 94 acres—among the best-preserved in ancient Italy. Four monumental gates: the Porta Maggiore (26-foot jambs, semicircular bastion) is the most spectacular, with walls standing up to 33-40 feet tall. Two acropolises (major and minor) housed temples to Diana and Juno Lucina (goddess of childbirth, bronze inscriptions found). In 81 BC, during the civil war between Marius and Sulla, the inhabitants sided with Marius. Besieged by Sulla's troops, they chose to burn their homes and kill each other rather than surrender—the 'House of Burnt Seeds' still bears traces of this conflagration. The city was never rebuilt. Free access.

San Felice Circeo — Village perched on the Circeo promontory (1,775 feet), which Greek mythology identifies as the island of the sorceress Circe (Odyssey, Book X). Homer describes Ulysses climbing to a 'lookout rock' to see the island surrounded by sea—the profile of Monte Circeo indeed resembles an island when viewed from the south. The acropolis of ancient Circeii preserves megalithic walls in opus polygonal at 985 feet elevation. The medieval borgo centers on the Torre dei Templari (13th century) in the main square. Parco Nazionale del Circeo (founded 1934, one of Italy's oldest) protects the lowland forest (one of Italy's last), coastal lakes (Paola, Caprolace, Monaci, Fogliano), dunes and the island of Zannone. Grotta Guattari, on the promontory's eastern flank, was sealed by a rockfall 53,000 years ago and rediscovered in 1939: a Neanderthal skull at the center of a stone circle, first interpreted as ritual cannibalism, later explained by hyena activity. In 2021, remains of 9 more Neanderthals were unearthed—one of Europe's most important sites for studying this species. Cave visitable a few times yearly by reservation (Soprintendenza/Pro Loco).

activities.text: Walk the paved streets of Norba between the two acropolises, explore the baths, cisterns and underground passages. Extraordinary view over the Ninfa oasis below and across the Pontine plain to the sea.

Climb to the Circeii acropolis for the megalithic walls and panorama. Possible excursion in the National Park: trail to Picco di Circe (summit 1,775 feet, 360° view) or walk in the mesophilic forest. Swimming at Cala dei Pescatori, the bay where legend says Ulysses landed.

days_plan.itin_title: Ulysses Riviera: Secret Gardens, Ancient Caves & Southern Lazio Islands

map_keywords: Torre dei Templari San Felice Circeo

places.name: San Fruttuoso

visits.text: Hike or boat crossing to the cove of San Fruttuoso, between sea and mountain.
Visit to the medieval abbey of San Fruttuoso, nestled by the water's edge.

activities.text: Swimming in the turquoise waters of the cove and relaxing on the beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters

map_keywords: Abbazia di San Fruttuoso San Fruttuoso
Baia di San Fruttuoso San Fruttuoso

places.name: San Ginesio

visits.text: Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta — founded in 1090 on an early Christian place of worship, considered the only true masterpiece of ornate Romanesque-Gothic architecture in Le Marche. The façade is divided in two registers: the lower in travertine with a richly carved portal, the upper in modelled fired brick (cotto) across five compartments, completed in the 15th century by master builder Enrico l'Alemano. The civic tower flanks the façade — note that this is not the campanile, which stands on the transept flank. Damaged in the 2016 earthquake and undergoing priority restoration. The square bears the name of Alberico Gentili (1552–1608), a jurist from San Ginesio at the court of Elizabeth I of England, considered one of the founding fathers of international law and author of the treatise De Jure Belli.

Porta Picena and Ospedale dei Pellegrini — the 14th-century Porta Picena is the main gateway, with a wide esplanade in front. The Ospedale dei Pellegrini (1295), built beside it in Romanesque style with a characteristic portico, once served as a staging post on the Rome–Loreto pilgrimage route. Its architecture remains clearly legible today.

Chiesa di San Francesco and Museo-Pinacoteca Gentili — the church (11th century) preserves a Giottesque fresco cycle in the Gothic apse, untouched by the earthquake. The Museo Gentili, in the adjacent former convent (now the town hall), brings together works from the 15th to 17th centuries: a canvas attributed to Ghirlandaio's workshop, Simone de Magistris' Pietà, the Pala della Battaglia tra i Ginesini e i Fermani by Nicola d'Ulisse (15th century), and communal parchments and manuscripts going back to 1199 — one of the best-preserved medieval archives in Le Marche. Partial closure post-earthquake: check access before visiting.

Colle Ascarano — the 'balcony of the Sibillini'. Via Capocastello leads to this viewpoint beyond the walls, where the eye sweeps from the Gran Sasso to Monte Conero, from the Sibillini to the Majella — and on clear days, all the way to the Adriatic. The hill's name recalls the betrayal of the 'Ascari' during a medieval siege, explained by a plaque at Porta Ascarana.

activities.text: Walk the mura di San Nicola — walls built in sandstone from 1308, completed 150 years later, with battlements and a walkway. Local tradition holds that Saint Nicholas braced his knee against the masonry to prevent it from falling during a siege.

Taste the Polentone di San Ginesio — oven-baked polenta served with meat ragù or sausage and pecorino cheese — and the award-winning Ciauscolo 'Il Campagnolo' from San Ginesio, which has claimed first prize at the national competition four years running.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta San Ginesio
Chiesa di San Francesco San Ginesio

places.name: San Leo

visits.text: Fortress of San Leo - Rising atop a limestone spur with vertical walls, this citadel has dominated the Valmarecchia from a position renowned as impregnable. A first Roman fortification already occupied the site. Between 961 and 963, King Berengar II of Italy withstood a siege here against Otto I of Saxony. The Malatesta and Montefeltro fought over the stronghold for two centuries; in 1441, the young Federico da Montefeltro retook it by a daring climb up the sheer walls. He entrusted its reconstruction to Sienese architect Francesco di Giorgio Martini, who designed circular bastions and crossfire adapted to firearms—a masterpiece of Renaissance military engineering.

Cagliostro's Cell - From 1631 onward, the fortress became a papal prison. Its most famous prisoner, Count Cagliostro (Giuseppe Balsamo, born in Palermo in 1743), alchemist, Freemason, and adventurer, was imprisoned here in 1791 after his condemnation by the Roman Inquisition. Initially held in a standard cell, he was transferred after an escape attempt to the 'cella del pozzetto': a dungeon of about 3 by 3 meters, accessible only through a trapdoor in the ceiling, with a single opening to the outside. He died there in 1795 of apoplexy.

Parish Church of Santa Maria Assunta - Considered the oldest place of worship in the Montefeltro, this small pre-Romanesque sanctuary reuses columns and capitals from Roman times. Its single, low, austere nave exudes an archaic atmosphere of contemplation.

Cathedral of San Leone - Built of cut stone in Lombard Romanesque style, this cathedral was consecrated to the patron saint Leone, who came from Dalmatia in the 3rd century to evangelize the region. The austere façade and cul-de-four apse dominate the main piazza. Dante Alighieri, struck by the vertiginous aspect of the place, cites San Leo in Canto IV of Purgatory: 'Vassi in Sanleo e discendesi in Noli...'

Piazza Dante and Historic Center - The small square is surrounded by the Palazzo Mediceo (1517–1523), the residence of the Severini-Nardini counts (13th–16th century), and the Palazzo Della Rovere. Together they form a condensed showcase of Montefeltro civic architecture. The sole access road to the village is carved into the rock—a feature that reinforced its impregnable character.

activities.text: Climb on foot to the fortress - From the piazza, a paved path of a few hundred meters climbs to the fort's entrance. The view from the ramparts embraces the Marecchia valley, wooded hills, and, on clear days, the Adriatic coast. Wear comfortable shoes.

Alchimia Alchimie Festival - Every late August, San Leo celebrates the memory of Cagliostro with a festival dedicated to mystery and the occult sciences: markets, street performances, lectures, and fireworks animate the village for three days.

days_plan.itin_title: Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages

map_keywords: Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta San Leo

places.name: San Luigi Dei Francesi

visits.text: The church of Saint Louis of the French (built 1518-1589), constructed by Domenico Fontana following plans by Giacomo della Porta, has served as France's national church in Rome since its consecration in 1589. Its Renaissance travertine façade bears the coat of arms of France and statues of Francis I (with the royal salamander) and Charlemagne. Inside, the fifth chapel on the left—the Contarelli Chapel—houses Caravaggio's three canvases painted between 1599 and 1602: the Calling of Saint Matthew, the Martyrdom of Saint Matthew, and Saint Matthew and the Angel. This was the painter's first major public commission in Rome and the official starting point of the Baroque. The initial version of Saint Matthew and the Angel was rejected by the patrons: the saint was depicted as an old peasant with dirty feet, deemed unworthy of an evangelist. The bearded figure at the back of the Martyrdom is likely a self-portrait of Caravaggio.

activities.text: Spot the French royal symbols on the façade: fleur-de-lis from the coat of arms, Francis I's salamander. The church also holds the tomb of Frédéric Ozanam, founder of the Society of Saint Vincent de Paul, and that of Blessed Pauline de Beaumont, erected by her lover Chateaubriand.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Luigi dei Francesi Roma

visits.text: In Gavinana, the Museo Ferrucciano honors Francesco Ferrucci, condottiere of the Florentine Republic killed here on August 3, 1530, in the final battle of the Florentine republican resistance against Charles V and the Medici. The house on the square—according to tradition, the very threshold where Ferrucci was finished off with dagger blows by Fabrizio Maramaldo as he lay mortally wounded and told him: "You kill a dead man"—was transformed into a museum in 1931. The enemy commander Filiberto di Chalon, Prince of Orange, also died that day beneath Gavinana's walls, struck by two arquebuses.

activities.text: The Ponte Sospeso delle Ferriere connects Mammiano Basso to Popiglio via a steel-cable footbridge inaugurated in June 1923: 227 meters long, 36 meters above the Lima torrent, only 80 centimeters wide. It was built on the idea of Vincenzo Douglas Scotti, director of the Mammiano Basso rolling mill, to allow workers from Popiglio to reach the factories in 3 minutes instead of a 6-kilometer detour. In 1990, the Guinness Book of Records certified it as the world's longest pedestrian suspension bridge—a title it held until 2006, when it was surpassed by Japan's Kokonoe Yume bridge.

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

visits.text: Rocca Guaita (First Tower) - The oldest and largest of San Marino's three towers, built directly on the rock of Monte Titano in the 11th century, without foundations. Encircled by the first of the city's three fortified walls, it houses an interior courtyard, the chapel of Santa Barbara, and former prisons. The view from the battlements sweeps across the Adriatic coast, the Romagna plain, and the Apennines.

Torre Cesta (Second Tower) - Erected in the 13th century at the highest point of Monte Titano (755 m), this pentagonal tower houses the Museum of Ancient Weapons: over 700 pieces (crossbows, swords, armor, firearms) from the Middle Ages to the 19th century. The path linking Guaita to Cesta, carved along the rocky ridge, offers vertiginous panoramas on both sides of the mountain.

Torre del Montale (Third Tower) - The smallest of the three, connected to Cesta by a woodland path. Not accessible inside, it contains a prison 8 meters deep called the 'fondo della torre' (tower pit). Its strategic role was crucial during wars against the Malatesta of Rimini. Large stones primitively stacked around its base testify to very ancient construction.

Piazza della Libertà and Palazzo Pubblico - The political heart of the Republic, this compact square overlooks the valley. The Palazzo Pubblico (1894), designed by Roman architect Francesco Azzurri and inaugurated with a speech by Giosuè Carducci on 'perpetual liberty,' is the seat of government and the Consiglio Grande e Generale. At the center of the square, the statue of Liberty in Carrara marble brandishes a lance shaped like a halberd (symbol of San Marino) and a crown adorned with the three towers. The square rests on ancient cisterns that once supplied the city with water.

Basilica of San Marino - Neoclassical church housing beneath the main altar the relics of the founding saint, rediscovered on March 3, 1586. The small adjacent Chiesetta di San Pietro preserves, according to tradition, the niches carved in the rock where Saint Marinus and his companion Saint Leo slept.

State Museum - On Piazza Titano, this museum gathers about 5,000 pieces: archaeology (including fine ancient Greek vases), paintings (Guercino, Pompeo Batoni, Elisabetta Sirani), and the Gothic goldwork jewel of the Domagnano Treasure, the only piece of this ensemble still preserved in the Republic (the others are scattered in the world's great museums).

activities.text: Path of the Three Towers (Passo delle Streghe) - The ridge path linking the three towers of Monte Titano (about 2 km) winds between cliffs, oak woods, and viewpoints. The 'Witches' Passage,' between Cesta and Montale, crosses steep rocky terrain. On clear days, the view extends to the Croatian coast.

La Cava dei Balestrieri - This pit carved in the rock at the foot of the ramparts still serves as a competition ground for San Marino's crossbowmen. Every year on September 3 (national holiday, anniversary of the Republic's foundation in 301 AD), a crossbow tournament in medieval costume takes place here.

days_plan.itin_title: Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages

places.name: San Marino Di Bentivoglio

visits.text: Parco di Villa Smeraldi - an 18th-century estate with an English-style landscape park and centuries-old trees. Home to the Museo della Civiltà Contadina, one of Emilia-Romagna's most important museums of rural life, with over 50,000 objects documenting agricultural work and artisan crafts across the Po plain from the 19th to the 20th century.

activities.text: Stroll the villa's landscape park among the centuries-old trees before continuing onward to Bentivoglio.

days_plan.itin_title: 10 Natural Wonders Around Bologna

map_keywords: Villa Smeraldi San Marino di Bentivoglio
Chiesa di San Marino di Bentivoglio

places.name: San Miniato

visits.text: The Rocca di Federico II of San Miniato, whose tower was erected between 1217 and 1221 (reconstructed in 1958 after bombing), is the borgo's emblem. From the summit, views carry across the Valdarno, the Volterra hills, the Apennines, and the sea. In the Middle Ages, the borgo was called San Miniato al Tedesco: Otto I of Saxony made it a center of imperial administration in 962, and Frederick II of Swabia in 1218 a tax collection point for all central Italy. Bishop Sigeric of Canterbury stopped here on the Via Francigena around 990, confirming its place among the route's historic stations.

The cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta and San Genesio, begun in the 12th century and elevated to cathedral status in 1622, presents a sober façade adorned with 26 ceramic bowls mainly of Tunisian origin (the originals are preserved in the Diocesan Museum). The Baroque interior houses several large altarpieces, including works by Francesco d'Agnolo known as Lo Spillo, Aurelio Lomi, and Cosimo Gamberucci. A tragic episode: on July 22, 1944, an American artillery shell killed 55 people gathered in the cathedral cemetery by German soldiers.

The Palazzo Comunale houses on the first floor the Sala delle Sette Virtù, the building's oldest room, decorated with frescoes by Cenni di Francesco di Ser Cenni: the Virgin nursing the Child surrounded by the four cardinal virtues and three theological virtues. The Palazzo del Seminario, on Piazza della Repubblica, is recognizable by its length and façade entirely painted with repetitive motif frescoes and Latin maxims.

The archaeological area of San Genesio, a few kilometers from the historic center, is one of the historic stages of the Via Francigena mentioned by Sigeric. Recent excavations have uncovered the foundations of an important medieval city, now an archaeological site open to visitors.

activities.text: White truffle hunting in the forests of San Miniato, with a guide and his dogs. The white truffle of San Miniato (Tuber Magnatum Pico) is one of Italy's most renowned. Local production represents about one-fifth of national production. The National White Truffle Fair has taken place the last three weekends of November since 1969; in 1954, a truffle weighing over 2 kg was offered to President Eisenhower.

Tasting of local products in the historic center: white truffle tagliatelle, mallegato (medieval charcuterie still produced by peasant families), spuma di gota di maiale (pink pork pâté). San Miniato is a Slow Food member.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province
The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Rocca di Federico II San Miniato
Duomo di San Miniato San Miniato
Area Archeologica San Genesio San Miniato

places.name: San Quirico Orcia

visits.text: Collegiate Church of Saints Quirico and Giulitta: One of the masterpieces of Romanesque architecture in Val d'Orcia, first mentioned in 712 during a dispute between the bishoprics of Siena and Arezzo. Its three portals are of different styles and periods: the main 12th-century Romanesque portal, supported by two columns on two lions with crocodiles in the architrave; the 13th-century Lombard portal attributed to Giovanni Pisano (Gothic inscription 'Iohes' in the spire); and a third Baroque one. Inside, a polyptych by Lucas Cranach the Elder offered by Frederick III of Germany in 1504.

Horti Leonini: An Italian garden created around 1581 by Diomede Leoni on land offered by Francesco I de' Medici. The diamond-shaped plan of the lower part, with its boxwood hedges and triangular parterres, is one of the rare Renaissance public garden achievements open to travelers—Leoni had wanted it accessible to Via Francigena pilgrims. At the center, a statue of Cosimo III de' Medici by Giuseppe Mazzuoli (1688). Above, a Roman-era double-headed Janus marks the boundary between the geometric garden and the wild ragnaia.

Palazzo Chigi Zondadari: Built in the second half of the 17th century by Cardinal Flavio Chigi, nephew of Pope Alexander VII, this palazzo faces the Collegiate Church and dominates Via Dante Alighieri. Seriously damaged during World War II, it now belongs to the commune.

Chapel of the Madonna di Vitaleta: Three kilometers southeast of San Quirico, at the end of a marked 800-meter dirt path, this small 16th-century chapel surrounded by cypresses is one of the most photographed images of Val d'Orcia. The cypress avenue is most evocative at sunrise.

activities.text: Festa del Barbarossa: Every last weekend in June, San Quirico commemorates the 1155 meeting between Emperor Frederick Barbarossa and Pope Adrian IV's emissaries. Medieval costume parades, flag throwing, and archery competitions.

Val d'Orcia hiking trails: San Quirico lies at the intersection of stage 35 and stage 36 of the Tuscan Via Francigena. The section toward Bagno Vignoni (4 km) runs along cypress fields and vineyards of the Orcia DOC appellation.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

Palazzo Chigi Zondadari San Quirico d'Orcia
Cappella della Madonna di Vitaleta San Quirico d'Orcia
