places.name: Sanremo

visits.text: Enter La Pigna through the Porta di Santo Stefano, a Gothic stone arch from the 14th century marking the boundary between the medieval town and the modern city. The quarter takes its name from its spiral form wrapping around the hillside, recalling the scales of a pine cone—a deliberately defensive design conceived after the 9th-century Saracen raids, ensuring that any invader entering the alleys would become lost without being able to advance. The Piazza della Cisterna, with its large underground medieval reservoir, shows how the quarter compensated for the absence of springs by collecting rainwater in a network of cisterns.

Climb to the Sanctuary of the Madonna della Costa, built in 1630 on the site of an earlier chapel, at the summit of the Pigna hill. The church was erected to celebrate Sanremo's liberation from the tutelage of Genoa's Doria family in 1361—an event the city still celebrates today. The Baroque interior houses wooden statues by Maragliano, paintings by Domenico Fiasella, and a 14th-century Madonna and Child. The facade is visible from the sea and has long served as a landmark for navigators.

Visit the Cathedral of San Siro, one of Sanremo's oldest churches, built in the 12th century in Romanesque style with round arches and an ashlar stone nave. It occupies the western edge of La Pigna, steps from the medieval quarter. In 1538, the Manara house at number 21 Via Palma hosted Pope Paul III, en route to Nice where he would sign a ten-year truce between Charles V and Francis I.

Spot the Saracen Tower, built between August and October 1550 in response to repeated attacks by Barbary corsairs. Cylindrical and slender in form, it was connected to the Pigna ramparts and its single vaulted interior room was occupied by guards during alerts. It now stands isolated from the rest of the quarter but remains visible from the base of the hill.

Admire the exterior of the Casino di Sanremo, designed by French architect Eugène Ferret and inaugurated on January 12, 1905 under the name Kursaal. Mussolini's 1927 decree officially made it a casino. Sanremo is one of only four Italian cities authorized to have one, alongside Venice, Campione d'Italia, and Saint-Vincent. Its Art Nouveau halls have welcomed figures like King Farouk of Egypt (a regular in the 1950s after his exile), King Gustav of Sweden, and Princess Grace of Monaco. Poet Francesco Pastonchi created the tradition of Literary Mondays there.

Visit the park of Villa Ormond, on Corso Felice Cavallotti. The villa was built in 1889 by Swiss architect Émile Réverdin for Michel Louis Ormond, a wealthy Swiss entrepreneur, after the 1887 earthquake damaged the foundations of the original villa purchased in 1875. The municipality bought the villa and park in 1928. The garden is organized into themed zones: the palm grove, the orangery, the ficus area, and a Japanese garden in homage to Atami, Sanremo's sister city. Empress Elisabeth of Austria and the Princes of Prussia were among the Villa's guests.

Visit Villa Nobel, on the same Corso Felice Cavallotti, 200 meters from Villa Ormond. Alfred Nobel chose Sanremo in the late 19th century for health reasons and to set up a laboratory. The Moorish-style villa dates from 1870. It was here that he drafted the 1895 will in which he bequeathed his entire fortune to the foundation that would create the prize bearing his name. The salons, study, and bedroom are preserved with original furnishings; the exhibition traces the technological evolution of the 19th century and the role of his inventions.

Walk to the Fort of Santa Tecla, near the old port. Built by the Genoese in just eleven months and inaugurated on March 12, 1756, this star-shaped fort was erected to suppress Sanremo's independence aspirations vis-à-vis the Republic of Genoa. It now hosts cultural exhibitions and events.

activities.text: Stroll through the lanes of La Pigna from Piazza Santo Stefano to the summit: take the Rivolte San Sebastiano, a covered alley with high, uneven steps, then Via Palma—the former main street of La Pigna where the most illustrious families lived. Push open the door of the Oratory of San Sebastiano, built after the 1502 plague and restored: the 1642 inscription on the entrance colonnade and the 18th-century interior frescoes are well preserved.

Evening walk along the Corso Imperatrice, named in honor of Tsarina Maria Alexandrovna who gifted the city with palm trees during a stay on the Riviera. These palms, some of which are the Tsarina's originals, still line this marble promenade adorned with a Spring statue by sculptor Vincenzo Pasquali. Steps away, the Russian Orthodox Church of Saint Basil, inaugurated on December 23, 1913 in the presence of the Tsar's ambassador, served Sanremo's Russian community, which then numbered a thousand people.

Bike rental and ride on the Pista Ciclabile Riviera dei Fiori, built on the former Genoa-Ventimiglia railway line relocated inland from the coast starting in 1998. The path follows the sea for 32 kilometers between Ospedaletti and San Lorenzo al Mare, passing through converted former stations and coastal tunnels. The first Sanremo-San Lorenzo al Mare section was inaugurated in 2009; the extension to Imperia was completed in 2025. Bike rental points available at Sanremo's former station.

Stroll along Corso Matteotti, Sanremo's main shopping street: on the sidewalk slabs, brass plaques engraved with the name of each Italian Song Festival winner since its creation. The Teatro Ariston, which has hosted the festival every year since the 1970s, faces this same street.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days
Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days

map_keywords: Pista Ciclabile della Riviera dei Fiori Sanremo

visits.text: Civic Museum of Sansepolcro — The Resurrection: Piero della Francesca was born in Sansepolcro around 1412 and died there on October 12, 1492. The Museo Civico, housed in the Palazzo della Residenza whose current structure dates from 1456, is the only museum in the world to hold more than three works by the master. The Resurrection — a 225 × 200 cm fresco from the 1460s — was painted directly on the wall of the audience hall where the town council sat. Aldous Huxley called it in 1925 "the greatest painting in the world." In 1944, a British officer, Anthony Clarke, remembering that essay, halted the order to bomb Sansepolcro, then believed occupied by German soldiers — saving the fresco. The Polyptych of the Misericordia (commissioned in 1445) is Piero's first documented work.

Historic center and cathedral of Sansepolcro: the town, founded according to tradition by two pilgrims returning from the Holy Land in the 9th century with relics of the Holy Sepulchre, retains the character of a Renaissance art city with its stone palaces and 14th–16th-century buildings. The cathedral, rebuilt between the 14th and 16th centuries, houses a 12th-century wooden crucifix and frescoes. The birthplace of Piero della Francesca, in present-day Via Aggiunti, can be glimpsed from outside.

activities.text: Palio della Balestra: a crossbow competition pitting Sansepolcro against Gubbio, held every second Sunday of September on Piazza Torre di Berta since 1594. In medieval dress, the balestrieri of the two rival towns compete at shooting a wooden target — one of the oldest crossbow tournaments in Italy.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

places.name: Sant Angelo In Grotte

visits.text: Grotta di San Michele Arcangelo: sanctuary carved into rock, a stop on the medieval pilgrimage route connecting Rome to the Gargano (Monte Sant'Angelo, Puglia). The walls preserve votive inscriptions and traces of rock paintings left by centuries of pilgrims.
Sant'Angelo in Grotte historic village: stone houses carved directly into the cliff face, stepped streets. The village grew around the grotto, which gave it both name and purpose.

activities.text: Walk the viewpoints surrounding the village: vistas over the deep valleys of Isernia province.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Grotta di San Michele Arcangelo Sant'Angelo in Grotte
Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Sant'Angelo in Grotte

places.name: Santa Cecilia Trastevere

visits.text: The basilica of Santa Cecilia in Trastevere is built on the house where, according to tradition, Cecilia—a 3rd-century Christian Roman noblewoman—suffered her martyrdom: she was locked for three days in the calidarium (steam bath) without perishing, then beheaded. In 1599, during the church's renovation, Cardinal Sfondrati had the saint's sarcophagus opened: the body was found intact, dressed in white, wounds to the neck visible. Stefano Maderno sculpted from Carrara marble an effigy reproducing the exact position of the body lying on its side—it's one of the most moving sculptures of the early Baroque. Pietro Cavallini's fresco of the Last Judgment (1293), hidden in the choir, was covered during a 1725 restoration and rediscovered only in 1900. Cavallini introduced a new realism in the faces and drapery that prefigured Giotto. In the crypt, excavations uncovered a 2nd-century domus and remnants of the baths of the ancient house.

activities.text: Access Cavallini's frescoes in the nuns' choir (paid entry, restricted morning hours): one of Rome's least known and most powerful medieval works.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Cecilia in Trastevere Roma

places.name: Santa Fiora

visits.text: The Pieve delle Sante Flora e Lucilla, a parish church built before the year 1000 and reconstructed in the 13th century, preserves one of the world's most important collections of glazed terracotta from Andrea della Robbia's workshop. The pieces, created between 1464 and 1490, include a Coronation of the Virgin, a Saint Francis Receiving the Stigmata, a Madonna della Cintola, and several polychrome altarpieces with the characteristic blue background. The reliquary of Saints Flora and Lucilla, jointly offered by the Sforza and Aldobrandeschi families, bears the arms of both houses and materializes the county's passage from one dynasty to the other in 1439.

The Palazzo Sforza Cesarini, erected in 1575 on the foundations of the Aldobrandeschi fortress whose two medieval towers still survive on Piazza Garibaldi, now serves as the town hall. Dante mentioned Santa Fiora in Purgatorio (VI, 111) when the Ghibelline county of the Aldobrandeschi was prey to pillaging—the plaque affixed to the square reproduces this verse. Since 2002, the palace has housed the Museum of Mercury Mines of Monte Amiata, with 22 panels documenting the technical and social history of mercury extraction, the dominant activity on the massif between the 19th and mid-20th centuries.

The church of Madonna della Neve, a small Renaissance building from the 15th century adjoining the Peschiera, contains a unique feature: its floor is a transparent glass slab beneath which flows the official source of the Fiora River. The river literally springs to life beneath visitors' feet. The interior frescoes, attributed to Francesco Nasini, and a glazed terracotta bas-relief by Della Robbia complete the ensemble.

activities.text: La Peschiera, a medieval basin redesigned in the 16th century by the Sforza for trout farming in the Fiora's waters, was transformed into an English garden in the 19th century. Art critic Cesare Brandi wrote that it alone justified the journey. From its shaded banks, the village walls reflect in the water. It's one of the most photographed sites in Grosseto province, virtually unknown outside Italy.

Hiking in the beech forests of Monte Amiata, an extinct volcano reaching 1,733 meters, the highest mountain in southern Tuscany. The massif is geothermally active—steam columns from geothermal plants (including Bagnore 3 and 4) punctuate the landscape. Marked trails depart from Santa Fiora toward the state forest of Selva.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

map_keywords: Pieve delle Sante Flora e Lucilla Santa Fiora
Chiesa della Madonna delle Nevi Santa Fiora

places.name: Santa Margherita Ligure

visits.text: Santa Margherita Ligure - Port and waterfront - Santa Margherita Ligure developed its tourist vocation from the 19th century when aristocrats from across Europe discovered its Gulf of Tigullio. The town was long known as Pescino before being renamed in 1863. Its marina, Belle Époque villas, and tree-lined promenade form the backdrop of a resort that maintains its elegance without Portofino's ostentation. The town is included in the Portofino Regional Natural Park, created in 1935.

Portofino - Piazzetta and harbor - Portofino, founded in the 10th century as a simple fishing port, became in the 19th century one of the most sought-after destinations on the Italian Riviera. Its Piazzetta, surrounded by pastel houses overlooking the horseshoe-shaped harbor, concentrates cafés, boutiques, and prestige yachts. Lord Byron swam in the bay. The Castello Brown, a medieval fortress renovated in the 16th century and purchased by British consul Montague Yeats Brown in the 19th century, offers the most complete panorama over the village.

Paraggi - Protected bay and beach - Nestled between Santa Margherita and Portofino, the bay of Paraggi is one of the rare sandy beaches on this Ligurian coast. Its clear, shallow waters, protected by the Portofino promontory, make it one of the most frequented swimming spots on the Riviera.

Camogli - Port and waterfront - Camogli is one of the most preserved fishing villages on the Ligurian Riviera. Its name reportedly comes from 'Casa dei Mogli' (house of wives). Its five- to six-story houses, painted in bright colors to be visible to sailors, overlook a lively harbor. The Basilica of Santa Maria Assunta, built directly at the water's edge on rocks, was rebuilt several times after storms. Camogli is known for the Sagra del Pesce, fish festival celebrated on the second Sunday of May since 1952, when thousands of sardines are fried in a giant 4-meter diameter pan.

Abbey of San Fruttuoso - Accessible only by boat or on foot, the abbey occupies a secret cove between Camogli and Portofino. Its foundation dates to the 7th century, when Greek monks fleeing the Muslim conquest of Hispania brought the relics of Saint Fructuosus of Tarragona. The current structure dates largely from the 11th century, built at the request of Adelaide of Burgundy, widow of Emperor Otto I. In the 13th century, the Doria family added the Gothic cloister and family tombs in black and white marble. A defensive tower was erected in 1562 to repel pirates. In 1954, the Christ of the Abyss statue was submerged at 17 meters depth—a 2.5-meter bronze created by Guido Galletti in memory of diver Dario Gonzatti who died here in 1947. The FAI has managed the site since 1983.

activities.text: Coastal trail Santa Margherita → Portofino via Paraggi - This 5.3 km trail follows the coast overlooking the sea, passing through the bay of Paraggi before descending to Portofino. Elevation gain of only 47 meters: one of the most accessible coastal routes on the Riviera. Local boats provide the return service.
Boat crossing Camogli → San Fruttuoso - Golfo Paradiso company boats connect Camogli to San Fruttuoso in about 30 minutes. The crossing follows the Portofino promontory.

Hike San Rocco di Camogli → San Fruttuoso - The trail descends through the holm oak forest of Portofino Natural Park to the bay of San Fruttuoso. The descent takes about 1h30 but the climb back is demanding—prefer the boat for the return.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages

map_keywords: Abbazia di San Fruttuoso San Fruttuoso

visits.text: Santa Severina appears from afar like a stone ship perched on a tufa cliff, overlooking the Neto valley. This village of 2,000 inhabitants, classified among the "Borghi più Belli d'Italia," bears the traces of more than a thousand years of history. Begin with the Castello, one of the best-preserved medieval fortresses in Calabria. Built in the 11th century by Robert Guiscard on an earlier Byzantine fortification, it features a square keep flanked by four cylindrical towers. The archaeological museum it houses displays artifacts ranging from the 3rd century BC to the Norman era - don't miss the skeleton with an ancient coin placed on the jaw, testimony to ancient funerary rites.

Right next to the castle, on Piazza Campo, the cathedral of Santa Anastasia (13th century) preserves its original portal despite numerous renovations. But the real treasure hides alongside: the Byzantine Baptistery, the only one still intact in Calabria. With a circular plan and four arms in a Greek cross, it preserves frescoes dating from the 10th to 12th centuries. This small edifice - discovered and studied by archaeologist Paolo Orsi in 1911 - takes you directly back to the era when Santa Severina was the Byzantine metropolitan seat in Calabria.

Wander through the lanes of the historic Grecìa and Iudìa quarters. The former, of Byzantine origin, clings to the panoramic spur with houses pressed against each other. The latter, the old Jewish ghetto, testifies to the coexistence of different cultures over the centuries. Push on to the church of Pozzoleo, in the Grecìa quarter - considered the last spark of Byzantine civilization in 12th-century Calabria.

activities.text: Enjoy the panoramic view from the castle ramparts at sunset - the Neto valley turns golden and you can glimpse the Ionian Sea in the distance. If you visit between May and June, you might attend the Medieval Festival, with extras in Norman costumes, jugglers, flag throwers, and tastings.

Taste sàuza, a spring fava bean soup with mint flavored with a drizzle of vinegar and served warm with pancetta. The aranciàru, a local orange variety with particular taste properties, is another emblematic village product.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea

places.name: Santa Teresa Gallura

visits.text: The Longosardo Tower, built under Philip II of Spain in the 16th century, is the largest Spanish tower in Sardinia. From its summit, the view extends to the white cliffs of Bonifacio 12 km away, the La Maddalena archipelago, and on clear days, all of Corsica. The town was founded in 1808 by Victor Emmanuel I of Savoy, who named it after his wife Maria Teresa.

The Capo Testa promontory, connected to the mainland by a narrow isthmus, is the ancient granite quarry from which the Romans extracted stone for their columns. Unfinished column shafts abandoned in the rock are still visible on site. The Valley of the Moon on the isthmus is a chaos of white granite blocks sculpted by wind into rounded, labyrinthine forms.
Rena Bianca Beach, right in the town center, is a 700-meter cove framed by granite rocks. A Blue Flag beach since 1987, its shallow waters allow direct views of the Corsican cliffs on the horizon.

activities.text: Ferry crossing to Bonifacio in Corsica (50 minutes), a town perched on white limestone cliffs directly across the strait. A day trip is possible from the port of Santa Teresa.
Hike the trails of Capo Testa among the granite blocks and maquis, with access to Rena di Ponente and Rena di Levante beaches, sheltered from winds depending on their orientation.
Sample Vermentino di Gallura DOCG, a dry white wine produced on the Gallura granites, at the waterfront restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Sardinia Coast: Beaches & Hidden Coves
Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

map_keywords: Torre di Longosardo Santa Teresa Gallura
Spiaggia Rena Bianca Santa Teresa Gallura
Porto di Santa Teresa Gallura Santa Teresa Gallura

places.name: Santarcangelo Di Romagna

visits.text: Rocca Malatestiana - Towering over the village from the summit of Colle Giove, this fortress was built in the 14th century by the Malatesta family and remodeled in the 15th century by Sigismondo Pandolfo Malatesta. The crenellated walls and square tower offer sweeping views across the Romagna plain to the Adriatic Sea. The rocca is now privately owned but open for guided tours.

Tufa Caves - Beneath the historic center lies a network of underground galleries carved into the calcareous tufa, whose origin remains debated: some archaeologists date them to Roman times (cisterns or silos), others to the Middle Ages (storage cellars). Part of the network is accessible by guided tour; the cool, constant temperature and rounded vaults create a unique atmosphere.

Parish Church of San Michele Arcangelo - One of the oldest Romanesque churches in Romagna, dating back to the 6th–7th century. It reuses ancient Roman columns and capitals. The simplicity of the single nave and semicircular apse contrasts with the medieval additions.

Historic Center - Sloping lanes climb from Piazza Ganganelli (with its neoclassical triumphal arch dedicated to Pope Clement XIV, a native son) toward the rocca. Ochre and brick-faced palaces, artisan workshops practicing mangano (printing on fabric with carved wooden blocks, a centuries-old technique still alive here), and shaded piazzas compose a village of real character.

activities.text: Wandering among palaces and artisan workshops - Santarcangelo's mangano is one of the last workshops in Italy still printing fabrics using rust-based dyes and hand-carved pear-wood blocks, a technique inherited from the 17th century. The production of fabrics with motifs of roosters, grape clusters, and foliage is a living craft tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages

map_keywords: Pieve di San Michele Arcangelo Santarcangelo

places.name: Santo Stefano Di Sessanio

visits.text: Santo Stefano di Sessanio - a village of 115 inhabitants at 1,251 meters altitude, built in concentric circles of gray limestone house-ramparts. The medieval defensive structure remains virtually intact: the outer houses themselves form the village perimeter, without a separate wall.

Torre Medicea - a 20-meter cylindrical tower, the village's symbol, erected in the 15th century under Medici rule from Florence, who controlled the town from 1579 to 1743. Collapsed during the 2009 earthquake, it was completely rebuilt and reopened to the public in 2014.

Palazzo Mediceo del Capitano - a 16th-century palace, residence of the Medici representative in the village. Now partially restored, it testifies to two centuries of Florentine domination over this mountain territory.

activities.text: Excursion to Campo Imperatore (2,130 m) - a 30-kilometer high-altitude plateau nicknamed the 'Little Tibet.' Here Mussolini was imprisoned in 1943 before being exfiltrated by German glider on September 12.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

map_keywords: Torre Medicea Santo Stefano di Sessanio
Palazzo Mediceo del Capitano Santo Stefano
Centro Storico Santo Stefano di Sessanio

places.name: Sarnano

visits.text: Piazza Alta — the highest point and original historic nucleus of the village, built entirely in cotto (fired brick) in an elliptical form. The square brings together all the buildings of medieval power: the Palazzo del Popolo (converted into the 150-seat Teatro della Vittoria in 1831, with three horseshoe tiers of boxes), the Palazzo dei Priori, the Palazzo del Podestà with its civic tower, and the Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta founded in 1265 with an even older campanile. A stone egg — an archaeological marker of Celtic origin and unknown purpose — sits beside the Palazzo del Podestà.

Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta — Romanesque façade, single-nave interior. The incised stone portal leads to 15th–16th-century frescoes, including the Incoronazione della Vergine, Bambino ed angeli musicanti by Lorenzo d'Alessandro, the master of Marchigian Renaissance painting. The 11th-century bell still rings today.

Pinacoteca Civica (former convent of Santa Chiara) — brings together works recovered from churches across the territory, including the Madonna adorante il Bambino con angeli musicanti by Vittore Crivelli (late 15th century), the polyptych Compianto sul Cristo deposto by Vincenzo Pagani (1529), the Crocefissione by Stefano Folchetti (1513), and the Madonna della Misericordia by Pietro Alemanno (1494). Partial closure since the 2016 earthquake: check access before visiting.

Chiesa di San Francesco — early 14th century, marking the memory of a halt made by Saint Francis between 1214 and 1216. Remodelled in the neoclassical style, it preserves in the adjacent former convent (now the town hall) the Biblioteca Comunale with ancient illuminated codices.

activities.text: Walk the spiral streets climbing toward Piazza Alta from the historic Porta Brunforte, retracing the layout of the original medieval castrum. Sarnano is built entirely in cotto: the warm colour of its rooftops, walls and paving stones shifts with the light throughout the day.

Try Sarnano's soft ciauscolo (spreadable pepper salami) with fresh pecorino, polentone baked with sausage and mushrooms, and the crostata al torrone sarnanese — a purely local speciality made with candied and dried fruits.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Sarnano

visits.text: Castello Manenti: Built in the 12th-13th centuries and restructured in the 15th century, the castle is surrounded by a double wall enclosing a square keep and round towers. It was a Sienese bastion throughout the Middle Ages. Francesco Todeschini Piccolomini was born in Sarteano in 1439—he became pope for less than a month in 1503 as Pius III, immediate successor to Alexander VI.

Civic Archaeological Museum: Housed in the 16th-century Palazzo Gabrielli (whose structure dates to the Middle Ages), this museum inaugurated in 1997 traces Sarteano's history from the 9th to the 1st century BC from the surrounding Etruscan necropolises. The lower floor reconstructs in life-size—a technique unique in Italy—the 4th-century BC Tomb of the Infernal Quadriga, whose frescoes depict the god Charun driving a chariot pulled by two lions and two griffins, followed by a three-headed serpent and a funeral banquet scene.

Pianacce Necropolis: One kilometer from the center, this necropolis discovered in 1954—with major revelations in 2003—contains 21 tombs carved into travertine. Visits to the original Tomb of the Infernal Quadriga are done only with the archaeological museum, which provides guides.

activities.text: Sarteano thermal baths: Freshwater thermal pools (23-28°C) are found in the Sarteano area, in a natural setting among vineyards and olive groves of Val di Chiana.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

visits.text: Piazza d'Italia is Sassari's monumental heart, bordered by the neoclassical Palazzo della Provincia (1872) and the Teatro Civico. Each year on the second-to-last Sunday in May, the Cavalcata Sarda unfolds here—a parade on foot and horseback born in 1899 during the visit of King Umberto I and Queen Margherita of Savoy.

San Nicola Cathedral layers five centuries of styles: the 13th-century Romanesque bell tower is the sole vestige of the original church, first mentioned in 1135. Fifteenth-century Catalan Gothic coexists with an 18th-century Baroque façade adorned with statues of Sassari's martyrs. Writer Elio Vittorini noted that "one would not want to remove a thing."

The Fontana di Rosello, built by the Genoese in 1606 on an older medieval fountain, is Sassari's most symbolic monument. Its bas-reliefs represent the four seasons and the four winds; water flows from twelve marble dolphins.

activities.text: Visit the G. A. Sanna Archaeological and Ethnographic Museum, one of Sardinia's most important, preserving nuragic objects, bronzes, Punic and Roman ceramics, and discoveries from the megalithic site of Monte d'Accoddi.
Excursion to the megalithic site of Monte d'Accoddi, 4 miles from Sassari: a great stepped altar dating from 2400 BC, without equivalent in Western Europe, whose form evokes Mesopotamian ziggurats.
Wander through the historic center along Corso Vittorio Emanuele, the main artery lined with Baroque palaces and 16th-century houses showing Spanish influence.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

visits.text: Saturnia rises on a travertine promontory at 294 meters elevation, near the remains of an Etruscan necropolis at Pian di Palma. The Porta Romana, dating from the 2nd century BC, is built over preexisting Etruscan defensive structures. In the village center, the Archaeological Museum of Saturnia preserves artifacts from local excavations spanning prehistory to the Roman era. According to mythology, the town's name derives from the god Saturn himself: angered at mankind, he struck the ground with his thunderbolt, causing the thermal spring to gush forth.

Montemerano, a hamlet of Manciano municipality 7 km from Saturnia, ranks among Italy's "Most Beautiful Villages." Its historic center, developed around the early 11th-century Aldobrandeschi fortress (of which a square tower survives), is a labyrinth of perfectly preserved paved alleys. The church of San Giorgio, built in the 13th century on Piazza del Castello, houses a polyptych by Sano di Pietro, a 15th-century Sienese painter, along with precious medieval frescoes.

activities.text: The Cascate del Mulino (Mill Waterfalls) are a system of natural travertine pools created by Saturnia's thermal spring. The water, laden with sulfur, bicarbonate, and calcium, gushes at 37.5°C with a flow of 800 liters per second and has carved into the rock tiered pools with turquoise waters. Used since Etruscan and then Roman antiquity—a square Roman pool is still visible near the church of Maddalena—these cascades are freely accessible 24/7, year-round. Arrive early morning to enjoy the pools before the summer crowds.

Tasting of Morellino di Scansano DOCG wines in the cellars between Saturnia and Montemerano: this Sangiovese-based red produced in the Grosseto hinterland is one of the most renowned Tuscan reds outside the Chianti circuits.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

visits.text: Scalea - a medieval town on a rocky promontory overlooking the sea, with an 11th-century historic center preserving stepped lanes, archways, and a 12th-century Norman tower. The modern lower town, developed from the 1960s onward, contrasts sharply with the virtually abandoned hilltop old town, which has been undergoing gradual restoration since the 2000s.

activities.text: One last swim on Scalea's beaches before continuing to Tropea and the Calabria circuit - the coast between Scalea and Paola offers another 60 kilometers of fine sandy beaches before the shoreline turns rocky.

days_plan.itin_title: From Naples to Calabria: The Tyrrhenian Coast

visits.text: Scanno - a village of 1,800 inhabitants at 1,050 meters, photographed since the 1950s by Henri Cartier-Bresson, Mario Giacomelli, and Josef Koudelka, who saw in it a peasant world of rare visual coherence. The historic center preserves 17th-century carved portals, noble palaces, and Renaissance fountains.

Museo della Lana - a museum housed in an 18th-century palace, tracing two centuries of local textile industry. Scanno was a wool cloth production center until the early 20th century, exporting to Naples and Rome.

Lago di Scanno - a heart-shaped natural lake as seen from the SS479 road belvedere, formed 12,000 years ago by a landslide that blocked the Tasso valley. At 1,000 meters altitude, its waters reach 32 meters deep.

activities.text: Climb to the Scanno panoramic viewpoint on the SS479 - aerial view of the heart-shaped lake, 10 minutes on foot or by car from the village.

Hike through the Gole del Sagittario - 3-kilometer limestone gorges between Scanno and Anversa degli Abruzzi, traversed by a narrow road along the torrent. Habitat of the griffon vulture reintroduced in 2000.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Scarperia

visits.text: The Palazzo dei Vicari has dominated Scarperia's main square since the town's foundation by the Florentine Republic on September 7, 1306. Conceived as a military outpost on the only road between Florence and Bologna, it became in 1415 the seat of the Florentine Vicariate administering all of Mugello. The facade bears the stone and Della Robbia majolica coats of arms of all the vicars who served here. Inside: a fresco from the school of Ghirlandaio (1501) depicting Saint Barnabas offering Scarperia to the Virgin surrounded by Florence's patron saints, and the ancient bell tower clock documented as the work of Brunelleschi—the only proof of his activity in clockmaking.

The Museum of Cutting Blades (Museo dei Ferri Taglienti), installed in the Palazzo's north wing restored in 1999, traces 700 years of knife-making in Scarperia. Since the town's founding in 1306, its strategic position on the trans-Apennine route ensured the spread of local knives throughout Europe. By the 15th century, Scarperia's reputation was international. In 1908, a law prohibiting knives exceeding the palm of the hand nearly ruined the craft; it survived and has experienced a revival since the 1990s. The museum presents the different phases of production—forging, tempering, sharpening, handle assembly.

activities.text: Visit an artisan knife workshop in the historic center. Several independent cutleries offer demonstrations of forging and assembly using traditional Mugello techniques.

The Palio del Diotto, every September 8: the town's neighborhoods compete in Renaissance costumes on the main square, celebrating the Nativity of the Virgin. The evening before features Renaissance Days with processions, falconers, and fire spectacles.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Oratorio della Madonna dei Terremoti Scarperia

visits.text: Scilla is one of the most photogenic villages in all of Italy. Clinging to a rocky spur facing the Strait of Messina, it takes its name from the sea monster Scylla of the Odyssey. The Chianalea quarter, nicknamed the "Little Venice of the South," is a maze of colorful houses whose foundations plunge directly into the sea. Stroll in the morning, when fishermen repair their nets and the light is soft - the narrow lanes lead to tiny fish restaurants built over the water.

At the promontory's summit, Castello Ruffo dominates the strait. This fortress, whose origins date back to the Norman era, offers vertiginous views over Sicily, Messina, and Marina Grande beach below. The castle's halls host temporary exhibitions. Descending, you can see the feluccas - these large traditional boats with a watchtower - still used for swordfish fishing in the strait.

activities.text: Marina Grande beach, a long strip of pebbles and sand at the castle's foot, is the perfect spot for a swim with a view of Sicily. The water is deep and crystal clear, the strait's currents bring rich marine life - diving enthusiasts will find interesting spots.

Dine in Chianalea, in one of the restaurants literally built over the water. Swordfish (pesce spada) reigns here - grilled, in involtini, or alla ghiotta (with tomato and capers). Scilla is a village best experienced in the evening, when the houses' lights reflect in the sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea
Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

visits.text: Saepinum (Altilia): 12-hectare Roman city enclosed by limestone walls with four monumental gates topped by circular towers. Founded 1st century BC on a Samnite site captured in 293 BC during the Third Samnite War. Cardo-decumanus layout that precisely follows the ancient tratturi—grassy transhumance paths. Often compared to a "little Pompeii": equally well-preserved, but without the tourist crowds.

Saepinum Roman theater: 3,000-seat capacity, stage and tiers in local stone still standing. Medieval colonial houses built into the tier curvature remain inhabited today—Saepinum is a living archaeological site, unique in central Italy.

Forum, macellum and Fontana del Grifo: the rectangular porticoed forum gave onto the civic basilica to the north. The macellum (covered market) preserves its tabernae around a courtyard fountain. Near the Porta di Bovianum, a 168 AD inscription still regulated shepherds' transit rights on the tratturo.
Medieval village of Sepino: developed within the Roman ruins using their materials, stone houses, cobbled lanes and small squares. A living village coexisting with ancient remains just meters away.

activities.text: Walk the Pescasseroli-Candela tratturo from Saepinum: one of Italy's oldest transhumance routes (111m wide, equivalent to 60 passi regi), declared a national monument. Walkable on the local section.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

places.name: Sermoneta

visits.text: Giardino di Ninfa — Twenty acres of English-style gardens created in 1921 by Gelasio Caetani on the ruins of a medieval city abandoned after its destruction in 1382 and the spread of malaria. Over 1,300 botanical species—magnolias, ornamental cherries, Japanese maples, antique climbing roses on stone ruins—surround the remnants of towers, seven churches, bridges and city walls. The New York Times called it 'one of the most beautiful and romantic places on Earth.' The name comes from a Roman temple dedicated to the Naiad nymphs, on a small island in the lake where the Ninfa river springs forth. In the 12th century, the town had nine churches; Pope Alexander III was crowned here in 1159 at Santa Maria Maggiore. In 1298, Benedetto Caetani (Pope Boniface VIII) purchased Ninfa for his nephew Pietro II for 200,000 gold florins. The 19th-century writer Gregorovius dubbed it 'the Pompeii of the Middle Ages.' Gelasio's English mother, Ada Wilbraham, guided the first plantings. Lelia Caetani, the last heiress and a painter, tended the garden like a living canvas and founded the Roffredo Caetani Foundation in 1972. Designated a Monumento Naturale by the Regione Lazio in 2000. Guided tour mandatory (~1h), booking at giardinodininfa.eu. Adjacent: the Parco Pantanello (250 acres of wetlands restored in 2004).

Castello Caetani di Sermoneta — Medieval fortress dominating the Pontine plain from 785 feet altitude. Built in the 13th century by the Annibaldi barons, purchased in 1297 by Pietro II Caetani (nephew of Boniface VIII) for 140,000 gold florins. The Maschio (keep) rises 138 feet. In 1470, the Camere Pinte were adorned with mythological frescoes by the school of Pinturicchio, including a room depicting the seven virtues and their opposites. In 1499, Pope Alexander VI Borgia excommunicated the Caetani and confiscated the castle: Cesare Borgia transformed it into a military fortress (rivellino, camminamento di ronda, Casa del Cardinale by Antonio da Sangallo), destroying the church of San Pietro and the Caetani tombs out of pure spite. After Alexander VI's death, Julius II returned the castle in 1504. Charles V was received here on April 2, 1536 with 1,000 cavalry and 4,000 infantry. The Sala dei Baroni (72 feet, four bays of Gothic arches) served as the banquet hall. Scenes from 'Non ci resta che piangere' (Troisi/Benigni) were filmed in the stables. Guided tour mandatory (~1h), Fondazione Caetani.

Medieval Borgo of Sermoneta — One of Lazio's best-preserved medieval towns, mentioned in the Aeneid (Virgil calls it Sulmo). Intact 13th-15th century urban fabric. The Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta (13th century) was built on a Roman temple to Cybele; it preserves Quattrocento frescoes attributed to the school of Benozzo Gozzoli. The Loggia dei Mercanti housed the covered market. The Museo della Ceramica (Palazzo Ada Caetani, 16th century) traces the history of local ceramics.

activities.text: Stroll through Ninfa's gardens among ruins draped in roses and wisteria—observe the ponte romano and ponte del Macello spanning the river. After the tour, access the hortus conclusus (17th-century Italian-style garden with citrus grove and swans).

Guided castle tour: Piazza d'Armi, Camere Pinte, Sala dei Baroni with period table and canopy bed, dungeons with prisoners' graffiti (open Sundays Oct-March). Panoramic view over Monte Circeo and the Pontine Islands on clear days.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast
Ulysses Riviera: Secret Gardens, Ancient Caves & Southern Lazio Islands

map_keywords: Giardino di Ninfa Cisterna di Latina

places.name: Serravalle Pistoiese

visits.text: The Torre del Barbarossa rises 42 meters at the village's highest point, on the ruins of the Rocca Vecchia built by Pistoia in the 12th century. Its name remains enigmatic: no historical document connects it to Frederick I Barbarossa. What's certain is its function: from its summit, sentries watched over the Valdinievole to Lucca and the sea, communicating by light signals with neighboring towers at Monsummano Alto and Montecatini Alto. The entrance door sits 5.20 meters above ground level, following medieval military architecture standards.

The Rocca Nuova, also called Castruccio's fortress, was built in 1302 after Serravalle's conquest by the Lucchese, under the direction of Uguccione della Faggiola, then completed by Castruccio Castracani. It preserves its powerful walls, a central hexagonal-base tower and a corbelled turret connected by a parapet walk. The Gabella Gate—so named for its medieval toll-collection role—opens through a rounded arch on molded consoles. Entry is free.

The Oratorio della Vergine Assunta is considered by specialists a masterpiece of late Trecento Pistoiese painting. The frescoes on its walls, discovered by chance in the 1980s, reveal a Crucifixion facing the entrance, a Last Judgment on the right wall with the damned below and apostles at mid-height, and the Virgin with Christ in glory at the summit—colors of startling brilliance and expressions of rare intensity for the period.

The Pieve di Santo Stefano and San Michele Arcangelo church (12th-13th centuries) form with the two towers the characteristic panorama of Serravalle, visible from the Firenze-Mare motorway. San Michele preserves a 14th-century fresco of the Miracle of San Biagio and a triptych by Bartolomeo di Andrea Bocchi dated 1438.

activities.text: Walk along the ramparts and through Serravalle's medieval lanes: the entire village can be explored by following the single main street linking Porta Gabella to Torre del Barbarossa, passing through stone houses with arched doorways decorated with coats of arms, remnants of the 13th-century prosperity when Serravalle controlled the obligatory passage between Pistoia and Lucca on the ancient Via Cassia.

Visit Serra Pistoiese (850m altitude) and the Museum of Peasant Culture: 15km to the northeast, this small Apennine village presents in its ethnographic museum the tools and farming practices of the Pistoiese mountains, from charcoal production to high-altitude haymaking.

days_plan.itin_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas

places.name: Servigliano

visits.text: The three gates and the quadrilateral — Servigliano is a rarity in Europe: a town built entirely from scratch in 1772, after a landslide destroyed the hilltop village above. Pope Clement XIV entrusted the project to architect Virginio Bracci. The result is an almost perfect rectangle of 144 × 137 metres, crossed by a cardo and decumanus in the ancient Roman fashion, with three monumental gates (Porta Clementina, Porta Pia, Porta Santo Spirito). Walking through any one of them is enough to grasp the whole idea.

Piazza Roma — Collegiata di San Marco and Palazzo Municipale — the collegiata was built between 1774 and 1778 by Luigi Paglialunga (the same architect as Grottammare's Teatro dell'Arancio). It holds the reliquaries of San Servigliano Martire and San Gualtiero, including one in 1403 Marchigian goldsmithery, and an 18th-century Fedeli organ. Opposite the church, the Palazzo Municipale (1784–1789) was built with stones salvaged from the demolished old village. Its courtyard shelters a century-old magnolia.

Parco della Pace — outside the walls, a vast grassy enclosure ringed by a brick wall: this is the former internment camp, built in 1915 and used during both World Wars. More than 50,000 people passed through it. A memorial (Casa della Memoria) housed in the old railway station documents its history.

Santuario di Santa Maria del Piano — outside the quadrilateral, a former convent of the Observant Friars Minor, built over the ruins of a 1st-century Roman villa. It preserves a wooden altar and a wooden crucifix — the only traces of ancient occupation.

activities.text: Explore the quadrilateral freely by walking the cardo and decumanus: observe how each side street branches off at right angles from the main axes, and how the craftsmen's arcaded houses line the inner perimeter — a pocket-sized lesson in Enlightenment urban planning.

Try the serpe di cioccolato — a local shortbread shaped like a snake and filled with chocolate — and vincisgrassi al forno, the rich mixed-meat ragù lasagne that is the defining dish of the Macerata kitchen.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

places.name: Siena

visits.text: Piazza del Campo: Laid out between 1293 and 1349, this shell-shaped square is divided into nine sectors symbolizing the Council of Nine that governed Siena at the height of its power. The red brick paving is bordered by eleven lanes converging toward the center—an urban composition unmatched in Italy. The Palazzo Pubblico (13th century) closes the concave arc and the Torre del Mangia rises to 102 meters, built from 1338 to 1348, the third tallest medieval tower in Italy.

Civic Museum (Palazzo Pubblico): On the palace's first and second floors, the Hall of the Mappamondo and the Hall of Peace preserve Ambrogio Lorenzetti's frescoes on the Effects of Good and Bad Government (1338-1340)—one of the first secular painting cycles in Western history, representing the city of Siena itself.

Santa Maria della Scala Complex: A former pilgrims' hospital founded in the 11th century, one of Europe's oldest, this 19,000 m² complex is now a vast museum. The Sala del Pellegrinaio, painted between 1440 and 1444 by Domenico di Bartolo and Vecchietta, documents medieval hospital life with striking documentary precision.

Cathedral (Duomo di Siena): Built in white Carrara marble and black Montagnola marble, the polychrome 13th-14th century façade is one of the peaks of Italian Gothic. The interior, with three naves of 50 alternating white and black marble columns, houses a floor in marquetry of 56 inlaid marble panels created between 1369 and 1547, long covered to protect it. The Piccolomini Library (1495), painted by Pinturicchio, documents the life of Pope Pius II in ten large scenes of exceptional Renaissance realism.

Baptistery of San Giovanni: Attached to the cathedral's apse, this 14th-century Gothic baptistery houses the monumental baptismal font (1427-1430), a collective work by Jacopo della Quercia, Donatello, Lorenzo Ghiberti, and other Florentine and Sienese masters—a unique synthesis between the two schools.

National Picture Gallery: Housed in the 14th-century Palazzo Buonsignori, this collection traces 400 years of Sienese painting, from the gold backgrounds of Duccio di Buoninsegna to Simone Martini, Ambrogio Lorenzetti, and the 15th-century masters. Nowhere else can you measure so clearly the specificity of the Sienese school versus Florence.

Sanctuary of Saint Catherine: On St. Anthony's Hill, the old dyers' quarter houses the birthplace of Catherine Benincasa (1347-1380), Doctor of the Church. The ensemble of chapels, oratories, and loggias built around the family home in the 15th century constitutes one of Siena's most intimate places of devotion.

activities.text: Walk through the contrade: Siena is divided into 17 contrade (districts), each with its own church, museum, and animal emblem. Getting lost in the steep lanes between Campo and Duomo—passing by the 12th-century Fonte Branda and Via di Città—reveals a remarkably preserved Gothic city, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Palio di Siena: Italy's most famous horse race pits ten of the seventeen contrade against each other each year on July 2 and August 16 on the Piazza del Campo covered with sand. Documented origins date back to the 14th century. The silk banner bearing the image of the Madonna—the 'Cencio'—rewards the victorious district. Book grandstand seats several months in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

Northern to Central Italy Circuit: Milan to Rome
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

map_keywords: Complesso Santa Maria della Scala Siena

visits.text: Piazza del Campo - shell-shaped medieval square with a 333-meter perimeter, built from 1293 by gently sloping the ground toward Palazzo Pubblico. The nine radiating sectors recall the Government of Nine (1287-1355), Siena's period of greatest prosperity. The Palio dei Cavalli has been run here each year on July 2 and August 16 since the 17th century, with ten jockeys representing ten of the seventeen contrade.

Torre del Mangia - Palazzo Pubblico tower rising 102 meters, built between 1338 and 1348. Its summit exactly matches the height of the cathedral nave, signifying the balance between civil and religious power desired by the Government of Nine. The tower owes its name to its first bell-ringer, Giovanni di Duccio, nicknamed Mangiaguadagni for his prodigality.

Duomo di Siena - Gothic cathedral built between 1196 and 1348, with alternating white and green marble cladding. The interior floor of 56 inlaid marble panels (14th-16th centuries) is fully visible only during a few weeks in August-September. The Piccolomini Library (1502) preserves illuminations by Attavante degli Attavanti of exceptional precision.

Museo dell'Opera del Duomo - museum installed in the unfinished bay of the cathedral expansion, halted by the Black Death of 1348 which killed two-thirds of Siena's population. Duccio di Buoninsegna's Maestà (1308-1311), a monumental painting measuring 4.5 by 4.9 meters, was carried in procession from the artist's workshop to the cathedral by the entire population in 1311.

Contrade di Siena - Siena's seventeen historic neighborhoods, each with its own museum, church, baptismal font, and colors. Rivalry between contrade dates to the 12th century and still structures the city's social life today.

activities.text: Visit to a contrada museum - each contrada has a museum recounting its Palio victories, with costumes, flags, and heads of winning horses.
Walk along Siena's medieval ramparts - the 7 kilometers of 14th-century walls offer views over the Crete Senesi hills to the south and Chianti to the north.

days_plan.itin_title: Milan to Chianti Circuit: Design, Art, and Wine

visits.text: Ortygia, a 1-square-kilometer island linked to Sicily by two bridges, forms the ancient heart of Syracuse, founded by Corinthian colonists in 734 BC. Cicero described it as "the greatest and most beautiful of all Greek cities." Its alleyways layer Greek foundations, Arab arches, Baroque façades, and 18th-century balconies.

Syracuse Cathedral, Baroque since 1728, incorporates the Doric columns of the former 5th-century BC Temple of Athena: twelve 8.70-meter columns remain visible within the nave walls. One of the world's rare cases of an ancient temple converted into a mosque then a church without destruction.
The Fountain of Arethusa, a freshwater spring bubbling meters from the sea on Ortygia, is surrounded by papyrus—the only papyrus growing naturally in Europe outside Egypt.

Syracuse's Greek Theatre, carved from the Temenite rock in the 5th century BC, seats 15,000 spectators. Aeschylus premiered The Persians here in 476 BC in the presence of tyrant Hieron I. Each spring it hosts a festival of ancient plays performed in Greek—one of the few worldwide staged in an intact ancient theatre.

The Ear of Dionysius, a 5th-century BC quarry, is an S-shaped cave 65 meters deep with extraordinary acoustics. Caravaggio, visiting in 1608, gave it this name, imagining the tyrant imprisoned captives here to eavesdrop on their amplified conversations.
The Altar of Hieron II, 198 meters long, is the largest altar in the ancient world, built in the 3rd century BC. During the Eleutheria festival, 450 oxen were sacrificed here in a single day.

activities.text: Morning wander through Ortygia Market, then lunch with arancini al ragù and granita di mandorla (almond granita) at a historic bar on Via Roma.
Swimming at Punta Castelluccio beach south of Ortygia, a 15-minute walk, with views of the old town walls.

Visit to the Paolo Orsi Archaeological Museum, Sicily's richest museum of prehistory and archaeology, featuring the Venus Anadyomene of Syracuse—a 5th-century BC marble fragment considered one of the finest preserved Greek sculptures.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily
Complete Tour of Sicily

places.name: Sirmione

visits.text: Admire the Scaliger Castle, a majestic medieval fortress built after the mid-fourteenth century standing at the entrance to the old town. Surrounded by the crystal-clear waters of Lake Garda, the castle with its dock, courtyard and corridors evokes the poignant atmosphere of the Della Scala era, when it served as a strategic defensive position for the entire area.

Explore the Grotte di Catullo, the evocative remains of an ancient Roman villa from the first to second century AD in a spectacular position at the tip of the peninsula. Set among ancient olive trees and overlooking the lake panorama, the ruins are linked by popular tradition to the poet Catullus. The adjacent museum houses precious artefacts from Sirmione's and Lake Garda's history.

Visit the Church of San Pietro in Mavino, the oldest in the area dating from the eighth century AD. Nestled among olive trees on the hill of the same name, it offers an atmosphere of peace and silence with its Romanesque bell tower renovated in 1070 and evocative fourteenth-century frescoes, including a fine Madonna Enthroned with Child and Saints.

Discover the Palazzo Callas Exhibitions, dedicated to the memory of the Divine Maria Callas who chose Sirmione as her Italian refuge. Follow the evocative Callas Arie On Air trail, which through ten stops at the peninsula's most characteristic locations lets you relive the unique atmosphere of the most beautiful arias performed by the celebrated soprano.

activities.text: Relax at Spiaggia Giamaica, an exotic-feeling spot at the tip of Sirmione below the Grotte di Catullo. Formed of smooth white rocks polished by the waves, surrounded by crystal-clear waters and wild vegetation, it offers spectacular views towards the Rocca di Manerba and the northern part of the lake.

Treat yourself to a boat trip around the Sirmione peninsula to admire the harbour, Scaliger Castle, the Grotte di Catullo, the thermal facilities and Villa Callas from a unique perspective, interspersed with wonderful natural panoramas and lush Mediterranean vegetation.

Stroll through the old village from the Scaliger Castle to the Grotte di Catullo, immersing yourself in local history through evocative lanes, the remains of the Church of San Salvatore and Roman-era sites, with refreshing stops in the parks and characteristic establishments of the old town.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Italian Lakes Road Trip: Como, Maggiore, Orta and Garda
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

map_keywords: Chiesa San Pietro in Mavino Sirmione

places.name: Soncino

visits.text: Wander through Soncino's medieval center, encircled by red-brick ramparts that Venice rebuilt in 1454 to mark its border with the Duchy of Milan. The village developed at a strategic crossroads between the republics of Venice and Milan, which earned it multiple sieges during the 12th and 13th centuries. Its urban fabric is among the best-preserved in the Lombard plain.

Visit the Rocca Sforzesca, a brick fortress built between 1473 and 1475 on orders from Galeazzo Maria Sforza, Duke of Milan. Architect Bartolomeo Gadio of Cremona—the same who designed Milan's Castello Sforzesco—conceived the structure in three years. Four corner towers flank the inner courtyard with its siege well; a unique cylindrical tower testifies to adaptation to preexisting walls. The main hall's ceiling is painted as a pergola, just like in the Sforza castle in Milan.

Push open the door to the Hebrew Printing Museum, housed in the former print shop founded in 1483 by physician Israel Nathan, who came from Speyer in the Rhineland. His son Joshua Solomon printed the first Talmud treatise ever to emerge from a press here on December 19, 1483—the Berakhot. The Soncino family—who adopted the town's name—produced 137 Hebrew works here between 1483 and 1547, including the first complete vocalized Hebrew Bible (1488). Legend has it that the Sforzas invited Jewish financiers to raise funds for the Rocca's construction.

activities.text: Walk the ramparts and cobbled alleys of Soncino: the circuit around the 15th-century walls lets you measure the defensive system's scope and discover the fortified gates still standing. Soncino today draws pilgrimage from scholars and Jewish communities worldwide, who come tracing the footsteps of the Soncino printers.

days_plan.itin_title: Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria delle Grazie Soncino

places.name: Sorano

visits.text: Sorano, the "Matera of Tuscany," clings to a volcanic tufa cliff above the gorges of the Lente River. The Rocca degli Orsini, of medieval Aldobrandeschi origin and entirely renovated in 1552, is a multi-level fortress whose underground galleries partially date back to Etruscan times. It houses the Civic and Archaeological Museum. At the village's opposite end, the Masso Leopoldino—a fortress built by order of the Lorraine family in the 18th century—offers from its panoramic terrace a 360° view over the Tufa Valley, the Albegna and Fiora valleys, and neighboring villages. Its crenellated tower and clock make it the village's visual landmark.

The Cathedral of San Pietro in Sovana (a hamlet of 350 inhabitants 15 km from Sorano) is considered one of the most important Romanesque monuments in the Maremma. The building, whose origins trace back to the 7th century, exemplifies the transition from Romanesque to Gothic. Its 8th-century ciborium—a rare survivor from this era—ranks among the oldest in Tuscany. Sovana was also the birthplace of Pope Gregory VII (Ildebrando di Soana, c. 1020-1085—his exact birth date remains uncertain, historians place it between 1015 and 1025), whose Gregorian Reform transformed the medieval Church.

The Etruscan necropolis of Sovana, within the Città del Tufo Archaeological Park, is the region's most varied for the quality of its monumental tombs carved into tufa. The Tomba Ildebranda (3rd-2nd century BC) is the most spectacular: a temple with fluted columns of which only one of eight survives, all sculpted directly from the cliff. The Tomba della Sirena, whose entrance is adorned with a bas-relief depicting a siren holding two figures, and the Tomba del Tifone complete the circuit. All are accessible from the park's forest trails.

activities.text: Hike along the Via Cava di San Rocco from Sorano: this Etruscan path carved into tufa descends toward the San Rocco necropolises with walls reaching 20 meters in places. It leads to a panoramic viewpoint over the Lente Valley and Sorano below. A trail then allows walking to Sovana on foot through the countryside.

Visit the site of Vitozza (5 km from Sorano, 2 km from San Quirico): a cave village abandoned in the 18th century, one of Italy's largest troglodyte sites with over 200 caves carved into a hill's tufa. Inhabited since at least the 11th century, and probably from Etruscan times, it preserves the remains of a fortress, several churches, and a medieval columbarium for pigeon breeding.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

map_keywords: Via Cava di San Rocco Sorano

visits.text: Sorrento perches on a long limestone cliff 53 km south of Naples, overlooking the Bay of Naples facing Vesuvius. The historic center revolves around Piazza Tasso (named for poet Torquato Tasso, born here in 1544) and a network of commercial lanes. The town is renowned for its wood marquetry craft (intarsia) and for limoncello, a liqueur produced from the enormous lemons of the Sorrento Peninsula. Its port, Marina Piccola, is the departure point for boats to Capri.

activities.text: Depart by boat from Sorrento's port to Capri (approximately 25-minute crossing). The island, former residence of Emperor Tiberius (14-37 AD) who built Villa Jovis there, is famous for its Faraglioni—three limestone stacks rising from the sea—and for the Grotta Azzurra, a sea cave where light filtered through an underwater opening tints the water an intense electric blue. The center can be explored on foot or by funicular.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

places.name: Sovicille

visits.text: Historic center of Sovicille: Perched at the base of the Montagnola Sienese and encircled by 15th-century walls, the town was contested in the Middle Ages between the bishoprics of Siena and Volterra, then between the Republic of Siena and the noble Ardengheschi family. The 12th-century Palazzo dei Vescovi, property of the bishops of Siena, is its most visible legacy.

Abbey of Torri: A few kilometers from Sovicille, this monastic complex is distinguished by a spectacular triple-colonnade cloister—a 13th-century stone row, a 14th-century terracotta one, and a 15th-century wooden one. The 14th-century Baroque church has a superb inlaid choir.

Pieve di Ponte allo Spino: Among the most remarkable rural Romanesque churches in Tuscany, this pieve preserves in its adjacent courtyard the remains of a 13th-century cloister and double-arched windows from a building where bishops stayed.

activities.text: Visit to Villa Cetinale: Built in the late 17th century for the Chigi family by architect Carlo Fontana, this villa is accompanied by a citrus garden and the Thebaid Park—a wood crossed by a meditation path leading to chapels, grottoes, ponds, and statues. The Hermitage of the Scala, accessible by 300 penitential steps carved in stone, dominates the whole.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province

map_keywords: Pieve di Ponte allo Spino Sovicille

places.name: Spello

visits.text: Chiesa di Santa Maria Maggiore and Cappella Baglioni - This Romanesque collegiate church founded in the 11th-12th centuries houses Spello's major artistic treasure. In 1500, Prior Troilo Baglioni, future Bishop of Perugia, commissioned painter Bernardino di Betto known as Pinturicchio to decorate a side chapel completely. Pinturicchio had just finished decorating the Borgia Apartment in Rome for Pope Alexander VI. Between summer 1500 and spring 1501, he covered the three walls with the Annunciation, the Nativity, and the Dispute of Jesus with the Doctors, and the vault with the four Sibyls. This cycle, unanimously considered the pinnacle of his production, includes a self-portrait of the artist on the right wall, signed "BERNARDINVS PICTORICIVS PERVSINVS." The floor is paved with Deruta ceramics from 1566. This was Pinturicchio's last important commission in Umbria before his departure for Rome and Siena where he died in 1513.

Roman gates and ramparts - Spello, called Hispellum in Roman times, preserves several sections of its Augustan ramparts from the 1st century BC. The Porta Venere, flanked by two polygonal towers from the 1st century, is one of the best preserved in Italy. The Porta Consolare, opening onto Corso Cavour, dates from the same century and still bears on its facade three Roman statues from the imperial era reused in the Middle Ages. Julius Caesar already described Hispellum as a haven of peace.

Villa dei Mosaici di Spello - Discovered during construction work in 2012, this 4th-century AD Roman villa is one of Umbria's most important archaeological sites. Its polychrome mosaics depict mythological scenes and geometric patterns in exceptional preservation. The ensemble is sheltered under a modern museographic structure open to the public.

Centro storico - The alleyways of Spello, with houses of pink and yellow stone quarried from Monte Subasio, are covered with climbing plants and flower pots. The annual competition among residents for the most beautiful floral decoration of the vicoli makes Spello one of Italy's most flowered towns.

activities.text: Infiorate of Corpus Domini - Every year in June, Spello's residents spend Saturday night to Sunday morning covering the hamlet's streets with elaborate flower carpets depicting sacred scenes, prepared collectively by neighborhood. Petals, leaves, colored sands, and other natural plant materials form compositions several dozen meters long. The carpets are blessed in the morning during the Corpus Domini procession, then trampled. This tradition dates back to the 19th century.

Hiking on Monte Subasio - From Spello, a trail climbs through garrigue of olive trees and broom toward the summit plateau of Monte Subasio (1,290 m), from which the panorama embraces the Umbrian valley, Assisi, Perugia, and the Sibillini Mountains. The round-trip route to the rifugio takes about 4 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Maggiore Spello

visits.text: Villa of Tiberius and Cave — In 1957, coastal road builders stumbled upon an imperial villa identified as Tiberius's praetorium (emperor 14-37 AD), cited by Suetonius and Tacitus. The core dates to the late Republican era; the complex extended 985 feet with sea-facing terraces, baths, port and barracks. The centerpiece: a natural cave transformed into a mythological landscape, preceded by a large rectangular seawater fish pond with an artificial island serving as a summer dining room (caenatio). Inside the cave, a circular pool (40 feet diameter) was surrounded by monumental sculptural groups—a 'marble Odyssey.' Four groups identified: the blinding of Polyphemus by Ulysses, Scylla's assault on the ship, the theft of the Palladium by Ulysses and Diomedes, and Ulysses carrying Achilles's body. A Ganymede abducted by Zeus's eagle crowned the entrance. The sculptures bear the signatures of the same Rhodian artists as the Vatican's Laocoön (Agesander, Athenodorus, Polydorus). In 26 AD, Tiberius nearly died in a cave-in—Sejanus saved his life. The Julio-Claudian gens considered itself descended from Ulysses and Circe: the cave faced Monte Circeo, mythical home of the sorceress. The Museo Archeologico Nazionale (1963, Giorgio Zama) was a victory for residents who opposed transferring the works to Rome.

Borgo di Sperlonga — Listed among the 'borghi più belli d'Italia,' a whitewashed village with blue shutters clinging to a promontory between two beaches. Narrow lanes, arches, stairways, sea views at every turn. The Torre Truglia (1532), symbol of the village, was built on a coastal watchtower, destroyed by the corsair Barbarossa then by the Turks (1623), rebuilt each time. The spiaggia di Levante extends toward Tiberius's cave; the spiaggia di Ponente toward the north. Lago Lungo beach (sand spit with dunes separating an artificial lake from the sea) offers a wild atmosphere.

activities.text: Visit the archaeological museum: plaster reconstructions of complete groups, original Greek marble fragments, Faustinus inscription describing the works. Descend through the macchia toward the villa, fish ponds and cave. Observe the circular pool and the view toward Monte Circeo.
Wander the whitewashed alleys of the centro storico at sunset. Climb to Torre Truglia for the panorama. Dinner of fresh fish in a seaside trattoria.

days_plan.itin_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast
Ulysses Riviera: Secret Gardens, Ancient Caves & Southern Lazio Islands

visits.text: Crossing to the island of Elba and discovery of Sansone beach, famous for its white pebbles and translucent water.
Exploration of marine caves and observation of colorful fish while snorkeling.

activities.text: Coastal navigation around Portoferraio and swimming in neighboring coves.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Beaches in Italy: Secluded Coves and Turquoise Waters

places.name: Spilimbergo

visits.text: Duomo di Santa Maria Maggiore - Built in 1284 against the old fortified walls, this Gothic gem incorporated a defensive tower converted into a bell tower. Inside, the three apses preserve 14th-century frescoes: Christ the Judge, the Annunciation to the Shepherds, and scenes from the Old and New Testaments. In the crypt rests the sarcophagus of Walterpertoldo IV, 14th-century lord of Spilimbergo.

Castello di Spilimbergo - Founded in the 11th century by the Spengenberg counts of Carinthia, the castle forms a circular courtyard surrounded by noble palaces. Destroyed and rebuilt multiple times, burned in 1511 during a popular revolt, it preserves two remarkable elements: the Palazzo Dipinto and Palazzo Tadea with its 16th-century stucco salon.

Palazzo Dipinto - The sole building to survive the 1511 fire, this palace is the castle's oldest section, restored between 1390 and 1400 by Count Wanzel. Its façade is entirely covered with frescoes attributed to Andrea Bellunello (15th century) and Pordenone, with Gothic and Renaissance mullioned windows framing two balconies. Emperor Charles V stayed here with his court in 1574.

Corso Roma and historic center - The main street cuts through the medieval borough east to west between two 14th-century towers. Pastel-colored façades line up painted palaces and antique signs. Pause before Palazzo Monaco and its painted façade, then Casa Dipinta (Palazzo Ercole) adorned with 16th-century frescoes depicting scenes from the life of Hercules.

Piazza Duomo and Loggia della Macia - On the square, notice the corner column still bearing the Macia, the ancient length measure used for medieval commercial exchanges. The Palazzo del Daziario, where taxes were paid to the lords, completes the picture of a borough that was an important commercial transit center.

Scuola Mosaicisti del Friuli - Founded in 1922 by mayor Ezio Cantarutti on a suggestion from Milan's Umanitaria, this school trains mosaicists from around the world. Its graduates have created decorations for the Library of Congress in Washington, the dome of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, and the Ground Zero metro station in New York. The visitor route covers techniques from Greco-Roman to Byzantine to contemporary mosaic, with over 800 works on display. You can watch students and masters breaking tesserae with the martellina hammer.

activities.text: Open-air mosaic trail - Spilimbergo is an open-air museum: works created by the Scuola Mosaicisti are scattered throughout town—on façades, at the entrances to the Town Hall and library, in parks, and even on tree trunks. A map available at the tourist office guides visitors from one piece to the next.

days_plan.itin_title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

map_keywords: Duomo di Santa Maria Maggiore Spilimbergo

places.name: Stia

visits.text: Piazza Tanucci and Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta: the heart of Stia is an irregular, gently sloping square lined with porticoes and decorated balconies — one of the most charming in the entire Casentino. It is named after Bernardo Tanucci, born in Stia in 1698, a jurist and royal adviser to the Kingdom of Naples. The Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta, erected in Romanesque style in the 12th century with a later-modified facade, preserves a glazed-terracotta Madonna and Child from the Della Robbia workshop.

Museum of the Art of Wool — Lanificio di Stia: housed in the former Stia wool mill — one of the most important in Italy in the first half of the 20th century, employing over 500 people — the museum traces the history of the Panno Casentino, a woolen cloth with its famous looped texture produced in the valley since the 14th century. The mill bell set the rhythm of village life for decades. The factory ceased production in 2000 before being converted into a museum. Samples, period photographs and pre-mechanization machines illustrate the evolution of this fabric, which became an internationally recognized fashion article in the 20th century.

activities.text: Hike to the source of the Arno from Passo della Calla: drive or cycle to the pass on the Romagna border, then about 2 hours' walk round trip to reach the source of the Arno on the slopes of Monte Falterona, at around 1,350 meters. The river, which will go on to flow through Florence before reaching the sea at Pisa after 241 km, begins here as a thin stream among the beeches.

Castle of Porciano: an 11th-century Conti Guidi di Romena fortress commanding the upper Casentino valley from a hill near Stia. The 13th-century crenellated tower and keep are accessible. Local legend says Dante Alighieri found refuge here during his exile from Florence. The castle was abandoned, then restored from the 1960s onward.

days_plan.itin_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries

map_keywords: Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta Stia

visits.text: La Cattolica di Stilo—a 10th-century Byzantine church, one of the most important monuments of Byzantine architecture in southern Italy. The centrally-planned building with five cylindrical domes on drums rests on ancient marble columns with mismatched capitals (some inverted—a sign of hasty reuse). Stilo is also the birthplace of Tommaso Campanella (1568-1639), philosopher and author of The City of the Sun, imprisoned for 27 years by the Spanish Inquisition.

activities.text: Walking through Stilo's historic center to see the medieval stone houses and the view over the Ionian plain before continuing toward Catanzaro.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

visits.text: La Pelosa Beach, 2.5 km north of the village, takes its name from the posidonia seagrass that carpets its seabed — 'sa pelosa' in Sardinian. Its 10-meter Aragonese tower, built in 1578 on a small island accessible on foot through the shallow waters, has become the beach's iconic symbol. Access is now limited to 1,500 people per day to protect the fragile ecosystem.

Stintino was founded in 1885 by 45 fishing families expelled from Asinara Island when the Italian state established a penal colony there. The 'Communion of the 45' — the cooperative that gave birth to the village — is commemorated by the Piazza dei 45 in the town center. The old tonnara (tuna fishery), active until the 1970s, is now a museum of tuna fishing.

The Torre del Falcone, perched atop Capo Falcone promontory at 200 meters elevation, offers a 360° panorama over the Stintino peninsula, Asinara National Park, and Sardinia's west coast. The name comes from the peregrine falcons that nest in the rocky crevices.

activities.text: Boat excursion to Asinara National Park, an island closed to the public for 115 years due to the presence of a maximum-security penal colony. Now a nature reserve, it's home to albino donkeys, dolphins, and pristine vegetation.
Swimming at La Pelosetta, the neighboring beach to La Pelosa, less crowded, with the same Aragonese tower in the background and shallow waters perfect for children.
Sample bottarga di tonno and lobster soup at the harbor trattorias — specialties drawn directly from the village's tuna-fishing heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Sardinia Coast: Beaches & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Marvel at Villa Pisani, lauded as the Queen of the Venetian Villas, a palatial estate as grand as a royal residence. This magnificent 18th-century villa features fully frescoed rooms including Tiepolo's stunning ceiling masterpiece, a lush garden with an intricate hedge maze, and fascinating historical connections to Napoleon.

Visit the adjacent Villa Foscarini Rossi, home to a unique Shoe Museum displaying hundreds of examples of Italian footwear craftsmanship. The elegant villa provides insight into the refined lifestyle of Venetian nobility along the Brenta Riviera.

activities.text: Explore the extensive gardens of Villa Pisani, featuring formal parterres, ornamental ponds, and the famous labyrinth. The grounds offer a perfect setting for a leisurely stroll through centuries of landscape design.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Stresa's Belle Époque lakefront - The Lungolago promenade is lined with grand late-19th-century hotels and palm gardens. Stresa became an international resort from the 1860s onward, attracting aristocrats, writers (Hemingway set part of 'A Farewell to Arms' here), and crowned heads.

Isola Bella and Palazzo Borromeo - Until 1630, this was merely a fishermen's rock. In 1632, Count Vitaliano Borromeo launched its transformation into a baroque palace, completed around 1670. The island was named for his wife Isabella. The four-story palace houses a domed hall of honor, neoclassical ballroom, 16th-century Flemish tapestries in silk and gold, and grottos decorated with pebbles and shells. Napoleon slept here in August 1797. The Italian gardens descend in ten pyramidal terraces (37 m high) planted with orange trees and camellias, crowned by the Teatro Massimo and its statues by Carlo Simonetta. White peacocks roam freely.

Isola dei Pescatori - The smallest of the Borromean Islands (350 x 100 m) never belonged to the Borromeo family. About twenty fishing families inhabit it year-round. The narrow lanes, wooden balconies, and Romanesque church of San Vittore form a village frozen in time. Have lunch here: grilled lavarello (whitefish) is the local specialty.

Villa Pallavicino - On Stresa's southern outskirts, this neoclassical villa is surrounded by an 18-hectare English park that serves as a refuge for about forty animal species in semi-freedom: flamingos, llamas, Tibetan goats, macaques.

Hermitage of Santa Caterina del Sasso - Clinging to a cliff on Lake Maggiore's eastern shore, this monastery was founded in the 12th century by Alberto Besozzi, a merchant from Leggiuno who survived a shipwreck. The complex comprises three chapels built at different periods: the Cappella della Vergine (1270), the Cappella di Santa Caterina, and the Cappella di San Nicola with a 14th-century Crucifixion. Access is by 268 steps (or an elevator installed in 2009), or by boat from Stresa in 20 minutes. The courtyard's wine press dates from 1759.

Botanical Gardens of Villa Taranto in Verbania - In 1931, a Scottish captain acquired this villa with the aim of creating a dream garden on the shores of Lake Maggiore. The result: 16 hectares, 7 km of paths, and over 20,000 plant varieties, including Victoria Amazonica (giant Amazon water lily), Antarctic Dicksonia (tree ferns), and dahlias in spectacular bloom from August to October. From spring to autumn, species succeed each other in a symphony of colors.
