Pallanza quarter in Verbania - The old center of Pallanza, with its narrow streets and waterside squares, faces Stresa across the Borromean Gulf. The Museo del Paesaggio presents collections of art and sculpture related to the lake landscape.

activities.text: Boat shuttle Stresa → Isola Bella → Isola Pescatori → Stresa - Boats depart approximately every 30 minutes from Stresa's landing pier. Allow half a day for the two main islands.

Ferry crossing Stresa → Santa Caterina del Sasso → Verbania - From Stresa, the boat reaches the hermitage in 20 minutes (check schedules, limited frequency). From the hermitage, reach Verbania by boat or by car via the eastern shore.

days_plan.itin_title: Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Italian Lakes Road Trip: Como, Maggiore, Orta and Garda
Italian Lakes Road Trip: Como, Maggiore, Orta and Garda
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

visits.text: Stromboli, in continuous eruption for 2,000 years, ranks among the world's most active volcanoes. Its summit eruptions — every 15 to 30 minutes — hurl lava bombs down the Sciara del Fuoco, a scree slope plunging directly into the sea. Ancient mariners called it the "Lighthouse of the Mediterranean" because its glowing eruptions served as a navigation beacon.

Ginostra, a hamlet of 30 permanent residents on the island's southwest flank, is accessible only by sea and claims Europe's smallest port — a natural cleft that fills with just two boats. No roads, no cars, no ATMs.

activities.text: Night ascent of Stromboli (924 m) with mandatory licensed guide above 400 meters: departure at 5 PM, summit arrival at 8 PM to observe eruptions after dark, descent at 11 PM via the Sciara del Fuoco.
Swimming at Ficogrande black sand beach, 10 minutes from the port, where volcanic lava sand retains heat until nightfall.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Subiaco

visits.text: Monastero di Santa Scolastica - The oldest Benedictine monastery in the world (founded by Saint Benedict in the 6th century, sole survivor of the twelve monasteries he established in the Aniene valley before departing for Monte Cassino). Its Romanesque cloister and Cosmatesque cloister frame a still-active community. The Biblioteca Statale holds approximately 150,000 volumes, 440 manuscript codices, and 213 incunabula. In 1465, two disciples of Gutenberg from Mainz, Konrad Sweynheym and Arnold Pannartz, installed Italy's first printing press here and produced the peninsula's first printed book: Cicero's De Oratore, followed by Saint Augustine's De Civitate Dei, each in 275 copies. The typographical style created here is called "stile Subiaco."

Monastero di San Benedetto (Sacro Speco) - Clinging to the cliff like an eagle's nest, this sanctuary is built around the cave where Saint Benedict lived as a hermit for three years (around 500 AD) before writing his Rule, foundation of Western monasticism. The walls of the cave and the superimposed chapels (upper church and lower church) are entirely covered with frescoes from the 11th to 14th centuries. Among them, the oldest known portrait of Saint Francis of Assisi - painted during his visit in 1223 - shows him without a halo and without stigmata, evidence it was made during his lifetime. The stairway carved into the rock leading to different levels offers vertiginous views over the valley.

Rocca Abbaziale - This fortress has dominated the town of Subiaco since the 11th century. Built for the abbots who controlled the valley, it was remodeled in the 16th century and now hosts temporary exhibitions. From its terraces, plunging views over the tufa rooftops, the medieval bridge of San Francesco, and the Aniene valley.

activities.text: Stroll through the medieval town of Subiaco - The sloping alleys of this town, listed among Italy's most beautiful, descend from the Rocca toward the bridge of San Francesco, a Gothic arch spanning the Aniene. The path between the two monasteries (about 2 km) traverses a holm oak forest in the Regional Park of the Simbruini Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

map_keywords: Monastero di San Benedetto Sacro Speco Subiaco

visits.text: Sulmona - a town of 24,000 inhabitants in the Valle Peligna basin, birthplace of the Latin poet Ovid (43 BC - 17 AD), who opens his Tristia with 'Sulmo mihi patria est' ('Sulmona is my homeland'). The medieval historic center is built on Roman ruins and preserves a coherent urban fabric from the 13th to 17th centuries.

Palazzo dell'Annunziata - a 14th-15th century Gothic-Renaissance building, considered the most representative monument of Abruzzese civil architecture. The façade combines a flamboyant Gothic portal, Renaissance windows, and bas-relief sculptures depicting the Annunciation.

Acquedotto Medievale - a 13th-century aqueduct crossing Piazza Garibaldi on Gothic arches. Built by Charles I of Anjou to supply the town with water from the mountains, it is still partially functional.

activities.text: Discover the confetti di Sulmona - sugar-coated almonds produced in Sulmona since the 15th century. The confectioners on Via Mazara are organized in a cooperative; they create floral compositions from silk threads and dragées, exported worldwide.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Sutri

visits.text: Roman Amphitheatre of Sutri - Carved between the 2nd and 1st centuries BC directly into the tufa hillside, this elliptical amphitheatre measuring 49 by 40 meters could hold between 5,000 and 9,000 spectators across three tiers. Not a single masonry stone was used—everything was sculpted from volcanic rock. Buried under vegetation for centuries, locals rediscovered and excavated it between 1835 and 1838.

Etruscan Necropolis - Along the cliff below the town, 64 tombs carved into tufa date from the 6th to 4th centuries BC. You'll find niches for cinerary urns, chamber tombs with dromos entrances, and columbaria. A paved path winds between the sculpted rock faces. Sutri bore the title "Gateway to Etruria"—this monumental cemetery proves it.

Mithraeum and Church of Santa Maria del Parto - Three adjoining Etruscan tombs were transformed into a sanctuary dedicated to the god Mithras under the Roman Empire, then converted into a Christian church. Step inside to discover 14th-century frescoes depicting the Virgin and sacred scenes, painted directly onto the tufa walls. Local legend claims this cave was where Bertha, Charlemagne's sister, gave birth to Roland, the legendary paladin.

Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta - Built in the 12th century in Romanesque style, remodeled in the 17th century as Baroque. The original Romanesque bell tower and medieval crypt survive. In 1046, Emperor Henry III convened a council here to resolve the crisis of three rival popes.

Palazzo Doebbing - Former episcopal palace until 1986, restored between 2010 and 2018. The museum occupies five levels and blends ancient and contemporary art: sculptures, architectural fragments, and frescoes from surrounding churches.

activities.text: Walk through the Archaeological Park - Lazio's smallest regional nature park (7 hectares) connects all the sites via shaded trails winding between oaks and meadows. At the summit, the 17th-century Villa Savorelli overlooks the landscape, surrounded by an Italian garden featuring a boxwood labyrinth.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio

map_keywords: Mitreo Santa Maria del Parto Sutri

visits.text: Tagliacozzo - a medieval town of 7,000 inhabitants in the Marsica, near the border with Lazio. The Battle of Tagliacozzo in 1268 saw Charles of Anjou crush the forces of Conradin of Hohenstaufen, the last Swabian claimant to the throne of Sicily. Dante cites this confrontation in the Divine Comedy.

Piazza dell'Obelisco - the main 15th-century square, bordered by Renaissance palaces and arcades. The central obelisk, erected in 1770, commemorates the 1268 Angevin victory. The square is considered one of the region's most harmonious.
Chiesa e Chiostro di San Francesco - a 13th-century Franciscan complex with a double-gallery cloister and a church preserving 15th-century frescoes attributed to the school of Benozzo Gozzoli.

activities.text: Excursion to Lago del Salto - an artificial lake created in 1940 by the dam on the Salto river, 12 km from the center. Offers water activities and forest walks along its shores.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

visits.text: Mount Etna, at 3,357 meters, is Europe's highest active volcano. Its eruptive activity has been documented since 425 BC. Parco dell'Etna, created in 1987 across 59,000 hectares, encompasses over 300 secondary cones.

The Funivia dell'Etna cable car ascends from Rifugio Sapienza (1,923 m) to 2,500 meters in 15 minutes. From there, the summit craters are accessible on foot in 2 hours round-trip, with a guide mandatory above 2,900 meters.

Taormina's Greek Theatre, built in the 3rd century BC and remodeled by the Romans, is Sicily's second-largest ancient theatre. Its unique feature worldwide: the stage frames smoking Mount Etna 30 km away and the Bay of Naxos, Greece's first Sicilian colony.
Isola Bella, a rock connected to the coast by a natural sand bar, remained private property until 1990. Its botanical garden shelters rare Mediterranean species and its waters form a nature reserve.

Corso Umberto I, a 500-meter medieval artery, preserves 14th-century Gothic palaces. Piazza IX Aprile with its terrace overlooking the sea and Etna constitutes one of Sicily's most celebrated panoramas.

activities.text: Hike in the Valle del Bove, a collapsed caldera measuring 7 by 5 km at 1,800 meters altitude, ringed by 1,000-meter walls. Historic lava flows layered here read like geological strata.
Tasting of Etna DOC wines (red Nerello Mascalese, white Carricante) at cantine in Randazzo or Linguaglossa—vineyards at 800-1,000 meters on volcanic soil.
Swimming at Mazzarò beach, accessible by cable car from Taormina (5 minutes, ~€3 round-trip). Pebble beach nestled in a cove with views of Isola Bella.
Excursion to Giardini Naxos, 4 km downhill, site of Sicily's first Greek foundation (734 BC) with a museum of Corinthian ceramics.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

visits.text: Egnazia - Messapian and Roman archaeological site: A few kilometers from Fasano, on the Adriatic coast, this site preserves the superimposed remains of a Messapian city (5th century BC), then Roman, crossed by the Via Traiana. Excavations have uncovered a necropolis, thermal baths, a forum and an ancient port. The on-site museum displays the characteristic two-tone geometric pottery called 'Egnazia ware,' present in collections across European museums. Horace describes a stop at Egnazia in his Satires during his journey from Rome to Brindisi in 37 BC.

Taranto - MArTA National Archaeological Museum: Founded in 1887 in the former Alcantarini convent, this is the second most important archaeological museum in southern Italy after Naples. Its collection of Ori di Taranto — Hellenistic gold jewelry from monumental tombs of the 4th-3rd centuries BC — is unique in the world: diadems, laurel crowns, earrings and necklaces of extraordinary fineness. The museum also holds the world's largest collection of Greek terracotta figurines, and a 6th-century BC bronze statue of Zeus found at Ugento. In 2011, a statue from the Orpheus and the Sirens group, stolen then exhibited at the Getty Museum in Los Angeles, was restituted after decades of negotiations.

Taranto - Temple of Poseidon and old town: The two Doric columns of the Temple of Poseidon (6th century BC) are the oldest remains of Tarentum and among the first Doric columns in Magna Graecia — predating the temples of Paestum and Syracuse. Founded by Spartan colonists around 706 BC under the name Taras, the city reached its zenith under the government of philosopher and mathematician Archytas (430-350 BC), friend of Plato. The old town occupies an islet between the Mar Grande and Mar Piccolo; the 19th-century Ponte Girevole, a still-functioning swing bridge, connects the island to the mainland.

activities.text: Tasting mussels from the Mar Piccolo: Taranto's inner harbor (Mar Piccolo) has been renowned since antiquity for its mussels, cultivated in its calm, nutrient-rich waters. Harbor caterers and restaurants offer platters of gratinated or raw mussels — cozze — in the local style.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Puglia: Frederick II's Castles, Hidden Hinterland & Forgotten Villages
Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

places.name: Tarquinia

visits.text: Necropoli dei Monterozzi - Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2004, this 130-hectare necropolis is the largest known in Etruria. It contains over 6,000 sepulchers carved into rock, of which approximately 200 are adorned with frescoes - the only major testimony of pre-Roman classical painting in the Mediterranean. Archaeologist Massimo Pallottino defined it as "the first chapter in the history of Italian painting." The oldest date back to the 7th century BC. Tombs are accessible through long dromoi (sloping corridors) and visible through climate-controlled glass doors that maintain constant temperature and humidity. Among the most famous: the Tomb of the Leopards (5th century BC, vividly colored banquet scene), the Tomb of Hunting and Fishing (hunting and diving scenes), the Tomb of the Augurs (funeral games scenes), the Tomb of the Lionesses (acrobatic dolphins, music and dance).

Museo Archeologico Nazionale - Palazzo Vitelleschi - Housed in this palace built between 1436 and 1439 for Cardinal Giovanni Vitelleschi, a blend of late Gothic and early Renaissance, this museum presents its collections on three levels. On the ground floor, stone sarcophagi from the Hellenistic period, including that of the Priest (Laris, head of the Partunu family). On the first floor, the famous Cavalli Alati (Winged Horses), a 4th-century BC terracotta high relief from the pediment of the Ara della Regina temple - one of the best-known works of Etruscan art. On the second floor, a panoramic loggiato and climate-controlled rooms reconstructing painted tombs whose frescoes have been detached for conservation: Tomb of the Triclinium, of the Chariots, of the Olympics, of the Ship.

Medieval historic center of Tarquinia - The perched medieval town overlooks the Tyrrhenian coast and the Maremma countryside. The Chiesa di Santa Maria in Castello (12th century), the most representative of the town's medieval architecture, stands near the cylindrical torrione known as "Matilde di Canossa's tower." The alleys contain Gothic palaces, Romanesque towers, and peperino façades.

activities.text: In-depth visit to the painted tombs - About twenty tombs are open to the public in rotation along a marked route on the grassy hillside. QR codes along the path enable 3D virtual tours of the interiors, to appreciate fresco details invisible from the glass doors. For enthusiasts, it's possible to request extraordinary opening of normally closed tombs (Tomb of the Ogre, of the Shields, of Typhon) - by advance written request to the superintendency.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

map_keywords: Museo Archeologico Nazionale Palazzo Vitelleschi Tarquinia
Chiesa Santa Maria in Castello Tarquinia

places.name: Terminillo

visits.text: Cittaducale, founded in 1309 by King Charles II of Anjou (hence its name meaning "ducal city"), preserves a typical medieval bastide orthogonal plan with rectilinear streets converging on the central square. The Chiesa di Santa Maria del Popolo (14th century) and the Chiesa di San Francesco with its Gothic portal merit a stop. From the ramparts, view the Velino valley and Terminillo.

The Terme di Cotilia, a few miles from Cittaducale, were known to the Romans as Aquae Cutiliae. Emperor Vespasian, born in nearby Sabina (at Vicus Phalacrinae, today's Cittareale), stayed here regularly and died here in 79 AD. Titus also died here in 81 AD. The sulfurous springs still gush into a small lake.

The Tempio di San Francesco al Terminillo, a contemporary church built at 5,322 feet at Pian de' Valli, stands out with its 171-foot bell tower and its 3,767-square-foot apse mosaic, one of the world's largest modern mosaics, depicting the Creation. Engraved on the main bell is a prayer for peace meant to resonate across the mountains.

activities.text: Climb toward Terminillo on the scenic road from Cittaducale via Rieti. The road winds through beech forests, gaining 3,300 feet over 12 miles, with viewpoints over the Rieti plain opening at each hairpin turn.

Panorama from Pian de' Valli (5,315 feet) over the Valle Santa: on clear days, you can distinguish the four Franciscan sanctuaries arranged in a cross around the Rieti plain—Fonte Colombo, Greccio, La Foresta and Poggio Bustone.

Hike to the summit of Monte Terminillo (7,274 feet), nicknamed "Rome's Mountain." The massif, whose meltwater feeds the lakes of the nature reserve below, offers trails through beech forests and alpine meadows with views of the Abruzzo, Sibillini Mountains and, on exceptional days, the Tyrrhenian coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit
Rieti, Holy Valley & Sabine Lakes Circuit

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria del Popolo Cittaducale

visits.text: Terricciola developed around a hill inhabited since Etruscan times, as evidenced by 4th-century BC hypogea (underground chamber tombs) discovered beneath Via della Fonte. These underground vaults still served as wine cellars last century. Several are being reopened to the public thanks to European funds. The clayey, sandy soil, rich in Pliocene marine fossils, is ideal for Sangiovese, Trebbiano, and Merlot.

The Pieve di San Verano is the main parish church of the Terricciola territory, a few kilometers from the borgo. A sober Romanesque building whose interior houses medieval decorative elements. The hamlet of Morrona, in the municipality, contains a Hellenistic-era necropolis with hypogeal chamber tombs among the best documented in the Pisan hills.

activities.text: Terricciola has about 25 independent wineries and is a member of the National Association of Wine Cities. The municipality is part of the Pisan Hills Wine Route. Several estates offer year-round tastings: red Sangiovese, white Vermentino and Trebbiano, with a mineral signature linked to sea breezes rising up the valley. The White Night of Wine takes place every first weekend in July.
Panorama over the Pisan hills and Valdera from the heights of the medieval borgo, whose alleyways and Renaissance houses have retained their original appearance.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

visits.text: Villa d'Este - Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2001, this villa was commissioned by Cardinal Ippolito II d'Este, appointed Governor of Tivoli in 1550. Frustrated at not being elected pope, he hired architect Pirro Ligorio to transform a former Benedictine convent into a prestigious residence. The 4.5-hectare terraced gardens house over 500 fountains, 300 water jets, and some sixty cascades - all powered solely by gravity, without a single pump, thanks to water channeled from the Aniene River through an underground conduit dug in 1564-1565. The Fontana dell'Organo (1568), a hydraulic organ built by Frenchmen Luc Leclerc and Claude Venard, still plays melodies every two hours. Franz Liszt lived here for twenty years and composed "Fountains of the Villa d'Este."

Villa Adriana - A UNESCO site since 1999, this imperial residence was built between 118 and 138 AD by Emperor Hadrian on a 120-hectare plateau (40 hectares visitable), 28 km from Rome. A great traveler and amateur architect, Hadrian recreated monuments that had impressed him across the Empire: the Pecile recalls Athens' Stoa, the Canopus evokes an Egyptian canal lined with statues, and the Teatro Marittimo is an artificial island encircled by a canal where the emperor withdrew to meditate. A network of underground carriage-accessible galleries allowed servants to move about without disturbing the imperial spaces. After centuries of pillaging (marble, mosaics, and sculptures scattered across European museums), the villa inspired the greatest Renaissance architects from Bramante to Palladio.

Villa Gregoriana - This nature park, landscaped in 1835 by Pope Gregory XVI, occupies the gorge of the Aniene River, where the river plunges in a Grande Cascata with a 120-meter drop. Steep paths wind through dense vegetation to reach caves (including Neptune's Cave and the Sirens' Cave) and the ruins of the Roman villa of Manlio Vopisco. On the ancient acropolis overlooking the gorge stand the Temple of Vesta (1st century BC), a tholos with Corinthian columns, and the more austere rectangular Temple of the Sibyl.

activities.text: Stroll on the acropolis of ancient Tibur - From the esplanade of the temples of Vesta and the Sibyl, panoramic views embrace the entire valley of waterfalls and the wooded gorges of the Aniene. Below, the Ponte Gregoriano and ruins of Roman villas complete the scene.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

places.name: Torino

visits.text: The Palazzo Reale di Torino, erected from 1584 on the foundations of the old bishop's palace, was the main residence of the House of Savoy until Italian Unification. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1997 as part of the Residences of the Royal House of Savoy, the complex (Polo Reale) groups the palace with its royal apartments and baroque frescoes, the Galleria Sabauda (Italian and Flemish paintings), the Armeria Reale (weapons and armor), the Biblioteca Reale, and the royal gardens designed in part by André Le Nôtre. Piazza Castello is framed by Palazzo Madama—a blend of Roman gate, medieval castle, and Juvarra's baroque facade (1721)—and the church of San Lorenzo with Guarino Guarini's dome (1680).

Turin's Museo Egizio is the world's second-largest museum devoted to ancient Egypt, after Cairo. Founded in 1824, it assembles over 3,000 pieces including mummies, sarcophagi, papyri, and the reconstruction of the rock temple of Thutmose III (around 1450 BC), the statue of Amenhotep I, and the stele of Ramses II. Explanatory panels are translated into French.

The Mole Antonelliana (167.5 meters), designed by Alessandro Antonelli from 1863 as a synagogue then reclaimed by the city, is Turin's architectural symbol and one of Europe's tallest masonry structures. It houses the National Cinema Museum, whose spectacular scenography exploits the building's height across seven levels. The central panoramic elevator rises to 85 meters with views of the Alps on clear days.

Piazza San Carlo, nicknamed Turin's 'drawing room,' was inaugurated in 1638. Surrounded by columned porticos, it's flanked by the near-twin churches of San Carlo Borromeo and Santa Cristina. At its center, the equestrian statue of Emanuele Filiberto of Savoy. Under the arcades stand the historic 18th-19th century cafés, meeting places for royalty and Turinese writers. The pedestrian Via Roma links the square to Palazzo Reale over 700 meters of porticos.

Parco del Valentino (50 hectares along the Po) houses the Borgo Medievale, an 1884 historical recreation of a Piedmontese and Valdostan village inspired by actual regional castles. The Rocca reproduces 14th-century military architecture. Castello del Valentino (17th century, Savoy royal residence, UNESCO listed) adjoins the park.

The Reggia di Venaria Reale, 8 km north of Turin, is one of Europe's largest royal residences (80,000 m² of buildings, 80 hectares of gardens). Designed from 1658 by Amedeo di Castellamonte for Vittorio Amedeo II, remodeled by Juvarra, it was abandoned in the 19th century and restored over 25 years to reopen in 2007. UNESCO listed since 1997. Juvarra's galleries, the Sant'Uberto chapel, and the French gardens are highlights.

The Basilica of Superga (672 m altitude), accessible by rack railway from Turin (30 min), is Filippo Juvarra's masterwork, built between 1717 and 1731 by order of Vittorio Amedeo II in thanksgiving for a 1706 victory over the French. Its 65-meter dome is visible from across the Po plain. The royal crypt houses the tombs of most Sardinian kings of the House of Savoy. At the rear, a plaque commemorates the May 4, 1949 crash that killed the entire Torino Football Club team, then Europe's best.

activities.text: Wander the Quadrilatero Romano, old Roman Turin with its grid plan dating to Augustus. Maze of narrow streets linking 17th-century palaces, Romanesque churches, and interior courtyards. In the evening, the district comes alive at aperitivo hour (Negroni, Martini, Punt e Mes—born in Turin or Piedmont).

Sample Turinese specialties at the Porta Palazzo market (Europe's largest open-air market, Piazza della Repubblica): Piedmont wines (Barolo, Barbaresco, Moscato), gianduja chocolates, vitello tonnato, agnolotti del plin. The market runs every morning except Sunday.

Optional excursion to Sacra di San Michele, a Romanesque abbey perched at 962 meters on Monte Pirchiriano in Valle di Susa (40 km from Turin). Founded around 983, it was one of Western Europe's most important abbeys and reportedly inspired Umberto Eco's The Name of the Rose. The Porta dello Zodiaco (12th century) and the Stairway of the Dead lead to the three-nave church.
Turin is a major rail hub: direct TGV Paris-Turin in under 4h, connections to Milan (1h), Florence (2h30), Rome (4h30). Turin-Caselle airport is 16 km from downtown (shuttle, 45 min).

Closing lunch in the San Salvario district or in one of Via Roma's historic cafés. Specialties not to miss: tajarin (fine pasta with 30 egg yolks), bagna cauda (hot anchovy-garlic sauce), bonet (chocolate and amaretti flan).

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days
Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy
Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

map_keywords: Reggia di Venaria Reale Venaria Reale

visits.text: Marvel at the Castle of Torrechiara, one of the most beautiful and best-preserved castles in Italy, rising majestically above the Parma hills. Built in the 15th century by Pier Maria Rossi for his beloved mistress Bianca Pellegrini, the fortress combines military strength with Renaissance elegance, its four corner towers connected by crenellated walls creating a fairytale silhouette against the sky.

Explore the castle's famous Camera d'Oro (Golden Chamber), decorated with frescoes depicting the love story of Pier Maria and Bianca, making this fortress a rare example of a castle built for love rather than war. The interior preserves remarkable Renaissance decorations while the exterior walls have defended the hilltop for over five centuries.

activities.text: Admire the castle illuminated at night, when carefully placed lighting transforms this romantic fortress into a magical spectacle visible from the valley below. The strategic position on the road to the mountains makes Torrechiara the perfect castle to visit when combining a mountain excursion with cultural heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

places.name: Trani

visits.text: Foresta Umbra: at the heart of the Gargano promontory, this 10,000-hectare forest is Puglia's only large broadleaf woodland. Beech, oak, yew, and pine cover a territory that was once an island joined to the peninsula by alluvial deposits. The visitor center offers an introduction to local fauna — roe deer, foxes, buzzards.

Trani — Cathedral of San Nicola Pellegrino: built from 1099 in response to the canonization of a young Greek pilgrim who died in Trani, this is one of the finest examples of Apulian Romanesque. Trani stone, a pinkish limestone tufa quarried locally, gives it a unique color that shifts with the hour. The 59-meter bell tower and double flight of stairs leading to the central portal make the first impression. The bronze door, a masterpiece by Barisano da Trani completed in 1175, is preserved inside — a faithful replica stands in the facade. The construction was consecrated in 1143 before even being finished.

Trani — Swabian castle and seafront: built by Frederick II of Swabia in 1233 on the waterfront, this square-plan castle with corner towers faces the cathedral from the same esplanade. The two buildings sum up the medieval duality of the city: spiritual power versus temporal power, stone against stone.

Trani — old port and Jewish quarter: Trani was one of the most active commercial ports on the Adriatic during the Middle Ages. Its Jewish quarter, among the best preserved in southern Italy, houses the Scolanova Synagogue from the 12th century, converted to a church then returned to the Jewish community in 1993.

Monte Sant'Angelo — Sanctuary of the Archangel Michael: carved into the rock of Mount Gargano at 796 meters elevation, this sanctuary is one of the oldest Christian pilgrimage sites in the West. According to tradition, the Archangel Michael appeared here to the Bishop of Sipontum at the end of the 5th century. Pope Gelasius I formalized the cult. The Lombards made it their dynastic sanctuary — King Rothari (died 652) is buried here. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site with other Lombard sites in 2011.

Manfredonia — Swabian-Angevin castle and archaeological museum: founded by Manfred of Sicily in the 13th century, the city takes its name from its founder. The castle holds the Daunian stelae in its collections — sculpted funerary slabs dating from the 8th to 6th century BC, among the oldest figurative testimonies of Puglia.

activities.text: Scenic descent from Gargano to the plain: the road from Monte Sant'Angelo to Manfredonia offers views across the eponymous gulf and the distant Murge. This crossing marks the passage from mountainous, forested Gargano to the agricultural plain of central Puglia.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Puglia: Frederick II's Castles, Hidden Hinterland & Forgotten Villages
Grand Tour of Puglia: Salento, Matera & the Trulli Valley

map_keywords: Santuario San Michele Arcangelo Monte Sant'Angelo

visits.text: Trapani stretches along a sickle-shaped promontory—its name derives from the Greek Drepanon (sickle). Its old town preserves Arab streets aligned parallel to the prevailing wind and 17th-century Baroque palaces. The port, 160 km from North Africa, served for centuries as the maritime gateway to Tunisia.

Trapani's salt pans have operated continuously since Phoenician antiquity. The 18th-century windmills, now national monuments, pumped seawater through the evaporation basins. Trapani salt has been protected by European PGI status since 2018.

Erice, a medieval town perched at 751 meters, was founded by the Elymians—a pre-Greek people of Anatolian origin according to Thucydides—then successively occupied by Carthaginians, Romans, Arabs, Normans, and Spanish. Its 70 churches for 700 inhabitants make it Sicily's most church-dense town.

Marsala, 30 km south, is renowned for its fortified wine discovered by Englishman John Woodhouse in 1773. It's also where Garibaldi landed on May 11, 1860 with his Thousand to conquer the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies.

activities.text: Marsala DOC wine tasting at Florio or Pellegrino cellars, the two historic producers founded in the 19th century—available in dry, semi-dry, or sweet styles.
Sunset over the salt pans from Nubia's viewpoint: the low-angle light turns the basins pink and orange, with windmills silhouetted against the sky.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Sicily

places.name: Trastevere

visits.text: Trastevere ('beyond the Tiber') is one of Rome's oldest neighborhoods: from the Republican era, it welcomed foreigners, merchants, sailors, and the city's first Jews. Julius Caesar owned a villa here with his famous gardens (horti Caesaris). Its medieval urban fabric—cobbled alleys, ochre and brick façades, arches between houses—formed between the 10th and 13th centuries. The inhabitants, the Trasteverini, still spoke a dialect close to Corsican as late as the 19th century, a legacy of the Corsican and Sardinian presence in the quarter. Piazza di Santa Maria in Trastevere is the neighborhood's heart: in the evening, musicians, neighbors, and travelers occupy the church steps.

The basilica of Santa Maria in Trastevere is one of Rome's oldest churches dedicated to Mary, founded in the 3rd century. Its 12th-century reconstruction gave it its golden mosaic façade—visible from the square—and its Romanesque bell tower. The interior preserves granite columns salvaged from Roman baths and extraordinary apse mosaics by Pietro Cavallini (13th century).

activities.text: Dine in the alleys behind the piazza rather than facing it: Via della Paglia, Via dei Vascellari. Neighborhood trattorias serve classic Roman cuisine—cacio e pepe, coda alla vaccinara—in a persistent village atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Piazza di Santa Maria in Trastevere Roma
Basilica di Santa Maria in Trastevere Roma

visits.text: Piazza della Repubblica — Treia's finest square and one of the most theatrical in all of Le Marche. Its horseshoe shape opens eastward onto a wide panoramic terrace behind a late 18th-century balustrade. On clear days the view reaches Monte Conero and the Adriatic. The square is framed by three remarkable buildings: the Palazzina dell'Accademia Georgica (designed by Valadier, 19th century), which holds 15,000 volumes and the 1,196 parchments of the municipal historical archive; the Palazzo Comunale (the result of merging two medieval palaces, restructured in the Renaissance), which houses the Pinacoteca with portraits of Treia's nobility and paintings by Pietro Tedeschi, Antonio Balestra and Agostino Bonisoli; and the Cattedrale della SS. Annunziata, designed by Andrea Vici, a pupil of Vanvitelli — one of the largest churches in Le Marche by floor area. In the sacristy: a lunette by Vincenzo Pagani (Deposition of Christ) and a panel by Giacomo da Recanati.

Porta Vallesacco and Teatro Condominiale — Porta Vallesacco is the most celebrated of Treia's seven medieval gates — declared a national monument because it was here in 1263 that the Montecchiesi (the inhabitants of what is now Treia) repelled the troops of Corrado d'Antiochia. Just beyond it, the Teatro Condominiale (1801, architect Lucatelli) is a rare example of a theatre with entirely wooden balconies. Its stage curtain, painted by Silverio Copparroni in 1865, depicts exactly this Battle of Vallesacco. The theatre also houses the Dolores Prato Study Centre, dedicated to the great novelist born here — her autobiographical novel Giù la piazza non c'è nessuno, published when she was 87, is considered a masterwork of 20th-century Italian literature.

Museo Civico Archeologico (Convento San Francesco) — housed in the former San Francesco convent, this museum holds sculptural and architectural fragments from Roman Treia, along with an exceptional collection of statuettes and cult objects of Isis and other Egyptian deities of the Ptolemaic period — unearthed in the early 20th century on the site of the ancient Roman city, where the Santuario del SS. Crocifisso now stands. The outline of an amphitheatre is still visible in the vegetation of a nearby field. Key pieces include two portrait heads, a headless togated statue and a small head of Mercury.

Torre Onglavina — a 12th-century tower on a polygonal plan, the only surviving remnant of one of the three castles that once formed Treia under the name Montecchio (del Cassero, dell'Elce and dell'Onglavina). Restored in the 1950s, it offers the best view in the village: the Sibillini, the Majella, the Gran Sasso, the Conero and the Adriatic all in one sweep. Not to be confused with the Torre San Marco, another crenellated 12th-century torrette still standing near Porta Palestro.

activities.text: Disfida del Bracciale (event, first Sunday of August) — Treia is the Italian capital of the Pallone col Bracciale — a team sport once as popular as football, with neighbourhood sides competing in an annual tournament dating back to 1978. Carlo Didimi, the greatest player of the 19th century, was born here. For ten days before the event, the village transforms: taverns, costumed craftsmen, street performances. A Museo del Gioco del Pallone col Bracciale presents trophies, bracelets and 19th-century balls.

Calcione and Treia specialities — the calcione is Treia's savoury-sweet speciality: a flaky pastry filled with eggs, pecorino, sugar and olive oil. Eaten as a snack or dessert. Annual festival in May (Sagra del Calcione). Best paired with the quintessential vincisgrassi and ciauscolo from the Macerata province.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

visits.text: Piazza Duomo forms the beating heart of Trento: the Cathedral of San Vigilio, built between the 12th and 16th centuries, faces the baroque Fountain of Neptune (1767). This is where numerous sessions of the Council of Trent (1545-1563) took place—one of the pivotal events of the Catholic Counter-Reformation.

Castello del Buonconsiglio, begun in the mid-13th century, served for five centuries as the residence of Trento's prince-bishops. It comprises three distinct architectural cores: the medieval Castelvecchio, the Renaissance Magno Palazzo commissioned by Cardinal Bernardo Clesio (1485-1539), and the Baroque Giunta Albertiana. Don't miss the Torre Aquila and its Cycle of the Months—early 15th-century International Gothic frescoes attributed to a Bohemian artist, depicting peasant and aristocratic activities month by month. Legend has it that an underground passage once connected the castle to the cathedral.

activities.text: Wander through the historic center's narrow lanes, admiring the painted facades characteristic of Trentino. Sample local wines—Trentino produces exceptional Pinot Grigio and Gewürztraminer—at a traditional enoteca in the center.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

visits.text: Explore Treviso, the birthplace of tiramisu, radicchio rosso, and prosecco - three delights that make life more delicious. This beautiful Northern Italian city features breathtaking frescoed buildings, mighty defensive walls, and narrow curving streets often overlooked by travelers heading straight to Venice.

Visit the Church of St. Francis (Chiesa di San Francesco), a late Romanesque/early Gothic masterpiece with important 13th-century frescoes. Inside are the tombs of Petrarch's daughter Francesca and Dante's son Pietro - remarkable literary connections.

Discover Piazza dei Signori, the main square surrounded by Palazzo del Podesta with its 13th-century Civic Tower, the Gothic Palazzo dei Trecento, and Palazzo Pretorio. The Loggia dei Cavalieri, a 13th-century structure with faded frescoes, served nobility for meetings and games.

activities.text: Explore La Pescheria, a small island where the fresh fish market has operated for centuries, and the Church of San Nicolo for its frescoes including the earliest known depiction of spectacles in Europe.
Visit the Duomo San Pietro, Treviso's seven-domed cathedral housing a Titian and an atmospheric crypt, plus the Civic Museums of Santa Caterina and Luigi Bailo for fine art and exhibitions.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Castello di Miramare - residence of Archduke Maximilian of Habsburg on a promontory facing the gulf
Richly decorated interiors - rooms with ornate ceilings and period furniture from the 19th century
Parco di Miramare - vast botanical garden transformed from karst moorland into a green oasis

activities.text: Visit to the castle and discovery of the tragic history of Maximilian, Emperor of Mexico who was assassinated
Walk through the large park with numerous tree species, ponds and gardens
Panoramic view of the Gulf of Trieste from the castle promontory

days_plan.itin_title: Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Italy: Roman Aquileia to Alpine Villages Tour

visits.text: Tropea is Calabria's most photographed face - and you'll understand why at first glance. The historic center sits on the edge of a white tufa cliff plunging straight down to a fine sandy beach and turquoise sea. Before you, on a rock connected to the beach by stairs carved into stone, the church of Santa Maria dell'Isola seems to float between sky and sea. Founded in the Middle Ages, it has survived multiple earthquakes. Climb the steps - the view from the summit over the coast and, on clear days, the smoking Stromboli on the horizon, is worth the effort.

Wander through Tropea's medieval historic center, with its noble palaces, interior courtyards, and alleys opening onto panoramic terraces. The Norman cathedral preserves a Renaissance black crucifix. Tropea is also the homeland of cipolla rossa, the sweet red onion that goes into many local dishes - you'll find it everywhere, in jam, focaccia, or simply grilled.

Take the coastal road toward Capo Vaticano, about twenty minutes south. This granite cape cut into wild coves offers some of the most beautiful beaches on the Tyrrhenian Sea: Grotticelle, Praja i Focei, Tono. The shallow, crystal-clear waters are perfect for snorkeling. From the lighthouse viewpoint, on clear days, you can spot the Aeolian Islands and Mount Etna.

activities.text: Spend the afternoon on Spiaggia della Rotonda, Tropea's main beach, at the foot of the cliff and Santa Maria dell'Isola. If you seek more tranquility, Michelino beach (accessible by a steep path) is less crowded.

In the evening, dine in the centro storico enjoying panoramic terraces with sea views. Fish restaurants serve swordfish, tuna, and seafood, accompanied by local nduja - this spicy, spreadable Calabrian sausage.

days_plan.itin_title: Calabria: From Cosenza to Reggio, Between Mountains and Ionian Sea
Complete Calabria Loop: Tyrrhenian and Ionian Coasts

places.name: Tuscania

visits.text: Basilica di San Pietro - On the colle di San Pietro, probably the ancient Etruscan acropolis, this Romanesque-Lombard basilica (8th-13th century) stands among three watchtowers and the Palazzo dei Canonici, on a vast meadow dotted with Etruscan sarcophagi. The grey tufa façade is pierced by a Cosmatesque portal with mosaics and surmounted by a large white marble rose window with three concentric circles (symbol of the Trinity), flanked by sculptures of the four Evangelists and two dragons. Inside, the central nave preserves a rare Cosmatesque pavement with geometric motifs. The crypt, supported by 28 reused columns (Roman and early medieval), forms a stone forest beneath groin vaults. Orson Welles shot scenes of his Othello here, Monicelli filmed L'Armata Brancaleone, and Pasolini his Gospel According to St. Matthew.

Basilica di Santa Maria Maggiore - At the foot of the colle di San Pietro, this former cathedral of the Diocese of Tuscania (consecrated October 6, 1206) combines Romanesque and pre-Gothic styles. The façade associates a superb rose window, mosaics, and reused carved fragments. The interior houses a monumental 14th-century Last Judgment attributed to Tuscan painters Gregorio and Donato d'Arezzo, painted columns, and a very rare 13th-century immersion baptismal font. The massive, squat bell tower stands detached from the church body.

Museo Archeologico Nazionale - Installed in the former Franciscan convent of Santa Maria del Riposo, this free museum assembles dozens of sarcophagi in nenfro (local volcanic tufa) and terracotta from surrounding necropolises, notably those of the aristocratic Curunas and Vipinana families (4th-2nd century BC). The Amazons' Sarcophagus, decorated in relief with scenes of Amazonomachy on three sides, is one of the major pieces. In the early 19th century, the Campanari family exported sarcophagi to London (1837), arousing the enthusiasm of archaeologist George Dennis who baptized Tuscania "the city of Etruscan sarcophagi."

Medieval historic center - Tuscania's walls, erected by the Etruscans then expanded in the 13th century to a perimeter of 4,700 meters, encircle a town of plastered palaces and tufa houses. Etruscan sarcophagi are scattered everywhere: in streets, squares, embedded in church walls, used as street furniture. Piazza Basile lines semi-recumbent figures from sarcophagus lids along a low wall. The Parco Torre di Lavello, on the ancient walls, offers a panorama over the valley and the Colle di San Pietro.

activities.text: Excursion to the Necropoli Madonna dell'Olivo - 2 km southwest of town, this Etruscan necropolis arranges its tombs on three rocky terraces in the Marta valley. On the upper level, the Grotta della Regina, a vast L-shaped hypogeum with a network of underground corridors on several levels, is one of the most important in central-northern Etruria. Visit by reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Maria Maggiore Tuscania

places.name: Udine

visits.text: Piazza della Libertà - Considered the finest Venetian square outside Venice itself, it gathers the Loggia del Lionello, Loggia di San Giovanni, and the column crowned by the Lion of Saint Mark in one space—a striking summary of four centuries of Venetian rule over Friuli.

Loggia del Lionello - This Venetian Gothic town hall was commissioned in 1441 and built between 1448 and 1456. Its architect, Nicolò Lionello, wasn't a trained architect but a goldsmith, which explains the refinement of every detail. The façade measuring 35.5 meters long and 15.2 meters high alternates bands of white and pink stone, ten pointed arches at ground level, and a central balcony above. Ravaged by fire in 1876, it was reconstructed identically. The Gothic inscription "UTINUM" recalls Udine's Latin name.

Loggia di San Giovanni and Torre dell'Orologio - Facing the Loggia del Lionello, this Renaissance loggia shelters a clock tower inspired by—though squatter than—the one in St. Mark's Square in Venice. It underscores the Serenissima's constant influence on city life.

Arco Bollani and ascent to the Castello - Pass through the arch designed by Andrea Palladio in 1556: it grants access to the ramp climbing toward the castle hill. Along the way, walk beneath the Loggia del Lippomano, an elegant 16th-century portico.

Castello di Udine - Rebuilt in the mid-16th century after the 1511 earthquake, this palace-fortress atop the hill houses the Civic Museums: galleries of Venetian and Friulian paintings from the 14th to 19th centuries, archaeological collections, and prints. From the terrace, views sweep across the city rooftops and, on clear days, the snow-capped Julian and Carnic Alps.

Museo Diocesano e Gallerie del Tiepolo - Set in the Palazzo Patriarcale, residence of the Patriarchs of Aquileia from 1593 to 1751, this museum holds around 700 works. Young Giambattista Tiepolo painted masterful frescoes here between 1726 and 1730: The Fall of the Rebel Angels in the stairwell, Rachel Hiding the Idols in the Gallery of Guests, and The Judgment of Solomon in the Red Room. An entire floor displays polychrome wood sculptures from the 12th to 18th centuries.

Oratorio della Purità - This small chapel shelters a ceiling Assumption painted by Tiepolo: a luminous Madonna surrounded by cascading angels. The intimacy of the space makes the work all the more striking.

Duomo di Santa Maria Annunziata - A Latin cross cathedral rebuilt after the 1348 earthquake, whose entire floor is covered by one of northern Italy's largest Roman mosaic pavements. The side chapels preserve works by Tiepolo and Pordenone.

Piazza Matteotti (Piazza San Giacomo) - A lively square ringed by Renaissance and Neoclassical façades, bordered by café terraces. This is Udine's social heart: locals gather here evenings for a tajut, the glass of Friulian white wine shared at the counter.

Casa Cavazzini - Museo d'Arte Moderna e Contemporanea - This restructured 16th-century palace presents a substantial collection of 20th-century Italian artists including De Chirico, Morandi, and Severini, through several donations tracing the evolution of modern art in Italy.

activities.text: Wander the Venetian historic center - Meander through lanes between Via Mercatovecchio and its arcades, Via Vittorio Veneto and its colorful palaces. Every Thursday morning, the Mercato del Giovedì sets up stalls of fresh produce, cheeses, and Friulian cured meats on Piazza Matteotti.

Friulian wine tasting - Udine is the gateway to Friuli's great wines: Friulano, Ribolla Gialla, Refosco dal Peduncolo Rosso. Wine bars in the historic center offer tastings paired with frico (Montasio cheese fritter) and musetto (local cotechino sausage).

days_plan.itin_title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

map_keywords: Museo Diocesano Gallerie del Tiepolo Udine

places.name: Vada

visits.text: Castagneto Carducci - Medieval village - Perched on a hill overlooking the Etruscan Coast, Castagneto Carducci bears the name of poet Giosuè Carducci (1835-1907), Italy's first Nobel Prize winner in Literature in 1906, who spent his childhood here from 1838 to 1848. The medieval village preserves its ramparts and stone alleyways. The municipality is surrounded by vineyards producing Bolgheri and Bolgheri Sassicaia DOC wines.

Bolgheri - Viale dei Cipressi and medieval borgo - The avenue of cypress trees leading to Bolgheri from San Guido, planted in 1831, was immortalized by Giosuè Carducci in his poem 'Davanti San Guido' (1874). The cypresses, now centuries old, form a vegetal corridor nearly 5 kilometers long. Inside the medieval village, small shops sell Bolgheri DOC wines including Sassicaia, created in 1948 and which became the first Super Tuscan classified in an individual appellation in 1994.

Vada and beaches of the Etruscan Coast - Vada was the ancient port of Volterra from the 4th century BC and was mentioned in Cicero's writings. Amphorae discovered on the seabed testify to the intense commercial activity of this port. The beach of Vada, stretching 5 kilometers of fine sand, has regularly received the Blue Flag certification.

Populonia - Medieval village and Etruscan acropolis - Populonia is the only one of the twelve great Etruscan cities built directly on the sea, on the Piombino promontory overlooking the Gulf of Baratti. Founded around the 6th century BC, it became the wealthiest mining city in the ancient Mediterranean thanks to iron processing extracted from Elba Island. The medieval village built on the Etruscan acropolis offers a 180° view over the gulf and the islands of the Tuscan Archipelago.

Archaeological Park of Baratti and Populonia - This 80-hectare park preserves Etruscan and Roman remains, accessible via developed trails. The necropolis of San Cerbone is the only Etruscan necropolis built by the sea. The 7th-century BC Tumulo dei Carri with its bronze chariot is particularly well preserved. Iron slag accumulated during centuries of metallurgical activity still forms black dunes on the beach.

Gulf of Baratti - The beach of the Gulf of Baratti, nestled between wooded hills and the sea, is one of the most preserved beach sites on the Etruscan coast. Submerged remains from the Roman era have been discovered offshore.

Castiglioncello - Baia del Quercetano and Castello Pasquini - Originally an Etruscan village (4th-3rd century BC), Castiglioncello owes its modern fame to patron Diego Martelli, who established his residence here (Castello Pasquini, 1889) and hosted the Macchiaioli painters (Giovanni Fattori, Odoardo Borrani, Telemaco Signorini), Italian precursors of French Impressionism. These artists depicted the coves, pine forest, and red cliffs of Castiglioncello in hundreds of paintings.
Coastal path Punta Righini and coves - The coastal path follows the characteristic red limestone cliffs, overlooking turquoise, shallow coves. Some coves are accessible only on foot or by swimming.

Archaeological Museum of Castiglioncello - Installed in the Palazzo Bombardieri of Rosignano Marittimo since 1996, this museum preserves collections from a necropolis of over 350 Etruscan and Roman tombs discovered in Castiglioncello, testifying to connections with the city of Volterra in the 3rd-2nd centuries BC.

activities.text: Walk on the Viale dei Cipressi of Bolgheri - The 4.7 km cypress avenue between San Guido and Bolgheri can be walked or cycled in both directions. The road is lightly trafficked and shaded.
Swimming at Vada - The beaches of Vada offer accessible swimming areas. The northern zones (Secche di Vada) are rockier and appreciated by divers; the southern zones are sandy.

Hiking in the archaeological park of Baratti and Populonia - The main trail (necropolis circuit) covers about 3 km through the necropolis, ancient quarries, and Mediterranean forests of the promontory.
Swimming in the Gulf of Baratti - Fine sand and crystalline water protected by the promontory. Free access from the park parking lot.

Swimming in the coves of Castiglioncello - The coves between Punta Righini and Porticciolo offer clear waters appreciated for snorkeling. Baia del Quercetano and Cala delle Morelle are accessible on foot from the village center.
Coastal hike Castiglioncello - The panoramic trail along the cliffs from Punta Righini offers views of the islands of the Tuscan Archipelago. About 5 km round trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages

map_keywords: Castello di Castagneto Carducci Castagneto Carducci

places.name: Valdobbiadene

visits.text: Continue through Prosecco Country to Valdobbiadene, at the heart of the production zone for the finest DOCG sparkling wines. The steep hillsides, many cultivated by hand, produce grapes of exceptional quality that have made this region world-famous.

Explore the charming town and surrounding hamlets where winemaking traditions have been passed down through generations. The landscape of terraced vineyards cascading down precipitous slopes is a testament to centuries of viticultural dedication.

activities.text: Enjoy stunning hiking through the vine-covered hills, stopping at small wineries along the way. The combination of natural beauty, traditional agriculture, and excellent wine makes this region a paradise for wine lovers and outdoor enthusiasts alike.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Valdobbiadene

visits.text: The Abbey of Vallombrosa (Santa Maria di Vallombrosa) was founded in 1036 by Giovanni Gualberto—a Florentine nobleman who had renounced his court life after granting forgiveness to his brother's murderer before the Crucifix of San Miniato al Monte, seeing Christ bow his head in approval. He retreated to these Apennine forests at 1,000 meters altitude to found the Vallombrosian Congregation, recognized by Pope Urban II in 1090. The abbey took its current form in the 15th century, with expansions in the 16th and 17th centuries. The church preserves Lorenzo Lippi's Trinity, a glazed terracotta altarpiece from Andrea della Robbia's workshop, and the reliquary of the arm of Saint John Gualberto by Florentine goldsmith Paolo Sogliano (1500). Perugino's Assumption (1498-1500), once on the altar, is now in Florence's Accademia Gallery. John Milton visited the abbey in 1638 and evoked its forests in Paradise Lost. Lamartine dedicated a poem to it in his Harmonies (1830).

activities.text: Forest walk in Vallombrosa forest, a biogenetic nature reserve since 1973: centuries-old beeches, white firs, and Scots pines in a dense, silent undergrowth. The Sentiero del Calvario climbs from the abbey toward the Hermitage of Paradisino (11th century), where anchorite monks retreated to isolated cells—panoramic view over the Arno valley.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

visits.text: The Sacro Monte of Varallo is the oldest and most important of Piedmont's Sacred Mountains, inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage in 2003. Founded in 1491 by Franciscan friar Bernardino Caimi, who had lived in the Holy Land, it recreates Jerusalem's Holy Places on the hillside for pilgrims who couldn't travel there. Over 40 chapels spread across the cliff, decorated with frescoes and polychrome terracotta sculpture groups depicting scenes from Christ's life, were created between the 15th and 19th centuries. Gaudenzio Ferrari, who executed his major works here between 1517 and 1530, developed a narrative and expressive style that prefigures baroque.

Varallo, founded in the 13th century, is the main town of Valsesia. Its historic center preserves arcaded medieval houses, the Pinacoteca del Sacco (15th to 18th-century paintings including several works by Gaudenzio Ferrari), and the Collegiata di San Gaudenzio. Valsesia climbs about 80 km from the plain to the foot of Monte Rosa (4,634 m), second highest summit in the Alps.

Upper Valsesia opens onto Alagna Valsesia, a Walser village at the foot of Monte Rosa at 1,191 meters altitude. As in Valle del Lys, the Walser built their typical stadel here from the 13th century. The local Walser museum traces the history of these pioneers from Valais. Alagna is the starting point for Monte Rosa ascents and the Monterosa Ski domain.

activities.text: Rafting or kayaking on the Sesia torrent from Varallo or Borgosesia: the rapids of the Sesia (class III to IV depending on season and sector) are among Piedmont's most popular. Providers offer guided 2 to 4-hour runs.
Canyoning in gorges of a Sesia tributary (several equipped sectors in the valley, beginner to intermediate level, wetsuit and helmet provided by outfitters).
Catch-and-release sport fishing on authorized Sesia stretches (salmonids in alpine setting, daily permit required, available from local fishing associations).

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont Wine & Truffle Road Trip: Turin, Alba & Barolo in 9 Days
Grand Alpine Valleys of Piedmont: Occitan Alps, Lakes & Baroque Savoy

places.name: Varano De Melegari

visits.text: Visit the Castle of Varano de' Melegari, a fortress of significant importance in the regional history of the Parma province. This medieval stronghold, strategically positioned at the entrance to the Ceno Valley, played a crucial role in controlling the mountain passes and trade routes through the Apennines for centuries.

Explore the village nestled below the castle walls, where traditional stone architecture reflects centuries of mountain community life. The settlement marks the gateway to the more dramatic castle landscapes of the upper valleys, serving as a perfect transition point between the plains and the Apennine mountains.

activities.text: Take in views of the surrounding valley from the castle grounds, where the landscape begins its dramatic rise toward the Apennine peaks. The area offers the first taste of the mountain scenery that characterizes the famous castles further up the valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

map_keywords: Castello di Varano de' Melegari Varano de' Melegari
Chiesa di San Martino Varano de' Melegari
Valle del Ceno Varano de' Melegari

visits.text: Arrive at Varenna by ferry to immediately take the Passeggiata degli Innamorati, a 1.3 km suspended corniche linking the landing stage to the village center by following the lake beneath red iron arches draped with climbing plants. Then climb toward Piazza San Giorgio, the medieval heart of the village, dominated by the 13th-century church of San Giorgio whose austere facade shelters medieval frescoes.

Visit Villa Monastero and its botanical gardens. The villa takes its name from a Cistercian monastery founded in the 12th century and closed by papal decree in 1567 for the nuns' indecorous behavior. Bought by the Mornico family then resold several times, it was enlarged at the end of the 19th century by a Hamburg family and completed in 1904. Now state-owned, its 14 furnished rooms constitute a house-museum. The botanical garden extends nearly 2 kilometers along the shore, in terraces, with some 900 plant species: African palms, Lebanon cedars, cypresses, magnolias, wisteria, citrus trees, and some rarities like Erythea armata.

Climb to Castello di Vezio, a medieval fortress perched on the hill overlooking Varenna for over a thousand years. The tower offers 360° panoramas of the lake and mountains. The castle houses a falconry with regular demonstrations and a permanent exhibition dedicated to the Lariosaurus, a primitive marine reptile whose first fossils were discovered in the province of Lecco and which, according to local legend, was spotted in the lake in 1946.

Stroll through the gardens of Villa Cipressi, dating from the 15th century: terraces descend to the lake, planted with cypresses, exotic flowers, and aromatic plants. Paired with Villa Monastero, it completes Varenna's botanical circuit.

activities.text: Ferry crossing between Varenna and Menaggio or Bellagio: Lake Como's public boat service connects the three points of the 'golden triangle' in 15 to 30 minutes. It's the best way to grasp the lake's inverted-Y geography and appreciate the villas and villages from the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona
Lombardy's Most Beautiful Villages in 12 Days

visits.text: Vasto - a medieval town perched on a 144-meter promontory overlooking the Adriatic Sea, founded by the Romans as Histonium. The historic center preserves its medieval street layout with Renaissance palaces and cobblestone lanes paved with river stones.

Castello Caldoresco - a 15th-century fortress built by Giacomo Caldora, Lord of Abruzzo, on the ruins of a Norman tower. Four corner towers and a central keep dominate the town. Now houses the Museo Civico and its Roman archaeological collection.

Loggia Amblingh - a 16th-century Renaissance gallery, the sole surviving remnant of the D'Avalos palace. This Spanish-origin family ruled Vasto for two centuries. The poet Gabriele D'Annunzio, a native of Abruzzo, used to stroll here as a child during visits to the town.

Punta Penna and lighthouse - a rocky point at the southern tip of the promontory, offering views of the Trabocchi Coast to the north and the Tremiti Islands on clear days. The lighthouse dates from 1905.

activities.text: Stroll along the Lungomare di Vasto - the seafront promenade below the promontory, with Vasto Marina beach and its 8 km of fine sand.

Explore the Costa dei Trabocchi heading north - this coastline, designated a nature reserve, is famous for the trabocchi, ancient fishing machines on stilts planted in the sea, some dating back to the 17th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Italy's Most Beautiful Villages in Abruzzo

places.name: Venafro

visits.text: Castello Pandone: 16th-century Renaissance residence, rebuilt by Enrico Pandone on a Norman fortress. The hall of honor preserves a fresco cycle depicting the count's 44 horses with their names inscribed—unique documentation of southern Renaissance horse breeding.
Museo Nazionale del Molise (at Castello Pandone): regional archaeological and artistic collections, including Roman pieces from Venafrum, a Roman colony founded in the 1st century BC.
Roman amphitheater of Venafro (1st century AD): 95 × 75m oval, could accommodate 10,000 spectators. One of southern Italy's best-preserved Roman amphitheaters after Capua's.
Venafro historic center: nicknamed "the city of 33 churches," dense medieval fabric with Romanesque portals, baronial palaces and Norman fortification remnants.

activities.text: Panoramic walk on Castello Pandone's ramparts: views over the Volturno valley and the Mainarde mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta Venafro

places.name: Venezia

visits.text: The Grand Canal is Venice's main artery: 3.8 km long, 50 to 70 m wide, 5 to 10 m deep, in an inverted S-shape. It's lined by more than 170 remarkable buildings constructed between the 13th and 18th centuries. Four bridges span it: the Rialto (1591, resting on 12,000 elm piles), the Accademia (1854), the Scalzi (1934), and the Constitution (2008). Vaporetto line 1 goes up the canal from the station to San Marco, stopping at every stop—the slowest and most beautiful way to discover the city.

St. Mark's Basilica was founded in 828 to house the relics of Saint Mark, stolen from Alexandria by Venetian merchants that same year. Rebuilt after a fire in 976, it was consecrated in 1094 in its current form. Its interior decoration covers 4,240 m² of mosaics. The Pala d'Oro, a Byzantine gold altar retable, is set with 1,300 pearls, 300 emeralds, 300 sapphires, and 400 garnets. The four bronze horses on the façade are copies—the originals, dating from the 2nd-3rd century AD, are preserved in the interior museum to protect them from pollution.

The Doge's Palace (Palazzo Ducale) was the seat of the Venetian Republic's government for nearly a thousand years. Its Venetian Gothic style—arcades in white and pink marble lace, openwork loggia—is one of the most accomplished examples of this style in the world. The Hall of the Great Council (1,300 m²), the largest room in Europe at the time of its construction, could accommodate up to 1,800 patricians. The Bridge of Sighs (1600) connects the palace to the prisons and owes its name to the supposed sighs of condemned prisoners glimpsing the lagoon for the last time.

The sestiere Dorsoduro (hard back) takes its name from the rockier nature of its soil, making it less subject to acqua alta than other districts. It concentrates three major cultural institutions: the Gallerie dell'Accademia (Venetian painting from the 14th to 18th century, including Bellini, Giorgione, Titian, and Veronese), the Peggy Guggenheim Collection installed in her unfinished palace on the Grand Canal, and the Basilica of Santa Maria della Salute—a Baroque dome built between 1631 and 1687 in thanksgiving for the end of the 1630 plague, resting on more than one million alder wood piles.

Murano is an island 1.5 km north of Venice, famous for its glassmaking since 1291—the year the Venetian Republic ordered the transfer of all the city's furnaces to the island to reduce fire risks. Master glassmakers there enjoyed exceptional privileges: their daughters could marry Venetian nobles. The Museo del Vetro traces the evolution of the technique since Antiquity. The Basilica of Santi Maria e Donato (12th century) preserves a Byzantine floor mosaic among the best preserved in Italy.

Burano is an island 7 km from Venice, recognizable by its brightly painted houses—red, yellow, blue, green. Tradition has it that fishermen chose these colors to find their own house in the lagoon fog. The island is also known for its needle lace, inherited from the 16th century, displayed at the Scuola dei Merletti (lace school). Its bell tower leans noticeably—visible from anywhere on the island.

Torcello is the first inhabited island of the Venetian lagoon, settled as early as the 5th-6th century by refugees fleeing barbarian invasions. Today almost deserted (about ten inhabitants), it preserves the Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta—founded in 639, the oldest in the lagoon—with its 11th-12th century Byzantine mosaics including a Last Judgment on the counter-façade. The cathedral also contains Attila's Throne, a carved stone block that tradition says served the Hun chief—actually a seat reserved for medieval judges.

The Rialto Bridge was built between 1588 and 1591 by architect Antonio Da Ponte, after a long debate between a single arch (simpler for boats) and three arches (more stable). The single arch won—48 m span, without joints, the bridge has stood for over four centuries. It rests on 12,000 elm piles. It's the first stone bridge over the Grand Canal, preceded by wooden bridges that regularly collapsed from the 12th century onward. Two rows of shops frame the central passage.

The Rialto market is divided into two side-by-side parts: the Pescheria (fish market) in a neo-Gothic building from 1907, and the Erberia (vegetables and fruits). Commercial activity at Rialto dates back to the 10th century, when this slightly elevated district was the first colonized—Venice was actually called Rialto (from Rivus Altus, high shore) in its early centuries. The market is active Tuesday to Saturday, early morning until noon.

The sestiere of Cannaregio, in northern Venice, houses the world's oldest Jewish ghetto—founded in 1516 when the Venetian Republic concentrated the Jewish population in an area isolated by gates locked at night. The word ghetto comes from the Venetian term for foundry (geto), as the area previously housed a foundry. Five synagogues dating from the 16th and 17th centuries still stand and some remain active.

The sestiere of Castello, in the eastern part of Venice, houses the Arsenal—a military shipyard founded in the 12th century that enabled Venice to dominate the Mediterranean. At its peak in the 15th century, it employed 16,000 workers (the arsenalotti) capable of assembling a complete warship in a single day. Dante drew inspiration from it to describe Hell in the Divine Comedy. The Arsenal buildings are undergoing cultural reconversion.

activities.text: Cross the Grand Canal by traghetto: a large gondola with two oarsmen that connects the two banks for €2, in 3 minutes. You travel standing, like the Venetians. Several crossing points exist between Santa Sofia and San Tomà. It's the only way to cross the canal without using one of the four bridges.

Visit St. Mark's Basilica early in the morning (opening 9:45am) or book a slot online to avoid lines. Basic entry is free but access to the museum, Pala d'Oro, and horses is paid separately. No entry with luggage—paid lockers are available nearby.

Attend a glass-blowing demonstration in one of the workshops open to the public in Murano. Most workshops offer free demonstrations, with an attached shop. Master glassmakers work glass molten at 1,400°C—a technique whose secrets were so jealously guarded that artisans who fled Murano risked the death penalty under the Venetian Republic.

Organize your day by vaporetto from Fondamente Nove: Murano in the morning (line 4.1), then Burano (line 12 from Murano or Fondamente Nove), then Torcello 5 minutes from Burano by shuttle boat. The return is via Burano and Fondamente Nove. Plan a full day—boats are not very frequent to Torcello.

Get lost in the calli (alleyways) and campi (small squares) of San Polo and Dorsoduro, far from the main tourist arteries. Venice has about 3,000 streets, most less than 2 m wide. Moving away from San Marco and the Rialto, the city becomes a village again: children play on the campi, residents do their shopping, delivery boats supply grocery stores. It's the Venice that tourists who stick to the main routes never see.

Head to Piazzale Roma (bus and car terminal) or Santa Lucia station by vaporetto or on foot. From Piazzale Roma, trains connect Venice to Milan (2h20), Bologna (1h40), or Rome (4h). For Marco Polo airport, the Alilaguna water bus directly connects St. Mark's Square in 1h15, or an ACTV bus from Piazzale Roma in 25 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice

map_keywords: Chiesa Santa Maria e San Donato Murano

visits.text: Thirty minutes east and you're in Venosa, ranked among Italy's most beautiful towns. Founded as a Roman colony (Venusia) in 291 BC, it's the birthplace of the poet Horace (65-8 BC)—a statue depicts him in the central square. The "House of Horace," reconstructed using experimental archaeological techniques on the foundations of a 2nd-century AD domus, can be visited. This is also where Hannibal ambushed two Roman consuls in 208 BC.

The Archaeological Park of Venosa comprises the remains of ancient Venusia: a Roman amphitheater, baths, a domus with mosaic fragments, and sections of the Via Appia that crossed the city. But the crown jewel is the Abbey of the Trinity complex. The old church (5th-6th century) is built on the ruins of a Roman temple and houses the tombs of the Hautevilles—the Norman conquerors of southern Italy, including Robert Guiscard. The church is still active, run by the Trinitarian Order.

Just behind it, the Incompiuta (the Unfinished) is a striking sight. Begun in the late 11th century to replace the old church that had become too small, this basilica was never completed. You walk between massive stone walls and pillars that support nothing—the sky is the ceiling, grass the floor. The columns and capitals recycled from Roman, Lombard, and Norman monuments create a unique architectural blend. It's one of the most moving sites in all of Basilicata.

In central Venosa, the Castello Pirro del Balzo (1470) imposes itself with its square plan flanked by four cylindrical towers and its drawbridge over the moat. It houses the National Archaeological Museum with coins, pottery, and funerary elements. If you have time, visit the Jewish catacombs (3rd-7th century), testimony to one of Italy's oldest Jewish communities.

activities.text: Enjoy the evening tasting Aglianico del Vulture, Basilicata's great red wine. This grape variety grown on Vulture's volcanic soils produces tannic, deep wines often compared to Piedmont's Barolo. Several wineries around Venosa and Rionero in Vulture offer tastings. Pair your glass with soppressata, caciocavallo podolico, and taralli with fennel seeds.

days_plan.itin_title: Basilicata: From Matera to Pollino, Castles, Ghost Towns, and the Lucanian Dolomites

visits.text: Mura medievali di Venzone - The only 14th-century fortified borough preserved intact in the entire region, Venzone is ringed by a double 13th-century wall pierced by three gates: Porta San Genesio, Porta Italia, and Porta Friuli. In 1258, Patriarch of Aquileia Gregorio da Montelongo granted the borough fortified city status. Declared a National Monument in 1965 and ranked among the "Borghi più Belli d'Italia" (voted Italy's most beautiful borough in 2017), Venzone occupies a strategic position at the confluence of the Tagliamento valley and the Canal di Ferro, on the route of the ancient Amber Road linking the Baltic to Aquileia.

Duomo di Sant'Andrea Apostolo - Consecrated in 1338 by Patriarch Bertrando di San Genesio, this Gothic cathedral was almost entirely destroyed by the May 6, 1976 earthquake (magnitude 6.5), then finished off by September 15 aftershocks. Reconstruction by anastylosis—each hewn stone replaced in its exact original position—mobilized 9,500 blocks inventoried one by one thanks to photographic archives. The work, completed in 1995, is considered one of the world's greatest examples of medieval building reconstruction. The Baroque organ dates from 1792.

Cappella cimiteriale di San Michele - A 13th-century rotunda built on the Duomo's forecourt, this chapel shelters five natural mummies visible to the public in its crypt (out of some forty discovered since the 17th century). The first, nicknamed "il Gobbo" (the Hunchback), was found in 1647 during expansion work. Mummification was caused by a rare fungus, Hypha Bombicina Pers, which dehydrates tissues in about one year. The bodies, dating from the 14th to 19th centuries, weigh between 4 and 8 kg. Napoleon's soldiers in 1797 were so impressed by "il Gobbo" that they removed strips of skin as souvenirs—since then, the mummies wear small protective aprons.

Palazzo Orgnani-Martina - Tiere Motus - This historic center palace houses the permanent museum dedicated to the 1976 earthquake and Friuli's reconstruction. The route traces the stages: the shock of May 6, the summer under tents, the September aftershocks that finished destroying what May had spared, the exodus of 40,000 people to the beach resorts of Grado and Lignano, and complete reconstruction in ten years following the principle "first factories, then houses, finally churches." A multimedia room projects the sequence of tremors on a regional model and displays the names of 989 victims.

activities.text: Walk through the borough rebuilt stone by stone - Stroll lanes where every façade results from meticulous reconstruction work. New stones are still distinguishable from original ones by their slightly different surface: this deliberate choice lets visitors "read" the earthquake's history on the walls. Four 15th-century votive chapels dot a trail on the surrounding heights, following the route of an ancient Celtic path.

days_plan.itin_title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures

places.name: Verbania

visits.text: Lose yourself in the magnificent Botanical Gardens of Villa Taranto, twenty hectares of pure wonder on the shores of Lake Maggiore. Created by Scottish Captain McEacharn from 1931, these enchanting English-style gardens feature labyrinths of dahlias, water lily pools, valleys of rhododendrons, waterfalls and fountains in a place where care and love for nature are palpable at every turn.

Visit Villa San Remigio on the Colle della Castagnola, built in the early twentieth century by the poet and musician Marquis Silvio della Valle di Casanova and the painter Sofia Browne. Their dream garden is a true hymn to love, composed of several themed gardens overlooking the lake in a romantic atmosphere suspended in time.

Discover Villa Giulia and its park in the heart of Pallanza, built in the mid-nineteenth century by the Branca family, inventors of the famous Fernet. Now municipal property, it hosts temporary exhibitions while the park, always open and free, offers a privileged view of the lake and the picturesque village of Pallanza.

activities.text: Walk the lakeside promenade from Verbania to Pallanza, a pedestrian and cycle path of about 2 km offering breathtaking panoramas, ancient villas and dream palaces. An itinerary for daydreaming of a less hectic life on the lakefront, imagining yourself in an age of noble titles and grand balls.

Venture along the path to the Church of Buon Rimedio, an ancient mule track through jasmine-scented woods to a small eighteenth-century church. From this natural balcony overlooking Lake Maggiore you can enjoy breathtaking panoramic views and picnic at the equipped area with lakeside tables.

Explore the historic village of Pallanza with the Museo del Paesaggio housed in the Palazzo Viani Dugnani, rich in paintings, sculptures and archaeological finds. Then treat yourself to a stop at one of the lakeside terraces on the main square to savour the relaxed atmosphere of this enchanting corner of Piedmont.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona

map_keywords: Santuario Madonna del Buon Rimedio Verbania

visits.text: The Abbey of Casamari, 10 km east of Veroli, was founded in 1053 on the site of Cereatae Marianae, a Roman municipium dedicated to the goddess Ceres and birthplace of Caius Marius—the Roman general who was consul seven times.
