Originally Benedictine, it passed to the Cistercians in 1144 after Bernard of Clairvaux advocated for this affiliation during the schism of Anacletus II. Reconstruction in Burgundian Cistercian Gothic was carried out between 1203 and 1217. A national monument since 1874.

The cloister of Casamari is a square of galleries with pointed arches typical of Cistercian architecture: alabaster windows diffuse golden light, sober capitals without figurative decoration (Cistercian rule), and a central fountain in the courtyard. The abbey church with three naves reproduces identically the plan of Fossanova Abbey—the only other preserved example in Italy.

On May 13, 1799, French soldiers pillaged the abbey and killed six monks, known since their beatification on April 17, 2021 as the Martyrs of Casamari. Napoleon definitively closed the monastery in 1811; it only reopened in 1929 and today houses the headquarters of the Congregation of Casamari, which manages a dozen abbeys and, since 1930, a mission in Ethiopia.

Veroli's historic center preserves several witnesses to Roman antiquity: polygonal walls supporting the acropolis, remains of the ancient forum beneath the cathedral, and Latin inscriptions reused in medieval palace facades.

activities.text: The monastic pharmacy of Casamari and its liqueur laboratory are open for visits. The monks have distilled several preparations from Ernici Mountain herbs for generations; products are sold at the abbey shop.

days_plan.itin_title: Ciociaria Circuit: Abbeys and Medieval Hilltop Villages

visits.text: The Arena di Verona is a Roman amphitheater built around 30 AD in pink marble from Valpolicella, the third largest in Italy after the Colosseum and the amphitheater of Capua. Its exterior dimensions reach 152 × 128 m and 32 m in height, with a cavea that could accommodate 30,000 spectators in Roman times. An earthquake in 1117 destroyed almost the entire outer ring—only a fragment of four arches survives, visible from Piazza Bra. The interior, remarkably well-preserved, has hosted an open-air opera festival since 1913 that welcomes up to 22,000 spectators per evening.

Piazza delle Erbe occupies the site of the ancient Roman forum and constitutes Verona's oldest square. It's bordered by 16th-century frescoes of Casa Mazzanti, Palazzo Maffei and its 17th-century Baroque façade, and the Torre dei Lamberti (accessible by 368 steps or elevator, panoramic view over the rooftops). At the center, the Madonna Verona Fountain dates from 380 AD. Under the arch leading to Piazza dei Signori, a suspended whale rib has served as a talisman since the Middle Ages—according to tradition, anyone who lies while touching it will have it fall on them.

Piazza dei Signori, called Dante Square due to the poet's statue at its center, was the seat of power for the Scaligeri (della Scala), lords of Verona from 1262 to 1387. On its eastern side open the Arche Scaligere, an ensemble of Gothic sarcophagi suspended among spires and pinnacles—funeral monuments of the della Scala lords, considered among the masterpieces of 14th-century Italian Gothic sculpture.

The Casa di Giulietta at 23 Via Cappello is a 13th-century medieval palace that belonged to the Dal Cappello family—Shakespeare's Capulets. The famous balcony was added in the 1930s to meet the influx of visitors. In the courtyard, the bronze statue of Juliet has been replaced by a copy: the original was too worn from the tradition of touching her right breast, supposedly bringing good luck in love. The entrance walls are covered with love messages left by visitors from around the world.

Castelvecchio is a fortress built between 1354 and 1356 by Cangrande II della Scala on the north bank of the Adige. It now houses the Civic Museum of Verona, one of the most important art collections in Veneto with works by Mantegna, Giovanni Bellini, Pisanello, Veronese, and Tintoretto. The Ponte Scaligero (1356), reconstructed identically in 1951 after its destruction during World War II, connects the fortress to the opposite bank and offers one of the city's most photographed panoramas.

activities.text: Attend an open-air opera at the Arena: the Verona Festival runs from June to early September with productions of Aida, Carmen, or Turandot staged in this Roman setting. The performance begins at sunset and spectators traditionally bring candles to illuminate the tiers at the beginning of the evening—a truly unique atmosphere.

Stroll through the historic center between Piazza Bra, Piazza delle Erbe, and Piazza dei Signori in the evening. The three squares are connected by pedestrian streets animated by shops and restaurants—Verona has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2000 for the exceptional density of its Roman, medieval, and Renaissance heritage in such a compact space.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Italian Lakes & Dolomites Road Trip: Piedmont to Venice
Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona
Italy Road Trip: From Trentino to the Amalfi Coast

visits.text: Rocca Malatestiana di Verucchio - Perched on a rocky spur at 330 meters above sea level commanding the entire Marecchia valley to the sea, this fortress is known as the 'Rocca del Sasso' (Rock Fortress). This is where the Malatesta dynasty was born—Dante cites in the Inferno the 'Mastin Vecchio' of Verucchio, father of Gianciotto and Paolo, protagonists of the tragedy of Paolo and Francesca. Sigismondo Pandolfo Malatesta renovated the rocca between 1442 and 1449, turning it into a defensive stronghold facing the Montefeltro of Urbino.

Castle of Montebello (Poggio Torriana) - A ten-minute drive from Verucchio, this rocca perched at 436 meters dominates the valleys of the Marecchia and Uso rivers. Built in the 12th century around a 3rd-century BC Roman tower (the Latin name Mons Belli means 'mountain of war'), the fortress passed from the Malatesta to the Guidi di Bagno and then to the Montefeltro. It owes its fame to the legend of Azzurrina: on June 21, 1375, the summer solstice, little Guendalina, daughter of lord Uguccione, born albino with bluish reflections, disappeared into the castle's icehouse and was never found. A 17th-century account recorded the story and gave her the nickname 'Azzurrina' (Little Azure). Tradition holds that her ghost manifests every five years on the summer solstice. Period furnishings (14th–18th century) and nighttime ghost tours make this one of Italy's most famous haunted castles.

Civic Archaeological Museum - Installed since 1985 in the former Augustinian monastery (13th century), this museum displays across three floors and eight rooms the treasures from the Villanovan necropolises excavated at the foot of Verucchio. Over 600 burials dating from the 10th to the 7th century BC yielded objects of exceptional preservation: a wooden throne carved with human figures (a unique piece in the world), textiles, baskets of plant fibers, jewelry in gold and Baltic amber. Verucchio controlled the Adriatic trade routes for amber between Greece, the Orient, and northern Europe.

Convent of San Francesco at Villa Verucchio - At the foot of the village, this convent houses in its cloister a monumental cypress tree estimated to be about 700 years old, planted according to tradition by Saint Francis of Assisi himself. The church preserves a 14th-century Crucifix.

activities.text: Walk through the medieval village - Stone lanes climbing toward the rocca offer views over the valley and, on clear days, to the nearby Republic of San Marino. The route passes the church of Sant'Agostino, the monastery of the Poor Clares, and several 15th–16th-century palaces.

days_plan.itin_title: Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages

places.name: Vetralla

visits.text: Historic center of Vetralla - A major stop on the Via Francigena, Vetralla occupies a hill inhabited since the 6th century BC. Enter through Piazza Marconi and its medieval fountain, then follow Via Cassia Interna: Palazzo Franciosoni (Vignola school), the 18th-century Duomo, and two baroque fountains on Piazza Umberto I punctuate the walk.

Church of San Francesco - Vetralla's oldest, erected in the 7th century then rebuilt in Romanesque style in 1207. The peperino stone facade incorporates Roman fragments from the nearby Forum Cassii. Inside: a Cosmatesque pavement, a marble tabernacle from Andrea Bregno's workshop (15th century), and the sarcophagus of condottiere Briobris, who died in 1353 at age 33.

Etruscan Necropolis of Norchia - 15 km from Vetralla, one of Etruria's most spectacular necropolises. The tombs (4th–2nd centuries BC) are carved into tufa cliffs: some reproduce Doric temple facades with pediments, friezes, and busts. The Lattanzi tomb, dated around 320 BC, featured a facade nearly 18 meters high with two levels of columns. The Via Cava Buia, a 400-meter gallery cut through rock with 10-meter-high walls, once led to the city. A semi-abandoned site—bring sturdy shoes.

activities.text: Forum Cassii and Santa Maria di Forcassi - 2 km from Vetralla, the ruins of the ancient Roman mansio (relay station on the Via Cassia) appear on the Tabula Peutingeriana, Europe's oldest road atlas. The medieval chapel preserves 14th-century frescoes beneath a partially collapsed roof. The Order of Hospitallers cared for pilgrims here during the Middle Ages.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio

map_keywords: Forum Cassii Santa Maria di Forcassi

places.name: Vetulonia

visits.text: Vetulonia—Vatluna in Etruscan—was one of the twelve cities of the Etruscan League, prospering from the 9th to 3rd centuries BC through metalwork and maritime trade via Lake Prile, now silted by alluvium. The city was rediscovered in 1880 by physician Isidoro Falchi, who identified the site beneath the medieval village of Colonna di Buriano. The archaeological zone of Poggiarello Renzetti, below the village, reveals the remains of the main street and Etruscan shops. The cyclopean walls of the Mura dell'Arce, which encircled a living space of about 16 hectares, are partially visible in the village center.

The necropolis spreads across the hills at the village's foot along the Via dei Sepolcri. The Tomba della Pietrera is the site's largest funerary monument: two tholoi stacked one atop the other, surrounded by a tumulus 60 meters in diameter, dating from the 7th century BC. One of the eight statues that once decorated it—a mourning figure—is now visible in Florence. The Tomba del Diavolino II, accessible through a long partially uncovered corridor, is another major tumulus from the same period. The panorama from the tombs embraces the Grosseto plain, the opposite hill of Roselle, and the sea in the distance.

The Civic Archaeological Museum Isidoro Falchi, dedicated to the archaeologist who rediscovered the city, houses a remarkable collection of objects from excavations: bronze banquet ware, jewelry, helmets, Greek and Phoenician imports testifying to Vetulonia's commercial openness across the entire Mediterranean.

activities.text: Walk along the Mura dell'Arce walls and through the medieval village's narrow streets perched on its hill, with views over the Maremma plain and the Tyrrhenian Sea. The village retains a preserved atmosphere, far removed from the usual tourist circuits.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province

map_keywords: Area Archeologica di Vetulonia Castiglione della Pescaia
Museo Civico Archeologico Isidoro Falchi Vetulonia

places.name: Via Dei Condotti

visits.text: Via dei Condotti takes its name from the condotti—the conduits of the Aqua Virgo aqueduct, built in 19 BC by Agrippa to supply the thermal baths in the area. Transformed into a fashion artery in the 18th century following the construction of the nearby scalinata, it became one of the three 'Trident streets' converging on Piazza di Spagna. In 1905, Bulgari opened its first workshop at number 10; in 1921, Guccio Gucci inaugurated his first boutique at number 8. The apartment where Guglielmo Marconi, inventor of the radio, lived for several years sits on this same street.

activities.text: Stroll through the Trident: beyond Via dei Condotti, the parallel streets Via Borgognona and Via Frattina extend the neighborhood experience toward Via del Corso with its 18th-century palaces and balustrade façades.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Piazza San Lorenzo in Lucina Roma

places.name: Vicchio

visits.text: Vicchio is the birthplace of two painting giants: Giotto di Bondone (born around 1267 in Vespignano) and Fra Angelico (born around 1395 in Vicchio). The Giotto House-Museum in Vespignano—a medieval farmhouse on the hill where, according to tradition, Cimabue discovered him drawing his sheep on stones—is now a cultural space open on weekends and during events. Vasari recounts this tradition and turned it into an emblematic story of artistic transmission. A bronze statue of Giotto with palette and brushes stands in Vicchio's main square.

The Museum of Sacred Art and Popular Religiosity Beato Angelico, inaugurated in 2000, was created in the 1960s to save artworks from rural churches abandoned during agricultural exodus. It preserves paintings and sculptures from the Middle Ages to the 18th century: a glazed terracotta Madonna and Child attributed to Andrea della Robbia, a Saint John the Baptist from the same workshop, and Gothic altarpieces from about fifteen Mugello churches. Of note: the former house of Benvenuto Cellini, who stayed in Vicchio between 1559 and 1571, now houses a goldsmithing school.

activities.text: Painters' Trail: a marked hiking route linking Vespignano to the Cimabue Bridge, a late 13th-century medieval bridge over the Sieve River, symbolic site of the legendary meeting between Cimabue and the young Giotto. The route crosses the same landscapes of cultivated hills that inspired the painters of Mugello.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

map_keywords: Pieve di San Giovanni Battista Vicchio

visits.text: Teatro Olimpico - Climb the steps from Piazza Matteotti and step into one of the world's last surviving Renaissance theaters. Palladio designed it in 1580 for the Accademia Olimpica within the walls of the old medieval castle prison, but died just three months after construction began. His son Silla and then Vincenzo Scamozzi completed the building, inaugurated on March 3, 1585 with a performance of Sophocles' Oedipus Rex. The stage is the highlight: seven wooden and stucco corridors painted in trompe-l'oeil recreate the seven streets of Thebes in just 12 meters of actual depth, creating the illusion of perspectives stretching to infinity. This set, installed for the opening performance, is the world's oldest surviving stage scenery still in its original location. The elliptical cavea with tiered seating draws inspiration from ancient Roman theaters like those described by Vitruvius in his treatise De Architectura. The building still hosts performances and concerts today.

Basilica Palladiana - On Piazza dei Signori, this building marks Palladio's consecration as the city's architect. In 1546, the Council of One Hundred chose the 38-year-old architect to save the old Gothic Palazzo della Ragione, one corner of which had collapsed. His solution—a double marble loggia in white Istrian stone punctuated by the serliana, an arched central opening flanked by two rectangular bays—wraps the medieval building without destroying it. It took 60,000 ducats and sixty-five years of construction to complete the ensemble in 1614, thirty years after Palladio's death. The great Salone on the first floor measures 52 meters long by 22 wide, covered by an inverted ship's hull wooden ceiling. Climb to the upper loggia for views over the rooftops and the 12th-century bell tower. The ground floor houses the Jewelry Museum across 410 square meters.

Loggia del Capitaniato - Facing the Basilica across Piazza dei Signori stands this loggia that Palladio designed in 1571 to serve as residence for Venice's governor of the city. Only three of the planned five bays were built, but the facade is one of the architect's most expressive: colossal composite half-columns, projecting balconies, reliefs celebrating the victory at the Battle of Lepanto (1571) against the Ottomans. The broken entablature and sculptural details anticipate the Baroque. Palladio himself never saw it completed.

Palazzo Barbaran da Porto (Museo Palladio) - Walk down Corso Palladio to this palace built between 1569 and 1575 for Montano Barbarano, one of the few Palladian buildings that Palladio personally supervised to an advanced stage. The facade creates a dialogue between two superimposed orders—Ionic below, Corinthian above—with reliefs of exceptional precision. The interior now houses the Museo Palladio: models, original drawings and videos connect the architect's work with his heirs from the White House to the University of Virginia.

Palazzo Chiericati - At the far end of Piazza Matteotti, this palace commissioned by Count Girolamo Chiericati in 1550 stands as a masterpiece of Palladian civic architecture. Its two-level facade of colonnaded loggias was originally built at the edge of a river port. On the ground floor, Doric columns support an open colonnade; on the upper floor, the central rooms advance onto a covered Ionic terrace. The building houses Vicenza's Civic Museums with works by Memling, Tintoretto, Veronese and Giambattista Tiepolo.

Chiesa di Santa Corona - Walk up Corso Palladio to this Dominican church founded in 1261 by Bishop Bartolomeo da Breganze to house a thorn from Christ's crown, brought from Paris as a gift from Saint Louis. Its Gothic naves conceal two treasures: the Baptism of Christ by Giovanni Bellini (1502), painted when the master was over seventy, and the Adoration of the Magi by Paolo Veronese (1573). Both works remain in the chapels for which they were commissioned.

Villa Rotonda (Villa Almerico Capra) - Take the road climbing south from the city to reach this villa commissioned in 1566 by Paolo Almerico, a retired Vatican ecclesiastic, on a hill overlooking the Vicentine countryside. Palladio himself classified it as a palazzo rather than a villa: no agricultural outbuildings, no utilitarian function, only beauty. The plan is unique in architectural history: a perfect square with four identical facades, each featuring a six-column Ionic portico, all converging on a circular central hall topped by a dome. The villa is oriented 45 degrees from the cardinal points to capture sunlight at every hour. Its influence is immense: Thomas Jefferson drew on it for his own home at Monticello in Virginia, and five copies exist in England alone, including Chiswick House in London.

Villa Valmarana ai Nani - A few hundred meters from La Rotonda, this 17th-century villa is famous for its frescoes, painted in 1757 by Giambattista Tiepolo in the main palazzina and by his son Giandomenico in the foresteria (guest quarters). Giambattista's frescoes depict scenes from the Iliad, the Aeneid, Jerusalem Delivered and Orlando Furioso with the luminous palette characteristic of Venetian Rococo. Giandomenico's in the foresteria show scenes of peasant life and commedia dell'arte characters—a radical break from his father's heroic register. The name 'ai Nani' comes from twenty-one stone dwarf statues lined along the perimeter wall, protagonists of a local legend.

Santuario della Madonna di Monte Berico - Climb on foot or by car to this sanctuary perched on Monte Berico hill, accessible from the center via a long covered portico of 150 arches built in the 18th century. The current church dates from 1703 and replaces a 15th-century Gothic building constructed following a Marian apparition in 1426, during a plague epidemic. The interior preserves a Pietà by Bartolomeo Montagna (1500) and a painting by Paolo Veronese depicting the Feast of St. Gregory (1572). The terrace offers panoramic views of Vicenza, the Venetian plain and the Prealps.

activities.text: Stroll along Corso Palladio - The historic center's main artery connects Piazza del Castello to Piazza Matteotti over roughly 600 meters. Lined with Renaissance and medieval palaces, it's the city's pedestrian heart. Note Palazzo Porto-Colleoni at No. 19 (late 15th-century facade), Palazzo Pojana, and at the street's end, the unfinished fragment of Palazzo Porto-Breganze, Palladio's last project, of which only two of seven bays were built by Scamozzi.

Piazza dei Signori at dusk - The square was the Roman forum of the city founded in the 2nd century BC. At sunset, the two Venetian columns—one topped by the winged lion of St. Mark, the other by Christ the Redeemer—catch a golden light. Locals fill the terraces beneath the Basilica's arcades for aperitivo, following a habit that dates back centuries: the shops under the loggias were already active in Palladio's time.

Ponte di San Michele and the Retrone river - Walk down to this bridge built between 1621 and 1623 to span the Retrone River. The view from the banks reveals the rear facades of medieval palaces lining the waterway: a rarely photographed image of Vicenza, quite different from that of the grand squares.

Giardini Salvi - These historic gardens, 500 meters from the train station, are laid out around a canal and a Palladian loggia built by Lorenzo Rubini in the 16th century. The adjacent English garden dates from the 19th century. Ideal for a break after morning visits before heading out to the villas.

Cycling excursion in the Colli Berici - The hills bordering Vicenza to the south, between 100 and 400 meters altitude, are crossed by quiet roads passing through medieval villages and vineyards producing DOC Colli Berici (Garganega, Tai Rosso, Cabernet). Local outfitters offer road bikes or mountain bikes with marked routes. The climb to Brendola or Barbarano offers views over the Po plain.

Parco Querini - This vast 13-hectare park in the heart of the city is laid out as an English garden around a small artificial lake, with wooded hills, canals and two small neoclassical temples. Locals use it for daily walks. A rosarium and several rare shrub species also make it a botanical site.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes
Vicenza Circuit: Palladian Villas and the Venetian Countryside
Vicenza Circuit: Palladian Villas and the Venetian Countryside

visits.text: The Rocca del Brunelleschi is one of the few military fortresses designed by Filippo Brunelleschi, commissioned by Florence after conquering Vicopisano in 1406—the siege had lasted nine months, with Florence even using a galley rowed up the Arno to cut supply lines. Brunelleschi presented his design in clay and wood before a commission that included the young Francesco Sforza, future Duke of Milan. Construction began in 1435: a 31-meter keep, a 60-meter crenellated wall descending to the Torre del Soccorso at the Arno's edge, and a drawbridge system designed to isolate sections of the fortress if enemies penetrated. Restored in 1995, open to the public since 1998.

The Palazzo Pretorio, adjacent to the Rocca, houses a permanent exhibition of excavations from the San Michele Arcangelo alla Verruca monastery. Its walls still bear marks from centuries of different uses: seat of the podestà, prison, residence of the Florentine vicar.

Vicopisano's medieval center preserves 13 towers from the 11th-15th centuries, including the 12th-century Clock Tower, converted into a bell tower. Walking the narrow streets reveals layers of successive defenses: Pisan walls, Florentine additions, filled moats. Dante mentions the nearby Caprona castle in the Divine Comedy.

activities.text: Panoramic view from the Rocca's wall-walk across the Arno plain, the Pisan Mountains, and on clear days, to the sea. From the signal tower, messages to Florence once departed in visual code using flags, smoke, and artillery fire.

Olive oil tasting from the Pisan Mountains at San Giovanni alla Vena, a Vicopisano hamlet known for its Arno clay and ceramic tradition. Olives are hand-harvested following the tradition of the Pisan Mountains Oil Route.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province

places.name: Vieste

visits.text: Vieste historic center (Vieste Vecchia): perched on the eastern tip of the Gargano promontory, this labyrinth of whitewashed lanes sprawls across two rocky headlands. The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta, episcopal seat from 918 to 1817, dates to the 11th century. Inside stands a wooden statue of the Virgin of Merino, object of local pilgrimage for centuries.

Pizzomunno monolith and Castello Beach: this 25-meter limestone sea stack rises directly from the sand at the town's edge. Local legend tells of a lover turned to stone when sirens dragged his beloved into the sea. The nearby Swabian castle, built by Frederick II then rebuilt by the Spanish in 1537, commands the promontory from its triangular perch.

Baia delle Zagare and Gargano north coast: between Vieste and Mattinata, the coastal road traces white limestone cliffs plunging into the Adriatic. Baia delle Zagare (Bay of Orange Blossoms) is framed by two faraglioni rising straight from the sea. Spiaggia di Vignanotica, reached via a steep path from a clifftop parking area, offers white pebbles and turquoise water hemmed by towering cliffs riddled with caves.

Peschici historic center: ranked among Italy's most beautiful villages, this medieval town clings to a cliff above the sea, its whitewashed houses topped with Arab-influenced flat roofs. A small harbor at the cliff's base serves as departure point for excursions to surrounding coves.

San Nicola Island — Abbey of Santa Maria a Mare: San Nicola concentrates the entire history of the archipelago. The Benedictine abbey, founded in the 9th century and rebuilt many times since, has crowned this rock for a thousand years. Inside, a mosaic floor and a Byzantine-era wooden cross bear witness to its long past. The island served as a place of exile since Roman times — Emperor Augustus banished his granddaughter Julia here for adultery, where she died after twenty years of confinement.

San Domino Island — pine forest and coves: largest of the five islands, San Domino is blanketed in Aleppo pine that descends to the sea. Three marked trails cross the island end to end. The archipelago's only sandy beach lies here: Cala delle Arene. Cars are forbidden — only residents may drive.

activities.text: Sunset walk along Lungomare Enrico Mattei in Vieste: this seaside boulevard runs between the town's two beach zones. Watch fishermen haul in their nets while the trabucchi — ancient wooden fishing machines on stilts, now protected heritage of Gargano National Park — cast their silhouettes across the water.

Boat excursion from Vieste to the sea caves: the coast between Vieste and Mattinata hides dozens of limestone caves accessible only by water — Grotta Sfondata, Grotta dei Due Occhi, Grotta delle Campane. Half-day guided tours depart from Vieste harbor with swimming stops in coves unreachable by road.

Ferry crossing Vieste → Tremiti (about 2 hours) and boat tour of the archipelago: from San Domino, boat excursions circle the five islands along limestone cliffs, faraglioni, and sea caves — Grotta del Bue Marino, Grotta delle Viole, Grotta delle Rondinelle. Waters here have been a protected marine reserve since 1989.

map_keywords: Abbazia Santa Maria a Mare Isole Tremiti
Grotta del Bue Marino Isole Tremiti

visits.text: Explore the fortified village of Vigoleno, certified among the Most Beautiful Villages in Italy and Orange Flag of the Italian Touring Club. This perfect example of medieval residential logic is characterized by the elegance of its forms, with imposing crenellated walls traversed by a panoramic patrol walkway.

Climb the quadrangular keep equipped with arrow slits, machicolations, and Ghibelline merlons, with four floors of visits offering spectacular views over the surrounding countryside and hills. Inside the castle, the small theater with oriental images is a hidden gem not to be missed.

Visit the Romanesque Church of San Giorgio from the second half of the 12th century, reachable by continuing east from the main square where the 16th-century fountain stands. The intimate and spiritual atmosphere of this sacred place transports you back in time.

activities.text: Taste the Vin Santo di Vigoleno, a DOC passito wine whose vinification and aging operations must be carried out exclusively in the territory of the municipality of Vernasca. A golden nectar representing the oenological excellence of the Piacenza Hills.

Walk the Brigands' Ring, a circuit of about 21 km that starts from Vigoleno and arrives in Vernasca passing through Monte Ciocca. During flowering season, the golden broom creates an unforgettable natural spectacle.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles of the Piacenza Hills: A 4-Day Medieval Road Trip Through Northern Italy
Emilia-Romagna's Hidden Treasures: Castles, Apennines & the Great Labyrinth

visits.text: Marvel at Villa Capra 'La Rotonda', Palladio's most famous villa and one of the most copied buildings in the world. Its perfectly symmetrical design, with identical temple-front porticos on all four sides, inspired countless buildings including the White House in Washington D.C.

Visit Villa Valmarana ai Nani, named for the stone dwarf statues lining its walls, featuring interior frescoes by father and son Tiepolo that rank among their finest work. The splendid garden and café overlooking Mount Berico provide a perfect setting for reflection.

activities.text: Walk between the two villas through the gentle countryside of Vicenza's outskirts, enjoying views of Mount Berico and the Church of St. Mary of Mount Berico overlooking the city from its hilltop position.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Capo Carbonara, a limestone promontory at Sardinia's southeastern tip, has been a protected marine area since 1998. Its waters shelter posidonia meadows, dolphins, and sea turtles. The 16th-century Aragonese tower crowning the cape served to signal Barbary pirate raids.
The Archaeological Museum of Villasimius preserves artifacts recovered from ancient shipwrecks in the gulf, including a 4th-century BC Punic anchor and Roman amphorae discovered in shallow waters.

activities.text: Swim at Simius Beach, a long stretch of white sand with shallow waters, bordered to the north by Notteri Lagoon where flamingos nest.
Boat excursion around Capo Carbonara to explore the marine reserve's coves and observe some of the Mediterranean's richest seabeds.
Swim at Porto Giunco, a beach separated from Notteri Lagoon by a narrow tongue of sand, with direct views of the Aragonese tower.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Sardinia Loop: Coastline, Cities & Heartland

places.name: Vinchiaturo

visits.text: Chiesa Santa Maria della Croce: dominating the village from its hilltop position, neoclassical façade and four-level bell tower. Inside, an 18th-century wooden organ and 13 tablets depicting Christ and the Apostles painted by Ciriaco Brunetti. Beneath the campanile, remnants of a tower and drawbridge reveal the medieval settlement's first fortified nucleus.

Historic center and Fontana dei Quattro Leoni: Vinchiaturo derives its name from Latin "vincula catenis" (prison chains), referencing Samnite prisoners chained here by Romans after the Samnite Wars (3rd century BC). The Palazzo Marchesale, residence of the Longo marquises until feudalism's abolition in 1806, dominates the medieval quarter.

Santuario Santa Maria delle Macchie (4 km away, at 1,000m elevation): built in the 16th century on ruins of a Benedictine monastery founded in 1163. Inside, a 14th-century Virgin statue whose restoration revealed a golden veil hidden beneath layers of paint. The site corresponds to the ancient Samnite Ruffirium, with visible remnants of megalithic walls still standing.

activities.text: Stroll through the medieval quarter's lanes, between the former Convento di Santa Lucia (17th century, now private) and the Monument to the Emigrant—memorial to waves of departure toward the Americas in the 19th and 20th centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Molise Route - 10 Days Through Italy's Hidden Region

map_keywords: Chiesa Santa Maria della Croce Vinchiaturo
Santuario Santa Maria delle Macchie Vinchiaturo

visits.text: The Museo Leonardiano occupies two buildings: the Palazzina Uzielli and the 11th-century Castello dei Conti Guidi, given to the municipality in 1919 for the 400th anniversary of Leonardo's death and opened to the public in 1953. It presents Leonardo's machines reconstructed from his notebooks—war engines, hydraulic devices, flying mechanisms—as well as a section dedicated to the Battle of Anghiari, the unfinished mural work Leonardo designed for the Salone dei Cinquecento in Palazzo Vecchio in Florence, known only through contemporary copies. The Leonardian Library, founded in 1883, preserves manuscripts and facsimiles of the codices.

The birthplace in Anchiano, 3 km from central Vinci, is the rural farmhouse where Leonardo was born on April 15, 1452. The birth was noted by his paternal grandfather Antonio da Vinci on the last page of his family notebook: 'A grandson was born to me, son of my son Ser Piero, on April 15, a Saturday at three in the morning. He received the name Lionardo.' The house, documented since 1427, was donated to the municipality in 1952 by Count Giovanni Rasini. Since 2012, a life-size holographic installation of the aging Leonardo, speaking from Amboise while looking back toward his homeland, greets visitors. The landscape of vineyards and olive groves of Montalbano has remained very close to what Leonardo knew as a child.

activities.text: Strada Verde (Green Route): a marked 3 km trail linking Vinci to Anchiano through the vineyards and olive groves of Montalbano, in the same landscape that inspired Leonardo. Details from the landscape of the Mona Lisa have been identified in this area.

days_plan.itin_title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks

places.name: Visso

visits.text: Piazza dei Martiri Vissani — one of the finest medieval squares in Le Marche. Together with the adjacent Piazza Capuzi, it forms a C-shaped space bordered by 15th and 16th-century arcaded palazzetti, carved by the local petraioli vissani stonecutters. Art historian André Chastel cited it as a prime example of the Italian genius for urban scenography.

Collegiata di Santa Maria — built in the 11th–12th century in Romanesque-Gothic style and substantially remodelled in the 13th and 14th centuries. The portal preserves an Annunciation fresco by Paolo da Visso in the lunette. The single-nave interior was reworked in the 17th century. Miraculously spared by the 2016 earthquake.

Ex-Chiesa di Sant'Agostino (Museo Civico) — a 14th-century Gothic building to the left of the collegiata. Before the earthquake it held around 200 paintings and sculptures from the 12th to 18th centuries, including works by Paolo da Visso and, most remarkably, the original manuscripts of six Idylls by Giacomo Leopardi, among them L'Infinito. These manuscripts have since been moved to Bologna for safekeeping. Partially closed since the earthquake.

Santuario della Madonna di Macereto — 9 km away, on a high plateau at 1,000 metres at the foot of Monte Bove, one of the most singular Renaissance buildings in Le Marche: an octagonal plan in white stone, built in the first half of the 16th century in the Bramantesque tradition. Isolated among high-altitude pastures, it was built, according to tradition, on the site of a Marian apparition. Partially closed since the earthquake.

activities.text: Walk the medieval ramparts and through the four gates — the walls date to the 13th century, the gates to the following century. The local stone portals are engraved with family coats of arms and Latin mottoes, evidence of a trading town that once controlled commerce between Le Marche and Umbria.

Taste the ciauscolo dei Sibillini and local cheeses — Visso is nicknamed 'the Norcia of Le Marche' for the quality of its cured meats, complemented by black truffle, mountain lentils and Sibillini honey.

days_plan.itin_title: The Hidden Villages of Le Marche

map_keywords: Santuario della Madonna di Macereto Visso

visits.text: Palazzo dei Papi (Papal Palace) - Built from 1257 onward for Pope Alexander IV fleeing Rome. The Sala del Conclave hosted the longest papal election in history: 33 months (1268–1271). Exasperated, the Viterbese locked the cardinals in—hence the word "conclave" (cum clave, "with a key")—then tore off the roof to expose them to the elements. Gregory X was finally elected. The Loggia of Blessings (1267), with its trilobed Gothic arches, offers sweeping valley views.

Cathedral of San Lorenzo - A 12th-century Romanesque edifice, remodeled during the Renaissance and Baroque periods. Tradition holds it was built atop the foundations of a temple dedicated to Hercules. In 1277, part of the adjacent palace collapsed onto Pope John XXI, killing him—the only pope to die in a construction accident.

Museo del Colle del Duomo - The ticket grants access to the palace, cathedral, and museum. Collections include Etruscan and Roman archaeological finds, papal objects, reliquaries, and paintings. French audio guide available.

San Pellegrino Quarter - One of Italy's best-preserved medieval centers. The "case a ponte" (houses connected by upper-floor arches), profferli (external stone staircases), towers, and small squares have scarcely changed since the 13th century. Every September 3rd, the Macchina di Santa Rosa—a 30-meter tower weighing 5,100 kilograms—is carried through the streets by the facchini: this ritual is inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list.

Fontana Grande and Palazzo dei Priori - Viterbo's largest fountain stands on the piazza bearing its name. The Palazzo dei Priori (seat of civil power) houses halls adorned with Mannerist frescoes and the Cappella Palatina painted by Filippo Cavarozzi.

Villa Lante in Bagnaia - 5 km from Viterbo, this Renaissance garden attributed to Vignola (1560s) is considered one of Italy's most perfect. The central water axis descends 230 meters along four terraces: cascades, a chain of water sculpted in stone, a stone table crossed by a channel (to cool dishes during open-air dinners), and Giambologna's Fountain of the Four Moors. The 16th-century hydraulic system still functions without modern pumps.

activities.text: Bathing in the Bullicame thermal springs - Mentioned by Dante in Canto XIV of Inferno and sketched by Michelangelo, these sulfurous springs gush at 58°C from a natural crater. Already frequented by Etruscans and Romans, they attracted numerous popes: Nicholas V built a thermal palace in 1450. Several open-air pools are free to access (Piscine Carletti, Bullicame), open year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio
Grand Tour of Lazio: Etruscan Sites and Volcanic Lakes

visits.text: Step into Serravalle (now part of Vittorio Veneto), a medieval fairytale town that ranks among the most picturesque in Italy. Cobbled streets, stone houses with frescoed facades, and an ancient castle create an atmospheric setting that transports visitors centuries back in time.

Visit the Museo del Cenedese for fabulous frescoes, paintings, and works of art, including access to a fully frescoed chapel where babies born in the local hospital were once baptized. The artistic heritage here rivals better-known destinations.

activities.text: Wander the medieval streets photographing the unique architecture and peaceful squares. Unlike more touristy medieval towns, Serravalle offers authentic atmosphere without crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: Veneto's Palladian Villas: Day Trips from Padova to Brenta Canal & Lakes

visits.text: Vogogna appears in a parchment dated 970 and preserves much older traces: a Celtic steatite mask from the third century BC (the Mascherone Celtico) and a Roman inscription commemorating the construction of the Ossola road in 196 BC are displayed in Palazzo Pretorio. Vogogna reached its apex in the fourteenth century: in 1348, Giovanni Visconti, bishop of Novara and lord of Milan, made it the administrative capital of Lower Ossola, rival to Domodossola for Upper Ossola.

Castello Visconteo, commissioned in 1348 by Giovanni Visconti, dominates the village with its semi-circular round tower. Its irregular plan follows the terrain's orography and its battlements still evoke the defensive role the duke assigned it to bar Swiss access to the lakes. In 1798, after three centuries under the Borromeo family, the castle passed to the municipality which used it as a prison until the nineteenth century. Restored from 1990, it now hosts permanent exhibitions on the Celts, Leonardo da Vinci, and the wolf's return.

Palazzo Pretorio (1348), with ogival arches on squat columns in the Lombard broletto tradition, was the seat of Lower Ossola government until 1819. It still preserves traces of original painted decoration, including the Visconti coat of arms on the facade. The mule trail Il respiro della storia starts from the castle, climbs to the hamlet of Genestredo with its oratory of San Martino, then to la Rocca (eleventh century) - a medieval watchtower from which the view over the Ossola valley and the Alps is striking.

activities.text: Hiking in Val Grande National Park - Italy's largest wilderness area, over 70,000 hectares without any permanent human infrastructure in the park's core. Marked trails from Vogogna cross the primeval forests. The Vogogna-Premosello geological trail, marked by the Park, descends to Premosello station. In November, the Montagna e Dintorni festival animates the valley's cultural life for ten days.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: Volpedo is the birthplace of Giuseppe Pellizza (1868-1907), divisionist painter whose work Il Quarto Stato - The Fourth Estate - became the worldwide icon of twentieth-century workers' struggle. Pellizza spent ten years on this monumental canvas (5.45m × 2.93m), completed between 1898 and 1901: he drew inspiration from his native village's Piazza Malaspina as the backdrop for a workers' strike. Bernardo Bertolucci chose this painting for the poster of Novecento (1978). The painting is now preserved at Milan's Museo del Novecento.

The Pellizza museum circuit comprises three sites: the painter's studio (via Rosano, donated to the town hall by his daughters in 1966), the didactic museum presenting preparatory studies for major works (including three large versions of the Fourth Estate), and the Romanesque parish church of San Pietro, built in the tenth century and remodeled in the fifteenth, which holds a painting of San Luigino painted by Pellizza in 1894. Marked trails connect the three sites.

The Romanesque Pieve of San Pietro, whose square bell tower has marked Volpedo's horizon since the tenth century, houses Renaissance frescoes in its side chapels. The historic village nestles on a small hill on the right bank of the Grue torrent, around which the medieval castrum walls were built - some remnants survive at the village edge.

activities.text: Walk through the rolling countryside of the Colli Tortonesi, production zone of Timorasso - a local white grape variety revived in the 1990s after nearly disappearing - and Barbera d'Asti. The vineyards extend to the hamlet of Vho (frazione of Tortona), 10km away.

days_plan.itin_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages in 3 Weeks

visits.text: The Palazzo dei Priori, begun in 1208 and completed in 1254, is Tuscany's oldest town hall—it directly inspired the design of Florence's Palazzo Vecchio. Its local limestone façade bears three rows of mullioned windows and the glazed terracotta coats of arms of Florentine governors. The Council Hall is entirely adorned with 14th-century frescoes. From the monumental tower, views embrace the entire city and hills to the sea.

The Museo Etrusco Guarnacci, founded in 1761 through nobleman Mario Guarnacci's donation of his private collection—over 50,000 volumes and hundreds of funerary urns—is one of Europe's oldest public museums. It houses the world's largest collection of Etruscan alabaster funerary urns, spanning the 9th to 1st century BC. Among the masterpieces: the Urn of the Married Couple and the famous "Shadow of the Evening," a bronze statuette with an elongated body that fascinated Giacometti.

The Alabaster Ecomuseum, installed in the medieval Minucci-Solaini tower, traces the history of this translucent stone from the Etruscans to today's workshops. Volterra has worked alabaster for 2,500 years—the Etruscans already sculpted funerary urns from it. In the 18th century, the local industry reached its peak; today, artisans still work by hand in the center's workshops. The neighboring Pinacoteca preserves Rosso Fiorentino's Deposition from the Cross (1521), considered one of the major works of Tuscan Mannerism.

The Fortezza Medicea, built between 1472 and 1475 at the Medici's request at the city's highest point, is one of the Renaissance's best-preserved defensive works. Its penitentiary remains in operation—a rare European feature. The Roman Theater, rediscovered in the 1950s, dates from the late 1st century BC and could accommodate 1,700 spectators. Names of great Roman families are engraved on the marble seats of the front rows.

Visit Palazzo Incontri-Viti, a 16th-century private residence acquired in 1850 by a wealthy alabaster merchant. Its 12 rooms, still inhabited by descendants, display period furniture, Oriental porcelain, and alabaster pieces, including a candelabra designed for Emperor Maximilian of Mexico—who was executed in 1867 before he could collect it.

activities.text: The Balze: spectacular erosion formations on Volterra's northwest edge, where layers of clay and sand slowly collapse into cliffs and ravines. Several medieval churches have already been engulfed; a 10th-century abbey remains visible on the cliff face. Walk along the edge on foot from the center in 20 minutes.

Alabaster carving workshop: several artisans in the historic center offer demonstrations or introductions to this fragile, translucent material, worked exclusively by hand—a mechanical lathe would shatter the stone. A tradition the Etruscans had already mastered 25 centuries ago.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Pisa Province
Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages

visits.text: Vulcano's Gran Cratere, the summit of a stratovolcano at 391 meters, last erupted in 1890 but continuously vents bright yellow sulfur fumaroles. The 500-meter-wide crater releases plumes visible from the sea. The ascent from Porto Ponente takes 45 minutes up a trail of ash and consolidated lava.

The Laghetto di Fanghi, a pool of gray-yellow mud heated to 35°C by geothermal springs, is the Aeolians' most popular therapeutic site. Bathers come for the sulfurous clay's renowned dermatological properties.

activities.text: Thermal bathing in the warm waters of Porto Levante, where underwater hot springs heat the sea to 40-50°C between the rocks. Free and accessible on foot from the port.
Bike rental for the island circuit (17 km) to the Belvedere del Gelso in the south, facing Filicudi.

days_plan.itin_title: Sicily's Island Circuit: Aeolian, Aegadian & Pantelleria

places.name: Zagarolo

visits.text: Palazzo Rospigliosi di Zagarolo — A 16th-century noble palace built by the Colonna family on the remains of a medieval citadel, acquired in 1629 by Camillo Rospigliosi, whose nephew became Pope Clement IX (1667-1669). The complex includes a palatine church and terraced gardens. The Porta di San Martino, a medieval tuff arch with crenellations, marks the entrance to the historic center.

Centro storico di Zagarolo — A medieval village on wooded hills with sloping lanes, several 16th-17th century noble palaces, and the 17th-century Collegiata di San Lorenzo e Santa Maria Maddalena. Zagarolo was a stop on the ancient Via Praenestina, the Roman road departing from Porta Praenestina in Rome.

activities.text: Tasting Zagarolo DOC wines — The Zagarolo wine appellation, one of Lazio's smallest, produces whites based on Malvasia and Trebbiano cultivated on the volcanic soils of the hills. Local cellars sell directly from the production.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani

map_keywords: Collegiata di San Lorenzo e Santa Maria Maddalena Zagarolo

places.name: Cupola San Pietro

visits.text: The ascent of the dome (Cupolone) offers one of Rome's most breathtaking viewpoints: first the basilica terrace (elevator access), then 320 spiral steps between the dome's two shells to the lantern. Midway, an interior walkway lets you contemplate the basilica's interior from 53 m high. At the summit, at 120 m, the 360° view embraces St. Peter's Square, the Vatican Gardens, Castel Sant'Angelo, the Tiber, and Rome's seven hills.

activities.text: Stop at Castel Sant'Angelo to complete the day: Hadrian's Mausoleum (built 135-139 AD) is the only monumental building from ancient Rome still standing in its entirety, not ruined. After serving as a tomb for emperors until Caracalla (217), then as a fortress, prison (Giordano Bruno, Benvenuto Cellini, and Beatrice Cenci were detained here), and papal residence connected to the Vatican by the Passetto di Borgo, it's now a museum. In 590, Pope Gregory I allegedly had a vision of the archangel Michael sheathing his sword at the summit, announcing the end of a plague epidemic—hence the current name.

Ponte Sant'Angelo: the ten marble angels lining it were designed by Bernini between 1667 and 1669, each carrying an instrument of Christ's Passion. The two statues sculpted by Bernini himself are preserved at Sant'Andrea delle Fratte—the angels currently on the bridge are copies. Remarkable view of the castle at sunset.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Cupola di San Pietro Città del Vaticano

places.name: Musei Vaticani

visits.text: The Vatican Museums form one of the world's largest museum complexes, with approximately 1,400 rooms. The core of the collections was founded by Julius II at the beginning of the 16th century with the installation of the Laocoön and other ancient sculptures in the Belvedere courtyard. The Sistine Chapel (built 1477-1481, 40.93 m by 13.41 m, height 20.7 m) was decorated by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1512 for Pope Julius II: 500 m² of fresco, 350 individual figures painted almost single-handedly, working lying down on scaffolding 20 meters high. The vault depicts nine scenes from Genesis; the Creation of Adam is its most famous central panel. In 1534, Clement VII commissioned Michelangelo to paint the Last Judgment on the altar wall, completed in 1541. This is where cardinals gather in conclave to elect the pope: white smoke rises from the chimney on the chapel's roof.

The Raphael Rooms (papal chambers on the 3rd floor) were decorated between 1509 and 1520 by Raphael and his pupils at Julius II's request. The School of Athens (Stanza della Segnatura) depicts ancient Greece's greatest philosophers around Plato and Aristotle—Plato has Leonardo da Vinci's face, and Raphael included himself in a discreet self-portrait on the right. The Pinacoteca gathers paintings by Raphael, Leonardo da Vinci, Giotto, Caravaggio, and Titian.

activities.text: Book Vatican Museums tickets several weeks in advance during high season: queues without reservations can exceed 2 to 3 hours. A complete visit (Museums + Sistine Chapel) requires a minimum of 3 to 4 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Rome: Historic Center, Ancient Ruins & Vatican Treasures

map_keywords: Stanze di Raffaello Città del Vaticano
Cortile del Belvedere Città del Vaticano

places.name: San Pietro

visits.text: St. Peter's Basilica is the world's largest Christian church: 218.7 m long, 154.8 m transept, 22,067 m² surface area. Its construction, begun in 1506 by Julius II following Bramante's plans, wasn't completed until 1626 under Urban VIII—120 years and at least seven successive architects (Bramante, Raphael, Sangallo, Michelangelo, della Porta, Maderno, Bernini). Michelangelo, appointed chief architect at age 72 in 1546, designed the double-shelled dome inspired by Brunelleschi's dome in Florence—its interior diameter of 42 m and height of 136.57 m to the top of the cross make it the world's tallest dome. He died in 1564 without seeing it completed; Giacomo della Porta and Domenico Fontana finished it in 22 months with 800 workers laboring day and night, in 1588-1590. Inside, Michelangelo's Pietà (1499) was sculpted at age 24 from a single block of Carrara marble—it's the only work he signed, his name carved on Mary's sash after a visitor mistakenly attributed it to another master. Bernini's bronze baldachin (1623-1634), 29 m high, is placed directly above St. Peter's tomb.

St. Peter's Square (1657-1667) is Bernini's elliptical colonnade: 284 columns and 88 pilasters in 4 rows, crowned by 140 statues of saints. On the ground, two marble discs mark the ellipse's focal points—from these points, the four rows of columns merge into a single line, creating the illusion of a simple colonnade. The central obelisk (25.31 m) was erected in Egypt under Caligula then transported to Rome.

activities.text: Descend to the Vatican Grottoes (free entry through the basilica) where 91 popes are buried. The deeper scavi necropolis reveals excavations conducted under Paul VI starting in 1939 that uncovered what's identified as St. Peter's tomb—visit by reservation with the excavations office.
